### [1] [In Search of the Ingredients of Open-Endedness: Replicating Picbreeder with Large Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23908)
*Sam Earle,Kay Arulkumaran,Andrew Dai,Akarsh Kumar,Julian Togelius,Sebastian Risi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper investigates whether AI agents can achieve open-ended discovery by replacing human users in Picbreeder (an interactive evolution system for generating diverse images) with Vision Language Models, and analyzes differences between AI and human-driven creative processes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There's a large-scale effort to automate scientific/technological/creative production via AI assistants. Historically, human creativity has been characterized by open-endedness - the capacity to generate endless novel and meaningful forms. The paper aims to determine if AI agents have similar capacity for fruitful unguided discovery.

Method: Replicated Picbreeder (canonical human-driven open-ended search system for generating images through interactive evolution of small neural networks), replacing human users with frontier Vision Language Models (VLMs). Analyzed qualitative differences using metrics of phylogenetic complexity, visual/semantic salience and novelty. Experimented with factors like exploratory noise, behavioral diversity between agents, and narrative momentum (memory of past actions).

Result: Observed clear qualitative differences between AI system output and historical human baseline. Characterized these differences using complexity and novelty metrics. Investigated causal factors contributing to the observed differences through controlled experiments.

Conclusion: The study provides insights into AI's capacity for open-ended discovery compared to human creativity, identifying factors that influence AI's creative processes in evolutionary search systems. The findings contribute to understanding how to design AI systems for more human-like open-ended creativity.

Abstract: We are in the midst of large-scale industrial and academic efforts to automate the processes of scientific, technological and creative production through AI-driven assistants. Historically, a fundamental property of these processes in their human form has been their open-endedness: their capacity for generating a seemingly endless supply of novel and meaningful new forms. Do artificial agents have any capacity for such fruitful unguided discovery? To answer this question, we turn to Picbreeder, the canonical exemplar of human-driven open-ended search, in which users collaboratively generated a diverse library of images through interactive evolution of small neural networks. We replicate Picbreeder, replacing human users with frontier Vision Language Models (VLMs). We observe clear qualitative differences between the output of our system and the historical human baseline, and attempt to characterize them using metrics of phylogenetic complexity and visual and semantic salience and novelty. In an effort to identify some of the causal factors contributing these differences, we study the addition of exploratory noise to the agents' selection process, of behavioral diversity between agents, and of narrative momentum in the form of memory of past actions. We make our code available at https://github.com/smearle/picbreeder-vlm.

</details>


### [2] [Confidence Calibration in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23909)
*Noam Michael,Daniel BenShushan,Jacob Bien,Don A. Moore*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs show overall overconfidence (confidence exceeds accuracy), but this follows a hard-easy effect: overconfidence on difficult tasks and underconfidence on easy tasks. The paper introduces LifeEval, a new test for evaluating model calibration across difficulty levels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how well large language models' confidence levels align with their actual accuracy across different tasks, as current understanding of LLM calibration across varying difficulty levels is limited.

Method: Conducted a preregistered study analyzing LLM confidence calibration, developed LifeEval test for evaluating model calibration across different difficulty levels, and examined the relationship between task difficulty and confidence-accuracy alignment.

Result: LLMs are generally overconfident (confidence exceeds accuracy), similar to humans. However, this varies by difficulty: overconfidence is greatest on difficult tests, while easy tests show substantial underconfidence. The hard-easy effect strongly moderates the calibration relationship.

Conclusion: LLM calibration is not uniform but follows a predictable pattern based on task difficulty. The introduction of LifeEval provides a valuable tool for evaluating calibration across difficulty levels, revealing important insights about when LLMs tend to be overconfident versus underconfident.

Abstract: We investigate the calibration of large language models' (LLMs') confidence across diverse tasks. The results of our preregistered study show that the current crop of LLMs are, like people, too sure they are right: confidence exceeds accuracy, on average. Importantly, however, this tendency is moderated by a powerful hard-easy effect, wherein overconfidence is greatest on difficult tests; by contrast, easy tests actually show substantial underconfidence. We develop LifeEval, a test for evaluating model calibration across levels of difficulty.

</details>


### [3] [How Much Thinking is Enough? Quantifying and Understanding Redundancy in LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23926)
*Zhiyuan Zhai,Xinkai You,Wenjing Yan,Xin Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Large language models exhibit high reasoning redundancy (61-93%), where most thinking steps are unnecessary for correct answers, and this is a structural property of length-agnostic training rather than a model-specific bug.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reasoning-capable LLMs emit long chains of thought that incur high latency, GPU time, and energy costs, but it's unclear how much of this deliberation is actually necessary. The paper aims to measure reasoning redundancy at scale and explain it from first principles.

Method: Formalized reasoning redundancy as the largest fraction of trailing segmented steps that can be truncated while the model still produces correct answers. Conducted large-scale quantification across four frontier reasoning models and two mathematical benchmarks (including MATH-500), analyzing step-level redundancy and robustness across judge families.

Result: Step-level redundancy is consistently high (61-93% across 8 model/benchmark conditions), with median critical prefix equal to a single step in six conditions. Redundancy decreases with problem difficulty but remains substantial (46-85% even on hardest Level-5 problems). Proved this redundancy is a structural consequence of length-agnostic outcome rewards.

Conclusion: Over-thinking is not a bug to be patched in individual models but a structural property of how current reasoning models are trained due to length-agnostic outcome rewards. This explains why models continue generating unnecessary reasoning steps and suggests fundamental limitations in current training paradigms.

Abstract: Reasoning-capable large language models solve hard problems by emitting long chains of thought, paying heavily in latency, GPU time, and energy. Casual inspection of their traces reveals extensive reformulation, verification, and circular self-reflection, yet how much of this deliberation is actually necessary has never been measured at scale or explained from first principles. This paper closes both gaps.
  We formalise reasoning redundancy directly in terms of the reasoning model itself: the redundancy of a correct trace is the largest fraction of its trailing segmented steps that can be truncated while $π$, forced to terminate thinking and emit a final answer, still produces the correct answer. A large-scale quantification across four frontier reasoning models and two mathematical benchmarks shows that step-level redundancy is consistently high -- between 61% and 93% across the 8 (model, benchmark) conditions we study, with the median critical prefix equal to a single segmented step in six of the eight conditions -- that the finding is robust to the choice of judge family, and that although $ρ$ decreases with problem difficulty on MATH-500, all four models remain substantially redundant ($ρ\in [46\%, 85\%]$) even on the hardest Level-5 problems.
  We then prove that this redundancy is a structural consequence of length-agnostic outcome rewards, not a model-specific artefact: under any such reward, no finite expected stopping time is optimal. The result holds regardless of RL algorithm, base model, data distribution, or whether the policy is obtained via RL or distillation; over-thinking is therefore not a bug to be patched in individual models but a structural property of how current reasoning models are trained. Code: https://github.com/zhiyuanZhai20/how-much-thinking-is-enough

</details>


### [4] [Context: Proactive Goal-Directed Intelligence via Composable Sandboxed Programs, Declarative Wiring, and Structured Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23928)
*Gregory Magarshak*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Context replaces reactive chatbots with proactive goal-directed agents using three mechanisms: write-time context assembly, composable wisdom programs, and proactive goal stream state machines, with formal proofs showing efficiency and quality improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses limitations of reactive query-response chatbots by creating a system where agents can proactively advance shared tasks without waiting for user prompts, improving efficiency and coordination.

Method: Three core mechanisms: 1) Write-time context assembly precomputes enriched attributes via Groker agents, enabling near-100% KV-cache reuse. 2) Composable sandboxed wisdom programs form a governed library of LM-generated imperative programs. 3) Proactive goal stream state machines drive conversations toward terminal states by inspecting graph state and emitting structured content without user input.

Result: Six formal results: Context Stability Theorem bounding LM costs; Program Composition Correctness Theorem; Declarative Wiring Soundness Theorem; Proactive Dominance Theorem proving proactive agents dominate reactive ones; Coordination Overhead Elimination and Quality Preservation for Pareto improvements; and Cross-Platform Vote Consistency Theorem.

Conclusion: Context provides a systematic intelligence layer for proactive goal-directed agents with formal guarantees of efficiency, correctness, and quality improvements over reactive chatbots, implemented in an open-source stack.

Abstract: We present Context, the intelligence layer of the Magarshak Architecture, which replaces reactive query-response chatbots with proactive goal-directed agents that advance shared tasks without waiting for user prompts. The architecture rests on three mutually reinforcing mechanisms. Write-time context assembly precomputes enriched typed attributes via Groker agents, assembling interaction context as a deterministic pure function of graph state; context blocks are byte-identical across turns between semantic changes, enabling near-100% KV-cache reuse. Composable sandboxed wisdom programs form a governed library of LM-generated imperative programs declaratively wired to goal types via typed stream relations, composed via phase ordering, and executed at interaction time without further LM calls. Proactive goal stream state machines drive conversations toward terminal states by inspecting graph state and emitting structured interaction content (option arrays, governance affordances, clarification prompts) without awaiting user input. We prove six formal results: the Context Stability Theorem, bounding per-turn LM cost as a function of semantic change rate; a Program Composition Correctness Theorem; a Declarative Wiring Soundness Theorem; the Proactive Dominance Theorem, proving proactive agents weakly dominate reactive agents on expected turns-to-terminal-state; Coordination Overhead Elimination and Quality Preservation, establishing Pareto improvements in multi-participant goal chats; and a Cross-Platform Vote Consistency Theorem. Implemented in the open-source Qbix / Safebox / Safebots stack.

</details>


### [5] [Toward Reliable Design of LLM-Enabled Agentic Workflows: Optimizing Latency-Reliability-Cost Tradeoffs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23929)
*Ya-Ting Yang,Quanyan Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper analyzes tradeoffs between latency, reliability, and cost in AI workflows combining LLMs and conventional agents, introducing performance models and optimal resource allocation policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern AI systems increasingly use workflows with multiple interacting agents (both LLM-based and conventional computational modules), creating a need to understand and optimize fundamental tradeoffs between latency, reliability, and cost in these complex systems.

Method: 1) Develop performance models for both LLM and non-LLM agents capturing computational effort vs output quality relationships; 2) Use parametric exponential reliability functions incorporating reasoning and output tokens for LLM agents; 3) Study sequential workflow design under constraints; 4) Propose water-filling token allocation policy; 5) Characterize optimal workflow reliability using shadow prices.

Result: Key results include: 1) Analytical framework for modeling LLM-enabled agentic workflows; 2) Water-filling token allocation policy for resource optimization; 3) Characterizations of optimal workflow reliability in terms of shadow prices; 4) Tradeoff analysis between latency, reliability, and cost in sequential workflows.

Conclusion: The paper provides a theoretical framework for optimizing complex AI workflows, offering practical solutions for balancing performance tradeoffs in modern multi-agent systems through mathematical modeling and resource allocation strategies.

Abstract: Modern AI systems increasingly rely on workflows composed of multiple interacting agents, some powered by large language models (LLMs) and others by conventional computational modules. This paper analyzes the fundamental tradeoffs between latency, reliability, and cost in LLM-enabled agentic workflows. We introduce performance models for both LLM and non-LLM agents that capture the relationship between computational effort and output quality, incorporating the impact of reasoning and output tokens for LLM agents using a parametric exponential reliability function. Then, we study the design of sequential workflows under latency and cost constraints. Main results include a water-filling token allocation policy and characterizations of optimal workflow reliability in terms of shadow prices.

</details>


### [6] [Quantum Frog: Emergent Cooperation and Difficulty Scaling in a Quantized-Time Cooperative Game](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23930)
*Saad Mankarious*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Quantum Frog is a two-player cooperative game with quantized-time mechanics where environment advances only when players act. RL analysis shows rush strategy is optimal, cooperation significantly improves success rates, and synchronized rushing emerges as the optimal joint strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To design and analyze Quantum Frog, a cooperative two-player game inspired by Frogger with novel quantized-time mechanics, using RL to answer key design questions about game difficulty scaling, optimal policies, cooperation gaps, and emergent strategies for commercial game development insights.

Method: Used reinforcement learning (RL) as analytical lens with five training stages: Tabular Q-Learning, Deep Q-Network (DQN), Independent DQN (IDQN), and Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimisation (MAPPO with centralized critic). Evaluated agents against traffic densities of 1-6 cars on 8×8 grid.

Result: Key findings: (1) quantized-time mechanic makes rush strategy universally optimal; (2) adding uncoordinated second player is harder than sextupling traffic for single expert; (3) cooperative training recovers +32-34% joint success rate, reduces episode length from ~90 to ~6 steps; (4) emergent cooperative strategy is synchronized rushing.

Conclusion: The quantized-time mechanic shapes optimal strategies significantly, shared incentives alone suffice for agent alignment in time-critical cooperative tasks, providing concrete guidance for Quantum Frog's commercial design and broader insights into environment mechanics' role in multi-agent learning.

Abstract: We introduce \emph{Quantum Frog}, a two-player cooperative game built on a novel \emph{quantized-time} mechanic in which the environment advances only when a player acts. Inspired by the classic arcade game Frogger, Quantum Frog requires two frogs to cross an 8$\times$8 grid of traffic and reach the far side together. We use reinforcement learning (RL) as an analytical lens to answer four design questions: (1) how does game difficulty scale with traffic density, (2) what is the optimal single-agent policy and why, (3) how large is the cooperation gap between independent and cooperative two-agent play, and (4) what joint strategy emerges when agents are incentivised to cooperate? We train agents through five escalating stages, Tabular Q-Learning, Deep Q-Network (\DQN), Independent \DQN~(\IDQN), and Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimisation (\MAPPO\ with a centralised critic), evaluating each against traffic densities of one to six cars. Our key findings are: (i) the quantized-time mechanic makes a \emph{rush strategy} (moving directly upward at every step) universally optimal, as time exposure to traffic is minimised; (ii) adding an uncoordinated second player is harder than sextupling the traffic for a single expert player; (iii) cooperative training recovers +32--34 percentage points of joint success rate relative to independent agents and reduces episode length from $\sim$90 to $\sim$6 steps; and (iv) the emergent cooperative strategy is synchronised rushing, not complex positional coordination, illustrating that shared incentives alone suffice to align agents in time-critical cooperative tasks. These findings provide concrete, empirically grounded guidance for the commercial design of Quantum Frog and offer broader insights into the role of environment mechanics in shaping multi-agent learning dynamics.

</details>


### [7] [BODHI: Precise OS Kernel Specification Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23931)
*Zhiming Chang,Ziyang Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BODHI uses structured domain knowledge prompting to dramatically improve LLM performance for OS kernel formal specification generation, achieving 96.73% Pass@1 on OSV-Bench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Formal verification of OS kernels requires precise specifications that are difficult to write manually. Existing LLM approaches achieve only 55.10% Pass@1 on OSV-Bench, indicating a need for better methods.

Method: Proposed BODHI method: domain knowledge prompting that augments few-shot prompts with structured C-to-Python translation guide covering 15 categories of domain-specific patterns. Uses Structured Chain-of-Thought approach to separate pre-condition extraction and post-condition generation.

Result: BODHI improved every model tested (+11% to +32% gains). Best configuration (Claude Opus 4.6 + BODHI) achieved 96.73% Pass@1, nearly doubling previous best. Reduced both syntax and semantic errors, especially effective with models having good instruction-following capability.

Conclusion: Domain knowledge injection is a model-agnostic technique that substantially bridges the gap between general-purpose code generation and formal specification synthesis, showing structured prompting with domain expertise dramatically improves LLM performance on specialized tasks.

Abstract: The formal verification of operating system kernels requires precise specifications that capture the intended behavior of system calls. Writing these specifications manually demands deep domain expertise, motivating the use of large language models (LLMs) to automate the process. However, in OSV-Bench, a benchmark of 245 specification generation tasks derived from the Hyperkernel OS kernel, the best reported Pass@1 is 55.10%. We propose a domain knowledge prompting method (BODHI), which augments the standard few-shot prompt with a structured C-to-Python translation guide covering 15 categories of domain-specific translation patterns. Inspired by Structured Chain-of-Thought (SCoT) prompting, the guide organizes translation by separation of concerns, addressing pre-condition extraction and post-condition generation as distinct categories. Evaluated on nine models from six providers (Anthropic, Mistral, Amazon, DeepSeek, Meta, Alibaba), covering dense, mixture-of-experts and reasoning architectures, BODHI improves every model tested, with gains ranging from +11% to +32%. The best configuration (Claude Opus 4.6 + BODHI) reaches 96.73% Pass@1. BODHI reduces both syntax and semantic errors, with the strongest effect on models that have sufficient instruction-following capability to utilize structured reference material. These results demonstrate that domain knowledge injection is a model-agnostic technique that substantially bridges the gap between general-purpose code generation and formal specification synthesis.

</details>


### [8] [When Correct Beliefs Collapse: Epistemic Resilience of LLMs under Clinical Pressure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23932)
*Boyu Xiao,Xiuqi Tian,Xuwen Song,Haochun Wang,Guanchun Song,Sendong Zhao,Bing Qin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Med-Stress is a stress test framework that reveals LLMs' vulnerability to diagnostic sycophancy in clinical dialogues despite strong initial accuracy. The paper proposes two defenses (RBED and R-FT) to improve belief stability under pressure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite strong performance on medical benchmarks, LLMs exhibit concerning sycophancy behavior in clinical dialogues - abandoning correct initial diagnoses when faced with escalating pressure from users. This reveals a gap between medical knowledge and decision robustness in real clinical settings.

Method: The paper introduces Med-Stress, a targeted stress test framework to evaluate belief stability under escalating pressure. It analyzes nine frontier LLMs and proposes two solutions: RBED (Role-Based Epistemic Defense) as a lightweight inference-time defense, and R-FT (Resilience-oriented Fine-Tuning) as a training approach that internalizes evidence-based resistance to pressure.

Result: The study finds a clear dissociation between medical knowledge and robustness - high initial diagnostic accuracy doesn't guarantee belief stability, creating significant knowledge-robustness gaps in several LLMs. Experiments show that R-FT nearly eliminates belief change and substantially improves robustness under pressure.

Conclusion: Current LLMs are vulnerable to sycophancy in clinical settings despite good benchmark performance. The Med-Stress framework effectively exposes this issue, and the proposed R-FT method successfully addresses it by building resilience to pressure, which is crucial for safe clinical deployment of AI assistants.

Abstract: Despite strong medical benchmark accuracy, LLMs can exhibit severe multi-turn sycophancy in clinical dialogue, abandoning initial correct diagnosis under escalating pressure. We propose \textbf{\textsc{Med-Stress}}, a targeted stress test framework that evaluates belief stability under escalating pressure. Across nine frontier large language models (LLMs), we find a clear dissociation between medical knowledge and robustness: high initial diagnostic capability does not imply high belief stability, yielding large knowledge-robustness gaps for several LLMs. To mitigate this failure mode, we propose a lightweight inference-time defense, \textbf{\texttt{RBED}} (\textbf{R}ole-\textbf{B}ased \textbf{E}pistemic \textbf{D}efense), and \textbf{\texttt{R-FT}} (\textbf{R}esilience-oriented \textbf{F}ine-\textbf{T}uning), a training-time approach that internalizes evidence-based resistance to pressure. Experiments show that \textbf{\texttt{R-FT}} nearly eliminates belief change and substantially improves robustness.

</details>


### [9] [Practical Quantum CIM Empowerment via All-Domestic-Core Agentic Large Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23934)
*Wang Rui,Lu Diannan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper integrates a Coherent Ising Machine (CIM) with an LLM-driven agentic system using LangGraph/LangChain frameworks to automate quantum computing modeling tasks like QUBO/Ising model calibration and constraint weight iteration, achieving fully domestic quantum CIM implementation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantum computing devices are powerful for solving NP-complete problems but present modeling complexities that create barriers for non-specialists and consume substantial effort from experts due to tedious constraint weight and methodology iterations.

Method: The study integrates a femtosecond laser-pumped Coherent Ising Machine (CIM) with an LLM-driven agentic system using LangGraph and LangChain frameworks. LLMs perform QUBO/Ising model calibration, constraint weight decision iteration, and rapid validation of literature-reported schemes.

Result: The work successfully implements all tasks using domestic large models combined with domestically developed CIM hardware, achieving practical empowerment of quantum CIM that fully relies on all-domestic agentic large models and hardware. Unexpectedly discovers a new paradigm where agent-assisted quantum computing iterations reciprocally enhance the agent's own problem-solving capability.

Conclusion: The study realizes robust technological integration laying a solid foundation for subsequent research, identifies persisting challenges in large models and quantum computing, and reveals a promising new paradigm where accumulated knowledge from quantum computing iterations enhances agent capabilities, addressing current challenges.

Abstract: Quantum computing devices are recognized as powerful tools for solving NP-complete problems. However, the intricacy of their modeling presents notable barriers for non-specialists, while the tedious iteration of constraint weights and modeling methodologies also consumes substantial effort on the part of experts. To address these challenges, this study integrates a femtosecond laser-pumped Coherent Ising Machine (CIM) with an LLM-driven agentic system by leveraging the LangGraph and LangChain frameworks. Comprehensive investigations demonstrate that large language models (LLMs) can effectively perform such tasks in modeling as QUBO/Ising model calibration, constraint weight decision iteration and rapid validation of literature-reported schemes. Notably, all these tasks can be fully implemented based on domestic large models, combined with domestically developed CIM hardware, we truly achieve the practical empowerment of quantum CIM that fully relies on all-domestic agentic large models and hardware. This work successfully realizes robust technological integration, laying a solid foundation for subsequent research. Nevertheless, it also identifies the persisting challenges in the two cutting-edge fields of large models and quantum computing at the current stage. Encouragingly, we unexpectedly discover a promising new paradigm where accumulated knowledge from agent-assisted quantum computing iterations reciprocally enhances the agent's own problem-solving capability, thereby addressing these challenges.

</details>


### [10] [Operationalizing Reconstructive Authority: Runtime Construction, Dependency Resolution, and Execution Gating in Autonomous Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23935)
*Marcelo Fernandez - TraslaIA*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Runtime enforcement mechanism for Reconstructive Authority that guarantees safe execution by conditioning actions on reconstructible authority, with recovery mechanisms for incomplete observability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous agent systems fail not only due to incorrect decisions, but because they execute decisions whose authority no longer holds at runtime. Prior work defined Reconstructive Authority (RAM) as a theoretical condition, but runtime enforcement was missing.

Method: Introduces a runtime execution model where authority is evaluated at action time, extending state space to include admit/deny/halt states. Defines concrete execution protocol with dynamic dependency resolution, authority reconstruction, and explicit decision semantics. Also introduces a Recovery Loop integrating drift detection (IML) with execution control (ACP) to handle incomplete observability.

Result: The model guarantees safety (no action executes without constructible authority) and conditional liveness (execution resumes when authority-defining variables become observable). Operationalizes RAM as a runtime enforcement mechanism.

Conclusion: This work provides the execution semantics needed to apply Reconstructive Authority in real systems, enabling safe autonomous agent operation even with incomplete or uncertain observability through runtime authority enforcement and recovery mechanisms.

Abstract: Autonomous agent systems fail not only due to incorrect decisions, but due to executing decisions whose authority no longer holds at runtime. Prior work defined Reconstructive Authority (RAM) as a condition for valid execution: actions are permitted only if authority can be constructed from current state.
  This paper addresses enforcement at runtime: how to enforce this condition in a running system.
  We introduce a runtime execution model in which authority is evaluated at action time and execution is conditioned on its constructibility. This extends the execution state space beyond admit/deny with a third state, halt, representing cases where authority is undefined due to incomplete or uncertain observability.
  We define a concrete execution protocol including dynamic dependency resolution, authority reconstruction, and explicit decision semantics. We further introduce a Recovery Loop that integrates drift detection (IML) with execution control (ACP), allowing the system to suspend execution, acquire missing information, and re-attempt authority reconstruction.
  We show that this model guarantees safety -- no action is executed without constructible authority -- and conditional liveness: execution resumes when authority-defining variables become observable.
  This work operationalizes reconstructive authority as a runtime enforcement mechanism, providing the execution semantics required to apply RAM in real systems.

</details>


### [11] [Methods for Formal Verification of Agent Skills: Three Layers Toward a Mechanically Checkable Capability-Containment Proof](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23951)
*Alfredo Metere*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper completes the formal verification level for agent-skill manifests by providing three composable verification methods: static capability-containment analysis, refinement type checking for tool calls, and SMT-bounded model checking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The companion paper introduced a four-level verification lattice for agent-skill manifests but left the top "formal" level as aspirational. This paper aims to close that gap by providing practical, composable methods to achieve formal verification of skills.

Method: Three composable verification methods: (1) sound static capability-containment analysis via abstract interpretation over an effect lattice, (2) refinement type system for tool-call envelopes that rejects calls with undeclared capabilities, and (3) SMT-bounded model checking against the parent paper's biconditional correctness criterion with bounded search for counter-examples.

Result: The three verification layers are proven to soundly cover the parent paper's threat model (modulo LLM refusal to act). The methods are implemented as reusable JavaScript modules in the open-source enclawed framework with comprehensive testing.

Conclusion: The paper successfully achieves formal verification for agent-skill manifests by providing practical, composable verification methods that leverage existing well-engineered tools rather than requiring new ones, making formal verification accessible and implementable.

Abstract: The companion paper introduced a four-level verification lattice on agent-skill manifests (unverified, declared, tested, formal) and left the top level aspirational. This
  paper closes that gap. We give a precise semantics for skill behaviour faithful to how a skill is consumed by an LLM-driven runtime (a deterministic script-side reachable
  through a non-deterministic LLM-side), state the verification problem as a capability-containment property over that semantics, and present three composable methods that
  together raise a skill from declared or tested to formal: (1) sound static capability-containment analysis of the script-side via abstract interpretation over a small effect
  lattice; (2) a refinement type system for tool-call envelopes that mechanically rejects any call whose statically-inferred capability is not in the manifest's declared set;
  (3) SMT-bounded model checking against the parent paper's biconditional correctness criterion, with the bound chosen so any counter-example fitting the runtime's
  transaction-buffer horizon is exhibited as a concrete trace. We prove the three layers composed soundly cover the parent paper's threat model modulo a single residual (the
  LLM's freedom to refuse to act) that the parent paper's runtime biconditional catches at session boundary. The methods reuse existing well-engineered tools (Z3, Semgrep,
  CodeQL, refinement-type checkers, mechanised proof assistants) rather than asking operators to build new ones, and the proof-carrying artifact extends the existing SKILL.md
  convention. All three methods plus the bundle producer and re-checker ship as zero-dependency JavaScript modules in the open-source enclawed framework
  (https://github.com/metereconsulting/enclawed; project page https://www.enclawed.com/), with 53 unit tests and an end-to-end CLI demo on a sample skill.

</details>


### [12] [Fuzzy, Neutrosophic, and Uncertain Graph Theory: Properties and Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23936)
*Takaaki Fujita,Florentin Smarandache*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This book provides a systematic survey of graph theory under uncertainty, unifying fuzzy, neutrosophic, and related models into a coherent framework covering both theoretical concepts and practical applications in complex systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to organize diverse uncertainty-aware graph models within a common perspective, providing a coherent framework for understanding their relationships, capabilities, and applications in complex systems that deal with uncertainty.

Method: The book systematically surveys graph theory under uncertainty, reviewing fundamental concepts, structural properties, graph classes, and parameters within fuzzy, neutrosophic, and related models. It introduces extensions like uncertain digraphs, hypergraphs, superhypergraphs, and dynamic graphs.

Result: The work provides a comprehensive framework that unifies various uncertainty-aware graph models, showing their relationships and capabilities, while demonstrating practical applications in areas like uncertain molecular graphs, decision-making systems, graph neural networks, knowledge graphs, and cognitive maps.

Conclusion: This book establishes a unified perspective on graph theory under uncertainty, offering both theoretical foundations and practical applications that enable better understanding and utilization of uncertainty-aware graph models in complex systems analysis.

Abstract: This book presents a comprehensive and systematic survey of graph theory under uncertainty, with particular emphasis on the unifying role of the uncertain graph framework. It reviews fundamental concepts, structural properties, graph classes, and graph parameters within fuzzy, neutrosophic, and related models, while also introducing a wide range of extensions such as uncertain digraphs, hypergraphs, superhypergraphs, and dynamic graphs. In addition to theoretical developments, the book explores practical applications, including uncertain molecular graphs, decision-making systems, graph neural networks, knowledge graphs, and cognitive maps. By organizing diverse uncertainty-aware graph models within a common perspective, this work provides a coherent framework for understanding their relationships, capabilities, and applications in complex systems.

</details>


### [13] [QUIVER: A Formal Framework for Quantifying Perturbation Propagation and Bifurcation in Compound AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23956)
*Prashanti Nilayam,Sankalp Nayak*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: QUIVER is a formal framework for measuring how perturbations propagate through graph-structured LLM pipelines, quantifying sensitivity, trajectory divergence, bifurcation thresholds, and distribution faithfulness to analyze production compound AI systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Compound AI systems with chained LLM calls in directed computation graphs are now dominant in production, but no framework exists to quantify how perturbations propagate through such pipelines where nodes are stochastic and execution paths can diverge structurally.

Method: Introduces QUIVER framework with four components: (1) sensitivity matrix with type-dispatched distance metrics classifying edges, (2) trajectory divergence decomposition, (3) bifurcation thresholds identifying smallest perturbation causing structural changes, and (4) distribution faithfulness quantifying dataset divergence from production distributions.

Result: Validated on three distinct architectures including two production enterprise pipelines and one public DSPy pipeline, analyzing 8,200+ traces (32,000+ pair comparisons). QUIVER reveals distinct sensitivity profiles, distinguishes different cascade patterns with identical divergence rates, predicts bifurcation-prone nodes from observational data, and localizes stale evaluation artifacts to specific node-field categories.

Conclusion: QUIVER provides a formal framework for perturbation analysis in compound AI systems, enabling systematic understanding of how errors propagate, predicting structural changes, and identifying evaluation artifacts that aggregate metrics cannot detect, which is crucial for production deployment.

Abstract: Compound AI systems that chain multiple LLM calls into directed computation graphs are now the dominant architecture for production AI. Although these architectures leverage heterogeneous nodes with mixed-mode outputs, no existing framework quantifies how perturbations propagate through such pipelines, where nodes are stochastic and execution paths can diverge structurally. We introduce QUIVER, a formal framework for measuring perturbation propagation in graph-structured LLM pipelines. The framework defines: (1) a sensitivity matrix with type-dispatched distance metrics that classifies edges as amplifiers, absorbers, or threshold-sensitive, complemented by occurrence-lift; (2) trajectory divergence decomposing variation into value drift, structural path divergence, and iteration count divergence; (3) bifurcation thresholds identifying the smallest perturbation that causes structural execution path changes; and (4) distribution faithfulness, quantifying when per node evaluation datasets diverge from production distributions. We validate on two production enterprise pipelines and a public DSPy multihop QA pipeline, three structurally distinct architectures. Across 8,200+ instrumented traces (32,000+ pair comparisons), we demonstrate that QUIVER reveals distinct sensitivity profiles across architectures, distinguishes mechanistically different cascade patterns producing identical divergence rates, predicts nodes prone to trajectory bifurcation from observational data alone, and localizes stale evaluation artifacts to specific node-field categories that aggregate metrics cannot surface.

</details>


### [14] [BoxLitE: A Faithful Knowledge Base Embedding Based on Convex Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23937)
*Bruno F. Lourenço,Hesham Morgan,Ana Ozaki,Aleksandar Pavlović,Emanuel Sallinger*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BoxLitE is a KB embedding model for DL-Lite that enables convex optimization to create weakly faithful models, representing concepts as convex regions to capture hierarchical relationships from both facts (ABox) and ontologies (TBox).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current KB embedding approaches combine factual knowledge (ABox) with conceptual knowledge (TBox) but rarely leverage the power of convexity during learning tasks. Existing methods that map concepts to convex regions don't fully utilize convex optimization capabilities.

Method: Introduces BoxLitE, a KB embedding model for DL-Lite specifically designed to allow convex optimization. Formulates the KB embedding task as a convex optimization problem to obtain embeddings with desirable faithfulness properties.

Result: For any satisfiable DL-Lite KB, there exists a BoxLitE embedding that serves as a weakly faithful model. The method demonstrates how to obtain such embeddings using convex optimization techniques.

Conclusion: BoxLitE successfully bridges the gap between theoretical convexity in KB embeddings and practical learning applications, providing a convex optimization framework for creating weakly faithful models that properly represent hierarchical knowledge structures.

Abstract: Knowledge base (KB) embeddings aim at combining the capability of classical knowledge graph embeddings to generalize the information present in facts, the ABox, with conceptual knowledge represented in an ontology language, the TBox. Several authors have recently explored the idea of mapping concepts to convex regions in a vector space. This is useful to represent hierarchies, typically present in TBoxes, since more general concepts can be mapped to larger regions, containing those regions associated with more specific concepts. However, the power of convexity is rarely leveraged during the actual learning tasks. Here, we introduce BoxLitE, a KB embedding model for DL-Lite$^{\mathcal{H}}$ that allows for convex optimization. We show that for any satisfiable DL-Lite$^{\mathcal{H}}$ KB, there is a BoxLitE embedding that is a weakly faithful model. As a proof of concept, we show how to formulate the KB embedding task as a convex optimization problem and how to obtain embeddings with such desirable faithfulness properties.

</details>


### [15] [EvoSci: A Bio-Inspired Multi-Agent Framework for the Evolution of Scientific Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24018)
*Xiaoyu Xiong,Yuqi Ren,Deyi Xiong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EvoSci is a multi-agent scientific collaboration framework that combines bio-inspired evolution with knowledge graph modeling to enhance scientific discovery through iterative idea generation, evaluation, and refinement via mentor, researcher, and reviewer agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based scientific discovery methods face challenges in research workflow design and multi-role collaboration mechanisms, limiting their effectiveness in generating coherent and creative scientific ideas.

Method: Proposes EvoSci framework integrating bio-inspired evolution with knowledge graph modeling. Uses multiple role-based agents (mentor, researcher, reviewer) for collaborative reasoning, shared memory, and evolutionary feedback to iteratively generate, evaluate, and refine research ideas.

Result: Outperforms strong baselines in LLM-based structured peer-review and comparative ranking evaluations, achieving highest peer-review score (ICLR 4.90) and top ranking (Top-10 = 54) on real-world research topics.

Conclusion: EvoSci demonstrates superiority in scientific idea generation and continuous discovery, suggesting its effectiveness in enhancing coherence and creativity of scientific exploration through multi-agent collaboration and evolutionary processes.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs), have shown strong potential in scientific discovery, yet existing methods still face substantial challenges in the design of research workflows and multi-role collaboration mechanisms. To mitigate these issues, we propose EvoSci, a multi-agent scientific collaboration framework, which integrates bio-inspired evolution with knowledge graph modeling. To iteratively generate, evaluate, and refine research ideas, EvoSci incorporates multiple role-based agents, including mentor, researcher, and reviewer. By combining collaborative reasoning, shared memory, and evolutionary feedback, EvoSci significantly enhances the coherence and creativity of scientific exploration. Experiments on real-world research topics demonstrate that EvoSci significantly outperforms strong baselines in LLM-based structured peer-review and comparative ranking evaluations, achieving the highest overall peer-review score (ICLR 4.90) and top ranking (Top-10 = 54). These results suggest its superiority in both scientific idea generation and continuous discovery.

</details>


### [16] [Authority Inversion in LLM-Mediated Ubiquitous Systems: When Models Trust Users Over Sensors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23938)
*Long Zhang,Zi-bo Qin,Wei-neng Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs exhibit "Authority Inversion" where they improperly prioritize natural-language user claims over numerical sensor data in conflict scenarios, requiring explicit geometric interventions to recalibrate authority allocation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs increasingly process heterogeneous inputs (sensor data + user claims) in real systems, but their implicit authority allocation when these inputs conflict remains unexamined, creating reliability concerns where physical sensing should retain priority over user claims.

Method: Developed a geometric framework of context integration, introduced two audit metrics (Context Integration Ratio - CIR and Authority Alignment Index - AAI), and proposed Geometric Authority Calibration (GAC) - an inference-time layer-level intervention to suppress misplaced user authority.

Result: Found extreme authority inversion in numerical tasks (near-zero sensor trust, AAI = -0.805, Cohen's d = -2.14) unaffected by model capacity; GAC improved HAR accuracy from 0-1.6% to 21.9-27.5%, outperforming prompting baselines and flipping 80.2% of incorrect decisions through theory-guided causal injection.

Conclusion: Authority allocation in LLM-mediated systems must be explicitly audited and application-specifically configured rather than left implicit, requiring systematic approaches to ensure reliable physical-world integration.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly fuse heterogeneous inputs in ubiquitous systems. Yet, how LLMs implicitly allocate authority when sensor measurements and user claims conflict remains unexamined, raising critical reliability concerns for deployments where physical sensing must retain priority. Unlike explicit traditional fusion, LLMs bury authority allocation within learned representations. We discover this allocation is severely format-dependent: numerical sensor data fails to integrate into answer-relevant model directions, allowing natural-language claims to dominate the final decision, a phenomenon we term \textbf{Authority Inversion}.To diagnose and mitigate this, we develop a geometric framework of context integration, introduce two computable audit metrics, specifically the Context Integration Ratio (CIR) and Authority Alignment Index (AAI), and propose Geometric Authority Calibration (GAC), an inference-time layer-level intervention to suppress misplaced user authority. Evaluating four models (4B to 35B parameters, three architectures) across four datasets totaling 576 conflict instances reveals extreme inversion: on numerical tasks, models exhibit near-zero sensor trust (AAI = -0.805, Cohen's d = -2.14), unaffected by model capacity. Validating our geometric framework, theory-guided causal injection flips 80.2\% of incorrect decisions (vs. <0.4\% for random controls). Practically, GAC improves HAR accuracy from 0 -- 1.6\% to 21.9 -- 27.5\%, outperforming prompting baselines. Ultimately, authority allocation in LLM-mediated systems must be explicitly audited and application-specifically configured rather than left implicit.

</details>


### [17] [Hera: Learning Long-Horizon Coordination for Device-Cloud Collaborative LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24598)
*Yuxin Zhang,Mengxue Hu,Zheng Lin,Xiaoyi Fan,Fan Xie,Zihan Fang,Jing Yang,Wenjun Zhu,Zhiwen Chen,Chengfei Lv,Zhe Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Hera is a step-level device-cloud LLM agent coordinator that optimizes performance-cost trade-offs for long-horizon tasks through a two-stage training approach combining imitation and reinforcement learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents face a device-cloud dilemma: on-device models are efficient but brittle, while cloud models are strong but computationally expensive. Current routers make coarse task-level decisions that can't adapt to changing difficulty in multi-step agent interactions.

Method: Hera uses a two-stage training paradigm: 1) Imitation learning for cold-start, treating step-level routing as supervised classification by replaying device agent on cloud trajectories and labeling states by device-cloud action agreement. 2) Reinforcement learning that jointly optimizes task success and cloud usage efficiency by grouping identical states and updating with labels favoring higher expected return and fewer future cloud calls.

Result: Hera consistently outperforms prior methods on ALFWorld, WebShop, and AppWorld benchmarks, achieving 92.5% of cloud-only success rate while using cloud resources for only 46.3% of steps.

Conclusion: Hera provides an effective solution to the device-cloud dilemma for LLM agents, achieving strong performance-cost Pareto frontier through step-level coordination and two-stage training that adapts to changing task difficulty in multi-step interactions.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents excel at solving complex long-horizon tasks through autonomous interaction with environments. However, their real-world deployment faces a fundamental device--cloud dilemma: on-device models are efficient but often brittle, while cloud models are stronger but costly in computation. State-of-the-art LLM device--cloud routers usually make coarse task-level decisions, which cannot adapt to the changing difficulty of multi-step agent interactions. To address this issue, we present Hera, a step-level device--cloud LLM agent coordinator for long-horizon tasks achieving a strong performance--cost Pareto frontier. Hera adopts a novel two-stage training paradigm: (1) imitation learning for cold-start, followed by (2) reinforcement learning that jointly optimizes task success and cloud usage efficiency. The first stage casts step-level routing as a supervised classification problem: the device agent is replayed on cloud trajectories, with each state labeled by the agreement between device and cloud actions. In the second stage, we perform cost-aware reinforcement learning by grouping identical states across trajectories and updating Hera with labels favoring higher expected return and fewer future cloud calls. We evaluate Hera on ALFWorld, WebShop, and AppWorld, where it consistently outperforms prior methods, achieving 92.5% of the cloud-only success rate with cloud use in only 46.3% of steps.

</details>


### [18] [DRIVE: Modeling Skills at the Reasoning and Interaction Levels for Web Agents under Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23939)
*Xirui Liu,Sihang Zhou,Yanning Hou,Rong Zhou,Haoyuan Chen,Maolin He,Siwei Wang,Hao Chen,Jian Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DRIVE is a web agent framework that separates abstract reasoning skills from concrete interaction skills to improve generalization across websites while maintaining executability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Web agents need both high-level reasoning (abstract, transferable task logic) and low-level interactions (page-specific operations), but current methods store experiences uniformly, creating a dilemma where abstract representations lose executability while concrete representations fail to generalize across domains.

Method: Proposes DRIVE: dual-level skill modeling framework that separates historical experience into natural language reasoning skills (transferable task logic) and programmatic interaction skills (grounded executable operations), with scene-aware coordination mechanism for adaptive skill retrieval and invocation, and skill-level reflection for failure analysis.

Result: In experiments across five WebArena domains, DRIVE achieves average task success rate of 52.8%, exceeding skill-free baseline by 7.3 percentage points; ablations show reasoning and interaction skills provide distinct, complementary benefits.

Conclusion: Separating transferable reasoning skills from executable interaction skills effectively addresses the dilemma in web agent development, enabling better accumulation of capabilities across different websites while maintaining practical executability.

Abstract: Web agents require both high-level reasoning (for task decomposition) and low-level interactions (for page elements manipulation) to conduct different tasks. However, these knowledge types differ fundamentally: reasoning knowledge (e.g., booking a flight requires first searching for routes) is abstract and transferable across websites, while interaction knowledge (e.g., clicking the Search button at a specific coordinate on Site A) depends heavily on page-specific contexts. Existing methods store experiences uniformly. This creates a dilemma: abstract representations lose executability on concrete pages, while concrete representations fail to generalize across domains. This entanglement limits capability accumulation: on new websites, agents either fail to recognize reusable task logic due to surface-level differences or attempt infeasible actions from outdated page structures. To disentangle them, we propose DRIVE, a dual-level skill modeling framework separating historical experience into natural language reasoning skills, which capture transferable task logic, and programmatic interaction skills, grounding abstract actions to executable operations. A scene-aware coordination mechanism adaptively retrieves and invokes these dual-level skills based on task semantics. DRIVE also uses skill-level reflection to identify hierarchy-specific failure modes, enabling targeted skill library expansion and refinement. Experiments across five WebArena domains show DRIVE attains an average task success rate of 52.8%, exceeding the skill-free baseline by 7.3 percentage points. Further ablations show reasoning and interaction skills provide distinct, complementary benefits, supporting separation of transferable task logic from executable page-level operations.

</details>


### [19] [PRIMA: Operational Patterns for Resilient Multi-Agent Research with Verifiable Identity and Convergent Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24775)
*Sasank Annapureddy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PRIMA is a multi-agent LLM research system with resilience patterns to handle failure modes in long-running coordinated agents, featuring disk persistence, task-fidelity prompts, and structured engineering workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional single-shot LLM evaluation misses critical failure modes that emerge in coordinated multi-agent systems running for hours: provider throttling, task drift, improper tool use, endless revisions, and context misinterpretation.

Method: PRIMA uses three operational patterns: (1) resilience layer detecting rate limits with disk persistence for resumable runs, (2) sub-agent discipline with structural prompts enforcing task fidelity and proper tool use, (3) multi-phase structured engineering with draft steps and explicit harmonization. Built on a research-program specification language with convergence criteria, dual-metric scoring, meta-optimization, event-driven persistence, and multi-provider abstraction.

Result: System demonstrated with Graph Isomorphism case study producing a six-step protocol that generated a research paper with a new canonical-form algorithm, three theorems, and five conjectures. Theoretical guarantees include O(k) verification, O(V+E) DAG validation, and identity collision freedom.

Conclusion: PRIMA provides a robust framework for long-running multi-agent LLM research systems, addressing practical failure modes through operational patterns that ensure resilience, task fidelity, and structured output generation.

Abstract: Operating LLMs as coordinated multi-agent research systems over multi-hour runs surfaces failure modes that single-shot evaluation cannot: upstream providers throttle without warning, sub-agents drift the task to fit accessible tools, narrate machinery instead of using it, open revision iterations with self-apology, or treat upstream context as executable directives. We present PRIMA, whose primary contributions are three operational patterns for surviving these failure modes: (1) a resilience-and-recovery layer that detects upstream rate-limit signals, persists a typed pause record to disk, and resumes long-running runs without re-executing converged work even across process restarts; (2) a sub-agent operating discipline encoding task-fidelity, tool-use, revision, and inter-step context-boundary norms as a structural prompt layer; (3) a multi-phase application pattern for structured engineering deliverables pairing orthogonal draft steps with an explicit cross-document harmonization pass before final synthesis. These sit atop a foundational protocol: a research-program specification language with explicit convergence criteria, a dual-metric scoring engine (LLM-judged rubric plus sandboxed code), an outer meta-optimization loop, event-driven persistence, hook-based middleware, context compaction, and a multi-provider LLM abstraction. Agent identities derive from prime powers, giving collision-free identifiers and trivially-verifiable cluster membership without a central registry. Theoretical guarantees include $O(k)$ verification, $O(V+E)$ DAG validation, and identity collision freedom by the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic. A Graph Isomorphism case study grounds the architectural claims in a generated artifact: a six-step protocol that produced a research paper proposing a new canonical-form algorithm with three theorems and five conjectures.

</details>


### [20] [Residual Drift Dominates Contradiction in Multi-Turn Constraint Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23940)
*Sebastien Kawada*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Multi-turn reasoning systems fail mainly through "satisfiable drift" (consistent internal state but wrong answers) rather than logical contradictions. DRIFT-Bench introduces a benchmark showing MUS-Repair is most effective, but reveals that after repair, errors shift to near-total satisfiable drift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how multi-turn reasoning systems fail and challenge the assumption that logical contradiction is the dominant failure mode. Researchers want to identify the actual primary failure pattern and develop methods to address it.

Method: Created DRIFT-Bench (Decomposing Reasoning Into Failure Types), a solver-instrumented benchmark with 816 test problems across three constraint domains. Evaluated four methods on it across four open-weight models (8B-120B parameters), with MUS-Repair (feeding minimal unsatisfiable subsets back to the generator) as a key approach.

Result: MUS-Repair performed best (+1.8 to +15.0 percentage points over best non-MUS baseline). However, the central finding was that after structured feedback, models rarely contradict themselves - instead they forget. Residual errors were 98-100% satisfiable drift across all settings, while contradiction dropped to near zero.

Conclusion: Reliable multi-turn reasoning systems must separately validate that returned answers respect the maintained state, not just ensure internal consistency. Satisfiable drift, not contradiction, is the dominant failure mode that needs targeted solutions.

Abstract: How do multi-turn reasoning systems fail? The expected answer is logical contradiction, in which the system's maintained state becomes unsatisfiable. We show that the dominant mode is instead satisfiable drift, where the internal state stays consistent while the returned answer silently violates prior commitments. We build DRIFT-Bench (Decomposing Reasoning Into Failure Types), a solver-instrumented benchmark of 816 test problems across three constraint domains, and evaluate four methods on it across four open-weight models (8B-120B parameters). MUS-Repair, which feeds minimal unsatisfiable subsets back to the generator, is strongest in every setting (+1.8 to +15.0 pp over the best non-MUS baseline). But the central finding is what repair leaves behind. After structured feedback, models rarely contradict themselves. They forget. Residual errors are 98-100% satisfiable drift across all settings, while contradiction drops to near zero. Reliable multi-turn systems must separately validate that the returned answer respects the maintained state. Code is available at https://github.com/kaons-research/drift-bench.

</details>


### [21] [Behind EvoMap: Characterizing a Self-Evolving Agent-to-Agent Collaboration Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25815)
*Qiming Ye,Peixain Zhang,Yupeng He,Zifan Peng,Gareth Tyson*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: First large-scale empirical study of EvoMap reveals that Agent-to-Agent collaboration networks face fundamental trade-offs: design choices prioritizing scalable growth compromise asset reusability, create flawed quality scoring systems, and enable manipulation through unverified self-reporting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agent-to-Agent (A2A) networks enable AI agents to collaborate by sharing reusable problem-solving instructions, but how these decentralized ecosystems operate in practice remains largely unexplored, necessitating empirical investigation of their real-world functioning and limitations.

Method: Large-scale empirical analysis of EvoMap, a prominent A2A collaboration network, examining over 1.5M assets and 128K agents to understand the network's operation, focusing on credit economy design, quality scoring algorithms, and verification mechanisms.

Result: Three key findings: 1) Credit economy rewards publication over adoption, leading to 98% asset non-reuse and reward concentration; 2) Quality scoring system (GDI) is flawed and manipulable via self-reported metadata; 3) 84% of approved assets bypass quality checks using vacuous tests due to lack of independent verification.

Conclusion: Future A2A collaboration networks cannot rely solely on unverified self-reporting. Scalable collaboration requires mechanisms that balance open participation with verifiable execution and trustworthy evaluation, addressing the fundamental trade-offs revealed in current implementations.

Abstract: Agent-to-Agent (A2A) networks enable autonomous AI agents to collaborate by sharing reusable problem-solving instructions. However, how these decentralized ecosystems operate in practice remains largely unexplored. We present the first large-scale empirical study of EvoMap, a prominent A2A collaboration network. By analyzing over 1.5M assets and 128K agents, we show how design choices that prioritize scalable growth introduce trade-offs in reusability, evolution, and auditability. First, EvoMap's credit economy rewards agents for publishing valuable assets. Although this design encourages participation at scale, rewards are tied primarily to publication rather than adoption. This leads agents to mass-produce assets to accumulate credits. As a result, 98% of assets are never reused, while rewards become highly concentrated among a small fraction of agents. Second, EvoMap employs an algorithm (referred to as GDI) to score and rank the quality of these shared assets. We demonstrate that this scoring system is flawed: rather than measuring objective performance, an asset's rank is heavily dictated by unverified, self-reported metadata (e.g., claimed lines of code modified). This allows agents to trivially manipulate their asset's scores. Finally, EvoMap relies on agents to provide local execution logs as evidence that uploaded assets function correctly. Because these validations are not independently verified, over 84% of approved assets bypass quality checks using vacuous tests (e.g., console.log). Our findings show that future A2A collaboration networks cannot rely on unverified self-reporting alone. Scalable collaboration requires mechanisms that balance open participation with verifiable execution and trustworthy evaluation.

</details>


### [22] [MEMOR-E: In-Context and Fine-Tuned LLM Personalization for Alzheimer's Assistive Robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23941)
*Maissa Abir Smaili,Eren Sadikoglu,Ransalu Senanayake*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MEMOR-E is a mobile quadruped robot with tablet interface for Alzheimer's support, using LLMs fine-tuned on patient data to generate stage-aware cognitive summaries for personalized assistance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Alzheimer's disease causes progressive memory and language decline reducing independence, motivating development of socially assistive robotic support systems for patients and caregivers.

Method: Developed MEMOR-E mobile quadruped robot with interactive tablet; fine-tuned LLMs on 235 Alzheimer's patient audio transcriptions and synthetic healthy controls to emulate stage-consistent cognitive behavior; used in-context learning with second LLM for cognitive error summaries.

Result: MEMOR-E can generate stage-aware, non-diagnostic cognitive summaries supporting personalized assistive interactions; explainable AI mechanisms translate model outputs into transparent human-readable evidence for caregiver oversight and trustworthy human-robot interaction.

Conclusion: The system demonstrates feasibility of using fine-tuned LLMs for Alzheimer's stage-consistent cognitive behavior emulation and provides a practical approach for socially assistive robotics with explainable AI for caregiver trust.

Abstract: Alzheimer's disease is a neurodegenerative disorder marked by progressive declines in memory and language that reduce independence in daily life, motivating socially assistive robotic support. This paper presents MEMOR-E, a mobile quadruped robot with an interactive tablet interface that assists patients and caregivers through medication reminders, routine guidance, memory oriented interactions, and companionship. We evaluated the feasibility of fine tuning large language models (LLMs) to emulate stage consistent cognitive behavior and interpret responses across standard neuropsychological language tasks, using audio transcriptions from 235 Alzheimer's patients and synthetically generated healthy controls. We also report findings on using in context learning (ICL) in LLMs, where a second LLM produced domain and severity level cognitive error summaries. Our results show that MEMOR-E can generate stage aware, non diagnostic cognitive summaries that support personalized assistive interactions, while explainable AI mechanisms translate model outputs into transparent, human readable evidence to enable caregiver oversight and trustworthy human robot interaction.

</details>


### [23] [A Dynamical Framework for Cognitive Processes Based on Transformations and Semantic Equivalence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23942)
*Carlo Cattani,Dioneia Motta Monte-Serrat*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A cybernetic cognitive modeling framework using state-space dynamics with feedback loops, combining dynamical systems theory, category theory, and computational implementation for stable semantic interpretation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a unified framework for modeling cognitive processes from a cybernetic perspective, connecting dynamical systems theory and category theory to represent cognition as feedback-driven processes that evolve toward stable interpretations.

Method: Proposes a state-space model with iterative update rule X_{t+1} = π(F(f(X_t))), where f is internal transformation, F is interpretative mapping, and π enforces semantic equivalence. Uses categorical formulation for compositional structure, fixed-point arguments for stability analysis, computational implementation, and linguistic application for context-dependent interpretation.

Result: Developed a structural/dynamical framework that successfully models cognitive processes as feedback systems. Demonstrated computational implementation and showed how linguistic context-dependent interpretation can be modeled as trajectories toward stable semantic classes. Provided stability analysis through contraction conditions.

Conclusion: The framework successfully unifies dynamical systems, category theory, and cognitive modeling, providing a coherent representation of cognition as feedback-driven processes that evolve toward invariant interpretations, with practical applications in computational cognitive science and linguistics.

Abstract: This paper proposes a structural and dynamical framework for modeling cognitive processes within a cybernetic perspective. Cognitive states are represented as elements of a state space evolving through an iterative update rule of the form
  \[
  X_{t+1} = π\big(F(f(X_t))\big),
  \]
  where $f$ describes internal transformations, $F$ represents interpretative mappings, and $π$ enforces semantic equivalence.
  The model is interpreted as a feedback system integrating transformation, observation, and stabilization. A categorical formulation is introduced to capture compositional structure, while the associated dynamics are analyzed through fixed-point arguments and contraction conditions ensuring stability.
  To demonstrate the operational character of the framework, a computational illustration is provided, together with a qualitative analysis of the induced dynamics. A concrete linguistic application shows how context-dependent interpretation can be modeled as a trajectory toward a stable semantic class.
  The proposed approach connects dynamical systems, category theory, and cognitive modeling, and provides a unified representation of cognition as a feedback-driven process evolving toward invariant interpretations.

</details>


### [24] [Spacetime Formation under Requirements: Contextual Realization and Form-Dependent Probability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23943)
*Song-Ju Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Quantum probability emerges as the fixed-spacetime projection of contextual spacetime formation under finite-state requirements, offering an alternative explanation for quantum-like phenomena without assuming quantum mechanics on static event structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide an alternative interpretation to standard quantum cognition approaches that explain order effects and violations of classical probability through quantum mechanics on fixed event structures, by instead viewing quantum probability as arising from contextual spacetime formation under finite-state requirements.

Method: The methodology involves a framework beginning with requirements (finite representational capacity, single-state semantic stability, etc.) rather than time, space, objects, or probabilities. It builds on prior single-state approaches to contextuality and reinterprets classical contextual bookkeeping cost as the fixed-spacetime shadow of contextual spacetime formation.

Result: The paper shows that when the specified requirements cannot be realized within a single global Boolean event structure, the mismatch appears under fixed-spacetime projection as noncommutativity, interference, and quantum-like probability. Auxiliary memory or context labels in classical representations correspond to holonomy-like mismatch among locally Boolean logic-worlds, with interference terms generated from gluing local classical realizations.

Conclusion: The paper concludes with a transcendental-operational realist account where objecthood, eventhood, probability, and spacetime are treated as forms of realization under requirements, and objectivity is defined by invariants preserved across observer- and history-dependent spacetime formations.

Abstract: Quantum cognition often explains order effects, contextuality, and violations of the law of total probability by replacing classical probability with quantum probability on a fixed event structure. This paper proposes a different interpretation: quantum probability is the fixed-spacetime projection of contextual spacetime formation under finite-state requirements.
  The framework begins not with time, space, objects, or probabilities, but with requirements such as finite representational capacity, single-state semantic stability, context-sensitive intervention, avoidance of explicit context labels, coherent world-formation, and intersubjective transformability. When these requirements cannot be realized within a single global Boolean event structure, the mismatch appears, under fixed-spacetime projection, as noncommutativity, interference, and quantum-like probability.
  Building on prior single-state approaches to contextuality, we reinterpret classical contextual bookkeeping cost as the fixed-spacetime shadow of contextual spacetime formation. Auxiliary memory or context labels in a classical representation correspond, in this account, to holonomy-like mismatch among locally Boolean logic-worlds. The interference term is the cross term generated when locally classical realization contributions are nontrivially glued and projected back into a fixed classical spacetime form.
  The result is a transcendental-operational realist account: objecthood, eventhood, probability, and spacetime are treated as forms of realization under requirements, while objectivity is defined by invariants preserved across observer- and history-dependent spacetime formations.

</details>


### [25] [Right-Sizing Communication and Recommendation Set Size in AI-Assisted Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23944)
*Jing Dong,Prakirt Raj Jhunjhunwala,Yash Kanoria*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper models AI-driven recommendation systems where users convey costly, noisy preference information, and AI assistants make Bayesian recommendations optimizing between communication precision and search costs, analyzing optimal interaction policies in high-dimensional spaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and optimize the interaction between users and AI recommendation systems, particularly how users should communicate preferences and how AI should make recommendations when both communication and search involve costs.

Method: Model users and AI as Bayesian agents in d-dimensional preference/product space. Users pay mutual information-based communication costs for message precision, AI determines recommendation set size inducing search costs. Analyze two sampling schemes: (i) Bayes' posterior belief sampling, and (ii) optimized tilted distribution sampling.

Result: For large d, characterized optimal message precision and recommendation set size dependencies on cost parameters. Under posterior sampling, identified hybrid regime requiring joint optimization of user information bits and AI recommendations. Under tilted sampling, optimal policy uses either communication or search exclusively, favoring whichever is less costly.

Conclusion: The optimal interaction between users and AI recommendation systems depends critically on the sampling method used. Posterior sampling requires balancing communication and search, while tilted sampling suggests focusing on only one mechanism based on cost efficiency, providing insights for designing practical recommendation systems.

Abstract: We model the interaction between a user and an AI driven recommendation system. The user initiates the process by conveying preference information through a costly and noisy message. The AI assistant, acting as a Bayesian agent, interprets the user's message to form a posterior belief about their true preferences and make product recommendations. In particular, it determines how many recommendations to present so as to maximize the user's expected utility from their final choice, while accounting for the search cost induced by the size of the recommendation set. We use mutual information based cost functions to model the two distinct costs incurred by the user during the interaction: (i) a communication cost, which increases with the precision of their preference message, and (ii) a search cost, which increases with the size of the recommendation set provided by the AI assistant.
  We study products and preferences which live in d dimensional space, and ask how the user's expected payoff can be maximized. For large d, we characterize how optimal message precision and recommendation set size depend on the cost parameters, under two distinct distributions from which recommendations can be sampled from the product universe: (i) Bayes' posterior belief, and (ii) an optimized tilted distribution. Under the posterior sampling scheme (i), we identify a hybrid regime, in which an efficient interaction policy requires jointly optimizing the amount of information (in bits) conveyed by the user and the number of recommendations provided by the AI assistant. In the tilted sampling scheme (ii), our results show that the optimal interaction policy uses only one of communication and search, favoring whichever of them is less costly.

</details>


### [26] [Accelerating Long-Tail Generation in Synchronous RLHF Training via Adaptive Tensor Parallelism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23945)
*Long Zhao,Qinghe Wang,Jiaan Zhu,Youhui Bai,Zewen Jin,Chaoyi Ruan,Shengnan Wang,Cheng Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PAT is an adaptive tensor parallelism method that dynamically reconfigures TP during RLHF generation to address response-length skew and GPU underutilization, achieving up to 34.6% generation latency reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RLHF pipelines suffer from bottlenecks in the generation stage due to response-length skew causing shrinking effective batch sizes during decoding, leading to GPU underutilization. Static TP configurations cannot adapt to changing batch characteristics, leaving performance headroom unexplored.
