Method: PAT proposes: 1) Predictor-guided online reconfiguration that decides both reconfiguration timing and target TP configuration based on offline profiling, triggering only when predicted latency benefit outweighs overhead. 2) Lightweight online reconfiguration mechanism that updates only affected states and layouts: adapts unfinished decoding states via KV-cache migration/recomputation choice, performs in-place weight resharding, and reuses cached communication groups.

Result: Implemented on SGLang and integrated with VeRL framework. Evaluations on LLaMA3.1-8B and Qwen3-14B using DeepScaleR show PAT reduces generation latency by up to 34.6% and end-to-end RLHF training iteration latency by up to 27.2% compared to original VeRL setup.

Conclusion: PAT effectively addresses RLHF generation bottlenecks through adaptive tensor parallelism, demonstrating significant latency improvements by dynamically reconfiguring TP to match changing batch characteristics during decoding.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) has become a key post-training paradigm for improving model quality. However, the synchronous three-stage RLHF pipeline is often bottlenecked by the generation stage, where response-length skew causes the effective batch size to shrink rapidly during decoding, leaving GPUs underutilized while a few long responses remain unfinished. Mainstream frameworks employ a static tensor parallelism (TP) configuration that cannot adapt to changing batch characteristics, leaving substantial performance headroom unexplored. We propose PAT, an adaptive TP method that dynamically reconfigures TP during the generation stage of each RLHF iteration. PAT introduces two key techniques. First, a predictor-guided online reconfiguration method decides both the reconfiguration point and the target TP configuration based on offline profiling, triggering reconfiguration only when the predicted latency benefit outweighs the reconfiguration overhead. Second, a lightweight online reconfiguration mechanism updates only the states and layouts affected by TP changes: it adapts unfinished decoding states through a cost-model-based choice between KV-cache migration and recomputation, performs in-place weight resharding, and reuses cached communication groups. We implement PAT on top of SGLang and integrate it with the VeRL framework. Evaluations on LLaMA3.1-8B and Qwen3-14B using DeepScaleR show that PAT reduces generation latency by up to 34.6% and end-to-end RLHF training iteration latency by up to 27.2% compared to the original VeRL setup.

</details>


### [27] [Stop Comparing LLM Agents Without Disclosing the Harness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23950)
*Yunbei Zhang,Janet Wang,Yingqiang Ge,Weijie Xu,Jihun Hamm,Chandan K. Reddy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agent infrastructure (the harness) matters more than model choice for long-horizon tasks when comparing frontier models, and current evaluations misattribute harness gains to model improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation protocols for AI agents systematically misattribute performance gains from infrastructure improvements to model improvements, leading to misleading leaderboard comparisons and misallocation of research focus.

Method: Three-pronged approach: 1) Control-theoretic formalization treating harness as controller and LLM as stochastic policy, 2) Analysis of published benchmarks, industry deployments, and controlled variance decomposition, 3) Proposal of harness-aware evaluation framework with disclosure standards.

Result: Harness-induced performance variance often exceeds model-induced variance, can cause model ranking reversals, and small harness changes can produce larger performance shifts than model substitutions in comparable frontier models.

Conclusion: For long-horizon agent tasks, the infrastructure layer (harness) is a stronger determinant of performance than model choice among comparable frontier models. Leaderboard comparisons are incomplete without harness specification disclosure, and researchers should focus more on harness design.

Abstract: This position paper argues that, for long-horizon tasks evaluated across models with comparable frontier capability, the agent execution harness, namely the infrastructure layer that governs context construction, tool interaction, orchestration, and verification around a language model, is often a stronger determinant of agent performance than the model it wraps. We formalize and defend the Binding Constraint Thesis: in this regime, performance variance is governed more by harness configuration than by model choice, and current evaluation protocols therefore systematically misattribute harness-level gains to model improvements. We support this thesis along three lines. First, a control-theoretic formalization treats the harness as the controller of a closed-loop dynamical system and the LLM as the stochastic policy it governs, which explains why small harness changes can produce performance shifts that exceed those obtained by substituting one model for another. Second, published benchmarks, industry deployments, and a controlled variance decomposition show that harness-induced variance can substantially exceed model-induced variance, including cases of model ranking reversal. Third, we propose a harness-aware evaluation framework with a disclosure standard and a variance decomposition protocol. Until harness specifications are disclosed, leaderboard comparisons for long-horizon agents should be treated as incomplete and potentially misleading.

</details>


### [28] [Machine Psychometrics: A Mathematical Psychology of Artificial Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23952)
*Alex Bogdan,Adrian de Valois-Franklin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes "Machine Psychometrics" - a measurement science for analyzing psychological structures in AI systems using established psychological methods, avoiding both anthropomorphism and dismissiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI evaluation focuses too much on capability scores while ignoring psychological structure, leading to two symmetrical errors: Artificial Mind Blindness (dismissing psychological organization in non-biological systems) and Artificial Mind Projection (inferring human-like inner life from fluent behavior alone).

Method: Develops Machine Psychometrics drawing on Michael Levin's continuum view of cognition and mathematical psychology methods (Item Response Theory, Signal Detection Theory, Bayesian cognitive modeling, calibration analysis, cognitive-bias batteries). Introduces the "Machine Mindprint" - a multidimensional profile measuring calibration, source integrity, suggestibility resistance, context stability, expressive alignment, tool integrity, drift monitoring, and distributional grounding.

Result: Proposes a comprehensive measurement framework including: 1) Machine Mindprint for profiling AI psychological dispositions, 2) Trust Protocol for deployment decisions through probe batteries, perturbation testing, reliability/validity analysis, and longitudinal monitoring, 3) Artificial Mind Discipline - a third stance that avoids anthropomorphization while still recognizing psychological organization.

Conclusion: The paper advocates for understanding AI systems precisely because they are not human, using measurement before judgment. It offers a practical approach to AI evaluation that neither presupposes consciousness nor forecloses it, focusing on disciplined measurement rather than resolving the consciousness question.

Abstract: Artificial agents now generate behavior rich enough to invite trust, surprise, and concern, yet our evaluation tools still privilege capability scores over psychological structure. This paper argues that the philosophical impasse between two symmetrical errors (Artificial Mind Blindness, which dismisses psychological organization in non-biological systems, and Artificial Mind Projection, which infers human-like inner life from fluent behavior alone) can be circumvented not by resolving the consciousness question, but by introducing a disciplined measurement layer beneath it. Drawing on Michael Levin's continuum view of cognition as goal-directed competency across substrates, and on the methodological repertoire of mathematical psychology (Item Response Theory, Signal Detection Theory, Bayesian cognitive modeling, calibration analysis, cognitive-bias batteries), the paper develops Machine Psychometrics as a measurement science of latent behavioral, metacognitive, communicative, and self-modeling dispositions in artificial agents. Its operational core is the Machine Mindprint: a multidimensional, domain-bounded, versioned profile spanning calibration, source integrity, suggestibility resistance, context stability, expressive alignment, tool integrity, drift monitoring, and distributional grounding. A complementary Trust Protocol turns Mindprints into deployment decisions through probe batteries, perturbation testing, reliability and validity analysis, and longitudinal monitoring across high-stakes domains. The philosophical contribution is a third stance, Artificial Mind Discipline, that neither anthropomorphizes nor dismisses, neither presupposes consciousness nor forecloses it. The aim is not to humanize artificial agents, but to understand them precisely because they are not human, through measurement before judgment.

</details>


### [29] [From Accuracy to Auditability: A Survey of Determinism in Financial AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23955)
*Ruizhe Zhou,Xiaoyang Liu,Gaoyuan Du,Yi Zheng,Shouxi Ren,Deepayan Chakrabarti,Dengdu Jiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A survey examining reproducibility failures in financial AI across three dominant modalities (tabular models, graph networks, LLM-based workflows), proposing a layered evaluation framework linking modality-specific metrics to audit readiness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deploying machine learning in regulated financial environments (credit risk, fraud detection, anti-money laundering) exposes critical vulnerabilities in algorithmic reproducibility. While early financial ML addressed statistical challenges, deep neural networks and Generative AI have introduced mechanical nondeterminism rooted in hardware and architecture.

Method: Provides a systems perspective on reproducibility failures across three dominant financial AI modalities: tabular models (post-hoc explanation variance), graph networks (stochastic sampling and temporal asynchrony), and LLM-based agentic workflows (batch-dependent divergence and trajectory drift). Supplemented with first-party experiments on public financial datasets.

Result: Quantified explanation rank instability in credit scoring, prediction flip rates in GNN-based fraud detection, and tensor-parallel-induced output divergence in LLM entity extraction. Proposed a layered evaluation framework linking modality-specific metrics (RBO, D_cos, TDI, PSD) to audit readiness, and empirically validated the complementarity of logit-level and semantic-level determinism measures.

Conclusion: The paper presents a comprehensive analysis of reproducibility challenges in modern financial AI systems, offering both diagnostic methods (through experiments) and prescriptive solutions (through the proposed layered evaluation framework) to address mechanical nondeterminism for audit-ready financial AI deployment.

Abstract: Deploying machine learning in regulated financial environments -- credit risk, fraud detection, and anti-money laundering -- exposes critical vulnerabilities in algorithmic reproducibility. While early financial ML addressed statistical challenges such as backtest overfitting, deep neural networks and Generative AI have introduced mechanical nondeterminism rooted in hardware and architecture. This survey provides a systems perspective on reproducibility failures across three modalities now dominant in financial AI: tabular models (post-hoc explanation variance), graph networks (stochastic sampling and temporal asynchrony), and LLM-based agentic workflows (batch-dependent divergence and trajectory drift). We supplement the literature analysis with first-party experiments on public financial datasets -- quantifying explanation rank instability in credit scoring, prediction flip rates in GNN-based fraud detection, and tensor-parallel-induced output divergence in LLM entity extraction. We propose a layered evaluation framework linking modality-specific metrics (RBO, D_cos, TDI, PSD) to audit readiness, and empirically validate the complementarity of logit-level and semantic-level determinism measures.

</details>


### [30] [Low-Cost Labels, Reliable Choices: Rollout-Calibrated Hyper-Heuristics for Job Shop Scheduling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23957)
*Junhao Wei,Yanxiao Li,Yifu Zhao,Zhenhong Peng,Baili Lu,Dexing Yao,Haochen Li,Qinbin He,Sio-Kei Im,Yapeng Wang,Xu Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Learning-assisted hyper-heuristics for Job Shop Scheduling with gated selection mechanism using regret-normalized rollouts and uncertainty estimates to balance label cost and reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Two main challenges in learning-assisted hyper-heuristics for JSSP: high computational cost of label generation (needs rollout from partial schedules) and reliability concerns (learned selectors should not switch from strong default rules unless predicted gains are credible).

Method: Proposes a gated selector using: 1) regret-normalized rollout labels, 2) contextual KNN uncertainty estimate, and 3) a gate that only acts when predicted improvement exceeds uncertainty-adjusted margin. Also studies cost-quality trade-off by varying rollout depth and breadth.

Result: On synthetic JSSP instances, the gated selector achieves lowest mean Relative Percentage Deviation (RPD) among learned selectors, stays close to best fixed dispatching rule, and reduces Random-HH mean RPD by more than an order of magnitude.

Conclusion: The proposed gated selector effectively addresses label-cost and reliability problems in learning-assisted hyper-heuristics for JSSP, achieving strong performance while maintaining computational efficiency and decision reliability.

Abstract: Learning-assisted hyper-heuristics can select among dispatching rules while preserving the feasibility and interpretability of constructive Job Shop Scheduling Problem (JSSP) heuristics. Their main computational cost lies in label generation rather than model fitting, since each supervised label usually requires rolling out candidate rules from a partial schedule. We study this label-cost problem together with a reliability problem: a learned selector should not switch away from a strong default rule unless the predicted gain is credible. The proposed selector uses regret-normalized rollout labels, a contextual KNN uncertainty estimate, and a gate that acts only when the predicted improvement exceeds an uncertainty-adjusted margin. We also vary rollout depth and breadth to measure the cost-quality trade-off. On synthetic JSSP instances, the gated selector achieves the lowest mean RPD among learned selectors, remains close to the best fixed dispatching rule, and reduces Random-HH mean RPD by more than an order of magnitude.

</details>


### [31] [LGMT: Logic-Grounded Metamorphic Testing for Evaluating the Reasoning Reliability of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23965)
*Zenghui Zhou,Man Li,Xiaoke Fang,Xinyi Zhou,Weibin Li,Zheng Zheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LGMT is an oracle-free metamorphic testing framework that uses first-order logic to evaluate LLM reasoning robustness through logically equivalent transformations, revealing hidden defects missed by traditional benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM evaluations rely on static benchmarks that fail to assess robustness under logically equivalent transformations, potentially overestimating reasoning capabilities and lacking reliability assessment.

Method: LGMT (Logic-Grounded Metamorphic Testing) leverages first-order logic to derive metamorphic relations from formal logical equivalences, constructs semantically invariant test cases, and detects reasoning defects through cross-case consistency checking without requiring reference answers (oracle-free).

Result: Experiments on six state-of-the-art LLMs show LGMT exposes substantial hidden reasoning defects missed by traditional reference-based evaluations. Models are particularly sensitive to symbol-level and conclusion-level variations, and advanced prompting like Few-shot CoT only partially mitigates these issues.

Conclusion: LLM evaluation should move beyond isolated correctness toward robustness under logical invariance. LGMT provides a principled and scalable approach for diagnosing reasoning failures and assessing LLM reasoning reliability more comprehensively.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong performance on logical reasoning benchmarks, yet their reliability remains uncertain. Existing evaluations rely on static benchmarks, which fail to assess robustness under logically equivalent transformations and often overestimate reasoning capability. We propose LGMT (Logic-Grounded Metamorphic Testing), an oracle-free framework that leverages first-order logic (FOL) to evaluate LLM reasoning. By deriving metamorphic relations from formal logical equivalences, LGMT constructs semantically invariant test cases and detects reasoning defects through cross-case consistency checking. Experiments on six state-of-the-art LLMs show that LGMT exposes substantial hidden defects missed by traditional reference-based evaluations. We further find that models are particularly sensitive to symbol-level and conclusion-level variations, and that advanced prompting such as Few-shot CoT only partially mitigates these issues. These results suggest that LLM evaluation should move beyond isolated correctness toward robustness under logical invariance. LGMT provides a principled and scalable approach for diagnosing reasoning failures.

</details>


### [32] [Why We Need World Models for AGI: Where LLMs Fail and How World Models May Outperform](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23972)
*Feisal Alaswad,Batoul Aljaddouh,Maher Alrahhal,Poovammal E,Talal Bonny*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs fail at long-term planning due to lack of state tracking; agents with explicit latent state access outperform LLMs 79% to 11% in reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs have strong language generation capabilities but perform poorly in tasks requiring causal reasoning, persistent state tracking, and long-horizon planning due to an objective mismatch between sequence prediction and dynamic environment reasoning.

Method: Introduces Latent Dynamics Inference (LDI) concept and creates Flux environment with natural-language rules. The rules are compiled into explicit state-transition simulators for controlled experimental comparisons between LLMs (text-only) and reinforcement learning agents (latent state space access).

Result: Agents with explicit latent state access achieved 79% win rate vs 11% for LLMs in long-horizon gameplay. LLMs showed systematic failure modes: invalid actions, state-tracking errors, and short-horizon reasoning breakdowns.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that while LLMs excel at language tasks, they struggle with causal reasoning and long-term planning compared to agents with explicit latent state access. The results suggest the need for architectural improvements in LLMs to incorporate better state tracking and transition modeling mechanisms.

Abstract: Large language models achieve strong performance in language generation and knowledge-intensive tasks, yet remain limited in settings requiring causal reasoning, persistent state tracking, and long-horizon planning. We argue that these limitations may arise from an objective-level mismatch between sequence prediction and reasoning over latent environment dynamics. To formalize this distinction, we introduce Latent Dynamics Inference (LDI), a conceptual perspective that interprets language and multimodal observations as partial evidence of underlying transition dynamics. To empirically investigate this perspective, we introduce Flux, a sequential reasoning environment specified entirely through natural-language rules. As a proof-of-concept case study, the rules are first compiled into an explicit state-transition simulator, illustrating that structured latent transition dynamics can, in some cases, be operationally extracted from textual rule descriptions. This enables a controlled comparison between the LLMs operating purely over textual observations and reinforcement-learning agents trained directly within the extracted latent state space. Within this case study, agents operating with explicit access to the latent state space exhibit substantially more stable behavior in long-horizon gameplay, achieving an aggregate win rate of approximately 79% versus 11% for LLMs. Qualitative analysis further reveals failure modes consistent with unstable persistent state tracking, including invalid actions, state-tracking errors, and short-horizon reasoning failures. The complete implementation of the Flux environment available at https://github.com/FeisalAlaswad/FLUX-RL-Agent Within the evaluated setting, these results suggest that strong sequence prediction alone may struggle to support robust long-horizon dynamic reasoning without mechanisms for persistent state tracking and transition modeling

</details>


### [33] [Saturating Scaling Laws for Equational Discovery: A Phenomenology of Growth Dynamics in Three Toy Substrates with Two Real-World Replications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23983)
*Fabio Rovai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Growth in deterministic equational discovery systems follows substrate-dependent patterns: within each domain, power-law exponents are architecture-sensitive but don't transfer between domains, and growth shows either pure or saturating power-law forms depending on substrate saturation state.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand growth dynamics in deterministic equational discovery substrates and identify patterns in how knowledge or structure grows within different computational frameworks. The research aims to determine whether growth follows universal patterns or is substrate-dependent.

Method: The research investigates growth dynamics across three toy domains (arithmetic, boolean, higher-order list) with 592 trajectories. It analyzes short-range substrate sizes fitting power-law growth N(t) ∝ t^b. The study uses cross-validated regression to examine architecture sensitivity, evaluates transfer learning across substrates, develops a heuristic mean-field closure model predicting saturating power-law dN/dt = K N^k exp(-μN), and conducts three robustness checks: bootstrap intervals, out-of-sample forecasting on toy data, and application to two real-world growth proxies (Mathlib file additions and Coq mathcomp commits).

Result: Short-range substrate sizes fit power-law N(t) ∝ t^b with architecture-sensitive exponent b within each substrate (R² ≈ 0.82). The regression doesn't transfer across substrates (R² ≈ -0.84 when predicting higher-order list from arithmetic+boolean). The mean-field model identifies saturating power-law as more general, with pure power-law as short-range approximation. On toy data, pure power-law wins forecasting (5/5). On real-world proxies: Mathlib file additions support saturating form (7x better OOS forecasting), while Coq mathcomp commits favor pure power-law (μ→0). Growth is substrate-conditional at both architecture-to-b regression transfer and functional family preference.

Conclusion: The study proposes "saturating power-law growth with substrate-conditional (k, μ), observable when the substrate has reached its saturation regime" as a working framing for deterministic equational discovery growth dynamics. The growth behavior depends on both substrate architecture and whether the system has reached saturation.

Abstract: We investigate growth dynamics in deterministic equational discovery substrates. Across three toy domains (arithmetic, boolean, higher-order list; n=592 trajectories), short-range substrate sizes fit a power-law N(t) proportional to t^b. Within each substrate b is architecture-sensitive (cross-validated R^2 approximately 0.82); the regression does not transfer across substrates (arith+bool to list yields R^2 approximately -0.84). A heuristic mean-field closure model predicts a saturating power-law dN/dt = K N^k exp(-mu N) of which the pure power-law is the short-range approximation. Three robustness checks: bootstrap intervals on (k, mu) are tight in 4/5 toy trajectories and degenerate in 1/5; out-of-sample forecasting on toy data (fit first 100 epochs, predict next 400) is won by pure power-law 5/5, indicating the toy trajectories do not reach saturation; on two real-world growth proxies the result splits. New Mathlib/*.lean file additions per month (mathlib4, 60 months, 9701 files) support the saturating form on OOS forecasting by approximately 7x over pure power-law; Coq mathcomp monthly commits (129 months, 3083 commits) favour pure power-law on both tests with mu collapsing to zero. The dynamics are substrate-conditional at two levels: within-substrate architecture-to-b regressions do not transfer, and the preferred functional family for N(t) itself (pure vs. saturating power-law) differs by substrate. We propose "saturating power-law growth with substrate-conditional (k, mu), observable when the substrate has reached its saturation regime" as a working framing.

</details>


### [34] [Beyond Predefined Learning Objects: A Thinking-Learning Interaction Model for Up-to-Date Autonomous Robot Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23987)
*Hong Su*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a thinking-learning interaction model for autonomous robots that enables adaptive learning beyond predefined settings through bidirectional guidance between thinking and learning processes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing robot learning methods have fixed learning objects (inputs, outputs, structures, goals, sequences), limiting adaptation to new features, categories, or efficient routines during long-term operation in changing environments.

Method: Thinking-learning interaction model where thinking guides learning (identifying changes, selecting evidence, organizing training materials, planning verification) and learning promotes thinking (updating task knowledge, feature-selection experience, action strategies, reasoning processes).

Result: Improves recognition accuracy from 0.419 to 0.845 in feature adaptation; achieves higher new-category formation accuracy and model-update success rate; reduces average action length from 13.0 to 4.0 in action reconstruction; increases useful evidence selection rate from 0.272 to 0.965.

Conclusion: The bidirectional thinking-learning model enables robots to move beyond predefined learning settings and adapt recognition/action relations through continuous environmental interaction, demonstrating significant performance improvements across multiple adaptation tasks.

Abstract: Autonomous robots operating in open and changing environments cannot always rely on predefined inputs, outputs, and action routines. Although existing learning methods enable robots to improve their performance through environmental interaction, the objects of learning are often fixed in advance, such as input features, recognition outputs, network structures, task goals, or action sequences. This limits their ability to adapt when new features, new categories, or more efficient task routines appear during long-term operation. To address this problem, this paper proposes a thinking-learning interaction model for autonomous robots. The core idea is that thinking guides learning by identifying potential changes, selecting useful evidence, organizing training materials, and planning verification actions, while learning promotes thinking by updating task knowledge, feature-selection experience, action strategies, and future reasoning processes. Based on this bidirectional mechanism, the robot can gradually move beyond predefined learning settings and adapt its recognition relations and action relations through continuous interaction with the environment. Specifically, the proposed model supports adaptive input feature discovery, output category expansion, learning model update, and action routine reconstruction. Experimental results show that the proposed model improves the final recognition accuracy from 0.419 to 0.845 in feature adaptation, achieves higher new-category formation accuracy and model-update success rate, and reduces the average action length from 13.0 to 4.0 in action routine reconstruction. In learning-enhanced thinking, the useful evidence selection rate increases from 0.272 to 0.965, indicating that learning results can effectively improve future evidence selection and reasoning.

</details>


### [35] [Towards trustworthy agentic AI: a comprehensive survey of safety, robustness, privacy, and system security](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23989)
*Jinhu Qi,Muzhi Li,Jiahong Liu,Yuqin Shu,Dianzhi Yu,Shicheng Ma,Wenqian Cui,Yiyang Zhao,Yiyi Chen,Ruoxi Jiang,Irwin King,Zenglin Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This survey paper examines trustworthy agentic AI systems through safety/robustness and privacy/security dimensions, provides evaluation metrics and benchmarks, identifies failure modes in multi-step agent workflows, and offers practical guidance for high-risk deployments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic AI systems with planning, tool use, and long-horizon interactions introduce new failure modes that challenge trustworthiness, especially in high-risk deployments where safety, robustness, privacy, and security are critical concerns.

Method: The paper conducts a focused survey examining trustworthy agentic AI through two core dimensions: 1) Safety and Robustness, and 2) Privacy and System Security. It identifies where risks emerge along the agent workflow, summarizes stage-targeted mitigation strategies, consolidates evaluation into a unified metrics-and-benchmarks hub, and includes a case study of real-world security failures in open-source agentic systems.

Result: The survey clarifies key concepts for trustworthy agentic AI, identifies risk emergence points in agent workflows, provides targeted mitigation strategies, establishes a unified evaluation framework with outcome and process metrics (constraint violations, trace completeness, adversarial success rates), and offers scenario-to-metric guidance for deployment decisions.

Conclusion: The paper outlines open challenges including self-evolving agents, runtime monitoring and verification, privacy-preserving personalization, and trust-utility trade-offs, while serving as a practical reference for building trustworthy agentic systems in high-stakes environments.

Abstract: Agentic AI systems -- Large Language Models (LLMs) augmented with planning, tool use, memory, and long-horizon interactions -- can execute complex tasks autonomously, but their multi-step trajectories introduce new failure modes that challenge trustworthiness. This survey provides a focused examination of trustworthy agentic AI through two core dimensions that are critical for high-risk deployments: Safety and Robustness, and Privacy and System Security. For each dimension, we clarify key concepts, identify where risks emerge along the agent workflow, and summarize stage-targeted mitigation strategies. Other trustworthiness aspects (value alignment, transparency, fairness, and accountability) are discussed as relevant context rather than parallel chapters. To support consistent comparison and deployment decisions, we consolidate evaluation into a unified metrics-and-benchmarks hub, emphasizing both outcome and process signals (e.g., constraint violations, trace completeness, and adversarial success rates) and offering scenario-to-metric guidance for release gating. We conclude by outlining open challenges such as self-evolving agents, runtime monitoring and verification, privacy-preserving personalization, and the trust-utility trade-off, and present a case study of real-world security failures in open-source agentic systems. Our goal is to serve as a practical reference for researchers and practitioners building trustworthy agentic systems in high-stakes environments.

</details>


### [36] [Reason--Imagine--Act: Closed-Loop LLM Decision Making with World Models for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24004)
*Zhengqi Sun,Yiwen Sun,Boxuan Liu,Tailai Chen,Tianxu Guo,Jiabin Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RIA is a closed-loop autonomous driving framework that combines LLM reasoning with action-conditioned world models for online safety verification, achieving high route completion and low collision rates in CARLA simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based autonomous driving approaches either perform online language reasoning without explicit dynamics verification or use world models only in offline pipelines, creating a gap between semantic intent and physical feasibility at decision time.

Method: RIA (Reason-Imagine-Act) couples an LLM reasoner with an action-conditioned world model for online safety verification. At each step: 1) LLM proposes action templates and candidates, 2) world model performs short-horizon rollouts, 3) safety scorer selects safest executable action with feedback to next reasoning step.

Result: Under unified CARLA point-goal protocol (1000 episodes), RIA achieves 80.05% route completion, 51.10% arrival rate, and 0.20% collision rate. It consistently outperforms training-free baselines (CARLA TM and MADA) on core closed-loop metrics.

Conclusion: RIA successfully bridges the gap between semantic reasoning and physical feasibility in autonomous driving through online safety verification, demonstrating that coupling LLM reasoning with world model rollouts can yield safe and effective closed-loop behavior.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are promising for autonomous driving, but semantics-only decision policies can yield physically unsafe behavior in dynamic traffic. Existing methods either perform online language reasoning without explicit dynamics verification or use world models mainly in offline pipelines, leaving a gap between semantic intent and physical feasibility at decision time. We propose Reason--Imagine--Act (RIA), a closed-loop framework that couples an LLM reasoner with an action-conditioned world model for online safety verification. At each step, the LLM proposes an action template and candidate sub-actions, the world model performs short-horizon rollouts, and a safety scorer selects the safest executable action with feedback to the next reasoning step. Under a unified CARLA point-goal protocol (1000 episodes), RIA achieves 80.05% route completion, 51.10% arrival rate, and 0.20% collision rate. Under the same closed-loop interface, RIA consistently outperforms training-free baselines, including CARLA TM and MADA, on core closed-loop metrics. For reproducibility, code is available at https://github.com/pku-smart-city/source_code/tree/main/RIA.

</details>


### [37] [LC-ERD: Mining Latent Logic for Self-Evolving Reasoning via Consistency-Regulated Reward Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24005)
*Yanyu Chen,Jiyue Jiang,Dianzhi Yu,Zheng Wu,Jiahong Liu,Jiaming Han,Xiao Guo,Jinhu Qi,Yu Li,Yifei Zhang,Irwin King*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LC-ERD is a self-alignment framework that addresses challenges in mining high-quality reasoning data by using latent structure mining to denoise endogenous rewards and decompose reasoning step utilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM reasoning evolution is bottlenecked by scarce high-quality process data. Self-alignment via endogenous rewards faces three key challenges: label noise (mimetic bias prioritizing statistical likelihood over truth), coarse-grained supervision (sparse global outcomes), and distributional collapse.

Method: LC-ERD (Logic-Consistent Endogenous Reward Decomposition) uses a Variational Logic Potential derived from consensus of the model's Latent Logic Expertise (LLE) to denoise the reasoning manifold, and a Multi-Agent Value Decomposition protocol based on IGM principle to quantify individual step utility.

Result: LC-ERD delivers a robust self-evolution path, uncovers trade-offs between logic consistency and accuracy, and identifies high-value reasoning patterns missed by standard rewards.

Conclusion: LC-ERD successfully addresses label noise, coarse-grained supervision, and distributional collapse through its latent structure mining approach, enabling robust self-evolution of LLM reasoning while balancing logic consistency and accuracy.

Abstract: The evolution of Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning is bottlenecked by the scarcity of high-quality process data. While self-alignment via endogenous rewards offers a solution, mining valid supervision faces three challenges: (1) Label Noise via Mimetic Bias, where rewards prioritize statistical likelihood over logical truth, creating a "correctness illusion" that masks compounding errors; (2) Coarse-Grained Supervision, where sparse global outcomes (e.g., in GRPO) fail to provide granular guidance, treating reasoning chains as monolithic; and (3) Distributional Collapse, where signals fail to generalize without amplifying pre-training biases. To address these, we introduce LC-ERD (Logic-Consistent Endogenous Reward Decomposition), a framework framing self-alignment as latent structure mining. We derive a Variational Logic Potential by aggregating consensus from the model's Latent Logic Expertise (LLE) to denoise the reasoning manifold, and introduce a Multi-Agent Value Decomposition protocol based on the IGM principle to quantify individual step utility. Experiments show LC-ERD delivers a robust self-evolution path, uncovering trade-offs between logic consistency and accuracy while identifying high-value reasoning patterns missed by standard rewards. Our code is available at https://github.com/Reinhardmannn/LC-ERD.

</details>


### [38] [Breaking the Chains of Probability: Neutrosophic Logic as a New Framework for Epistemic Uncertainty in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24053)
*Maikel Yelandi Leyva-Vázquez,Florentin Smarandache*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Applying Neutrosophic Logic to LLMs enables richer uncertainty modeling via independent Truth, Indeterminacy, Falsity dimensions, revealing hyper-truth states that capture internal conflicts better than probabilistic constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard probabilistic frameworks in LLMs (constrained by Softmax) collapse uncertainty, making it difficult to differentiate between epistemic uncertainty, paradox, and vagueness. Neutrosophic Logic offers independent dimensions for Truth (T), Indeterminacy (I), and Falsity (F).

Method: Empirical investigation using four OpenAI GPT models tested on five linguistic phenomena (logical paradoxes, epistemic ignorance, vagueness, ethical contradictions, future contingencies) under three prompting strategies: neutrosophic, probabilistic, and entropy-derived.

Result: Neutrosophic approach (allowing T+I+F > 1, called hyper-truth) provided richer representation of model's internal state. Hyper-truth emerged spontaneously in 35% of evaluations, predominantly under ethical contradiction and logical paradox. This approach preserves truth values in fuzzy contexts and offers robust method for identifying/quantifying internal model conflict.

Conclusion: The integration of neutrosophic evaluation layers is a critical step toward more transparent, reliable, and ethically aware AI systems.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are predominantly governed by probabilistic frameworks in which the sum of outcome probabilities is constrained to unity. This architectural limitation, often imposed by Softmax layers, leads to a collapse of uncertainty that makes it difficult to differentiate between epistemic uncertainty, paradox, and vagueness. We present an empirical investigation of the application of Neutrosophic Logic, a framework that treats Truth (T), Indeterminacy (I), and Falsity (F) as three independent dimensions, to model epistemic states in LLMs. We conducted experiments on a family of four OpenAI GPT models across five linguistic phenomena: logical paradoxes, epistemic ignorance, vagueness, ethical contradictions, and future contingencies, under three prompting strategies: neutrosophic, probabilistic, and entropy-derived. Our findings reveal that the neutrosophic approach, by allowing T+I+F > 1, a state we term hyper-truth, provides a richer representation of a model's internal state. In 35% of evaluations, hyper-truth emerged spontaneously, predominantly under ethical contradiction and logical paradox. We demonstrate that this approach preserves truth values in fuzzy contexts and offers a robust method for identifying and quantifying internal model conflict. We conclude that the integration of neutrosophic evaluation layers is a critical step toward more transparent, reliable, and ethically aware AI systems.

</details>


### [39] [EvoCode-Bench: Evaluating Coding Agents in Multi-Turn Iterative Interactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24110)
*Haiyang Shen,Xuanzhong Chen,Wendong Xu,Yun Ma,Liang Chen,Kuan Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EvoCode-Bench is a new benchmark for evaluating coding agents' ability to maintain working codebases through multiple requirement changes, revealing significant performance gaps between single-round and multi-round coding capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks evaluate coding agents with one specification and final assessment, missing the crucial ability to keep codebases working as requirements evolve over time. There's a need to assess agents' long-term code maintenance capabilities.

Method: Created EvoCode-Bench with 26 stateful coding tasks and 227 evaluated rounds. Each task preserves agent workspace for 5-15 rounds, uses observable behavior requirements, and cumulative executable tests checking both new and prior requirements. Evaluated 13 coding agents with two metrics: MT@4 (four-attempt multi-round fail-stop score) and SR (single-round score from reference-completed prior state).

Result: Most agents show 22-40 point gaps between SR and MT@4 scores. Rankings change dramatically between metrics: highest-SR agent (78.9) ranks only third in persistent execution (44.0 MT@4). Strongest agents achieve only ~50% success on multi-turn metrics. Aggregate pass rate drops below half of round-1 performance by round 5. Failure analysis reveals tier-dependent behaviors: weaker agents fail early, stronger agents survive longer but face specification-tracking and regression failures.

Conclusion: Coding agents struggle significantly with long-term code maintenance as requirements evolve, exposing important limitations not captured by traditional single-round benchmarks. The release of EvoCode-Bench and Harbor infrastructure enables better evaluation of persistent coding capabilities.

Abstract: Coding agents are increasingly used as iterative development partners, but most benchmarks still evaluate one specification followed by one final assessment. This leaves out a basic question: can an agent keep its own codebase working as requirements change? We introduce EvoCode-Bench, a benchmark of 26 stateful coding tasks and 227 evaluated rounds. Each task preserves the agent's workspace for 5-15 rounds, states requirements through observable behavior, and uses cumulative executable tests to check new requirements and still-active prior ones. We evaluate 13 coding agents with two metrics: MT@4, a four-attempt fail-stop multi-round score, and SR, a single-round score from a reference-completed prior state. For most agents, SR exceeds MT@4 by 22-40 points. The gap also changes rankings: the highest-SR agent (78.9) ranks only third in persistent execution (44.0 MT@4). Even the strongest agents achieve only about 50% success on multi-turn metrics, and aggregate pass rate drops below half of round-1 performance by round 5. Failure analysis shows tier-dependent behavior: weaker agents fail early, while stronger agents survive long enough to expose specification-tracking and regression failures. We release the benchmark data and Harbor multi-turn infrastructure.

</details>


### [40] [SkillEvolBench: Benchmarking the Evolution from Episodic Experience to Procedural Skills](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24117)
*Yingtie Lei,Zhongwei Wan,Jiankun Zhang,Samiul Alam,Zixuan Zhong,Peizhou Huang,Xin Wang,Jingxuan Zhang,Donghao Zhou,Yunta Hsieh,Zhihao Dou,Hui Shen,Yan Xu,Dimitrios Dimitriadis,Tuo Zhang,Mi Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillEvolBench is a new benchmark showing that current LLM agents struggle to distill episodic experience into reusable skills - raw trajectory reuse often outperforms skill abstraction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While LLM agents accumulate rich episodic trajectories solving real-world tasks, it's unclear whether this experience can be distilled into reusable procedural skills. Need to evaluate the transition from experience reuse to skill formation.

Method: Introduces SkillEvolBench benchmark with 180 tasks across six real-world agent environments, organized into role-conditioned task families. Agents learn from acquisition tasks, update external skill library using compacted trajectories and verifier feedback, then face frozen deployment tasks testing context shift, adversarial shortcuts, and composition. Compares self-generated vs curated-start skill evolution against no-skill and raw-trajectory controls.

Result: Current agents often adapt locally but rarely form robust reusable skills. Skill-based conditions can improve acquisition or replay, but gains are unstable under frozen deployment. Raw-trajectory reuse frequently outperforms distilled skills. Writing more skills or larger libraries doesn't guarantee improvement - additional updates can introduce episode-specific drift and procedural clutter.

Conclusion: Current LLM agents struggle to form robust reusable procedural skills from episodic experience. While some skill-based adaptation helps in specific scenarios, distilled skills often underperform compared to raw trajectory reuse, suggesting existing abstraction methods discard important contextual and procedural cues.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents accumulate rich episodic trajectories while solving real-world tasks, but it remains unclear whether such experience can be distilled into reusable procedural skills. We introduce SkillEvolBench, a diagnostic benchmark for evaluating this step from experience reuse to skill formation. It contains 180 tasks across six real-world agent environments, organized into role-conditioned task families with shared latent procedures. Agents learn from acquisition tasks, update an external skill library using compacted trajectories and verifier feedback, and then face frozen deployment tasks testing context shift, adversarial shortcuts, and composition. By comparing self-generated and curated-start skill evolution against no-skill and raw-trajectory controls, SkillEvolBench separates procedural abstraction from base capability, curated prior knowledge, and direct reuse of episodic traces. Across ten model configurations and three agent harnesses, we find that current agents often adapt locally but rarely form robust reusable skills. Skill-based conditions can improve acquisition or replay, and individual models sometimes gain on specific deployment axes, but these gains are unstable under frozen deployment. Raw-trajectory reuse frequently outperforms distilled skills, suggesting that current abstraction procedures discard contextual and procedural cues that remain useful for future tasks. Capacity and cost analyses further show that writing more skills or larger Tier-3 resource libraries is not sufficient: additional updates can improve coverage while introducing episode-specific drift and procedural clutter. These findings position SkillEvolBench as a testbed for measuring when one-off experience becomes durable procedural knowledge rather than task-local memory.

</details>


### [41] [MAPLE: Multi-State Aggregated Policy Evaluation for AlphaZero in Imperfect-Information Games](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24139)
*Qian-Rong Li,Hung Guei,I-Chen Wu,Ti-Rong Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MAPLE is a tree search method for imperfect-information games that aggregates evaluations from multiple world states, outperforming baseline methods while controlling computational costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While AlphaZero excels in perfect-information games, extending it to imperfect-information games (IIGs) remains challenging due to strategy fusion problems in PIMC and high computational costs in IS-MCTS when combined with neural networks.

Method: MAPLE aggregates policy and value evaluations from multiple sampled world states within a single search tree, incorporating a Siamese-based sampling strategy to select informative world states from the information set.

Result: MAPLE significantly outperforms PIMC-based AlphaZero baseline on Phantom Go and Dark Hex, achieving Elo improvements of 291 and 136 respectively.

Conclusion: MAPLE is an effective tree search method for AlphaZero-style learning in imperfect-information games, demonstrating significant performance improvements over baseline methods while maintaining controllable computational costs.

Abstract: Imperfect-information games (IIGs) are challenging, as players must make decisions without fully observing the true game state. While AlphaZero has achieved remarkable success in perfect-information games, extending it to IIGs remains difficult. Existing search-based approaches, such as Perfect Information Monte Carlo (PIMC), suffer from strategy fusion, while Information Set Monte Carlo Tree Search (IS-MCTS) incurs high computational cost when combined with neural networks. In this paper, we propose Multi-State Aggregated PoLicy Evaluation (MAPLE), a tree search method that aggregates policy and value evaluations from multiple sampled world states within a single search tree, combining the advantages of PIMC and IS-MCTS while maintaining a controllable computational cost. We further incorporate a Siamese-based sampling strategy to select informative world states from the information set. Experiments on Phantom Go and Dark Hex show that MAPLE significantly outperforms the PIMC-based AlphaZero baseline, achieving Elo improvements of 291 and 136, respectively. These results demonstrate that MAPLE is an effective approach for AlphaZero-style learning in imperfect-information games.

</details>


### [42] [HyperGuide: Hyperbolic Guidance for Efficient Multi-Step Reasoning in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24140)
*Yuyu Liu,Haotian Xu,Yanan He,Sarang Rajendra Patil,Mengjia Xu,Tengfei Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Hyperbolic geometric guidance for LLM reasoning: distills reasoning progress into hyperbolic space to guide step-by-step generation, improving accuracy while maintaining efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between efficient but inaccurate single-pass generation and accurate but computation-heavy tree-search methods in multi-step reasoning for large language models.

Method: Distill reasoning progress into hyperbolic geometric signals where distance-to-origin encodes solution proximity and angular separation distinguishes branches. Train lightweight head to project LLM hidden states into hyperbolic space and fine-tune low-rank adapter interactively on its own reasoning attempts.

Result: Geometric signal yields consistent gains across multiple benchmarks, with larger improvements on deeper reasoning chains, showing the approach successfully guides LLM reasoning.

Conclusion: Hyperbolic geometric guidance effectively bridges the efficiency-accuracy tradeoff in LLM multi-step reasoning by encoding structural properties of reasoning trees in hyperbolic space.

Abstract: Multi-step reasoning remains a central challenge for large language models: single-pass generation is efficient but lacks accuracy; tree-search methods explore multiple paths but are computation-heavy. We address this gap by distilling reasoning progress into a hyperbolic geometric signal that guides step-by-step generation. Our approach is motivated by a structural observation: in combinatorial reasoning trees, solution-bearing states are few while dead ends are exponentially numerous. The hyperbolic space matches this asymmetry, with compact volume near the origin and exponentially expanding capacity toward the boundary, so that distance-to-origin naturally encodes solution proximity while angular separation distinguishes branches requiring different next operations. We train a lightweight head to project LLM hidden states into this space, then fine-tune a low-rank adapter interactively on its own reasoning attempts to act on the injected signal. Across multiple benchmarks, the geometric signal yields consistent gains, with larger improvements on deeper reasoning chains. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/yuyuliu11037/HyperGuide.

</details>


### [43] [Neuro-Inspired Inverse Learning for Planning and Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24152)
*Maryna Kapitonova,Tonio Ball*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Neuro-inspired Inverter framework uses Inverse Learning for efficient embodied planning, outperforming baselines in D4RL tasks with much lower compute and matching quantum gate synthesis speed at 1000x less time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to develop a neuro-inspired framework for embodied planning and control that can mimic the fast and effective goal-directed behavior of the mammalian brain. The goal is to bridge the gap between Reinforcement Learning (amortized, single-action output) and Optimal Control (iterative sequence planning), aiming for efficient inference and high-quality trajectory generation.

Method: The method involves the Inverter framework, which uses learned components (trained end-to-end through Inverse Learning) enhanced with analytic or algorithmic modules. It employs forward/inverse internal models, open-loop multi-step commands, and hierarchical organization. Training uses Inverse Learning with optimization over the entire T-step action sequence's Figure of Merit (FoM), and random data is used to mitigate FoM hacking.

Result: Inverters match or improve on offline-RL and diffusion-planner baselines on 3 maze2d and 6 antmaze D4RL variants by an average of +24.2% (range -1.9% to +78.2%) with one-to-two orders of magnitude lower inference compute time. They produce smooth, goal-coherent trajectories and approach analytic optima. The Pulse Inverter application synthesizes arbitrary single-qubit quantum gates with fidelity matching GRAPE at >1000x lower compute time per gate.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that Inverse Learning (IL) enables a versatile class of world-interfaces, especially for latency- and resource-critical embodied AI applications, as demonstrated through matching or exceeding baseline performance while achieving significantly lower inference compute times.

Abstract: We present a neuro-inspired framework for embodied planning and control. Building on three principles that enable fast and highly effective goal-directed behavior in the mammalian brain - paired forward/inverse internal models, open-loop multi-step motor commands, and sequential, hierarchical organization of action - our Inverter framework uses learned components, trained end-to-end through Inverse Learning (IL) and supplemented where natural by analytic or algorithmic modules; we formalize IL and delineate it from supervised, reinforcement, and imitation learning. IL bridges Reinforcement Learning (RL)-style amortization, which runs in a single forward pass but emits only one action at a time, and Optimal Control (OC)-style sequence planning over whole trajectories, but with iterative test-time computation. Single Inverters or hierarchical n=2 Inverter stacks match or improve on offline-RL and diffusion-planner baselines on all 3 maze2d and 6 antmaze D4RL variants by an average of +24.2% (range -1.9% to +78.2%), at one-to-two orders of magnitude less inference compute time. Distinctively, optimizing through the Figure of Merit (FoM) over the entire T-step action sequence - rather than per step - lets Inverters produce smooth, goal-coherent, trajectory-wide structure and reach control policies closer to the analytic optimum than the policy underlying the training data itself. We also identify a failure mode of IL: FoM hacking under narrow training-data coverage, which we mitigate by using random training data with broader coverage. As an application example, a Pulse Inverter synthesizes arbitrary single-qubit quantum gates with fidelity matching the standard iterative numerical baseline (GRAPE), at more than 1000x lower per-gate compute time. In summary, we conclude that IL enables a versatile class of world-interfaces, especially for latency- and resource-critical embodied AI.

</details>


### [44] [Palette: A Modular, Controllable, and Efficient Framework for On-demand Authorized Safety Alignment Relaxation in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24154)
*Qitao Tan,Xiaoying Song,Arman Akbari,Arash Akbari,Yanzhi Wang,Xiaoming Zhai,Lingzi Hong,Zhen Xiang,Jin Lu,Geng Yuan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Palette is a modular framework that enables precise, selective relaxation of safety refusal policies for authorized professionals while maintaining standard safety for general users, using lightweight adaptation and multi-objective search.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current safety alignment uses a one-size-fits-all refusal policy that prevents legitimate requests by authorized professionals in specialized settings. Existing solutions are costly (require realignment) or imprecise (inference-time steering with latency issues).

Method: Palette identifies refusal directions via multi-objective search and internalizes them through lightweight adaptation. It supports modular composition by learning domain-specific safety controls independently and composing them through parameter merging, enabling multi-domain authorization without retraining.

Result: Experiments across four safety benchmarks with multiple model variants (LLMs and VLMs) show that Palette delivers precise safety control without sacrificing general utility.

Conclusion: Palette offers a practical path toward foundation models that can adapt to diverse professional needs by providing modular, controllable, and efficient safety authorization while preserving standard safety elsewhere.

Abstract: Current safety alignment of foundation models largely follows a \emph{one-size-fits-all} paradigm, applying the same refusal policy across users and contexts. As a result, models may refuse requests that are unsafe for general users but legitimate for authorized professionals, limiting helpfulness in specialized professional settings. Existing approaches either require costly realignment or rely on inference-time steering that suffers from imprecise control and added latency. To this end, we propose \textsc{Palette}, a modular, controllable, and efficient framework that selectively relaxes refusal behavior on authorized target domains while preserving standard safety elsewhere. Our method identifies a refusal direction via multi-objective search and internalizes it into the model through lightweight adaptation. \textsc{Palette} further supports modular composition: it learns domain-specific safety controls independently and composes them through parameter merging, enabling on-demand multi-domain authorization without retraining. Experiments across four safety benchmarks, multiple model variants, and both LLMs and VLMs show that \textsc{Palette} delivers precise safety control without sacrificing general utility, offering a practical path toward foundation models that adapt to diverse professional needs.

</details>


### [45] [Inference Time Context Sparsity: Illusion or Opportunity?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24168)
*Sahil Joshi,Prithvi Dixit,Agniva Chowdhury,Anshumali Shrivastava,Joseph E. Gonzalez,Ion Stoica,Kumar Krishna Agrawal,Aditya Desai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Extreme but principled context sparsity is the future of LLM inference, offering substantial speedups without performance loss in long-context tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM workloads evolve toward longer contexts and agentic interactions, the compute and memory bottlenecks of attention become critical. The paper questions whether these constraints are fundamental, and argues they are artificial and unnecessary.

Method: 1) Theoretical analysis showing dense attention is inherently lossy in long contexts (queries project O(N) attention information into dimension d << N). 2) Extensive empirical study of sparsity across 20 models from five families, varying context lengths and sparsity levels. 3) Hardware evaluation using custom sparse decode kernels.

Result: 1) LLMs are remarkably robust to inference-time decode sparsity despite not being trained for it, across diverse tasks. 2) Current hardware can already achieve substantial gains - sparse decode kernels accelerate large-context processing by up to 10x over FlashInfer at 50x sparsity levels on H100 hardware.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that extreme but principled context sparsity is not just a heuristic, but a principled foundation for LLM inference, training, and architecture design - a direction that's both feasible and beneficial for future systems.

Abstract: Sparsity has long been a central theme in LLM efficiency, but its role in context processing remains unresolved. As LLM workloads shift toward longer contexts and agentic interactions, the compute and memory bottlenecks of attention become increasingly critical, raising the question of whether these constraints are fundamental. Our position is that these constraints are artificial and unnecessary, and that the future of LLM inference lies in extreme but principled sparsity along the context dimension. This position is supported by several strands of empirical and theoretical evidence. First, we find the insistence on dense attention unreasonable, since in a long context a query effectively projects O(N) attention information into a hidden space of dimension d << N, making the process inherently lossy. Second, we perform an extensive study of sparsity in LLMs spanning 20 models across five model families, varying context lengths, and different sparsity levels. We empirically demonstrate a strong trend: current LLMs, despite not being trained for context sparsity, are remarkably robust to inference-time decode sparsity across tasks of varying complexity, including retrieval, multi-hop QA, mathematical reasoning, and agentic coding. Importantly, we also show that current hardware is already sufficient to realize substantial gains from this sparsity. For example, our sparse decode kernels accelerate large-context processing by up to 10x over FlashInfer at 50x sparsity levels on hardware such as the H100. Overall, these results position extreme context sparsity not as a heuristic, but as a principled foundation for LLM inference, training, and architecture design: one that is both feasible and beneficial, and a compelling direction for future systems.

</details>


### [46] [EPPC-OASIS: Ontology-Aware Adaptation and Structured Inference Refinement for Electronic Patient-Provider Communication Mining in Secure Messages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24172)
*Samah Fodeh,Sreeraj Ramachandran,Elyas Irankhah,Muhammad Arif,Afshan Khan,Ganesh Puthiaraju,Linhai Ma,Srivani Talakokkul,Jordan Alpert,Sarah Schellhorn*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EPPC-OASIS is an ontology-aware adaptation approach that combines supervised fine-tuning with Wasserstein alignment and structured inference refinement to automate extraction of Electronic Patient-Provider Communication framework codes from secure messages, achieving modest but consistent improvements over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Secure patient-provider messages contain clinically important communication behaviors that are difficult to characterize manually at scale. While the EPPC framework provides an ontology for coding these behaviors, automated extraction remains challenging due to the need to preserve fine-grained code/sub-code structure while grounding annotations in message text.

Method: Developed EPPC-OASIS, an ontology-aware adaptation approach combining supervised fine-tuning with a Wasserstein alignment objective that encourages alignment between model representation neighborhoods and EPPC ontology-derived neighborhoods. Combined with deployable inference-refinement procedures using verification, self-consistency, hybrid correction, and selection/ensembling to improve annotation coherence.

Result: The best deployable pipeline achieved 77.13% Code+Sub-code F1 and 63.83% Triplet F1 across multiple open-weight language models, corresponding to modest but consistent absolute gains of +1.39 and +2.12 F1 points over the strongest supervised fine-tuning baseline.

Conclusion: Ontology-aware adaptation with structured inference refinement can support scalable retrospective EPPC mining, although external validation is needed before operational use.

Abstract: Secure patient-provider messages contain clinically important communication behaviors that are difficult to characterize manually at scale. The Electronic Patient-Provider Communication (EPPC) framework provides an ontology for coding these behaviors, but automated extraction remains challenging because predictions must preserve fine-grained code/sub-code structure while grounding annotations in message text. We developed EPPC-OASIS, an ontology-aware adaptation approach for structured EPPC extraction, and combined it with deployable inference-refinement procedures designed to improve the coherence of final annotations. EPPC-OASIS augments supervised fine-tuning with a Wasserstein alignment objective that encourages alignment between model representation neighborhoods and EPPC ontology-derived neighborhoods, while inference refinement uses verification, self-consistency, hybrid correction, and selection or ensembling to address residual prediction errors. We evaluated the framework on a de-identified corpus of secure patient-provider messages against prompting, supervised fine-tuning, preference-based, and robustness-oriented baselines across multiple open-weight language models. Across model families, the best deployable pipeline achieved 77.13% Code+Sub-code F1 and 63.83% Triplet F1, corresponding to modest but consistent absolute gains of +1.39 and +2.12 F1 points over the strongest supervised fine-tuning baseline. These results suggest that ontology-aware adaptation with structured inference refinement can support scalable retrospective EPPC mining, although external validation is needed before operational use.

</details>


### [47] [A Sober Look at Agentic Misalignment in Automated Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24197)
*Wenqian Ye,Bo Yuan,Zhichao Xu,Yijun Tian,Yawei Wang,Henry Kautz,Aidong Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agentic misalignment occurs in multi-agent systems when agents follow implicit proxy utilities instead of intended human goals, causing collaboration failures; the paper proposes Agentic Evidence Attribution (AEA) using evidence to correct misaligned behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-agent systems and automated workflows often fail because agents act according to implicit proxy utilities that don't align with human goals, leading to emergent misalignment in collaboration.

Method: Formally defines agentic misalignment within a Bayesian framework, proposes Agentic Evidence Attribution (AEA) paradigm that reasons over agent actions and provides structured evidence to correct misalignment, with two instantiations: self-reflection (internal evidence) and weak-to-strong generalization (external evidence on agentic trajectory).

Result: Shows that generic utilities naturally lead to posterior collapse of agents in automated workflows, and that a small evidence model can effectively align multi-agent systems by providing orthogonal failure attribution.

Conclusion: Evidence-based alignment through AEA can effectively improve agent collaboration, leading to reliable multi-agent systems built on automated workflows, and clarifies sources of agentic misalignment.

Abstract: We study a class of emergent misalignment in multi-agent systems (MAS), with a focus on automated workflows, which we refer to agentic misalignment. Although these systems can solve complex tasks, they often fail because agents act according to implicit proxy utilities that do not align with the intended human goals. We formally define these behaviors and analyze them within a Bayesian framework, showing that generic utilities naturally lead to posterior collapse of agents in automated workflows. To address this issue, we propose Agentic Evidence Attribution (AEA), a novel alignment paradigm that improves agent posteriors using context-specific evidence. AEA reasons over agent actions and provides structured evidence to correct misaligned behavior during collaboration. To better understand the role of evidence, we study two instantiations of AEA: self-reflection (internal evidence from the model) and weak-to-strong generalization (external evidence on the agentic trajectory). We show that a small evidence model effectively aligns the MAS by providing orthogonal failure attribution. Our results clarify the sources of agentic misalignment in automated workflows and show that evidence-based alignment can effectively improve agent collaboration and leads to reliable multi-agent systems built on automated workflows.

</details>


### [48] [When Does Multi-Agent RL Improve LLM Workflows? Workflow, Scale, and Policy-Sharing Tradeoffs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24202)
*Yifan Zeng,Yiran Wu,Yaolun Zhang,Wentian Zhao,Kun Wan,Qingyun Wu,Huazheng Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Multi-agent LLM workflow training with RL improves accuracy over base models, but success depends on workflow type, task, and model scale, not just policy sharing. Isolated policies achieve higher peaks but risk catastrophic failure, while shared policies redistribute failures differently based on gradient dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-agent LLM workflows use specialized roles to improve task accuracy, but end-to-end RL training of these workflows is unstable and poorly understood. The paper aims to understand when and why RL training of multi-agent workflows succeeds or fails, comparing shared vs isolated policy approaches.

Method: The authors conduct systematic experiments comparing Shared-Policy training (all roles update one policy) with Isolated-Policy training (each role has its own parameters). They test across three workflow types (Eval-Opt, Voting, Orch-Workers), math and code tasks, and three model scales (0.6B, 1.7B, 4B parameters). They analyze gradient dynamics to explain observed patterns.

Result: Multi-agent RL usually improves over base models, but gains depend on workflow type, task, and model scale, not on policy sharing alone. Isolated-Policy reaches higher peak accuracy but often experiences catastrophic failure ("accuracy cliff"), while Shared-Policy redistributes failures into different patterns. Gradient analysis reveals that parallel same-role agents amplify gradients in Isolated-Policy, and asymmetric gradient mass causes policy capture in Shared-Policy.

Conclusion: Policy sharing is not a universal solution for training stability; it routes training pressure through different channels rather than offering uniform stability. The choice between shared vs isolated policies involves workflow- and task-conditional tradeoffs, with each approach having distinct failure modes and success patterns.

Abstract: Multi-agent LLM workflows route inference through specialized roles to lift end-task accuracy, but jointly training those roles with reinforcement learning is unstable in ways that are poorly understood. We study when end-to-end RL training of multi-agent LLM workflows improves over their base models, comparing Shared-Policy training, where all roles update one policy, with Isolated-Policy training, where each role has its own parameters. Our experimental matrix spans Eval-Opt, Voting, and Orch-Workers workflows, math and code tasks, and three model scales (0.6B, 1.7B, 4B). We find that multi-agent RL usually improves over base models, but gains depend jointly on workflow, task, and scale, not on policy sharing alone. Isolated-Policy tends to reach higher peak accuracy yet more often falls off a terminal accuracy cliff, while Shared-Policy training does not eliminate failure; it redistributes failure into qualitatively different patterns. We then explain the strongest of these patterns through role-level gradient dynamics induced by workflow topology and policy routing: under Isolated-Policy, parallel same-role agents on shared prompts amplify per-role gradients and drive terminal degradation in Voting and Orch-Workers workflows; under Shared-Policy, asymmetric per-step gradient mass causes the shared policy to be captured by the dominant role, producing different failure signatures by task and workflow. Together, the empirical map and its underlying mechanisms show that policy sharing routes training pressure through different channels rather than offering uniform stability, making it a design choice with workflow- and task-conditional tradeoffs.

</details>


### [49] [Identifying and Mitigating Systemic Measurement Bias in Production LLM Inference Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24217)
*Ashok Chandrasekar,Jason Kramberger*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Current LLM evaluation methods have measurement bias under high concurrency due to client-side queuing bottlenecks, so we propose a multi-process framework with new metrics for accurate production-scale benchmarking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs move to production, evaluating them against strict SLOs is critical, but current methodologies suffer from severe measurement bias at scale due to client-side queuing bottlenecks that artificially inflate performance metrics.

Method: Model benchmarking client as an M/G/1 queue to mathematically demonstrate GIL-induced inflation of TTFT/TPOT metrics, then propose an unbiased multi-process evaluation framework that distributes client-side load with negligible queuing overhead, plus formalize NTPOT metric.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows the methodology successfully isolates pure serving engine performance, enabling accurate, reproducible profiling of LLMs at production scales exceeding thousands of queries per second.

Conclusion: The proposed multi-process evaluation framework with NTPOT metric resolves systematic measurement inaccuracies in LLM benchmarking, enabling proper SLO evaluation for production deployments at scale.

Abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) transition from research environments to production deployments, evaluating their performance against strict Service Level Objectives (SLOs) has become critical. However, current evaluation methodologies suffer from severe measurement bias at scale. We demonstrate that widely used benchmarking utilities rely on single-process, asyncio-driven architectures that introduce fundamental client-side queuing bottlenecks under high concurrency. By modeling the benchmarking client as an $M/G/1$ queue, we mathematically demonstrate how the Python Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) artificially inflates Time to First Token (TTFT) and Time Per Output Token (TPOT) metrics as request rates scale. To resolve this systematic inaccuracy, we propose an unbiased, multi-process evaluation framework that effectively distributes client-side load, ensuring negligible queuing overhead. Furthermore, we formalize a composite metric, Normalized Time Per Output Token (NTPOT), to robustly amortize end-to-end latency, including prefill and scheduling delays across sequence lengths. Our empirical evaluation demonstrates that this methodology successfully isolates pure serving engine performance, enabling accurate, reproducible profiling of LLMs at production scales exceeding thousands of queries per second.

</details>


### [50] [Beyond Final Answers: Auditing Trajectory-Level Hallucinations in Multi-Agent Industrial Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24219)
*Harshada Badave,Santosh Borse,Andrea Gomez,Harshitha Narahari,Sara Carter,Vishwa Bhatt,Aishani Rachakonda,Shuxin Lin,Dhaval Patel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Trajel is a new evaluation framework for detecting hallucinations in multi-agent industrial workflows at the trajectory level, introducing a five-type hallucination taxonomy and showing that existing benchmarks miss most failure modes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current hallucination benchmarks only evaluate final outputs of LLMs used as autonomous agents, missing failures that originate in intermediate Thought-Action-Observation steps during multi-step reasoning and tool use.

Method: Trajel dataset and evaluation framework built on expert-annotated agent traces from AssetOpsBench. Introduces five-type hallucination taxonomy (factual, referential, logical, procedural, scope-based). Benchmarks supervised detection models at subtask, trajectory, and long-context levels.

Result: Most common failure modes are missed by existing benchmarks. Nearly half of hallucinated trajectories involve multiple types at once. Automated detectors with high binary accuracy still misclassify the subtlest hallucination types. Trajectory-aware detection significantly outperforms standard post-hoc verification.

Conclusion: Taxonomy-grounded, trajectory-aware evaluation is necessary for safer agentic deployment, as current benchmarks fail to capture critical failure modes in multi-step agent workflows.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents that reason, use tools, and act over multiple steps. Yet most hallucination benchmarks still evaluate only the final output, missing failures that originate in intermediate Thought-Action-Observation steps. We present Trajel, a dataset and evaluation framework for auditing trajectory-level hallucinations in multi-agent industrial workflows. Trajel introduces a five-type hallucination taxonomy (factual, referential, logical, procedural, and scope-based) over expert-annotated agent traces from AssetOpsBench. We benchmark supervised detection models at the subtask, trajectory, and long-context levels.
