Our results show that the most common failure modes are missed by existing benchmarks, that nearly half of hallucinated trajectories involve multiple types at once, and that automated detectors with high binary accuracy still misclassify the subtlest types. Trajectory-aware detection significantly outperforms standard post-hoc verification, making taxonomy-grounded evaluation necessary for safer agentic deployment.

</details>


### [51] [How Well Do Models Follow Their Constitutions?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24229)
*Arya Jakkli,Senthooran Rajamanoharan,Neel Nanda*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes an audit pipeline to test AI models' adherence to their behavioral specifications under adversarial multi-turn pressure, finding that newer models violate their own lab's specifications less frequently than older ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Frontier AI labs now train models against long written behavioral specifications (like Anthropic's constitution and OpenAI's Model Spec), but it's unclear how well models actually follow these specifications under adversarial, multi-turn pressure similar to real-world deployment scenarios.

Method: The authors develop a multi-method audit pipeline that: 1) decomposes specifications into atomic testable tenets (205 for Anthropic, 197 for OpenAI), 2) generates multi-turn adversarial scenarios using the Petri auditing agent, 3) runs a modified SURF-style rubric search to catch shallow single-turn failures, 4) validates flagged transcripts against specifications, and 5) compares findings against labs' own published system cards. They apply this across seven models per specification.

Result: Models follow their own lab's specification substantially better with each generation. For Anthropic's constitution: violation rate falls from 15.0% (Sonnet 4) to 2.0% (Sonnet 4.6). For OpenAI's Model Spec: violation rate falls from 11.7% (GPT-4o) to 3.6% (GPT-5.2 medium reasoning), with severity ceiling falling from 10/10 to 7/10. Remaining failures cluster around operator-imposed personas under AI-identity questioning, irreversible action in agentic deployments, and fabricated quantitative claims.

Conclusion: While AI models are improving at following their behavioral specifications, significant gaps remain under adversarial testing. The audit pipeline provides a methodology for external evaluation of specification adherence, though it cannot isolate whether improvements come from specification-specific training, broader post-training improvements, or evaluation awareness.

Abstract: Frontier AI developers now train models against long written behavioral specifications, such as Anthropic's constitution (Anthropic, 2025a) and OpenAI's Model Spec (OpenAI, 2025a), integrated into post-training via methods like character training (Anthropic, 2024) and deliberative alignment (Guan et al., 2024). These documents serve a governance function, but it is unclear how well models actually follow them under adversarial, multi-turn pressure similar to what they would face in real-world deployment. We propose a multi-method audit pipeline that treats each lab's published specification as an auditable target: it decomposes the specification into atomic testable tenets (205 for Anthropic, 197 for OpenAI), generates multi-turn adversarial scenarios with the Petri auditing agent (Anthropic, 2025b), runs a modified SURF-style rubric search (Murray et al., 2026) to catch shallow single-turn failures Petri misses, validates flagged transcripts against the relevant specification, and compares the findings against the lab's own published system card. Applying the pipeline across seven models per specification, we find that models follow their own lab's specification substantially better with each generation. On Anthropic's constitution, the Claude family falls from a 15.0% violation rate (Sonnet 4) to 2.0% (Sonnet 4.6); on OpenAI's Model Spec, the GPT family falls from 11.7% (GPT-4o) to 3.6% (GPT-5.2 medium reasoning), with the severity ceiling falling from 10/10 to 7/10. We cannot externally isolate whether these gains come from specification-specific training, broader post-training improvements, or evaluation awareness. Remaining failures cluster around operator-imposed personas under AI-identity questioning, irreversible action in agentic deployments, and fabricated quantitative claims with false precision.

</details>


### [52] [Toward Enactive Artificial Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24238)
*Banafsheh Rafiee,Richard Sutton*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Paper argues for integrating enactive cognitive science into AI to move beyond passive perception models toward active, embodied engagement with the world, identifying key enactive concepts and analyzing resonances with reinforcement learning while noting gaps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitation of mainstream AI approaches that treat cognition as detached internal processing, neglecting the dynamic, embodied, and interactive nature of perception advocated by enactive cognitive science.

Method: The paper identifies and develops four key enactive concepts (experience, action-perception inseparability, autonomy, embodiment) and analyzes their relevance to AI. It examines structural resonances between reinforcement learning and enactive principles, while noting limitations.

Result: Reinforcement learning exhibits structural resonance with enactive principles through its emphasis on action, agent-environment interaction, feedback-driven adaptation, and agent-centered evaluation. However, this is not theoretical equivalence, as RL approximates some enactive insights but misses key elements.

Conclusion: Enactive approaches offer valuable insights for AI by viewing perception as active, skillful engagement. While RL resonates with some enactive principles, full integration requires deeper attention to enactive concepts like experience, action-perception inseparability, autonomy, and embodiment.

Abstract: In this paper, we advocate for incorporating enactive approaches to perception and cognition into artificial intelligence (AI). Enactive approaches view perception as an active, skillful engagement with the world, where agents perceive by acting and by understanding how their actions shape their experience. This contrasts with classical views that treat perception as a passive internal process in which the brain receives sensory input, processes it, and issues commands for action. Enactive views emphasize the dynamic, embodied, and interactive character of perception, grounded in the lived experience of agents embedded in their environments. We identify and develop four key enactive concepts that we find most relevant to AI: experience, action perception inseparability, autonomy, and embodiment. Much of mainstream AI, from classical rule based systems to large language models, has largely neglected these insights, treating cognition as internal processing detached from embodied interaction and intrinsic normativity. Reinforcement learning (RL), however, exhibits structural resonance with enactive principles through its emphasis on action, agent environment interaction, feedback driven adaptation, and agent centered evaluation. However, this resonance should not be taken as theoretical equivalence, as RL approximates some enactive insights, but key elements remain absent or weakly developed. Building on this analysis, we suggest a broader incorporation of enactive ideas into both mainstream AI and RL.

</details>


### [53] [Safety-Oriented Routing Analysis of Mixtral MoE Under Benign and Harmful Prompts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24270)
*Md Nurul Absar Siddiky*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Analysis of routing behavior in Mixtral 8x7B-Instruct shows safety-relevant routing is subtle, depth-dependent, and distributed across experts rather than dominated by fixed expert sets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse mixture-of-experts (MoE) models activate only a subset of parameters per token, making router behavior crucial for understanding model computation and safety properties, especially under both benign and harmful prompts.

Method: Used two complementary signals: 1) activation-based routing scores from expert selection frequencies, and 2) gradient-based scores from router-gate sensitivities. Analyzed expert- and layer-level routing behavior and conducted expert-suppression interventions on Mixtral 8x7B-Instruct.

Result: Activation-based expert usage is broad and long-tailed, while gradient-based importance is concentrated. Benign/harmful prompts show modest separation at expert level. Routing selectivity peaks at layers 8-15 (activation-based) vs final layers (gradient-based). Most experts are shared across prompt types, with limited subsets showing group preference. Intervention experiments show suppressing top benign-dominant experts reduces restricted responses.

Conclusion: Safety-relevant routing in Mixtral is subtle, depth-dependent, and distributed rather than dominated by a fixed set of experts, suggesting safety mechanisms operate through complex routing patterns across the model architecture.

Abstract: Sparse mixture-of-experts (MoE) language models activate only a small subset of parameters for each token, making router behavior a central part of model computation. This paper studies routing behavior of Mixtral 8x7B-Instruct under benign and harmful prompts using two complementary signals: activation-based routing scores derived from expert selection frequencies and gradient-based scores derived from router-gate sensitivities. We analyze expert- and layer-level routing behavior and conduct expert-suppression interventions. The results show that activation-based expert usage is broad and long-tailed, whereas gradient-based importance is concentrated. At expert level, benign and harmful prompt groups remain close under both signals with modest separation. At layer level, activation-based routing is most selective around layers 8-15, while gradient-based importance is concentrated in final layers. Expert classification shows most experts are shared across benign and harmful prompts, though a limited subset shows clear group preference. Top-ranked expert sets show stronger benign-malicious overlap under gradient scores than activation scores, suggesting concentration on a common late-layer expert set. In intervention experiments, suppressing top five benign-dominant experts from activation scores reduces restricted responses from 24 to 14 over 100 prompts, while suppressing gradient-derived experts reduces them from 34 to 22 with fewer unintended reversals. Overall, safety-relevant routing in Mixtral is subtle, depth-dependent, and distributed rather than dominated by a fixed set of experts.

</details>


### [54] [When Does Synthetic Patent Data Help? Volume-Fidelity Trade-offs in Low-Resource Multi-Label Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24296)
*Amirhossein Yousefiramandi,Ciaran Cooney*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM-generated synthetic data mainly helps low-resource multi-label patent classification through volume rather than quality, with synthetic data effectiveness being highly context-dependent and metric-specific.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand when and how LLM-generated synthetic data genuinely helps low-resource multi-label patent classification, separating real synthetic value from the confounding effect of simply having more data volume.

Method: Comprehensive experimental design across six LLMs (3.8-12B), four real-data regimes, 64 WIPO assistive-technology labels, two generation strategies, and three classifier families. Used controlled experiments including duplicate-to-match real-only controls, fixed-budget mixing experiments, fidelity metrics analysis, and various leakage controls including label masking and numerical artifact investigation.

Result: The apparent large performance jump (BERT-for-Patents micro-F1 from 0.120 to 0.702) is mostly volume-driven. Controlled synthetic gain is only +0.024 over duplicate control but +0.219 over focal-loss baseline. Key findings: fidelity metrics change meaning with scale, optimal mixing is 20-30% real/70-80% synthetic, paraphrase scaling collapses, shuffled mixing works best, and ModernBERT "collapse" was due to numerical artifacts. Synthetic data helps classification but hurts retrieval performance (26% nDCG@10 drop).

Conclusion: Synthetic patent text effectiveness is highly task- and metric-specific, not reducible to prompt genre alone, and requires careful evaluation to distinguish volume effects from genuine quality improvements.

Abstract: We study when LLM-generated synthetic data helps low-resource multi-label patent classification, separating true synthetic value from the confound that larger augmented sets can win by volume alone. Across six open-source LLMs (3.8-12B), four real-data regimes, 64 WIPO assistive-technology labels, two generation strategies, and three classifier families, the headline BERT-for-Patents micro-F1 jump from 0.120 to 0.702 is largely volume-driven. A duplicate-to-match real-only control that resamples 165 patents to the augmented size reaches 0.678; the controlled synthetic gain is only +0.024 over this control, but +0.219 over focal-loss reweighting, the strongest non-augmentation baseline. The main finding is that fidelity metrics change meaning with scale: at extreme scarcity, MMD correlates positively with classification gain (r=+0.95), but at 1:10 the relation flips (r=-0.73; Fisher z=+6.47, p<0.001). Fixed-budget mixing finds a 20-30% real / 70-80% synthetic optimum; paraphrase scaling collapses from a 165-document seed; and shuffled mixing beats curriculum ordering, ensembling, and classifier-based filtering. Leakage controls -- label-name masking, instruction-level label removal, fine-grained evaluation, and keyword-overlap audits -- argue against label-string dependence as the main driver for BERT-for-Patents. The apparent ModernBERT collapse under label removal is traced to a Flash-Attention-2 + bf16 numerical artifact, recovering 65% of lost performance with fp32 eager attention. Finally, the same corpus that improves classification by up to +0.58 raw micro-F1 hurts a Jaccard-label-overlap retrieval proxy; even a standard-patent-only filter leaves a 26% nDCG@10 drop. Thus, synthetic patent text is task- and metric-specific, not reducible to prompt genre alone.

</details>


### [55] [Adaptive Human-AI Coordination via Hierarchical Action Disentanglement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24343)
*Adnan Ahmad,Bahareh Nakisa,Mohammad Naim Rastgoo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: IAD is a deep hierarchical RL framework that learns disentangled low-level action sequences conditioned on high-level latent skills, enabling robust adaptation to diverse and unseen partners through interpretable, partner-aware behaviors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human-AI collaboration requires agents that can adapt to diverse partner behaviors and skill levels while remaining robust to unseen partners. Existing methods often collapse to a single dominant behavior or learn poorly aligned skills, limiting effective coordination.

Method: Proposes Intrinsic Action Disentanglement (IAD), a deep hierarchical reinforcement learning framework that learns distinct, partner-aware low-level action sequences conditioned on high-level latent skills. Introduces an intrinsic reward that explicitly encourages disentangled action distributions of the agent's low-level policy across skills.

Result: Evaluated in Overcooked-AI domain across multiple layouts and diverse partner settings. IAD consistently outperforms strong baselines and achieves more reliable, adaptive coordination across all settings, including with unseen simulated partners, a human-proxy model trained on human-human gameplay, and real human partners.

Conclusion: IAD enables flexible adaptation to heterogeneous partner dynamics under distributional shift by capturing temporally extended interaction patterns, yielding an interpretable mapping between high-level decisions and partner-specific behavioral responses for effective human-AI collaboration.

Abstract: Human-AI collaboration requires agents that can adapt to diverse partner behaviors and skill levels while remaining robust to unseen partners. Existing methods often collapse to a single dominant behavior or learn poorly aligned skills, limiting effective coordination. We propose Intrinsic Action Disentanglement (IAD), a deep hierarchical reinforcement learning (DHRL) framework that learns distinct, partner-aware low-level action sequences conditioned on high-level latent skills. IAD introduces an intrinsic reward that explicitly encourages disentangled action distributions of the agent's low-level policy across skills, yielding an interpretable mapping between high-level decisions and partner-specific behavioral responses. By capturing temporally extended interaction patterns, IAD enables flexible adaptation to heterogeneous partner dynamics under distributional shift. We evaluate IAD in the Overcooked-AI domain across multiple layouts and diverse partner settings, including unseen simulated partners, a human-proxy model trained on human-human gameplay, and real human partners. Results show that IAD consistently outperforms strong baselines and achieves more reliable, adaptive coordination across all settings.

</details>


### [56] [Partner-Aware Hierarchical Skill Discovery for Robust Human-AI Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24352)
*Adnan Ahmad,Bahareh Nakisa,Mohammad Naim Rastgoo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PASD is a DHRL framework that learns partner-conditioned skills with contrastive rewards to improve adaptation to diverse human partners in collaborative tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current DHRL methods focus on agent-centric rewards and ignore partner behavior, leading to shortcut learning where agents exploit spurious information rather than genuinely adapting to dynamic partner behaviors. This limits agents' ability to effectively coordinate with novel human partners in human-AI teaming scenarios.

Method: PASD learns skills conditioned on partner behavior through a contrastive intrinsic reward that captures interaction patterns. It aligns skill representations across similar partners while maintaining discriminability across diverse strategies, structuring the skill space based on partner interactions to mitigate shortcut learning and promote behavioral consistency.

Result: PASD consistently outperforms existing population-based and hierarchical baselines in the Overcooked-AI benchmark with diverse partner populations. It demonstrates transferable skill learning that generalizes across varying skill levels and play styles, and shows effective adaptation even with human proxy models trained from human-human gameplay trajectories.

Conclusion: PASD enables robust and adaptive coordination in human-AI collaboration by learning partner-aware skills that effectively adapt to diverse partner behaviors, overcoming the limitations of conventional DHRL methods that overlook partner dynamics.

Abstract: Multi-agent collaboration, especially in human-AI teaming, requires agents that can adapt to novel partners with diverse and dynamic behaviors. Conventional Deep Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning (DHRL) methods focus on agent-centric rewards and overlook partner behavior, leading to shortcut learning, where skills exploit spurious information instead of adapting to partners' dynamic behaviors. This limitation undermines agents' ability to adapt and coordinate effectively with novel partners. We introduce Partner-Aware Skill Discovery (PASD), a DHRL framework that learns skills conditioned on partner behavior. PASD introduces a contrastive intrinsic reward to capture patterns emerging from partner interactions, aligning skill representations across similar partners while maintaining discriminability across diverse strategies. By structuring the skill space based on partner interactions, this approach mitigates shortcut learning and promotes behavioral consistency, enabling robust and adaptive coordination. We extensively evaluate PASD in the Overcooked-AI benchmark with a diverse population of partners characterized by varying skill levels and play styles. We further evaluate the approach with human proxy models trained from human-human gameplay trajectories. PASD consistently outperforms existing population-based and hierarchical baselines, demonstrating transferable skill learning that generalizes across a wide range of partner behaviors. Analysis of learned skill representations shows that PASD adapts effectively to diverse partner behaviors, highlighting its robustness in human-AI collaboration.

</details>


### [57] [Distilling Game Code World Model Generation into Lightweight Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24375)
*Tyrone Serapio,Arjun Prakash,Haoyang Xu,Kevin Wang,Amy Greenwald*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Large Language Models can be distilled to generate executable game environments (GameCWMs) through post-training with SFT and RLVR, improving rule adherence while using smaller models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current approaches for generating code world models from natural language rely on frontier LLMs with inference-time refinement loops, which limits accessibility and scalability. The authors aim to investigate whether these capabilities can be distilled into smaller models through post-training.

Method: The authors introduce: (1) a curated dataset of 30 games spanning perfect and imperfect information games, (2) a verification framework that evaluates generated code against structural and semantic game properties, and (3) a post-training pipeline combining Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). They experiment with Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct model.

Result: SFT increases syntactic correctness of generated code, while RLVR improves execution-level adherence to game rules. The pipeline makes Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct more capable of generating valid GameCWMs for both perfect and imperfect information games.

Conclusion: The proposed approach offers a scalable path toward automatic environment generation from natural language by distilling GameCWM generation capabilities into smaller models, addressing accessibility and scalability limitations of current frontier-model approaches.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown great ability in generating executable code from natural language, opening the possibility of automatically constructing environments for AI agents. Recent work on Code World Models (CWMs) demonstrates that LLMs can translate game rules into Python implementations compatible with solvers like Monte Carlo Tree Search. We study this problem in game settings, where generated environments must implement rules, legal actions, state transitions, observations, and rewards. We refer to these game-specific executable models as Game Code World Models (GameCWMs). However, current approaches to generating code world models rely on frontier models and inference-time refinement loops, limiting accessibility and scalability. This work investigates whether GameCWM generation capabilities can be distilled into smaller models through post-training. We introduce: (1) a curated dataset of 30 games spanning perfect and imperfect information games, (2) a verification framework that evaluates generated code against structural and semantic game properties, and (3) a post-training pipeline combining Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). We experiment with Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct and find that SFT can increase syntactic correctness, while RLVR can improve execution-level adherence to game rules, thereby improving Qwen's ability to generate valid GameCWMs in both perfect and imperfect information games. Overall, our pipeline makes Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct more capable of generating valid GameCWMs, thereby offering a scalable path toward automatic environment generation from natural language.

</details>


### [58] [A governance horizon for ethical-use constraints in open-weight AI models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24383)
*Weiwei Xu,Hengzhi Ye,Haoran Ye,Kai Gao,Vladimir Filkov,Minghui Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Disclosure-based governance of open-weight AI models fails to maintain traceability across deep model lineages, with governance signals decaying rapidly and creating a "governance horizon" beyond which accountability collapses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ethical constraints and AI governance policies for open-weight models rely on voluntary metadata disclosures that should propagate to downstream derivatives, but it's unclear if this disclosure-based approach can sustain traceability across deep model lineages.

Method: Audit of 2,142,823 model repositories on Hugging Face Hub to test traceability, measure restriction evidence decay, and formalize the "governance horizon." Conduct platform interventions to test different policy designs (inheritance-only vs mandatory-declaration) and compare with PyPI where governance signals use explicit machine-readable declarations.

Result: Restriction evidence decays with a half-life of 1.31 derivation steps (R²=0.98). Beyond 7 downstream generations, ≥80% of descendants lack sufficient evidence for governance determination (governance horizon). Policy design matters more than enforcement: inheritance-only designs require near-complete enforcement, while mandatory-declaration designs with orphan resolution shift the horizon even at moderate enforcement. Orphan lineage components remain undecidable under inheritance-only policies regardless of enforcement.

Conclusion: Disclosure-based governance has structurally limited reach in open-weight AI ecosystems. Achieving deep supply-chain accountability requires provenance mechanisms that propagate governance signals through the derivation process itself, rather than relying on voluntary metadata disclosures.

Abstract: Ethical constraints on open-weight AI models are both a reflection of societal concerns and a foundation for AI governance policy. They are expected to propagate to downstream derivatives while implemented as voluntary metadata disclosures that must be restated at each generation of reuse. We audit 2,142,823 model repositories on Hugging Face Hub to test whether this disclosure-based governance infrastructure can sustain traceability across deep model lineages. Restriction evidence decays with a half-life of 1.31 derivation steps ($R^2$=0.98), and beyond seven downstream generations at least 80% of descendant models lack sufficient public evidence for a governance determination, a depth boundary we formalize as the governance horizon. Platform-level interventions to restore missing licence metadata reveal that policy design (not enforcement alone) is the binding factor: inheritance-only designs require near-complete enforcement to move the horizon, whereas a mandatory-declaration design that explicitly resolves orphan lineage components shifts the horizon already at moderate enforcement. The structural bottleneck is lineages with no inheritable upstream intent: such orphan components remain undecidable under any inheritance-only policy regardless of enforcement rate, and unresolved upstream nodes additionally create direct downstream undecidability bottlenecks that inheritance rules alone cannot recover. Comparison with PyPI, where governance signals are carried by explicit machine-readable declarations, corroborates that the collapse is topology-specific to open-weight derivation rather than inherent to open ecosystems. These results establish that disclosure-based governance has a shallow, structurally determined reach in open-weight AI, and that achieving deep supply-chain accountability requires provenance mechanisms propagating governance signals through derivation itself.

</details>


### [59] [Understanding and Mitigating Premature Confidence for Better LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24396)
*Jingchu Gai,Guanning Zeng,Christina Baek,Chen Wu,J. Zico Kolter,Andrej Risteski,Aditi Raghunathan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A new reinforcement learning method called "progressive confidence shaping" trains models to gradually update confidence during reasoning rather than committing early, improving accuracy and reasoning quality across various tasks without needing external labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current language models show premature confidence - committing to answers early and using remaining tokens to rationalize them, which strongly predicts flawed reasoning. This limits gains from additional test-time compute. While process reward models could help, they require expensive step-level annotations that are scarce.

Method: Progressive confidence shaping uses a reinforcement learning objective that rewards gradual confidence growth and penalizes early commitment during reasoning. It exploits the signal from how a model's confidence evolves during reasoning, training models to update their confidence as they reason rather than committing early, with no external labels or reward models needed.

Result: The method significantly improves accuracy and reasoning quality across model scales (1.5B to 8B parameters) and tasks: On Countdown arithmetic, accuracy improved 3.2x (+42.0pp) with flawed reasoning dropping 48pp; on AIME math, Pass@64 improved 6.6pp; on science tasks, it also showed improvements. The method also improves faithfulness in safety benchmarks, making models more transparent about misleading content in reasoning traces.

Conclusion: Premature confidence is a fundamental scaling problem that grows with model size and task difficulty, but addressing it through progressive confidence shaping provides corresponding gains. The approach offers an effective way to improve reasoning quality without expensive external annotations by leveraging the internal confidence evolution signal.

Abstract: Long chains of thought (CoT) from current language models frequently contain logical gaps and unjustified leaps, limiting the gains from additional test-time compute. Improving reasoning quality directly would require process reward models, but the step-level annotations needed to train them are expensive and scarce. We find such a signal in how the model's confidence evolves during reasoning: premature confidence, the tendency to commit to an answer early and use the remaining tokens to rationalize it, strongly predicts flawed reasoning across tasks and model scales. We exploit this in progressive confidence shaping, a reinforcement learning objective that trains models to update their confidence as they reason rather than commit early -- rewarding gradual confidence growth and penalizing early commitment, with no external labels or reward models. The method improves accuracy and reasoning quality from 1.5B to 8B parameters across arithmetic (Countdown), math (DAPO, AIME), and science (ScienceQA): on Countdown, accuracy improves 3.2x (+42.0pp) and flawed reasoning drops 48pp; on AIME, Pass@64 improves 6.6pp. Consistent with this mechanism, the method also improves faithfulness: on a safety benchmark, our models more transparently surface misleading content in their reasoning traces rather than concealing it. Controlled experiments reveal that the problem and its remedy scale together: premature confidence grows with model size and task difficulty, and so do the gains from addressing it.

</details>


### [60] [ConceptM$^3$oE: Concept-Guided Multimodal Mixture of Experts for Interpretable Computational Pathology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24399)
*Xuan Wang,Zhongling Xu,Gopi Kannedhara,Joakim Nguyen,Jian Yu,Jinrui Fang,Abdurrahmaan Baghdadi,Tianlong Chen,Awais Naeem,Chandra Krishnan,Edward Castillo,Andrew H. Song,Ankita Shukla,Ying Ding,Nicholas Konz,Hairong Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ConceptM³oE is a multimodal AI framework for pathology that embeds interpretable concept learning within mixture-of-experts pathways, achieving competitive performance while maintaining clinical interpretability and improved data efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Healthcare AI needs to move beyond unimodal prediction to multimodal reasoning that can handle heterogeneous diagnostic inputs (whole-slide images, pathology reports, molecular data). Existing models fail to explain how different diagnostic signals combine to form recognizable diagnostic concepts, especially for complex tumor subtypes where morphology alone is insufficient.

Method: ConceptM³oE (Concept Multimodal MoE) uses interaction-aware mixture-of-experts pathways to decompose evidence into modality-specific, redundant, and synergistic experts. These are projected into structured concept bottlenecks that map latent features to a hierarchy of morphology and biomarker concepts. Residual pathways within each expert allow task-relevant signals to flow both through concepts and directly to final prediction, preventing information loss while maintaining interpretability.

Result: The framework achieves competitive performance with unconstrained models while producing clinically validated reasoning traces. In data-limited regimes, it improves macro-F1 from 56.41% to 66.70% at small training sizes compared to non-concept-informed baselines, and shows faster training convergence. Results were validated across institutional pediatric brain tumor and public glioma cohorts.

Conclusion: ConceptM³oE provides a scalable path toward high-performance medical AI that is inherently verifiable and better aligned with clinical decision-making by embedding interpretable concept formation directly within multimodal reasoning pathways.

Abstract: Healthcare models are transitioning from unimodal prediction toward multimodal reasoning over heterogeneous diagnostic inputs. In computational pathology, for complex tumor subtypes where morphology alone can be challenging to distinguish, pathology reports and molecular measurements may provide additional diagnostic evidence alongside whole-slide images, yet existing models often fail to clarify how diverse signals assemble into recognizable diagnostic concepts. We propose ConceptM$^3$oE (Concept Multimodal MoE), which embeds concept formation directly within interaction-aware mixture-of-experts (MoE) pathways. The architecture decomposes evidence into modality-specific, redundant, and synergistic experts, which are then projected into structured concept bottlenecks mapping latent features to a hierarchy of morphology and biomarker concepts. To prevent the information loss typical of interpretable bottlenecks, we utilize residual pathways within each expert to allow task-relevant signals to flow both through the concepts and directly to the final task prediction, so that high performance is maintained alongside interpretability. Across an institutional pediatric brain tumor cohort and a public glioma cohort, the framework delivers competitive performance to unconstrained models while producing reasoning traces validated by an independent neuropathologist. In data-limited regimes, ConceptM$^3$oE improves limited-data performance, increasing macro-F1 from 56.41% to 66.70% at small training sizes compared to non-concept-informed baselines, while also showing faster training convergence consistent with the regularizing effect of concept learning. This work offers a scalable path toward high-performance medical AI that is inherently verifiable and better aligned with the complex decision-making of clinical practice.

</details>


### [61] [Advancing Graph Few-Shot Learning via In-Context Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24410)
*Renchu Guan,Yajun Wang,Chunli Guo,Bowen Cao,Fausto Giunchiglia,Wei Pang,Yonghao Liu,Xiaoyue Feng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VISION advances graph few-shot learning via in-context learning, enabling fine-tuning-free inference by integrating local structures with global dependencies and leveraging unlabeled data through unsupervised task generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph few-shot learning methods face two key limitations: 1) They predominantly rely on supervised tasks and fail to leverage abundant unlabeled nodes in graphs, and 2) They often require complex task adaptation or fine-tuning during inference, which limits efficiency and applicability. Inspired by large language models' in-context learning capabilities, we aim to address these challenges by developing a fine-tuning-free approach that can effectively utilize unlabeled data.

Method: We propose VISION, a novel graph few-shot learning model that reframes the problem as a fine-tuning-free sequence reasoning task. Our model employs: 1) Role embeddings for node initialization, 2) A dual-context fusion module to integrate local topological structures and global task-level dependencies, 3) Dynamic generation of class-aware representations conditioned on support set context in a single forward pass, 4) An unsupervised task generator that creates structure-adaptive features and diverse pseudo-tasks from unlabeled data, and 5) Unification of unsupervised meta-learning with graph in-context learning.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate the superiority of our VISION model over existing methods. The model shows improved performance on graph few-shot learning tasks while achieving efficient inference without requiring fine-tuning. The approach effectively leverages unlabeled data through our unsupervised task generation strategy.

Conclusion: Our proposed VISION model successfully addresses key limitations in graph few-shot learning by introducing a fine-tuning-free approach through in-context learning. By integrating local structures and global dependencies, generating class-aware representations dynamically, and leveraging unsupervised tasks, our framework achieves state-of-the-art performance while maintaining computational efficiency during inference.

Abstract: Graph few-shot learning, which aims to classify nodes from novel classes with only a few labeled examples, is a widely studied problem in graph learning. However, existing methods often face two key limitations. First, the predominant graph few-shot learning paradigm relies on supervised tasks, failing to leverage the vast number of unlabeled nodes in the graph. Second, many approaches require complex task adaptation or fine-tuning during inference, limiting their efficiency and applicability. Inspired by the powerful in-context learning capabilities of large language models, we propose a novel model named VISION for adVancIng graph few-Shot learning via In-cOntext LearNing to address these challenges. Our model reframes graph few-shot learning as a fine-tuning-free sequence reasoning problem. At its core is a context-aware network that initializes nodes with role embeddings and employs a dual-context fusion module to synergistically integrate local topological structures and global task-level dependencies. This allows our model to dynamically generate class-aware representations for the query set conditioned on the support set context in a single forward pass. To effectively train our model, we introduce an unsupervised task generator that creates structure-adaptive features and constructs diverse pseudo-tasks from abundant unlabeled data. Our method unifies unsupervised meta-learning with graph in-context learning, achieving efficient inference. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate the superiority of our model. Our public code can be found

</details>


### [62] [The Model Is Not the Product: A Dual-Pillar Architecture for Local-First Psychological Coaching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24411)
*Alexander Mihalcea*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Psych LM is an iOS app with on-device LLM that achieves near-infinite context via automated memory corpus, enabling deep persistent emotional support through local-first architecture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current language model apps fail to provide adequate emotionally oriented support due to inability to maintain deep, persistent context across sessions, especially for behavioral coaching applications.

Method: Uses local on-device LLM with automated memory corpus that converts conversations into structured memory cards (facts, goals, events) and dynamically injects them via semantic/vector search, creating active-learning, retrieval-augmented generative architecture.

Result: Achieves near-infinite context window effect, provides four contributions: local-first privacy, persistent memory corpus, deterministic orchestration layer, and benchmark framework for system reliability in mobile environments.

Conclusion: Complex context-aware interaction can be reliably achieved in mobile environments by prioritizing architectural control and resource management over simple model size, with surrounding architecture being paramount for emotional support applications.

Abstract: Existing language model applications struggle to meet the demand for emotionally oriented support, primarily due to their inability to maintain deep, persistent context across sessions. This report introduces Psych LM, an iOS application that validates the thesis that, for such applications, the surrounding architecture is paramount. Psych LM runs a local, on-device language model within a purpose-built, local-first runtime designed for behavioral and life-coaching applications. The system achieves the practical effect of a near-infinite context window through an automated, user-inspectable memory corpus that converts conversations into structured memory cards, including facts, goals, and events, and dynamically injects them into the prompt via semantic and vector search. As such, the system can be defined as an active-learning, retrieval-augmented generative, on-device architecture. This architecture delivers four primary contributions: a local-first design where privacy is a core property; a detailed description of the memory corpus for persistent context of key user information; a deterministic orchestration layer that provides a stable behavioral spine independent of the model's internal state; and a benchmark framework focused on evaluating the integrated system's reliability under realistic operating conditions. The R and D process confirms that complex, context-aware interaction can be reliably achieved under the strict constraints of a mobile environment by prioritizing architectural control and resource management over simple model size.

</details>


### [63] [JT-SAFE-V2: Safety-by-Design Foundation Model with World-Context Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24414)
*Junlan Feng,Fanyu Meng,Chong Long,Pengyu Cong,Duqing Wang,Yan Zheng,Yuyao Zhang,Xuanchang Gao,Ye Yuan,Yunfei Ma,Zhijie Ren,Fan Yang,Na Wu,Di Jin,Chao Deng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: JT-Safe-V2 is a safety-enhanced LLM combining intelligence optimization with safety-by-design innovations. Safe-MoMA framework orchestrates multiple models and agents for cost-efficient deployment. Both show strong benchmark results and cost savings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To advance safety and trustworthiness of foundation models beyond previous JT-Safe model, toward comprehensive safety-by-design paradigm for enterprise-oriented agentic capabilities.

Method: JT-Safe-V2 uses contextual world knowledge enrichment in pre-training data, high-certainty pre-training procedures, and safety strengthening post-training mechanisms. Safe-MoMA is a mixture of models and agents framework for traceable, efficient inference.

Result: JT-Safe-V2 achieves SOTA performance on general intelligence and safety benchmarks. Safe-MoMA reduces inference costs by >30% vs largest standalone model baseline while maintaining performance.

Conclusion: JT-Safe-V2 advances safety-by-design foundation models through joint optimization of intelligence and safety, while Safe-MoMA enables cost-efficient deployment. The released model checkpoint supports further safety research.

Abstract: We introduce JT-Safe-V2, a large language model designed to advance the safety and trustworthiness of foundation models, extending our previous JT-Safe model toward a more comprehensive safety-by-design paradigm. JT-Safe-V2 emphasizes the joint optimization of general intelligence and safety-by-design through several key innovations: enriching pre-training data with contextual world knowledge, high-certainty pre-training procedures, and safety strengthening post-training mechanisms for enterprise-oriented agentic capabilities. Building on these safety-enhanced foundation models, we propose Safe-MoMA (Safe Mixture of Models and Agents), a framework that enables traceable and efficient inference through the orchestrated deployment of multiple models and agents. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that JT-Safe-V2 achieves state-of-the-art performance across both general intelligence and safety benchmarks. Moreover, Safe-MoMA reduces inference costs by more than 30\% compared to using the largest standalone model baseline while maintaining comparable performance. To facilitate future research on safety-by-design foundation models, we publicly release the post-trained JT-Safe-V2-35B model checkpoint.

</details>


### [64] [Benchmarking the Limits of In-Context Reinforcement Learning for Ad-Hoc Teamwork](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24423)
*Yuheng Jing,Kai Li,Ziwen Zhang,Jiajun Zhang,Zeyao Ma,Jiaxi Yang,Lei Zhang,Zhe Wu,Jinmin He,Junliang Xing,Jian Cheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: In-Context RL methods like AD and DPT fail to achieve robust adaptation in Ad-Hoc Teamwork settings with unknown partners, performing worse than random baselines on new teammates and layouts in Overcooked-V2.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While In-Context Reinforcement Learning (ICRL) has shown success in single-agent domains, its effectiveness in Ad-Hoc Teamwork (AHT) - where agents must coordinate with unknown partners - remains unexplored. The researchers aim to evaluate whether ICRL methods can enable foundation agents to adapt to novel team coordination scenarios.

Method: The authors introduce ICRL4AHT, a large-scale benchmark built on a high-throughput JAX implementation of Overcooked-V2. The benchmark includes: 1) A diverse teammate suite with both RL and heuristic policies, 2) Controlled train-test shifts, 3) An end-to-end pipeline for teammate generation, learning-history collection, dataset construction, and online multi-episode evaluation. They evaluate representative history-conditioned ICRL algorithms (Algorithm Distillation and Decision-Pretrained Transformer) across millions of transitions.

Result: The results show significant limitations: contrary to their success in single-agent domains, ICRL baselines fail to exhibit robust test-time adaptation in multi-agent settings. These methods frequently underperform random baselines across both unseen teammate and unseen layout tracks, with no clear in-context improvement over long horizons.

Conclusion: The findings highlight the challenges of strategic inference under partial observability in the Overcooked-V2 AHT protocol. The benchmark establishes a critical testbed for next-generation coordination algorithms, showing that current ICRL methods are insufficient for robust ad-hoc teamwork adaptation.

Abstract: In-Context Reinforcement Learning (ICRL) has enabled foundation agents to adapt instantaneously to novel tasks, yet its efficacy in Ad-Hoc Teamwork (AHT)-where coordination with unknown partners is required-remains unexplored. To rigorously evaluate this, we introduce a large-scale benchmark ICRL4AHT, built upon a high-throughput JAX implementation of Overcooked-V2. Our benchmark includes a large, diverse teammate suite spanning both RL and heuristic policies, enabling controlled train-test shifts, and provides a reproducible end-to-end pipeline for teammate generation, learning-history collection, dataset construction, and online multi-episode evaluation. We evaluate representative history-conditioned ICRL algorithms, including Algorithm Distillation (AD) and Decision-Pretrained Transformer (DPT), across millions of transitions. Results reveal notable limitations: contrary to their success in single-agent domains, these baselines fail to exhibit robust test-time adaptation in multi-agent settings. Specifically, these methods frequently underperform random baselines across both unseen teammate and unseen layout tracks, with no clear in-context improvement over long horizons. These findings highlight the challenges of strategic inference under partial observability within the OvercookedV2 AHT protocol, establishing our benchmark as a critical testbed for next-generation coordination algorithms.

</details>


### [65] [SAM: State-Adaptive Memory for Long-Horizon Reasoning Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24468)
*Yuyang Hu,Hongjin Qian,Shuting Wang,Jiongnan Liu,Ziliang Zhao,Jiejun Tan,Zheng Liu,Zhicheng Dou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SAM (State-Adaptive Memory) is a framework that improves long-horizon agentic reasoning by creating compact memory cues for lightweight access to distant historical information based on the agent's current state, without requiring retraining of the underlying LLM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon agentic reasoning requires LLMs to act over long interaction histories where relevant information may be scattered across distant steps and only become important later. Existing approaches (truncation, compression, retrieval) don't explicitly model how access to past interactions should adapt to the agent's evolving state.

Method: Proposes SAM framework that consolidates ongoing interaction into compact memory cues while preserving raw trajectory pages. These cues serve as lightweight handles for intent-driven recall, allowing agents to reconstruct temporally distant information according to current needs. Memory module is optimized through expert-guided supervision and reinforcement learning aligned with trajectory-level utility.

Result: SAM consistently outperforms strong baselines across four benchmarks: BrowseComp, BrowseComp-ZH, WideSearch, and HLE, demonstrating effectiveness over diverse agent backbones.

Conclusion: Explicit memory modeling provides a simple and effective foundation for long-horizon agentic reasoning, with SAM showing that state-adaptive memory can significantly improve performance without requiring retraining of the underlying LLM.

Abstract: Long-horizon agentic reasoning requires large language models to act over long interaction histories containing thoughts, tool calls, observations, and partial conclusions. The challenge is not merely that these histories grow long, but that information needed for the current decision may be scattered across distant steps and only become relevant later. Existing approaches address this difficulty by truncating the interaction history, compressing it into shorter surrogates, or retrieving selected parts of it for reuse, but they do not explicitly model how access to past interaction should adapt to the agent's evolving state. We instead cast long-horizon reasoning as a problem of state-adaptive memory. To this end, we propose State-Adaptive Memory~(SAM), a standalone framework that consolidates ongoing interaction into compact memory cues while preserving raw trajectory pages for intent-driven recall. These cues are not treated as replacements for history; rather, they serve as lightweight handles that allow the agent to reconstruct temporally distant information according to its current needs, without retraining the underlying backbone. We further optimize the memory module through expert-guided supervision and reinforcement learning, aligning it with trajectory-level utility. Across BrowseComp, BrowseComp-ZH, WideSearch, and HLE, SAM consistently outperforms strong baselines over diverse agent backbones. Our results suggest that explicit memory modeling provides a simple and effective foundation for long-horizon agentic reasoning.

</details>


### [66] [SPACE: Unifying Symmetric and Asymmetric Routing Problems for Generalist Neural Solver](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24484)
*Rongsheng Chen,Changliang Zhou,Canhong Yu,Yuanyao Chen,Yu Zhou,Zhuo Chen,Zhenkun Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SPACE framework unifies node representation for symmetric and asymmetric VRPs using spatial pivot-aligned coordinate-free embedding and adaptive decoding, achieving strong zero-shot generalization across 110 VRP variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generalist neural routing solvers are limited to symmetric settings or degrade in asymmetric settings due to input inconsistencies and structural differences, limiting practical real-world applications that encompass both scenarios.

Method: Proposes Spatial Pivot-Aligned Coordinate-free Embedding (SPACE) framework: 1) Defines spatial position based on relative distances to pivots, 2) Uses bidirectional Frchet representation with furthest pivot sampling for invariant node representations, 3) Introduces weight-decomposed adaptive decoding mechanism to decouple geometric perception from problem representations.

Result: Extensive experiments on 110 VRP variants (55 symmetric + 55 asymmetric) demonstrate SPACE achieves promising zero-shot generalization in both symmetric and asymmetric VRPs.

Conclusion: SPACE framework successfully unifies node representation and solution generation across symmetric and asymmetric VRPs through spatial pivot-aligned coordinate-free embedding and weight-decompled adaptive decoding, achieving promising zero-shot generalization across diverse vehicle routing problems.

Abstract: Generalist neural routing solvers have shown great potential in solving diverse vehicle routing problems (VRPs) with a unified model. However, existing solvers are typically limited to symmetric settings or degrade in performance when switching to asymmetric settings due to input inconsistencies or inherent structural differences, substantially limiting their practicality in real-world scenarios that encompass both scenarios. To address this limitation, we define the spatial position of each node based on the relative distances to a specific set of pivots and further propose a Spatial Pivot-Aligned Coordinate-free Embedding (SPACE) framework that unifies node representation and solution generation across symmetric and asymmetric VRPs. Specifically, we construct a bidirectional Frechet representation using a novel furthest pivot sampling strategy to enable invariant node representations across distinct problem settings. Furthermore, we introduce a weight-decomposed adaptive decoding mechanism that decouples geometric perception from problem representations, mitigating the overfitting of constraint decisions to a specific geometry setting. Extensive experiments on 110 VRP variants, comprising 55 symmetric problems and their asymmetric counterparts, demonstrate that SPACE achieves promising zero-shot generalization in both symmetric and asymmetric VRPs.

</details>


### [67] [AgentFugue: Agent Scaling for Long-Horizon Tasks through Collective Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24486)
*Yuyang Hu,Hongjin Qian,Shuting Wang,Jiongnan Liu,Tong Zhao,Xiaoxi Li,Zheng Liu,Zhicheng Dou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentFugue is a collective reasoning framework where multiple peer agents work on the same task simultaneously, sharing their intermediate reasoning through a centralized hub to improve performance without requiring explicit role specialization or workflow orchestration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While scaling individual agents (stronger models, better tools, scaffolding) has driven progress on long-horizon agentic tasks, little is understood about "scaling out" - whether multiple peer agents working on the same task can provide additional capability without relying on explicit role specialization or workflow orchestration.

Method: AgentFugue uses a shared reasoning hub that records concise notes on what each agent has established, attempted, or ruled out during parallel task exploration. The hub enables selective access to other agents' discoveries in a useful format for each agent's current search, creating an ecology of reusable intermediate reasoning without centralized planning. The hub is implemented as a plug-in communication layer trained with supervised fine-tuning and end-to-end reinforcement learning.

Result: Across challenging long-horizon settings, AgentFugue improves over strong baselines, demonstrating that collective reasoning can turn scaling out peer agent systems into a distinct source of capability gains rather than just spending more compute.

Conclusion: Collective reasoning through frameworks like AgentFugue enables scaling out (multiple peer agents) to become a distinct source of capability improvements for long-horizon agentic tasks, complementing traditional scaling up approaches and providing more than just computational efficiency gains.

Abstract: Recent progress on long-horizon agentic tasks has been driven largely by scaling up individual agents through stronger models, better tools, and more effective scaffolding. In contrast, much less is understood about scaling out: whether multiple peer agents, all targeting the same task, can become an additional source of capability without relying on explicit role specialization or workflow orchestration. We study this question and propose AgentFugue, a collective reasoning framework built around a shared reasoning hub. As peer agents explore the same task in parallel, the hub records concise notes on what each agent has established, attempted, or ruled out, and enables each agent to selectively access what other agents have discovered in a form useful for its current search. This design turns otherwise isolated trajectories into a connected ecology of reusable intermediate reasoning without requiring centralized planning. We instantiate the hub as a plug-in communication layer, trained with supervised fine-tuning and end-to-end reinforcement learning. Across the challenging long-horizon settings we study, AgentFugue improves over strong baselines. Our results suggest that collective reasoning can turn scaling out peer agent systems into a distinct source of capability gains, rather than merely a way of spending more compute.

</details>


### [68] [TIGER: Text-Informed Generalized Enzyme-Reaction Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24489)
*Yuhang Zhang,Keyan Ding,Peilin Chen,Han Liu,Can Lin,Ruixi Chen,Shiqi Wang,Qi Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TIGER is a Text-Informed Generalized Enzyme-Reaction Retrieval framework that uses protein-to-text generation models and dynamic feature fusion to significantly improve bidirectional enzyme-reaction mapping across diverse distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing enzyme-reaction retrieval approaches suffer from poor generalization across tasks and distributions, with performance sensitivity to dataset splits and substantial asymmetry between retrieval directions. These limitations hinder enzyme characterization, reaction mechanism elucidation, and rational design of metabolic pathways and biocatalysts.

Method: The TIGER framework leverages protein-to-text generation models to distill textual semantic knowledge from enzyme sequences. It uses a Dynamic Gating Network to adaptively fuse text-derived knowledge with sequence features for consistent enzyme representations, and a Structure-Shared Feature Projector to align enzyme and reaction representations in a unified latent space.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that TIGER significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across diverse distributions, exhibiting strong robustness and transferability across tasks in bidirectional enzyme-reaction retrieval.

Conclusion: TIGER provides an effective solution for generalized enzyme-reaction retrieval by leveraging textual semantic knowledge and adaptive feature fusion, addressing key limitations of existing approaches and enabling more robust bidirectional mapping between enzymes and biochemical reactions.

Abstract: Enzyme-reaction retrieval is a fundamental problem in computational biology, underpinning enzyme characterization, reaction mechanism elucidation, and the rational design of metabolic pathways and biocatalysts. As a bidirectional task, it entails both enzyme-to-reaction and reaction-to-enzyme mapping. However, existing approaches suffer from poor generalization across tasks and distributions, with performance highly sensitive to dataset splits and substantial asymmetry between retrieval directions. To address these challenges, we present TIGER, a Text-Informed Generalized Enzyme-Reaction Retrieval framework that leverages protein-to-text generation models to distill textual semantic knowledge from enzyme sequences, providing a generalized representation that bridges enzymes and biochemical reactions. To ensure the quality and reliability of textual semantics, we design a Dynamic Gating Network that adaptively fuses text-derived knowledge with sequence features, enabling more consistent and informative enzyme representations, while a Structure-Shared Feature Projector aligns enzyme and reaction representations within a unified latent space. Extensive experiments demonstrate that, under bidirectional retrieval supervision, TIGER significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across diverse distributions and exhibits strong robustness and transferability across tasks.

</details>


### [69] [Market Regime Council for Dynamic Credit Assignment in Multi-Agent LLM Decision Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24490)
*Yunhua Pei,Zerui Ge,Jin Zheng,John Cartlidge*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MRC is a multi-agent system using Shapley values for credit assignment in portfolio management, achieving superior risk-adjusted returns through cooperative agent integration and regime-aware weighting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multi-agent LLM decision systems for portfolio management lack principled credit assignment across specialist agents, are vulnerable to cold-start issues during regime shifts, and offer limited transparency in allocation decisions.

Method: Market Regime Council (MRC) computes exact Shapley credits across single, pairwise, and Grand-coalition outputs for online agent weighting. With N=3 specialist agents, it recomputes Shapley weights from performance histories, uses Bayesian adaptive mixture for early stability, applies regime-dependent multipliers, and records rebalances through five-layer causal trace.

Result: Over 1,037 trading days across 13 crypto assets and five seeds, MRC achieves Sharpe ratio of 1.51 and cumulative return of 440.1%, ranking first on CR, SR, and IR among active baselines, with lowest MDD among active methods.

Conclusion: MRC demonstrates that gains come from Shapley-weighted integration across coalition outputs rather than any single stage, providing a principled, transparent framework for multi-agent portfolio management with superior risk-adjusted performance.

Abstract: Multi-agent LLM decision systems for portfolio management still lack a principled way to assign credit across specialist agents, remain vulnerable to cold-start dominance under regime shifts, and offer limited transparency into how final allocations are formed. We propose Market Regime Council (MRC), a cooperative multi-agent decision system that computes exact Shapley credits across all single, pairwise, and Grand-coalition outputs for online agent weighting. Instantiated with N=3 specialist agents, at each trading period, MRC recomputes coalition-based Shapley weights from exponentially weighted performance histories, uses a Bayesian adaptive mixture to stabilize early periods, applies regime-dependent multipliers to adjust agent authority, and records each rebalance through a five-layer causal trace. Over 1,037 trading days across 13 crypto assets and five seeds, MRC achieves a Sharpe ratio of 1.51 and a cumulative return of 440.1%, ranking first on CR, SR, and IR among active baselines and attaining the lowest MDD among active methods. Ablation results show that the gains come from Shapley-weighted integration across coalition outputs rather than from any single stage in isolation. Code and demo data are included in the supplementary material.

</details>


### [70] [Reasoning as an Attack Surface: Adaptive Evolutionary CoT Jailbreaks for LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24497)
*Jianan Li,Simeng Qin,Xiaojun Jia,Lionel Z. Wang,Tianhang Zheng,Xiaoshuang Jia,Yang Liu,Xiaochun Cao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AE-CoT is an adaptive evolutionary framework for jailbreaking Large Reasoning Models by dynamically evolving chain-of-thought prompts to bypass safety mechanisms, outperforming existing static methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large Reasoning Models with chain-of-thought mechanisms are vulnerable to jailbreak attacks, but existing static CoT templates have limited diversity, adaptability, and effectiveness, requiring a more flexible approach.

Method: 1) Rewrites harmful goals into mild prompts using teacher role-play and decomposes them into reasoning fragments; 2) Performs multi-generation evolutionary search with fragment-level crossover and adaptive mutation-rate control; 3) Uses scoring model for harmfulness evaluation and enhances high-scoring candidates with harmful CoT templates.

Result: Extensive experiments across multiple models and datasets show AE-CoT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art jailbreak methods, demonstrating superior effectiveness in bypassing model safety mechanisms.

Conclusion: AE-CoT provides an adaptive evolutionary approach to CoT jailbreaking that overcomes limitations of static templates, highlighting new security vulnerabilities in Large Reasoning Models that need to be addressed.

Abstract: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in reasoning and generation tasks and are increasingly deployed in real-world applications. However, their explicit chain-of-thought (CoT) mechanism introduces new security risks, making them particularly vulnerable to jailbreak attacks. Existing approaches often rely on static CoT templates to elicit harmful outputs, but such fixed designs suffer from limited diversity, adaptability, and effectiveness. To overcome these limitations, we propose an adaptive evolutionary CoT jailbreak framework, called AE-CoT. Specifically, the method first rewrites harmful goals into mild prompts with teacher role-play and decomposes them into semantically coherent reasoning fragments to construct a pool of CoT jailbreak candidates. Then, within a structured representation space, we perform multi-generation evolutionary search, where candidate diversity is expanded through fragment-level crossover and a mutation strategy with an adaptive mutation-rate control mechanism. An independent scoring model provides graded harmfulness evaluations, and high-scoring candidates are further enhanced with a harmful CoT template to induce more destructive generations. Extensive experiments across multiple models and datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed AE-CoT, consistently outperforming state-of-the-art jailbreak methods.

</details>


### [71] [Hypothesis Generation and Inductive Inference in Children and Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24528)
*Jeffrey Qin,Wasu Top Piriyakulki,Zhuangfei Gao,Mia Radovanovic,Jessica Sommerville,Kevin Ellis,Marta Kryven*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Children and LLM-based agents show similar adaptation patterns to environmental uncertainty in inference tasks, but differ in information-seeking behavior and inductive biases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the computational principles of human inference under uncertainty (evidence, causal rules, world state) and compare them to LLM-based agents under similar constraints.

Method: Used an inductive inference Box Task where human children and LLM-based agents infer latent causes through sequential interaction with uncertain environments. Formalized the task in two complementary ways: (1) as constraint satisfaction over hypotheses, and (2) as program synthesis where hypotheses are executable programs evaluated against evidence. Used LLM-based agents as "model organisms" for systematic manipulation of task conditions.

Result: Children's behavior is best explained by subjective evidence reliability and online hypothesis generation. LLM-based agents replicated children's responses to evidence reliability/observability changes (discounting unreliable evidence, seeking to resolve partial information, dissociating task completion from causal generalization). However, LLMs tend to over-observe and over-comply with instructions compared to children.

Conclusion: While children and LLM-based agents adapt similarly to environmental structure, their information-seeking behavior exhibits distinct underlying costs and inductive biases, suggesting differences in their computational approaches despite surface-level similarities.

Abstract: Real world decision-making requires constructing mental models under uncertainty over evidence, over the underlying causal rules, and over the state of the world itself. Which computational principles underpin human inference under such conditions, and do LLM-based agents exhibit similar behavior given matching constraints? We address these questions using an inductive inference Box Task in which participants, human children and LLM-based agents, infer a latent cause through sequential interaction with an uncertain environment. We formalize this task as program induction with Bayesian particle-based inference, admitting two complementary interpretations: (1) as a constraint satisfaction process over hypotheses, and (2) as a program synthesis problem in which hypotheses are executable programs evaluated against evidence. Using the constraint-based formulation, we show that children's behavior is best explained by a combination of subjective evidence reliability and online hypothesis generation, accounting for both their evidence-seeking patterns and their dissociation between task completion and rule generalization. Using the program synthesis formulation, we treat LLM-based agents as model organisms: controllable systems that allow systematic manipulation of task conditions. Across backends, LLM-based agents replicate children's responses to changes in evidence reliability and observability, including discounting unreliable evidence, seeking to resolve partial information, and dissociating between task completion and causal generalization. At the same time, LLM-based agents tend to over-observe and over-comply with instructions relative to children. These results suggest that while children and LLM-based agents adapt similarly to environmental structure, their information-seeking behavior exhibits distinct underlying costs and inductive biases.

</details>


### [72] [DemoEvolve: Overcoming Sparse Feedback in Agentic Harness Evolution with Demonstrations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24539)
*Lirong Che,Yuzhe yang,Peiwen lin,Chuang wang,Xueqian wang,Jian su*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DemoEvolve is a demonstration-bootstrapped approach that improves harness evolution for frozen language-model agents by using human expert trajectories to guide harness editing in long-horizon stochastic environments where self-practice fails.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current harness evolution methods using self-generated rollouts fail in long-horizon stochastic environments due to sparse rewards, high outcome variance, and difficulty attributing failures to specific harness mechanisms. There's a need for more stable and diagnosable approaches to harness evolution.

Method: DemoEvolve uses human demonstration trajectories as expert reference experience to guide harness-level diagnosis and editing. Instead of relying solely on sparse reward signals or self-generated rollouts, it leverages competent human demonstrations to bootstrap the harness evolution process.

Result: Experiments show that while self-rollout evolution works in simple environments (Liar's Dice), it fails in harder long-horizon stochastic settings (Balatro). DemoEvolve outperforms both self-rollout evolution and tutorial-based approaches, producing more effective, auditable harness edits and achieving better performance under limited budgets.

Conclusion: Demonstration-bootstrapped harness evolution (DemoEvolve) makes sparse-feedback harness evolution more diagnosable, localizable, and stable by leveraging human expert trajectories to guide the editing process, enabling sample-efficient adaptation without modifying base model weights.

Abstract: Agent harness evolution improves frozen language-model agents by modifying the executable structures around them. We study this paradigm as a form of sample-efficient fast adaptation: instead of updating model weights, an agent can acquire task-specific competence by changing its external harness, while leaving the base model's general capabilities intact. Prior work shows that self-generated rollouts can support harness search, suggesting that agents may acquire new task competence through practice. Yet in long-horizon stochastic environments, self-practice becomes fragile: rewards are sparse, outcomes are high-variance, and failures are hard to attribute to concrete harness mechanisms. We introduce DemoEvolve, a demonstration-bootstrapped approach to harness evolution. When reward-only search is too broad and noisy, competent human trajectories serve as expert reference experience for the coding proposer, guiding harness-level diagnosis and editing. Experiments on Liar's Dice show that self-rollout evolution can work when episodes are short and failures are attributable. In contrast, Balatro exposes a harder long-horizon stochastic regime, where self-rollout evolution is misled by sparse feedback and candidate-selection noise, while tutorial-like textual knowledge alone does not yield stable improvement. Under the same limited budget, DemoEvolve produces more effective and auditable harness edits and achieves better performance. Overall, demonstrations make sparse-feedback harness evolution more diagnosable, localizable, and stable.

</details>


### [73] [Emission-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Sustainable Electric Vehicle Charging and Carbon Dioxide Reduction Under Varying Renewable Penetration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24543)
*Ninglin Ou,Mohammad A. Razzaque,Iftekher Islam Shovon,Shafkat Khan Siam,Shafiuzzaman K Khadem,Krishnendu Guha,Mayeen U Khandaker,Md. Noor-A-Rahim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes an emission-aware reinforcement learning strategy (SAC algorithm) for optimizing EV charging to reduce carbon emissions while maintaining grid stability, outperforming existing methods like MPC and heuristics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rapid EV growth challenges power grids with peak loads and instability from uncoordinated charging. Existing MPC and RL methods rarely prioritize real-time carbon intensity or renewable energy availability, leaving decarbonization potential unrealized.

Method: Uses emission-aware RL based on Soft Actor-Critic algorithm with multi-objective reward penalizing carbon emissions, curtailed renewables, and unmet user demand. Trained on EV2Gym platform with realistic data including solar/wind profiles, time-varying carbon intensity, and workplace EV behavior across 25 EVSE units.

Result: RL agent achieves carbon intensity as low as 23.96 gCO₂/kWh under 50% wind penetration (87% reduction vs baseline), outperforms PDN benchmark. Transformer overload <7 kWh vs 1093 kWh for AFAP heuristic. Renewable self-consumption reaches 52% under combined wind/solar.

Conclusion: Embedding carbon intensity forecasts into RL state/reward effectively aligns charging with low-emission periods while preserving grid compliance and user satisfaction, demonstrating superior decarbonization potential compared to existing control strategies.

Abstract: The rapid growth of Electric Vehicle (EV) adoption challenges power distribution networks through peak load spikes, voltage instability, and transformer overloads from uncoordinated charging. While Model Predictive Control (MPC) and standard Reinforcement Learning (RL) methods have addressed these issues, existing approaches rarely treat real-time carbon intensity or fluctuating renewable energy (RE) availability as primary scheduling objectives, leaving substantial decarbonisation potential unrealised. This paper proposes an emission-aware RL strategy based on the Soft Actor Critic (SAC) algorithm, with a multi-objective reward that penalises carbon emissions, curtailed on-site renewables, and unmet user demand. The agent is trained within a unified benchmarking framework on the EV2Gym platform, incorporating behind-the-meter solar and wind profiles, time-varying EirGrid carbon intensity data, and realistic workplace EV behaviour across 25 Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment (EVSE) units. Nine control strategies, including heuristics, emission-aware MPC variants, and the proposed RL agent, are compared under five renewable penetration scenarios (0%-50%) over ten independent runs each. The RL agent achieves a carbon intensity as low as 23.96 grams of carbon dioxide per kilowatt-hour under 50% wind penetration, representing up to 87% emission reduction versus the uncontrolled baseline, and outperforms the external graph-based Power Distribution Network (PDN) benchmark. Transformer overload remains below 7 kWh across scenarios, against up to 1093 kWh for the As Fast As Possible (AFAP) heuristic, and renewable self-consumption reaches 52% under combined wind and solar supply. Embedding carbon intensity forecasts into the RL state and reward aligns charging with low-emission periods while preserving grid compliance and user satisfaction.

</details>


### [74] [Beyond Control-Flow: Integrating the Resource Perspective into Multi-Collaborative Process Modeling from Text](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24546)
*Anton Antonov,Humam Kourani,Alessandro Berti,Gyunam Park*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A resource-aware pipeline that generates BPMN 2.0 collaboration diagrams from natural language descriptions using LLMs, addressing the limitation of current approaches that ignore collaborative aspects and focusing on both control flow and organizational structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current text-to-model approaches using LLMs for process modeling focus only on control-flow perspective (activity ordering) without considering collaborative aspects of business processes, which is crucial for comprehensive business process representation.
