Method: Introduced a resource-aware generation pipeline that: 1) Uses a compact executable intermediate language with mandatory resource details (pools for organizations, lanes for roles), 2) Implements cross-organization dependencies using standard message events, 3) Includes orthogonal layout routine for automatic spatial arrangement of elements within pools and lanes.

Result: Experiments on ten business processes with nine LLMs showed strong resource discovery capabilities while preserving control-flow quality, with only marginal runtime overhead added by the resource-aware approach.

Conclusion: The approach advances generative modeling toward more comprehensive, multi-collaborative representations of business operations by addressing both control flow and organizational structure in BPMN diagrams.

Abstract: Process modeling is a sub-domain of Business Process Management (BPM) focused on the translation of process artifacts into formal models. This task traditionally requires extensive human input and domain expertise in both BPM notations and the specific business context. While Large Language Models (LLMs) can now automate much of this manual work, current text-to-model approaches focus predominantly on the control-flow perspective-ordering activities without considering the collaborative aspect of the processes. In this paper, we introduce a resource-aware generation pipeline that produces formal BPMN 2.0 collaboration diagrams from natural-language descriptions. Rather than solely prompting an LLM for raw XML, we describe a compact, executable intermediate language with mandatory resource details defining both the organization (pool) and the role (lane). Cross-organization dependencies are materialized using the standard formal notation for such interactions-message events-while an orthogonal layout routine automatically handles the spatial arrangement of elements within pools and lanes. Experiments on ten business processes with nine LLMs show strong resource discovery while preserving control-flow quality and adding only marginal runtime overhead. This approach moves generative modeling toward a more comprehensive, multi-collaborative representation of business operations.

</details>


### [75] [PALoRA: Projection-Adaptive LoRA for Preserving Reasoning in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24549)
*Mustafa Hayri Bilgin,Mariam Barry,Albert Bifet,Azzedine Idir Ait Said,Soumya Banerjee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PALoRA is a two-stage framework that preserves reasoning skills while updating LLMs with new factual knowledge by identifying skill-critical components via singular value analysis and constraining knowledge injection to avoid interference with those components.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs face a plasticity-stability dilemma when updating with new knowledge - parameter-efficient adaptation often erodes previously acquired reasoning abilities. There's a need for methods that can incorporate new factual knowledge while preserving skill-critical representations.

Method: Two-stage approach: 1) Train a Singular Value Fine-Tuning (SVF) expert on reasoning datasets and use its learned singular scaling vector to identify skill-critical components in weight matrices. 2) Perform factual knowledge injection using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) with structural orthogonality constraints to avoid interfering with identified skill-relevant subspaces.

Result: Across Llama 3.1 8B and Mistral 7B models, PALoRA preserves 95% of reasoning performance (on mathematical, coding, scientific benchmarks) while maintaining competitive factual recall. It improves skill retention over prior spectral PEFT methods with less than 0.006% parameter overhead.

Conclusion: By leveraging spectral analysis to identify distributed reasoning representations and constraining knowledge updates to orthogonal subspaces, PALoRA effectively addresses the plasticity-stability dilemma in LLM updating, enabling efficient knowledge injection without sacrificing reasoning abilities.

Abstract: Efficiently updating Large Language Models (LLMs) with new or evolving factual knowledge remains a central challenge, as even parameter-efficient adaptation can erode previously acquired reasoning abilities. This tension reflects a plasticity-stability dilemma: models must incorporate new knowledge while preserving skill-critical representations. In this work, we study this trade-off through the spectral structure of multilayer perceptron weight matrices. We show, both theoretically and empirically, that information essential for reasoning is not localized only in dominant singular directions, but is instead distributed across the singular spectrum. Motivated by this observation, we introduce PALoRA, a two-stage framework for knowledge injection with reduced interference. PALoRA first trains a Singular Value Fine-Tuning (SVF) expert on a reasoning dataset and uses its learned singular scaling vector as a frozen geometric probe to identify components that are critical for the target skill. It then performs factual knowledge injection with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) under a structural orthogonality constraint, ensuring that updates avoid the identified skill-relevant subspace. Across Llama 3.1 8B and Mistral 7B, and across mathematical, coding, and scientific reasoning benchmarks, PALoRA preserves on average 95% of the SVF expert's reasoning performance while maintaining competitive factual recall. It consistently improves skill retention over prior spectral Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods while adding less than 0.006% parameter overhead.

</details>


### [76] [Jailbreak to Protect: Buffering and Reinforcing via Temporary Jailbreaking for Safe Fine-Tuning in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24550)
*Seokil Ham,Jaehyuk Jang,Wonjun Lee,Changick Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Paper proposes Buffer-and-Reinforce fine-tuning framework using temporary jailbreaking analysis to defend against harmful updates while preserving benign task performance in LLM personalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-tuning-as-a-Service enables LLM personalization but can weaken safety alignment under harmful fine-tuning attacks. Existing defense methods that activate harmful-behavior modules lack clear understanding of their mechanisms.

Method: The framework consists of two components: 1) BufferLoRA - a removable adapter that induces temporary jailbreaking to reduce harmful updates during user fine-tuning; 2) ReinforceLoRA - trained to recover refusal behavior under jailbroken state and integrated with UserLoRA via QR decomposition-based merging to reinforce safety while preserving task performance.

Result: Extensive experiments show superior safety and utility with no additional safety data during user fine-tuning and minimal computational cost.

Conclusion: The proposed Buffer-and-Reinforce fine-tuning framework effectively maintains safety alignment while preserving user-task performance during LLM personalization, achieving superior safety and utility without additional safety data during user fine-tuning.

Abstract: Fine-tuning-as-a-Service (FaaS) enables personalization of large language models (LLMs), but it can weaken safety-alignment under harmful fine-tuning attacks. Recent work has shown that activating harmful-behavior modules during fine-tuning can prevent models from learning undesired behaviors, but its mechanism remains unclear. In this paper, we revisit temporary jailbreaking as a defense against harmful fine-tuning and provide a gradient-level analysis showing that it saturates safety-degrading gradients while preserving benign task-relevant gradients. Based on this insight, we propose a Buffer-and-Reinforce fine-tuning framework that buffers harmful updates during user fine-tuning and reinforces safety after adaptation. Specifically, BufferLoRA induces temporary jailbreaking as a removable adapter to reduce harmful updates during user fine-tuning. After adaptation, ReinforceLoRA, trained to recover refusal behavior under the temporarily jailbroken state, is integrated with UserLoRA via QR decomposition-based merging to reinforce safety while preserving user-task performance. Extensive experiments show that our framework achieves superior safety and utility with no additional safety data during user fine-tuning and minimal computational cost.

</details>


### [77] [Summoning the Oracle to Slay It: Mitigating Look-Ahead Bias in Financial Backtesting with Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24564)
*Weixian Waylon Li,Mengyu Wang,Tiejun Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FinCAD addresses parametric look-ahead bias in LLM backtesting by suppressing memory of historical outcomes during inference, improving the reliability of financial predictions while preserving general reasoning capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional backtesting of LLMs on historical financial data is unreliable because pre-trained models already "know" historical outcomes (parametric look-ahead bias). LLMs trained in 2024 have memorized 2018-2020 stock movements, making backtests misleading.

Method: FinCAD uses inference-time adaptation of Context-Aware Decoding without retraining. It combines: 1) adversarial bias-discovery pipeline that learns model-specific memory-activating prior prompts, 2) entity- and date-adaptive rules that scale CAD strength per-(entity, date) memorization, so penalties apply only on memorized in-sample dates and decay to zero out-of-sample.

Result: Across five 7-14B LLMs and five mega-cap equities: reduced in-sample backtest returns by up to -67.1% on memorized dates; out-of-sample 2025 returns within $8K and Sharpe within 0.10 of baseline; preserved general-purpose reasoning within 1.7 points. Raised in-sample/out-of-sample Spearman correlation from +0.779 to +0.846 on eleven-model leaderboard.

Conclusion: FinCAD effectively mitigates parametric look-ahead bias in financial LLM applications, enabling more reliable backtesting by suppressing historical outcome memorization while maintaining predictive performance out-of-sample and preserving general reasoning capabilities. The method improves model ranking reliability for genuine out-of-sample performance prediction.

Abstract: Backtesting large language models (LLMs) on historical financial data is unreliable because pre-training cuts off after the events happened. An LLM trained in 2024 already "knows" which way 2018-2020 stocks moved. We name this failure parametric look-ahead bias and propose FinCAD, an inference-time adaptation of Context-Aware Decoding that suppresses an LLM's memory of historical outcomes without retraining. FinCAD pairs an adversarial bias-discovery pipeline that learns a model-specific memory-activating prior prompt with an entity- and date-adaptive rule that scales the CAD strength to per-(entity, date) memorisation, so the penalty fires on memorised in-sample dates and decays to zero out-of-sample. Across five 7-14B LLMs and five mega-cap equities, FinCAD cuts in-sample backtest returns by up to -67.1% on memorised dates while leaving 2025 out-of-sample returns within $8K and Sharpe within 0.10 of baseline, and preserves general-purpose reasoning within 1.7 pts. On an eleven-model leaderboard, it raises the in-sample / out-of-sample Spearman correlation from +0.779 to +0.846, recovering rankings that genuinely predict out-of-sample performance.

</details>


### [78] [Associations between echocardiographic traits and AI-ECG predictions of heart failure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24576)
*Elias Stenhede,Eivind Bjørkan Orstad,Torbjørn Omland,Henrik Schirmer,Arian Ranjbar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI-ECG predictions of heart failure risk correlate strongly with echocardiographic measures of systolic dysfunction (especially global longitudinal strain), and also capture diastolic abnormalities in patients with preserved ejection fraction, with notable sex differences in correlation patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While AI-ECG can detect heart failure including cases not captured by LVEF, the underlying cardiac phenotypes driving these predictions remain unclear. The researchers sought to investigate whether AI-ECG-predicted HF risk aligns with established echocardiographic measures of myocardial dysfunction, remodeling, and filling pressures.

Method: Retrospective analysis of 8,147 patients with ECG and echocardiography performed within 3 days at Akershus University Hospital. Applied a previously validated AI-ECG model for HF detection to all ECGs, then used Spearman's rank correlation to quantify associations between echocardiographic parameters and AI-ECG risk. Conducted subgroup analyses by sex and LVEF, with external validation using 36,286 ECG-echocardiography pairs from Columbia University.

Result: Global longitudinal strain (GLS) showed strongest correlation with AI-ECG risk (ρ=0.57), followed by mitral annular plane systolic excursion (MAPSE) (ρ=-0.49) and LVEF (ρ=-0.45). In patients with preserved LVEF (>50%), correlations remained substantial for GLS, MAPSE, and diastolic parameters. Sex differences emerged: volumetric left ventricular indices correlated less strongly in women, while diastolic indices showed stronger correlations in women than men.

Conclusion: AI-ECG HF risk predictions primarily align with measures of systolic function (especially global longitudinal strain), while also capturing diastolic abnormalities in patients with preserved LVEF. This physiological validation improves clinical interpretability and identifies opportunities for model refinement, with sex differences suggesting potential areas for model improvement.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence-enabled electrocardiography (AI-ECG) can detect heart failure (HF), including disease not captured by left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF), but the cardiac phenotypes underlying model predictions remain unclear. We therefore investigated whether AI-ECG-predicted HF risk aligns with established echocardiographic measures of myocardial dysfunction, remodelling, and filling pressures.
  We retrospectively analysed ECG and echocardiography data from 8147 patients who underwent both examinations within three days at Akershus University Hospital between 1 January 2023 and 1 June 2025. A previously validated AI-ECG model for HF detection was applied to all ECGs. Spearman's rank correlation $ρ$ quantified associations between echocardiographic parameters and AI-ECG risk. Subgroup analyses were performed by sex and left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF). External validation included 36,286 ECG-echocardiography pairs from Columbia University Irving Medical Center.
  Global longitudinal strain (GLS) showed the strongest correlation ($ρ$=0.57), followed by mitral annular plane systolic excursion (MAPSE) ($ρ$=-0.49) and LVEF ($ρ$=-0.45). In patients with LVEF>50%, correlations remained substantial for GLS, MAPSE, and diastolic-related parameters. Volumetric left ventricular indices correlated less strongly in women, whereas diastolic indices showed stronger correlations in women than in men.
  Physiological validation showed that AI-ECG HF risk predictions align primarily with measures of systolic function, particularly global longitudinal strain, while also capturing diastolic-related abnormalities in patients with preserved LVEF. This approach may improve clinical interpretability and identify opportunities for model refinement.

</details>


### [79] [HeartBeatAI: An Interpretable and Robust Deep Learning Framework for Multi-Label ECG Arrhythmia Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24588)
*Shubham Gupta,Nikhil Panwar,Partha Pratim Roy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HeartBeatAI is a DL framework for 12-lead ECG classification combining domain generalization, multi-scale feature aggregation, and clinical explainability to address class imbalance and generalization gaps in clinical deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical deployment of DL for ECG analysis is hindered by class imbalance and generalization gap problems, despite DL's enhancement of automated ECG analysis.

Method: Framework integrates SE ResNet to isolate diagnostic leads with Multi-Layer Concentration Pipeline for macro-rhythm and micro-morphological anomalies. Uses MixStyle regularization and Label Smoothing for domain shift mitigation. Benchmarked across four datasets using intra-source and Leave-One-Domain-Out protocols.

Result: Achieves 98% Macro F1-score under intra-source conditions, but LODO evaluations show significant degradation in detecting rare anomalies, revealing persistent challenges in cross-institutional deployment.

Conclusion: HeartBeatAI demonstrates high performance in controlled conditions but highlights the ongoing challenge of domain generalization, particularly for rare anomalies, in cross-institutional clinical ECG analysis deployment.

Abstract: While Deep Learning (DL) enhances automated electrocardiogram (ECG) analysis, clinical deployment is hindered by class imbalance and the generalization gap. This paper presents HeartBeatAI, a deep learning framework combining domain generalization, multi-scale feature aggregation, and clinical explainability for robust 12-lead ECG classification. Moving beyond image-based paradigms, HeartBeatAI integrates a Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) ResNet to isolate diagnostic leads alongside a Multi-Layer Concentration Pipeline to capture macro-rhythm and micro-morphological anomalies. To mitigate domain shift, the framework employs MixStyle regularization and Label Smoothing. Rigorous benchmarking across four large-scale datasets using intra-source and Leave-One-Domain-Out (LODO) protocols demonstrates high performance (98% Macro F1-score) under intra-source conditions. However, LODO evaluations reveal significant degradation in detecting rare anomalies, highlighting a persistent challenge in cross-institutional deployment.

</details>


### [80] [Learning to Reason Efficiently with A* Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24597)
*Andreas Opedal,Francesco Ignazio Re,Abulhair Saparov,Mrinmaya Sachan,Bernhard Schölkopf,Ryan Cotterell*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs trained with A* search guidance can learn to generate correct and efficient natural language proofs, significantly improving reasoning accuracy even in small models (1B-3B parameters).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models often produce incorrect or redundant inference steps in deductive reasoning tasks, but classical search algorithms like A* guarantee optimal efficiency. The authors investigate whether LLMs can learn to generate correct proofs with A* guidance.

Method: Frame natural language inference as a search problem where the answer is a valid proof. Use two training techniques: 1) Supervised fine-tuning on A* execution traces, and 2) Reinforcement learning with A*-informed process reward models. Test on Llama-3.2 models in 1B-3B parameter range.

Result: Small models (1B-3B) go from near-zero accuracy to outperforming much larger models like DeepSeek-V3.2. Models balance accuracy and efficiency with A*-informed signals. In larger search spaces, models trained with imperfect heuristics show superior accuracy.

Conclusion: A* post-training significantly improves LLM reasoning capabilities. There's a trade-off between correctness rewards (maximizing accuracy) and A*-informed signals (balancing accuracy-efficiency). Classical search algorithm guidance is a promising direction for enhancing LLM reasoning.

Abstract: Many applications of large language models (LLMs) require deductive reasoning, yet models frequently produce incorrect or redundant inference steps. We frame natural language inference as a search problem where the final answer is the valid proof itself, requiring a reasoning procedure in which intermediate inferences are correct. Specifically, we investigate whether LLMs can learn to generate correct and efficient proofs with guidance from A* search -- an algorithm that guarantees an optimally efficient path to a goal. We explore two training techniques: supervised fine-tuning on execution traces from A* and reinforcement learning with A*-informed process reward models. Empirically, we find that Llama-3.2 models in the 1B--3B range benefit substantially from A* post training, going from near-zero accuracy to outperforming DeepSeek-V3.2 -- a much larger model. Our analysis uncovers a trade-off: while simple correctness rewards maximize accuracy, A*-informed signals strike a balance between accuracy and efficiency. Furthermore, we find that on larger search spaces, models trained with imperfect heuristics exhibit superior accuracy. Our results demonstrate a promising direction towards reasoning guided by principles derived from classical search algorithms.

</details>


### [81] [Agent-as-Peer-Debriefer: A Multi-Agent Framework with Perspective-Based Refinement for Qualitative Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24600)
*Zhimin Lin,Kun Cheng,Fan Bai,Jie Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI peer debriefing framework improves LLM coding for qualitative analysis by simulating human peer feedback with three analytical perspectives, reducing gap with human analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly used for qualitative data analysis but lack the depth and nuance of human analysis due to missing credibility practices like peer debriefing, where analysts seek feedback from disinterested peers to refine their coding.

Method: Proposed Agent-as-Peer-Debriefer framework with Hierarchical Coding Agent and three Peer-Debriefing Agents (Theory-Driven, Data-Driven, Applied) that refine codes through peer debriefing practice. Evaluation on three datasets across two domains with three LLMs measuring semantic similarity to human codes.

Result: Perspective-based peer-debriefing refinement aligns more closely with human codes than single-LLM baseline across all settings (datasets, domains, LLMs). The three perspectives produce distinct trade-offs, showing perspective choice is meaningful and controllable. Ablation confirms gains aren't just from additional refinement.

Conclusion: The Agent-as-Peer-Debriefer framework demonstrates that simulating peer debriefing with explicit analytical perspectives is a promising approach to make LLM-assisted qualitative data analysis more credible and nuanced.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for qualitative data analysis (QDA), yet their outputs often miss the depth and nuance of human analysis. We argue this gap reflects a missing credibility practice from human QDA: peer debriefing, in which an analyst seeks feedback from a disinterested peer and uses it to refine their coding. To bring this practice into LLM-assisted QDA, we propose Agent-as-Peer-Debriefer, a multi-agent QDA framework that builds peer debriefing into key coding steps. In our framework, a Hierarchical Coding Agent follows the standard QDA process to generate codes, sub-themes, and themes, along with self-explanations and reflection memos. It then shares these outputs with three Peer-Debriefing Agents, each applying a distinct analytical perspective (Theory-Driven, Data-Driven, or Applied) and refining the codes by keeping, renaming, reassigning, merging, or splitting them. These perspectives are drawn from established human QDA practices that generalize across domains and datasets. To evaluate the framework, we test it on three datasets across two domains with three LLMs, measuring semantic similarity to human-annotated codes. Across all settings, perspective-based, peer-debriefing refinement aligns more closely with human codes than a single-LLM baseline, and an ablation further shows the gain is not merely from additional refinement. The three perspectives also produce distinct trade-offs, showing that the choice of perspective is a meaningful and controllable design decision. More broadly, these findings suggest that simulating peer debriefing with explicit perspectives is a promising route to more credible LLM-assisted QDA.

</details>


### [82] [Lattice theory and algebraic models for deep convolutional learning based on mathematical morphology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24608)
*Gustavo,Angulo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper develops an algebraic framework for deep convolutional networks using lattice theory and mathematical morphology, showing how standard CNN operations correspond to morphological operators across different lattices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a rigorous mathematical foundation for understanding deep convolutional architectures (CNNs, ResNets, UNet) using lattice theory and morphological algebra, revealing the underlying algebraic structures of standard operations.

Method: Apply the Matheron-Maragos-Banon-Barrera (MMBB) universal representation theory for translation-invariant operators to analyze each layer of deep networks, modeling operations as cross-lattice morphological operators across different algebraic structures.

Result: Shows that standard CNN pipeline (convolution+ReLU+max-pooling) is a cross-lattice operator where convolution is an erosion in Fourier lattice, ReLU is lattice-join closing, and max-pooling is a dilation in max-plus lattice. Identifies three genuinely idempotent opening layer designs and provides fixed-point convergence theory.

Conclusion: The algebraic framework explains why depth introduces representational power in CNNs (non-idempotence of composed layers), unifies various network operations under morphological theory, and provides foundation for designing networks with specific algebraic properties.

Abstract: We develop a rigorous algebraic framework for deep convolutional architectures, CNNs, ResNets, and encoder--decoder networks such as UNet, grounded in lattice theory and mathematical morphology. The central tool is the Matheron--Maragos--Banon--Barrera (MMBB) universal representation theory for translation-invariant operators, which we apply systematically to every layer of a standard deep network.
  The principal finding is that the standard CNN pipeline (linear convolution~$+$ ReLU~$+$ flat max-pooling) is a cross-lattice operator: the convolution is an erosion in the Fourier inf-semilattice while ReLU is a lattice-join closing and max-pooling is a dilation in the pointwise max-plus lattice, and their composition is a morphological opening in neither. A second finding is that the upper adjoint of ReLU in the pointwise lattice is a global (non-local) operator, the identity on globally non-negative functions and $-\infty$ otherwise, so no local morphological erosion can form an adjunction pair with ReLU. These two results together provide the precise algebraic reason why depth in standard CNNs introduces genuine representational power: the composed layer is not idempotent.
  Three layer designs that are genuine idempotent openings are identified and fully characterised: the pure max-plus morphological layer (pointwise lattice), the spectral Wiener layer (Fourier lattice), and the self-dual morphological layer. We establish a complete fixed-point and convergence theory.
  The framework also unifies max-pooling, strided convolution, and the Laplacian pyramid under the Goutsias--Heijmans adjoint pyramid theory, and gives the Activation--Pooling Dilation (APD) factorisation with its correct adjoint.

</details>


### [83] [GlobalDentBench: A Multinational Benchmark for Evaluating LLM Clinical Reasoning in Dentistry with Expert Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24636)
*Junjie Zhao,Jingyi Liang,Zhenyang Cai,Jiaming Zhang,Zhenwei Wen,Shuzhi Deng,Wenjing Yi,Chunfeng Luo,Hexian Zhang,Junying Chen,Tianrui Liu,Zhuhui Bai,Zixu Zhang,Pradeep Singh,Xiang Liu,Jianquan Li,Nhan L Tran,Falk Schwendicke,Zuolin Jin,Lijian Jin,Liangyi Chen,Wei-fa Yang,Benyou Wang,Junwen Wang,Shan Jiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GlobalDentBench is a multinational dental benchmark that reveals critical limitations in LLMs' medical reasoning and safety, showing performance degradation with increased complexity and high unsafe rates in clinical recommendations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While LLMs have transformative potential for medicine, their reasoning robustness and safety in real-world clinical scenarios, particularly in dentistry, remain critically underexplored. There is a need for rigorous validation before safe deployment in healthcare.

Method: Created GlobalDentBench - a multinational dental benchmark covering 14 dental specialties across 88 countries/regions with 8,978 expert-validated questions in three formats (multiple-choice, short-answer, case-based). Uses a framework calibrated by six senior dentists with high expert agreement rates. Evaluated 12 frontier LLMs across three progressive reasoning levels (knowledge recall, routine reasoning, individualized reasoning).

Result: LLMs show sharp stepwise performance degradation with increasing reasoning complexity: accuracy drops from 81.34% on multiple-choice to 22.34% on case-based questions, and declines from 74.01% at L1 to 35.71% at L3. Alarmingly, LLM-generated clinical recommendations have an overall unsafe rate of 31.01%, with 4.51% posing risks of irreversible patient harm, particularly in orthodontics.

Conclusion: Current LLMs have fundamental limitations in medical reasoning and safety. GlobalDentBench provides a scalable foundation for trustworthy clinical AI evaluation, underscoring the urgent need for rigorous validation before safe deployment in healthcare settings.

Abstract: While large language models (LLMs) hold transformative potential for medicine, their reasoning robustness and safety in real-world clinical scenarios remain critically underexplored, particularly in dentistry. Here we introduce GlobalDentBench, the first multinational dental benchmark, featuring a taxonomy that encompasses 14 dental specialties across 88 countries and regions spanning six continents. The benchmark comprises 8,978 expert-validated questions across three formats (multiple-choice, short-answer, and case-based questions) and assesses three progressive reasoning levels: knowledge recall (L1), routine reasoning (L2), and individualized reasoning (L3). To ensure data quality, the automated construction framework was calibrated by six senior dentists, achieving expert agreement rates of 99.98% for multiple-choice and short-answer questions and 96.78% for the more complex case-based questions. Evaluation of 12 frontier LLMs on GlobalDentBench revealed a sharp, stepwise performance degradation with increasing reasoning complexity. Specifically, accuracy plummeted from 81.34% on multiple-choice to 64.53% on short-answer and 22.34% on case-based questions, while declining markedly from 74.01% at L1 to 55.64% at L2 and 35.71% at L3. More critically, risk analysis of real-world dental cases demonstrated an alarming overall unsafe rate of 31.01% in LLM-generated clinical recommendations, with 4.51% posing risks of irreversible patient harm and risks particularly pronounced in specialties such as orthodontics. These findings expose fundamental limitations in the medical reasoning and safety of current LLMs. Consequently, GlobalDentBench provides a scalable foundation for trustworthy clinical AI evaluation, underscoring the urgent need for rigorous validation before the safe deployment of these models in healthcare.

</details>


### [84] [AVBench: Human-Aligned and Automated Evaluation Benchmark for Audio-Video Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24652)
*Jialiang Yang,Bin Xia,Ruihang Chu,Dingdong Wang,Wanke Xia,Zhun Mou,Tianyang Zhong,Yiting Zhao,Wenming Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AVBench is an automated benchmark for human-centric audio-video generation evaluation with specialized metrics and preference-learned evaluators for more accurate assessment than existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AV generation evaluation is at an early stage with limited benchmarks only for human-related scenarios, relying on coarse-grained metrics and generic multimodal LLMs that lead to inaccurate assessments of model capabilities.

Method: Two key designs: (1) Human-centric fine-grained metrics covering 10 evaluation dimensions for visual quality, audio quality, and multi-level cross-modal consistency; (2) Specialized evaluators trained via preference learning on large-scale supervision data created by transforming real-world videos with controlled perturbations, using probabilistic scoring from model prediction confidence.

Result: AVBench enables automated evaluation for AV generation with more reliable assessment than traditional VQA-style evaluation, aligns closely with human judgment, demonstrates potential for data filtering, and serves as differentiable reward signal for RLHF.

Conclusion: AVBench addresses current limitations in AV generation evaluation by providing comprehensive, accurate, automated benchmarking through specialized human-centric metrics and preference-learned evaluators.

Abstract: Rapid advances in audio-video (AV) generation have enabled high-fidelity synthesis with synchronized sound, particularly for human-related scenarios involving speech and interactions. Yet evaluation for AV generation remains at an early stage, with only a few coarse-grained benchmarks for human-related scenarios and relying on limited preset evaluations with generic multimodal LLMs, leading to inaccurate assessments of model capabilities. To address these issues, we introduce AVBench, a fully automated benchmark tailored for human-centric AV generation. AVBench is built on two key designs for comprehensive and accurate evaluation: (i) Human-centric and fine-grained metrics. AVBench integrates ten evaluation dimensions designed for human-centered real-world scenarios, covering visual quality, audio quality, and multi-level consistency across modalities. These practical metrics capture human-related details that existing benchmarks often overlook. (ii) Specialized evaluators via preference learning. To address the lack of specialized training data, we construct large-scale supervision by transforming real-world videos into diverse training pairs with controlled perturbations. After fine-tuning on this high-quality dataset, the evaluators learn to reliably detect subtle cross-modal inconsistencies. Crucially, instead of producing discrete textual judgment, AVBench derives continuous evaluation scores from the model's prediction confidence on binary decisions. This probabilistic scoring mechanism enables a more reliable assessment than traditional VQA-style evaluation and aligns closely with human judgment. Taken together, AVBench offers automated evaluation for AV generation, demonstrates strong potential for data filtering, and serves as a differentiable reward signal for Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF).

</details>


### [85] [Beyond Inference-Only Deployment: Comparing Weight-Based Consolidation Against Cascading Compaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24657)
*Simon Dennis,Kevin Shabahang,Hao Guo,Rivaan Patil*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Nightly LoRA consolidation on consumer GPUs preserves 80% of user knowledge vs 37% for cascading context methods, enabling persistent personalization while revealing median validation cross-entropy as better performance signal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM deployments use inference-only configurations where models never update per-user weights, forcing users to repeatedly re-teach preferences, corrections, and project context. Context-based workarounds consume limited context-window space and degrade under cascading compaction.

Method: Nightly consolidation of interaction knowledge via reflection, synthesis, and Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) fine-tuning on a single consumer GPU, with evaluation across ten realistic software development conversations (1,146 test questions across three memory types) comparing consolidation against cascading compaction baselines.

Result: Consolidation retains 80.4 +/- 1.3% of knowledge compared to cascading compaction's 36.8 +/- 3.0% retention - a 43.6 percentage point gain (paired t(9) = 14.8, p < 0.001) that more than doubles what compaction preserves. Largest gains on procedural corrections (36.3% to 74.6%) and episodic project facts (31.5% to 78.2%).

Conclusion: The study concludes that persistent personalization of LLMs requires moving beyond inference-only deployment toward architectures designed to consolidate knowledge into model weights, rather than relying solely on context-based methods which degrade significantly under cascading compaction. Key methodological insight reveals that median per-token validation cross-entropy (rather than mean) is the faithful signal for evaluating performance under surface-form variation.

Abstract: Major LLM platforms deploy models in an inference-only configuration: the model serves requests but never updates per-user weights. Users must repeatedly re-teach preferences, corrections, and project context, and context-based workarounds consume context-window space and degrade under cascading compaction. We evaluate an alternative: nightly consolidation of interaction knowledge into model weights via reflection, synthesis, and Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) fine-tuning on a single consumer GPU. Across ten realistic software development conversations (n = 10, 1,146 test questions across three memory types), three cycles of cascading compaction retain 36.8 +/- 3.0% of knowledge (between an 11.8% no-context floor and a 90.1% full-context ceiling), while consolidation retains 80.4 +/- 1.3% -- a 43.6 pp gain (paired t(9) = 14.8, p < 0.001) that more than doubles what compaction preserves, with the largest gains on procedural corrections (36.3% -> 74.6%) and episodic project facts (31.5% -> 78.2%). As a methodological aside, mean per-token validation cross-entropy is negatively correlated with LLM-judged accuracy (r = -0.51) while median per-token validation cross-entropy tracks accuracy almost exactly (r = +0.99): under evaluators that tolerate surface-form variation, the mean is misleading and a heavy-tail-robust statistic is the faithful signal. Persistent personalization requires moving beyond inference-only deployment toward architectures that consolidate knowledge into weights.

</details>


### [86] [Measuring Reasoning Quality in LLMs: A Multi-Dimensional Behavioral Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24661)
*Ali Şenol,Garima Agrawal,Huan Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a multi-dimensional framework to evaluate LLM reasoning quality beyond just final-answer correctness, revealing behaviors that accuracy-only metrics miss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM evaluation primarily focuses on final-answer correctness, providing limited insight into the underlying reasoning processes that produce those answers. There's a need for a more comprehensive assessment of reasoning quality.

Method: Developed a unified multi-dimensional framework with six theoretically grounded dimensions: Correctness, Consistency, Robustness, Logical Coherence, Efficiency, and Stability. Conducted extensive experiments on seven LLMs across 975 items from four benchmarks.

Result: The framework reveals behaviors invisible to accuracy-only metrics: logical coherence is orthogonal to correctness (r = -0.172), correct answers can arise from incoherent reasoning, Claude-Haiku-4.5 achieves highest multi-dimensional score. Shows critical ranking inversions (e.g., DeepSeek-V3 ranks 2nd under accuracy-priority but 5th under legal/compliance weighting). 11/15 dimension pairs are independent, supporting psychometric validity.

Conclusion: The proposed multi-dimensional framework provides deeper insights into LLM reasoning quality, supports deployment decisions for accountability, prevents ranking errors, and ensures comprehensive evaluation beyond single-metric approaches.

Abstract: LLMs have achieved remarkable success in complex reasoning tasks, yet current evaluation approaches predominantly rely on final-answer correctness, offering limited insight into the underlying reasoning processes that produce those answers. To address this gap, this study proposes a unified multi-dimensional framework for measuring reasoning quality in LLMs from a behavioral perspective, operationalizing six theoretically grounded dimensions: Correctness (CQ), Consistency (CS), Robustness (RS), Logical Coherence (LS), Efficiency (ES), and Stability (SS). Extensive experiments on seven LLMs across 975 items from four benchmarks demonstrate that the framework reveals behaviors invisible to accuracy-only metrics. Notably, logical coherence is orthogonal to correctness (r = -0.172, ns), confirming that correct answers can arise from incoherent reasoning, while Claude-Haiku-4.5 achieves the highest multi-dimensional score (Q_bal = 0.778). Furthermore, the framework exposes critical ranking inversions: DeepSeek-V3 ranks second under accuracy-priority but fifth under legal/compliance weighting, a reversal that single-metric evaluation cannot detect. Discriminant validity confirms 11/15 dimension pairs are independent (|r| < 0.50), providing psychometric support for treating each dimension as a distinct signal. The dimensional profiles produced by the framework directly support three classes of deployment decision: identifying models whose reasoning traces would fail accountability audits despite correct final answers (LS--CQ orthogonality); preventing ranking errors caused by accuracy-only benchmarking; and ensuring that no single metric silently substitutes for the six independent signals the framework captures.

</details>


### [87] [When Mean CE Fails: Median CE Can Better Track Language Model Quality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24667)
*Hao Guo,Simon Dennis,Rivaan Patil,Kevin Shabahang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Mean cross-entropy (CE) fails to track model quality during training; median CE correlates better with task performance. Analysis shows training reshapes CE distributions, with bulk behavior more important for evaluation than tail behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The standard validation metric of mean cross-entropy (CE) can fail to track actual model quality during training, potentially leading to misleading conclusions about model performance and selection decisions.

Method: The authors conduct two experiments: (1) Qwen2.5-1.5B SFT on synthetic fact-learning where mean CE rises after initial learning while fact-recall accuracy remains high, and (2) top-K distillation on TinyStories where decreasing K improves median CE but worsens mean CE. They analyze how percentile CE metrics move during training and examine the reshaping of empirical per-token CE distributions.

Result: In both scenarios, median CE correlates much more closely with task performance than mean CE. Training reshapes CE distributions: in top-K distillation, smaller K yields distributions with more mass at both extremes (decreasing median, increasing mean); in Qwen SFT, bulk saturates quickly while tail extends later. Task-evaluation metrics appear more sensitive to bulk behavior than tail behavior.

Conclusion: Researchers should report multiple percentile CE summaries alongside mean CE and use concordance among them to track distribution reshaping. When mean and median CE disagree, this serves as a low-cost diagnostic for model selection issues.

Abstract: Mean cross-entropy is the standard validation metric for language models, but it can fail to track model quality during training. We examine this in two common scenarios. First, in Qwen2.5-1.5B SFT on synthetic fact-learning, we find that mean CE rises substantially after the initial learning phase while held-out fact-recall accuracy remains near its peak. Second, we find that in top-K distillation on TinyStories, decreasing K improves median CE while worsening mean CE; the Top-5 student attains the highest LLM-judge score and crosses below its teacher on median CE, despite having the worst mean CE. In both cases, median CE correlates much more closely with task performance than does mean CE.
  Analyzing how bulk and tail percentile CE move during training reveals that training reshapes the empirical per-token CE distribution. In top-K distillation, smaller K yields a distribution with more mass at both extremes, decreasing the median and increasing the mean. In Qwen SFT, the bulk saturates quickly while the tail extends in the latter half of training. In both, the task-evaluation metric appears more sensitive to the bulk than to the tail.
  Practically, we recommend reporting a small set of percentile CE summaries alongside the mean, and using concordance among them as a tool to keep track of distribution reshaping, as well as a low-cost diagnostic for when mean and median CE disagree on model selection.

</details>


### [88] [Exploration of Perceptual Speech Features for Clinical Decision-Support in Mental Health Care](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24678)
*Vassilis Lyberatos,Edmund G. Dervakos,Eleni Adamidi,Athanasios Voulodimos,Giorgos Stamou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A systematic framework using interpretable speech and language features (acoustic, linguistic) with explainable ML to analyze associations with depression, anxiety, and ADHD symptoms across controlled and clinical datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Speech and language technologies offer objective, interpretable cues for mental health assessment, addressing the need for transparent clinical tools that can analyze vocal and linguistic patterns associated with mental health conditions.

Method: Feature-based analysis framework leveraging perceptually grounded acoustic (prosody, vocal quality) and linguistic (semantic coherence, syntactic structure, sarcasm) characteristics. Uses statistical analysis and interpretable machine learning (XGBoost with SHAP and LIME) to examine associations with depression, anxiety, and ADHD symptoms. Evaluated on benchmark datasets (StressID, DAIC-WOZ, Androids, EATD) and real-world clinical data, with ablation studies to identify most informative features.

Result: Reveals stable and consistent relationships between symptom severity and vocal irregularities (shimmer, jitter), lexical-syntactic patterns, and affective tone across datasets. Identifies most informative feature groups through ablation studies.

Conclusion: The work presents a transparent and clinically interpretable approach to speech-based mental health analysis, demonstrating that interpretable feature frameworks can provide valuable insights for mental health assessment through objective vocal and linguistic cues.

Abstract: Speech and language technologies offer valuable opportunities for supporting mental health assessment through objective and interpretable cues. We present a systematic feature-based analysis framework leveraging perceptually grounded acoustic and linguistic characteristics, including prosody, vocal quality, semantic coherence, syntactic structure, and sarcasm. Using statistical analysis and interpretable machine learning (XGBoost with SHAP and LIME), we examine associations between speech features and validated symptom measures of depression, anxiety, and ADHD. Evaluated on both controlled benchmark datasets (StressID, DAIC-WOZ, Androids, EATD) and a real-world clinical dataset, the framework reveals stable and consistent relationships between symptom severity and vocal irregularities (e.g., shimmer, jitter), lexical-syntactic patterns, and affective tone. An ablation study conducted across all datasets further identifies the most informative feature groups. This work explores a transparent and clinically interpretable approach to speech-based mental health analysis.

</details>


### [89] [Emotional intelligence in large language models is fragmented across perception, cognition, and interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24686)
*Minghao Lv,Lu Chen,Enchang Zhang,Anji Zhou,Xiaoran Xue,Hanyi Zhang,Fenghua Tang,Zhuo Rachel Han,Mengyue Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FACET is a psychometrically grounded framework for evaluating LLM emotional intelligence, revealing that emotional skills don't scale uniformly with model size and that current RLHF may optimize for superficial empathy mimicry rather than genuine affective reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used in emotionally sensitive domains, current benchmarks often conflate superficial politeness with deep affective reasoning, failing to distinguish between perceptual accuracy and interactive efficacy in emotional intelligence.

Method: The researchers introduce FACET (Functional Affective Competence and Empathy Test), a psychometrically grounded framework with 480 expert-crafted items based on the Mayer-Salovey-Caruso four-branch ability model. They evaluated nine frontier models (including GPT-5, Claude-Sonnet-4) using this framework.

Result: Emotional intelligence is fragmented across cognitive and interactive dimensions. Frontier models show robust proficiency in objective emotion recognition but this doesn't consistently translate to interactive success. Three performance profiles emerged: cognitive-dominant, interactive-dominant, and context-dependent. Hidden emotion recognition is a universal bottleneck, and RLHF may optimize for "stochastic empathy" rather than integrated affective reasoning.

Conclusion: Emotional skills don't scale uniformly with general intelligence or model size; they're shaped by specific alignment paradigms. The findings challenge assumptions of linear emotional scaling and provide a roadmap for developing genuinely emotionally intelligent agents with clinical resonance.

Abstract: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into emotionally sensitive domains, the structural integrity of their emotional intelligence (EI) becomes a critical frontier for safety and alignment. Current benchmarks often conflate superficial politeness with deep affective reasoning, failing to distinguish between perceptual accuracy and interactive efficacy. Here, we introduce FACET (Functional Affective Competence and Empathy Test), a psychometrically grounded framework comprising 480 expert-crafted items. Unlike previous metrics, FACET is theoretically anchored in the Mayer-Salovey-Caruso four-branch ability model, operationalizing EI through perception, facilitation, understanding, and management of emotions. Through an evaluation of nine frontier models (including GPT-5, Claude-Sonnet-4), we demonstrate that emotional intelligence is not a monolithic capability but is fragmented across cognitive and interactive dimensions. While frontier models demonstrate robust proficiency in objective emotion recognition and social reasoning, this does not consistently translate to interactive success. We categorize these discrepancies into three distinct performance profiles: cognitive-dominant, interactive-dominant, and context-dependent. These typologies indicate that emotional skills do not scale uniformly with general intelligence or model size; rather, they are shaped by specific alignment paradigms. Notably, we identify hidden emotion recognition as a universal performance bottleneck across all architectures. Our results suggest that current RLHF processes may optimize for "stochastic empathy", a statistical mimicry of emotional syntax, at the expense of integrated affective reasoning. These findings challenge the assumption of linear emotional scaling and provide a rigorous roadmap for developing socially aware agents capable of genuine clinical resonance.

</details>


### [90] [MDIA: A Multi-Agent Diagnostic Intelligence Pipeline on HealthBench Professional](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24699)
*Roberto Cruz,David Rey-Blanco*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MDIA, a multi-agent diagnostic intelligence system using specialty-routed clinical reasoning architecture, achieves significant performance improvements on HealthBench Professional benchmark without model fine-tuning, showing that architectural design is as important as the underlying LLM for clinical agent performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To demonstrate that significant performance gains in clinical LLM benchmarks come from system architecture and orchestration design, not just prompt engineering or larger models, challenging the common attribution of improvements primarily to prompt engineering.

Method: MDIA is implemented as a 7-node specialty-routed clinical reasoning graph that incorporates multiple architectural innovations: specialty routing, multi-turn context preservation, drug-state safety gating, site-filtered search, length-aware synthesis, and engine-level reliability mechanisms.

Result: MDIA achieves 0.6272 score on HealthBench Professional (n=525) using GPT-5.4-2026-03-05, which is +3.72 percentage points above OpenAI's ChatGPT for Clinicians. With Gemini 2.5 Pro as grader, MDIA scores 0.6585, showing grader variability affects reported performance.

Conclusion: Clinical agent benchmark performance is shaped by both foundation model capabilities and orchestration architecture, with architectural innovations providing substantial performance lifts. Robust LLM evaluation requires assessment across multiple independent grader models due to significant grader variability effects.

Abstract: Most reported gains on agentic-LLM clinical benchmarks are often attributed to prompt engineering, yet our results suggest that larger improvements can come from architectural and engine-level design. We present MDIA, a Multi-agent Diagnostic Intelligence Agent implemented as a 7-node specialty-routed clinical reasoning graph, on the full HealthBench Professional benchmark (n = 525), on a non-fine-tuned LLM. MDIA achieves 0.6272 under OpenAI's GPT-5.4-2026-03-05, which is +3.72 pp above the performance of OpenAI's ChatGPT for Clinicians. The experimental work shows that performance lift is attributable to system architecture: specialty routing, multi-turn context preservation, drug-state safety gating, site-filtered search, length-aware synthesis, and engine-level reliability. These findings support the view that agentic clinical benchmark performance is shaped both by the underlying foundation model and the orchestration architecture. Nevertheless, we also noticed notable differences when using other models as a grader; in particular, when using Gemini 2.5 Pro, MDIA scored 0.6585, which suggests that the choice of grader is a source of variability. Robust evaluation of LLMs would therefore require assessment across several independent grader models.

</details>


### [91] [Fundamental Limitation in Explaining AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24727)
*Atsushi Suzuki,Jing Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proves a fundamental quadrilemma showing AI explanations cannot simultaneously satisfy environmental complexity, good AI performance, interpretability, and complete faithfulness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite the importance of explainable AI for governance, existing methods don't provide completely faithful explanations for large-scale AI systems. There's uncertainty about whether theoretically possible to provide both faithful and interpretable explanations for complex AI systems.

Method: The authors use mathematical proof to establish a fundamental quadrilemma in AI explanation. They demonstrate that four desirable properties cannot be satisfied simultaneously through formal mathematical reasoning.

Result: The paper proves that AI and its explanation cannot simultaneously satisfy: 1) complex operational environment, 2) good AI performance, 3) interpretable explanation, and 4) completely faithful explanation. This creates a fundamental trade-off in AI explainability.

Conclusion: In most practical applications, we should abandon complete faithfulness and focus on explaining only important parts for applications. AI governance should be designed assuming explanations' faithfulness is always incomplete, acknowledging this fundamental limitation.

Abstract: While large-scale models such as LLMs and diffusion models have achieved practical success, public institutions have emphasized the importance of explainability in AI. Existing methods for explaining AI, however, are not designed to provide completely faithful explanations of the behavior of large-scale AI systems. Although a completely faithful and interpretable explanation of the behavior of an AI system might be useful for AI governance, it has not been known whether providing such an explanation is theoretically possible. In this paper, we mathematically prove a fundamental quadrilemma in explaining AI, stating that AI and its explanation cannot satisfy the following four conditions simultaneously: 1) the complexity of the operation environment, 2) the goodness of the AI's performance, 3) the interpretability of the AI's explanation, and 4) the complete faithfulness of the AI's explanation. This quadrilemma suggests that, in most applications where we cannot change the environment or sacrifice good AI performance and an interpretable explanation, we should give up complete faithfulness of explanations and should instead aim to explain only the parts that are important for applications. As a consequence, the quadrilemma implies that AI governance should be designed on the premise that the faithfulness of AI explanations is always incomplete.

</details>


### [92] [Hylos: Operability Contracts for Model-Native Spatial Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24728)
*Christopher Da Silva*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Hylos introduces a contract-bounded spatial intelligence architecture for making generated 3D content operable and actionable for agents, focusing on spatial transactions and causal repair rather than just visual quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current foundation models can generate visually plausible 3D content but lack operability - generated objects and environments aren't useful to agents unless they can identify entities, frames, surfaces, constraints, provenance, admissible actions, expected effects, and validation failures.

Method: Hylos architecture maintains scene-scale operability state and introduces SpatialTransactions as commit boundaries that resolve references, check admissibility, protect invariants, project effects, and return outcomes (commit, review, rollback, deferral, capability-gap). Uses focused artifact study examining causal repair through scene dependencies.

Result: The system enables successful causal repair interactions that trace symptoms through scene dependencies, select supported upstream interactions, and apply validated changes instead of directly editing visible geometry.

Conclusion: Spatial AI should be evaluated not only by visual quality but by whether generated/edited 3D can become reliable substrate for CAD, robotics, simulation, inspection, manufacturing, and interactive world authoring.

Abstract: Foundation models can increasingly describe, reconstruct, and generate 3D objects, assemblies, scenes, and environments, but visually plausible spatial output is not yet operable 3D. A generated object or environment becomes useful to an agent only when the system can identify its entities, frames, surfaces, constraints, provenance, admissible actions, expected effects, and validation failures.
  This paper introduces Hylos, a systems architecture for contract-bounded spatial intelligence. Hylos maintains scene-scale operability state over objects, assemblies, assets, surface anchors, assertions, action candidates, solver jobs, shared actuator invocations, capability gaps, and effect diffs. Durable spatial changes are routed through a SpatialTransaction: a commit boundary that resolves references, checks admissibility, protects invariants, projects effects, and returns commit, review, rollback, deferral, or capability-gap outcomes.
  The paper is framed as a systems/position preprint with a focused artifact study rather than a broad benchmark. The study examines causal repair: a visible misalignment appears on a dependent component, while the supported repair lies upstream in the placement structure that controls it. The successful interaction traces the symptom through scene dependencies, selects a supported upstream interaction, and applies a validated change instead of directly editing visible geometry.
  The broader claim is that spatial AI should be evaluated not only by visual quality, but by whether generated or edited 3D can become reliable substrate for CAD, robotics, simulation, inspection, manufacturing, and interactive world authoring.

</details>


### [93] [Automated Detection and Classification of Delusion-related Content in Naturalistic Audio Diaries Using Multi-Agent Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24755)
*Feng Chen,Justin Tauscher,Changye Li,Meliha Yetisgen,Alex Cohen,Adam Kuczynski,Angelina Pei-Tzu Tsai,Benjamin Buck,Dror Ben-Zeev,Trevor Cohen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An automated multi-agent LLM pipeline for detecting and classifying delusional beliefs, affective responses, and behavioral responses from naturalistic speech transcripts, achieving robust performance through majority voting adjudication.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Speech monologues in naturalistic settings can characterize mental illness phenomenology and detect symptom exacerbation. LLMs offer new possibilities for automating this analysis with minimal annotated training data.

Method: Novel multi-agent LLM pipeline using an ensemble of three foundation models with detailed diagnostic prompt instructions. Compared different adjudication frameworks including complex conversational debate versus majority voting for classifying delusional beliefs, affective responses, and behavioral responses.

Result: Detailed diagnostic prompts reduce false positives for delusional theme classification but constrain interpretation of affective/behavioral responses. Complex conversational debate diminishes accuracy on clinically ambiguous text by inducing premature consensus. Majority voting achieves robust performance with Micro F1 of 0.872 for delusion detection and 0.779 for classification.

Conclusion: The work provides a validated and scalable pipeline for automated detection and characterization of content suggesting delusional beliefs in naturalistic speech, demonstrating effective use of LLMs with majority voting adjudication for clinical applications.

Abstract: Speech monologues recorded in naturalistic settings provide opportunities to characterize mental illness phenomenology and detect symptom exacerbation. Large language models (LLMs) offer new possibilities for automating this process, as they require annotated data primarily for evaluation rather than training. In this paper, we present a novel automated, multi-agent LLM pipeline for the fine-grained, multi-label extraction of language suggestive of delusional beliefs, associated affective responses, and behavioral responses from transcripts of naturalistic audio diaries collected from people with moderate persecutory ideation. Evaluating an ensemble of three foundation models, we demonstrate that detailed diagnostic prompt instructions successfully reduce false positives for delusional theme classification, but also constrain the interpretation of affective or behavioral responses. Furthermore, comparing multi-agent adjudication frameworks shows that complex conversational debate between agents diminishes accuracy on clinically ambiguous text by inducing premature consensus. Instead, majority voting establishes robust performance (Micro F1 of 0.872 and 0.779 for delusion detection and classification respectively). This work provides a validated and scalable pipeline for the automated detection and characterization of content suggesting delusional beliefs in naturalistic speech.

</details>


### [94] [Proper Scoring Rules for Agentic Uncertainty Quantification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24756)
*Suresh Raghu,Satwik Pandey,Shashwat Pandey*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Trajectory Proper Score (TPS), a strictly proper scoring rule for evaluating uncertainty calibration in language model agents throughout their complete trajectories, with extensions for censored trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing agentic uncertainty quantification (UQ) evaluations conflate ranking usefulness with probabilistic truthfulness. Current metrics like AUROC, AUPRC, risk-coverage, and Trajectory ECE only evaluate discrimination, binwise calibration, or collapsed summaries, but don't strictly elicit the full prefix-conditioned success-probability trace throughout the trajectory.

Method: Builds on prequential proper scoring to introduce Trajectory Proper Score (TPS), a predictor-agnostic family of strictly proper trajectory-level scoring rules. Extends the construction to administratively censored trajectories by projecting the complete-data score onto the observable stopped prefix, yielding exact q_Z-weighted reduced scores and tractable approximations when q_Z is unestimated.

Result: Proves that TPS strictly elicits the success-probability process under complete observation. Shows theoretical distinctions between common trajectory evaluators and TPS: Trajectory ECE is resolution-blind, while scalarized Trajectory Brier elicits only collapsed scalar, not the full trace. Experimental results on StrategyQA, Tau2-Bench, HotpotQA, and WebShop demonstrate operational visibility of these theoretical distinctions.

Conclusion: The proposed TPS framework provides theoretically grounded evaluation of uncertainty calibration in agent trajectories, addressing limitations of existing metrics and enabling proper assessment of success-probability traces throughout complete and censored trajectories.

Abstract: Language-model agents increasingly emit uncertainty signals throughout a trajectory, but existing agentic UQ evaluations often conflate ranking usefulness with probabilistic truthfulness. AUROC, AUPRC, risk-coverage, Trajectory ECE, and scalarized trajectory scores evaluate discrimination, binwise calibration, or collapsed summaries, but do not strictly elicit the full prefix-conditioned success-probability trace $q_t = P^π(Y=1 | H_t)$. Building on prequential proper scoring, we introduce the Trajectory Proper Score (TPS), a predictor-agnostic family of strictly proper trajectory-level scoring rules for any per-step uncertainty signal calibrated into a probability of eventual success. We prove that TPS strictly elicits the success-probability process under complete observation, within the chosen score family and weight schedule. We extend the construction to administratively censored trajectories by projecting the complete-data score onto the observable stopped prefix, yielding an exact $q_Z$-weighted reduced score and a tractable approximation when $q_Z$ is unestimated. We further show that common trajectory evaluators target weaker objects than the full prefix-conditioned probability process: Trajectory ECE is resolution-blind, while scalarized Trajectory Brier elicits only the collapsed scalar, not the full trace. Experiments on StrategyQA, Tau2-Bench, HotpotQA, and WebShop show that these theoretical distinctions are operationally visible: probability recalibration can substantially change TPS while leaving rank metrics nearly unchanged, and the tractable censored approximation can change the verdict relative to complete-only evaluation.

</details>


### [95] [Uncertainty Decomposition via Cyclical SG-MCMC and Soft-label Learning for Subjective NLP](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24773)
*Keito Inoshita,Takato Ueno*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A Bayesian deep learning method using cSG-MCMC with soft-label learning achieves better uncertainty quantification for emotion classification by capturing annotator disagreement across multiple evaluation axes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Annotator disagreement in emotion classification reflects intrinsic ambiguity in emotion concepts and is crucial for assessing predictor quality in subjective NLP tasks. Prior work has not integrated soft-label learning with Bayesian deep learning to evaluate uncertainty, particularly in terms of annotator-distribution fidelity.

Method: Train a linear head on a frozen RoBERTa model using cyclical stochastic gradient Markov chain Monte Carlo (cSG-MCMC). Use a soft-label objective targeting the empirical annotator distribution, evaluated across five axes including Jensen-Shannon divergence, Spearman correlation for uncertainty-disagreement alignment, and selective prediction metrics.

Result: On the GoEmotions benchmark, the method outperforms Monte Carlo Dropout and Deep Ensemble simultaneously on three axes: Jensen-Shannon divergence to annotator distribution, Spearman correlation between aleatoric uncertainty and disagreement, and selective-prediction metrics (AURC and AUROC). Shows independent evaluation axes can be jointly attained from a single posterior.

Conclusion: Post-hoc temperature scaling reveals bidirectional effects, establishing hard-label calibration and annotator-JSD as independent dimensions. Motivates joint reporting of both metrics as an honest evaluation protocol for uncertainty quantification in subjective NLP tasks.

Abstract: Annotator disagreement in emotion classification reflects ambiguity intrinsic to emotion concepts and is essential for predictor-quality assessment in subjective NLP. Yet no prior work integrates soft-label learning with Bayesian deep learning to evaluate uncertainty along axes including annotator-distribution fidelity. We train a linear head on a frozen RoBERTa via cyclical stochastic gradient Markov chain Monte Carlo (cSG-MCMC), targeting the empirical annotator distribution with a soft-label objective under a five-axis evaluation. On the 28-emotion GoEmotions benchmark, the proposed method outperforms Monte Carlo Dropout and Deep Ensemble simultaneously on three axes -- Jensen-Shannon divergence (JSD) to the annotator distribution, Spearman correlation between per-emotion aleatoric uncertainty and disagreement, and selective-prediction Area Under the Risk-Coverage Curve (AURC) and Area Under the ROC Curve (AUROC) -- showing independent axes are jointly attainable from one posterior. Post-hoc temperature scaling exhibits a bidirectional effect, establishing hard-label calibration and annotator-JSD as independent dimensions and motivating joint reporting as an honest protocol.

</details>


### [96] [GRAIL: AI translation for scientists application workflow on satellite data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24784)
*Zhuocheng Shang,Ahmed Eldawy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GRAIL is an agentic translation system that converts Python geospatial workflows into scalable Spark-based programs using structured LLM-guided translation, eliminating the need for scientists to learn new frameworks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Domain scientists increasingly use Python for satellite imagery analysis but face scalability limitations with large-scale data, while lacking expertise in big data frameworks like Spark.

Method: GRAIL adapts RDPro (Scala library for satellite data) to be LLM-ready through structured documentation, API alias functions, and repair-oriented error logs. It uses a LangGraph pipeline that decomposes code generation into explicit sections with guided inputs/outputs, enabling targeted repair without full program regeneration.

Result: Demonstrates GRAIL on real-world geospatial workflows, showcasing correctness and scalability of translated Spark-based programs.

Conclusion: GRAIL successfully enables domain scientists to scale their Python geospatial workflows to large datasets through automated translation to Spark without requiring them to learn new frameworks, using agentic LLM-based translation with targeted repair mechanisms.

Abstract: Domain scientists increasingly develop Python scripts to analyze satellite imagery but they lack scalability to large-scale data. This paper demonstrates GRAIL, an agentic translation system that converts Python geospatial workflows into executable Spark-based programs without requiring scientists to learn a new framework. Rather than fine-tuning a specialized LLM model, GRAIL adapts RDPro, a Scala library for satellite data analysis, to make it LLM-ready using structured documentation, API alias functions, and repair-oriented error logs. Translation is structured as a LangGraph pipeline that decomposes code generation into explicit sections with guided inputs and outputs, enabling targeted repair without regenerating the full program. We demonstrate GRAIL on real-world geospatial workflows and showcase the correctness and scalability of the translated code.

</details>


### [97] [PANDO: Efficient Multimodal AI Agents via Online Skill Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24785)
*Yubo Li,Yidi Miao,Haotian Shen,Yuxin Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PANDO is an efficient multimodal web agent framework that learns reusable skills from experience to improve both performance (58.3% success rate) and efficiency (58-61% fewer tokens) compared to prior methods, using online skill distillation rather than expensive inference-time computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal web agents rely on expensive inference-time computation (rollout search, verifier passes, specialist models) that makes them more expensive as they accumulate experience. The authors want to create agents that become more efficient rather than more expensive with experience.

Method: PANDO uses single-rollout online skill distillation with a structured Skill Library that incorporates progress reflection, confidence-based skill demotion, hierarchical routing, visual compression, and cache-aware prompting. It learns reusable skills from web interaction experience to avoid common inefficiencies like repeat-action loops.

Result: On 910 VisualWebArena tasks, PANDO achieves 58.3% success rate, outperforming SGV (54.0%) and WALT (45.2%), while using 58% fewer tokens than SGV and 61% fewer tokens than WALT without pre-evaluation discovery budget. Rules and routines provide most success gains, while routing/compression/cache-aware prompting reduce marginal token cost.

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates that web agents can become more efficient rather than more expensive through experience accumulation via online skill distillation. It introduces new trajectory-level efficiency metrics (Action Repetition Rate, Step Overhead Ratio, Prompt Cache Utilization) to better evaluate agent efficiency beyond just success rates.

Abstract: Recent advances in multimodal web agents often rely on increased inference-time computation, including rollout search, verifier passes, offline skill discovery, and specialist model stacks. This raises a central question: can a web agent become more efficient as it accumulates experience, rather than more expensive? We first analyze trajectories from VisualWebArena and identify three recurring sources of inefficiency: repeat-action loops, hidden discovery costs, and low prompt-cache reuse. We then introduce PANDO, a single-rollout online skill-distillation framework that maintains a structured Skill Library and combines progress reflection, confidence-based skill demotion, hierarchical routing, visual compression, and cache-aware prompting. On the full set of 910 VisualWebArena tasks, PANDO achieves a 58.3% success rate, outperforming SGV (54.0%) and our WALT reproduction (45.2%), while using 58% fewer tokens than SGV and 61% fewer tokens than WALT, without any pre-evaluation discovery budget. A 300-task ablation further shows that rules and routines provide most of the success gains, while routing, compression, and cache-aware prompting convert the larger skill library into lower marginal token cost. Finally, we introduce three trajectory-level efficiency metrics -- Action Repetition Rate, Step Overhead Ratio, and Prompt Cache Utilization -- to make efficiency visible beyond terminal success.

</details>


### [98] [CoRe-Code: Collaborative Reinforcement Learning for Code Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24812)
*Zhihao Dou,Qinjian Zhao,Zhongwei Wan,Xiaoyu Xia,Sumon Biswas*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CoRe-Code is a multi-agent framework for code generation that uses role-specialized LLM agents (Planner and Coder) with collaboration-aware reinforcement learning to improve code accuracy and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based code generation methods rely on autoregressive decoding without global planning, resulting in locally coherent but globally suboptimal code (failing tests, inefficient complexity). While approaches like CoT and multi-agent systems introduce planning, their limited role specialization and coordination hinder performance on complex tasks.

Method: Proposes CoRe-Code framework with a Planner-Coder paradigm: Planner generates high-level plans, Coder executes them to generate code. Introduces collaboration-aware reinforcement learning using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to enhance role specialization and alignment between agents.

Result: Outperforms existing RL-based and multi-agent methods across multiple benchmarks of varying difficulty using three base models. Shows consistent improvements in accuracy, higher efficiency in execution time and memory usage. Demonstrates flexibility by generalizing to other multi-agent frameworks (e.g., Retrieval and Debugging agents).

Conclusion: CoRe-Code effectively addresses coordination and specialization challenges in multi-agent code generation, producing more accurate and efficient code while being flexible and scalable to various multi-agent frameworks.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved strong performance in code generation, but most methods rely on autoregressive decoding without global planning, often leading to locally coherent yet globally suboptimal solutions (e.g., failing test cases or inefficient complexity). While recent approaches such as Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and multi-agent systems (MAS) introduce planning, their limited role specialization and coordination hinder performance on complex tasks. To address the challenges of coordination and specialization in multi-agent code generation, we propose Collaborative Reinforcement Code (CoRe-Code), a framework for role specialized LLM agents that enhances inter-agent coordination to generate more accurate and efficient code. CoRe-Code adopts a simple Planner-Coder paradigm, where the Planner produces high-level plans and the Coder executes them to generate code. We further introduce a collaboration-aware reinforcement learning stage based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to enhance role specialization and alignment. Experiments show that CoRe-Code outperforms a wide range of existing RL-based and multi-agent methods. In addition, we demonstrate that CoRe-Code can generalize to other multi-agent frameworks (e.g., Retrieval and Debugging agents), highlighting its flexibility and scalability.
