We evaluate CoRe-Code on multiple benchmarks of varying difficulty using three base models. Compared to existing baselines, the results show consistent improvements in accuracy, while also achieving higher efficiency in terms of execution time and memory usage, demonstrating the effectiveness and practicality of CoRe-Code.

</details>


### [99] [Agent Manufacturing: Foundation-Model Agents as First-Class Industrial Entities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24823)
*Yilei Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agent Manufacturing is a new manufacturing paradigm where foundation-model-based autonomous agents take over the coordinative cognition work traditionally done by humans, moving beyond previous paradigms that automated physical and routine cognitive tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Previous manufacturing paradigms (mechanization, electrification, programmable automation, Smart Manufacturing) have automated various physical and cognitive tasks, but one layer remained fundamentally human: coordinative cognition involving interpretation, allocation, diagnosis, negotiation, and governance. The authors argue this final human-centric layer is now being redistributed through foundation-model-based autonomous agents.

Method: The paper proposes a new paradigm called "Agent Manufacturing" with a specific operational definition: a manufacturing system qualifies as Agent Manufacturing when its principal coordination mechanism involves reasoning performed by foundation-model agents that can interpret open-ended goals, plan over long horizons, invoke tools and machines, and negotiate with other agents and humans.

Result: The authors provide a narrower, more falsifiable definition than existing concepts like cognitive manufacturing or Industry 5.0, and sharply distinguish Agent Manufacturing from classical multi-agent manufacturing systems that were autonomous only within closed protocol spaces.

Conclusion: Manufacturing is undergoing a fifth transition where foundation-model-based autonomous agents are taking over the coordinative cognition layer that remained fundamentally human through previous industrial revolutions, creating a new paradigm called Agent Manufacturing with specific, operational characteristics.

Abstract: Manufacturing has passed through four widely recognized paradigms - mechanization, electrification, programmable automation, and Smart Manufacturing - each defined by the kind of work it shifted from humans to machines. In every case, one layer of industrial work remained fundamentally human: the coordinative cognition of production, comprising the interpretive, allocative, diagnostic, negotiative, and governance work exercised by engineers, planners, and operational managers. We argue that a fifth transition is now underway in which this layer, rather than the physical or routine-cognitive layers below it, is what foundation-model-based autonomous agents primarily redistribute. We name this paradigm Agent Manufacturing and define it operationally: a manufacturing system is an instance of Agent Manufacturing when its principal coordination mechanism is reasoning performed by foundation-model agents that can interpret open-ended goals, plan over long horizons, invoke tools and machines, and negotiate with other agents and humans. This is a narrower and more falsifiable definition than the existing literature on cognitive manufacturing or Industry 5.0 provides, and it distinguishes the paradigm sharply from classical multi-agent manufacturing systems, which were autonomous only within closed protocol spaces.

</details>


### [100] [Test-Time Deep Thinking to Explore Implicit Rules](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24828)
*Wentong Chen,Xin Cong,Zhong Zhang,Yaxi Lu,Siyuan Zhao,Yesai Wu,Qinyu Luo,Haotian Chen,Yankai Lin,Zhiyuan Liu,Maosong Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TTExplore framework trains specialized LLM thinkers to infer implicit environment rules through test-time exploration, improving agent performance by 14-19 points on embodied tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents struggle in environments with implicit rules that cannot be directly observed but must be inferred through interaction, causing them to fall into repetitive trial-and-error loops and fail tasks.

Method: Proposes TTExplore framework with thinker component that analyzes interaction history to infer implicit rules. Uses novel stable RL pipeline with task-level scores as indirect rewards and single thinking node per trajectory to address evaluation instability and reward sparsity.

Result: Trains specialized 7B model Exp-Thinker using the proposed pipeline. Experiments on five text-based embodied tasks show TTExplore with Exp-Thinker improves baseline agent performance by average of 14-19 points.

Conclusion: Explicit reasoning about implicit rules through test-time exploration framework significantly improves agent performance in rule-governed environments, demonstrating the effectiveness of specialized thinker components.

Abstract: With the continuous advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs), intelligent agents are becoming increasingly vital. However, these agents often fail in environments governed by implicit rules--hidden constraints that cannot be observed directly and must be inferred through interaction. This causes agents to fall into repetitive trial-and-error loops, ultimately leading to task failure. To address this challenge, we propose Test-Time Exploration (TTExplore), a framework where a thinker component analyzes interaction history to infer these implicit rules and guide an actor. Effective exploration in this setting critically depends on the reasoning ability of the thinker. However, evaluating deep reasoning trajectories is inherently unstable and difficult, which poses a major obstacle to effective training. To overcome this issue, we introduce a novel and stable reinforcement learning pipeline. The core idea is to use accurate task-level scores as indirect rewards to bypass the difficulty of evaluating intermediate reasoning, and to retain only a single thinking node per trajectory to alleviate reward sparsity. Using this pipeline, we train a specialized 7B model, Exp-Thinker. Experiments on five text-based embodied tasks show that TTExplore equipped with Exp-Thinker improves baseline agent performance by an average of $14$-$19$ points, demonstrating the effectiveness of explicitly reasoning about implicit rules.

</details>


### [101] [Geo-Expert: Towards Expert-Level Geological Reasoning via Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24844)
*Chenyou Guo,Zongqi Liu,Yizhou Zhang,Zhaorui Jiang,Ze Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Geo-Expert is a family of parameter-efficient geological LLMs that outperform much larger generalist models on specialized geological reasoning tasks through domain-specific fine-tuning on curated geological instruction data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI applications in Earth sciences focus predominantly on surface remote sensing and GIS, while general-purpose LLMs hallucinate when reasoning about subsurface structures and deep-time geological evolution. There is a critical need for specialized geological AI systems that can accurately model and reason about complex underground formations and deep-time processes.

Method: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (using LoRA) of three base models (Qwen3-8B, Qwen3-32B, Gemma-3-27B) on a custom-curated, high-quality geological instruction dataset synthesized through a dedicated pipeline, with systematic evaluation using the novel Geo-Eval benchmark.

Result: The domain-aligned 8B model outperforms open-weight 70B generalists and proprietary GPT-4o on specialized geological reasoning tasks. The 32B variant approaches the performance of frontier reasoning models. The optimized 8B model offers a competitive cost-performance ratio suitable for practical deployment.

Conclusion: Geo-Expert demonstrates that moderately-sized LLMs (8B-32B parameters) can deliver state-of-the-art performance on geological reasoning through domain-specific fine-tuning, achieving results competitive with or superior to much larger generalist models. This work provides a reproducible framework for developing scientific domain-specific LLMs while offering cost-effective deployment solutions.

Abstract: While general-purpose Large Language Models (LLMs) applied to Geology often hallucinate when reasoning about subsurface structures and deep-time evolution, current AI in Earth sciences predominantly targets surface remote sensing and GIS. To bridge this gap, we introduce Geo-Expert, a family of parameter-efficient geological LLMs fine-tuned on a custom-curated, high-quality instruction dataset processed using our custom instruction synthesis pipeline. We investigate the impact of model scaling and architecture by fine-tuning three base models: Qwen3-8B, Qwen3-32B, and Gemma-3-27B, with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) method. Our extensive evaluation on a novel domain-specific benchmark, Geo-Eval, reveals that a domain-aligned 8B model can outperform open-weight 70B generalists and proprietary GPT-4o on specialized geological reasoning, while a 32B variant approaches frontier reasoning models. The optimized 8B model further offers a competitive cost-performance ratio for deployment. This work provides a reproducible recipe for democratizing scientific LLMs and establishes a baseline for geological artificial intelligence.

</details>


### [102] [Solving Combinatorial Counting Problems with Weighted First-Order Model Counting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24845)
*Yuanhong Wang,Juhua Pu,Yuxu Zhou,Yuyi Wang,Ondřej Kuželka*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Cofola is a typed declarative language for combinatorial counting problems that compiles to weighted first-order model counting (WFOMC) while preserving symmetries, enabling practical automated counting of combinatorial structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Combinatorial counting problems are common in AI, statistics, and discrete math, but solving them remains a manual exercise. Existing automated approaches (propositional model counting or constraint satisfaction) destroy the exchangeability that makes combinatorial counting tractable, and closed-form derivations are brittle.

Method: Cofola provides a typed declarative language with primitives for common combinatorial objects (sets, bags, tuples, sequences, circles, partitions, compositions) with relational and arithmetic constraints. It uses a three-phase compilation pipeline: preprocessing, decomposition, and symmetry-preserving encoding to WFOMC with coefficient-extraction constraints. The encoding groups indistinguishable entities, breaks symmetry lexicographically, and encodes sequences/circles via order axioms to stay within domain-liftable fragments.

Result: On a suite of representative combinatorial counting problems (from textbook math problems to multi-object scenarios that prior frameworks cannot express), Cofola produces concise specifications and a uniform solving pipeline that is practical end-to-end.

Conclusion: Cofola enables automated combinatorial counting by preserving symmetries through a domain-liftable compilation to WFOMC, addressing limitations of manual derivations and existing automated approaches that break exchangeability.

Abstract: Combinatorial counting problems pervade artificial intelligence, statistics, and discrete mathematics. Whether the task is enumerating subsets, multisets, permutations, partitions, or compositions under structural and arithmetic constraints, solving it remains a stubbornly manual exercise. Closed-form derivations are powerful but brittle, while naive encodings to propositional model counting or constraint satisfaction destroy the exchangeability that makes counting tractable in the first place. We present Cofola (COmbinatorial counting LAnguage with First-Order logic), a typed declarative language whose primitives are the combinatorial objects that recur in everyday counting questions, including sets, bags, tuples, sequences, circles, partitions, and compositions, together with natural relational and arithmetic constraints over them. A denotational semantics maps every Cofola program to a well-defined combinatorial counting problem, and a three-phase compilation pipeline (preprocessing, decomposition, and symmetry-preserving encoding) reduces this problem to a weighted first-order model counting (WFOMC) instance augmented with coefficient-extraction constraints. To stay inside known domain-liftable fragments whenever possible, the encoding groups indistinguishable entities, breaks the symmetry of unordered groupings lexicographically, and encodes sequences and circles via order axioms. On a suite of representative combinatorial counting problems, ranging from textbook math problems to multi-object scenarios that the closest prior framework cannot express, Cofola produces concise specifications and a uniform solving pipeline that is practical end-to-end.

</details>


### [103] [Clustering as Reasoning: A $k$-Means Interpretation of Chain-of-Thought Graph Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24867)
*Xuanting Xie,Zhaochen Guo,Bingheng Li,Xingtong Yu,Zhifei Liao,Zhao Kang,Yuan Fang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: KCoT: A unified framework integrating Chain-of-Thought reasoning with graph representation learning by interpreting reasoning as iterative clustering operations, achieving state-of-the-art performance on graph benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph Chain-of-Thought methods use disjoint architectures and fixed graph representations, limiting semantic-topological interaction and interpretability during step-by-step reasoning.

Method: Proposes KCoT framework revealing mathematical correspondence between Transformer blocks and k-means algorithm, enabling reasoning as iterative assignment/update steps. Introduces Semantic Discriminating Prompt for structured CoT reasoning and structure-grounded alignment to fuse topological priors with thought-conditioned representations.

Result: Experiments on standard benchmarks show consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods, validating the clustering-based approach.

Conclusion: Validates clustering as a principled mechanism for CoT-based graph learning, offering better integration of reasoning with graph structure and improved interpretability.

Abstract: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting has shown promise in enhancing the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) on text-attributed graphs (TAGs). This work reframes CoT-based graph learning through the principle of clustering as reasoning, offering a $k$-means interpretation of how iterative reasoning operates over graph-structured data. We observe that existing graph CoT methods rely on disjoint architectures and fixed graph representations, limiting step-by-step semantic-topological interaction and interpretability. To overcome this limitation, we propose a unified framework named KCoT that integrates CoT reasoning with graph representation learning. Our key theoretical result reveals a formal mathematical correspondence between a Transformer block and the $k$-means algorithm, allowing reasoning to be interpreted as iterative assignment and update steps. Based on this insight, we introduce a Semantic Discriminating Prompt that explicitly formulates these steps as structured CoT reasoning, together with a structure-grounded alignment strategy to fuse topological priors with evolving thought-conditioned representations. Experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods, validating clustering as a principled mechanism for CoT-based graph learning.

</details>


### [104] [Inverting the Shield: Systematically Generating Safety Tests from Policy Specifications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24883)
*Xiaoyue Lu,Xianglin Yang,Haijun Liu,Jiahao Liu,Kuntai Cai,Yan Xiao,Jin Song Dong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: POLARIS introduces a formal, specification-based testing framework for LLM safety that converts natural-language policies into logic representations, enabling systematic discovery of policy violations through graph traversal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM safety evaluation approaches (benchmarks and red-teaming) heavily depend on expert domain knowledge, offer limited systematic guarantees, and are vulnerable to rapid obsolescence, necessitating a more rigorous and systematic safety testing framework.

Method: POLARIS formalizes unstructured natural-language policies into First-Order Logic (FOL) representations, constructs a Semantic Policy Graph to encode complex policy violation scenarios as traversable paths, systematically explores the graph to uncover compositional violation patterns, and instantiates them into executable natural-language test queries.

Result: Experiments show POLARIS achieves higher policy coverage and attack success counts compared to established baselines, demonstrating its effectiveness in uncovering safety violations.

Conclusion: POLARIS successfully bridges formal methods with AI safety by providing a principled, automated framework that enables rigorous, systematic, and reproducible safety testing of LLMs with verifiable traceability between policies and test cases.

Abstract: The widespread integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) necessitates rigorous and systematic safety evaluation. Existing paradigms either rely on constructed benchmarks to assess safety from predefined perspectives, or employ dynamic red-teaming to probe potential vulnerabilities. While effective, these approaches face challenges, as they depend heavily on expert domain knowledge, offer limited systematic guarantees, and are vulnerable to rapid obsolescence. To address these limitations, we introduce a novel framework POLARIS that brings the rigor of specification-based software testing to AI safety. POLARIS first compiles unstructured natural-language policies into First-Order Logic (FOL) representations, establishing a traceable link between high-level rules and concrete test cases. This formalization enables the construction of a Semantic Policy Graph, where complex policy violation scenarios are encoded as traversable paths. By systematically exploring this graph, POLARIS uncovers compositional violation patterns, which are then instantiated into executable natural-language test queries, enabling coverage-driven and reproducible safety testing. Experiments demonstrate that POLARIS achieves higher policy coverage and attack success counts compared to established baselines. Crucially, by bridging formal methods and AI safety, POLARIS provides a principled, automated approach to ensuring LLMs adhere to safety-critical policies with verifiable traceability. We release our code at https://github.com/huac-lxy/POLARIS.

</details>


### [105] [TaBIIC2: Interactive Building of Ontological Taxonomies using Weighted Self-Organizing Maps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24899)
*Mathieu d'Aquin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Interactive tool combining self-organizing maps and manual input for building ontological taxonomies from tabular data


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual construction of ontological taxonomies from tabular data is challenging, while purely automatic approaches (like clustering or LLMs) give users overwhelming results with little control over the process.

Method: Weighted self-organizing maps for clustering with arbitrary number of clusters that show distinct distributions of entity characteristics, combined with interactive tools for concept creation and organization

Result: Tool provides progressive and interactive construction of taxonomies by identifying clusters and their intentional definitions, representing a middle ground between manual and automatic approaches

Conclusion: The interactive approach with weighted self-organizing maps offers a balanced solution between user control and automation for building ontological taxonomies from tabular data

Abstract: Ontologies represent the conceptual knowledge of a domain. At the core of an ontology is the taxonomy of concepts and subconcepts that represent specific entities, which can be complex to build. In many cases, information is available in the form of records describing the characteristics of relevant entities, i.e., tabular data. Identifying patterns and similarities in such data can serve as a basis for identifying concepts and organizing them. However, doing so manually can be challenging, and purely automatic approaches, such as agglomerative clustering or relying on a large language model to analyze the data, can leave the user with overwhelming results and little control. In this paper, we describe a tool that enables the progressive and interactive construction of a taxonomy of concepts by identifying clusters as well as their intentional definitions. To do so, we rely on weighted self-organizing maps as a clustering method because they enable the creation of an arbitrary number of clusters that are distinct with respect to the distributions of values of specific characteristics of the clustered entities. We show that, by integrating this mechanism and others for rapidly creating concepts that group together instances from tabular data, this tool represents a middle ground between purely manual analysis and automatic methods for building ontological taxonomies.

</details>


### [106] [ProActor: Timing-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Proactive Task Scheduling Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24900)
*Lei Ding,Bin He,Chenguang Wang,Yang Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ProActor introduces a unified framework for proactive conversational task scheduling using automated annotation, systematic metrics, and RL optimization with composite rewards, achieving better timing while maintaining action consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches lack generalizable end-to-end solutions for measuring and optimizing anticipatory behaviors in proactive task-oriented agents. Current systems are reactive and await explicit instructions, whereas proactive agents should autonomously anticipate user needs and trigger software actions at appropriate moments.

Method: The paper introduces ProActor framework with three components: (1) domain-agnostic automated annotation methodology generating full opportunity time windows instead of point labels, (2) systematic proactiveness metrics for timing quality and reference action alignment, and (3) RL optimization using GRPO with composite rewards including RULER-based rewards with proactiveness rubrics. Experiments use 4-bit Qwen2.5-14B model with ART-F infrastructure combining request-adaptive inference clusters and DDP-based training.

Result: Experiments on two newly auto-annotated datasets show significant improvements in proactive timing while maintaining action consistency comparable to SOTA baselines. Ablation studies validate the effectiveness of distinct composite reward variations. The ART-F framework enables LoRA training of 4-bit Qwen2.5-14B-ProActor-Q4 with 4-8x speedups.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that ProActor represents a significant advancement in proactive conversational agents by enabling RL optimization of anticipatory timing through automated domain-agnostic annotation and novel proactiveness metrics. The proposed ART-F framework demonstrates practical feasibility with efficient training infrastructure achieving 4-8x speedups.

Abstract: Proactive task-oriented agents must autonomously anticipate user needs, identify actionable opportunities, and trigger software actions at appropriate moments - fundamentally shifting from reactive systems that await explicit instructions. However, existing approaches lack generalizable end-to-end solutions for measuring and optimizing such anticipatory behaviors.
  This paper introduces ProActor, a unified framework for conversational task scheduling that integrates: (1) a domain-agnostic automated annotation methodology that enables scalable proactiveness reinforcement learning (RL) by generating full opportunity time windows instead of rigid point labels, (2) systematic proactiveness metrics capturing both timing quality and reference action alignment, and (3) RL optimization using GRPO with various reward designs. Our insight is that RULER-based rewards with proactiveness rubrics are crucial for improving timing quality, and that proactiveness optimization enabled by stage-aware composite rewards is key to balancing timing quality and reference action alignment.
  Timing-aware RL requires extensive exploration, demanding efficient infrastructure. We develop ART-F, an adaptive framework combining request-adaptive inference clusters with DDP-based training on single-node multi-GPU systems, enabling LoRA training of 4-bit Qwen2.5-14B-ProActor-Q4 with 4-8x speedups. Experiments on two newly auto-annotated datasets demonstrate significant improvements in proactive timing while maintaining action consistency comparable to state-of-the-art (SOTA) baselines. Ablations validate the effectiveness of distinct composite reward variations.

</details>


### [107] [Noise-Robust Financial Numerical Entity Attribute Tagging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24910)
*Hsin-Min Lu,Chen-Yang Lai,Yi-Jhen Li,Ju-Chun Yen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NORA is a noise-robust tagging system for rich financial numerical entity attributes that addresses noisy XBRL labels and predicts multiple FNE attributes beyond just concept names.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing FNE understanding methods have two key limitations: 1) labels derived from inline XBRL are often noisy due to manual preparation of filings, and 2) they primarily focus on concept name prediction while neglecting other important FNE attributes like reporting-time relation, measurement scale, and accounting sign.

Method: NORA uses task-aware instance-specific weighting to attenuate noisy label influence during training. It also proposes Neighborhood Prior-adjusted KNN (NPK) filtering for more reliable evaluation on real-world noisy test sets. The authors construct a large-scale benchmark with 6.6 million instances with multi-attribute labels and filing metadata.

Result: NORA outperforms state-of-the-art noisy-label baselines (Co-teaching, Mixup, SSR, SelfMix) and shows robustness under both unfiltered and noise-filtered test settings. It achieves best Accuracy, Macro F1, and Weighted F1 for concept name and time-relation prediction, while remaining competitive on scale and sign prediction.

Conclusion: The results demonstrate the value of jointly modeling rich FNE attributes while accounting for label noise in real-world financial filings. NORA effectively addresses both the noise problem and the limited scope of previous FNE understanding approaches.

Abstract: Financial Numerical Entity (FNE) understanding aims to recover the meaning of numerical mentions in financial reports. Existing studies primarily focus on concept name prediction and face two important limitations. First, labels derived from inline XBRL may contain errors because filings are usually prepared manually. Second, other important FNE attributes, such as reporting-time relation, measurement scale, and accounting sign, are less emphasized. We propose \textbf{NO}ise-\textbf{R}obust Tagging for Rich Financial Numerical Entity \textbf{A}ttributes (\textsc{NORA}) to address these gaps. NORA uses task-aware instance-specific weighting to attenuate the influence of noisy labels during training, and we further propose the Neighborhood Prior-adjusted KNN (NPK) filtering method for more reliable evaluation on real-world noisy test sets. In addition, we construct a large-scale benchmark containing 6.6 million instances with multi-attribute labels and filing metadata. Experiments show that \textsc{NORA} performs strongly compared with state-of-the-art noisy-label baselines, including Co-teaching, Mixup, SSR, and SelfMix. Moreover, NORA is robust under both unfiltered and noise-filtered test settings. It achieves the best Accuracy, Macro F1, and Weighted F1 for concept name and time-relation prediction, while remaining competitive on scale and sign prediction. These results demonstrate the value of jointly modeling rich FNE attributes while accounting for label noise in real-world financial filings.

</details>


### [108] [Energy Shields for Fairness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24926)
*Filip Cano,Thomas A. Henzinger,Konstantin Kueffner*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Energy shields introduce a novel, probabilistic approach to runtime fairness using physics-inspired energy functions that nudge decision sequences toward fairness, providing both short-term safety and long-term liveness guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Runtime fairness is a dynamic property requiring consideration of past decisions, which conventional static classifiers neglect. Traditional fairness shields intervene deterministically and abruptly when fairness violations occur, lacking smooth adaptation to unfairness levels.

Method: Energy shields use lightweight, adaptive controllers that monitor decision sequences and intervene probabilistically. They utilize physics-inspired energy functions that apply stronger nudging forces when decisions become more unfair, ensuring smooth transitions toward fairness.

Result: Energy shields provide both short-term safety (running fairness measure stays within target interval with high probability) and long-term liveness (limit of fairness measure lies within limit target interval). The paper includes a synthesis procedure for constructing least-intrusive energy shields and demonstrates experimental efficiency.

Conclusion: Energy shields represent the first fairness shield approach offering both short-term safety and long-term liveness guarantees, providing a smoother, probabilistic alternative to deterministic traditional shields that better handles runtime fairness dynamics.

Abstract: Runtime fairness is not a one-time constraint but a dynamic property evaluated over a sequence of decisions.
  To ensure fairness at runtime, it is necessary to account for past decisions, information neglected by conventional, static classifiers.
  Traditional fairness shields enforce runtime fairness abruptly,
  by intervening \emph{deterministically} whenever a sequence of decisions violates the target for a running fairness measure. This motivates our \emph{main conceptual contribution: \textbf{energy shields}.}
  An energy shield is a novel, lightweight, adaptive controller that monitors a sequence of decisions and intervenes \emph{probabilistically} to ensure runtime fairness smoothly, by utilizing physics-inspired energy functions to nudge the sequence toward fairness:
  the more unfair the decisions, the stronger the nudging force becomes. This makes energy shields the \emph{\textbf{first}} fairness shields to provide both \emph{short-term safety and long-term liveness guarantees}.
  Safety ensures that the running fairness measure stays within a running target interval with high probability,
  and liveness ensures that the limit of the fairness measure lies within the limit target interval.
  Intuitively, the short-term specifies the tolerated fairness values and the long-term specifies the desired fairness values.
  We also provide a synthesis procedure for constructing the least intrusive energy shield for a given target specification, and demonstrate its efficiency experimentally.
  We evaluate our energy shields against existing fairness shields through the lens of short- and long-term fairness.

</details>


### [109] [Towards Multi-Turn Dialog Systems for Industrial Asset Operations and Maintenance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24953)
*Chengrui Li,Rujing Li,Yitong Bai,Rui Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Multi-turn industrial QA system using supervisor-specialist multi-agent architecture with structured artifact reuse and parallel execution for better tool efficiency and response quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional plan-execute single-agent systems have limitations in maintaining cross-turn context and reusing intermediate results for industrial asset operations and maintenance QA, which is inherently multi-turn and tool-dependent.

Method: Developed a multi-turn dialog system based on supervisor-specialist multi-agent architecture with structured artifact reuse, dynamic replanning, and parallel tool execution to address tool invocation bottlenecks.

Result: System achieves 54.5% improvement in planning effectiveness and 37.8% better task completion compared to baseline. Artifact reuse reduces tool-time share from 47.3% to 26.3%, making turns 2-5 approximately 4.2x faster than first turn.

Conclusion: The multi-agent architecture with structured artifact reuse and parallel tool execution effectively addresses limitations of single-agent systems for industrial multi-turn QA, significantly improving performance and efficiency.

Abstract: Industrial asset operations and maintenance question answering is inherently multi-turn, iterative, and highly dependent on external tool invocation. However, the conventional plan-execute single-agent architecture exhibits clear limitations in maintaining cross-turn context, and reusing intermediate results. In this paper, we present a multi-turn dialog system designed for industrial scenarios based on a supervisor-specialist multi-agent architecture. To alleviate tool invocation bottlenecks, the system incorporates structured artifact reuse, dynamic replanning, and parallel tool execution. Evaluation results show that our system achieves better response quality compared with the baseline, with planning effectiveness increasing by 54.5% and task completion improving by 37.8%. System profiling further shows that cross-turn artifact reuse effectively reduces redundant tool invocation, decreasing the tool-time share from 47.3% to 26.3% and making turns 2-5 approximately 4.2x faster than the first turn.

</details>


### [110] [Mitigating Object Hallucinations in Vision-Language Models through Region-Aware Attention Recalibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24957)
*Yuanzhi Xu,Qian Gao,Jun Fan,Guohui Ding,Zhenyu Yang,Sixue Lin,Yuteng Xiao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A novel training-free inference strategy using region-aware adaptive weighting to reduce object hallucinations in Large Vision-Language Models without compromising efficiency or feature space continuity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods to address object hallucination in LVLMs have significant limitations: data-driven fine-tuning is expensive, contrastive decoding has high latency, and attention head truncation compromises model feature space continuity. There's a need for a solution that maintains both computational efficiency and feature space integrity.

Method: The paper introduces a training-free inference strategy that acts as a region-aware adaptive weighting mechanism. It computes an outlier-resistant statistical midpoint across attention heads to establish a stable anchor for visual representations, then uses inter-head disagreement mapped across regions to dynamically determine intervention budgets. This approach gently suppresses hallucination-inducing attention paths through continuous penalty modulation.

Result: Comprehensive evaluations on standard multimodal benchmarks (CHAIR, POPE, and MME) show substantial reduction in both instance- and sentence-level hallucinations. The method achieves state-of-the-art performance against contemporary baselines while confirming efficiency and algorithmic robustness.

Conclusion: The proposed region-aware adaptive weighting strategy effectively reduces object hallucination in LVLMs without compromising computational efficiency or feature space continuity, representing a promising training-free solution to this persistent challenge.

Abstract: The generation of factually incorrect objects, commonly known as object hallucination, remains a persistent challenge in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs). Current approaches to address this issue - ranging from expensive data-driven fine-tuning and high-latency contrastive decoding to rigid attention head truncation - frequently compromise either computational efficiency or the continuity of the model's feature space. To overcome these limitations, we introduce a novel, training-free inference strategy that operates as a region-aware adaptive weighting mechanism to dynamically correct semantic drift without relying on abrupt heuristic truncations. By computing an outlier-resistant statistical midpoint across various attention heads, we establish a stable anchor for reliable visual representations. We then utilize the inter-head disagreement mapped across regions to dynamically determine intervention budgets, gently suppressing hallucination-inducing attention paths through a continuous penalty modulation. This recalibration process effectively rectifies visual-semantic misalignments while fully preserving generative fluency and language priors. Comprehensive evaluations on standard multimodal benchmarks, including CHAIR, POPE, and MME, reveal that our strategy substantially curtails both instance- and sentence-level hallucinations. The results demonstrate state-of-the-art performance against contemporary baselines, confirming our method's efficiency and algorithmic robustness. Our code will be public.

</details>


### [111] [NeurIPS: Neuro-anatomical Inductive Priors for Sphere-based Brain Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24993)
*Sijin Yu,Zijiao Chen,Zhenyu Yang,Zihao Tan,Jiakun Xu,Zhongliang Liu,Shengxian Chen,Wenxuan Wu,Xiangmin Xu,Xin Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NeurIPS improves fMRI decoding by treating anatomical variation as a predictive signal rather than a nuisance, achieving state-of-the-art performance with unprecedented efficiency through spherical tokenization and structure-guided mixture of experts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current fMRI decoders face a performance-fidelity trade-off where efficient ID encoders outperform geometrically faithful surface-based models. This is partly due to inefficient surface tokenization and the failure to use anatomy as a predictive signal.

Method: NeurIPS combines two innovations: 1) Selective ROI Spherical Tokenizer (SRST) for efficient geometric encoding, and 2) Structure-Guided Mixture of Experts (SG-MoE) that explicitly models individual anatomy using cortical features.

Result: On the Natural Scenes Dataset, NeurIPS establishes new SOTA for surface decoders and achieves performance comparable to strong 1D baselines. The model converges dramatically faster (10 vs. 600 epochs), enables rapid adaptation to new subjects using only 20% of data, and scales robustly with larger cohorts.

Conclusion: By leveraging anatomical priors, NeurIPS provides a principled and scalable path toward robust, generalizable brain decoding. Ablations confirm gains come from cortical features rather than memorizing subject IDs, reframing anatomy from nuisance to powerful inductive prior.

Abstract: Current fMRI decoders face a performance-fidelity trade-off where efficient ID encoders outperform geometrically faithful surface-based models. We argue this is partly driven by inefficient surface tokenization and the failure to use anatomy as a predictive signal. We present NeurIPS, a framework that improves surface-based decoding by reframing anatomical variation from a nuisance to a powerful inductive prior. NeurIPS unites two innovations: a Selective ROI Spherical Tokenizer (SRST) for efficient geometric encoding, and a Structure-Guided Mixture of Experts (SG-MoE) that explicitly models individual anatomy using cortical features. On the Natural Scenes Dataset, NeurIPS establishes a new state-of-the-art for surface decoders and achieves performance comparable to strong 1D baselines. This is achieved with unprecedented efficiency, as the model converges dramatically faster (10 vs. 600 epochs). This efficiency enables rapid adaptation to new subjects using only 20% of data and ensures robust scalability as the training cohort is expanded. Ablations provide causal evidence that these gains are driven by the model's use of cortical features, not by memorizing subject IDs. By leveraging anatomical priors, NeurIPS provides a principled and scalable path toward robust, generalizable brain decoding.

</details>


### [112] [Privacy-Preserving Local Language Models for Longitudinal Data Retrieval in Chronic Dermatologic Disease: Implementation in Pemphigus Patients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25020)
*Abdurrahim Yilmaz,Ayşe Esra Koku Aksu,Duygu Yamen,Vefa Asli Erdemir,Mehmet Salih Gurel,Gulsum Gencoglan,Joram M. Posma,Burak Temelkuran*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A locally deployed small language model (SLM) can effectively summarize longitudinal dermatology records and extract clinical features from pemphigus patients' documentation with high accuracy and clinician-rated quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chronic dermatologic diseases like pemphigus generate extensive longitudinal clinical documentation that is difficult to review comprehensively during routine visits, increasing clinician workload and risk of missing critical historical information.

Method: Retrospective case series of 30 pemphigus patients with 541 visit notes aggregated into longitudinal records. Used locally deployed SLM (Qwen3 4B Thinking 2507) to retrieve 56 clinically relevant features and generate summaries, compared against expert dermatologist annotations.

Result: Mean accuracy of 82.25% across 1,680 feature retrieval tasks. Dermatologists rated AI-generated summaries highly for overall quality (8.23-8.47), clinical accuracy (7.93-8.20), and usefulness (8.47-8.50), with preference for AI summaries in 53.3% of evaluations.

Conclusion: Privacy-preserving, locally deployed SLMs can reliably generate clinically meaningful longitudinal summaries and may support clinical decision-making when integrated with appropriate oversight.

Abstract: Chronic dermatologic diseases such as pemphigus require long-term follow-up, generating extensive longitudinal clinical documentation that is difficult to review comprehensively during routine visits and increasing clinician workload as well as the risk of missing critical historical information. We evaluated whether a locally deployed, privacy-preserving small language model (SLM) could retrieve structured clinical features and generate longitudinal summaries from long-term dermatology follow-up records. In this retrospective case series, thirty pemphigus patients contributed 541 visit notes that were aggregated into full longitudinal records (89,336 words); 56 clinically relevant features were annotated by two expert dermatologists. The locally deployed SLM (Qwen3 4B Thinking 2507) was queried with each complete record to retrieve 56 features and generate one final report summaries. Across 1,680 feature retrieval tasks, mean accuracy was 82.25%. Dermatologists' ratings of AI-generated summaries were high for overall quality (8.23-8.47), clinical accuracy (7.93-8.20), and usefulness (8.47-8.50), with no significant inter-evaluator differences and an overall preference for AI summaries in 53.3% of evaluations. These findings suggest that privacy-preserving, locally deployed SLMs can outperform medical experts and reliably generate clinically meaningful longitudinal summaries. SLMs may support clinical decision-making when integrated with appropriate oversight.

</details>


### [113] [AION: Next-Generation Tasks and Practical Harness for Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25045)
*Tianxiang Zhan,Xiaobao Song,Tong Guan,Shirui Pan,Ming Jin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AION is a time series harness with agents, skills, and reliability mechanisms that enables more realistic forecasting tasks with better process traces, artifacts, and review steps than standard approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Time series research needs to move beyond fixed forecasting benchmarks to realistic tasks that combine prediction, contextual reasoning, tool use, and structured decision support. Current benchmarks use clean data and short evaluation loops, while agents alone may miss temporal constraints, evidence checks, or review processes.

Method: Formalizes next-generation time series tasks as three-component tuples (task file, workspace, validation interface) and presents AION - a time series harness with six component groups: agents, skills, rules, memory, evaluation, and protocols. Uses three design principles: temporal grounding, temporal knowledge-grounded reasoning, and reliability mechanisms (post-experiment analysis, layered review).

Result: Kaggle Store Sales case study shows that the harness produces more detailed process traces, more artifacts, and more review steps than the same base agent operating in OpenCode direct build mode.

Conclusion: The results argue for a paradigm shift from fixed tasks to realistic ones under real-world constraints, demonstrating the value of comprehensive harness systems over simple agent-only approaches.

Abstract: Time series research is moving beyond fixed forecasting benchmarks toward realistic tasks that combine prediction, contextual reasoning, tool use, and structured decision support. Most benchmarks are built around clean data and short evaluation loops; agents alone may miss temporal constraints, evidence checks, or review before finalizing outputs. We first formalize next-generation time series tasks as three-component tuples consisting of a task file, a workspace, and a validation interface. We then present AION, a time series harness built from six component groups: agents, skills, rules, memory, evaluation, and protocols. In this harness, we use three design principles: temporal grounding, temporal knowledge-grounded reasoning, and reliability mechanisms such as post-experiment analysis and layered review. A Kaggle Store Sales case study shows that the harness produces more detailed process traces, more artifacts, and more review steps than the same base agent operating in OpenCode direct build mode. Taken together, these results argue for a paradigm shift from fixed tasks to realistic ones under real-world constraints.

</details>


### [114] [Evolutionary Enhanced Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Cooperative Air Combat](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25091)
*Chengwei Li,Junlin Liu,Yang Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ACE-MAPPO: A hybrid MARL framework combining evolutionary algorithms with MAPPO for multi-UCAV air combat decision-making, achieving better training stability, convergence speed, and win rates through genetic soft updates, prioritized trajectory replay, and adversarial curriculum learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern BVR multi-aircraft cooperative engagements pose significant challenges for UCAV autonomous decision-making due to high-dimensional state spaces, discrete action commands, and strongly adversarial dynamic environments. Existing MARL methods suffer from insufficient exploration efficiency, low sample utilization, and poor policy generalization in such complex air combat scenarios.

Method: Proposes ACE-MAPPO, a hybrid learning framework integrating evolutionary algorithms with MAPPO. Key components: 1) Genetic soft update mechanism to enhance population diversity and prevent local optima convergence, 2) Evolutionary-augmented prioritized trajectory replay strategy to better utilize sparse high-value samples, 3) Adversarial evolutionary curriculum learning mechanism for adaptive training with progressively increasing difficulty.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that ACE-MAPPO outperforms MAPPO and other baseline algorithms in training stability, convergence speed, and win rate. The method shows effectiveness in multi-aircraft cooperative air combat scenarios, validating the benefits of the evolutionary-enhanced hybrid approach.

Conclusion: The ACE-MAPPO framework successfully addresses limitations of existing MARL methods for complex UCAV decision-making in BVR multi-aircraft engagements. By integrating evolutionary algorithms with MAPPO, it achieves superior performance through improved exploration, sample efficiency, and adaptive training, making it suitable for challenging adversarial air combat environments.

Abstract: As modern air combat evolves toward beyond-visual-range (BVR) multi-aircraft cooperative engagements, autonomous decision-making for unmanned combat aerial vehicles (UCAVs) faces significant challenges due to high-dimensional state spaces, discrete action commands, and strongly adversarial dynamic environments. To overcome the limitations of existing multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) methods in such settings, namely insufficient exploration efficiency, low sample utilization, and poor policy generalization, we propose Adversarial Curriculum and Evolutionary-enhanced Multi-agent Proximal Policy Optimization (ACE-MAPPO), a hybrid learning framework that integrates evolutionary algorithms with MAPPO. Specifically, a genetic soft update mechanism is introduced to enhance population diversity and mitigate convergence to local optima. An evolutionary-augmented prioritized trajectory replay strategy is further employed to improve the utilization of sparse high-value samples. In addition, an adversarial evolutionary curriculum learning mechanism is designed to enable adaptive training with progressively increasing difficulty. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms MAPPO and other baseline algorithms in terms of training stability, convergence speed, and win rate, validating its effectiveness in multi-aircraft cooperative air combat scenarios.

</details>


### [115] [RECTOR: Priority-Aware Rule-Based Reranking for Compliance-Aware Autonomous Driving Trajectory Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25095)
*Hadi Hajieghrary,Benedikt Walter,Chaitanya Shinde,Paul Schmitt,Miguel Hurtado*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RECTOR is a rule-enforced constrained trajectory orchestrator that reranks autonomous driving trajectory candidates using a tiered rulebook and deterministic lexicographic optimization, significantly improving compliance without retraining the predictor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current autonomous driving systems select trajectories from candidate sets using model confidence alone, ignoring important constraints related to safety, traffic laws, and comfort.

Method: A post-generation reranking layer scores multimodal trajectory candidates using differentiable proxies for a tiered rulebook (Safety > Legal > Road > Comfort) with scene-conditioned applicability, then selects trajectories using deterministic ε-lexicographic optimization that preserves cross-tier priority by construction.

Result: On Waymo Open Motion Dataset validation set, rule-aware selection reduced Safety+Legal violations from 28.58% to 20.42% and Total violations from 40.32% to 32.41% compared to confidence-only selection. Under adversarial confidence corruption, rule-aware selectors rejected problematic modes in ~96% of scenarios while confidence-only failed in 100%.

Conclusion: RECTOR provides a practical post-hoc solution for rule-aware trajectory selection that enhances compliance with safety and traffic rules without retraining prediction models, while offering stronger structural guarantees than weight calibration approaches.

Abstract: Autonomous driving stacks must pick one trajectory from a multi-modal candidate set; choosing by model confidence ignores safety, traffic-law, and comfort constraints. We present \textsc{RECTOR} (Rule-Enforced Constrained Trajectory Orchestrator), a post-generation reranking layer that scores candidates against a tiered rulebook (Safety~$\succ$~Legal~$\succ$~Road~$\succ$~Comfort) via differentiable proxies and a scene-conditioned applicability mechanism, then selects with a deterministic $\varepsilon$-lexicographic rule that preserves cross-tier priority by construction -- without retraining the predictor.
  On the Waymo Open Motion Dataset \texttt{validation\_interactive} split (43{,}219 augmented instances, $K{=}6$), under Protocol~B (28-rule proxy catalog, oracle applicability) rule-aware selection cuts Safety+Legal violations from 28.58\% to 20.42\% and Total from 40.32\% to 32.41\% versus confidence-only on the same candidates. A uniform-weight weighted-sum baseline matches binary compliance on this benchmark -- the empirical lift comes from rule-aware ranking, while the lexicographic guarantee is the structural differentiator no weight calibration can replicate. Under adversarial confidence corruption, confidence-only selection fails in 100\% of scenarios while both rule-aware selectors reject the injected mode in $\sim$96\%. All figures are proxy-evaluator results (not a safety certificate), open-loop, 5\,s horizon, U.S.\ rules, validation split.

</details>


### [116] [Trust but Verify: Prover-Verifier Deliberation for Selective LLM Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25133)
*João Sedoc,Baotong Zhang,Dean Foster*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PVD introduces an interactive proof protocol for selective prediction where models defend answers and assess confidence, separating reliable from unreliable responses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable confidence estimation in language models is crucial for safe deployment; current methods lack structured verification protocols to separate high-confidence from uncertain answers.

Method: Prover-verifier deliberation (PVD) protocol: a prover defends answers with checkable sub-claims while a verifier issues challenges and returns Accept/Challenge/Reject verdicts; uses frozen LLMs as imperfect provers/verifiers over noisy channels.

Result: ANC (Accept + No Change) answers yield ~30pp precision gap over non-ANC; high precision transfers across model families; verifier strictness and domain competence determine selection gap; PVD provides distinct argument-defensibility signal compared to other methods.

Conclusion: PVD enables selective prediction through structured deliberation, separating reliable from unreliable answers; though formal guarantees don't transfer, empirical characterization shows practical utility with careful model pairing and domain considerations.

Abstract: Reliably knowing when a language model is correct is almost as important as being correct. We introduce prover-verifier deliberation (PVD), an inference-time protocol grounded in interactive proof theory, as a mechanism for selective prediction: the protocol produces both an answer and a structured confidence verdict, allowing a system to report high-confidence answers while abstaining on uncertain cases. In each dialogue, a prover defends a candidate answer through checkable sub-claims while a verifier issues targeted challenges and returns \textsc{Accept}, \textsc{Challenge}, or \textsc{Reject}. Because frozen language models are imperfect provers and verifiers operating over a noisy channel, formal soundness and completeness guarantees do not transfer; instead, we characterize the protocol empirically through its coverage-precision behavior. Our main experiment uses Claude Sonnet 4.6 as prover and Claude Haiku 4.5 as verifier on GPQA Diamond. Questions accepted with no answer revision, which we call Accept + No Change (ANC), are reported as the high-confidence subset; we evaluate this subset by its precision and coverage. ANC separates reliable from unreliable answers, yielding a $\sim$30pp HC-Prec gap over the non-ANC complement. Robustness experiments with GPT and Gemini pairings show that high HC-Prec can transfer across model families, while verifier strictness and domain competence largely determine the size of the selection gap. On Humanity's Last Exam, weaker prover-verifier pairings can collapse or invert the ANC signal, illustrating a practical failure mode when the verifier operates outside its effective region. Comparisons with self-consistency, universal self-consistency, multi-agent debate, and Reflexion suggest that prover-verifier deliberation supplies a distinct argument-defensibility signal for selective prediction.

</details>


### [117] [Beyond the Frontier: Stochastic Backtracking for Efficient Test-Time Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25143)
*Dao Tran,Duc Anh Le,Ngoc Luu,Quan Pham,Tung Pham,Hung Bui*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A method using stochastic backtracking over persistent prefix pools improves language model reasoning efficiency by revisiting historical states instead of only expanding current frontier, achieving better accuracy-token trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current test-time scaling methods for language model reasoning use PRM-guided frontier-only search that irreversibly prunes prefixes using noisy scores, leading to premature commitment, diversity collapse, and loss of potentially correct continuations.

Method: Introduces stochastic backtracking over a persistent pool of historical prefixes with two complementary mechanisms: (1) Subpool Selection using Top-N selection within random subpools to give historical prefixes a chance to bypass over-scored frontier candidates, and (2) Power Backtrack Sequential Monte Carlo extending SMC-style resampling to the persistent pool with powered PRM scores and mixture-corrected weights.

Result: Across mathematical reasoning benchmarks and model scales, the methods consistently achieve higher accuracy per token count and reach the same accuracy levels using only a fraction of the token count compared to strong PRM-guided baselines.

Conclusion: Persistent-pool stochastic backtracking provides a simple and effective way to improve the accuracy-token trade-off in test-time scaling for language model reasoning, addressing limitations of frontier-only methods.

Abstract: Test-time scaling improves language model reasoning by spending additional compute to explore multiple solution trajectories. The key challenge is to maximize accuracy while minimizing the total number of generated tokens during reasoning. Recent PRM-guided methods score intermediate prefixes to steer this search, but most are frontier-only: they keep only the current active prefixes and irreversibly prune or resample away the rest using noisy PRM scores. This can cause premature commitment, diversity collapse, and the loss of prefixes that still admit correct continuations. We introduce stochastic backtracking over a persistent pool of historical prefixes, allowing test-time compute to revisit previously generated states instead of only expanding the current frontier. To make this efficient, we propose two complementary mechanisms. Subpool Selection strengthens greedy PRM-guided search by applying Top-N selection within random subpools, giving historical prefixes a chance to bypass over-scored frontier candidates. Power Backtrack Sequential Monte Carlo extends SMC-style resampling to the persistent pool using powered PRM scores and mixture-corrected weights. Across mathematical reasoning benchmarks and model scales, our methods consistently achieve higher accuracy per token count, and the same level of accuracy using only a fraction of the token count in comparison to strong PRM-guided baselines, demonstrating that persistent-pool stochastic backtracking provides a simple and effective way to improve the accuracy-token trade-off in test-time scaling.

</details>


### [118] [Representation Without Control: Testing the Realization Effect in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25151)
*Ciarán Walsh,Emilio Barkett*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs show systematic sensitivity to realization status prompts but don't reproduce human realization effect patterns; internal representations contain decodable realization signals but steering these doesn't reliably affect risk choices, showing behavioral sensitivity, latent readout, and causal control are distinct properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand when LLM outputs reflect human-like cognitive mechanisms vs. prompt-sensitive surface patterns, using the realization effect (systematic differences in risk-taking after paper vs. realized gains/losses) from behavioral economics as a test case.

Method: Three-level evaluation: 1) prompt-only behavioral sensitivity testing, 2) linear readout of internal representations to detect realization-status signals, 3) causal control via activation steering along identified representation directions to test effects on downstream risk choices.

Result: Prompt-only shows systematic condition sensitivity but not human-like directional patterns; Gemma's layer 18 contains linearly decodable realization-status signal generalizing to held-out prompts; however, steering along this direction doesn't reliably shift risk choices, even across positive scales and negative sign-symmetry runs.

Conclusion: Behavioral sensitivity, latent readout, and causal control are three distinct properties that don't automatically co-occur; successful latent readout is insufficient evidence that a model behaviorally relies on a representation during downstream decision-making.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly used as behavioral simulators, but it remains unclear when their outputs reflect human-like cognitive mechanisms rather than prompt-sensitive surface patterns. We study this question through the realization effect, a well-characterized finding in behavioral economics in which risk-taking differs systematically after paper versus realized gains and losses. We evaluate LLM behavior at three levels: prompt-only behavioral sensitivity, linear readout of internal representations, and causal control via activation steering. Prompt-only results show systematic condition sensitivity, but the directional pattern does not reproduce human realization-effect predictions. Gemma's residual stream contains a linearly decodable realization-status signal at layer 18 that generalizes to held-out prompts. Steering along this direction does not, however, reliably shift downstream risk choices, a null result that holds across positive scales and in a negative sign-symmetry run. Behavioral sensitivity, latent readout, and causal control are three distinct properties that do not automatically co-occur, and successful latent readout is insufficient evidence that a model behaviorally relies on a representation during downstream decision-making.

</details>


### [119] [SimuWoB: Simulating Real-World Mobile Apps for Fast and Faithful GUI Agent Benchmarking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25160)
*Guohong Liu,Jialei Ye,Pengzhi Gao,Wei Liu,Jian Luan,Yunxin Liu,Yuanchun Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SimuWoB is a synthetic benchmark for mobile GUI agents with 120 challenging tasks that addresses limitations of existing benchmarks by providing realistic evaluation without requiring real apps or complex rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing mobile GUI agent benchmarks have two main limitations: 1) They're limited to open-source apps or file-operation tasks due to difficulty constructing rewards on real applications, creating a gap between benchmark settings and real-world usage. 2) Most focus on basic grounding and navigation with limited coverage of complex, long-horizon interactions.

Method: Developed SimuWoB with a virtual environment generation framework that synthesizes high-fidelity tasks and environments. The framework automatically provides valid rewards for each task and deploys environments as backend-free webpages accessible via URL for efficient and reproducible evaluation.

Result: Tested several state-of-the-art mobile GUI agents showing average success rate of only 27.92%, dropping to 17.82% on long-horizon tasks. Evaluation results generalize well to real-world tasks, and diagnostic insights across key capability dimensions reveal substantial weaknesses in current agents under complex scenarios.

Conclusion: SimuWoB provides a comprehensive, reproducible synthetic benchmark that reveals significant gaps in current mobile GUI agents' capabilities, particularly for complex, long-horizon tasks, offering valuable insights for future development of more robust agents.

Abstract: Mobile GUI agents powered by large language models have progressed rapidly, creating urgent needs for realistic and comprehensive evaluation. Existing benchmarks prioritize reproducibility but are often limited to open-source apps or file-operation tasks for the difficulty of constructing rewards on real applications, leaving a gap between benchmark settings and real-world usage. Moreover, most benchmarks focus on basic grounding and navigation, with limited coverage of complex, long-horizon interactions. To address these limitations, we introduce SimuWoB, a fully synthetic benchmark for mobile GUI agents with 120 challenging tasks spanning diverse types and difficulty levels. We build a robust virtual environment generation framework that synthesizes high-fidelity tasks and environments, and automatically provides valid rewards for each task. Each environment is deployed as a backend-free webpage accessible via URL, enabling efficient and reproducible evaluation. We conduct comprehensive experiments on several state-of-the-art mobile GUI agents. The average success rate is only 27.92%, dropping to 17.82% on long-horizon tasks, which reveals substantial weaknesses in current agents under complex scenarios. Evaluation result comparison with real-world sample tasks demonstrate that agent assessments based on our synthetic environment generalize well. We further provide diagnostic insights across key capability dimensions and discuss implications for future mobile GUI agent development.

</details>


### [120] [SpecAlign: A Semantic Alignment Framework for SystemVerilog Assertion Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25181)
*Jaime Rafael Imperial,Hao Zheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SpecAlign is a framework for semantic evaluation and refinement of LLM-generated SystemVerilog Assertions that detects inconsistencies and improves alignment with natural language specifications without needing golden RTL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM approaches to SVA generation focus on syntactic validity and formal verification outcomes, but semantic alignment between generated assertions and natural language specifications is difficult to quantify. Hallucinated or misaligned SVAs reduce confidence and increase debugging efforts when golden RTL is unavailable.

Method: Introduces two iterative alignment loops that assess both natural language properties and SVAs against design specification using entailment-based classification. Uses chain-of-thought prompting to generate multiple reasoning paths and aggregates them via self-consistency voting. Misaligned assertions are analyzed to generate actionable feedback for refinement.

Result: SpecAlign effectively detects semantic inconsistencies and improves assertion alignment without relying on golden RTL. The framework provides a scalable complement to traditional formal verification evaluation metrics.

Conclusion: SpecAlign offers a practical solution for evaluating and refining LLM-generated SVAs, addressing the critical gap in semantic alignment assessment and providing valuable feedback for improvement when golden RTL is not available.

Abstract: Existing Large Language Model (LLM) approaches to SystemVerilog Assertion (SVA) generation primarily focus on syntactic validity and formal verification outcomes, while semantic alignment between generated assertions and natural language specifications remains difficult to quantify. As a result, hallucinated or misaligned SVAs can reduce confidence and increase debugging efforts in the absence of golden RTL. This paper presents SpecAlign, a framework for semantic evaluation and refinement of LLM-generated SVAs. SpecAlign introduces two iterative alignment loops that assess both natural language properties and SVAs against the design specification using entailment-based classification. We improve alignment decisions by generating multiple reasoning paths using chain-of-thought prompting and aggregating them via a self-consistency voting mechanism. Misaligned assertions are analyzed to generate actionable feedback for refinement. We further define a quantitative alignment score to measure semantic consistency across iterations. Experimental results demonstrate that SpecAlign effectively detects semantic inconsistencies and improves assertion alignment without relying on golden RTL, providing a scalable complement to traditional formal verification evaluation metrics.

</details>


### [121] [DarkForest: Less Talk, Higher Accuracy for Multi-Agent LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25188)
*Yi Li,Songtao Wei,Dongming Jiang,Zhichun Guo,Qiannan Li,Bingzhe Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DarkForest: A controlled-communication multi-agent LLM framework that clusters agent responses and estimates calibrated beliefs to improve reasoning while reducing communication overhead


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-agent LLM systems suffer from error propagation (incorrect intermediate reasoning gets amplified) and high communication overhead (multi-round exchanges increase token consumption, latency, and costs).

Method: 1. Keep agents independent to produce answers without seeing others' outputs. 2. Parse raw responses into structured candidate records. 3. Group semantically equivalent candidates into clusters. 4. Estimate calibrated belief distribution using multiple factors: agent reliability, confidence, parse quality, support-pattern reliability, and independence corrections. 5. Coordinator receives only policy-permitted evidence from belief state with controlled communication.

Result: Experiments on six reasoning benchmarks show DarkForest achieves leading overall quality, improves the strongest baseline by up to 30.7% on benchmark metrics, and reduces token consumption by up to 6.5× compared with communication-heavy baselines.

Conclusion: DarkForest provides an effective controlled-communication coordination framework that mitigates error propagation while significantly reducing communication overhead in multi-agent LLM reasoning systems.

Abstract: Multi-agent LLM systems improve reasoning by combining outputs from multiple agents, but interaction-heavy methods can introduce error propagation and high communication overhead. When agents exchange raw responses or reasoning traces, incorrect intermediate reasoning may be adopted and amplified, leading to confident but wrong consensus; multi-round communication also increases token consumption, latency, and inference cost. In this paper, we propose a controlled-communication coordination framework named DarkForest. DarkForest first keeps agents independent, so each agent produces an answer without seeing the others' outputs. It then parses the raw responses into structured candidate records, groups semantically equivalent candidates into clusters, and estimates a calibrated belief distribution over these clusters using agent reliability, confidence, parse quality, support-pattern reliability, and independence corrections. A coordinator receives only policy-permitted evidence from this belief state with controlled communication. Experiments on six reasoning benchmarks show that DarkForest achieves leading overall quality, improves the strongest baseline by up to 30.7\% on benchmark metrics, and reduces token consumption by up to $6.5\times$ compared with communication-heavy baselines.

</details>


### [122] [Boosting Inference with Guided Reasoning: Stochastic Exploration for Recursive Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25230)
*Andrew Corbett,Archit Sood,Anna Tzatzopoulou,Sai-Aakash Ramesh,Tim Dodwell*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Viewing deterministic recursive reasoning as approximate inference enables guided stochastic exploration that improves reasoning accuracy on structured tasks like Sudoku, with diagnostics that predict when the approach will help.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to better understand and improve the inference-time behavior of recursive neural architectures on structured reasoning tasks. The authors argue that deterministic recursion represents a one-particle, zero-noise limit of approximate inference over latent reasoning trajectories.

Method: The method proposes guided stochastic exploration: perturbing deterministic reasoning trajectories with noise to propose neighboring trajectories, then reweighting them using the model's existing early-stopping head. This is framed as approximate inference over latent reasoning trajectories, with three diagnostics derived: local stability, guide alignment, and cloud-token entropy.

Result: On Sudoku-Extreme, the approach lifts exact-solve accuracy from 85.9% to 98.0% without retraining. On Maze-Hard, the diagnostics successfully flag a misaligned guide, which is later confirmed by validation performance.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that viewing deterministic recursive reasoning as approximate inference provides a powerful framework for improving reasoning performance through stochastic exploration, with diagnostics that can predict when such improvements are possible and which outputs to trust.

Abstract: Recent work on recursive architectures has shown that tiny neural networks can be surprisingly powerful on structured reasoning tasks. The trick is to model reasoning trajectories with a latent dynamical system. We argue that the inference-time behaviour of these architectures is best understood as approximate inference over latent reasoning trajectories, with deterministic recursion as the one-particle, zero-noise limit. We make this view operational through guided stochastic exploration: stochastic perturbations of the reasoning dynamics propose neighbouring trajectories, and the model's existing early-stopping head reweights them online. The framework yields three label-free diagnostics: local stability, guide alignment, and cloud-token entropy. These predict, from inference traces alone, whether the procedure will help and which of its outputs to trust. On Sudoku-Extreme it lifts exact-solve accuracy from $85.9\%$ to $98.0\%$ without retraining; on Maze-Hard the diagnostics flag a misaligned guide, as validation performance later confirms. The same machinery thus characterises both when recursive reasoning has room to improve at the trajectory level and when the model's internal guide can recover it.

</details>


### [123] [Meta-Agent: From Task Descriptions to Verified Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25233)
*Andy Xu,Yu-Wing Tai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Meta-Agent is a two-phase framework that automatically builds and executes specialized multi-agent systems from natural language task descriptions, featuring planning, grounding, verification, and sophisticated error recovery mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-agent frameworks are brittle and unreliable as workflows scale - small errors propagate, insufficient grounding and weak verification limit reliability. There's a need for more robust multi-agent systems that can handle complex, multi-step tasks reliably.

Method: Two-phase framework: 1) Construction phase: Task planner decomposes problems into DAG of agent specifications with contracts/verification; web search grounds specifications; code generation produces prompts/tools; construction-time verification validates artifacts. 2) Execution phase: Coordinator dispatches subtasks across agent graph; execution-time verification gates outputs. Three-level error attribution distinguishes local, upstream, structural failures with targeted recovery strategies.

Result: Evaluation across coding, contextual learning, and open-ended reasoning tasks shows consistent improvements in task success rate, error recovery, and workflow stability compared to strong multi-agent baselines. Ablation studies validate framework components.

Conclusion: Meta-Agent demonstrates that tightly integrating planning, grounding, and verification is crucial for building reliable multi-agent systems that can handle complex tasks at scale while maintaining stability and recoverability.

Abstract: AI agents are increasingly used to solve complex, multi-step tasks, but existing multi-agent frameworks remain brittle as workflows grow in scale and depth. Small errors at intermediate stages can propagate through agent interactions, while insufficient grounding and weak verification mechanisms further limit reliability. We present Meta-Agent, a two-phase framework that automatically constructs and executes specialized multi-agent systems from natural-language task descriptions. In the construction phase, a task planner decomposes a problem into a directed acyclic graph of agent specifications with explicit input/output contracts and verification criteria. A web search module grounds each specification with external evidence, and a code generation module produces system prompts and tool configurations. A construction-time verification stage then validates generated artifacts and triggers targeted regeneration when failures are detected. In the execution phase, a coordinator dispatches subtasks across the agent graph while execution-time verification gates intermediate outputs. We further introduce a three-level error attribution mechanism that distinguishes local, upstream, and structural failures, enabling targeted recovery strategies ranging from localized retries to partial re-execution and re-decomposition. We evaluate Meta-Agent across coding, contextual learning, and open-ended reasoning tasks. Experiments against strong multi-agent baselines and ablation studies demonstrate consistent improvements in task success rate, error recovery, and workflow stability. The results highlight the importance of tightly integrating planning, grounding, and verification for building reliable multi-agent systems.

</details>


### [124] [FrontierOR: Benchmarking LLMs' Capacity for Efficient Algorithm Design in Large-Scale Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25246)
*Minwei Kong,Chonghe Jiang,Ao Qu,Wenbin Ouyang,Zhaoming Zeng,Xiaotong Guo,Zhekai Li,Junyi Li,Yi Fan,Xinshou Zheng,Xi Jing,Yikai Zhang,Zhiwei Liang,Seonghoo Kim,Runqing Yang,Zijian Zhou,Sirui Li,Han Zheng,Wangyang Ying,Ou Zheng,Chonghuan Wang,Jinglong Zhao,Hanzhang Qin,Cathy Wu,Paul Pu Liang,Jinhua Zhao,Hai Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FrontierOR is a new benchmark for evaluating LLM capabilities in designing efficient optimization algorithms for realistic large-scale problems, showing current frontier models still struggle to outperform commercial solvers like Gurobi in most cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM benchmarks for optimization are limited to small/simplified examples, while real-world operations research requires designing scalable algorithms that exploit problem structure. There's a gap in evaluating LLMs on practical large-scale optimization algorithm design.
