Method: Created FrontierOR benchmark with 180 tasks derived from diverse papers in top-tier OR venues, each with standardized instances and expert-verified evaluation suite. Evaluated 7 LLMs (frontier, cost-effective, open-source) in one-shot and test-time evolution settings.

Result: Strongest one-shot model outperformed Gurobi in only 31% of cases on both solution quality and computational efficiency. Even with test-time evolution, coding agents achieved only 50% on selected hard tasks. Models struggle to move from correct formulations to efficient algorithms.

Conclusion: FrontierOR provides a practical evaluation platform for LLM-based optimization algorithm design, enabling systematic testing of whether LLMs can progress beyond correct formulation toward feasible, high-quality, and efficient algorithms for real-world problems.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for optimization modeling and solver-code generation, yet practical operations research and optimization problems often require a harder capability: designing scalable algorithms that exploit problem structure and outperform direct formulation-and-solve baselines. Existing benchmarks are limited to small or simplified examples far below real-world scale and complexity. We introduce FrontierOR, among the first benchmarks to systematically evaluate LLM-based efficient algorithm design for realistic large-scale optimization problems. FrontierOR includes 180 tasks derived from methodologically diverse papers published in top-tier operations research venues, each with standardized instances and a hidden, expert-verified evaluation suite. We evaluate seven LLMs spanning frontier, cost-effective, and open-source models both in one-shot and test-time evolution settings. The results reveal that frontier models still struggle to move from executable formulations to efficient optimization algorithms: the strongest one-shot model outperforms Gurobi in only 31% of cases in both solution quality and computational efficiency, and even strong coding agents with test-time evolution achieve only 50% on selected hard tasks. FrontierOR establishes a practical evaluation platform for LLM-based optimization algorithm design, which enables future LLMs and agents to be systematically tested on whether they can move beyond correct formulation toward a feasible, high-quality, and efficient algorithm. Our FrontierOR Benchmark is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/efficient-opt-bench-F03D.

</details>


### [125] [LipoAgent: Coordinating Fine-Tuned LLM Agents for Safer Lipid Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25250)
*Leshu Li,An Lu,Haiyu Wang,Zhibin Feng,Conghui Duan,Qing Bao,Zongmin Zhao,Sai Qian Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LipoAgent is a safety-aware multi-agent LLM framework that improves lipid nanoparticle design by making toxicity a prerequisite for efficiency prediction, achieving 32% better mRNA transfection efficiency prediction and validated by wet-lab experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) are clinically advanced for nucleic acid delivery, designing lipids that are both effective and biologically safe remains challenging. In practical screening, toxicity acts as a decision-level constraint - if a lipid is toxic, its efficiency prediction becomes clinically irrelevant, creating a bottleneck in lipid discovery.

Method: The authors propose LipoAgent, a safety-aware multi-agent LLM framework that combines domain-specific finetuning with a conditional prediction objective enforcing toxicity as a prerequisite for efficiency prediction. It further improves reliability through multi-agent verification with lightweight human oversight when disagreements persist between agents.

Result: LipoAgent achieves an average 32% relative improvement in mRNA transfection efficiency prediction compared to other reported models for lipid design across multiple foundation models. Wet-lab validation confirms that virtual screening rankings reliably translate to biological transfection outcomes.

Conclusion: LipoAgent successfully addresses the safety-efficacy trade-off in lipid nanoparticle design by implementing a novel conditional prediction framework that prioritizes toxicity assessment, demonstrating both computational improvement and real-world translational validity through experimental confirmation.

Abstract: Lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) are among the most clinically mature platforms for nucleic acid delivery, yet designing lipids that are both effective and biologically safe remains a major bottleneck. In practical screening, toxicity is a decision-level constraint: if a lipid is toxic, its efficiency prediction is clinically irrelevant. We propose LipoAgent, a safety-aware multi-agent LLM framework for lipid discovery. LipoAgent combines domain-specific finetuning with a conditional prediction objective that enforces toxicity as a prerequisite for efficiency prediction, and further improves reliability via multi-agent verification with lightweight human oversight when disagreement persists. Across multiple foundation models, LipoAgent achieves an average 32% relative improvement in mRNA transfection efficiency prediction compared with other reported models for lipid design. Wet-lab validation confirms that virtual screening rankings reliably translate to biological transfection outcomes. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/SAI-Lab-NYU/LipoAgent.git.

</details>


### [126] [Whose Alignment? Comparing LLM Process Alignment Across Diverse Organizational Decision Contexts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25256)
*Niklas Weller,Emilio Barkett*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that aligning AI systems with organizations requires measuring process alignment (how decisions are made) rather than just output agreement, showing this approach works well for legal decisions but fails for contested domains where historical patterns may be discriminatory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to challenge the common framing of AI alignment as merely making models behave like organizations. The authors argue this single-target approach obscures a deeper pluralistic challenge - whether models actually internalize organizational decision-making processes or just approximate outcomes.

Method: The method uses a decision-policy capturing approach to measure process alignment (how LLMs weight information) rather than just output agreement. They apply this to two domains: ECHR Article 6 decisions (European Court of Human Rights) and German consumer credit decisions, comparing alignment metrics and testing externalization interventions.

Result: For ECHR Article 6 decisions, process alignment strongly predicts output accuracy (r = 0.85, p < .001) and externalization improves alignment for poorly-aligned models. For German consumer credit decisions, this relationship collapses (r = 0.15, p = .60) with inconsistent intervention effects, and the benchmark encodes potentially discriminatory historical patterns.

Conclusion: High process alignment is neither always achievable via externalization nor unconditionally desirable, especially in contested domains. Output agreement alone cannot distinguish between models that have internalized organizational policy versus those merely approximating outcomes. Process-level measurement is necessary for meaningful pluralistic alignment evaluation.

Abstract: Aligning AI systems with organizational decision-making is typically framed as a single-target problem: make the model behave like the organization. We argue this framing obscures a deeper pluralistic challenge. We rely on a decision-policy capturing method to measure process alignment: whether an LLM weights information as the organization does, not merely whether it reaches the same conclusions. Applying this method to ECHR Article 6 decisions, process alignment strongly predicts output accuracy (r = 0.85, p < .001) and externalization substantially improves alignment for poorly-aligned models. Applying it to German consumer credit decisions, this relationship collapses (r = 0.15, p = .60): interventions produce inconsistent effects and the benchmark encodes potentially discriminatory historical patterns. This contrast is itself a pluralistic alignment finding: in contested domains, high process alignment is neither achievable via externalization nor unconditionally desirable. Output agreement alone cannot distinguish a model that has internalized an organizational policy from one that merely approximates its outcomes; process-level measurement is a necessary component of any pluralistic alignment evaluation.

</details>


### [127] [AI Cartography: Mapping the Latent Landscape of AI Benchmark Ecosystems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25272)
*Michael Hardy,Anka Reuel,Lijin Zhang,Jodi M. Casabianca,Sang Truong,Yash Dave,Hansol Lee,Benjamin Domingue,Sanmi Koyejo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Current AI benchmark rankings contain substantial noise that isn't properly quantified, making it unclear when scores reflect genuine capability differences vs. measurement artifacts. The authors develop a framework to measure latent structures in benchmark ecosystems using factor analysis and find significant issues with current evaluation practices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While aggregate leaderboard scores drive AI development, they contain substantial measurement noise whose sources and magnitudes remain unquantified. This makes it unclear when rankings reflect genuine capability differences versus evaluation artifacts, raising concerns about the reliability of current benchmarking practices.

Method: The authors introduce a framework for measuring latent landscapes in AI benchmark ecosystems using Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA) and Generalizability Theory. They apply this framework to over 4,000 models from the Open LLM Leaderboard to decompose sources of ranking variance.

Result: Key findings: (1) Current reporting practices underestimate benchmark relationships; (2) Evidence of local dependence among leaderboard items undermines benchmarks as measurement instruments; (3) Contributor metadata explains more rank-relevant variance (~9%) than architecture or deployment categories; (4) Manifest-score "scaling law" slope has low reliability (R_β=0.53) while latent general-factor size slope is highly stable (R_g=0.97). The framework also identifies which benchmarks are functions of LLM size vs. post-training practices.

Conclusion: The study provides actionable diagnostics for determining how benchmark rankings can be trusted and how benchmark design can be improved. It reveals significant measurement issues in current AI evaluation practices and offers a framework for better understanding and quantifying the reliability of benchmark scores.

Abstract: While aggregate leaderboard scores drive AI development, they contain substantial measurement noise whose sources and magnitudes remain unquantified, making it unclear when rankings reflect genuine capability differences versus evaluation artifacts. We introduce a framework for measuring the latent landscape in AI benchmark ecosystems. Applying Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA) and Generalizability Theory to 4,000+ models from the Open LLM Leaderboard, we decompose sources of ranking variance and establish: (1) structures assumed in current reporting practice underestimate the strength of relationships between benchmarks; (2) evidence of local dependence among leaderboard items, undermining uses of benchmarks as measurement instruments under current scoring systems; (3) contributor metadata explains more rank-relevant variance ($\approx9\%$) than architecture or deployment categories in this context; (4) a manifest-score "scaling law" slope has low reliability ($R_β=0.53$); by contrast, the latent general-factor size slope is highly stable across ecosystem controls ($R_g=0.97$). We are able to provide unique insights into benchmark dynamics, such as which benchmarks are a function of LLM size and which can be oppositely impacted by post-training practices. We provide actionable diagnostics to determine how benchmark rankings can be trusted and how benchmark design can be improved.

</details>


### [128] [Context-CoT: Enhancing Context Learning via High-Quality Reasoning Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25354)
*Hongbo Jin,Mingnan Zhu,Jingqi Tian,Xu Jiang,Zhongjing Du,Haoran Tang,Siyi Xie,Qiaoman Zhang,Jiayu Ding*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs are poor at dynamically learning and applying new knowledge from complex contexts, solving only 17.2% of context-dependent tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLMs excel at using static pretrained knowledge but have significant limitations in dynamic context learning - the ability to extract, internalize, and apply new knowledge from complex, task-specific contexts. This represents a critical capability gap in their reasoning abilities.

Method: The paper uses the CL-Bench evaluation framework to assess LLMs' context learning capabilities. This benchmark measures how well models can dynamically extract and apply new knowledge from complex contexts rather than relying on static pretrained knowledge.

Result: Frontier LLMs solve only 17.2% of context-dependent tasks on average according to the CL-Bench evaluation, revealing a severe capability gap in dynamic context learning.

Conclusion: There is a critical need for improved context learning capabilities in LLMs, as current models demonstrate significant limitations in dynamically acquiring and applying new knowledge from complex contexts, which is essential for more sophisticated reasoning tasks.

Abstract: While LLMs excel at reasoning over prompts using static pretrained knowledge, they struggle significantly with context learning-the ability to dynamically extract, internalize, and apply new knowledge from complex, task-specific contexts. Recent evaluations on the CL-Bench reveal a critical capability gap: frontier models solve only 17.2% of context-dependent tasks on average.

</details>


### [129] [Second Guess: Detecting Uncertainty Through Abstention and Answer Stability in Small Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25394)
*Ashwath Vaithinathan Aravindan,Mayank Kejriwal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Second Guess is a lightweight prompting technique that helps small language models abstain from answering when uncertain in multiple-choice questions by analyzing response consistency when an "I don't know" option is added.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models often generate confident but incorrect answers instead of abstaining when uncertain. This problem is especially critical for small language models (SLMs) where computational constraints and autonomous operation demand reliable uncertainty detection, but existing methods like entropy-based approaches don't work well for fine-tuned models.

Method: A parameter-free prompting technique called Second Guess that exploits the insight that models truly knowing an answer will select it consistently, while uncertain models exhibit unstable behavior when an "I don't know" option is added to multiple-choice questions.

Result: Achieved highest composite risk improvement of 10.81% across four open models (2B-8B parameters) and four benchmarks. Maintains 8% composite risk improvement on fine-tuned models where entropy-based methods degrade, and improves most for lower-performing models.

Conclusion: Second Guess is an effective, lightweight approach for improving uncertainty detection and abstention in small language models for MCQA tasks, particularly valuable for resource-constrained settings where traditional methods fail.

Abstract: Large language models often generate confident but incorrect answers rather than abstaining when uncertain. This problem is particularly acute for small language models (SLMs), where computational constraints and autonomous operation amplify the need for reliable uncertainty detection. We propose _Second Guess_, a lightweight, parameter-free prompting technique for abstention in multiple-choice question answering (MCQA) that is well-suited for SLMs. Our key empirical insight is that models which truly know an answer will select it consistently, while uncertain models exhibit unstable behavior when an ``I don't know'' option is added. Evaluated on four open models (2B-8B parameters) and four benchmarks, Second Guess achieves the highest composite risk improvement of 10.81\%. Notably, it maintains an 8\% composite risk improvement on fine-tuned models where entropy-based methods degrade, and improves most for lower-performing models. All code and results required to reproduce this work is available in https://github.com/Mystic-Slice/second-guess

</details>


### [130] [Towards end-to-end LLM-based censoring-aware survival analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25399)
*Yishu Wei,Hexin Dong,Yi Lin,Jiahe Qian,Yi Liu,Yifan Peng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMSurvival enables large language models to perform censoring-aware survival analysis by reformulating time-to-event prediction as pairwise ranking among comparable subjects, achieving improved performance over traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Survival analysis is important for medical prediction, but large language models (LLMs) are rarely used as end-to-end survival models because censoring (incomplete follow-up data) prevents straightforward supervised fine-tuning of LLMs.

Method: LLMSurvival reformulates time-to-event prediction as pairwise ranking among comparable subjects, deriving test-time risk by aggregating comparisons against anchor individuals from the training cohort. It enables LLMs to work directly with tabular clinical data without model modification.

Result: Across two clinical tasks (ICU mortality prediction in MIMIC-IV and fragility fracture prediction), LLMSurvival improved overall concordance over Cox proportional hazards modeling by 3.1% for ICU mortality and 0.5% for fracture risk, and outperformed three established deep learning survival models by 2.1% on average for ICU mortality and 2.8% for fracture risk. It also demonstrated superior performance over expert-curated scores like SAPS-II and FRAX.

Conclusion: LLMSurvival serves as a proof of concept for an integrated, censoring-conscious approach to survival analysis via LLMs, showing high portability across clinical contexts and supporting local deployment with compact, publicly available base models.

Abstract: Objective: Survival analysis is central to medical prediction, yet large language models (LLMs) are rarely used as end-to-end survival models because censoring prevents straightforward supervised fine-tuning. Here we present LLMSurvival, a framework that enables censoring-aware survival analysis with unmodified LLMs operating directly on tabular clinical data.
  Materials and Methods: LLMSurvival reformulates time-to-event prediction as pairwise ranking among comparable subjects, and derives test-time risk by aggregating comparisons against anchor individuals from the training cohort.
  Results: Across two clinical tasks (ICU mortality prediction in MIMIC-IV and fragility fracture prediction in a NewYork-Presbyterian/Weill Cornell Medicine cohort), LLMSurvival improves overall concordance over Cox proportional hazards modeling by 3.1% for ICU mortality and 0.5% for fracture risk, 2.1% on average for ICU mortality and 2.8% for fracture risk over three established deep learning survival models.
  Discussion: The results show that survival modeling with censoring can be made compatible with LLM fine-tuning through comparison-based reformulation. The framework demonstrates high portability and superior performance over expert curated scores like SAPS-II and FRAX scores across diverse clinical context. Furthermore, the framework supports local deployment, as compact, publicly available base models provide sufficient performance.
  Conclusion: The LLMSurvival framework serves as a proof of concept for an integrated, censoring-conscious approach to survival analysis via LLMs.

</details>


### [131] [CODESKILL: Learning Self-Evolving Skills for Coding Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25430)
*Yanzhou Li,Yiran Zhang,Xiaoyu Zhang,Xiaoxia Liu,Yang Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CODESKILL trains an LLM-based learnable policy to extract and maintain reusable procedural skills from coding agent trajectories, improving downstream agent performance significantly across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing skill construction and maintenance methods rely on heuristic updates and fixed prompts, making knowledge selection, abstraction, and maintenance suboptimal for downstream coding agents.

Method: CODESKILL uses LLMs to reframe skill extraction and maintenance as a learnable policy, trains it with RL using hybrid rewards (dense rubric-based skill quality feedback and sparse execution feedback), and supports multi-granularity procedural skill extraction.

Result: Across EnvBench, SWE-Bench Verified, and Terminal-Bench 2, CODESKILL increases average pass rates by 9.69% over baseline without skills and by 4.01% over the strongest prompt-based/memory baseline, while keeping skill banks stable in size during iterative construction.

Conclusion: CODESKILL successfully demonstrates that learnable skill management policies extract reusable procedural skills from coding agent trajectories, effectively improving downstream agent performance while maintaining compact skill banks.

Abstract: Coding agents produce rich trajectories while solving software-engineering tasks. To enable agent self-evolution, these trajectories can be distilled into reusable procedural skills that compactly encode experience to guide future behavior. However, existing skill construction and maintenance methods often rely on fixed prompts and heuristic update rules, leaving it unclear how knowledge should be selected, abstracted, and maintained to best serve downstream agents. We propose CODESKILL, an LLM-based framework that reformulates skill extraction and skill-bank maintenance as a learnable management policy. CODESKILL extracts multi-granularity procedural skills from coding-agent trajectories, evolves skills with new experience, and maintains a compact skill bank for future task solving. We train CODESKILL with reinforcement learning, using a hybrid reward that combines dense rubric-based skill-quality feedback with sparse verifiable execution feedback from the frozen downstream agent. Experiments on EnvBench, SWE-Bench Verified, and Terminal-Bench 2 show that CODESKILL improves average pass rate by 9.69 over the no-skill baseline and by 4.01 over the strongest prompt-based or memory baseline, while maintaining the skill bank at a stable size during iterative construction.

</details>


### [132] [Security of OpenClaw Agents: Fundamentals, Attacks, and Countermeasures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25435)
*Yuntao Wang,Jianle Ba,Han Liu,Yanghe Pan,Jintao Wei,Zhou Su,Tom H. Luan,Linkang Du*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This survey paper comprehensively analyzes security threats and defenses in OpenClaw agents - a new class of open-source LLM-driven autonomous agent frameworks with persistent memory and high autonomy that create expanded attack surfaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OpenClaw agents represent a significant evolution in LLM-driven autonomous systems with capabilities like continuous operation, persistent memory, multi-channel interaction, and high autonomy. While enabling complex multi-step tasks and external application interaction, these capabilities substantially enlarge the attack surface, exposing agents to emerging threats like skill poisoning, cognitive manipulation, cascading failures, and supply-chain vulnerabilities.

Method: The survey employs a layered framework approach: 1) Examining OpenClaw agent architecture and distinguishing characteristics from traditional AI systems, 2) Categorizing security/privacy threats into a structured framework, 3) Analyzing how vulnerabilities arise during agent reasoning, action execution, and external interaction, and 4) Reviewing representative defense mechanisms.

Result: The paper presents a comprehensive security landscape analysis of OpenClaw agents, identifying key architectural features that create vulnerabilities, categorizing emerging threats systematically, and mapping the current defense mechanisms while highlighting unresolved reliability and trustworthiness issues.

Conclusion: OpenClaw agents introduce novel security challenges due to their persistent memory, high autonomy, and external interaction capabilities. While the survey maps existing threats and defenses, significant unresolved issues remain regarding the reliability and trustworthiness of OpenClaw ecosystems, requiring further research and security considerations.

Abstract: The rapid evolution of large language model (LLM)-driven autonomous agents has given rise to OpenClaw, a new class of open-source agent frameworks that operate as continuously running, skill-augmented systems with persistent memory, multi-channel interaction, and high degrees of autonomy. Such capabilities enable OpenClaw agents to autonomously execute complex, multi-step tasks and interact seamlessly with external applications, but simultaneously introduce a substantially enlarged attack surface. In particular, the combination of high-privilege operations and persistent memory exposes OpenClaw agents to various emerging threats, including skill poisoning, cognitive manipulation, multi-agent cascading failures, and supply-chain vulnerabilities. In this survey, we present a comprehensive study of the security landscape of OpenClaw agents. We first examine the general architecture and key characteristics that distinguish OpenClaw agents from traditional AI agent systems. We categorize existing security and privacy threats into a layered framework and analyze how vulnerabilities arise during agent reasoning, action execution, and external interaction. Representative defense mechanisms are also reviewed to draw the current defense landscape. Finally, several unresolved issues related to the reliability and trustworthiness of OpenClaw ecosystems are discussed.

</details>


### [133] [A Signal-Language Foundation Model for Broad-Spectrum Cardiovascular Assessment from Routine Electrocardiography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25446)
*Ziqing Yu,Yuhui Tao,Jiayu Huo,Lei Pan,Zilong Xiao,Juecheng Chen,Xiao Li,Jianxuan Li,You Zhou,Zhixing Li,Cong Wang,Beijian Zhang,Chen Chen,Hongyang Lu,Konstantinos Patlatzoglou,Daniel B. Kramer,Jonathan W. Waks,Yangang Su,Fu Siong Ng,Shuo Wang,Yixiu Liang,Junbo Ge*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ECGCLIP is a signal-language contrastive learning framework that aligns ECG waveforms with diagnostic reports, enabling broad cardiovascular assessment and rare disease detection from routine ECGs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional AI ECG models are limited to common arrhythmias, generalize poorly across populations, and miss clinically subtle or rare diseases.

Method: Developed ECG Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (ECGCLIP) using contrastive learning to align ECG waveforms with expert diagnostic reports. Pre-trained on 2.8M ECG studies from 1.3M patients, then evaluated on 1.5M ECGs across 89 downstream tasks.

Result: ECGCLIP consistently outperformed baselines on internal and external tests (9 cohorts). Achieved strong performance for atrial fibrillation (PRAUC 0.900) and improved detection of rare diseases (Ebstein anomaly: 0.253). Was data-efficient, matching baseline performance with only 10% training data.

Conclusion: Large-scale ECG-report contrastive pre-training can expand routine ECG interpretation beyond common arrhythmias to broad cardiovascular assessment and opportunistic screening of echocardiographic and rare conditions.

Abstract: Electrocardiography (ECG) is central to cardiovascular care, but conventional AI models are often restricted to common arrhythmias and may generalize poorly across populations or clinically subtle diseases. We developed ECG Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (ECGCLIP), a signal-language contrastive learning framework that aligns ECG waveforms with expert diagnostic reports. ECGCLIP was pre-trained on 2,837,962 ECG studies from 1,324,856 patients and evaluated on a held-out internal test set plus nine independent external cohorts comprising about 1.5 million ECGs. Evaluation covered 89 downstream tasks, including 45 ECG diagnoses, 39 echocardiographic targets, and 5 rare cardiac diseases, using PRAUC as the primary metric. ECGCLIP consistently improved performance over random initialization and Merl-R18 baselines. On the internal test set, ECGCLIP-R34 achieved strong performance for atrial fibrillation (PRAUC 0.900) and ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction (PRAUC 0.383), with robust generalization across all external cohorts. It also improved low-prevalence and diagnostically elusive diseases, including Ebstein anomaly, constrictive pericarditis, dextrocardia, and cardiac amyloidosis, with internal PRAUC values of 0.253, 0.175, 0.121, and 0.201, respectively. ECGCLIP was data efficient, matching or exceeding full-dataset baseline performance with only 10% of training data. Feature visualization and saliency analysis suggested clinically meaningful representations aligned with established electrocardiographic criteria. These findings indicate that large-scale ECG-report contrastive pre-training can expand routine ECG interpretation beyond common arrhythmias toward broad cardiovascular assessment and opportunistic screening of echocardiographic and rare conditions.

</details>


### [134] [ATWL: A Formal Language for Representing, Comparing, and Reusing Visual Analytics Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25489)
*Natalia Andrienko,Gennady Andrienko,Jürgen Bernard,Michael Sedlmair*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ATWL is a formal, domain-agnostic language for representing visual analytics workflows that enables systematic comparison, reuse, and knowledge extraction from unstructured research papers using LLM agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visual analytics workflows are complex but typically described in unstructured prose, which hinders systematic comparison, reuse of proven strategies, and training of novices. Current descriptions lack formal structure and standardization.

Method: Created Artifact-Transform Workflow Language (ATWL) based on a modular ontology of eight artifact types and standardized transforms. Used LLM agents to extract workflows from research papers with minimal human intervention, creating a library of 17 workflows.

Result: Successfully extracted workflows from published papers and identified structural regularities invisible in prose. Formal representation enabled explicit iteration structure, typed data flow, fragment-level adaptation provenance, and compactness. In experiments, ATWL outperformed prose libraries in LLM context for analytical problem-solving.

Conclusion: ATWL enables the transition from narrative descriptions to formally represented, comparable, and reusable analytical knowledge, addressing fundamental limitations in how visual analytics workflows are documented and shared.

Abstract: Visual analytics (VA) workflows are inherently complex, involving data transformation, feature engineering, visual representation, and human interpretation. They are typically described in unstructured prose, hindering systematic comparison, reuse of proven strategies, and training of novices. We present Artifact-Transform Workflow Language (ATWL), a domain-agnostic, declarative language that formally represents VA workflows by capturing their structure and underlying analytical intent. ATWL is built upon a modular ontology of eight artifact types (entities, features, arrangements, visualisations, patterns, models, knowledge, specifications) and transforms characterised by standardised intents (e.g., define-unit, characterise, contextualise, abstract). To show that formalisation effort need not impede adoption, we extract workflows from research papers through supervised interaction with LLM agents, reducing the human role to review and refinement. Using this process, we constructed a library of seventeen ATWL workflows from published VA papers. Cross-workflow analysis reveals structural regularities -- a recurrent meta-structure, recurring motifs, reusable building blocks, diverse iterative strategies, and cross-domain equivalences -- that remain invisible in prose. We further evaluate practical utility through a controlled experiment in which the same LLM addressed two analytical problems with the library supplied either as original papers or as ATWL representations. Both forms enabled useful recommendations, but the formal representation systematically added explicit iteration structure, typed data flow, fragment-level adaptation provenance, and compactness supporting scaling beyond what prose libraries can fit in an LLM's context. ATWL enables a transition from narrative descriptions to formally represented, comparable, and reusable analytical knowledge.

</details>


### [135] [Credit Assignment with Resets in Language Model Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25507)
*Ankur Samanta,Akshayaa Magesh,Ayush Jain,Youliang Yu,Daniel Jiang,Kavosh Asadi,Daniel Jiang,Kaveh Hassani,Paul Sajda,Jalaj Bhandari,Yonathan Efroni*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Two reset-based policy optimization methods (RRPO and SRPO) improve credit assignment in reasoning tasks by resetting to intermediate states, with SRPO outperforming standard methods through self-localization of errors and sampling multiple continuations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current reinforcement learning for language models uses uniform reward assignment across all tokens in reasoning trajectories, which ignores which specific steps contributed to success or failure. This limits targeted refinement of faulty reasoning.

Method: Two methods: Random-Reset Policy Optimization (RRPO) where reset states are randomly drawn from reasoning steps, and Self-Reset Policy Optimization (SRPO) where the model self-localizes erroneous steps and resets there. Both methods use resets to enable precise credit assignment by returning to intermediate states and resampling counterfactual continuations.

Result: SRPO consistently outperforms standard GRPO and RRPO across models and reasoning benchmarks by sampling multiple suffix continuations at self-localized resets and learning from their rewards, requiring no external supervision.

Conclusion: Reset mechanisms enable better credit assignment for language model reasoning, with self-localized resets (SRPO) providing provable improvements over random resets within the Conservative Policy Iteration framework, leading to more effective targeted refinement of reasoning steps.

Abstract: Contemporary reinforcement learning with verifiable reward methods post-train language models on multi-step reasoning by assigning a single outcome reward uniformly across all tokens in a trajectory. Such uniform assignment ignores which steps contributed to success or failure. Improving credit assignment can address this limitation by enabling targeted refinement of faulty reasoning steps, rather than updating entire trajectories uniformly. Resets are one such simple mechanism, enabling more precise credit assignment by returning to an intermediate state and resampling counterfactual continuations, so that outcome differences can be attributed to decisions made at that point. We propose two such methods: Random-Reset Policy Optimization (RRPO), where reset states are drawn randomly from reasoning steps, and Self-Reset Policy Optimization (SRPO), where the model self-localizes the erroneous step in an incorrect trajectory and resets there. We analyze these methods within the Conservative Policy Iteration (CPI) framework. Extending CPI with a credit-assignment oracle that targets improvable states yields provable improvements over random resets. Across models and reasoning benchmarks, SRPO consistently outperforms standard GRPO and RRPO by sampling multiple suffix continuations at a self-localized reset and learning from their rewards, using only the model itself with no external supervision.

</details>


### [136] [What Gets Cited: Competitive GEO in AI Answer Engines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25517)
*Rahul Vishwakarma,Shushant Kumar,Ratnesh Jamidar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI answer engines cite few sources, making visibility depend on being cited. Researchers study competitive Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) to determine what makes one source more likely to be cited first when two are retrieved.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI answer engines generate answers from retrieved pages but cite only a few sources, making visibility depend not just on ranking but on being cited. This creates a competitive environment where sources need to optimize for citation likelihood rather than just retrieval ranking.

Method: Built a controlled two-document retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) testbed that injects exactly two candidate sources into model context and measures which source gets the first citation. Conducted 252,000 trials across six LLMs using repeated paired comparisons over 18 content factors, with brand anonymization and counterbalanced source order to isolate content effects from position bias.

Result: Mixed-effects models showed that topical relevance and list position are the biggest drivers of being cited first. Including explicit price information and recent timestamps also helps consistently. Completeness and trust cues add smaller gains, while formatting-only edits have little impact.

Conclusion: Researchers released a reproducible evaluation protocol and prioritized GEO checklist for practitioners, which was tested in an internal pilot at Sprinklr with positive qualitative feedback on workflow usability. The study provides empirical evidence on factors influencing citation priority in AI answer engines.

Abstract: AI answer engines generate answers from retrieved pages but cite only a few sources. This makes visibility depend not just on ranking, but on being cited. We study competitive Generative Engine Optimization (GEO): when two retrieved candidates compete, what makes one more likely to be cited first? We build a controlled two-document retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) testbed that injects exactly two candidate sources into the model context and measures which source is referenced by the first citation marker in the output. Across six LLMs we execute 252,000 trials, repeated paired comparisons under one factorial program over 18 content factors. In each trial the two sources differ in exactly one factor; we use brand anonymization and counterbalanced source order to separate content effects from position bias. Mixed-effects models show that topical relevance and list position are the biggest drivers of being cited first. Including explicit price information and a recent timestamp also helps consistently. Completeness and trust cues add smaller gains, while formatting-only edits have little impact. We release a reproducible evaluation protocol and a prioritized GEO checklist for practitioners, and we exercised it in an early internal pilot at Sprinklr, where teams reported positive qualitative feedback on workflow usability.

</details>


### [137] [StructBreak: Structural Cognitive Overload-Induced Safety Failures in MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25534)
*Yang Luo,Xinran Liu,Tiantian Ji,Zhiyi Yin,Lingyun Peng,Shuyu Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: StructBreak framework reveals Structural Cognitive Overload in MLLMs, showing how structural consistency attacks trigger toxic outputs (92% avg ASR) despite safety alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal LLMs excel at structural reasoning but suffer from logical brittleness in structural consistency, termed Structural Cognitive Overload (SCO). Prior work focused on typographic/pixel perturbations, leaving SCO unexplored.

Method: Proposed StructBreak, an automated end-to-end framework to quantify SCO, enabling discovery of higher-order cognitive overload attacks under black-box settings. Created benchmark spanning 10 diverse threat scenarios and evaluated 6 leading MLLMs.

Result: SCO readily triggers toxic generation with 92% average Attack Success Rate (up to 97% on Gemini 2.5). Structural analysis reveals StructBreak acts as novel structural channel to circumvent safety filters.

Conclusion: Current safety alignment paradigms are insufficient for complex multimodal reasoning era. Limited efficacy of inherent safety mechanisms highlights need for new approaches to address structural cognitive vulnerabilities.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) excel at structural reasoning yet suffer from a sharp logical brittleness in structural consistency. We term this phenomenon Structural Cognitive Overload (SCO), a byproduct of the contention between deep reasoning and safety alignment. However, prior work has predominantly targeted typographic and pixel-level perturbations, leaving the study of SCO largely unexplored. To this end, we propose StructBreak, an automated end-to-end framework designed to quantify SCO. By leveraging StructBreak, we uncover a novel higher-order cognitive overload attack paradigm; notably, this attack operates under a practical black-box setting, requiring no internal model access. Consequently, we utilize this framework to establish a comprehensive benchmark spanning ten diverse threat scenarios. Empirical evaluations on six leading MLLMs reveal that SCO readily triggers toxic generation, yielding a 92% average ASR (up to 97% on Gemini 2.5). To elucidate the mechanism of SCO, we further conduct model-level interpretations spanning attention dynamics, latent space topology, and geometric analysis. Our findings reveal that StructBreak acts as a novel structural channel to circumvent safety filters. Furthermore, the limited efficacy of inherent safety mechanisms underscores that current alignment paradigms are insufficient for the era of complex multimodal reasoning.

</details>


### [138] [Personalize-then-Store: Benchmarking and Learning Personalized Memory for Long-horizon Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25535)
*Yeonjun In,Wonjoong Kim,Sangwu Park,Kanghoon Yoon,Chanyoung Park*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs need personalized memory systems because current universal static policies waste memory on transient interactions while missing critical long-term context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based memory systems use universal static policies that don't account for individual user differences, wasting limited memory budget on transient interactions while failing to preserve critical context for long-horizon tasks.

Method: 1) Introduce PerMemBench, the first benchmark for evaluating personalized memory systems with multi-year, multi-domain interaction histories across diverse user personas. 2) Propose session-level storage gating, a lightweight framework that selectively bypasses memory operations for transient sessions.

Result: Personalization yields substantial retention gains under perfect gating, but accurate gating remains an open and critical challenge.

Conclusion: LLM-based memory systems can benefit from personalization, but effective implementation requires solving the gating accuracy problem to determine which sessions should be stored in memory versus bypassed.

Abstract: Existing large language model (LLM) based memory systems apply universal, static policies that overlook a fundamental reality: the contexts that are worth storing in memory are different across users. This misalignment wastes limited memory budget on transient interactions while failing to preserve critical context for long horizon tasks. To address this gap, we investigate an underexplored question: can LLM based memory systems learn personalized memory policies? We introduce PerMemBench, the first benchmark for evaluating personalized memory systems, featuring multi year, multi domain interaction histories across diverse user personas. We further present the first empirical study of memory personalization, proposing session level storage gating, a lightweight framework that selectively bypasses memory operations for transient sessions. Our study confirms that personalization yields substantial retention gains under perfect gating, yet reveals that accurate gating remains an open and critical challenge.

</details>


### [139] [ADMFormer: An Adaptive-Decomposition Transformer with Time-Varying Masked Spatial Attention for Traffic Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25543)
*Ruiwen Gu,Qitai Tan,Yahao Liu,Xiao-Ping Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ADMFormer is an Adaptive-Decomposition Transformer that improves traffic forecasting by separately capturing heterogeneous temporal patterns (periodic regularities vs. event-driven fluctuations) and using dynamic sparse spatial attention to filter redundant interactions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traffic forecasting is challenging due to: 1) Heterogeneous temporal patterns where stable periodic regularities coexist with event-driven fluctuations, which existing methods often treat uniformly, limiting their ability to capture fine-grained temporal dynamics. 2) Spatial dependencies among nodes are inherently dynamic and sparse, while dense all-pairs attention introduces redundant interactions and amplifies noise.

Method: ADMFormer uses: 1) A time-node adaptive gating mechanism to decouple traffic signals into dominant regularities and residual fluctuations that vary across time and nodes. 2) A dual-branch temporal module to separately capture global periodic dependencies and high-frequency irregular variations from these decomposed components. 3) A time-varying masked spatial attention that sparsifies spatial interactions based on real-time traffic states to preserve dynamic and informative dependencies.

Result: Extensive experiments on four real-world datasets demonstrate that ADMFormer achieves state-of-the-art performance in traffic forecasting.

Conclusion: ADMFormer effectively addresses the challenges of heterogeneous temporal patterns and dynamic sparse spatial dependencies in traffic forecasting through adaptive decomposition and masked spatial attention, leading to superior forecasting performance.

Abstract: Accurate traffic forecasting is essential for intelligent transportation systems, supporting a wide range of real-world applications. However, it remains challenging due to two key factors:~(1) Traffic series contain heterogeneous temporal patterns, where stable periodic regularities coexist with event-driven fluctuations. Existing methods often treat them within a unified representation, limiting their ability to capture fine-grained temporal dynamics.~(2)Spatial dependencies among nodes are inherently dynamic and sparse, while dense all-pairs attention often introduces redundant interactions and amplifies noise. To address these issues, we propose ADMFormer, an Adaptive-Decomposition Transformer with Time-Varying Masked Spatial Attention. Specifically, ADMFormer first employs a time-node adaptive gating mechanism to decouple traffic signals into dominant regularities and residual fluctuations that vary across time and nodes. A dual-branch temporal module is then designed to separately capture global periodic dependencies and high-frequency irregular variations from these two decomposed components. Furthermore, ADMFormer introduces a time-varying masked spatial attention that sparsifies spatial interactions based on real-time traffic states, thereby effectively preserving dynamic and informative dependencies. Extensive experiments on four real-world datasets demonstrate that ADMFormer achieves state-of-the-art performance.

</details>


### [140] [PHGNet: Prototype-Guided Hypergraph Construction for Heterogeneous Spatiotemporal Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25554)
*Ruiwen Gu,Yahao Liu,Zhenyu Liu,Qitai Tan,Xiao-Ping Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PHGNet is a spatiotemporal traffic forecasting framework that uses prototype-guided hypergraph construction to capture high-order interactions among traffic nodes, overcoming limitations of traditional pairwise dependency modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traffic forecasting is crucial for intelligent transportation systems but is challenging due to complex spatiotemporal dependencies and spatial heterogeneity. Existing methods are limited to pairwise spatial dependency modeling, making it difficult to capture dynamic high-order interactions among nodes with similar traffic patterns.

Method: PHGNet uses a prototype learning mechanism to adaptively assign pattern-similar nodes to hyperedges, capturing high-order interactions with time-varying structures. It includes a global-local node representation module for time-consistent features, iterative residual refinement, and Temporal Query Attention for efficient parallel decoding.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple real-world datasets demonstrate that PHGNet achieves superior predictive performance compared with state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: PHGNet effectively addresses the limitation of existing methods in capturing high-order spatiotemporal dependencies through prototype-guided hypergraph construction, leading to improved traffic forecasting accuracy.

Abstract: As a core task in intelligent transportation systems, traffic forecasting plays a critical role in urban traffic management. Accurate traffic forecasting relies on modeling complex spatiotemporal dependencies, which is inherently challenging due to spatial heterogeneity in traffic systems.Despite significant progress, most existing methods are still limited to pairwise spatial dependency modeling, making it difficult to capture dynamic high-order interactions among nodes with similar traffic patterns. To address this issue, we propose PHGNet, a novel spatiotemporal forecasting framework based on prototype-guided hypergraph construction. At the core of PHGNet, a prototype learning mechanism is designed to adaptively assign pattern-similar nodes to hyperedges, thereby capturing high-order interactions with time-varying structures. To improve the reliability of dynamic hypergraph construction, we further develop a global-local node representation module to extract time-consistent features. For forecasting, iterative residual refinement and Temporal Query Attention are introduced to improve forecasting accuracy while supporting efficient parallel decoding. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world datasets demonstrate that PHGNet achieves superior predictive performance compared with state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [141] [Beyond Query Memorization: Large Language Model Routing with Query Decomposition and Historical Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25558)
*Bo Lv,Jingbo Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DecoR is a novel routing framework that treats LLM routing as a matching process of similar historical queries to avoid memorization traps, using query capability deconstruction to separate linguistic forms from task requirements for better OOD generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM routing methods are inefficient because they rely on direct mapping from queries to models based on surface-level features, which makes them susceptible to memorization traps and poor generalization on out-of-distribution data.

Method: DecoR recasts routing as matching similar queries from historical logs, introduces query capability deconstruction to decouple linguistic surface forms from task-intrinsic requirements, and develops CodaSet benchmark for evaluating routing generalization.

Result: Experimental results on the CodaSet benchmark demonstrate that DecoR maintains superior accuracy while substantially lowering inference costs across both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings.

Conclusion: DecoR effectively mitigates the memorization trap in LLM routing through capability-focused matching and demonstrates strong generalization performance, offering an efficient solution for balancing predictive performance with computational cost.

Abstract: Optimizing the trade-off among predictive performance and computational cost is a central focus in the deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs). Current routing methods primarily rely on direct mapping from queries to models based on surface-level features, making them susceptible to the memorization trap and leading to poor generalizability on out-of-distribution (OOD) data. In this paper, we propose DecoR, a novel routing framework that recasts the routing task as a matching process of sifting similar queries from historical logs, effectively mitigating the memorization trap. To enhance matching accuracy, we introduce a query capability deconstruction method that decouples linguistic surface forms from task-intrinsic requirements, directing matching toward capability dimensions to ground decisions in essential task attributes. Furthermore, we develop CodaSet, a comprehensive benchmark for assessing routing generalization, where experimental results demonstrate that DecoR maintains superior accuracy while substantially lowering inference costs across both in-distribution and OOD settings. All the codes and data are available at https://github.com/lvbotenbest/DecoR.

</details>


### [142] [Uncertainty Reasoning with Large Language Models for Explainable Disease Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25566)
*Xiaoyang Fan,Yufan Cai,Zhe Hou,Jin Song Dong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A neuro-symbolic framework combining LLMs with formal logic for explainable, verifiable medical diagnosis that extracts structured medical information from patient narratives and uses logic programming for auditable reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical decision-making requires reasoning over incomplete, imprecise patient narratives, but current LLMs lack verifiability and interpretability essential for trustworthy medical AI. There's a need for explainable and formally verifiable medical diagnosis systems.

Method: 1) Embed patient descriptions and clinical guidelines into a neural knowledge base using LLMs to extract structured medical entities, temporal relations, and fuzzy symptom patterns; 2) Decode into symbolic knowledge base using fuzzy logic and declarative rules; 3) Perform two-stage reasoning: inductive symbolic generalization to capture diagnostic patterns, and inference verification via logic programming; 4) Treat symptoms as fuzzy predicates with probabilistic weights; 5) Support iterative refinement where misalignments can be traced and corrected through formal rules.

Result: Validated on public benchmarks, showing performance comparable to state-of-the-art LLMs while providing interpretable reasoning paths and formally verifiable diagnostic conclusions. The framework effectively reconciles symbolic reasoning with LLMs on real-world clinical narratives.

Conclusion: The neuro-symbolic framework combines logic-based transparency, LLM adaptability, and probabilistic robustness to enable human-aligned healthcare inference with strong generalization and verifiable, step-by-step reasoning chains, addressing the limitations of purely statistical methods in medical AI.

Abstract: Clinical decision-making requires reasoning over incomplete, imprecise, and linguistically expressed patient narratives. While large language models (LLMs) excel at extracting latent information from natural language, they lack the verifiability and interpretability essential for trustworthy medical AI. We propose a neuro-symbolic reasoning framework that aligns LLMs with formal logic to enable explainable and formally verifiable medical diagnosis. Patient descriptions and clinical guidelines are embedded into a neural knowledge base, where LLMs extract structured medical entities, temporal relations, and fuzzy symptom patterns, which are decoded into a symbolic knowledge base expressed in fuzzy logic and declarative rules. We perform two-stage reasoning: (1) inductive symbolic generalization to capture diagnostic patterns from encoded narratives, and (2) inference verification via a logic programming engine to derive and validate diagnoses consistent with clinical standards. Each symptom is treated as a fuzzy predicate with probabilistic weights, and inference paths are auditable, adjustable, and compatible with physician feedback. Unlike purely statistical methods, our system supports iterative refinement: misalignment between LLM-generated diagnoses and ground truth can be traced, explained, and corrected through formal rules. By combining logic-based transparency, LLM adaptability, and probabilistic robustness, the framework enables human-aligned healthcare inference with strong generalization and verifiable, step-by-step reasoning chains. We validate our framework on public benchmarks, demonstrating effective reconciliation of symbolic reasoning and LLMs with real-world clinical narratives. Results show performance comparable to state-of-the-art LLMs, while additionally providing interpretable reasoning paths and formally verifiable diagnostic conclusions.

</details>


### [143] [Detecting Unfaithful Chain-of-Thought via Circuit-Guided Internal-External Discrepancy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25603)
*Xu Shen,Zhen Tan,Song Wang,Pingjun Hong,Rui Miao,Xin Wang,Tianlong Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CIE-Scorer is a framework that detects unfaithful chain-of-thought reasoning by comparing internal model computations with external generated rationales using efficient circuit tracing and graph discrepancy measurement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chain-of-thought reasoning improves LLM problem-solving but generated reasoning traces may not reflect actual decision processes. Existing detectors overlook internal model computation evidence, while full circuit tracing for long CoTs is costly and difficult to scale.

Method: CIE-Scorer efficiently traces compact sentence-level circuits from informative reasoning tokens, constructs internal and external reasoning graphs, and measures their discrepancy using Fused Gromov-Wasserstein distance to detect unfaithful CoT reasoning.

Result: Experiments on four datasets from FaithCoT-Bench show CIE-Scorer achieves state-of-the-art performance while reducing circuit construction cost, demonstrating effectiveness of combining mechanistic interpretability signals with external reasoning traces.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively detects CoT unfaithfulness by aligning reasoning traces with computational processes, offering a scalable solution that leverages both internal and external evidence for improved detection performance.

Abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning improves the problem-solving ability of large language models (LLMs), but generated reasoning traces may not faithfully reflect the model's actual decision process. Existing CoT unfaithfulness detectors mainly rely on external signals from generated rationales, such as textual plausibility or answer consistency, while overlooking evidence from the model's internal computation. Although recent circuit tracing methods provide a way to obtain model-internal evidence by tracing how information flows through model components during reasoning, constructing full reasoning circuits for long CoTs is costly and difficult to scale. To address these challenges, we propose Circuit-guided Internal-External Discrepancy Scorer (CIE-Scorer), a framework for instance-level CoT unfaithfulness detection. The key idea is that faithful reasoning traces should align with the model's computational process, whereas unfaithful traces may diverge from it. CIE-Scorer efficiently traces compact sentence-level circuits from informative reasoning tokens, constructs internal and external reasoning graphs, and measures their discrepancy using Fused Gromov--Wasserstein distance. Experiments on four datasets from FaithCoT-Bench show that CIE-Scorer achieves state-of-the-art performance while reducing the cost of circuit construction, demonstrating the effectiveness of combining mechanistic interpretability signals with external reasoning traces for CoT unfaithfulness detection.

</details>


### [144] [Back to Parsimonious Latents: Learning Task-Centric World Models from Visual Foundations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25620)
*Minghao Fu,Fan Feng,Nicklas Hansen,Biwei Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TC-WM transforms foundation-model embeddings into compact task-sufficient world representations for improved planning and control in reward-free offline settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current world model representations either have limited semantic structure (learned from pixels) or excessive task-irrelevant detail (from frozen foundation models), resulting in state spaces poorly matched for downstream planning and control, especially in reward-free offline settings.

Method: TC-WM treats pretrained embeddings as a semantic scaffold: linearly projects high-dimensional embeddings into compact latent space, aligns a subspace with agent's physical state via contrastive learning, and reconstructs embeddings to preserve useful visual structure.

Result: TC-WM achieves better world-modeling quality and more precise control than state-of-the-art approaches across diverse environments (Robomimic and D4RL), and theoretically identifies underlying task-centric latent factors up to simple transformations.

Conclusion: TC-WM effectively combines the generality of foundation features with controllability of task-centric dynamics, enabling superior test-time planning in reward-free offline settings.

Abstract: World models enable agents to predict future dynamics conditioned on actions, making the choice of latent representation central to planning and control. Such representations are often either learned directly from pixels with limited semantic structure or inherited from frozen visual foundation models with excessive task-irrelevant detail, yielding state spaces that are poorly matched to downstream planning and control. This is especially challenging in reward-free offline settings, where the model must learn from fixed trajectories without reward supervision or online interaction. To address this, we propose TC-WM, a framework for turning foundation-model embeddings into compact, task-sufficient world representations. The key design is to treat the pretrained embedding space as a semantic scaffold rather than as the final state space: TC-WM linearly projects high-dimensional visual embeddings into a compact latent as the dynamic space, aligns a subspace with the agent's physical state via contrastive learning, and reconstructs embeddings to preserve useful visual structure. This combines the generality of foundation features with the controllability of task-centric dynamics. Theoretically, we show that TC-WM suffices to identify the underlying task-centric latent factors up to a simple transformation. Empirically, TC-WM enables test-time planning across diverse environments (e.g., Robomimic and D4RL), achieving better world-modeling quality and more precise control than state-of-the-art approaches.

</details>


### [145] [CUA-Gym: Scaling Verifiable Training Environments and Tasks for Computer-Use Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25624)
*Bowen Wang,Dunjie Lu,Junli Wang,Tianyi Bai,Shixuan Liu,Zhipeng Zhang,Haiquan Wang,Hao Hu,Tianbao Xie,Shuai Bai,Dayiheng Liu,Que Shen,Junyang Lin,Tao Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CUA-Gym: A scalable pipeline for generating high-fidelity reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) training data for computer-use agents, including synthetic environments and 32K verified training tuples, enabling models that outperform prior open-source CUAs on benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has shown success in domains like math and software engineering, but extending it to computer-use agents (CUAs) is bottlenecked by scarce scalable training data with deterministic rewards. Current approaches either have high fidelity but limited coverage (hand-curated benchmarks) or broad coverage but unreliable verification (LLM-as-judge-based datasets).

Method: A scalable pipeline that co-generates task instructions, environment states, and reward functions using three agents: 1) Generator agent constructs initial and golden environment states, 2) Discriminator agent writes reward functions from task specifications, 3) Orchestrator agent drives iterative execution rounds. Generated tuples pass through a final filter combining LLM majority voting and agent rollouts. Additionally, they synthesize CUA-Gym-Hub, a suite of high-fidelity mock web applications grounded in real-world software-use distributions.

Result: Constructed CUA-Gym dataset with 32,112 verified RLVR training tuples grounded in 110 environments. Models trained with GSPO (CUA-Gym-A3B and CUA-Gym-A17B) achieve 62.1% and 72.6% on OSWorld-Verified benchmark, outperforming prior open-source CUAs at comparable scales. Performance scales smoothly with data volume and environment diversity. Models also show transfer learning capabilities, improving on the held-out WebArena benchmark.

Conclusion: The CUA-Gym pipeline successfully addresses the data scarcity problem for RLVR training in computer-use agents by generating scalable, high-quality training data with verifiable rewards. The approach enables training models that significantly outperform existing open-source CUAs while demonstrating transferability to new environments. The full synthesis pipeline, dataset, environments, and models will be open-sourced.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has driven breakthroughs in domains such as math, tool-use, and software engineering, yet its extension to computer-use agents (CUAs) has been bottlenecked by the scarcity of scalable training data with deterministic rewards. Constructing such data for CUAs requires consistent task instruction, executable environment, and verifiable reward. However, hand-curated benchmarks achieve high reward fidelity but cover few applications and LLM-as-judge-based datasets scale broadly but lack reliable verification. We present CUA-Gym, a scalable pipeline that co-generates task instructions, environment states, and reward functions. Concretely, a Generator agent constructs the initial and golden environment states, and a separate Discriminator agent writes the reward function from the task specification. An orchestrator agent drives the two through iterative rounds upon execution. Generated tuples then pass a final filter combining LLM majority voting and agent rollouts, ensuring quality beyond the per-task adversarial loop. To address the scarcity of training environments, we further synthesize CUA-Gym-Hub, a broad suite of high-fidelity mock web applications grounded in real-world software-use distributions, expanding the scale of CUA RLVR data by magnitude. Using this pipeline, we construct CUA-Gym, a dataset of 32,112 verified RLVR training tuples grounded in 110 environments. Trained with GSPO on CUA-Gym, our CUA-Gym-A3B and CUA-Gym-A17B achieve 62.1% and 72.6% on OSWorld-Verified, outperforming prior open-source CUAs at comparable scales, with performance scaling smoothly in both data volume and environment diversity. The same checkpoints also improve on the held-out WebArena benchmark, indicating transfer beyond the training environments. We will open-source the full synthesis pipeline, dataset, CUA-Gym-Hub environments, and models.

</details>


### [146] [Insuring Every Action: An Authority Frontier Framework for Runtime Actuarial Control of Autonomous AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25632)
*Hao-Hsuan Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AAI provides a runtime framework to price and gate autonomous AI actions using reserve capital, enabling deterministic control over side-effects while measuring released authority via Authority Frontier metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous AI agents increasingly perform actions with real-world side effects (database mutations, payments, refunds), creating need for deterministic runtime control mechanisms that prevent unintended consequences while enabling measurable authority delegation.

Method: AAI uses deterministic contracts that price actions against safe defaults via risk mapping, gates execution with per-boundary reserve capital budgets, establishes quote-bind-commit protocol with toll-bounded tokens, classifies actions into seven authority units, and measures authority via Capital@k metrics and full reserve demand.

Result: Implemented across four agentic environments showing common low-reserve refusal patterns, reserve capital variations up to 22x (Capital@50 from 289 to 6457), live Postgres panel prevented losses across three Azure models, and demonstrated that model identity affects underwriting persistence under denial.

Conclusion: AAI provides benchmark-ready evaluation framework for runtime actuarial control of autonomous-agent side effects, enabling deterministic authority measurement and capital-based risk management without forcing domains into uniform shapes.

Abstract: Autonomous AI agents increasingly issue side-effect-bearing actions: database mutations, refunds, payments, external commitments. We propose the Actuarial Action Interface (AAI), a deterministic runtime contract that prices each such action against a contractually fixed safe default under a time-consistent risk mapping, and gates execution against a per-boundary reserve capital budget. We then develop the Authority Frontier, an evaluation primitive measuring how much autonomous authority the runtime releases at each level of reserve capital. The framework provides (i) a deterministic quote-bind-commit protocol with toll-bounded capability tokens; (ii) a universal seven-class action taxonomy mapping heterogeneous tool calls to comparable authority units; (iii) replay determinism and pathwise reserve coverage under alpha-spending; (iv) cross-domain normalization via full reserve demand C_full and capital metrics Capital@k. We instantiate AAI across four agentic environments (database mutation, customer-service refund, and the public tau-bench retail and airline tool-use traces) and report a live Postgres panel in which three Azure-hosted models propose actions through the same contract. The frontier exhibits a common low-reserve refusal and intermediate-release pattern across domains, with saturation only where the budget grid reaches full reserve demand; required reserve capital varies by 22x (Capital@50 from 289 to 6457). The framework does not force domains into the same shape; it surfaces each domain's actuarial geometry. In the live panel the contract prevents realized loss across all three models at low budget while differing in underwriting persistence under denial: model identity is an actuarial underwriting variable. The contribution is a benchmark-ready evaluation framework for runtime actuarial control of autonomous-agent side effects.

</details>


### [147] [AgentHijack: Benchmarking Computer Use Agent Robustness to Common Environment Corruptions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25707)
*Jingwei Sun,Jianing Zhu,Yuanyi Li,Tongliang Liu,Xia HU,Bo Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentHijack is a benchmark to evaluate robustness of MLLM-based computer-use agents against real-world environmental corruptions like pop-ups and resolution changes, showing they're fragile; a framework with action generator and onlooker is proposed to improve robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world computer environments are imperfect with pop-ups, resolution changes, and competing applications that interfere with autonomous computer-use agents powered by multimodal LLMs, but current agents lack robustness against such common corruptions.

Method: Introduces AgentHijack benchmark with 9 configurable common corruptions to simulate realistic imperfect scenarios; proposes AgentHijack-Agent framework with action generator (enhanced grounding) and onlooker (behavior summarization & environment checking).

Result: Evaluation shows even minor corruptions cause substantial performance degradation, revealing fragility of MLLM-based agents; proposed AgentHijack-Agent framework demonstrates effectiveness in experiments.

Conclusion: Computer-use agents are fragile against common real-world corruptions, highlighting the need for robustness evaluation; the proposed framework provides a solution for improving agent resilience in dynamic, imperfect execution environments.

Abstract: Autonomous computer use agents that powered by multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are emerging as capable assistants for completing complex digital workflows. However, real-world execution environments are far from ideal: pop-ups, resolution changes, and competing applications frequently interfere with agent perception and control. We introduce AgentHijack, a benchmark designed to evaluate the robustness of computer-use agents under common corruptions, where the uncertainties in dynamic environment disrupt the execution flow without direct adversarial intent. Specifically, AgentHijack introduces 9 configurable common corruptions to replicate realistic imperfect scenarios. We evaluate a variety of desktop tasks that utilize MLLM-based agents and discover that even minor instances of corruption can result in substantial performance degradation, which emphasizes the fragility of agents and underscores the necessity of robustness evaluation. Afterward, we propose AgentHijack-Agent, a framework that integrates an action generator with enhanced grounding capabilities and an onlooker responsible for behavior summarization and environment checking. Extensive experiments validate its effectiveness. Our code, environment, baseline models and data are publicly available at: https://AgentHijack.github.io.

</details>


### [148] [FLOATBench: A Dataset and Benchmark for Floating Offshore Wind Turbine Tower Fatigue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25717)
*João Alves Ribeiro,Bruno Alves Ribeiro,Francisco Pimenta,Sérgio M. O. Tavares,Faez Ahmed*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FLOATBench is a public benchmark for fatigue-damage prediction in 22 MW floating offshore wind turbine towers, featuring 582,120 per-section labels from high-fidelity simulations with a regime-aware evaluation protocol.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The offshore wind industry is scaling to 22 MW turbines in deep waters where floating turbines are essential. Tower fatigue becomes critical with larger structures amplifying coupled loads, but current studies lack standardized benchmarks for comparing fatigue-damage prediction methods.

Method: Created FLOATBench with 582,120 per-section fatigue-damage labels from 19,404 high-fidelity OpenFAST simulations across three 22 MW FOWT tower geometries. Features a regime-aware alpha-shape partition of wind/wave operating envelope and a reproducible evaluation harness with three protocol levels.

Result: A comprehensive tabular benchmark dataset with structured evaluation protocols (E1: random validation, E2: within-tower regime-aware evaluation, E3: cross-tower transfer) that reveals performance rank shifts in extrapolation scenarios not detectable with random splits.

Conclusion: FLOATBench is the first FOWT fatigue benchmark for tabular surrogate modeling, providing a standardized framework for comparing methods and an evaluation protocol that generalizes to engineering surrogates over physical operating envelopes.

Abstract: Most of the world's offshore wind resource lies in waters too deep for fixed-bottom foundations, making floating offshore wind turbines (FOWTs) essential for deep-water deployment. As the industry scales toward $22$ MW class designs, tower fatigue becomes increasingly critical because larger structures amplify the coupled aero-hydro-servo-elastic loads induced by continuous wind and wave excitation. Accurate fatigue-damage prediction is therefore central to certification, design optimization, and cost reduction. Yet the field lacks a shared surrogate benchmark: studies report different simulations, splits, and metrics, making methods difficult to compare. We present FLOATBench, a public tabular benchmark with $582{,}120$ per-section fatigue-damage labels across three $22$ MW FOWT tower geometries, derived from $19{,}404$ high-fidelity OpenFAST simulations across the three towers ($6{,}468$ per tower: $1{,}078$ aligned wind/wave operating points $\times$ six turbulence seeds), labeled at $30$ cross-sections per tower. FLOATBench includes a regime-aware alpha-shape partition of the joint wind/wave operating envelope, stratifying test points into in-train, interpolation, and extrapolation regimes. It is paired with a reproducible evaluation harness covering three protocol levels: random validation (E1), within-tower regime-aware evaluation (E2), and cross-tower transfer (E3). The regime-aware protocol reveals rank shifts between global and extrapolation performance that random-split leaderboards cannot detect. To the authors' knowledge, FLOATBench is the first FOWT fatigue benchmark for tabular surrogate modeling, and offers an evaluation protocol that generalizes to engineering surrogates defined over physical operating envelopes. Dataset and code available at: https://github.com/Joao97ribeiro/FLOATBench.

</details>


### [149] [Learning to Search and Searching to Learn for Generalization in Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25720)
*Michael Aichmüller,Yannik Hesse,Hector Geffner*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A self-improving WA* learning framework combines relational graph neural network heuristics with search to achieve strong combinatorial generalization in deep reinforcement learning, solving challenging planning problems without search.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Combinatorial generalization remains a central challenge in Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL), especially in sparse-reward domains where standard RL exploration is ineffective. Existing methods often rely on expert demonstrations, hindsight relabeling, or random walks, while planners use search methods like A*, but there's a need for methods that can learn generalizable heuristics.

Method: Proposes a self-improving WA* learning framework combining relational graph neural network value heuristics with search: the heuristic guides WA* search, and resulting search data updates the heuristic via Q-learning, creating a feedback loop that improves both search and heuristic quality.

Result: The method achieves strong zero-shot generalization, solving new instances without search where DRL fails. Demonstrates on Sokoban, PushWorld, The Witness, and IPC benchmarks. Notably, heuristics trained on Blocksworld with <30 blocks successfully solve instances with 488 blocks without search.
