Thus, the key challenge is selecting informative observations to resolve uncertainty, shifting the focus from static inference to adaptive data acquisition. To address this, we propose LLM-AutoSciLab, a closed-loop framework that couples hypothesis generation with hypothesis-conditioned experiment selection and mechanism refinement. Rather than fitting models to passively collected data, LLM-AutoSciLab iteratively proposes plausible hypotheses, selects informative experiments to distinguish or refine them, and updates its state using the resulting evidence. To evaluate dynamic, closed-loop scientific discovery with active data acquisition, we introduce ActiveSciBench, comprising two datasets: ActiveSciBench-Chem with 57 enzyme-kinetics tasks and ActiveSciBench-GRN with 45 gene-regulatory-network tasks. These datasets model discovery as a budget-constrained process requiring adaptive experiment design, variable selection, and recovery of true mechanisms. Across NewtonBench, ActiveSciBench-Chem, and ActiveSciBench-GRN, LLM-AutoSciLab outperforms prior methods, achieving 67.6% and 35.1% symbolic accuracy on NewtonBench and ActiveSciBench-Chem, respectively, and 31.1% exact graph recovery on ActiveSciBench-GRN. Moreover, hypothesis-guided experimentation is 2-5x more sample-efficient than the strongest competing baselines. Code and data are available at: https://github.com/scientific-discovery/LLM-AutoSciLab

</details>


### [174] [A Large-Scale Dataset and Benchmark: Do Protein-Ligand Models Learn Binding Sites or Just Binding Likelihood?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24045)
*Zhaohan Meng,Zhen Bai,Ke Yuan,Iadh Ounis,Zaiqiao Meng,Hao Xu,Joseph Loscalzo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: InteractBind introduces a large-scale protein-ligand dataset with fine-grained benchmarks for binding-site localization using interaction maps, revealing current models' limitations despite good binary binding prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing protein-ligand benchmarks focus mainly on binary binding prediction and affinity regression, but don't evaluate whether models can localize binding sites or identify specific non-covalent interactions underlying molecular recognition.

Method: Created InteractBind - a large-scale dataset of ~100k protein-ligand pairs with protein-residue and ligand-atom interaction maps spanning six major non-covalent interaction types. Includes binding affinity and protein similarity-controlled splits for realistic generalization assessment.

Result: Evaluation of eight existing sequence-based and interaction-aware models shows limited binding-site localization performance despite strong binary binding prediction, with significant variation across different non-covalent interaction types.

Conclusion: InteractBind establishes a new benchmark paradigm that encourages development of more interpretable and physically grounded protein-ligand models by focusing on fine-grained interaction localization rather than just binary binding prediction.

Abstract: Protein-ligand modeling underpins computational drug discovery and molecular design. Existing protein-ligand benchmarks typically evaluate whether a protein and ligand interact and how strongly they bind, through tasks such as binary binding prediction and affinity regression. However, these evaluations provide limited evidence of whether models can localize binding sites or identify the non-covalent interactions underlying molecular recognition. To address this gap, we introduce InteractBind, a large-scale protein-ligand dataset comprising approximately 100k protein-ligand pairs, together with a benchmark for fine-grained evaluation. The core fine-grained task is that of binding-site localization, which uses protein-residue and ligand-atom interaction maps spanning six major types of non-covalent interactions to assess whether model-derived interaction maps localize binding sites. InteractBind further includes binding affinity and protein similarity-controlled splits to support realistic generalization assessment. Using InteractBind, we evaluate eight existing sequence-based and interaction-aware models, assessing binary binding prediction and binding-site localization. Results reveal limited binding-site localization despite strong binary binding prediction, with marked variation across non-covalent interaction types. Overall, InteractBind establishes a benchmark paradigm that encourages the development of more interpretable and physically grounded protein-ligand models.

</details>


### [175] [Mixture of Complementary Agents for Robust LLM Ensemble](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24048)
*Yichi Zhang,Kevin Lu,Yuang Zhang,Jie Gao,Lirong Xia,Fang-Yi Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposer selection in multi-AI collaboration systems is framed as a combinatorial selection problem focusing on complementarity between LLMs, with new greedy algorithms developed to address computational limitations of standard feature selection methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches for selecting proposer LLMs in multi-AI collaboration systems focus on either accuracy or diversity, overlooking the crucial interactions among proposers and with the summarizer LLM. Standard feature-selection algorithms are impractical due to prohibitive time complexity in LLM settings.

Method: Reframed proposer selection as a combinatorial selection problem akin to feature selection, where value is determined by complementarity. Developed and explored computationally feasible, greedy-style selection algorithms that assess complementarity using small labeled datasets, addressing the computational limitations of standard approaches.

Result: Experiments validated complementarity as an effective guiding principle for proposer selection and identified methods that achieve the best performance-cost trade-offs in practice.

Conclusion: Complementarity between LLMs is a crucial factor in proposer selection for multi-AI collaboration systems, and the proposed greedy algorithms offer practical solutions with good performance-cost trade-offs compared to traditional accuracy/diversity-focused approaches.

Abstract: Multi-AI collaboration, such as ensembling or debating large language models (LLMs), is a promising paradigm for aggregating information and boosting performance. A foundational step in these pipelines is to feed the responses of several proposer LLMs into a summarizer LLM, which synthesizes a better answer. However, choosing which proposers to include is non-trivial. Existing approaches primarily focus either on accuracy (picking the strongest models) or diversity (ensuring variety), and often overlook the interactions among proposers and with the summarizer. We reframe proposer selection as a combinatorial selection problem akin to feature selection, where the value of an LLM lies in its complementarity with others. However, directly applying standard feature-selection algorithms is impractical in the LLM setting due to prohibitive time complexity. Motivated by this limitation, we explore an extensive range of computationally feasible, greedy-style selection algorithms that assess complementarity using a small labeled set. Our experiments validate complementarity as a guiding principle for proposer selection and identify methods that achieve the best performance-cost trade-offs in practice.

</details>


### [176] [Truthful Online Preference Aggregation for LLM Fine-Tuning in Mobile Crowdsourcing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24052)
*Shugang Hao,Lingjie Duan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a truthful online preference aggregation mechanism for LLM fine-tuning in mobile crowdsourcing that prevents strategic worker misreporting and achieves sublinear regret (O(√T)), outperforming existing approaches with linear regret.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mobile crowdsourcing platforms need to align LLM-generated content with human feedback, but workers may strategically misreport preferences to maximize influence or payment. Existing methods fail to identify accurate workers in online settings, resulting in poor linear regret performance.

Method: The authors formulate a dynamic Bayesian game to model multi-agent online learning between platform and strategic workers, then propose an online weighted aggregation mechanism that dynamically adjusts worker weights based on feedback accuracy. The mechanism is extended to handle limited feedback scenarios.

Result: The mechanism ensures truthful feedback from strategic workers and achieves sublinear regret O(√T), which is significantly better than the O(T) regret of existing approaches. This result holds even with limited worker feedback per time slot. Real-world experiments show performance gains over benchmark schemes.

Conclusion: The proposed truthful online preference aggregation mechanism effectively solves the strategic misreporting problem in mobile crowdsourcing for LLM fine-tuning, providing theoretical guarantees for truthfulness and achieving optimal sublinear regret bounds, making it practical for real-world applications.

Abstract: To better serve users' demands in mobile applications (e.g., navigation), mobile crowdsourcing platforms can iteratively align large language model (LLM)-generated content (e.g., AI-generated traffic condition predictions) with human feedback collected from crowdsourcing workers (e.g., mobile users). However, workers may strategically misreport their online preference feedback to maximize their influence or payment. Existing pipelines in mobile crowdsourcing (e.g., EM-based weight estimation) fail to identify the most accurate worker in this online setting, resulting in a linear regret $\mathcal{O}(T)$ over $T$ time slots. In this paper, we study truthful online preference aggregation for LLM fine-tuning in mobile crowdsourcing. We formulate a new dynamic Bayesian game to model the multi-agent online learning process between the platform and strategic mobile workers. We propose a novel online weighted aggregation mechanism that dynamically adjusts each worker's weight in the preference aggregation according to their feedback accuracy. We prove that our mechanism ensures truthful feedback from strategic workers and achieves a sublinear regret $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{T})$ over $T$ time slots. We further extend our mechanism to a challenging scenario with limited worker feedback per time slot, still guaranteeing a sublinear regret $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{T})$. Experiments on LLM fine-tuning with real-world datasets further demonstrate significant performance gains of our mechanisms over benchmark schemes.

</details>


### [177] [Cascade-KDE: Robust Time-Series Restoration under Out-of-Distribution Impulse Corruptions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24055)
*Yuefeng Liu,Ning Yang,Ziyu Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Cascade-KDE: A training-free time-series restoration framework that preserves derivative peaks and task-critical features while handling Gaussian noise and impulse outliers, showing superior performance over classical and learning-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world time-series data (industrial sensing, healthcare, energy systems) often contains mixed Gaussian noise and large-magnitude impulse outliers. For tasks requiring local shape preservation (ECG morphology analysis, battery degradation monitoring), there's a need for restoration that minimizes reconstruction error while preserving derivative peaks and task-critical features.

Method: Cascade-KDE: A training-free restoration framework that: 1) estimates a 2D temporal-amplitude density, 2) applies Density-Truncated Robust Expectation to limit influence of distant abnormal points, and 3) refines the sequence through an exponential cascade with adaptive stopping. The design improves robustness against out-of-distribution impulse corruptions while keeping restored trajectory close to original local structure.

Result: Across several benchmark datasets, Cascade-KDE shows consistent gains over classical filters and representative learning-based baselines on: curve fidelity, derivative preservation, downstream classification accuracy, and runtime efficiency.

Conclusion: Bounded density-based restoration (Cascade-KDE) is a practical option for feature-preserving preprocessing in noisy time-series pipelines, offering robust handling of impulse corruptions while maintaining critical local structure.

Abstract: Real-world time-series data in industrial sensing, healthcare, and energy systems is often corrupted by a mixture of Gaussian noise and occasional large-magnitude impulse outliers. For tasks that depend on local shape, such as ECG morphology analysis and battery degradation monitoring, the main requirement is not only low reconstruction error but also preservation of derivative peaks and task-critical features. We propose Cascade-KDE, a training-free restoration framework for corrupted time series. The method first estimates a two-dimensional temporal-amplitude density, then applies a Density-Truncated Robust Expectation to limit the influence of distant abnormal points, and finally refines the sequence through an exponential cascade with adaptive stopping. This design aims to improve robustness under out-of-distribution impulse corruptions while keeping the restored trajectory close to the original local structure. Across several benchmark datasets, the proposed method shows consistent gains over classical filters and representative learning-based baselines on curve fidelity, derivative preservation, downstream classification, and runtime efficiency. These results suggest that bounded density-based restoration is a practical option for feature-preserving preprocessing in noisy time-series pipelines.

</details>


### [178] [Feature Lottery? A Bifurcation Theory of Concept Emergence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24057)
*Fuming Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a bifurcation theory framework to detect structured representation emergence in neural networks in real-time using label-free dynamical analysis, validated across diverse settings including language models, SSL, and grokking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for identifying when neural networks acquire structured representations rely on retrospective, label-dependent metrics, lacking real-time detection capability. There's a need for universal, label-free methods to detect representation transitions as they happen.

Method: Introduces bifurcation theory of representation dynamics using a passive GMM probe attached to the evolving encoder. Shows representation structure onset corresponds to a supercritical pitchfork bifurcation driven by loss Hessian, yielding a dynamic ratio β(t)/β_c(t) computed entirely from hidden states as a universal phase coordinate.

Result: Empirically validates four distinct transition regimes across diverse settings: SAEs on language models, SSL on CIFAR, and grokking on modular arithmetic. Reveals macroscopic symmetry-breaking can lag initial zero-crossing by orders of magnitude, explaining delayed escape in grokking. Shows early atom purity predicts final convergence purity remarkably early (by 5% of training).

Conclusion: The β/β_c ratio provides both theoretical insight into representation dynamics and practical utility as an early-warning indicator for training health, detecting onset of usable structure, feature identity crystallization, and representational collapse before downstream metrics react.

Abstract: Neural networks acquire structured representations at specific moments during training, yet identifying these transitions typically relies on retrospective, label-dependent metrics. We introduce a bifurcation theory of representation dynamics to detect these moments in real time. Analyzing a passive GMM probe attached to the evolving encoder, we show the onset of structure corresponds to a supercritical pitchfork bifurcation driven by the loss Hessian. The system exhibits a theoretically predictable zero-crossing ($β_c$) that, compared to the network's current state ($β$), yields a dynamic ratio $β(t)/β_c(t)$: a universal, label-free phase coordinate for representation dynamics, computable entirely from hidden states. We empirically validate four distinct transition regimes predicted by this coordinate across diverse settings: SAEs on language models (Pythia), SSL (CIFAR), and grokking (modular arithmetic). Crucially, under finite dissipation, macroscopic symmetry-breaking can lag the initial zero-crossing by orders of magnitude, which providing a rigorous dynamical account of the delayed escape observed in grokking. Microscopically, the bifurcation creates a shared unstable subspace, forcing collective symmetry breaking. We term this the "feature lottery" in SAE training: a feature's terminal interpretability becomes predictable remarkably early. By only 5% of training, early atom purity robustly predicts final convergence purity, with top-decile early atoms achieving over 12x the baseline purity at convergence. Beyond explaining concept emergence, $β/β_c$ provides a practical early-warning indicator for training health, detecting the onset of usable structure, the crystallization of feature identity, and representational collapse epochs before downstream metrics react.

</details>


### [179] [Signs Beat Floats: Low-Rank Double-Binary Adaptation for On-Device Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24058)
*Yoshihiko Fujisawa,Yuma Ichikawa,Yudai Fujimoto,Akira Sakai,Katsuki Fujisawa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoRDBA introduces a binary LoRA adapter that replaces low-rank factors with binary sign carriers and lightweight channel-wise scales, achieving similar quality to fp16 LoRA with significantly reduced adapter footprint and minimal latency overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current on-device LLM adaptation with LoRA adapters introduces dense floating-point branches that increase storage, communication, and computational overhead, especially in unmerged adapter-mode settings where adapters remain separate from base models.

Method: LoRDBA replaces both low-rank factors in standard LoRA with binary sign carriers while representing magnitudes through lightweight channel-wise scales. This converts the dense adapter branch into two sign-accumulation matrix multiplications interleaved with channel-wise scaling.

Result: LoRDBA outperforms low-bit baselines at matched model sizes while matching fp16 LoRA quality in selected regimes. It reduces adapter footprint by over 10x while adding at most 8% prefill latency overhead at rank r=16, with moderate training memory overhead of ~1.6x fp16 LoRA.

Conclusion: LoRDBA provides an efficient, LoRA-compatible adapter that significantly reduces storage and computational overhead while maintaining quality, making it suitable for on-device adaptation where adapter footprint and efficiency are critical.

Abstract: On-device adaptation of large language models commonly keeps a quantized base model frozen while training and deploying a small, task-specific LoRA adapter. In the unmerged adapter-mode setting, however, the adapter is more than a compact storage module; it introduces an additional dense floating-point branch, maintains a trainable state for local updates, and acts as a unit of communication and hot-swapping.We introduce LoRDBA, a LoRA-compatible adapter that replaces both low-rank factors with binary sign carriers while representing magnitudes through lightweight, channel-wise scales, converting the dense adapter branch into two sign-accumulation matrix multiplications interleaved with channel-wise scaling. A finite-sample analysis shows that reconstruction quality is governed by the residual-to-magnitude ratio of the original LoRA factors. In adapter-mode experiments, LoRDBA outperforms low-bit baselines at matched model sizes while matching fp16 LoRA quality in selected regimes. The unmerged adapter incurs at most 8% prefill latency overhead at matched rank r=16 despite an over 10x reduction in adapter footprint, with moderate training memory overhead of approximately 1.6x that of fp16 LoRA.

</details>


### [180] [Spectral Probe-Circuits: A Three-Step Recipe for Identifying Attention-Head Circuits in Pretrained Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24059)
*Yongzhong Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A three-step unsupervised method identifies specialized attention-head circuits in transformers using spectral analysis, task filtering, and causal ablation, revealing consistent computational structures across model sizes and architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for analyzing transformer attention heads often require labels, attribution gradients, or supervised signals. There's a need for an unsupervised, scalable approach to identify causally important attention-head circuits that perform specialized computations in pretrained transformers across different architectures and scales.

Method: 1) Spectral signal analysis: Uses per-head time-integrated participation ratio to rank heads doing sustained content-dependent computation without supervision. 2) Task-pattern screen: Filters spectral signal to identify task-specific candidate circuits. 3) Group ablation with matched-random control: Establishes causal necessity of identified circuits by comparing ablation effects against random controls.

Result: Successfully identified a 2-6 head induction circuit causally necessary in all tested models (51M to 1B/7B parameters) with 94-100% performance drop after ablation. The spectral signal works unsupervised across six independent seeds. Specialized computation is conserved at 17-19% of heads across Pythia family, while induction circuits remain stable at 3-11 heads despite increasing model size.

Conclusion: The three-step recipe provides an effective, unsupervised method for identifying causally important attention-head circuits across diverse transformer architectures and scales. The findings reveal computational conservation principles where specialized circuits remain small and stable despite model growth, suggesting fundamental architectural constraints in transformer computation.

Abstract: We present a three-step recipe for identifying attention-head circuits in pretrained transformers. A per-head spectral signal -- the time-integrated participation ratio of each head's attention output -- ranks heads doing sustained content-dependent computation without labels or attribution gradients. A task-pattern screen filters this general indicator into a task-specific candidate circuit, and group ablation against a matched-random control completes the causal claim. We validate across an 8x parameter range (51M to 1B-active / 7B-total), two architecture families (dense, mixture-of-experts), and four pretraining pipelines. The recipe ports: a 2-6 head induction circuit is causally necessary in every model tested, with a 94-100% drop in synthetic-induction top-1 after ablation. The spectral signal is predictive without supervision: on six independent seeds of a 51M-parameter probe model, the same computation identifies the seed-specific circuit on each seed. The fraction of heads doing identifiable specialized computation is conserved at 17-19% across the Pythia family (124M to 410M), while specific induction circuits stay 3-11 heads -- sublinear in total head count. This paper is the methodology anchor of a three-paper program; companion papers extend the recipe to developmental trajectories during pretraining and to composed-task circuits where pattern selectivity decouples from task-causal structure.

</details>


### [181] [Federated Learning over Human-Body Communication for On-Body Edge Intelligence: A Survey, Taxonomy, and BODYFED-HBC Scheduling Vignette](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24062)
*Koffka Khan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Survey paper exploring the intersection of Human-body Communication (HBC) and Federated Learning (FL) for wearable body-area networks, proposing a taxonomy for FL deployments and identifying body-channel-aware FL as an open research problem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HBC and FL literatures remain weakly connected despite both being promising technologies for wearable body-area networks - FL abstracts communication layer while HBC abstracts learning traffic. There's a need to integrate these technologies for more efficient and privacy-preserving wearable systems.

Method: Proposes a taxonomy for FL deployments (intra-body, body-hub, cross-user, clinical-cloud), introduces BODYFED-HBC as reference architecture, provides optimization formulation and scheduling algorithm, and specifies reproducible simulation vignette combining public wearable datasets with empirical body-coupled-communication models.

Result: Identifies the open problem of body-channel-aware FL where learning protocols (client selection, update compression, aggregation) are controlled by posture-dependent HBC links, energy, memory, and privacy risk. Provides concrete research agenda with reference architecture and simulation framework.

Conclusion: The paper establishes a research agenda at the intersection of HBC and FL for wearables, highlighting the need for body-channel-aware FL protocols and providing tools (taxonomy, architecture, optimization formulation) for future research in this emerging field.

Abstract: Human-body communication (HBC) is a promising physical substrate for wearable body-area networks because it can localize communication around the body and reduce the burden of conventional radio links. Federated learning (FL) is a promising learning substrate because it can reduce raw-data centralization for physiological and behavioral sensing. Yet these two literatures remain weakly connected: FL for wearables usually abstracts the communication layer, whereas HBC research usually abstracts learning and model-update traffic. This article surveys the intersection of HBC, wireless body-area networks, wearable FL, Internet-of-Bodies privacy, and edge-intelligence optimization. We propose a taxonomy that distinguishes intra-body, body-hub, cross-user, and clinical-cloud FL deployments, and we identify the open problem of body-channel-aware FL: learning protocols whose client selection, update compression, and aggregation are controlled by posture-dependent HBC links, residual energy, sensor memory, and privacy risk. To make the research agenda concrete, we introduce BODYFED-HBC as a reference architecture and provide an optimization formulation and scheduling algorithm. We further specify a reproducible simulation vignette that combines public wearable datasets with empirical body-coupled-communication signal-loss models. The article concludes with open datasets, evaluation metrics, limitations, and research directions for computer scientists working above the hardware layer.

</details>


### [182] [Generative Representation Learning on Hyper-relational Knowledge Graphs via Masked Discrete Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24064)
*Jaejun Lee,Seheon Kim,Joyce Jiyoung Whang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KREPE is a generative method for hyper-relational knowledge graphs that uses masked discrete diffusion to generate complete facts from partially masked queries, unifying link prediction and fact generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods treat HKG inference as simple link prediction, assuming only one component is missing per fact. However, real-world scenarios often have multiple or all components missing simultaneously in complex facts.

Method: KREPE uses a masked discrete diffusion approach to model probability distributions of missing components. It captures intra-fact dependencies through contextual message passing and inter-fact correlations by aggregating stochastically sampled contexts from the global HKG structure.

Result: KREPE achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard HKG link prediction benchmarks and outperforms LLM-based baselines in generating novel and correct facts.

Conclusion: The proposed KREPE framework successfully bridges the gap between traditional link prediction and real-world fact generation needs, providing a unified approach for completing partially observed facts and generating facts from scratch in hyper-relational knowledge graphs.

Abstract: Hyper-relational knowledge graphs (HKGs) effectively represent complex facts. While inferring new knowledge in HKGs is a critical problem, current methods cast it as a simple link prediction, assuming that nearly all entities and relations within a fact are known, leaving only a single blank to be filled. However, this restricted assumption may not hold in real-world scenarios in which multiple, or even all, constituent components of a fact may be missing simultaneously. To bridge this gap, we introduce a task called fact generation: generating a valid hyper-relational fact from an arbitrarily masked query, i.e., completing a partially observed fact or generating a fact from scratch. We propose KREPE, the first generative representation learning method for HKGs that learns to model the probability distributions of missing components conditioned on the local fact components and global structure of HKGs via a masked discrete diffusion. KREPE models both the intra-fact dependencies by contextual message passing and inter-fact correlations by aggregating stochastically sampled contexts. KREPE seamlessly unifies link prediction and fact generation within a single training framework, achieving state-of-the-art performance on standard HKG link prediction benchmarks and outperforming LLM-based baselines in generating novel and correct facts.

</details>


### [183] [Not All Transitions Matter: Evidence from PPO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24071)
*Ajhesh Basnet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Randomly dropping 25% of transitions from rollout buffers breaks redundant gradient structure in PPO training, improving stability without changing core algorithms or harming performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: On-policy RL suffers from correlated, redundant gradients due to causal chaining of consecutive states in rollouts, leading to repetitive gradient signals, value network struggles, and subtle training instabilities that reward curves don't reveal.

Method: Simple random dropout of a fixed fraction of transitions from rollout buffers at appropriate stage (preserving reward signal), with no new components or core algorithm modifications. Works with any PPO implementation.

Result: Across five environments (CartPole-v1, Acrobot-v1, LunarLander-v2, HalfCheetah-v5, Hopper-v5), matches vanilla PPO on reward while improving training consistency across KL divergence, policy entropy, and value estimates. 25% dropout identified as optimal.

Conclusion: Transition redundancy can be effectively removed through simple dropout, dramatically improving training stability without algorithm redesign or performance trade-offs, suggesting redundant gradient structure is a key source of PPO instability.

Abstract: Training a reinforcement learning agent on-policy means collecting fresh experience at every update, and that experience comes with a hidden problem. Each state in a rollout is the direct output of the previous one, causally chained together by the agent's own actions. Because of this, consecutive transitions are never truly independent. They carry overlapping information, and the gradient signal the network receives ends up far more repetitive than the batch size suggests. The same directions get reinforced over and over, the value network struggles to keep up as the policy shifts, and training becomes quietly unstable in ways that reward curves alone rarely reveal.
  This paper asks whether that redundancy can simply be removed. We show that randomly dropping a fixed fraction of transitions from the rollout, at the right stage so the reward signal stays intact, is enough to break the repetitive gradient structure and stabilize training. The change is minimal: one sampling step, no new components, no modification to the core algorithm, and it works with any PPO implementation. Across five environments of increasing difficulty, CartPole-v1, Acrobot-v1, LunarLander-v2, HalfCheetah-v5, and Hopper-v5, the method matches vanilla PPO on reward while producing more consistent training dynamics across KL divergence, policy entropy, and value estimates. Dropping 25% of transitions turns out to be the sweet spot: enough to disrupt the redundancy, not enough to thin the batch.

</details>


### [184] [Verified SHAP: Provable Bounds for Exact Shapley Values of Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24084)
*David Boetius,Shahaf Bassan,Guy Katz,Stefan Leue,Tobias Sutter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a novel algorithm that leverages neural network verification techniques to compute exact SHAP bounds for neural networks, enabling exact SHAP computation on much larger search spaces than previous methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SHAP (Shapley Additive Explanations) is computationally intractable for neural networks due to exponential search space over input features. Current exact methods cannot scale to practical neural network applications.

Method: Introduces an algorithm that uses recent advances in neural network verification to compute arbitrarily tight exact lower and upper bounds on SHAP values, ultimately recovering exact SHAP values through these bounds.

Result: The approach scales to orders of magnitude larger search spaces than state-of-the-art exact methods, enabling exact SHAP computation on practical neural network problems.

Conclusion: This represents an important first step toward practical exact SHAP computation and establishes a principled foundation for evaluating statistical approximation methods on larger search spaces.

Abstract: Shapley additive explanations (SHAP) are widely recognised as computationally intractable for neural networks, since they induce an exponential search space over the input features. In this work, we take a first step towards scaling exact SHAP computation to larger search spaces by introducing an algorithm that leverages recent advances in neural network verification to compute arbitrarily tight exact lower and upper bounds on SHAP values for neural networks, ultimately recovering the exact SHAP values. We demonstrate that our approach scales to orders of magnitude larger search spaces than state-of-the-art exact methods. This provides an important first step towards exact SHAP computation and establishes a principled cornerstone for evaluating statistical approximation methods on larger search spaces.

</details>


### [185] [Overcoming "Physics Shock" in Earth Observation A Heteroscedastic Uncertainty Framework for PINN-based Flood Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24106)
*Tewodros Syum Gebre,Jagrati Talreja,Matilda Anokye,Leila Hashemi-Beni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Uncertainty-Aware Physics-Informed Neural Network framework for flood mapping that uses dynamic physics constraints and uncertainty modeling to handle noisy SAR data while maintaining physical consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Deep Learning models for flood mapping from SAR data produce physically impossible predictions due to lack of hydrological constraints. Traditional Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) fail with real-world remote sensing data due to "Physics Shock" - catastrophic gradient divergence when enforcing rigid spatial derivatives on noisy data.

Method: Novel Uncertainty-Aware PINN framework with: 1) Dynamic Warm-Start protocol, 2) Heteroscedastic aleatoric uncertainty modeling via negative log-likelihood objective, 3) Probabilistic Attention-Gated FNO-UNet architecture. The framework dynamically relaxes physical constraints in high-noise regions while strictly enforcing them in high-confidence areas.

Result: Achieved +25% relative improvement in Intersection over Union (IoU) on Sen1Floods11 dataset compared to deterministic baselines. Successfully disentangled intrinsic sensor noise from out-of-distribution terrain ignorance using Deep Ensembles, providing highly calibrated, physically consistent confidence bounds.

Conclusion: The proposed framework stabilizes multi-objective optimization for applied Earth Observation, addressing the Physics Shock problem and enabling operational agencies to have robust, physically consistent confidence bounds for real-time disaster decision-making.

Abstract: Rapid and accurate flood extent mapping from Remote Sensing data, such as Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR), is critical for operational disaster response, but standard Deep Learning models often produce physically impossible predictions due to a lack of hydrological constraints. While PhysicsInformed Neural Networks (PINNs) attempt to address this by embedding governing laws directly into the loss function, their application to real-world remote sensing data frequently fails. Enforcing rigid spatial derivatives (e.g., the 2D Shallow Water Equations) onto unconditioned latent spaces attempting to fit noisy SAR speckle causes catastrophic gradient divergence, a phenomenon we term Physics Shock. In this paper, we propose a novel Uncertainty-Aware PINN framework tailored specifically for applied Earth Observation that addresses this instability. By integrating a dynamic Warm-Start protocol and modeling heteroscedastic aleatoric uncertainty via a negative log-likelihood objective, the network learns to dynamically relax physical constraints in regions of high sensor noise while strictly enforcing them in high-confidence areas. Evaluated on the Sen1Floods11 dataset, our probabilistic Attention-Gated FNO-UNet successfully stabilizes multi-objective optimization, achieving a +25% relative improvement in Intersection over Union (IoU) compared to deterministic baselines. Furthermore, through Deep Ensembles, we successfully disentangle intrinsic sensor noise from out-of-distribution terrain ignorance, providing operational agencies with highly calibrated, physically consistent confidence bounds for robust disaster mitigation and real-time decision-making.

</details>


### [186] [Riemannian Archetypal Analysis: Interpretable non-linear data analysis on deformed star distributions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24113)
*Willem Diepeveen,Deanna Needell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A Riemannian version of archetypal analysis that combines interpretability of classical archetypal analysis with expressive power of modern non-linear models using data-driven pullback geometry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical archetypal analysis has good interpretability but limited performance on non-linear data, while neural extensions improve flexibility but weaken geometric meaning. There's a need to combine interpretability with expressive power for non-linear data.

Method: Develops Riemannian archetypal analysis using data-driven pullback geometry: introduces deformed star distributions with associated pullback Riemannian geometry, defines Riemannian archetypal mapping (RAM) as projection onto manifold of geodesically convex combinations, and proposes optimization scheme with convex relaxation followed by non-convex refinement. Also includes learning scheme for deformed star distributions from data.

Result: The framework produces meaningful geodesics, useful denoising projections, and geometry-aware classifications. Experiments on synthetic examples and MNIST demonstrate these capabilities while highlighting current optimization limitations.

Conclusion: Successfully combines interpretability of classical archetypal analysis with expressive power of non-linear models through Riemannian geometry, creating a geometry-aware framework with practical applications, though optimization challenges remain.

Abstract: Classical archetypal analysis is appealing for its interpretability, but its linear geometry can limit performance on data with strongly non-linear structure; at the same time, existing neural extensions improve flexibility while often weakening the geometric meaning of archetypes and interpolations. In this work, we develop a Riemannian version of archetypal analysis based on data-driven pullback geometry for real-valued data, with the goal of combining the interpretability of classical archetypal analysis with the expressive power of modern non-linear models. We introduce a class of deformed star distributions together with associated pullback Riemannian geometry to provide a statistical interpretation of the resulting manifold mappings, define the Riemannian archetypal mapping (RAM) as a projection onto the manifold of geodesically convex combinations of archetypes, and propose a practical optimization scheme based on convex relaxation followed by non-convex refinement. We further propose a learning scheme that yields reasonable, albeit generally suboptimal, deformed star distributions from data. Experiments on synthetic examples and MNIST show that the resulting framework produces meaningful geodesics, useful denoising projections, and geometry-aware classifications, while also clarifying where current optimization limitations remain.

</details>


### [187] [Knowledge Graph Modulated Deep Learning for Limited-Sample Clinical Data Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24162)
*Yuwei Xue,Sakib Mostafa,James Zou,Joseph Liao,Maximilian Diehn,Ash A. Alizadeh,Lei Xing,Md. Tauhidul Islam*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Graph-in-Graph (GiG) is a knowledge graph-modulated deep learning framework that integrates biological knowledge graphs with patient data for improved clinical prediction, especially in limited-sample settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current biomedical AI models cannot directly use graph-encoded biological knowledge, requiring compressed representations that lose important structural information and reduce performance in limited-sample clinical studies.

Method: GiG represents each patient as a standalone modular graph where curated biological knowledge graphs define edges and patient-specific measurements (e.g., gene expression) define node features, allowing integration of multiple knowledge graphs while preserving gene-gene interactions and pathway topology.

Result: Across nearly 9,700 patients and five clinical tasks, GiG consistently outperforms traditional and state-of-the-art methods, with largest gains in limited-sample settings (e.g., 49 percentage point improvement in macro-F1 for prostate cancer diagnosis). Control experiments confirm gains come from biologically grounded knowledge graph structure rather than graph modeling alone.

Conclusion: Knowledge graph-modulated deep learning improves robustness, interpretability, and sample efficiency in clinical data analysis, providing a principled framework for integrating biological knowledge graphs into predictive modeling.

Abstract: Biological systems are governed by structured molecular interactions, where pathways, regulatory circuits, and functional gene relationships shape cellular behavior and disease progression. Much of this knowledge is naturally represented as graphs. However, most biomedical AI models cannot directly use graph-encoded biological knowledge and instead require compressed low-dimensional representations, which can lose important structure and reduce performance, especially in limited-sample clinical studies. Here, we introduce Graph-in-Graph (GiG), a knowledge graph-modulated deep learning framework for data-efficient clinical prediction. GiG represents each patient as a standalone modular graph, in which curated biological knowledge graphs define edges and patient-specific measurements, such as gene expression, define node features. This design allows multiple biological knowledge graphs to be integrated while preserving gene-gene interactions and pathway topology during patient-level representation learning. Across cohorts comprising nearly 9,700 patients and five clinical tasks, including liquid biopsy cancer detection, prostate cancer diagnosis, and 32-class pan-cancer classification, GiG consistently outperforms traditional and state-of-the-art methods, with the largest gains in limited-sample settings. On the challenging prostate cancer diagnosis task, GiG improves macro-F1 by up to 49 percentage points relative to competing methods. Control experiments replacing real pathway graphs with random topologies confirm that these gains arise from biologically grounded knowledge graph structure rather than graph modeling alone. These findings show that knowledge graph-modulated deep learning can improve robustness, interpretability, and sample efficiency in clinical data analysis, and provide a principled framework for integrating biological knowledge graphs into predictive modeling.

</details>


### [188] [PromptAudit: Auditing Prompt Sensitivity in LLM-Based Vulnerability Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24171)
*Steffen J. Camarato,Yahya Hmaiti,Mandana Ghadamian,David Mohaisen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PromptAudit analysis shows standard chain-of-thought prompts work best for vulnerability detection, while adaptive reasoning and self-consistency often harm performance. Prompt sensitivity varies significantly between models and must be characterized for reliable evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models are increasingly used for vulnerability detection, but their reliability under different prompt formulations remains uncharacterized. There's a need to understand how different prompting strategies affect detection performance systematically.

Method: Developed PromptAudit framework that isolates prompt effects by fixing dataset, decoding, and parsing while varying only prompting strategies. Evaluated five prompting strategies across five open-weight models on 1,000 CVEs (6,074 code samples across 16 programming languages), measuring accuracy, recall, abstention, coverage, and effective F1.

Result: Standard chain-of-thought prompting achieved the strongest overall operational performance. Few-shot prompting provided model-dependent benefits most pronounced for prompt-sensitive models. Adaptive chain-of-thought frequently suppressed recall, while self-consistency induced excessive abstention, sharply reducing effective performance.

Conclusion: Vulnerability detection performance is jointly determined by both the model and the prompt strategy, with prompt sensitivity being a crucial system property that must be explicitly characterized for reliable evaluation and deployment.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly used for vulnerability detection, yet their reliability under different prompt formulations remains uncharacterized. We present PromptAudit, a controlled evaluation framework that isolates prompt effects by fixing the dataset, decoding, and parsing while varying only the prompting strategy. Using five prompting strategies across five open-weight models on 1,000 CVEs (6,074 code samples spanning 16 programming languages), we evaluate accuracy, recall, abstention, coverage, and effective F1. We find that standard chain-of-thought prompting achieves the strongest overall operational performance, while few-shot prompting provides model-dependent benefits that are most pronounced for prompt-sensitive models. In contrast, adaptive chain-of-thought frequently suppresses recall and self-consistency induces excessive abstention, sharply reducing effective performance. These results show that vulnerability detection behavior is jointly determined by the model and the prompt, and that prompt sensitivity is a first-class system property that must be explicitly characterized in evaluation and deployment.

</details>


### [189] [Filtered Posterior Mean Collections: A Unified Framework for Analytical Models of Diffusion Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24192)
*Matthew Niedoba,Berend Zwartsenberg,Frank Wood*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Filtered Posterior Mean Collections (FPMCs), a unified model class for neural-network denoising in diffusion models that generalizes prior patch-based methods and shows improved performance through soft relaxations and source distribution augmentations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural-network denoising functions in image diffusion models show remarkable consistency in generalization behavior across different architectures and training procedures. Prior research has modeled these networks by aggregating posterior weighted averages of training dataset patches, and the authors seek to consolidate these approaches into a unified framework.

Method: Introduces Filtered Posterior Mean Collections (FPMCs), defined using three design axes: query precision vectors, response weights, and source distributions. Shows that existing patch-based methods are special cases of FPMCs with specific choices along these axes. Investigates each axis to improve performance through soft relaxations of prior methods and augmentations of source distributions.

Result: Demonstrates consistent sample improvement across three natural image datasets by applying the proposed FPMC enhancements to an existing method, showing that performance can be improved through the identified design modifications.

Conclusion: FPMCs provide a unified framework for understanding and improving patch-based denoising methods in diffusion models, with specific enhancements through soft relaxations and source distribution augmentations leading to measurable performance gains on natural image datasets.

Abstract: The neural-network denoising functions which form the backbone of image diffusion models are remarkably consistent in their generalization behaviour across a wide variety of network architectures and training procedure hyperparameters. A recent line of research has sought to model the outputs of these networks by aggregating posterior weighted averages of training dataset patches. In this work, we consolidate these approaches into a unified model class which we call Filtered Posterior Mean Collections (FPMCs). We define this model class using query precision vectors, response weights, and source distributions, and illustrate that existing methods are recoverable with specific choices of these design axes. Investigating each axis in turn, we find that FPMC performance can be improved with soft relaxations of prior patch-based methods, and through augmentations of source distributions. Applying these findings to an existing FPMC, we demonstrate consistent sample improvement across three natural image datasets.

</details>


### [190] [Characterizing the Representational Capacity of Neural Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24210)
*Robin Young*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper provides a theoretical analysis of Neural Processes architectures, establishing a strict hierarchy of representational capacity among different NP variants and characterizing what functions each can represent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the fundamental representational capabilities and limitations of different Neural Processes architectures, which is crucial for informed architecture selection based on task requirements rather than empirical trial-and-error.

Method: Theoretical analysis and mathematical proofs of representational capacity hierarchies, examining Conditional Neural Processes (CNPs), Attentive Neural Processes (ANPs), Transformer Neural Processes (TNPs), and their latent variants through formal mathematical characterization.

Result: Established a strict hierarchy: CNPs represent functions depending on finitely many expected features; ANPs strictly generalize CNPs via query-dependent reweighting; ConvCNPs and ANPs are incomparable; TNPs capture L-hop context interactions with L self-attention layers; finite-dimensional latents don't overcome encoder limitations and require dimension scaling to match GP posteriors.

Conclusion: The theoretical hierarchy provides a foundation for systematic architecture selection based on task structure, revealing fundamental trade-offs between different NP architectures and their ability to capture specific properties like stationarity, translation equivariance, and context interactions.

Abstract: What functions can Neural Processes represent? We analyze the representational capacity of popular NP architectures: Conditional Neural Processes (CNPs), Attentive Neural Processes (ANPs), Transformer Neural Processes (TNPs), and their latent variants. We prove these architectures form a strict hierarchy. CNP-representable functions are exactly those depending on finitely many expected features of the context distribution. ANPs strictly generalize CNPs via query-dependent reweighting, enabling kernel smoothers. ConvCNPs and ANPs are incomparable; each contains functions outside the other, separated by stationarity versus translation equivariance. TNPs with $L$ self-attention layers capture $L$-hop context interactions. For latent NPs, we show finite-dimensional latents provide coherent sampling but do not circumvent encoder limitations; matching GP posterior distributions requires latent dimension scaling with context size. These results provide a theoretical foundation for architecture selection based on task structure.

</details>


### [191] [Agent-ToM: Learning to Monitor Autonomous LLM Agents via Theory-of-Mind Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24216)
*Nesreen K. Ahmed,Nima Nafisi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Agent-ToM is a learning-to-monitor framework using Theory-of-Mind reasoning to detect covert malicious behavior in autonomous LLM agents by inferring beliefs, intentions, and deviations from expected behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monitoring autonomous LLM agents is challenging due to delayed, context-dependent, and long-horizon attack patterns. Agents can pursue hidden objectives while appearing benign, making detection difficult even with full trajectory access. Existing approaches treat each trajectory independently and don't learn from prior monitoring experience, lacking explicit reasoning about agent beliefs, intentions, and goal alignment.

Method: Agent-ToM performs structured full-trajectory analysis by: 1) Inferring beliefs and intent hypotheses with calibrated confidence, 2) Computing expected actions and deviations from task-consistent baselines, 3) Using a "Reason-Verify-Refine" pipeline at inference for monitoring decisions, and 4) Distilling critique signals into persistent semantic guardrail memory at training time for reusable belief- and intent-conditioned constraints across episodes.

Result: Agent-ToM achieves strong precision-recall balance and outperforms state-of-the-art monitoring baselines (including ensemble methods) on adversarial agent monitoring benchmarks (SHADE-Arena and CUA-SHADE-Arena), using a coherent two-call reasoning pipeline.

Conclusion: Learning at the monitoring layer combined with structured Theory-of-Mind reasoning and verification provides an effective and deployable foundation for securing autonomous LLM agents against covert malicious behavior.

Abstract: Monitoring autonomous large language model (LLM) agents for covert malicious behavior is challenging due to delayed, context-dependent, and long-horizon attack patterns. Agents may pursue hidden objectives while maintaining superficially benign behavior, making detection difficult even with full trajectory access. Prior monitoring approaches improve scaffolding or ensemble aggregation, but treat each trajectory independently and do not learn from prior monitoring experience. Moreover, standard reasoning methods explain observed behavior without explicitly reasoning about agent beliefs, intentions, and goal alignment required to distinguish benign task execution from covert deviation.
  We propose \textbf{Agent-ToM}, a learning-to-monitor framework grounded in Theory-of-Mind (ToM) reasoning for security analysis of autonomous agents. Agent-ToM performs structured full-trajectory analysis by inferring beliefs, intent hypotheses with calibrated confidence, expected actions, and deviations from task-consistent behavioral baselines. At inference time, it employs a \textit{Reason-Verify-Refine} pipeline to construct and validate monitoring decisions. At training time, Agent-ToM distills critique signals into a persistent \textit{semantic guardrail memory}, enabling reusable belief- and intent-conditioned constraints across episodes. We evaluate Agent-ToM on adversarial agent monitoring benchmarks (SHADE-Arena and CUA-SHADE-Arena). Agent-ToM achieves strong precision-recall balance and outperforms state-of-the-art monitoring baselines, including ensemble methods, while using a coherent two-call reasoning pipeline. These results demonstrate that learning at the monitoring layer, combined with structured ToM reasoning and verification, provides an effective and deployable foundation for securing autonomous LLM agents.

</details>


### [192] [PrivFusion: A Privacy-preserving Multi-Agent Framework for Harmonizing Distributed Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24249)
*Anisa Halimi,Liubov Nedoshivina,Kieran Fraser,Stefano Braghin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PrivFusion is a privacy-preserving federated learning framework that automates harmonization of heterogeneous clinical datasets across institutions without centralized data aggregation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical data aggregation is often infeasible due to privacy concerns, while federated learning faces adoption limitations because of substantial heterogeneity across institutional datasets that makes harmonization a critical but frequently overlooked prerequisite.

Method: PrivFusion uses a multi-agent framework where agents analyze local data, cluster semantically similar features across sites, and provide iterative transformation recommendations until alignment is achieved, all while preserving privacy.

Result: Evaluation across four heterogeneous COVID-19 datasets demonstrates that PrivFusion effectively and efficiently harmonizes multi-site data while substantially reducing manual effort.

Conclusion: PrivFusion provides an automated, privacy-preserving solution to the data harmonization problem that enables more effective federated learning in clinical settings.

Abstract: The growing availability of clinical data has increased the use of machine learning, yet centralized data aggregation is often infeasible for sensitive health information. Federated Learning (FL) offers a distributed alternative, but its adoption is limited by substantial heterogeneity across institutional datasets, making harmonization a critical but frequently overlooked prerequisite for multi-site analytics. We introduce PrivFusion, a privacy-preserving multi-agent framework that automates the harmonization of structured datasets prior to federated training. PrivFusion uses agents to analyze local data, cluster semantically similar features across sites, and provide iterative transformation recommendations until alignment is achieved. Evaluation across four heterogeneous COVID-19 datasets demonstrates that PrivFusion effectively and efficiently harmonizes multi-site data while substantially reducing manual effort.

</details>


### [193] [Rethinking Continual Anomaly Detection on the Edge: Benchmarking Under Realistic Industrial Conditions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24251)
*Chad Weatherly,Sen Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a comprehensive benchmark for Continual Anomaly Detection revealing current methods' limitations, and proposes DINOSaur - a training-free, highly efficient method that outperforms all existing approaches for edge deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Continual Anomaly Detection (CAD) methods have three critical gaps: unrealistic evaluation protocols, lack of systematic comparison between methods, and no consideration of edge deployment constraints needed for industrial inspection systems to adapt to evolving production conditions.

Method: 1. Created a unified benchmark for CAD with: discrete-task evaluation for structural/logical anomalies, continuous drift protocol, head-to-head comparison of all CAD methods, and computational efficiency profiling on edge hardware. 2. Proposed DINOSaur method: uses a frozen DINOv3 backbone with spatially-indexed coreset memory and neighborhood-restricted anomaly scoring for training-free inference.

Result: Benchmark results show existing CAD methods fail to consistently outperform traditional approaches with simple experience replay. DINOSaur achieves zero forgetting by construction, outperforms all evaluated methods across all five protocols, runs at sub-100ms inference on NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, and adapts to new tasks on-device in under 30 seconds.

Conclusion: The paper establishes a new comprehensive benchmark for Continual Anomaly Detection (CAD) and introduces DINOSaur, a training-free method that outperforms all existing approaches while being highly efficient for edge deployment.

Abstract: Continual anomaly detection (CAD) addresses the need for industrial inspection systems to adapt to evolving production conditions, yet existing methods share three critical gaps: unrealistic evaluation, no systematic comparison, and no consideration of edge deployment constraints. We introduce a unified benchmark combining discrete-task evaluation on structural and logical anomalies, a novel continuous drift protocol, the first head-to-head comparison of all published CAD methods, and computational efficiency profiling on edge hardware. Our results reveal that existing CAD methods do not consistently outperform traditional approaches with simple experience replay. Thus motivated, we propose DINOSaur, a training-free method combining a frozen DINOv3 backbone with spatially-indexed coreset memory and neighborhood-restricted anomaly scoring. DINOSaur achieves zero forgetting by construction, outperforms all evaluated methods across all five protocols, and runs at sub-100\,ms inference on an NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, with on-device adaptation to new tasks in under 30 seconds.

</details>


### [194] [Optimizing Digital Therapeutic Interventions: Online Learning under Endogenous Adherence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24261)
*Eric Pulick,Stephanie Carpenter,Matthew Buman,Yonatan Mintz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes UCB-BOLD, an online algorithm for digital therapeutics that models both treatment recommendation effects and past adherence on future adherence, achieving better patient outcomes than benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing digital therapeutics decision support systems either ignore the effect of past adherence on future adherence or treat adherence as exogenous, creating a gap in effectively managing patient interventions for chronic diseases with limited clinical resources.

Method: Uses a linear dynamical system (LDS) to model patient engagement capacity, capturing both treatment recommendation effects and adherence history, with adherence behavior linked via logit function. Proposes UCB-BOLD, an optimism-based algorithm for online treatment selection with proven sublinear regret guarantees.

Result: The model has finite-time identification guarantees. UCB-BOLD achieves 2-3x lower conditional value-at-risk regret than next-best benchmarks in synthetic patient cohort simulations using micro-randomized trial data, showing significant benefits for certain patient types.

Conclusion: Digital therapeutics decision support should incorporate dynamical models that capture both recommendation and adherence effects, as this enables more effective resource allocation and improved patient health outcomes compared to myopic approaches, especially for certain patient populations.

Abstract: A critical challenge facing clinicians managing chronic disease interventions is sustaining long-run patient health given limited information and resources. Digital therapeutics (DTs) provide a cost-effective way to manage interventions at scale through repeated interactions (e.g. daily treatment recommendations), but patient success is highly dependent on their adherence. Behavioral psychology suggests that both treatment recommendations and past adherence affect future adherence, yet existing decision support frameworks for DTs model only recommendation effects or treat adherence as exogenous context, leaving a key gap in model and algorithm development. To address this gap, we present a DT decision support framework that captures both recommendation and adherence effects, allowing clinicians to better plan treatment recommendations. We model a patient's time-varying capacity for engagement with treatment using a linear dynamical system (LDS) that captures both recommendation and adherence effects, endogenously connected to adherence behavior with a logit link. We establish finite-time identification guarantees for this model, extending LDS results to our setting. Next, we propose an optimism-based algorithm, UCB-BOLD, for online treatment selection and prove that it achieves sublinear regret. We evaluate UCB-BOLD against benchmarks via ablation studies on a synthetic patient cohort generated using micro-randomized trial data. DT decision support tools can include dynamical models to enable decision makers to efficiently use the data in DT settings to improve patient health through effective resource allocation. While myopic or heuristic approaches suffice for some patient types, the benefits of explicitly planning around recommendation and adherence effects are significant for others; UCB-BOLD achieves 2-3x lower conditional value-at-risk regret than the next-best benchmark.

</details>


### [195] [A lift for input-convex neural network training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24274)
*Ali Siahkoohi,Anirudh Thatipelli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes using a hypernetwork to generate non-negative ICNN weights instead of direct constraint methods like projected gradient descent or softplus reparametrization, solving gradient attenuation and training stall issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard methods for enforcing non-negative weight constraints in ICNNs (projected gradient descent and softplus reparametrization) have critical flaws: PGD uses hard, non-smooth projections with poor convergence guarantees on ICNN training landscape, while softplus causes exponential gradient attenuation leading to dead weights and plateaued loss.

Method: Instead of directly constraining inter-layer weights, train an unconstrained hypernetwork that emits these weights from a permutation-invariant summary of input batches. The approach adds stochasticity that softens the loss landscape and includes three key components: learnable bias as slack, hypernetwork body conditioned on target batch, and cross-covariance coupling through batch stochasticity.

Result: The lift method reaches lower test loss than both PGD and direct softplus across tasks including log-concave energy-based modeling (from 1D toy targets to image-flavored latents) and convex-potential normalizing flows on 21-dimensional tabular benchmarks. It transforms plateau-bounded training trajectories into valley-descending ones.

Conclusion: The hypernetwork-based approach effectively addresses fundamental limitations of existing constraint methods for ICNN training by introducing beneficial stochasticity and structural components that prevent gradient attenuation and training stalls, enabling better optimization performance across various applications.

Abstract: Input-convex neural networks (ICNNs) are widely used for log-concave density estimation, convex-potential normalizing flows, optimal transport, and transport-map inversion for high-dimensional Bayesian posteriors. These tasks share a structural constraint: the inter-layer weights of the ICNN must remain non-negative. The standard recipe, projected gradient descent (PGD) onto the non-negative cone, applies a hard, non-smooth projection -- the stiff-penalty limit of an ADMM-style constraint splitting -- and its classical convergence guarantees do not transfer to the non-smooth ICNN training landscape; the differentiable alternative, softplus reparametrization, attenuates the gradient exponentially in the weight magnitude, stalling training with dead inter-layer weights and plateaued loss. Inspired by parameter-extension lifts of PDE-constrained inverse problems, we propose the lift: instead of constraining the inter-layer weights directly, we train an unconstrained hypernetwork that emits them from a permutation-invariant summary of the input batch. This adds stochasticity to the training dynamics that softens the loss landscape, letting the iterates escape the gradient-attenuated region where direct softplus stalls. We trace this softening to three structural ingredients -- a learnable bias acting as slack, a hypernetwork body that conditions on the target batch, and a cross-covariance coupling the two through batch stochasticity -- and prove each one necessary: deleting any single ingredient collapses the cross-covariance that carries the softening. On log-concave energy-based modeling from one-dimensional toy targets to image-flavored latents, and convex-potential normalizing flows on a 21-dimensional tabular benchmark, we show that the lift reaches a lower test loss than both PGD and direct softplus, and turns a plateau-bounded training trajectory into a valley-descending one.

</details>


### [196] [Fourier Feature Pyramids for Physics-Informed Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24278)
*Brandon Zhao,Yixuan Wang,Jonathan T. Barron,Katherine L. Bouman,Dor Verbin,Pratul P. Srinivasan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: beignet is a neural field architecture with trainable multi-resolution Fourier feature pyramid that outperforms PINNs in accuracy and efficiency for solving PDEs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current PINNs struggle with accuracy and poor computational scaling with parameter count, requiring more efficient architectures for solving PDEs.

Method: Replaces random Fourier features with trainable multi-resolution Fourier feature pyramid, uses Fourier interpolation at each pyramid level, and decodes with fully-connected network trunk.

Result: Achieves significantly more accurate solutions with fewer parameters than state-of-the-art PINNs, solves self-similar inviscid Burgers blowup to near machine precision with Adam optimizer.

Conclusion: beignet provides efficient, accurate PDE solving with better computational scaling, spectral derivative computation, and controlled bandlimit for stable optimization.

Abstract: We present an improved neural field architecture for solving partial differential equations (PDEs). Current physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) provide a flexible framework for solving PDEs, but they struggle to achieve highly accurate solutions and require computation that scales poorly with parameter count. Our model, which we call beignet (Bandlimited Embedding with Interpolated Grid Network), replaces the random Fourier feature embedding used by existing PINN models with a trainable multi-resolution Fourier feature pyramid. To query beignet at a continuous coordinate, we use Fourier interpolation at each level of the pyramid to return features at the input coordinate, and then decode this vector with a fully-connected neural network trunk. Our model provides multiple benefits: 1) Spatial derivatives can be computed efficiently by using the chain rule to compose derivatives of the neural network computed with automatic differentiation with derivatives of the feature grid computed spectrally by the Fast Fourier transform (FFT). 2) beignet can achieve higher accuracy in a compute-efficient manner by scaling the parameter count of this Fourier feature pyramid, instead of the less-efficient strategy of scaling the neural network architecture. 3) beignet can directly control the representation bandlimit, resulting in more stable optimization for difficult PDEs. We demonstrate that beignet finds significantly more accurate solutions on PDE benchmarks using fewer parameters than state-of-the-art PINN methods. We further evaluate beignet on the self-similar inviscid Burgers blowup problem and show that it can minimize residuals to near machine precision using Adam, an accuracy regime previously attained only by using computationally expensive higher-order optimizers.

</details>


### [197] [Faithfulness as Information Flow: Evaluating and Training Faithful Chain-of-Thought Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24286)
*Jinghan Jia,Joe Benton,Eric Easley*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a framework to test and improve CoT reasoning faithfulness via information-flow diagnostics and interventions that strengthen mediated reasoning over shortcuts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning is useful only if the reasoning trace faithfully reflects the computation leading to the final answer. However, models may rely on prompt-to-answer shortcuts, making the visible reasoning misleading even when plausible.

Method: An information-flow perspective based on three properties (sufficiency, completeness, necessity) with entropy-based, masked-KL, and gradient-based diagnostics; update-time interventions like attention masking, backward-only gradient masking, CoT gradients, and adversarial perturbations of prompt representations were tested via verifier-based on-policy RL across hinted arithmetic, reward-hackable code repair, and DAPO-Math models.

Result: Interventions shifted behavioral and structural indicators toward stronger CoT mediation, making shortcut and reward-hacking behavior more transparent in the CoT and improving task-agnostic faithfulness metrics, with some reductions in wrong-hint susceptibility.

Conclusion: Controlling information flow during training via the proposed interventions offers a practical approach for achieving more faithful and monitorable CoT reasoning.

Abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning is useful for monitoring language models only when the reasoning trace faithfully reflects the computation that produces the final answer. However, models can rely on prompt-to-answer shortcuts that bypass the CoT, making the visible reasoning trace misleading even when it appears plausible. We study CoT faithfulness through a structural information-flow perspective: faithful reasoning should route answer-relevant information through the mediated path from prompt to CoT to answer, rather than through a direct prompt-to-answer shortcut. This perspective yields a task-agnostic framework based on three complementary properties, sufficiency, completeness, and necessity, which we instantiate with entropy-based, masked-KL, and gradient-based diagnostics. We show that these metrics recover externally judged faithfulness differences in hinted reasoning, and identify a low-entropy failure mode of KL-based diagnostics where gradient-based measures remain more stable. Building on this analysis, we introduce update-time interventions for verifier-based on-policy RL, including attention masking, backward-only gradient masking, CoT gradients, and adversarial perturbations of prompt representations. Across hinted arithmetic, reward-hackable code repair, and DAPO-Math models trained without hints but evaluated under wrong-hint injection, our interventions shift behavioral and structural indicators toward stronger CoT mediation. In particular, they make shortcut and reward-hacking behavior more transparent in the CoT and improve task-agnostic faithfulness metrics, while in some settings also reducing wrong-hint susceptibility. Our results suggest that controlling information flow during training is a practical route toward more faithful and monitorable CoT reasoning. Code is available at https://github.com/safety-research/faithful-cot.

</details>


### [198] [TUBE: Tangent Upper Bound on Evidence for Discrete Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24292)
*Arseny Ivanov,Sergei Kholkin,Vladislav Gromadskii,Grigoriy Ksenofontov,Ivan Oseledets,Alexander Korotin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces TUBE (Tangent Upper Bound on Evidence), a variational upper bound on log-likelihood for discrete diffusion models, revealing that masked diffusion models and any-order ARMs still underperform exact autoregressive models in likelihood evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation methods for discrete diffusion models rely on the evidence lower bound (ELBO), which leaves uncertainty about how much higher the true log-likelihood might be compared to autoregressive models that allow exact computation.

Method: The authors introduce TUBE (Tangent Upper Bound on Evidence), a variational upper bound on log-likelihood that admits an unbiased Monte Carlo estimator. This method extends across various latent-variable models including masked diffusion models, any-order ARMs, and their block variants.

Result: Application of TUBE to block masked diffusion models and block any-order ARMs shows that these models lie strictly below the exact ARM baseline, demonstrating that autoregressive models still dominate in likelihood performance.

Conclusion: The TUBE framework provides a way to upper-bound log-likelihood in models where exact computation is unavailable, and empirical results confirm that autoregressive models maintain superior likelihood performance compared to diffusion-based alternatives when evaluated properly.

Abstract: Log-likelihood is a standard metric for evaluating generative models. Unfortunately, in contrast to autoregressive models (ARMs), discrete diffusion models generally do not admit exact computation of this quantity. Existing evaluations, therefore, rely on the evidence lower bound (ELBO), leaving unclear how much higher the true value may be. We address this by introducing the Tangent Upper Bound on Evidence (TUBE), a variational upper bound on log-likelihood that admits an unbiased Monte Carlo estimator. Our TUBE extends across latent-variable models, including masked diffusion models (MDMs), any-order ARMs (AO-ARMs), and block variants of both. Applied to block MDMs and block AO-ARMs, TUBE reveals our key empirical finding that these models lie strictly below the exact ARM baseline, showing that ARMs still dominate in likelihood.

</details>


### [199] [Private Adaptive Covariance Estimation via Gaussian Graphical Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24295)
*Cecilia Ferrando,Miguel Fuentes,Brett Mullins,Cameron Musco,Daniel Sheldon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PACE-GGM: Data-adaptive differentially private covariance estimation that focuses privacy budget on most informative entries and reconstructs full covariance via maximum-entropy Gaussian graphical models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard differentially private covariance estimation applies uniform noise to all entries, wasting privacy budget on less informative entries. In real settings where variables have separate bounds, individual entries can be measured with less noise than full matrix.
