  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many machine learning applications involve nonconvex nonsmooth objective functions where only stochastic function value evaluations are available, often with heavy-tailed noise. There's a need for efficient zeroth-order methods that can handle such challenging optimization settings while matching the complexity of best-known algorithms.

Method: Proposes a stochastic zeroth-order algorithm that refines the online-to-nonconvex conversion framework by clipping the two-point gradient estimator to handle heavy-tailed noise. The method uses stochastic function value evaluations rather than gradients.

Result: The algorithm finds a (δ, ε)-Goldstein stationary point with zeroth-order oracle complexity of O(d^{p/(2(p-1))} δ^{-1} ε^{-(2p-1)/(p-1)}), where d is dimension and p∈(1,2] controls moment bounds. This matches best-known results for stochastic zeroth-order optimization in convex nonsmooth problems and is consistent with best stochastic first-order algorithms for nonconvex nonsmooth problems.

Conclusion: The proposed stochastic zeroth-order algorithm effectively solves nonconvex nonsmooth problems with heavy-tailed noise, achieving state-of-the-art complexity bounds that match both zeroth-order convex results and first-order nonconvex results. Numerical experiments confirm its practical effectiveness.

Abstract: This paper considers the nonconvex nonsmooth problem in which the objective function is Lipschitz continuous. We focus on the stochastic setting where the algorithm can access stochastic function value evaluations with heavy-tailed noise, which is prevalent in many popular machine learning applications. We propose a stochastic zeroth-order algorithm that refines the framework of online-to-nonconvex conversion by clipping the two-point gradient estimator. The theoretical analysis shows that our algorithm can find a $(δ, ε)$-Goldstein stationary point with zeroth-order oracle complexity of ${\mathcal O}(d^{\frac{p}{2(p-1)}}δ^{-1}ε^{-\frac{2p-1}{p-1}})$, where $d$ is the problem dimension and $p\in(1,2]$ is the order of bounded moments. Note that our dependence on dimension $d$ matches the best-known results of stochastic zeroth-order optimization for finding the sub-optimal solution of a stochastic convex nonsmooth problem. In addition, our dependence on accuracy parameters $δ$ and $ε$ is consistent with that of the best-known stochastic first-order algorithms for stochastic nonconvex nonsmooth problems. Finally, we conduct numerical experiments to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

</details>


### [226] [Lake Detection and Water Quality Estimation in Sentinel-2 Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24515)
*Iulia Pleşu,Alexandra Băicoianu,Ioana Cristina Plajer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper compares three ML models for water body detection against traditional NDWI thresholding, and introduces specialized color schemes for visualizing water quality indices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Climate change and human pressure are making inland water resources scarcer and harder to manage sustainably. There's a growing need for reliable, automated methods to detect, monitor, and assess surface water bodies. Current visualization methods for water quality indices are often inadequate or misleading.

Method: Three distinct machine learning architectures for water body identification are investigated and compared. Performance is evaluated through quantitative metrics and real-world examples. A direct comparison with classical NDWI thresholding is conducted on a representative test image. The paper also proposes a suite of meaningful color schemes adapted for water quality indices.

Result: The analysis identifies the best-performing model in terms of accuracy, robustness, and practical applicability. The proposed color schemes facilitate clearer interpretation, comparison, and decision-making for human users in water quality assessment.

Conclusion: Machine learning approaches show advantages over traditional index-based methods for water body detection, and specialized visualization techniques improve the interpretability of water quality data for environmental applications.

Abstract: With climate change and increasing human pressure on natural landscapes, inland water resources are becoming progressively scarcer, more vulnerable, and more difficult to manage sustainably. Reliable and automated methods for detecting, monitoring, and assessing surface water bodies are therefore of growing scientific and practical importance. In this paper, we investigate and compare three distinct machine learning architectures for water body identification and monitoring. Their performance is evaluated through quantitative metrics and real-world examples. Furthermore, a direct comparison with classical NDWI thresholding is conducted on a representative test image to highlight differences between data-driven and index-based approaches. This analysis allows us to identify the best-performing model in terms of accuracy, robustness, and practical applicability. Beyond detection, a major challenge for meaningful water quality assessment lies in the consistent and interpretable visualization of spectral water indices. Standard color mapping techniques are often inadequate or potentially misleading for environmental applications. To address this gap, we propose a suite of meaningful color schemes adapted for water quality indices, facilitating clearer interpretation, comparison, and decision-making for human users.

</details>


### [227] [ECHO: Terminal Agents Learn World Models for Free](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24517)
*Vaishnavi Shrivastava,Piero Kauffmann,Ahmed Awadallah,Dimitris Papailiopoulos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new method called ECHO improves CLI agents by training them to predict environment responses (stdout, errors, etc.) alongside generating commands, turning terminal feedback into dense supervision that doubles performance on benchmark tasks and enables self-improvement without expert demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current CLI agent RL (like GRPO-style training) discards valuable environment response data (stdout, errors, files, logs, traces) as supervision signals, treating them only as sparse outcome-level rewards while ignoring rich evidence about how the environment responds. Failed rollouts provide little policy-gradient signal despite containing valuable information.

Method: The authors introduce ECHO (Environment Cross-entropy Hybrid Objective), a hybrid objective combining standard policy-gradient loss on action tokens with an auxiliary loss that trains the policy to predict environment observation tokens resulting from its own actions. ECHO reuses the same forward pass as GRPO, requires no additional rollouts, and turns terminal feedback into dense supervision.

Result: ECHO doubles GRPO pass@1 on TerminalBench-2.0 with Qwen3 models showing significant improvements (Qwen3-8B: 2.70% to 5.17%, Qwen3-14B: 5.17% to 10.79%). It produces policies that better predict terminal dynamics even on held-out trajectories not generated by the policy, sharply reducing environment-token cross-entropy. ECHO matches expert-SFT-then-GRPO performance without expert demonstrations and recovers about half of the expert-SFT benefit. The environment prediction loss alone enables verifier-free self-improvement on unseen OOD tasks.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that environment observations provide dense, on-policy supervision signals that are already present in every rollout, and that leveraging these signals through the ECHO objective significantly improves CLI agent performance and enables self-improvement without expert demonstrations.

Abstract: CLI agents are the closest thing language models have to an embodied setting: the model emits commands, the terminal executes them, and the returned stream -- stdout, errors, files, logs, and traces -- records the consequences. We argue that this stream is a supervision signal, but standard agent RL discards it: GRPO-style training updates action tokens with sparse outcome-level rewards while ignoring environment responses already in the rollout. Failed rollouts provide little policy-gradient signal despite containing rich evidence about how the environment responds. We introduce ECHO (Environment Cross-entropy Hybrid Objective), a hybrid objective that combines the standard policy-gradient loss on action tokens with an auxiliary loss that trains the policy to predict environment observation tokens resulting from its own actions. ECHO reuses the same forward pass as GRPO, requires no additional rollouts, and turns terminal feedback into dense supervision for all rollouts. ECHO doubles GRPO pass@1 on TerminalBench-2.0: Qwen3-8B improves from 2.70% to 5.17%, and Qwen3-14B from 5.17% to 10.79%. ECHO also produces policies that better predict terminal dynamics, even on trajectories they did not generate: across held-out rollouts, it sharply reduces environment-token cross-entropy while GRPO alone barely changes it. From base Qwen3-8B, ECHO matches expert-SFT-then-GRPO performance on held-out terminal tasks without expert demonstrations, and recovers roughly half of the expert-SFT initialization benefit on TerminalBench-2.0. In some settings, the environment prediction loss alone enables verifier-free self-improvement, allowing policies to improve on unseen OOD tasks by learning only from environment interactions. Together, these results suggest that environment observations are not merely context for future actions, but a dense, on-policy supervision signal already present in every rollout.

</details>


### [228] [MindAlign: Bridging EEG, Vision, and Language for Zero-Shot Visual Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24523)
*Zexuan Chen,Sichao Liu,Runhao Lu,Huichao Qi,Alexandra Woolgar,Xi Vincent Wang,Lihui Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Tri-modal contrastive framework for EEG-based visual decoding achieves state-of-the-art performance by aligning EEG, visual, and textual representations, with compact embeddings outperforming larger backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visual decoding from brain signals is a key challenge at the intersection of computer vision and neuroscience, requiring methods that bridge neural representations and computational models of vision. There's a need for robust, semantically-grounded approaches to decode visual information from non-invasive neural signals like EEG.

Method: A two-stage approach: 1) Pre-train EEG encoder via masked reconstruction on unlabeled trials to learn spatio-temporal regularities; 2) Jointly align EEG, image, and LLM-generated textual descriptions through contrastive learning, using text as a semantic regularizer. The encoder includes subject-specific adaptation, graph-attention over channels, and temporal-spatial convolutional embeddings.

Result: On the Things-EEG2 200-way zero-shot benchmark: 54.1% Top-1 and 83.4% Top-5 accuracy, substantially exceeding prior baseline (32.4%/64.0%). Paired Wilcoxon tests confirm significance (p 

Conclusion: This paper presents a tri-modal contrastive framework that substantially advances EEG-based visual decoding, achieving state-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets while providing semantically-grounded neural representations that align with established neurophysiology.

Abstract: Visual decoding from brain signals is a key challenge at the intersection of computer vision and neuroscience, requiring methods that bridge neural representations and computational models of vision. We introduce a tri-modal contrastive framework for EEG-based visual decoding that aligns EEG, visual, and textual representations within a unified latent space. Our approach follows a two-stage design. First, we pre-train an EEG encoder via masked reconstruction on unlabeled trials, learning spatio-temporal regularities that transfer robustly to downstream tasks. Second, we jointly align EEG, image, and LLM-generated textual descriptions through contrastive learning, where text supervision acts as a semantic regularizer that injects linguistic structure into the shared space without overwhelming the primary EEG-image signal. The encoder integrates subject-specific adaptation, graph-attention over channels, and temporal-spatial convolutional embeddings. On the Things-EEG2 200-way zero-shot benchmark, our framework achieves 54.1% Top-1 and 83.4% Top-5 accuracy, substantially exceeding the strongest prior baseline (32.4% / 64.0%), with paired Wilcoxon tests confirming significance (p < 0.01) over all in-subject baselines. We validate generalization on Things-MEG. Analysis reveals that compact embedding geometries (CN-CLIP) outperform much larger backbones, and that decoding aligns with established neurophysiology of visual processing. This work is a critical step towards robust, semantically-grounded visual decoding from non-invasive temporal neural signals. The source code is publicly available in https://github.com/anon-eeg/eeg_image_decoding.

</details>


### [229] [What Are We Actually Decoding? Source Attribution for Non-Invasive Brain-to-Language Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24524)
*Xinyu Zhang,Sichao Liu,Runhao Lu,Alexandra Woolgar,Lihui Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes an auditing framework to attribute neural language decoding performance gains to specific sources (structural shortcuts, stimulus-evidence, contextual aggregation) rather than reporting inflated results, using signal-blind controls and a Group Context Bias intervention to measure contextual effects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current non-invasive neural language decoding results can be inflated by non-neural sources like decoder priors, embedding metrics, and structural nuisances (signal duration). The challenge is attribution - determining whether performance gains come from genuine neural evidence versus other factors.

Method: Reformulated stimulus-locked MEG-to-audio retrieval as an auditing framework that separates performance into three sources: 1) structural shortcuts (controlled via fixed-duration windows and stimulus-identity splits), 2) window-level stimulus-locked evidence, and 3) cross-window contextual aggregation. Introduced Group Context Bias (GCB) as an inference-time additive logit bias to pool sentence-consistent evidence across windows while keeping base retrieval scores and candidate pool fixed.

Result: Signal-blind Gaussian noise reached 66.3% Rank@1 under variable-length decoding but collapsed to near chance with controls, revealing structural leakage. Fixed-window retrieval showed measurable MEG-audio discriminability, with 95.7% of Top-1 errors being wrong sentence selections. GCB intervention increased R@1 from 44% to 52% (Gwilliams dataset) and from 22% to 29% (MOUS dataset) under fixed settings. GCB's effect collapsed under random-grouping perturbations and vanished when local evidence was attenuated, validating it as a controlled attribution tool.

Conclusion: Brain-to-language decoding performance should be source-attributed rather than merely reported. The proposed auditing framework with GCB intervention provides a way to measure and validate whether performance gains come from genuine neural evidence versus structural shortcuts or contextual aggregation.

Abstract: In non-invasive neural language decoding, results can be inflated by sources that are not stimulus-evoked neural evidence: decoder priors, embedding-based metrics, and non-neural structural nuisances such as signal duration. The methodological challenge is therefore attribution: a reported gain is more informative when it can be traced to a specific source. We recast stimulus-locked MEG-to-audio retrieval as an auditing framework that separates apparent performance into three sources - structural shortcuts, window-level stimulus-locked evidence, and cross-window contextual aggregation - and provides a diagnostic for each. Signal-blind Gaussian noise reaches 66.3% Rank@1 (R@1) under variable-length decoding but collapses to near chance once fixed-duration windows and stimulus-identity splits are enforced, isolating structural leakage. Under these controls, fixed-window retrieval recovers measurable MEG-audio discriminability, while an oracle sentence-bucket diagnostic shows that 95.7% of Top-1 errors select the wrong sentence, localising the residual bottleneck to sentence-level competition. We audit this contextual source with Group Context Bias (GCB), an inference-time additive logit bias that pools sentence-consistent evidence across windows while leaving the base retrieval scores and candidate pool fixed. Used as a score-space intervention, GCB makes the contextual source measurable: R@1 shifts from 44% to 52% on Gwilliams and from 22% to 29% on MOUS under the same fixed setting. GCB is auditable under this design: its effect collapses under random-grouping perturbations and vanishes when local evidence is attenuated in MEG or is near chance in EEG, supporting its use as a controlled source-attribution intervention. These results suggest that brain-to-language performance should be source-attributed, not merely reported.

</details>


### [230] [SemanticZip: A Pilot Framework for Lossy Text Compression with LLMs as Semantic Decompressors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24541)
*Natalia Trukhina,Vadim Vashkelis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SemanticZip is a framework for lossy text compression where LLMs decompress compact codes into task-relevant meaning, evaluating six representation regimes with structured prose performing best for recoverability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current text compression approaches (token deletion, retrieval, summarization, exact reconstruction) are limited. The paper studies a more aggressive lossy setting where text is compressed into codes that LLMs can expand into task-relevant meaning, bridging the gap between lossless compression and ordinary summarization.

Method: A pilot framework formalizing LLM-mediated decompression with protected/lossy packet architecture. Six representation regimes evaluated: structured prose, JSON, CCL-Core, CCL-Min, SemanticZip ASCII, and SemanticZip emoji. An independent decoder LLM reconstructs typed semantic atoms from compressed representations, scored using Critical Atom Recall, Weighted Atom Recall, precision, and tokenizer gain across five author-constructed diagnostic cases.

Result: Structured prose had highest recoverability (WAR = 0.956, 19.1% token gain). CCL-Min strongest balanced point (39.4% token gain, WAR = 0.874). SemanticZip ASCII provided largest useful compression (46.5% token gain, WAR = 0.802). Emoji-heavy SemanticZip performed worse on both compression and recovery.

Conclusion: Main contribution is not establishing universal performance benchmarks, but introducing a reproducible experimental interface for studying lossy, LLM-decompressible text codes. Key design principle: safety-critical/exact commitments should remain protected while predictable low-risk context can be semantically zipped.

Abstract: Text compression for large language model (LLM) systems is usually framed as token deletion, retrieval, summarization, or exact reconstruction. We study a more aggressive but explicitly lossy setting: compress text into compact codes that an LLM can expand into task-relevant meaning. We call this setting SemanticZip. Unlike lossless compression, SemanticZip does not require byte-identical reconstruction; unlike ordinary summarization, it treats model-based decompression as part of the codec and evaluates whether task-relevant semantic commitments are recovered.
  This paper is a pilot framework, not a benchmark claim. We formalize LLM-mediated decompression, define a protected/lossy packet architecture, and evaluate six representation regimes over five author-constructed diagnostic cases: structured prose, JSON, CCL-Core, CCL-Min, SemanticZip ASCII, and SemanticZip emoji. An independent decoder LLM reconstructs typed semantic atoms from each compressed representation, and we score Critical Atom Recall, Weighted Atom Recall, precision, and tokenizer gain. In this pilot, structured prose has the highest recoverability, with WAR = 0.956 and 19.1% o200k_base token gain. CCL-Min is the strongest balanced point, with 39.4% token gain and WAR = 0.874. SemanticZip ASCII provides the largest useful compression, with 46.5% token gain and WAR = 0.802, while emoji-heavy SemanticZip performs worse on both compression and recovery.
  The main contribution is not the claim that these numbers establish a universal frontier. Rather, we introduce a reproducible experimental interface for studying lossy, LLM-decompressible text codes and a design principle: safety-critical and exact commitments should remain protected, while predictable low-risk context may be semantically zipped.

</details>


### [231] [Rethinking Federated Unlearning via the Lens of Memorization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24545)
*Jiaheng Wei,Yanjun Zhang,He Zhang,Leo Yu Zhang,Chao Chen,Kok-Leong Ong,Jun Zhang,Yang Xiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new federated unlearning method that separates memorized patterns from overlapping ones via a novel metric, then prunes memorizing parameters to effectively unlearn while preserving useful shared knowledge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current federated unlearning methods are insufficient because they often fail to properly handle overlapping information between the data to be forgotten and the remaining data. This leads to ineffective unlearning (failing to properly remove all unique information) and potential unfairness between clients in the federated learning setting.

Method: This work proposes a metric called Grouped Memorization Evaluation which separates memorized knowledge from overlapping knowledge at the example level. It then introduces Federated Memorization Pruning (FedMemPrune), a pruning-based unlearning approach that resets the redundant model parameters responsible for memorization while preserving overlapping patterns supported by remaining data.

Result: Experimental results demonstrate that FedMemPrune achieves performance closely matching retraining-based unlearning baselines while eliminating memorization more effectively than existing federated unlearning algorithms. It achieves strong unlearning performance without sacrificing the utility of the retained knowledge.

Conclusion: Grouped Memorization Evaluation is a novel metric that can effectively partition memorized from overlapping knowledge, and the pruning approach leveraging this metric can achieve strong removal of memorization while preserving utility, matching or exceeding the effectiveness of existing federated unlearning methods.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) increasingly needs machine unlearning to comply with privacy regulations. However, existing federated unlearning approaches may overlook the overlapping information between the unlearning and remaining data, leading to ineffective unlearning and unfairness between clients. In this work, we revisit federated unlearning through the lens of memorization. We argue that unlearning should mainly remove the unique memorized information attributable to the data to be forgotten, while preserving overlapping patterns that are also supported by the remaining data. Specifically, we propose Grouped Memorization Evaluation, an example-level metric that separates memorized knowledge from overlapping knowledge. Building on this metric, we introduce Federated Memorization Pruning (FedMemPrune), a pruning-based unlearning approach that resets redundant parameters responsible for memorization. Extensive experiments show that FedMemPrune closely matches retraining-based unlearning baselines while more effectively eliminating memorization than existing federated unlearning algorithms, yielding strong unlearning performance without sacrificing the utility of retained knowledge.

</details>


### [232] [RL with Learnable Textual Feedback: A Bilevel Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24547)
*Utsav Singh,Sidhaarth Sredharan,Souradip Chakraborty,Amrit Singh Bedi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Bi-NAC introduces a bilevel optimization approach where both critic (feedback generator) and actor (reasoning model) are jointly trained, enabling the critic to learn to generate feedback that actually improves the actor's performance, leading to better sample and parameter efficiency in LLM reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL with textual feedback methods treat feedback as fixed or auxiliary, missing that feedback should not just be correct but should actually improve the policy when provided in context. Existing approaches are sample-inefficient with sparse terminal rewards.

Method: Formalizes RL with learnable feedback as a Stackelberg bilevel program. Introduces Bilevel Natural Language Actor-Critic (Bi-NAC) that jointly trains a critic to generate reward-improving feedback and an actor to exploit that feedback, optimizing both levels simultaneously.

Result: Bi-NAC improves over RL and fixed-critic baselines on MATH-500, MBPP, and GPQA datasets. A 2B model outperforms 3B GRPO baseline (46.6% vs 41.4% on MATH-500), and a 6B model surpasses 7B GRPO baseline (49.3% vs 43.6% on GPQA).

Conclusion: The bilevel formulation of RL with learnable textual feedback enables more effective training where critics learn to generate feedback that actually improves actor performance, leading to better sample and parameter efficiency in LLM reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards can improve LLM reasoning, but learning remains sample-inefficient when terminal rewards are sparse. This has motivated a growing line of work on RL with textual feedback, where a critic model generates natural language feedback to guide a reasoning model (the actor), augmenting scalar rewards with richer learning signals. However, existing methods typically treat feedback as fixed or auxiliary, which misses a key property: feedback should not merely be correct, but should improve the policy (actor model) when provided in context. This motivates a paradigm of learnable textual feedback for RL. Yet the learnability and usefulness of feedback depend on the policy's ability to learn from it, making RL with learnable feedback an inherently bilevel problem. We formalize this coupling as a Stackelberg bilevel program and derive Bilevel Natural Language Actor-Critic (Bi-NAC), which jointly trains a critic to generate reward-improving feedback and an actor to exploit it. Across MATH-500, MBPP, and GPQA, Bi-NAC improves sample and parameter efficiency over RL and fixed-critic baselines: our 2B model outperforms the 3B GRPO baseline, achieving 46.6% versus 41.4% on MATH-500, while our 6B model surpasses the 7B GRPO baseline, achieving 49.3% versus 43.6% on GPQA.

</details>


### [233] [Deep ZakaiJ: Structured Filtering for Jump-Diffusion Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24548)
*Yan Leng,Thibaut Mastrolia,Hao Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Deep ZakaiJ combines Zakai nonlinear filtering with neural networks to model partially observed jump-diffusion systems, improving distributional forecasts while inferring interpretable latent states.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing jump-diffusion models either rely on rigid parametric assumptions (classical models) or require full trajectory observability without inferring hidden states (recent neural models). There's a need for flexible latent-state models for partially observed jump-diffusion systems.

Method: Deep ZakaiJ combines the Zakai nonlinear filtering equation with a neural encoder-decoder architecture. The encoder recursively updates latent state beliefs using Strang splitting into three interpretable substeps: prior propagation, diffusion innovation, and jump innovation. The decoder is a structured jump-diffusion model conditioned on filtered beliefs.

Result: Deep ZakaiJ improves distributional forecasts while maintaining competitive point accuracy across synthetic, financial, and oceanographic datasets. It achieves calibrated predictive intervals and recovers interpretable latent structure in both synthetic and qualitative case studies.

Conclusion: Deep ZakaiJ effectively bridges classical jump-diffusion theory with modern neural architectures, providing a principled yet flexible approach to modeling partially observed jump-diffusion systems with interpretable latent state inference and well-calibrated forecasts.

Abstract: Time series driven by unobserved latent states frequently exhibit abrupt jump discontinuities whose timing and magnitude cannot be predicted from observed history alone. Classical jump-diffusion models offer a principled mathematical framework but assume rigid parametric forms, while recent neural jump models operate on fully observed trajectories without inferring the hidden states that govern the dynamics. We propose \textit{Deep ZakaiJ}, a latent-state model for partially observed jump-diffusion systems that embeds the Zakai nonlinear filtering equation into a neural encoder--decoder architecture. The encoder recursively updates a belief over the latent state via Strang splitting into three interpretable substeps: prior propagation, diffusion innovation, and jump innovation, yielding a differentiable, first-order-accurate approximation of the exact filtering evolution. The decoder is a structured jump-diffusion model explicitly conditioned on the filtered belief, preserving the separation between continuous dynamics and discontinuous shocks. On synthetic, financial, and oceanographic datasets, \textit{Deep ZakaiJ} improves distributional forecasts while remaining competitive in point accuracy, achieving calibrated predictive intervals and recovering interpretable latent structure in synthetic and qualitative case studies.

</details>


### [234] [Position: AI for Science Should Treat Measurement-to-Dataset Pipelines as Inference Components](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24558)
*Ling Zhan,Xiaoyao Yu,Tao Jia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper argues that treating measurement-to-dataset pipelines as fixed interfaces in AI4Science obscures uncertainty and leads to reliability issues, proposing computable inference frameworks instead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI4Science workflows often treat released datasets as fixed interfaces, but in domains with indirect observation (like neuroscience), the data comes through multi-stage measurement, reconstruction, and preprocessing pipelines. These pipelines should be viewed as inference components rather than fixed data sources.

Method: The paper identifies three failure modes from treating pipelines as fixed ("frozen lens"), and stress-tests these claims with a large-scale neuroscience empirical audit using cross-dataset stability criteria.

Result: The empirical audit found a survival rate of ≈0.0004% under cross-dataset stability criteria, demonstrating severe reliability issues when pipelines are treated as fixed rather than probabilistic inference components.

Conclusion: The AI4Science community should make pipelines computable inference objects via domain-specific Computable Observation Frameworks, enabling quantification of pipeline adequacy and stability, converting implicit implementation choices into auditable, reproducible, and cumulative scientific evidence.

Abstract: AI for Science (AI4Science) workflows often treat the released dataset as a fixed interface to the underlying system.
  However, in domains relying on \emph{indirect observation}, the learner observes a derivative representation produced by multi-stage measurement, reconstruction, and preprocessing pipelines.
  \textbf{We argue that these measurement-to-dataset pipelines are inference components: treating their outputs as ``given data'' freezes an observation model and obscures uncertainty over feasible pipeline choices.}
  We identify three failure modes arising from this ``frozen lens'': \textbf{(C1) hidden hypothesis space}, where the released dataset does not specify the pipeline configuration or its validity conditions; \textbf{(C2) uncertified transportability}, where a pipeline may be documented but its regime of validity is untested, so failures under distribution shift cannot be adjudicated; \textbf{(C3) ungoverned multiplicity}, where many defensible pipelines exist and dispersion is real but not propagated into uncertainty-aware evidence.
  We stress-test these claims with a large-scale neuroscience empirical audit, finding a survival rate of $\approx 0.0004\%$ under a cross-dataset stability criterion.
  We call on the AI4Science community to make pipelines \emph{computable} inference objects via domain-specific Computable Observation Frameworks.
  This shift enables quantifying pipeline adequacy and stability, converting implicit implementation choices into auditable, reproducible, and cumulative scientific evidence.

</details>


### [235] [PILOT: Policy-Informed Learned Optimization for Adaptive Deep Network Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24570)
*Sattam Altuuaim,Lama Ayash,Muhammad Mubashar,Naeemullah Khan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PILOT introduces an adaptive online optimizer that adjusts update behavior based on gradient-direction agreement, achieving state-of-the-art performance on FashionMNIST and CIFAR-10 with CNN and ResNet-18 architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most optimizers use fixed update structures that cannot adapt to changing gradient behavior across the loss landscape during training. This limits their effectiveness in different training regimes.

Method: PILOT is an online optimizer that adapts its update behavior based on gradient-direction agreement as a signal of local training stability. It conditions the update rule on this agreement to adjust its behavior when gradients are stable, noisy, or inconsistent.

Result: Experiments show PILOT achieves 94.13% on FashionMNIST (CNN), 81.94% on CIFAR-10 (CNN), 95.71% on FashionMNIST (ResNet-18), and 93.42% on CIFAR-10 (ResNet-18), outperforming other optimizers.

Conclusion: The experiments demonstrate that PILOT consistently achieves the highest accuracy across convolutional models on FashionMNIST and CIFAR-10, including both CNN and ResNet-18 architectures. The implementation is publicly available to support reproducibility and further research.

Abstract: Despite the central role of optimization in deep learning, most optimizers rely on update structures whose functional form is fixed before training begins. This static design can limit their ability to respond to changing gradient behavior across the loss landscape, where training may shift between stable, noisy, and inconsistent regimes.
  This study proposes PILOT (Policy-Informed Learned OpTimizer), an online optimizer that adapts its update behavior during training. Rather than using a fixed balance between momentum, normalization, and sign-based updates, PILOT uses gradient-direction agreement as a signal of local training stability. Conditioning the update rule on this agreement signal allows the optimizer to adjust its behavior when gradients become stable, noisy, or inconsistent.
  Experiments on FashionMNIST and CIFAR-10 show that PILOT consistently achieves the highest accuracy among the evaluated optimizers across convolutional settings. On the CNN architecture, PILOT reaches 94.13% on FashionMNIST and 81.94% on CIFAR-10. On ResNet-18, it further improves performance, reaching 95.71% on FashionMNIST and 93.42% on CIFAR-10. These results suggest that learning how to adapt the update structure during training can improve performance across both compact and deeper convolutional models while preserving a simple first-order optimization framework.
  The implementation of PILOT is publicly available at https://github.com/SattamAltwaim/PILOT.git

</details>


### [236] [Polymorphism Is Rotation: Operational Mechanistic Interpretability from a Two-Layer Transformer to Pythia-70m](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24577)
*Jordan F. McCann*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Independently trained transformers compute identical functions but use different random internal coordinate rotations. A single orthogonal Procrustes rotation can align these coordinate systems, enabling transfer of interpretability tools like sparse autoencoders between models without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the discovery that independently trained transformers, while computing the same function, use different internal coordinate representations that differ by random rotations. This "polymorphism" phenomenon makes interpretability tools like sparse autoencoders not directly transferable between models, despite high cosine similarity metrics suggesting universality.

Method: The authors use orthogonal Procrustes analysis to compute a rotation matrix between activation bases of independently trained models. They validate this on a 104k-parameter Dyck-3 transformer and nine independently trained Pythia-70m models. Statistical analysis includes Frobenius norm comparisons, Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests of eigenvalue spectra against Haar distribution, and a pre-registered four-bar operational framework.

Result: The rotation matrices are Haar-distributed (random orthogonal), matching theoretical predictions with high precision. A single Procrustes rotation restores sparse autoencoder reconstruction performance to near-ceiling levels. Steering vectors transfer in three regimes depending on their alignment with the rotation's invariant subspace. The phenomenon is invisible to standard universality metrics but dramatically affects practical interpretability transfer.

Conclusion: Transformer representations exhibit systematic polymorphism - same function computation but different coordinate systems related by random rotations. This has profound implications for interpretability: coordinate alignment is necessary for transferring interpretability tools between models, challenging current universality metrics while providing a simple solution via Procrustes rotation.

Abstract: Independently trained transformers compute the same function in residual-stream bases that differ by a uniform random rotation on $\mathrm{SO}(d_{\mathrm{model}})$. We call this phenomenon polymorphism: same function, mutually unintelligible interior coordinates. One matrix multiplication per model pair removes it: an orthogonal Procrustes fit on a single batch of activations transfers sparse-autoencoder feature dictionaries and steering vectors between independently trained models, with no retraining.
  The phenomenon is invisible to the standard SAE universality metric. Decoder-column cosine similarity matches across seeds at 98%, the SAE-universality headline number, while an SAE trained on one seed reconstructs another seed's activations at negative explained variance, worse than predicting the constant mean. The decoder columns align; the encoder reads from a rotated frame. A single Procrustes rotation $R$ restores reconstruction to within 0.025 EV of the within-seed ceiling at every internal site.
  $R$ is Haar-distributed: $\|R - I\|_F$ matches the random-orthogonal prediction $\sqrt{2 d_{\mathrm{model}}}$ to 0.1% at $d_{\mathrm{model}} = 512$, and a Kolmogorov-Smirnov test of $R$'s eigenvalue spectrum against Haar $\mathrm{SO}(d_{\mathrm{model}})$ returns $p \approx 1.000$ pooled and per-pair. Diff-of-means steering vectors transfer in three regimes by alignment with $R$'s invariant subspace: clean when pinned by shared output weights, partial when overlapping the rotated subspace, inverted otherwise. With no shared I/O (Pythia), all three collapse to universally inverted. The same rotation account holds across training checkpoints within a single run.
  Validated on a 104k-parameter Dyck-3 transformer and nine independently-trained Pythia-70m seeds on The Pile, via a pre-registered four-bar operational framework. Frontier-scale (10B+) replication remains open.

</details>


### [237] [An Effective-Rank Audit of Alignment-Induced Activation Shifts: Confound Control, Constructive Calibration, and Limits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24583)
*Yuki Nakamura*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper analyzes how safety alignment affects LLM activations by introducing a new metric (rho_eps) that measures the effective rank of alignment modifications, finding it's a diagnostic for fragility but not a target for mechanical optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to understand how instruction-tuned LLMs encode refusal behaviors for safety-aligned responses, moving beyond the single refusal direction observation to develop a continuous quantitative framework that can diagnose alignment-induced fragility.

Method: The authors audit three open-weight LLMs using a confound-controlled decomposition approach with four variants to separate chat-template formatting from alignment-stage shifts. They use effective rank measurements (rho_eps) on safety-relevant inputs and analyze via SVD, then test constructive calibration on 3-layer MLPs across different rho_eps values.

Result: Key findings: (1) rho_eps values for chat-template-controlled alignment are very small (0.0029-0.0048), (2) mild rank-maximization provides ablation robustness while strong regularization doesn't, (3) rho_eps is a diagnostic for fragility but not a robustness target, and (4) rank-based diagnostics have limitations including non-safety-specificity and structural constraints.

Conclusion: The effective rank metric (rho_eps) serves as a useful diagnostic for alignment-induced fragility in LLMs, but mechanical inflation of this metric doesn't improve robustness. The spectral-gap hypothesis fails empirically, leaving open the problem of establishing matching lower bounds for alignment robustness.

Abstract: We audit alignment-induced shifts in residual-stream activations of three open-weight instruction-tuned LLMs (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Gemma-2-9B-it, Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct) using the effective rank of the alignment modification matrix on safety-relevant inputs, rho_eps := rank_eps(M_Ds)/d, which formalizes the single-refusal-direction observation of Arditi et al. (2024) as a continuous quantity. The paper has three contributions. (1) Confound-controlled measurement: a four-variant decomposition (M_naive, M_template, M_aligned, M_DiD) separates chat-template formatting, alignment-stage shift, and the refusal-mediating direction, and recovers the Arditi refusal direction on M_DiD at |cos| in {0.77, 0.86, 0.50} (Llama/Gemma/Qwen); chat-template-controlled rho_eps is {0.0029, 0.0048, 0.0044}, and the centered SVD residual is 4-7x larger. (2) Constructive calibration on a 3-layer MLP across rho_eps in {0.008, 0.17, 0.33, 0.40} exhibits a sweet-spot vs. brittle distinction: mild rank-maximization (lambda=5) buys ablation robustness, while strong regularization at the same nominal rho_eps (lambda=50) does not. rho_eps is a diagnostic for fragility, not a target whose mechanical inflation buys robustness. (3) Limits of rank-based diagnostics: (a) not safety-specific (LRH baseline is 2-3x the safety value); (b) SVD principal ordering does not match causal ordering (Llama u_2 inert despite ranking second; cumulative ablation non-monotone at k=5); (c) the spectral-gap hypothesis required to upgrade the O(rho_eps * d) achievability bound to a matching Mirsky-route lower bound fails empirically (1/90 Llama layer-reference pairs, 0/36 MLP combinations) and structurally (kappa_lb <= 2/(eps * r)). The matching lower bound remains an open problem.

</details>


### [238] [LAPLEX: The FFT of Learnable Laplace Kernels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24584)
*Łukasz Struski,Hanna Blazhko,Piotr Kubaty,Jacek Tabor*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LAPLEX is a trainable Laplace-kernel operator class that enables exact computation with adaptive geometry, achieving FFT-like scaling for dense matrix operations on massive dimensions (up to 10⁹) without storing dense matrices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current linear algebra in deep learning presents a trade-off: either use fixed geometry with exact computation (like Fourier transforms) or pay for adaptive geometry with dense parameters, random features, or low-rank approximations. The authors want to move beyond this trade-off.

Method: Introduces LAPLEX, a class of exact, trainable (phased) Laplace-kernel operators. These layers are full-rank dense matrices implicitly defined by learnable coordinate anchors, enabling FFT-like scaling for matrix-vector operations.

Result: Enables trainable matrix-vector operations at dimensions up to 10⁹ on modern GPUs, supports compact projections and interpretable classification heads, serves as efficient Gram operators for high-dimensional covariance models on images up to 3×10⁶ dimensions, and preserves spatial structure without convolutional bias.

Conclusion: LAPLEX separates expressivity from storage cost - it behaves like a dense trainable matrix but is represented through small structured parameters, enabling data-adaptive global interactions in regimes where ordinary dense layers are impractical.

Abstract: Fast linear algebra in deep learning usually comes with a choice: fixed geometry and exact computation, as in the Fourier transform, or adaptive geometry paid for by dense parameters, random features, or low-rank surrogates. To move beyond this trade-off, we introduce LAPLEX, a class of exact, trainable (phased) Laplace-kernel operators.
  A LAPLEX layer is a typically full-rank dense matrix, implicitly defined by learnable coordinate anchors, with FFT-like scaling. Consequently, it supports trainable matrix--vector operations at vector dimensions up to $10^9$ on modern GPUs. As a neural layer, it yields compact projections and classification heads interpretable as soft, trainable routing models. The same primitive also serves as an efficient Gram operator, enabling high-dimensional covariance models on flattened images of dimension $3 \cdot 10^6$ that preserve visible spatial structure without imposing convolutional bias. These applications reflect a single principle: dense geometry can be learned without storing a dense matrix, which enables data-adaptive global interactions in regimes where ordinary dense layers are out of reach. In this sense, LAPLEX separates expressivity from storage cost: it behaves like a dense trainable matrix, but is represented and applied through a small structured set of parameters.

</details>


### [239] [Beyond Fixed Points: Superpolynomial Capacity of Asymmetric Hopfield Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24611)
*Aakash Kumar,Anatoly Khina,Frederik Mallmann-Trenn,Emanuele Natale*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Asymmetric Hopfield networks can store exponentially many long sequences with noise robustness using a novel construction combining combinatorics and number theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical symmetric Hopfield networks are limited to static patterns, while existing asymmetric networks struggle to achieve high-capacity storage of long temporal sequences.

Method: Simple and robust construction within classical asymmetric Hopfield model with binary neurons and synchronous updates, using techniques from combinatorics, number theory, and opinion dynamics analysis.

Result: n neurons can support exp(Ω(n/(log n)²)) distinct limit-cycle attractors, each with period exp(Ω(√n/log n)), robust to random noise with flip probability up to ½-o(1).

Conclusion: Synchronous asymmetric Hopfield networks have far greater sequence-memory capacity and robustness than previously recognized, showing robust sequence representation can be achieved through simple architectural motifs rather than complex nonlinearities.

Abstract: Classical Hopfield networks are limited to static patterns due to symmetric weights, whereas asymmetric networks can encode temporal sequences via limit-cycle attractors. Achieving high-capacity storage of long sequences in classical synchronous asymmetric networks, however, has remained a challenge. We present a simple and robust construction within the classical asymmetric Hopfield model with binary neurons and synchronous updates, that allows $n$ neurons to support $\exp\!\big(Ω(n/(\log n)^2)\big)$ distinct limit-cycle attractors, each with period $\exp\!\big(Ω(\sqrt n/\log n)\big)$ and robust to random noise with flip probability up to $\frac12-o(1)$, yielding superpolynomial capacity in both the number and length of stored sequences. This is the first demonstration of such capacity for asymmetric Hopfield networks, which we obtain by combining results from combinatorics, number theory and the analysis of opinion dynamics. Our findings show that synchronous asymmetric Hopfield networks possess a sequence-memory capacity which is larger and more robust than previously recognized, demonstrating that, in both biological and artificial neural systems, robust sequence representation can be achieved through coarse architectural motifs rather than complex nonlinearities.

</details>


### [240] [Beyond Generative Priors: Minority Sampling with JEPA-Guided Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24631)
*Sol Park,Soobin Um*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: JEPA guidance is a diffusion sampling framework that generates minority samples using real-world semantic priors from a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture, outperforming generator-centric approaches in capturing meaningful rarity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing minority sampling approaches define rarity relative to generative priors learned from training data, confining rarity to model-specific notions that may poorly reflect real-world semantics. There's a need for a world-centric perspective that defines rarity with respect to real-world priors.

Method: Proposes JEPA guidance, a diffusion sampling framework guided by a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) - world models encoding broad, semantically rich representations. The framework steers diffusion trajectories toward low-density regions under the implicit density induced by the JEPA. Also develops principled approximation strategies with theoretical error bounds to make computation practical.

Result: JEPA guidance consistently improves fidelity and semantic validity of minority samples across unconditional, class-conditional, and text-to-image generation tasks. It outperforms generator-centric baselines in capturing real-world notions of rarity. The method is computationally practical with significantly reduced guidance overhead.

Conclusion: JEPA guidance successfully aligns generated minorities with real-world semantic rarity by leveraging world models rather than generator-specific densities, providing a more meaningful approach to minority sampling that better reflects real-world semantics.

Abstract: Minority sampling aims to generate low-density instances on a data manifold and is of central importance in applications such as medical diagnosis, anomaly detection, and creative AI. Existing approaches, however, define minority samples relative to generative priors learned from training data, confining rarity to model-specific notions that may poorly reflect real-world semantics. In this work, we propose a world-centric perspective on minority sampling, which defines rarity with respect to real-world priors rather than generator-induced densities. To this end, we introduce JEPA guidance, a diffusion sampling framework guided by a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) -- a class of world models that encode broad, semantically rich representations. JEPA guidance steers diffusion trajectories toward low-density regions under the implicit density induced by the JEPA, thereby aligning generated minorities with real-world semantic rarity. To make JEPA guidance computationally practical, we develop principled approximation strategies accompanied by theoretical error bounds, significantly reducing the overhead of guidance computation. Extensive experiments across unconditional, class-conditional, and text-to-image generation demonstrate that JEPA guidance consistently improves the fidelity and semantic validity of minority samples, outperforming generator-centric baselines in capturing real-world notions of rarity. Code is available at https://github.com/soobin-um/jepa-guidance.

</details>


### [241] [On the Stability and Realizability of Recurrent Polynomial Surrogate Ternary Logic Gate Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24649)
*Sai Sandeep Damera,Ryan Matheu,Aniruddh G. Puranic,John S. Baras,Calin Belta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: R-DTLGN: A recurrent neural network architecture that operates over ternary logic to provide formal guarantees for runtime monitoring in safety-critical systems, ensuring graceful degradation under sensor failure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard RNNs used for runtime monitoring of Signal Temporal Logic (STL) lack guarantees about how they degrade under sensor failures - a single dropped input can silently flip a safety verdict from safe to unsafe, which is unacceptable for safety-critical systems.

Method: Introduces Recurrent Differentiable Ternary Logic Gate Network (R-DTLGN) that operates over Kleene's three-valued logic {-1, 0, +1} where 0 represents unknown. The network trains via continuous polynomial surrogates and hardens to discrete ternary logic circuits at inference. Two gate vocabularies ensure properties: numerically monotone gates guarantee stable recurrent dynamics, while information-monotone gates ensure principled abstention and monotonicity in input certainty.

Result: R-DTLGN provides formal degradation guarantees, with recurrent connections for bounded STL operators using exclusively AND/OR gates belonging to both vocabularies. Network sizing is formula-driven (not hyperparameter search). Evaluation on D4RL PointMaze navigation shows accurate prediction while maintaining graceful degradation under predicate dropout.

Conclusion: R-DTLGN is the first recurrent architecture coupling learned temporal prediction with formal degradation guarantees rooted in three-valued logic, addressing critical needs for runtime monitoring in safety-critical systems.

Abstract: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) can learn to predict Signal Temporal Logic (STL) verdicts online from partial trajectories, but deploying them as runtime monitors in safety-critical systems demands more than predictive accuracy. Standard RNN architectures offer no structural guarantee that outputs degrade gracefully under sensor degradation; a dropped input can silently flip a verdict from safe to unsafe. We introduce the Recurrent Differentiable Ternary Logic Gate Network (R-DTLGN), a recurrent architecture that operates over Kleene's three-valued logic $\{-1, 0, +1\}$, where $0$ explicitly represents unknown. The R-DTLGN trains through continuous polynomial surrogates and hardens to a discrete ternary logic circuit at inference. We analyze the hardened circuit through two gate vocabularies derived from two orderings on the ternary domain: numerically monotone gates ensure stable recurrent dynamics, while information-monotone gates, when present, guarantee principled abstention (unknown inputs never produce wrong outputs) and monotonicity in input certainty (more information can only improve the verdict). We show that the recurrent connections required by bounded STL operators use exclusively AND and OR, which belong to both vocabularies, linking the monitoring task to the architecture's guarantees. A realizability bound derived from the STL formula's temporal operators directly sizes the network's hidden state, replacing hyperparameter search with a formula-driven specification. We evaluate on STL specifications over D4RL PointMaze navigation data, testing prediction accuracy, degradation under predicate dropout, and the accuracy-versus-safety tradeoff between two label construction pipelines. The R-DTLGN is, to our knowledge, the first recurrent architecture that couples learned temporal prediction with formal degradation guarantees rooted in three-valued logic.

</details>


### [242] [WLNO: Wavelet-Laplace Neural Operator for Solving Partial Differential Equations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24658)
*Muhammad Abid,Arth Sojitra,Omer San*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: WLNO combines Laplace Neural Operator with wavelet transforms for improved PDE learning, outperforming LNO on multiple benchmark problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While Laplace Neural Operator captures temporal dynamics via learnable poles/residues, it lacks explicit mechanisms for extracting spatially localized multi-scale features inherent in complex PDE solutions.

Method: Augments LNO with parallel Haar wavelet transform branch: decomposes feature maps into four frequency subbands (LL, LH, HL, HH), applies independent 1x1 convolutions to each, reconstructs via inverse DWT, and fuses branches with learnable sigmoid-gated weight α_wav.

Result: WLNO consistently outperforms LNO on all five benchmark PDE problems (diffusion, Burgers, reaction-diffusion, Darcy flow, Navier-Stokes), with most pronounced improvements on problems with strong spatial multi-scale structure like Burgers with shock fronts and Navier-Stokes with vortical structures.

Conclusion: Wavelet-based multi-scale spatial decomposition is a principled and effective complement to Laplace-domain operator learning for neural operators.

Abstract: This work introduces the Wavelet-Laplace Neural Operator (WLNO), a novel neural operator that fuses Haar wavelet multi-scale spatial decomposition with the Laplace-domain pole-residue formulation of the Laplace Neural Operator (LNO). While LNO captures transient and steady-state dynamics through learnable system poles and residues, it lacks an explicit mechanism for extracting spatially localized multi-scale features inherent in complex PDE solutions. WLNO addresses this by augmenting the LNO core with a parallel single-level Haar discrete wavelet transform (DWT) branch that decomposes the lifted feature map into four frequency subbands: approximation (LL), horizontal detail (LH), vertical detail (HL), and diagonal detail (HH) and applies independent learned $1\times1$ convolutions to each subband before reconstruction via the inverse DWT. The two branches are fused through a learnable sigmoid-gated weight $α_\mathrm{wav}$, initialized to give a small initial contribution to the wavelet branch, allowing the model to adaptively balance Laplace-domain dynamics against spatial multi-scale features throughout training. WLNO is evaluated against LNO on five benchmark PDE problems using identical hyperparameters, training data, and evaluation protocols: the diffusion equation, the Burgers equation, the reaction-diffusion system, Darcy flow, and the two-dimensional Navier-Stokes equation. WLNO consistently outperforms LNO on all five problems, with the most pronounced improvement on problems with strong spatial multi-scale structure, such as the Burgers equation with sharp shock fronts and the Navier-Stokes equation with coherent vortical structures, while remaining consistent across smoother and elliptic problems. These results demonstrate that wavelet-based multi-scale spatial decomposition is a principled and effective complement to Laplace-domain operator learning.

</details>


### [243] [IterInject: Indirect Prompt Injection Against LLM Agents via Feedback-Guided Iterative Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24659)
*Zixuan Chen,Jiaxiang Chen,Li Luo,Ke Xu,Xiaoxiang Huang,Tanfeng Sun,Xinghao Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OPSIF is a feedback-guided framework that iteratively optimizes indirect prompt injections, significantly boosting attack success rates against defended agents, and uncovers a mechanistic vulnerability tied to attention patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based agents performing practical tasks are vulnerable to indirect prompt injection (IPI), where external content can hijack agent behavior. Existing attacks lack the ability to adapt to specific defenses or structured feedback for refinement, limiting effectiveness and understanding.

Method: The method builds on a feedback-guided iterative framework that interweaves attack generation, structured diagnosis of failure patterns, and LLM-based optimization. Key components are: (1) a rule-based diagnoser providing structured outcome labels, (2) an LLM-based optimizer refining payloads based on attack histories, and (3) a synthesis step generating novel disguise seeds from failures to evolve the attack strategy space.

Result: OPSIF substantially outperforms static baselines and existing adaptive methods on AgentDojo and InjectAgent benchmarks across four victim models. It achieved full success on five out of nine targets against Claude Code, a production-grade agent with layered defenses, and induced measurable improvement on remaining targets. A mechanistic analysis identified an attention-mediated threshold mechanism in mid-to-late layers and causal interventions supported this finding.

Conclusion: The paper concludes Optimized Prompt Injection via Structured Feedback (OPSIF) is a significant advancement in attacking agent defenses, offers insights into indirect prompt injection's mechanism, and identifies a potential defense avenue tied to attention patterns.

Abstract: LLM-based agents are increasingly deployed for complex tasks requiring planning, tool use, and interaction with external services. Their reliance on untrusted external content exposes them to indirect prompt injection (IPI), in which adversarial instructions embedded in retrieved data hijack agent behavior. Existing attacks rely on static payloads that cannot adapt to agent-specific defenses; even recent adaptive methods lack structured feedback to guide optimization. We introduce \oursys, a feedback-guided iterative framework that closes the loop between injection, diagnosis, and refinement: a rule-based diagnoser produces structured outcome labels with behavioral descriptions, and an LLM-based optimizer refines payloads conditioned on the full optimization history. A synthesis step generates new disguise seeds from failure patterns, enabling the strategy space to self-evolve. On AgentDojo and InjectAgent, \oursys substantially outperforms static baselines and existing adaptive methods across four victim models. Extension experiments on Claude Code, a production-grade coding agent with layered defenses, show that optimized payloads achieve full success on 5 of 9 targets; even those that resist full exploitation exhibit measurable improvement from iterative refinement. We further present a mechanistic analysis of IPI, identifying an attention-mediated threshold mechanism in mid-to-late layers; three causal interventions validate this finding and point to concrete defense directions.

</details>


### [244] [Trajectory-Based Difficulty Scoring for Reliable Learning on Tabular Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24680)
*Tomer Lavi,Bracha Shapira,Nadav Rappoport*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TDS (Trajectory-based Difficulty Score) is a novel instance-level difficulty estimator for gradient-boosted trees that uses per-tree prediction trajectories to identify hard-to-predict cases, enabling improved error analysis, active learning, and uncertainty quantification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gradient-boosted trees perform well on tabular data but leave a long tail of poorly predicted instances. Current methods lack effective instance-level difficulty estimation that can identify these hard cases to improve model understanding, active learning, and uncertainty quantification.

Method: TDS computes interpretable trajectory descriptors (variance, oscillation peaks, sign switches, tail stability) from per-tree cumulative prediction trajectories in boosted ensembles. A lightweight regression model predicts held-out loss, then an empirical CDF calibrates this into a [0,1] difficulty score.

Result: TDS shows strong rank correlation with error across diverse tabular benchmarks, outperforms established instance-hardness and uncertainty baselines on classification while remaining competitive on regression. It improves active learning for label efficiency, selective prediction for risk-coverage trade-offs, Mondrian conformal prediction for uniform coverage, and reveals coherent failure modes through SHAP-based clustering.

Conclusion: TDS provides an effective, interpretable instance-level difficulty estimator for gradient-boosted trees that enables multiple practical applications including error analysis, targeted data acquisition, and improved uncertainty quantification through trajectory-based analysis of model predictions.

Abstract: Gradient-boosted trees achieve strong performance on tabular data, yet often leave a long tail of poorly predicted instances. We introduce a Trajectory-based Difficulty Score (TDS), an instance-level difficulty estimator for boosted ensembles derived from per-tree cumulative prediction trajectories. For each instance, we compute interpretable trajectory descriptors (e.g., variance, oscillation peaks, sign switches, and tail stability) and train a lightweight regression model to predict held-out loss. An empirical CDF calibrates the resulting signal into a score in $[0,1]$ that supports ranking hard cases. Across diverse tabular benchmarks and ensemble sizes, TDS exhibits strong rank correlation with error and outperforms established instance-hardness and uncertainty baselines on classification, while remaining competitive on regression. We then show how a single difficulty signal improves multiple data mining workflows: difficulty-driven active learning for label-efficient training, difficulty-thresholded selective prediction for improved risk-coverage trade-offs, and TDS-stratified (Mondrian) conformal prediction for more uniform conditional coverage. Finally, clustering high-TDS instances using SHAP attributions reveals coherent failure modes characterized by compact feature-value ranges, supporting error analysis and targeted data acquisition.

</details>


### [245] [Beyond the Aggregation Dilemma: Prior-Retaining Decoupled Learning for Multimodal Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24684)
*Hao Yan,Xuanru Wang,Jun Yin,Shirui Pan,Senzhang Wang,Chengqi Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SUPRA is a new architecture that addresses performance degradation in Multimodal Attributed Graph Learning when using Large Foundation Models, by decoupling feature processing into topology-agnostic MLPs and a lightweight shared GNN, achieving better performance with lower computational costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper identifies a fundamental problem in Multimodal Attributed Graph Learning: when pretrained encoders evolve into Large Foundation Models, mandatory graph aggregation introduces topological noise that overwhelms discriminative signals, causing sophisticated MAGL architectures to underperform simple MLPs. This "performance inversion" occurs due to high-confidence LFM priors conflicting with traditional aggregation approaches.

Method: SUPRA (Shared-Unique Prior-Retaining Architecture) uses a decoupled dual-pathway paradigm: (1) modality-specific features are processed through topology-agnostic MLPs to preserve robust intrinsic features from LFMs, and (2) structural synergy is captured via a lightweight shared GNN. The approach includes auxiliary deep supervision to counteract gradient starvation issues.

Result: SUPRA achieves state-of-the-art performance while requiring 3.5x lower peak GPU memory and up to 4.4x faster training time compared to Multimodal Graph Transformers. The architecture successfully resolves the aggregation dilemma without compromising performance or efficiency.

Conclusion: The traditional mandatory aggregation approach in MAGL becomes problematic with Large Foundation Models, causing performance degradation. SUPRA's decoupled architecture provides an effective solution by preserving LFM priors while capturing structural relationships, establishing a new paradigm for multimodal graph learning with modern foundation models.

Abstract: Multimodal Attributed Graph Learning (MAGL) integrates intrinsic node attributes with structural topology via graph aggregation. However, as pretrained encoders evolve into Large Foundation Models (LFMs), the landscape of MAGL fundamentally shifts: under high-confidence LFM priors, mandatory aggregation introduces topological noise that overwhelms discriminative signals, triggering a counter-intuitive performance inversion where sophisticated MAGL architectures underperform simple topology-agnostic MLPs. Through systematic empirical and theoretical analysis, we identify that this inversion stems from a fundamental aggregation dilemma characterized by two concurrent pathologies: (1) Representational Pathology (SNR Degradation) - mandatory aggregation dilutes robust intrinsic features with topological noise, causing the noise penalty to outweigh its collaborative benefit; and (2) Optimization Pathology (Gradient Starvation) - topological aggregation attenuates gradient flow, while a shared task loss causes dominant modalities to prematurely suppress weaker ones. To resolve this dilemma, we propose SUPRA (Shared-Unique Prior-Retaining Architecture), a decoupled dual-pathway paradigm. SUPRA processes modality-specific features through topology-agnostic MLPs while capturing structural synergy via a lightweight shared GNN, with auxiliary deep supervision counteracting gradient starvation. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that SUPRA achieves state-of-the-art performance while requiring 3.5x lower peak GPU memory and up to 4.4x faster training time than Multimodal Graph Transformers.

</details>


### [246] [Streaming Reinforcement Learning under Partial Observability with Real-Time Recurrent Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24709)
*Noah Farr,Aryaman Reddi,Carlo D'Eramo,Jan Peters*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Streaming RL with recurrent trace units enables efficient online learning in partially observable environments using exact RTRL with linear complexity, outperforming truncated backpropagation methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current streaming RL methods struggle with partial observability because truncated backpropagation collapses to one-step gradients in streaming settings, and exact real-time recurrent learning is computationally prohibitive. There's a gap in scalable streaming RL for partially observable environments.

Method: Use recurrent trace units (RTUs), a diagonal recurrent architecture that enables exact Real-Time Recurrent Learning (RTRL) with linear time and memory complexity in parameter count. This integrates cleanly into existing streaming algorithms for both discrete and continuous control tasks.

Result: On MemoryChain diagnostics (chain lengths 2-128), RTU-based streaming maintains performance where streaming TBPTT(1) baselines with feedforward, GRU, and RTU networks collapse. On POPGym tasks and partially observable MuJoCo continuous control, streaming approach is competitive with batched PPO on POPGym and recovers substantial fraction of batched performance on masked MuJoCo, despite using no replay buffer or batched updates.

Conclusion: Recurrent trace units successfully close the gap for streaming RL in partially observable settings by enabling efficient exact RTRL, making online learning with deep function approximation viable for POMDPs while maintaining computational efficiency.

Abstract: Streaming reinforcement learning has emerged as an online learning paradigm that conforms to the restrictions of natural learning agents that process data incrementally, i.e. with a batch size of 1 and no replay buffer. While streaming RL has recently been shown to scale with deep function approximation with full observability, partially observable settings have remained out of reach. Truncated backpropagation through time collapses to a one-step gradient horizon under the streaming setting, and exact real-time recurrent learning is prohibitively expensive. We close this gap using recurrent trace units, a diagonal recurrent architecture that enables exact RTRL with linear time and memory complexity in the parameter count, and show that they integrate cleanly into existing streaming algorithms across both discrete and continuous control. On a MemoryChain diagnostic with chain lengths from 2 to 128, our method sustains performance where streaming TBPTT(1) baselines using feedforward, GRU, and RTU networks collapse. On five POPGym tasks and on partially observable MuJoCo continuous control, the streaming approach is competitive with batched PPO on POPGym and recovers a substantial fraction of batched performance on masked MuJoCo, despite using no replay buffer or batched updates.

</details>


### [247] [Feature Learning in Wide Neural Networks under $μ$P: Identifiability and Sparse-Dictionary Decomposition of the Mean-Field Limit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24710)
*Akmal Xodarev*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper establishes four key structural results about feature learning in wide two-layer neural networks under the Maximal Update Parametrization (μP), focusing on mean-field limits, identifiability, sparse decomposition of learned features, and error decomposition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to provide a rigorous theoretical foundation for understanding feature learning in wide neural networks, specifically addressing the structural properties of learned representations, identifiability of learned features, and error decomposition in the mean-field regime under μP.

Method: The authors use mean-field theory and noisy gradient descent analysis under Maximal Update Parametrization (μP). They establish existence/uniqueness results for mean-field limits, characterize parameter measure identifiability, analyze sparse dictionary decompositions under Barron-Hermite target conditions, and derive error decompositions with propagation-of-chaos analysis.

Result: Four main results: (1) Global existence/uniqueness of mean-field limit with O(N⁻¹) finite-particle approximation rate; (2) Characterization of parameter measure identifiability modulo finite-rank realization symmetry; (3) Sparse dictionary decomposition showing learned features are supported on at most S* atoms; (4) Complete error decomposition into statistical, optimization, propagation-of-chaos, and sparse-residual components.

Conclusion: The triple (w*, D*_orb, S*) - maximal admissible weight, orbit identifiability depth, and sparse-dictionary depth - forms the fundamental "learning cell" characterizing the architecture-data pair. The results provide a unified framework for understanding feature learning in wide two-layer networks under μP.

Abstract: We establish four structural results for feature learning in wide two-layer neural networks under the Maximal Update Parametrization ($μ$P).
  First, we prove global existence and uniqueness of the mean-field limit of noisy gradient descent under $μ$P, identifying the maximal admissible weight $w^*$ on the moment sequence of the initialization as the reciprocal parameter-moment-growth boundary, and hence the largest weighted moment class propagated by the flow. The finite-particle approximation has uniform-in-time squared-Wasserstein rate $O(N^{-1})$.
  Second, we characterize identifiability of the mean-field limit: two admissible parameter measures induce the same network function in $L^2$ exactly when their active components agree modulo the finite-rank realization symmetry of the architecture. The orbit depth $D^*_{\mathrm{orb}}$ is separated from the moment-variety depth $D^*_{\mathrm{var}}$.
  Third, under the Barron-Hermite target condition the active support of the long-time limit measure admits a sparse-dictionary decomposition: it is supported on at most $S^*$ atoms modulo finite-rank realization symmetry, with $S^*$ bounded by an explicit coefficient-threshold number.
  Fourth, we derive the total feature-learning-error decomposition into statistical, optimization, propagation-of-chaos, and sparse-residual components, with a target-dependent Hermite/Barron tail replacing any initialization-only residual.
  The four results are tied together by an architectural identity: the triple $(w^*, D^*_{\mathrm{orb}}, S^*)$ -- the maximal admissible weight, the orbit identifiability depth, and the sparse-dictionary depth at which the target is realizable -- is the natural learning cell of the architecture-data pair $(σ, ρ)$. The proofs are self-contained except for standard results from $μ$P and mean-field Langevin theory.

</details>


### [248] [Hardware-Aware Federated Learning for Speech Emotion Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24712)
*Beyazit Bestami Yuksel,Emrah Dikbiyik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hardware-aware FL framework for emotion recognition that reduces training time by 36.5% and communication cost by 40% compared to FedAvg while maintaining competitive accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated learning deployments face challenges with heterogeneous client hardware (different processing power, memory capacity, communication latency), which increases round duration and system costs, especially for emotion recognition tasks.

Method: Proposes a hardware-aware federated learning framework with hardware profiling, top-K client selection based on hardware capabilities, and adaptive local epochs within a unified training loop for emotion recognition on session-partitioned IEMOCAP dataset.

Result: Across 50 federated rounds and 5 independent trials under non-IID setup: achieves competitive validation accuracy (0.352), reduces total training time by 36.5% compared to FedAvg, and lowers cumulative communication cost by 40%.

Conclusion: The proposed hardware-aware FL framework effectively addresses client heterogeneity challenges in real deployments, significantly improving efficiency while maintaining competitive performance for emotion recognition tasks.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative training across distributed edge devices, but real deployments involve heterogeneous clients with different processing power, memory capacity, and communication latency, which often increase round duration and system cost. This paper proposes a hardware-aware federated learning framework for emotion recognition on session-partitioned IEMOCAP that integrates hardware profiling, top-K client selection, and adaptive local epochs within a unified training loop. We compare the method against FedAvg, FedProx, and random top-K selection under a non-IID setup and show that, across 50 federated rounds and 5 independent trials, the proposed approach achieves competitive validation accuracy (0.352), reduces total training time by about 36.5% compared to FedAvg, and lowers cumulative communication cost by 40%.

</details>


### [249] [Reinforcement Learning for Reachability: Guaranteeing Asymptotic Optimality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24740)
*Amogh Palasamudram,Jakub Svoboda,Suguman Bansal,Krishnendu Chatterjee*

Main category: cs.LG
