TL;DR: Proposes an alternative RL approach for reachability specifications that provides deeper theoretical convergence insights through iterative PAC learning with parameter refinement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL approaches for reachability specifications have limited theoretical convergence insights; while recent work achieves asymptotic convergence, it lacks understanding of convergence dynamics.

Method: Uses PAC learning with assumptions (requiring MDP parameters like minimum transition probability), and iteratively refines/estimates these parameters with increasing accuracy by satisfying PAC conditions iteratively.

Result: Shows that exact optimality can be achieved in the limit through iterative PAC learning, with empirical evaluations on standard benchmarks validating the theoretical convergence insights.

Conclusion: The iterative PAC learning approach provides deeper theoretical understanding of convergence dynamics for RL in reachability specifications while achieving exact optimality in the limit, addressing limitations of previous asymptotic convergence approaches.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) for reachability specifications is fundamental in sequential decision-making, yet theoretical guarantees remain less explored. A recent work achieves asymptotic convergence to optimal policies. However, this approach provides limited insight into convergence dynamics. In this work, we present an alternative approach that provides deeper theoretical insights into convergence. Our approach builds on PAC learning with assumptions. PAC learning guarantees near-optimal policies with high confidence in finite time but requires knowing internal MDP parameters like minimum transition probability. We argue that while these parameters are unknown in RL, they can be iteratively refined and estimated with increasing accuracy. By iteratively satisfying PAC conditions, we show that exact optimality can be achieved in the limit. Empirical evaluations on standard benchmarks validate our theoretical insights into convergence dynamics.

</details>


### [250] [Aligning Molecular Graph Explanations with Chemical Identity via InChIfied Invariants](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24742)
*Emanuele Guidotti,Sara Puglioli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: InChIfied Invariants are chemical identity-preserving molecular graph features that provide consistent predictions and explanations across different graph representations of the same molecule.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard molecular graph representations produce inconsistent predictions and explanations for chemically equivalent drawings of the same molecule, which violates chemical identity preservation principles needed for reliable machine learning in chemistry.

Method: Developed InChIfied Invariants - node, edge, and graph features based on the International Chemical Identifier (InChI) designed to be invariant under transformations that preserve chemical identity. Tested on one million molecular graphs from PubChem Substances and evaluated across MoleculeNet tasks.

Result: InChIfied Invariants produce identical representations for chemically equivalent graphs in 99.62% of cases vs 0.35% for standard Daylight invariants. Preserves predictive performance while significantly improving prediction consistency across alternative graph depictions. Enforces consistent attributions by construction compared to varying explanations with standard featurization methods.

Conclusion: InChIfied Invariants solve the consistency problem in molecular graph machine learning by providing chemically invariant features that ensure reliable predictions and explanations across different molecular representations, with open-source software available for adoption.

Abstract: Obtaining consistent explanations for machine learning on molecular graphs requires predictions and attributions to be aligned with chemical identity. However, chemically equivalent drawings of the same molecule can induce different molecular representations, leading to inconsistent predictions and explanations. Here, we introduce InChIfied Invariants, a class of node, edge, and graph features based on the International Chemical Identifier (InChI) and designed to be invariant under transformations that preserve chemical identity. Using one million molecular graphs from PubChem Substances, we show that InChIfied Invariants produce identical representations for chemically equivalent graphs in 99.62% of cases, whereas standard Daylight invariants do so in only 0.35% of cases. Across MoleculeNet tasks, InChIfied Invariants preserve predictive performance while significantly improving prediction consistency across alternative graph depictions of the same molecules. We further perform a quantitative attribution analysis and show that explanations produced with standard molecular featurization methods vary substantially across chemically equivalent graphs, while InChIfied Invariants enforce consistent attributions by construction. We release open-source software implementing InChIfied Invariants, which can be used as a drop-in replacement for standard molecular graph features.

</details>


### [251] [Bilevel Optimization of Synthetic Trajectories for Multi-Turn LLM Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24743)
*Shresth Verma,Mauricio Tec,Cheol Woo Kim,Kai Wang,Milind Tambe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BOOST: Bilevel Optimization framework for training LLMs on reweighted synthetic trajectories to improve multi-turn interaction performance without requiring external quality judges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle with long-horizon, multi-turn interactions despite excelling at single-turn generation. Offline RL offers scalability but depends on quality multi-turn trajectory data. Synthetic trajectory generation (by LLMs/simulators) helps but produces heterogeneous quality data - treating all synthetic data equally degrades performance.

Method: BOOST (Bilevel Optimization framework): Inner level trains LLM on reweighted synthetic+real data, outer level trains lightweight reweighting head on held-out real validation tasks. Assigns continuous trajectory-level weights without external judge. Grounded in PAC-Bayesian bound showing three-way trade-off between diversity, task-shift, and sample size.

Result: Empirically outperforms multiple baselines consistently. Analysis shows the method upweights synthetic trajectories that align with real data distribution and exhibit higher qualitative merit.

Conclusion: BOOST provides an effective solution for leveraging heterogeneous synthetic trajectory data in offline RL for LLM multi-turn interactions, addressing the quality-diversity trade-off through automatic trajectory reweighting without external judges.

Abstract: While LLMs excel at single-turn generation, they struggle with long-horizon, multi-turn interactions. Offline reinforcement learning (RL) offers a scalable approach, yet its performance hinges on the availability and quality of multi-turn trajectory data. A common remedy is to augment training with synthetic trajectories generated by LLMs or simulators, but synthetic data is highly heterogeneous in quality, and naively treating all trajectories as equally informative can degrade performance. We propose BOOST, a bilevel optimization framework where the inner level trains the LLM on reweighted data and the outer level trains a lightweight reweighting head on held-out real validation tasks, assigning continuous trajectory-level weights without requiring an external judge. To ground this approach, we derive a PAC-Bayesian bound revealing a three-way trade-off: synthetic data increases diversity but risks task-shift, while concentrating weight on high-quality trajectories improves empirical performance at the cost of effective sample size. Empirically, our method consistently outperforms multiple baselines. Analysis reveals it upweights synthetic trajectories that align with the real data distribution and exhibit higher qualitative merit.

</details>


### [252] [A computational phase transition for learning-to-sample from Ising models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24752)
*Andrej Risteski,Thuy-Duong Vuong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Learning-to-sample for Ising models undergoes a sharp computational phase transition at the spectral threshold λ_max(J)-λ_min(J)=1 - tractable below, computationally hard above under cryptographic assumptions, even with samples and parameter access.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the computational complexity of learning-to-sample (a fundamental task in generative modeling) for Ising models, which serve as a standard testbed for algorithmic ideas in both theoretical computer science and machine learning.

Method: Construct a family of Ising models with constantly bounded width that lie just beyond the spectral threshold (λ_max(J)-λ_min(J)=1). Show computational hardness of learning-to-sample for this family under standard cryptographic assumptions, even when given both polynomial samples and explicit parameter access.

Result: 1. Establishes a sharp computational phase transition at the spectral threshold for learning-to-sample in Ising models. 2. Shows learning-to-sample can be more difficult than parameter learning for bounded-width Ising models. 3. Demonstrates any efficient learner for these hard instances exhibits a memorization-hallucination dichotomy.

Conclusion: The spectral threshold λ_max(J)-λ_min(J)=1 marks a fundamental computational boundary for learning-to-sample in Ising models, separating tractable from computationally hard regimes under cryptographic assumptions, revealing that generating samples can be harder than learning parameters.

Abstract: We study \emph{learning-to-sample} -- a basic algorithmic task underlying generative modeling -- for Ising models, a standard testbed for algorithmic ideas in both theoretical computer science and machine learning. Given i.i.d. samples of an unknown target distribution, the goal of learning-to-sample is to learn a computationally efficient generation procedure that produces new samples following approximately the same distribution. We construct a family of Ising models of constantly bounded-width which lie just beyond the spectral threshold $λ_{\max}(J)-λ_{\min}(J)=1$, and show that learning-to-sample for this family is computationally hard under standard cryptographic assumptions, even when the learner is given both polynomially many i.i.d. samples from the model and explicit access to its parameters. Combined with results of [AJKPV24,KLV25] showing tractability of learning-to-sample below the spectral threshold, this establishes a sharp computational phase transition at the spectral threshold. Moreover, combined with prior results on parameter learning for bounded-width Ising models [KM17,WSD19,VML20], this shows that learning-to-sample can be more difficult than parameter learning. Finally, we show that any efficient learner for these hard instances exhibits a natural memorization-hallucination dichotomy: the learner must either output configurations that, after a simple transformation, match the (transformed) training data or place substantial mass on configurations of negligible probability under the target distribution.

</details>


### [253] [A Contractive Feedback Semantics for Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24759)
*Zuyuan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A compositional semantics views discounted reinforcement learning as wiring open stochastic components in contractive feedback loops. This yields congruence for approximate equivalence, commuting diagrams for abstractions, and lifting of quantale‑valued contracts for safety/risk reasoning, all via guarded Banach‑trace fixed points.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The work aims to move beyond the classical closed Markov decision process view of discounted reinforcement learning by developing a compositional, circuit-like semantics. This perspective seeks to enable more modular analysis, verification, and abstraction of RL systems, as well as to unify safety, risk, and resource reasoning under a common algebraic framework.

Method: The paper defines an architecture consisting of typed Bellman transformers assigned to open stochastic components, with composition and tensoring operations for series and parallel wiring. Feedback is represented formally as a guarded Banach trace, realized as a unique fixed point under discounted contractive conditions. Key technical tools include Banach-space operator semirings, guarded recursion, and quantale-valued contract specifications.

Result: The compositional semantics yields three main theoretical results: (1) approximate component equivalence remains a contextual congruence in guarded one-hole contexts, bounding global error from local error; (2) state abstractions correspond to commuting or near-commuting coalgebraic diagrams, preserving values with explicit distortion bounds; (3) safety, risk, and resource specifications can be lifted as quantale-valued contracts through wiring and feedback under monotone ω‑continuous contract‑transformer semantics.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that the compositional perspective, where discounted Bellman evaluation is modeled via contractive feedback semantics on a class of guarded circuits, provides a structured framework with useful theoretical implications. The central claim is that this semantics naturally admits a feedback-as-trace construction for admissible guarded circuits, rather than asserting a global traced monoidal structure for all reinforcement learning morphisms.

Abstract: Discounted reinforcement learning is usually presented through Bellman equations on closed Markov decision processes. This paper develops a compositional view: a one-step decision process is treated as an open stochastic component, and infinite-horizon policy evaluation is obtained by closing a contractive feedback loop. The resulting semantics assigns typed Bellman transformers to open components, interprets series and parallel wiring as composition and tensoring of transformers, and interprets feedback as an admissible guarded Banach trace realized by a unique fixed point. This perspective yields three theoretical consequences. First, approximate component equivalence is a contextual congruence for admitted well-typed guarded one-hole contexts: local operator error remains controlled after plugging the component into a surrounding circuit that uses the hole once and whose feedback nodes have certified uniform guardedness. Second, exact and approximate state abstractions become commuting or near-commuting coalgebraic diagrams, giving value-preservation and explicit sup-norm distortion bounds. Third, under monotone $ω$-continuous contract-transformer semantics, safety, risk, and resource specifications can be represented as quantale-valued contracts, where local inductive bounds lift through wiring and feedback by least-fixed-point reasoning. Its central claim is not that all RL morphisms form a global traced monoidal category, but that discounted Bellman evaluation admits a contractive feedback semantics on the admissible class of guarded circuits.

</details>


### [254] [High-fidelity Modeling of Full-scale Pressurized Water Reactor Flow Fields for Machine Learning Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24763)
*Logan A. Burnett,Hyungjun Kim,Hsien-Cheng Chou,Arsha Witoelar,Robert A. Brewster,Benoit Forget,Emilio Baglietto,Majdi I. Radaideh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: High-fidelity CFD combined with ML enables accurate prediction of heterogeneous flow distribution in PWR assemblies, with spatially-aware ML models outperforming sequence-based approaches for capturing complex spatio-temporal dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a computational framework for characterizing assembly-level flow in pressurized water reactors, addressing the need for accurate prediction of heterogeneous flow distributions and enabling data-driven surrogate models for reactor design and analysis.

Method: Combined high-fidelity computational fluid dynamics (CFD) with data-driven modeling using a full lower-plenum and core-inlet domain constructed from public geometry and operating data. The framework enabled transient simulations with pump-induced swirl boundary conditions and subsequent evaluation of machine learning models including 3D convolutional inpainting, ConvLSTM, LSTM, and DeepONet for flow reconstruction and prediction.

Result: CFD simulations revealed strongly heterogeneous assembly-wise inlet flow distributions near the lower core region due to cold-leg swirl and lower-plenum transport, with progressive homogenization at higher elevations. 3D convolutional inpainting successfully reconstructed missing mass flow rates with errors concentrated in turbulent bottom layers. ConvLSTM architectures significantly outperformed LSTM and DeepONet approaches by better capturing spatio-temporal dynamics.

Conclusion: High-fidelity CFD provides an essential foundation for developing data-driven surrogates, sparse sensing strategies, and future multiphysics coupling frameworks for nuclear reactor analysis, though challenges remain regarding turbulence sensitivity and validation data availability.

Abstract: This work presents a high-fidelity computational fluid dynamics (CFD) and data-driven modeling framework for assembly-level flow characterization in a four-loop pressurized water reactor (PWR). A full lower-plenum and core-inlet domain was constructed using publicly available geometry and operating conditions, enabling transient simulations with pump-induced swirl boundary conditions. The results show that cold-leg swirl and lower-plenum transport generate strongly heterogeneous assembly-wise inlet flow distributions, particularly near the lower core region, while axial resistance and mixing progressively homogenize the flow at higher elevations. These physics-informed datasets were subsequently used to evaluate machine learning (ML) applications for partial field reconstruction and short-term autoregressive prediction. A 3D convolutional-based inpainting model successfully recon-structed missing assembly-level mass flow rates from partial observations, with errors concentrated in the highly turbulent base (bottom) layer and diminishing significantly in upper layers. Comparative analysis across multiple ML models demon-strates that spatially aware architectures, particularly ConvLSTM, significantly outperform sequence-based (LSTM) and operator-learning (DeepONet) approaches by effectively capturing coupled spatio-temporal dynamics. The study also high-lights key challenges, including the sensitivity of inlet flow predictions to turbulence and mesh resolution, as well as the absence of full-scale experimental validation data. Despite these limitations, the results remain consistent with expected physical behavior. Overall, this work establishes high-fidelity CFD as a critical foundation for developing data-driven surrogates, sparse sensing strategies, and future multiphysics coupling frameworks.

</details>


### [255] [Muon in Vision Transformers: Optimizer-Recipe Interactions and Gradient Spectra](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24770)
*Ben S. Southworth,Shuai Jiang,Daniel McBride,Eric C. Cyr,Stephen Thomas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Muon outperforms AdamW in ViT training, with performance gains enhanced by data augmentation. Key differences appear in attention projections where Muon gradients have broader spectral basis compared to AdamW.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite Muon's success in transformer training, its behavior in vision transformers remains poorly understood. The research aims to investigate how Muon performs compared to AdamW in ViT training, particularly examining interactions with data augmentation recipes.

Method: Comparative analysis between Muon and AdamW for training Vision Transformers on ImageNet-100 and Pl@ntNet-300K using standard vision recipes with mixup, cutmix, smoothing, and random augmentation/erasing. Singular-value analysis of matrix gradients throughout the ViT architecture to understand spectral properties.

Result: Muon consistently outperforms AdamW, especially on long-tailed Pl@ntNet dataset. Gains are recipe-dependent, with Muon benefiting significantly more from advanced data augmentation. Spectral analysis reveals Muon spreads gradient energy across more singular modes in QKV gradients compared to AdamW, and full augmentation prevents late spectral concentration in deep MLP blocks. Muon also excels in segmentation and masked autoencoder tasks.

Conclusion: Muon demonstrates superior performance over AdamW for ViT training across multiple vision tasks, with key differences observed in attention projections and spectral characteristics of gradient matrices, highlighting the importance of optimizer-recipe interactions.

Abstract: Muon is a recently developed matrix-aware optimizer that has shown strong results in transformer training, but its behavior in vision transformers (ViTs) is not yet well understood. We study Muon for ViT training, largely on ImageNet-100 and Pl@ntNet-300K, comparing against AdamW under standard vision recipes involving mixup, cutmix, smoothing, and random augmentation and erasing. Muon consistently outperforms AdamW, with especially large gains on long-tailed Pl@ntNet macro top-1. These gains are also recipe-dependent, where Muon benefits much more than AdamW from advanced and significant data augmentation techniques. To understand this interaction, we analyze the singular-value structure of matrix gradients throughout the ViT. Within Muon training runs, removing heavy data augmentation induces a late-training spectral concentration and mode collapse in gradient matrices, primarily in deep MLP-down blocks. Under a fixed "full" augmentation recipe, the clearest Muon-AdamW contrast appears instead in QKV gradients, where AdamW gradient energy remains concentrated in a much narrower basis while Muon spreads energy across substantially more singular modes. Muon in ViTs is therefore best understood as an optimizer-recipe interaction. Under a fixed recipe, Muon differs from AdamW most clearly in attention projections, where its gradients consist of a broader spectral basis. Within Muon, a full training recipe is important for preventing late spectral concentration and mode collapse in deep feedforward blocks. We further demonstrate efficacy in training ViTs on image segmentation and masked autoencoder models, where Muon outperforms AdamW in all settings considered.

</details>


### [256] [Hermite-NGP: Gradient-Augmented Hash Encoding for Learning PDEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24774)
*Jinjin He,Zhiqi Li,Sinan Wang,Bo Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hermite-NGP is a gradient-augmented multi-resolution hash encoding that enables fast, accurate computation of spatial derivatives for neural PDE solvers using Hermite interpolation, achieving 20x lower error and 2-10x faster convergence than prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing NGP-based neural PDE solvers rely on automatic differentiation or finite differences, which suffer from instability or high computational cost when computing spatial derivatives.

Method: Hermite-NGP stores both function values and mixed partial derivatives at hash grid vertices, enabling fully analytic evaluation of gradients, Jacobians, and Hessians via Hermite interpolation. It also uses a multi-resolution curriculum training strategy similar to multigrid V-cycles for coarse-to-fine optimization.

Result: Across 2D and 3D PDE benchmarks, Hermite-NGP achieves up to 20x lower error than prior neural PDE methods, reduces wall-clock convergence time by 2-10x, and achieves per-epoch training times as low as 3.5 ms for models with up to 17M parameters.

Conclusion: Hermite-NGP provides an efficient, accurate approach for neural PDE solving by combining gradient-augmented hash encoding with analytic derivative computation, preserving NGP's efficiency while enabling stable, fast computation of differential operators up to second order.

Abstract: We propose Hermite-NGP, a gradient-augmented multi-resolution hash encoding designed to enable fast and accurate computation of spatial derivatives for neural PDE solvers. Unlike existing NGP-based approaches that rely on automatic differentiation or finite differences and suffer from instability or high cost, Hermite-NGP explicitly stores function values and mixed partial derivatives at hash grid vertices, allowing fully analytic evaluation of gradients, Jacobians, and Hessians via Hermite interpolation. This design preserves the efficiency and spatial adaptivity of NGP while supporting analytic differential operators up to second order. We further introduce a multi-resolution curriculum training strategy analogous to multigrid V-cycles to enable coarse-to-fine optimization. Across a range of 2D and 3D PDE benchmarks, Hermite-NGP achieves up to approximately 20 times lower error than prior neural PDE methods, and reduces wall-clock convergence time by 2 to 10 times compared to other solvers, with per-epoch training times as low as 3.5 ms for models with up to 17M parameters.

</details>


### [257] [Complement Submodular Information Measures for Balanced and Robust Data Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24779)
*Rishabh Iyer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Complement Submodular Information (CSI), a new class of complement-aware submodular objectives that preserve structural information between selected subsets and their complements, improving performance on tasks like robust subset selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical submodular optimization only optimizes selected subsets without preserving structural information between selected subsets and their complements. Many modern ML applications (train/validation/test splitting, benchmark construction, robust subset selection) require preserving balanced structure across both selected subsets and their complements.

Method: Introduces Complement Submodular Information (CSI), a new framework that induces complement-aware variants of classical submodular functions including Facility Location, Graph Cut, LogDet, Saturated Coverage, Set Cover, Probabilistic Set Cover, and Feature Based Functions.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows CSI objectives exhibit approximate monotonicity under bounded curvature conditions, leading to near-(1-1/e) greedy approximation guarantees. Empirically, CSI consistently outperforms standard submodular objectives on robust hidden-slice-aware subset selection, better preserving rare/tail semantic structure while suppressing noisy outliers.

Conclusion: CSI provides a principled framework for complement-aware submodular optimization that captures complementary notions of representativeness, diversity, connectivity, and balanced neighborhood preservation, leading to improved downstream predictive performance in various applications.

Abstract: Submodular optimization has become a fundamental paradigm for data selection, retrieval, summarization, and representation learning due to its ability to model coverage, diversity, and representativeness. However, classical submodular objectives optimize only the selected subset and do not explicitly preserve structural information between the selected subset and the remaining data. In many modern machine learning applications, including train/validation/test splitting, benchmark construction, and robust subset selection, the quality of a selection depends critically on preserving balanced structure across both the selected subset and its complement.
  In this work, we introduce Complement Submodular Information (CSI), a new class of complement-aware submodular objectives that quantify shared structural information between a subset and its complement. Our framework induces complement-aware variants of several classical submodular functions including Facility Location, Graph Cut, LogDet, Saturated Coverage, Set Cover, Probabilistic Set Cover, and Feature Based Functions. We analyze the theoretical properties of CSI objectives and show that they exhibit approximate monotonicity under bounded curvature conditions, leading to near-$(1-1/e)$ greedy approximation guarantees.
  Empirically, CSI objectives consistently outperform standard submodular objectives on robust hidden-slice-aware subset selection. In particular, CSI objectives significantly improve preservation of coherent rare/tail semantic structure while simultaneously suppressing noisy and isolated outliers, leading to substantially improved downstream predictive performance. Synthetic experiments further illustrate how different CSI instantiations capture complementary notions of representativeness, diversity, connectivity, and balanced neighborhood preservation.

</details>


### [258] [The Perception-Physics Paradox: Probing Scientific Alignment with TC-Bench](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24782)
*Dingling Yao,Andrea Polesello,Adeel Pervez,Caroline Muller,Francesco Locatello*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Current Vision Foundation Models for satellite imagery may appear accurate but rely on visual correlations rather than understanding physical systems, creating a Perception-Physics Paradox where models look correct but don't reason correctly about scientific phenomena.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision Foundation Models excel at predictive tasks on satellite imagery but their performance often arises from visual correlations rather than understanding underlying structural invariants. This means even perception-based out-of-distribution accuracy is a poor proxy for scientific utility, leading to models that may look correct without actually reasoning correctly about physical systems - a problem termed the Perception-Physics Paradox.

Method: The authors introduce scientific alignment as an implicit objective for representation learning in scientific domains. They study structural isomorphism as a principled, testable aspect of scientific alignment, requiring latent representations to uniquely identify physical systems up to linear reparameterization. This perspective induces a hierarchy of necessary conditions and yields a systematic probing protocol for physical and causal interpretability. They operationalize this framework by releasing TC-Bench, a global reproducible benchmark dataset with automated construction pipeline for tropical cyclone research.

Result: The authors show that current Vision Foundation Models rely on visual shortcuts that collapse in intense regimes, indicating that scientific alignment does not arise as a natural byproduct of scaling alone. Their TC-Bench benchmark demonstrates these limitations in tropical cyclone research.

Conclusion: There is a critical gap between perceptual accuracy and scientific understanding in Vision Foundation Models for scientific domains. The Perception-Physics Paradox highlights that scaling alone doesn't ensure scientific alignment. The proposed framework of scientific alignment and structural isomorphism provides a principled approach to evaluate and improve the scientific utility of models, with TC-Bench serving as a practical tool for assessing physical and causal interpretability in tropical cyclone research and potentially other scientific domains.

Abstract: While Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) excel at predictive tasks on satellite imagery, their performance can arise from visual correlations rather than underlying structural invariants, making even perception-based out-of-distribution accuracy a poor proxy for scientific utility. As a result, models may look correct without reasoning correctly, a discrepancy we term the Perception-Physics Paradox. To address this gap, we introduce scientific alignment as an implicit objective for representation learning in scientific domains. We study a principled, testable aspect of scientific alignment through structural isomorphism, which requires latent representations to uniquely identify physical systems up to a linear reparameterization. This perspective induces a hierarchy of necessary conditions and yields a systematic probing protocol for physical and causal interpretability. To operationalize this framework, we release TC-Bench, a global, reproducible benchmark dataset with an automated construction pipeline for tropical cyclone research, and show that current VFMs rely on visual shortcuts that collapse in intense regimes, indicating that scientific alignment does not arise as a natural byproduct of scaling alone.

</details>


### [259] [CONF-KV: Confidence-Aware KV Cache Eviction with Mixed-Precision Storage for Long-Horizon LLM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24786)
*Yubo Li,Yidi Miao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CONF-KV is a KV-cache manager that uses model uncertainty (derived from next-token distributions) to dynamically adjust cache retention, achieving near-full model performance with significantly reduced memory footprint.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon LLM inference causes KV cache to dominate GPU memory consumption and makes attention computation expensive. Existing eviction policies use static windows or historical attention but ignore the valuable signal of model uncertainty available at every decoding step.

Method: CONF-KV converts next-token distributions into scalar confidence scores to determine per-step cache budgets - retaining more context when uncertain, pruning aggressively when confident. Within budgets, tokens are ranked by accumulated attention mass and recency, with protected recent windows for local coherence. Combined with blockwise online-softmax attention, mixed FP16/INT8 storage, and pyramidal per-layer budgets.

Result: Across four model families up to 4K generated length: stays near 512-token sliding window footprint while within 1.5-2.1 perplexity points of full KV. Needle-in-a-Haystack (32K tokens): 91.4% retrieval accuracy vs 53.8% for sliding windows and 80.6% for H2O. VisualWebArena (75 tasks): retains 95.3% of full-KV success at 2.8x lower peak memory.

Conclusion: CONF-KV demonstrates that dynamic cache management based on model uncertainty enables efficient long-context LLM inference with minimal performance degradation, offering substantial memory savings and improved retrieval capabilities compared to existing methods.

Abstract: Long-horizon LLM inference turns the key--value (KV) cache into the dominant GPU memory consumer and makes per-token attention increasingly expensive. Many common eviction policies use static recency windows or historical attention, leaving unused a signal computed on every decoding step: the model's current uncertainty. We introduce CONF-KV, a KV-cache manager that converts the next-token distribution into a scalar confidence score and uses it to choose the per-step cache budget, retaining more context when the model is uncertain and pruning aggressively when it is confident. Within each budget, tokens are ranked by a composite of accumulated attention mass and recency, while a protected recent window preserves local coherence. We combine the policy with blockwise online-softmax attention, mixed FP16/INT8 storage, and a pyramidal per-layer budget variant. Across four model families and generated lengths up to 4K, CONF-KV stays near the footprint of a fixed 512-token sliding window while remaining within 1.5--2.1 perplexity points of full KV. On Needle-in-a-Haystack up to 32K tokens, CONF-KV reaches 91.4% retrieval accuracy versus 53.8% for sliding windows and 80.6% for H2O; on 75 VisualWebArena tasks it retains 95.3% of full-KV success at 2.8 times lower peak memory.

</details>


### [260] [Active Learning for Stochastic Contextual Linear Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24803)
*Emma Brunskill,Ishani Karmarkar,Zhaoqi Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An algorithm for contextual linear bandits that actively samples contexts (not just actions) to learn optimal policies faster, improving over minimax rates by up to √d factor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current contextual bandit algorithms passively sample contexts from the underlying distribution, but in many real-world applications (online recommendation, clinical trials, surveys), practitioners can actively select contexts. This active context sampling potential is underexplored despite its practical importance.

Method: Proposed algorithm that strategically samples rewards of context-action pairs, using an active context sampling strategy rather than passive sampling from the context distribution.

Result: Instance-dependent theoretical guarantees showing active context sampling can improve over minimax rate by up to √d factor (where d is linear dimension). Empirical results demonstrate reduced sample complexity for near-optimal policy learning in warfarin dose prediction and joke recommendation tasks.

Conclusion: Active context sampling in contextual linear bandits can significantly accelerate learning compared to passive approaches, with both theoretical and empirical validation showing practical benefits across various domains.

Abstract: A key goal in stochastic contextual linear bandits is to efficiently learn a near-optimal policy. Prior algorithms for this problem learn a policy by strategically sampling actions but naively (passively) sampling contexts from the underlying context distribution. However, in many practical scenarios -- including online content recommendation, survey research, and clinical trials -- practitioners can actively sample or recruit contexts based on prior knowledge of the context distribution. Despite this potential for active learning, the role of strategic context sampling in stochastic contextual linear bandits is underexplored. We propose an algorithm that learns a near-optimal policy by strategically sampling rewards of context-action pairs. We prove instance-dependent theoretical guarantees demonstrating that our active context sampling strategy can improve over the minimax rate by up to a factor of $\sqrt{d}$, where $d$ is the linear dimension. We show empirically that our algorithm reduces the number of samples needed to learn a near-optimal policy, in tasks such as warfarin dose prediction and joke recommendation.

</details>


### [261] [Disentangled Double Machine Learning for Accurate Causal Effect Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24808)
*Guodu Xiang,Kui Yu,Yujie Wang,Richang Hong,Fuyuan Cao,Jiye Liang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Disentangled Double Machine Learning (DDML) that uses covariate disentanglement and residual orthogonalization to overcome limitations of traditional DML for more accurate causal effect estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DML produces biased and unstable estimates in high-dimensional or finite-sample scenarios due to: 1) estimating nuisance functions using all covariates without disentangling distinct latent factors, and 2) imprecise nuisance estimation introducing residual dependence that undermines accuracy of causal effects.

Method: 1) Causal role disentanglement strategy to decompose covariates into confounders, treatment-specific factors, and outcome-specific factors for reliable nuisance function estimation. 2) Residual dependence orthogonalization strategy to mitigate residual dependence caused by nuisance estimation errors.

Result: DDML significantly outperforms 13 state-of-the-art baseline algorithms in both MAE and RMSE across synthetic, semi-synthetic, and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: DDML presents a promising approach for improving causal effect estimation by addressing limitations of traditional DML through covariate disentanglement and residual orthogonalization strategies.

Abstract: Confounding bias is a key challenge in causal effect estimation from observational data. Double Machine Learning (DML) addresses this issue by estimating treatment and outcome nuisance functions, constructing treatment and outcome residuals, and estimating causal effects from the residuals. However, DML often produces biased and unstable estimates in highdimensional or finite-sample scenarios. One reason is that DML estimates nuisance functions using all covariates without disentangling distinct latent factors, resulting in unreliable nuisance function estimation. Another is that imprecise nuisance estimation further introduces residual dependence between the treatment residual and the remaining outcome error, undermining the accuracy of causal effect estimates. To address these issues, in this paper, we propose Disentangled Double Machine Learning (DDML), a novel algorithm that integrates two key strategies. First, a causal role disentanglement strategy decomposes covariates into confounders, treatment-specific factors, and outcomespecific factors for enabling reliable nuisance function estimation. And second, a residual dependence orthogonalization strategy mitigates residual dependence caused by nuisance estimation errors for enhancing the precision of causal effect estimates. Experimental results on synthetic, semi-synthetic, and real-world datasets demonstrate that DDML significantly outperforms 13 state-of-the-art baseline algorithms in both MAE and RMSE.

</details>


### [262] [Cross-Domain Energy-Guided Diffusion Generation for Off-Dynamics Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24810)
*Yu Yang,Yihong Guo,Anqi Liu,Pan Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CEDGE proposes a cross-domain diffusion framework for off-dynamics offline RL that generates trajectory-level synthetic data through energy guidance rather than transition-level generation, enabling better adaptation to target domains with limited data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing off-dynamics offline RL methods are limited because they either: 1) are constrained to source dataset (reward augmentation/data filtering), 2) cannot synthesize new target behavior, or 3) generate experience at transition level leading to accumulated errors over long horizons. There's a need for trajectory-level generation to address these limitations.

Method: CEDGE trains a trajectory diffusion model on source-domain trajectories and adapts generated samples to target domain through energy guidance. The guidance minimizes distribution mismatch between source and desired target trajectories, decomposed into return, domain, and behavior energy components. Adaptation is achieved via energy guidance rather than retraining the diffusion model.

Result: Experiments on ODRL benchmark show that trajectory-level energy-guided generation improves diffusion planning under dynamics shifts and produces synthetic data that enhances downstream target policy learning. CEDGE can be efficiently adapted to new target dynamics compared to previous methods.

Conclusion: Trajectory-level energy-guided generation via CEDGE framework effectively addresses limitations of existing off-dynamics offline RL methods by enabling synthesis of target-domain behavior through efficient adaptation mechanisms, improving both planning and policy learning under dynamics shifts.

Abstract: Off-dynamics offline reinforcement learning seeks to learn a target-domain policy from a large source dataset and a limited target dataset under mismatched transition dynamics. Existing approaches such as reward augmentation and data filtering are constrained to the source dataset and cannot synthesize new target behavior to improve coverage beyond the collected source trajectories. While recent model-based methods attempt to address this by learning target-aware dynamics, the generated experience is constructed only at the transition level, which leads to accumulated errors over long horizons. These limitations necessitate a shift toward trajectory-level generation for off-dynamics offline RL. We propose CEDGE, a Cross-domain Energy-guided Diffusion GEneration framework. CEDGE trains a trajectory diffusion model on source-domain trajectories and adapts the generated samples to the target domain through energy guidance. This guidance is derived by minimizing the distribution mismatch between the source and desired target-domain trajectories and is decomposed into return, domain, and behavior energy components. The resulting energy-guided trajectories are useful both for direct planning and as synthetic data for policy learning. Since target adaptation is achieved via energy guidance rather than retraining the diffusion model, CEDGE can be efficiently adapted to new target dynamics compared to previous methods. Experiments on the ODRL benchmark demonstrate that trajectory-level energy-guided generation improves diffusion planning under dynamics shifts and produces synthetic data that improves downstream target policy learning.

</details>


### [263] [DriftingMol: Decoder-Coupled Drift for One-Pass Property-Conditional Molecular Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24841)
*Jiangjie Qiu,Yijun Li,Wentao Li,Xiaonan Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DriftingMol adapts diffusion models to SELFIES molecular space using decoder-coupled drift that maintains gradient flow through frozen decoder features for property-conditioned molecular generation with high correlation and uniqueness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Property-conditional molecular generation needs to produce valid, diverse molecules while responding to continuous target values efficiently with low sampling cost, addressing the challenge of maintaining chemical validity while controlling properties.

Method: Two-stage framework: 1) Uses frozen SELFIES beta-VAE for latent space, 2) Hidden decoder representations serve as drift feature map, 3) Decoder-coupled drift where decoder weights stay fixed but gradients backpropagate through decoder feature map to DiT generator, creating pullback metric aligned with molecular decoding.

Result: On ZINC250K: achieves QED Spearman correlation 0.493 with 94.7% uniqueness; strongest decoder-coupled condition reaches 0.510; under four-property conditioning reaches mean Spearman correlation up to 0.598. Models preserving gradient path through decoder features outperform latent-space, random-feature, and external-feature variants.

Conclusion: Decoder-coupled drift is an effective low-cost mechanism for property-biased molecular generation, requiring only one generator evaluation and one frozen decoder pass, while maintaining high property correlation and molecular uniqueness.

Abstract: Property-conditional molecular generation should produce valid, diverse molecules while responding to continuous target values at low sampling cost. We introduce DriftingMol, a two-stage framework that adapts drifting models to a SELFIES latent molecular space. A frozen SELFIES beta-VAE provides the latent space, and the hidden representation of its decoder serves as the drift feature map. In decoder-coupled drift, decoder weights remain fixed, but drift gradients are backpropagated through the decoder feature map to a DiT generator, inducing a pullback metric aligned with molecular decoding. On ZINC250K, the default setting achieves QED Spearman correlation 0.493 with 94.7% uniqueness, while the strongest decoder-coupled condition reaches 0.510. Under protocol-matched four-property conditioning, decoder-coupled drift reaches mean Spearman correlation up to 0.598. Across 15 controlled variants, models that preserve the gradient path through decoder features achieve higher correlations than the tested latent-space, random-feature, and external-feature drift variants, while detached or stop-gradient decoder controls yield near-zero QED correlation and very low uniqueness. These results indicate that decoder-coupled drift is a useful low-cost mechanism for property-biased molecular generation, requiring one generator evaluation and one frozen decoder pass.

</details>


### [264] [Tiny Brains, Giant Impact: Uncovering the Keystone Neurons of LLM with Just a Few Prompts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24846)
*Xiangtian Ji,Yuxin Chen,Zhengzhou Cai,Xiang Wang,An Zhang,Tat-Seng Chua*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMs have sparse keystone neurons crucial for capabilities that can be selectively fine-tuned for efficient adaptation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While LLMs demonstrate strong comprehensive abilities, their internal mechanisms remain poorly understood. The researchers aimed to identify specific neuron subsets that are critical for model capabilities across different tasks.

Method: Across multiple open-weight Transformers, they identified consistently highly activated neurons during inference across various capability tasks. They probed cross-task activation strength to isolate an extremely sparse subset of "keystone neurons," whose removal causes model behavior collapse. They then proposed a supervised fine-tuning approach that updates only these keystone neurons.

Result: Keystone neurons are a stable, intrinsic subset largely established during pretraining with tightly calibrated parameters critical for model capabilities. Fine-tuning only these neurons achieves task gains comparable to or better than full-parameter fine-tuning while better preserving performance in other capability dimensions, despite modifying far fewer parameters.

Conclusion: The discovery of keystone neurons provides insight into LLM mechanisms, and selective fine-tuning of these neurons offers an efficient approach to model adaptation that maintains multi-dimensional capabilities.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) display strong comprehensive abilities, yet the internal mechanisms that support these behaviors remain insufficiently understood. In this work, we show that across a wide range of open-weight Transformers, a subset of neurons remains consistently highly activated during inference across tasks of multiple capability dimensions. By probing along the cross-task activation strength, an extremely sparse subset is isolated, whose removal causes a collapse in model behavior, which we term keystone neurons. Our analysis reveals that keystone neurons are a stable and intrinsic neuron subset of the model that is largely established during pretraining. The parameters associated with these neurons are tightly calibrated during the training process, and their precise values are critical for the capabilities of the model. Building on these insights, we propose a supervised fine-tuning approach that updates only keystone neurons, achieving task gains comparable to or even better than full-parameter fine-tuning while better preserving performance in other capability dimensions, despite modifying a much smaller number of parameters.

</details>


### [265] [T2S-MPC: Time-Embedded Online Adaptive Model Predictive Control for Time-Varying Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24852)
*Zeyu Shen,Zhuoyuan Wang,Laixi Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: T2S-MPC is a learning-based MPC framework that adaptively learns residual dynamics models online with time-aware encoding and two-timescale updates to handle unpredictable time-varying disturbances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing learning-based MPC approaches primarily address specific or structured forms of dynamical variation, leaving general, unknown, and unpredictable time-varying dynamics insufficiently handled. There's a need for controllers that can adapt to fast-evolving, nonstationary system dynamics that deviate from nominal models.

Method: Proposes T2S-MPC framework that: 1) Adaptively learns a residual dynamics model online and integrates it with nominal model in MPC, 2) Uses structured time embedding to make model time-aware, 3) Employs two-timescale update scheme to balance rapid adaptation with stable learning for handling nonstationary dynamics.

Result: Experimental evaluation on 2D quadrotor for stabilization and trajectory tracking tasks under diverse time-varying disturbances (linear drifting and periodic perturbations) shows T2S-MPC consistently outperforms classical MPC, neural MPC, and ablated variants. Demonstrates strong robustness across wide disturbance conditions without additional tuning.

Conclusion: T2S-MPC effectively handles unpredictable time-varying dynamics through adaptive residual learning with temporal encoding and two-timescale updates, achieving superior control performance and robustness compared to existing approaches while requiring no additional parameter tuning.

Abstract: Recent advances in learning-based model predictive control (MPC) have leveraged neural networks for online model learning, achieving strong performance when nonstationary system dynamics deviate from nominal models. However, existing approaches primarily address specific or relatively structured forms of dynamical variation, leaving more general, unknown, and unpredictable time-varying dynamics insufficiently handled. To tackle this challenge, we propose T2S-MPC, a framework that adaptively learns a residual dynamics model online and integrates it with the nominal model within the MPC framework to enable fast-evolving online planning. To make the model time-aware, we explicitly encode temporal information through a structured time embedding and employ a two-timescale update scheme, allowing the controller to capture nonstationary dynamics while balancing rapid adaptation with stable learning. We evaluate the proposed method on a 2D quadrotor across stabilization and trajectory tracking tasks under diverse time-varying disturbances, including linear drifting and periodic perturbations. Experimental results show that T2S-MPC consistently outperforms classical MPC, neural MPC, and ablated variants in control performance, while also demonstrating strong robustness across a wide range of disturbance conditions without additional tuning. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/Zeyuu0920/T2S_MPC

</details>


### [266] [The Concept Allocation Zone: Tracking How Concepts Form Across Transformer Depth](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24856)
*James Henry*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformer LMs form concepts gradually across depth intervals called Concept Allocation Zones (CAZes), not at single layers. A CAZ is a depth region where a concept becomes measurably separable, identified via automated metrics without manual sweeps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard mechanistic interpretability methods identify a single "best layer" where class separation peaks, but this captures only a snapshot rather than the gradual emergence process of concepts across multiple layers in transformer models.

Method: Introduce Concept Allocation Zone (CAZ) framework with three layer-wise metrics: Separation, Concept Coherence, and Concept Velocity. Develop principled boundary detection for CAZes without manual layer sweeps. Validate across 34 models from 8 architectures and 7 concepts using empirical analysis and ablation studies.

Result: Separation curves are frequently multimodal; "gentle CAZes" (subtle allocation regions invisible to standard peak detection) are causally active in 93-100% of cases under ablation. The framework yields testable predictions with mixed support: four predictions show clear outcomes, one has invalid precondition, two underpowered. Cross-architecture alignment is depth-matched rather than monolithic.

Conclusion: Concept formation in transformers is a depth-extended process across contiguous regions (CAZes) rather than a single-layer event. A CAZ is the depth region where the model organizes geometry to make concepts separable; concepts can participate in multiple CAZes, and multiple concepts can share one CAZ. This provides a more accurate representation of concept emergence dynamics.

Abstract: Concept formation in transformer language models is depth-extended, not a single-layer event: concepts emerge gradually across a contiguous region of the residual stream. Mechanistic interpretability methods identify the single layer of peak class separation -- the "best layer" -- capturing a snapshot rather than the process itself. We introduce the Concept Allocation Zone (CAZ): the depth interval within which a concept becomes measurably separable, the region allocated to its geometric expression. We formalize the CAZ through three layer-wise metrics (Separation, Concept Coherence, Concept Velocity) and derive principled boundary detection without manual layer sweeps. A CAZ is not a concept: it is the depth region within which the model organizes its geometry to make a concept separable. A single concept typically participates in multiple CAZes; multiple concepts may share one. Empirical validation across 34 models from 8 architectural families and 7 concepts reveals that the separation curve S(l) is frequently multimodal. A scored detector uncovers "gentle CAZes" -- subtle allocation regions invisible to standard peak detection but causally active in 93-100% of cases under ablation (16 of 34 models; 26 in the companion validation paper). The framework generates seven testable predictions; four yield clear verdicts (two not supported, one partially supported, one supported), one had its precondition invalidated by the data, and two are underpowered -- with cross-architecture alignment confirmed as depth-matched rather than monolithic under leave-one-concept-out cross-validation. Reference implementation: rosetta_tools v1.3.1 (doi:10.5281/zenodo.20361433).

</details>


### [267] [Unifying Value Alignment and Assignment in Cross-Domain Offline Reinforcement Learning with Heterogeneous Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24862)
*Zhongjian Qiao,Jiafei Lyu,Chenjia Bai,Peisong Wang,Siyang Gao,Shuang Qiu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: V2A is a novel approach for heterogeneous cross-domain offline RL that integrates dynamics alignment, value alignment, and value assignment to address value misassignment issues when source datasets come from multiple domains with diverse behavior policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing cross-domain offline RL methods typically assume single-domain or single-behavior-policy source datasets, but real-world scenarios often involve heterogeneous source data from multiple domains collected by diverse behavior policies. This setting leads to value misassignment problems that degrade policy performance.

Method: V2A uses temporally-consistent modality representation learning to extract dynamics modalities from source data, followed by modality-aware advantage learning to correct value alignment. It then employs a data filtering paradigm to selectively share source data for policy learning.

Result: Empirical results demonstrate that V2A significantly outperforms strong baseline methods under general heterogeneous cross-domain offline RL settings.

Conclusion: The paper addresses value misassignment in heterogeneous cross-domain offline RL by integrating dynamics alignment, value alignment, and value assignment through V2A, which enables efficient policy transfer across diverse source domains and behavior policies.

Abstract: Cross-domain offline reinforcement learning (RL) aims to learn a policy in the target domain with a limited target domain dataset and a source domain dataset that exhibits a dynamics shift. Training directly on the original source dataset typically leads to performance collapse. Recent studies perform data filtering from the perspective of dynamics alignment or value alignment to enable efficient policy transfer. However, these studies are typically validated on single-domain or single-behavior-policy source datasets. In this work, we explore a more general heterogeneous cross-domain offline RL setting, where the source datasets may be collected from multiple source domains by diverse behavior policies. We first uncover a critical yet overlooked issue in this setting: value misassignment. Empirically and theoretically, we demonstrate that value misassignment can undermine value alignment, mislead data filtering toward selecting suboptimal samples, and loosen the suboptimality gap, thereby degrading the agent's performance. To address this issue, we propose V2A, which integrates dynamics alignment, value alignment, and value assignment. V2A first employs temporally-consistent modality representation learning to extract dynamics modalities from the source dataset, followed by modality-aware advantage learning to rectify value alignment. Finally, it adopts a data filtering paradigm to selectively share source data for policy learning. Empirical results show that V2A significantly outperforms strong baseline methods under general heterogeneous cross-domain offline RL settings.

</details>


### [268] [A comparative study of accuracy and rollout stability of temporal surrogate models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24868)
*Rajarshi Biswas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper compares deep learning architectures for chaotic system prediction, finding that integrator-like continuous-update models provide more stable long-horizon predictions with lower bias and perturbation amplification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Temporal surrogate models are needed for predicting chaotic dynamical systems where computational costs are prohibitive. Various deep neural network architectures exist, but there's a need for fair comparison to understand architectural impact on long-horizon prediction stability.

Method: The study compares common deep learning architectures using a common training protocol on three problems: double pendulum, Kuramoto-Sivashinsky equations, and Kolmogorov flow. Models are tested with matching capacity and individually optimized. Analysis includes stepwise error injections, perturbation amplifications using metrics like local Jacobian, relative one-step bias, and finite-time Lyapunov growth, plus attractor analysis and component ablation for continuous-update architectures.

Result: Models exhibit categorical differences in long-horizon rollouts in both scenarios (matching capacity and individually optimized). Integrator-like update models show lower bias and perturbation amplification, yielding more stable long-horizon rollouts and more accurate predictions.

Conclusion: Architecture choice significantly impacts long-horizon prediction stability for chaotic systems. Models with integrator-like continuous updates are superior for maintaining stability and accuracy over extended prediction horizons.

Abstract: Temporal surrogate models are effective for predicting chaotic dynamical systems where computational cost can be prohibitive. Several deep neural network architectures can be used for such purposes. In this work, a few commonly used architectures are compared using a common training protocol. The objective is to fairly assess the impact of model architectures for long-horizon prediction stability. Experiments are carried out for three problems, the double pendulum, the Kuramoto-Sivashinsky equations, and the Kolmogorov flow. The experiments are carried out with matching model capacity. Analysis is also carried out for a scenario where each model is individually optimized. It is observed that in both scenarios, the models exhibit categorical differences in long-horizon rollouts. For a concrete quantification, stepwise error injections and perturbation amplifications are analyzed using metrics such as local jacobian, relative one-step bias, and finite-time Lyapunov growth. Additionally, an attractor analysis is also conducted to assess how well the learned models replicate the underlying system geometry. An ablation study to isolate the impact of each component of a continuous-update architecture is also carried out. It is concluded that models that having integrator-like updates show lower bias and perturbation amplification yielding stable long-horizon rollout and more accurate predictions.

</details>


### [269] [Cluster Frequency Conformal Prediction for Local Coverage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24872)
*Tomer Lavi,Bracha Shapira,Nadav Rappoport*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CFCP is a conformal prediction framework that improves class coverage in many-class classification by leveraging cluster-based frequency estimates from learned representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard conformal prediction provides distribution-free coverage guarantees but may under-cover specific classes or subpopulations in many-class classification, preventing safe deployment in high-stakes applications where reliable per-class coverage is crucial.

Method: CFCP clusters learned embeddings, estimates cluster-level label-frequency distributions from calibration data, and for each test point constructs a sample-specific probability vector by softly mixing nearby cluster distributions regularized with global-prior and reliability-aware shrinkage, then conformalizes using standard set constructors.

Result: CFCP achieves the best class coverage in 15/16 dataset/score-family comparisons across image and text benchmarks, with competitive prediction set size efficiency and several settings substantially more efficient.

Conclusion: Cluster-frequency information provides an effective localized signal for improving classwise reliability in many-class conformal prediction, with CFCP inheriting standard finite-sample marginal validity and admitting local-validity interpretation under additional assumptions.

Abstract: Conformal prediction provides distribution-free coverage guarantees, but in many-class classification it may still under-cover specific classes or subpopulations, preventing safe deployment in high-stakes applications. We propose Cluster Frequency Conformal Prediction (CFCP), a plug-in framework that adapts conformal prediction to local structure in a learned representation space. CFCP clusters learned embeddings, estimates cluster-level label-frequency distributions from calibration data, and for each test point constructs a sample-specific probability vector by softly mixing nearby cluster distributions regularized with global-prior and reliability-aware shrinkage. This vector is then conformalized using standard set constructors. In the disjoint-split regime, CFCP inherits standard finite-sample marginal validity. Under additional assumptions, CFCP further admits a local-validity interpretation. Since representation clusters aggregate locally similar samples, their empirical class frequencies provide a stable estimate of local label ambiguity. Across image and text benchmarks, CFCP achieves the best class coverage in 15/16 dataset/score-family comparisons and a competitive prediction set size efficiency, with several settings substantially more efficient. Overall, our results show that cluster-frequency information provides an effective localized signal for improving classwise reliability in many-class conformal prediction.

</details>


### [270] [Efficient DP-SGD for LLMs with Randomized Clipping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24879)
*Enayat Ullah,Sai Aparna Aketi,Devansh Gupta,Huanyu Zhang,Meisam Razaviyayn*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DP-SGD-RC is a memory-efficient variant of DP-SGD that uses randomized clipping with stochastic trace estimation to reduce the O(B min{T², d²}) memory overhead of per-sample gradient norm computation, enabling differentially private fine-tuning of large language models on long contexts with competitive utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Differential privacy is essential for training LLMs on sensitive data, but current DP training implementations have prohibitive memory overhead (O(B min{T², d²})) that becomes impractical as model size and context length increase, limiting the ability to apply DP to large-scale LLMs.

Method: Proposes DP-SGD-RC, a novel variant of DP-SGD with randomized clipping that leverages stochastic trace estimation methods (Hutchinson's estimator and its improved variant Hutch++) to estimate per-sample gradient norms with reduced memory and computational complexity compared to deterministic clipping approaches.

Result: Experiments fine-tuning Llama 3.2-1B on long-context benchmarks for classification, question answering, and summarization tasks show that DP-SGD-RC matches baseline utility while significantly reducing memory and compute requirements, with privacy analysis demonstrating competitive noise multipliers compared to deterministic clipping.

Conclusion: DP-SGD-RC provides an efficient solution to the memory bottleneck problem in DP training for large language models, enabling practical differentially private fine-tuning on long contexts without sacrificing utility, which is crucial for privacy-preserving LLM deployment.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are trained on vast datasets that may contain sensitive information. Differential privacy (DP), the de facto standard for formal privacy guarantees, provides a principled framework for training LLMs with provable privacy protection. However, state-of-the-art DP training implementations rely on fast gradient clipping techniques with memory overhead $O(B \min\{T^2, d^2\})$, where $B$ is the batch size, $T$ is the sequence length, and $d$ is the model width. This becomes prohibitive as both model size and context length grow. We propose DP-SGD-RC, a novel variant of DP-SGD with randomized clipping that reduces memory and compute complexity. DP-SGD-RC leverages stochastic trace estimation methods, specifically Hutchinson's estimator[Hutchinson, 1989] and its improved variant, Hutch++[Meyer et al., 2021], to reduce the memory footprint of per-sample gradient norm estimation. We provide a tight privacy analysis showing that DP-SGD-RC achieves noise multipliers competitive with deterministic clipping. Experiments fine-tuning Llama~3.2-1B on long-context benchmarks spanning classification, question answering, and summarization tasks demonstrate that DP-SGD-RC matches baseline utility while significantly reducing memory and compute requirements.

</details>


### [271] [On the Impact of Class Imbalance on the Learning Dynamics of Deep Neural Networks:An Intuitive Insight](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24908)
*Ismail B. Mustapha,Shafaatunnur Hasan,Sunday O. Olatunji,Hatem S. Y. Nabus*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Class imbalance in DNNs causes early underfitting of minority classes, leading to non-generalizable minority representations despite eventual learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite increased research attention on class imbalance in DNNs, there is limited understanding of how this phenomenon actually impacts DNN performance, making it difficult to develop effective imbalance methods.

Method: Systematically investigates impact of class imbalance by monitoring DNN learning patterns on both majority and minority classes across datasets with varying imbalance ratios.

Result: Class imbalance severely deteriorates DNN performance by causing early underfitting of minority classes while simultaneously learning only majority classes. Although DNNs eventually learn minority samples, the resulting representations are non-generalizable because they are merely overfitted to minimize overall training loss.

Conclusion: The study reveals that class imbalance fundamentally alters DNN learning dynamics, leading to suboptimal minority class representations that don't generalize well, highlighting the need for better imbalance handling methods.

Abstract: Class imbalance in deep neural networks (DNNs) has witnessed a rapid increase in research attention in recent years. However, the varying accounts of the reasons behind the poor performance of DNN on imbalance data in pertinent literature shows that little is known about how this agelong phenomenon impacts the performance of DNNs. A better understanding of this problem is crucial to developing effective DNN-based imbalance methods. Thus, this study systematically investigates the impact of class imbalance on the learning dynamics of DNN by monitoring the learning pattern of DNN models on both the majority and minority classes of datasets of varying imbalance ratios. Experimental findings shows that as against learning from balanced datasets where DNN learns the classes similarly, class imbalance has severe deteriorating impact on the performance of DNN, driving the model to underfit the minority class samples in the early training epochs while simultaneously learning only the majority class. Although DNN ultimately learns the minority samples, learning in this manner only results in learnt minority representations that are non-generalizable at test phase because they are merely overfitted to keep the overall training loss as low as possible.

</details>


### [272] [Factorize to Generalize: Retrieval-Guided Invariant-Dynamic Decomposition for Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24911)
*Jinjin Chi,Lei Feng,Lulu Zhang,Yongcheng Jing,Yiming Wang,Ximing Li,Jialie Shen,Leszek Rutkowski,Dacheng Tao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A retrieval-guided framework decomposes time series into invariant (stable) and dynamic (context-dependent) components to improve robustness in zero-shot forecasting, addressing limitations of existing retrieval-based methods that over-oscillate predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current retrieval-based time series foundation models have a key limitation: retrieval induces oscillatory predictions that perform well on fluctuating series but degrade on smoother, trend-dominated ones. This suggests retrieved information is fused without distinguishing stable structure from instance-specific variations, reducing robustness under distribution shifts.

Method: Proposes Retrieval-guided Invariant-Dynamic DEcomposition framework (RIDE). Instead of using retrieval as predictive context, leverages retrieved sequences as implicit samples from related environments to guide representation decomposition. Key components: 1) Retrieval-aware representation via attention-based aggregation, 2) Retrieval-guided routing mechanism to decompose into invariant (stable shared structure) and dynamic (context-dependent variations) components, 3) Separate forecasting of components with fusion for final prediction, 4) Training objectives for invariant learning and disentanglement.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate the method consistently improves robustness under distribution shifts and outperforms existing time series foundation models and retrieval-based baselines in zero-shot forecasting settings. Theoretically shows that retrieval aggregation reduces variance and approximates invariant representation learning without explicit environment supervision.

Conclusion: The proposed RIDE framework effectively addresses limitations of current retrieval-based forecasting by explicitly decomposing time series representations into invariant and dynamic components, enabling preservation of transferable patterns while maintaining adaptability to evolving dynamics, leading to improved zero-shot forecasting robustness.

Abstract: Time series foundation models (TSFMs) have recently achieved strong zero-shot forecasting performance through large-scale pretraining and retrieval-augmented prediction. However, our empirical analysis reveals a non-trivial limitation of retrieval-based forecasting: retrieval tends to induce more oscillatory predictions, improving performance on highly fluctuating series while degrading accuracy on smoother, trend-dominated ones. This suggests that retrieved information may be fused into prediction without explicitly distinguishing stable temporal structure from instance-specific variations, which can reduce robustness under distribution shifts. We propose a Retrieval-guided Invariant-Dynamic DEcomposition framework for time series forecasting. Rather than using retrieval as auxiliary predictive context, we leverage retrieved sequences as implicit samples from related environments to guide representation decomposition. Specifically, we first construct a retrieval-aware representation via attention-based aggregation, and then introduce a retrieval-guided routing mechanism to decompose it into an invariant component capturing stable shared structure and a dynamic component modeling context-dependent variations. These two components are forecast separately and fused for final prediction, enabling the model to preserve transferable patterns while remaining adaptive to evolving dynamics. We further design training objectives that encourage invariant learning and disentanglement, and provide theoretical insight showing that retrieval aggregation reduces variance and approximates invariant representation learning without explicit environment supervision. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method consistently improves robustness under distribution shifts and outperforms existing TSFMs and retrieval-based baselines in zero-shot forecasting settings.

</details>


### [273] [Explainable Retinal Imaging for Prediction of Multi-Organ Dysfunction in Type 2 Diabetes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24912)
*Mini Han Wang,Liting Huang,Wei Hong,Boonthawan Wingwon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ML models using routine lab biomarkers can predict multi-organ dysfunction in Type 2 diabetes with high accuracy, revealing key drivers like hyperglycemia and inflammation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current clinical assessments often fail to capture the multi-dimensional burden of Type 2 diabetes, which is increasingly recognized as a systemic disease affecting multiple organ systems.

Method: Retrospective study of 1,195 patients using routine laboratory biomarkers to construct system-level abnormality indices. Multi-system involvement defined as abnormalities in ≥2 systems. Supervised ML models (logistic regression, random forest, gradient boosting) trained to predict multi-system dysregulation, with SHAP for interpretability.

Result: Gradient boosting model achieved near-perfect discrimination (AUC=1.000), significantly outperforming logistic regression (AUC=0.925). SHAP analysis identified hyperglycemia, renal impairment, dyslipidemia, and inflammation as dominant risk drivers. Dose-response relationships supported biological plausibility.

Conclusion: The study presents an interpretable, data-driven framework for quantifying systemic disease burden in T2DM, offering both predictive accuracy and mechanistic insights for improved risk stratification and precision medicine.

Abstract: Background: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) is increasingly recognised as a systemic disease characterised by coordinated dysfunction across metabolic, renal, lipid, and inflammatory pathways. Existing clinical assessments often fail to capture this multi-dimensional burden.
  Methods: We conducted a retrospective study of 1,195 patients using routinely collected laboratory biomarkers. System-level abnormality indices were constructed to quantify organ-specific dysfunction, and multi-system involvement was defined as abnormalities in two or more systems. Supervised machine learning models, including logistic regression, random forest, and gradient boosting, were trained to predict multi-system dysregulation. Model interpretability was achieved using SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP).
