  Results: The gradient boosting model demonstrated near-perfect discrimination (AUC = 1.000), significantly outperforming logistic regression (AUC = 0.925). Feature attribution analysis revealed that hyperglycaemia, renal impairment, dyslipidaemia, and inflammation were the dominant drivers of multi-system risk. Dose-response relationships observed in partial dependence analyses further supported the biological plausibility of model predictions.
  Conclusion: This study presents an interpretable, data-driven framework for quantifying systemic disease burden in T2DM. By linking routine biomarkers to multi-organ dysfunction, our approach provides both predictive accuracy and mechanistic insight, offering potential for improved risk stratification and precision medicine in diabetes care.
  The data and code used in this study are openly available on GitHub at: https://github.com/MiniHanWang/Type-2-Diabetes-1.git

</details>


### [274] [Quaternion Self-Attention with Shared Scores](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24920)
*Shogo Yamauchi,Tohru Nitta,Hideaki Tamori*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Shared-score quaternion self-attention reduces computational cost by 75% while maintaining performance by using a single real-valued score instead of four independent component scores.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing quaternion self-attention has high computational cost (4x score computations and softmax operations) and allows attention distributions to diverge across components, potentially limiting efficiency and consistency.

Method: Proposes shared-score quaternion self-attention that computes a single real-valued score using quaternion inner product and applies shared attention distribution across all components. Proves mathematically that component-wise and shared scores lie in same interaction subspace when queries/keys come from quaternion linear projections with component pre-mixing.

Result: Reduces score-computation multiplications by 75%, softmax operations from 4 to 1. Achieves 44.3% inference time reduction on GPU and 58.1% on CPU in speech enhancement while maintaining quality, with consistent improvements across vision and NLP tasks.

Conclusion: Shared-score quaternion self-attention is an efficient alternative that maintains performance while significantly reducing computational overhead, showing independent component-wise attention primarily re-parameterizes same interactions rather than expanding feature interaction space.

Abstract: Quaternion neural networks are parameter-efficient and model multidimensional dependencies by representing four related features as a single entity. However, existing quaternion self-attention computes component-wise scores and applies independent softmax operations to each component, which increases the computational cost and allows attention distributions to diverge across components. We propose a shared-score quaternion self-attention mechanism that computes a single real-valued score using the quaternion inner product and applies a shared attention distribution across all components. This reduces score-computation multiplications by 75% and the number of softmax operations from four to one. We prove that, when queries and keys are produced by quaternion linear projections that induce component pre-mixing, the component-wise and shared scores lie in the same interaction subspace, indicating that independent component-wise attention primarily re-parameterizes the same interactions rather than expanding the feature interaction space. In speech enhancement, our method reduces inference time by up to 44.3% on a GPU and 58.1% on a CPU while maintaining quality, with consistent trends across vision and natural language processing.

</details>


### [275] [BandVQ: Band-Wise Vector-Quantized EEG Foundation Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24921)
*Jamiyan Sukhbaatar,Satoshi Imamura,Toshihisa Tanaka*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BandVQ is a novel EEG foundation model that uses band-wise vector quantization to capture frequency-specific information, achieving superior cross-dataset transfer performance on cognitive and motor tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing EEG foundation models using broadband continuous patches or single discrete representations fail to adequately capture frequency-specific activity, which is crucial for diverse EEG applications across different tasks, montages, and spectral characteristics.

Method: Band-wise vector quantization with independent VQ-VAE tokenizers for delta, theta, alpha, beta, and gamma bands, followed by pretraining a shared Transformer encoder on discrete VQ code indices using masked modeling with specialized tokens and region-based masking.

Result: The model achieves state-of-the-art transfer performance on three cognitive tasks and competitive performance on three motor imagery tasks when evaluated on six subject-independent classification datasets after pretraining on 71 public EEG corpora.

Conclusion: BandVQ demonstrates that band-wise vector quantization for EEG foundation modeling effectively captures frequency-specific information, leading to superior transfer performance across diverse EEG datasets and tasks.

Abstract: A central challenge in electroencephalography (EEG) foundation modeling is learning transferable representations across recordings with diverse tasks, montages, references, and spectral characteristics. Existing masked modeling approaches often rely on broadband continuous patches or a single discrete representation, which may underrepresent frequency-specific activity. This paper proposes BandVQ, a band-wise vector-quantized EEG foundation model that decomposes EEG into delta, theta, alpha, beta, and gamma bands, trains an independent VQ-VAE tokenizer for each band, and pretrains a shared Transformer encoder on the resulting discrete VQ code indices. The encoder uses masked code tokens, quantized absolute log-power tokens, channel and temporal embeddings, and metadata prefix tokens representing reference, band, task family, and phase. Region-based masking is also introduced to reduce the trivial reconstruction of spatially adjacent electrodes. The model is pretrained on 71 public EEG corpora comprising over 9,200 subjects and 357,000 single-channel hours and evaluated on six subject-independent classification datasets. Under the current evaluation setting, the proposed model achieves strong transfer performance, with the highest reported results on three cognitive tasks and competitive performance on three motor imagery tasks.

</details>


### [276] [Global linear convergence of entropy-regularized softmax policy gradient beyond tabular MDPs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24939)
*Ziyue Chen,David Šiška,Lukasz Szpruch*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Global convergence of entropy-regularized policy gradient is proven for continuous MDPs with linear function approximation under certain feature realizability conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To extend the global convergence theory of entropy-regularized softmax policy gradient beyond the tabular setting to continuous state and action spaces with function approximation.

Method: Study log-linear softmax policies with linear function approximation, establish non-uniform Polyak-Łojasiewicz inequality under Qπτ-realizability, analyze two feature regimes (full-affine-span and simplex-valued features) to bound non-uniform constants, and prove radial unboundedness of KL regularizer.

Result: For both feature regimes, global linear convergence of the regularized objective along gradient flow is proven, with suboptimality decaying as O(e^{-Ct}) for some C>0.

Conclusion: The analysis successfully extends global convergence theory of entropy-regularized softmax policy gradient to continuous MDPs with linear function approximation, overcoming limitations of tabular-only results.

Abstract: We study the global convergence of policy gradient for infinite-horizon entropy-regularized Markov decision processes (MDPs) with continuous state and action spaces. We consider log-linear softmax policies with linear function approximation, which extend the tabular softmax parameterization while retaining a tractable policy class. Under $Q^π_τ$-realizability for the regularized state-action value function, we first establish a non-uniform Polyak--Łojasiewicz (PŁ) inequality. The non-uniformity arises through degeneracy of constants associated with the policy geometry, namely the Fisher information matrix or an uncentered feature covariance matrix. We then identify two feature regimes under which this non-uniform constant can be bounded along the gradient flow. For full-affine-span features, we prove radial unboundedness of the KL regularizer and show that the smallest eigenvalue of the Fisher information matrix remains bounded below by an initialization-dependent positive constant. For simplex-valued features, we prove an analogous radial unboundedness result in the subspace orthogonal to the all-ones vector and obtain a uniform lower bound for the smallest eigenvalue of the uncentered covariance matrix. These results imply global linear convergence of the regularized objective along the gradient flow, i.e. suboptimality decaying as $\mathcal{O}(e^{-Ct})$ for some $C>0$. Our analysis extends the global convergence theory of entropy-regularized softmax policy gradient beyond the tabular setting of Agarwal et al. (2020); Bhandari and Russo (2024); Mei et al. (2020).

</details>


### [277] [Riemannian-Manifold Steering: Geometry-Aware Generative Autoencoders for Label-Free Steering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24942)
*Narmeen Oozeer,Shivam Raval,Philip Quirke,Manikandan Ravikiran,Jeff Phillips,Shriyash Upadhyay,Amirali Abdullah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified Riemannian framework for manifold steering that operates without labeled centroids or boundary condition assumptions, using output-space distances via concept schemas instead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing manifold steering methods have restrictive assumptions: nonlinear methods lack explicit geometry, while geometry-aware methods require labeled class centroids and prescribed cyclic/sequential structure with compatible boundary conditions, limiting their applicability.

Method: The proposed approach computes Riemannian geodesics by pulling back the output-space Hellinger distance to activation space, approximated via a learned encoder trained on output distances from a small concept-token schema (without per-prompt labels or topology priors).

Result: The method reliably steers language models onto target classes across all tasks in a standard four-task language-model arithmetic benchmark, while following more behaviorally natural trajectories than baselines on smaller output spaces.

Conclusion: This paper reframed manifold steering as Riemannian geodesic computation on activation spaces, providing a unified framework that encompasses previous linear and spline-based methods as special cases.

Abstract: Steering a language model - intervening on its internal activations to change downstream behaviour - has recently expanded beyond linear interpolation to nonlinear methods such as angular and kernelized steering, which define intervention transformations without learning an explicit geometry over paths in activation space. Freshly introduced geometry-aware manifold methods do learn such a geometry, but require labelled class centroids together with prescribed cyclic or sequential structure. These assumptions restrict where manifold steering can be applied, since existing constructions require labelled centroids and compatible boundary conditions. We recast manifold steering more broadly as \textbf{Riemannian geodesic computation} on activation space, recovering linear and labelled-spline steering as geodesics under particular choices of metric. A principled metric within this framework is the output-space Hellinger distance pulled back to activations; we approximate this with a learned encoder trained on output distances over a small concept-token schema - no per-prompt labels, no topology prior, and no per-task curve fitting. Empirically, the method reliably drives the model onto the target class across all tasks in a standard four-task language-model arithmetic benchmark, while following more behaviourally natural trajectories than baselines on smaller output spaces. We thereby provide a unified Riemannian framework for manifold steering together with a schema-supervised, label-free instantiation that operates without labelled centroids or prescribed boundary conditions.

</details>


### [278] [RealBench: Benchmarking Data-Driven Numerical Weather Forecasting Under Operational Conditions and Extreme Event Challenges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24945)
*Ruize Li,Zhibin Wen,Tao Han,Hao Chen,Fenghua Ling,Wei Zhang,Song Guo,Lei Bai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RealBench is a new benchmark for AI weather forecasting that uses real-time operational data instead of delayed reanalysis, providing more realistic evaluation under actual forecasting constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks rely on ERA5 reanalysis products which use delayed data assimilation and don't reflect real-time operational constraints, creating a systematic mismatch between benchmark performance and real-world forecasting accuracy.

Method: Created RealBench with: 1) strictly out-of-distribution test set spanning 2025 to prevent data leakage, 2) integration of low-latency operational analysis data, 3) large-scale global in-situ observation dataset with over 10,000 stations for direct evaluation against real measurements, 4) comprehensive framework for evaluating high-impact extreme events like heatwaves, cold surges, and tropical cyclones with event-specific metrics.

Result: Evaluation reveals substantial discrepancies between reanalysis-based metrics and real-world performance, especially for extreme events, showing limitations of existing benchmarks.

Conclusion: RealBench establishes a more faithful and operationally relevant evaluation paradigm for AI weather forecasting systems, providing a rigorous foundation for advancing next-generation forecasting capabilities with better alignment to real-world deployment constraints.

Abstract: Accurate evaluation of weather forecasting models is critical for their reliable deployment in real-world applications. However, existing benchmarks predominantly rely on reanalysis products such as ERA5, which are generated through delayed data assimilation and do not reflect the constraints of real-time operational forecasting, thereby resulting in a systematic mismatch between benchmark performance and real-world forecasting. In this work, we introduce RealBench, a next-generation benchmark for AI weather forecasting that emphasizes realistic evaluation under operational conditions. RealBench features a strictly out-of-distribution test set spanning 2025 to eliminate data leakage and capture recent atmospheric regimes. It integrates multiple data sources, including low-latency operational analysis and a large-scale global in-situ observation dataset comprising over 10,000 stations, enabling direct evaluation against real atmospheric measurements. Beyond standard global metrics, RealBench provides a comprehensive evaluation framework for high-impact extreme events, including heatwaves, cold surges, and tropical cyclones, using event-specific metrics that better reflect real-world forecasting priorities. The evaluation results reveal substantial discrepancies between reanalysis-based metrics and real-world performance, particularly concerning extreme events. By highlighting the limitations of existing benchmarks, this work establishes a more faithful and operationally relevant evaluation paradigm, providing a rigorous foundation for advancing next-generation AI weather forecasting systems. The benchmark implementation is available at: https://github.com/lixruize-del/NWP-Benchmark.

</details>


### [279] [MedMamba: Multi-View State Space Models with Adaptive Graph Learning for Medical Time Series Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24961)
*Da Zhang,Bingyu Li,Zhiyuan Zhao,Hongyuan Zhang,Junyu Gao,Xuelong Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MedMamba is a novel end-to-end architecture for medical time series that uses multi-scale convolutional embeddings, a tri-branch differential state space encoder for nonstationarity handling, and a spatial graph Mamba module for latent channel correlation discovery, achieving SOTA performance with linear complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for medical time series analysis struggle with jointly modeling local-global dynamics, handling nonstationarities like baseline drift, and capturing latent channel interactions. These limitations hinder their effectiveness in continuous monitoring and clinical decision support.

Method: The MedMamba architecture has three main components: 1) Multi-scale convolutional embeddings to capture local morphology; 2) A tri-branch differential state space encoder that processes raw, temporal-difference, and frequency-domain views to mitigate nonstationarity and suppress drift; 3) A spatial graph Mamba module that learns a directed dependency structure regularized toward sparsity and acyclicity to uncover latent channel correlations.

Result: Extensive experiments on five real-world datasets demonstrate that MedMamba achieves state-of-the-art performance while maintaining linear computational complexity. Ablation studies validate the contribution of each component. The code is publicly available.

Conclusion: MedMamba is an effective end-to-end architecture that successfully addresses key limitations in medical time series analysis by jointly modeling local-global dynamics while handling nonstationarities and capturing latent channel interactions. It achieves state-of-the-art performance with linear computational complexity.

Abstract: Medical time series are central to healthcare, enabling continuous monitoring and supporting timely clinical decisions. Despite recent progress, existing methods struggle to jointly model local-global dynamics and handle nonstationarities like baseline drift, while often failing to capture latent channel interactions. To address these challenges, we propose MedMamba, an end-to-end architecture that integrates state space models with domain-specific inductive biases. Specifically, MedMamba first employs multi-scale convolutional embeddings to capture discriminative local morphology. Second, to mitigate nonstationarity, we introduce a tri-branch differential state space encoder that processes raw, temporal-difference, and frequency-domain views, fusing them to emphasize informative patterns while suppressing drift. Furthermore, to uncover latent channel correlations, we design a spatial graph Mamba module that learns a directed dependency structure regularized toward sparsity and acyclicity, which obviates the need for predefined graphs. Extensive experiments on five real-world datasets demonstrate that MedMamba achieves state-of-the-art performance while maintaining linear computational complexity, and ablation studies validate each component's contribution.Code is available at https://github.com/zhangda1018/MedMamba.

</details>


### [280] [OSDTW: Optimal Shared Depth and Task Weighting for Long-Tailed Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24969)
*Chang Chu,Qingyue Zhang,Shao-Lun Huang,Junxiong Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OSDTW is a principled task-decomposition framework for long-tailed recognition that partitions the problem into head and tail tasks using a shared encoder with task-specific decoders, with a three-stage training pipeline and bias-variance decomposition for hyperparameter selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current long-tailed recognition methods suffer from a persistent head-tail trade-off where improving tail performance degrades head accuracy and increases training instability. Existing approaches like re-weighting, decoupled training, and multi-expert methods rely heavily on heuristic design choices for representation sharing between head/tail classes and supervision weighting across class groups.

Method: Proposes OSDTW framework that partitions single-label recognition into head and tail tasks using a shared encoder with task-specific decoders. Uses factorized model to handle mutual exclusivity and statistical dependence between label groups. Develops three-stage training pipeline: 1) Independent task training to estimate optima and Fisher information matrix, 2) Weighted joint training to learn shared encoder, 3) Branch assembly to construct final decoupled model. Derives second-order expansion of generalization error under block-diagonal Fisher approximation.

Result: The framework decomposes KL divergence-based generalization error into sum of task-wise terms plus additive constant, enabling well-defined task-wise objectives. Bias-variance decomposition provides computable proxy to select shared depth and task weights, enabling efficient hyperparameter search. Experiments on standard long-tailed benchmarks demonstrate effectiveness over strong baselines.

Conclusion: OSDTW offers a principled approach to long-tailed recognition by providing theoretical grounding for representation sharing and supervision weighting decisions through task decomposition and bias-variance analysis, addressing key limitations of heuristic-based methods.

Abstract: Long-tailed recognition suffers from a persistent head--tail trade-off: improving tail performance often degrades head accuracy and can increase training instability. Despite strong empirical results from re-weighting, decoupled training, and multi-expert methods, key design choices about representation sharing between head and tail classes and supervision weighting across class groups remain largely heuristic. In this work, we propose OSDTW, a principled task-decomposition framework that partitions the original single-label recognition problem into a head task and a tail task, implemented with a shared encoder and task-specific decoders. To handle the mutual exclusivity and statistical dependence between the two label groups, we introduce a factorized model and show that the resulting Kullback--Leibler divergence-based generalization error can be written as the sum of task-wise terms up to an additive constant, yielding a well-defined task-wise objective. We further develop a three-stage training pipeline: independent task training to estimate task-wise optima and the Fisher information matrix, weighted joint training to learn a shared encoder, and branch assembly to construct the final decoupled model. Under a block-diagonal Fisher approximation, we derive a computable second-order expansion of the expected generalization error, decomposing it into encoder variance, encoder bias, and decoder variance. This bias--variance decomposition provides a computable proxy to select the shared depth and task weights, enabling efficient hyper-parameter search. Experiments on standard long-tailed benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach over strong baselines.

</details>


### [281] [TGFormer: Towards Temporal Graph Transformer with Auto-Correlation Mechanism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24971)
*Hongjiang Chen,Pengfei Jiao,Ming Du,Xuan Guo,Zhidong Zhao,Di Jin,Xiao Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TGFormer is a novel Transformer-based architecture for temporal graphs that introduces a trajectory framework and auto-correlation mechanism to better capture long-term dependencies and periodic patterns, achieving up to 9.35% precision improvement over state-of-the-art methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Temporal Graph Neural Networks (TGNNs) have limitations in capturing long-term dependencies and identifying periodic patterns in temporal graphs, which are crucial for modeling complex dynamics in real-world applications.

Method: TGFormer introduces a trajectory framework aligned with time series analysis principles, enabling systematic analysis of historical interactions. It develops an auto-correlation mechanism based on stochastic process theory to uncover periodic dependencies in node interactions, performing dependency discovery and representation aggregation at sub-interaction levels.

Result: Experimental results across six public benchmarks demonstrate TGFormer's effectiveness, achieving up to 9.35% precision improvement compared to state-of-the-art approaches, with superior efficiency and accuracy over conventional attention mechanisms.

Conclusion: TGFormer successfully addresses fundamental limitations of existing TGNNs by providing a novel Transformer architecture that better captures long-term dependencies and periodic patterns in temporal graphs through its trajectory framework and auto-correlation mechanism.

Abstract: The growing interest in Temporal Graph Neural Networks (TGNNs) stems from their ability to model complex dynamics and deliver superior performance. However, TGNNs encounter fundamental challenges in capturing long-term dependencies and identifying periodic patterns. To address these limitations, we propose TGFormer, a novel Transformer architecture specifically designed for temporal graphs. Our model redefines temporal graph learning by establishing a trajectory framework that aligns with time series analysis principles. This approach allows TGFormer to derive node representations through systematic analysis of historical interactions, enabling granular examination of node relationships across sequential timestamps. Building upon stochastic process theory, we develop an auto-correlation mechanism that systematically uncovers periodic dependencies in node interactions. This innovation empowers TGFormer to perform dependency discovery and representation aggregation at sub-interaction levels, demonstrating superior efficiency and accuracy compared to conventional attention mechanisms. Experimental validation across six public benchmarks confirms the effectiveness of our approach, with TGFormer at most achieving 9.35\% precision improvement compared to state-of-the-art approaches.

</details>


### [282] [Benchmarking non-conformity score functions in conformal prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24983)
*Sol Erika Boman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A comprehensive analysis of non-conformity score functions in conformal prediction, examining their properties, introducing modifications, and evaluating their effectiveness in producing optimal prediction set sizes, particularly for class-imbalanced scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While conformal prediction offers robust uncertainty quantification with guaranteed coverage, the performance heavily depends on choosing appropriate non-conformity score functions. However, there is a lack of systematic studies examining the properties and comparative effectiveness of different score functions available in the literature.

Method: The paper provides an overview of properties of non-conformity score functions, presents examples from existing literature, introduces original modifications to these functions, and develops a novel method to evaluate prediction set sizes of conformal predictors for comparative analysis.

Result: The study offers a systematic comparison between different non-conformity score functions, assessing their effectiveness in producing prediction sets, with particular focus on class-conditional conformal prediction in imbalanced classification settings.

Conclusion: Selection of appropriate non-conformity score functions is crucial for optimizing conformal prediction performance, and the paper provides valuable guidance and tools for practitioners to make informed choices, especially in challenging scenarios like imbalanced classification.

Abstract: Conformal prediction is a useful and versatile alternative to model calibration in machine learning classification. It replaces single-class prediction with prediction sets, guaranteeing that the \textit{a priori} probability of the prediction sets containing the true class is larger than or equal to a pre-specified rate. The size and usefulness of the prediction sets relies heavily on the choice of the non-conformity score function. The scientific literature contains many examples of non-conformity score functions but there is an absence of studies examining their properties and effectiveness. In this paper, we give an overview of properties of non-conformity score functions. We give examples of non-conformity score functions in the existing literature and introduce original modifications. We introduce an original method of evaluating the prediction set sizes of conformal predictors and use it to provide a comparison between non-conformity score functions. We also examine efficacy of different non-conformity score functions for class-conditional conformal prediction in a setting with imbalanced classes.

</details>


### [283] [Selective Test-Time Compute Scaling for Click-Through Rate Prediction via Uncertainty-Triggered Feature Path Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24989)
*Moyu Zhang,Yun Chen,Yujun Jin,Jinxin Hu,Yu Zhang,Xiaoyi Zeng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes UTTSI, a training-free framework for CTR prediction that scales inference depth based on per-instance uncertainty to improve predictions on sparse feature combinations, achieving significant offline and online gains with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CTR prediction models show a fundamental asymmetry: predictions are confident for well-represented feature combinations in training data but unreliable for sparse ones. Existing solutions in training (e.g., adaptive gating) use a fixed selection function that does not address per-instance uncertainty at deployment, leaving a gap in handling sparse feature combinations. The opportunity to scale test-time compute for improved CTR prediction is largely unexplored.

Method: UTTSI (Uncertainty-Triggered Test-Time Selective Inference) is a training-free, model-agnostic framework that determines inference depth based on per-instance uncertainty. It employs a dual-signal estimator (model logit confidence + data-level frequency prior) to differentiate epistemic uncertainty from aleatoric ambiguity. Adaptive feature filtering removes unreliable embeddings; uncertain instances undergo stochastic feature-path explorations, with predictions aggregated through consistency-weighted ensembling. Confident instances skip exploration to minimize overhead.

Result: Experiments across four datasets with three backbone architectures show consistent, statistically significant gains over all training-phase baselines. A seven-day online A/B test confirms a relative CTR gain of 5.3% (p < 0.01) with controlled latency; average overhead is approximately 2.8× the base model, while worst-case latency is unchanged.

Conclusion: The paper introduces UTTSI as a viable and effective training-free approach to allocate test-time computation for CTR prediction. The work demonstrates substantial gains in offline and online evaluations, suggesting selective test-time compute is a promising complement to existing training-phase advances.

Abstract: Scaling test-time compute has proven highly effective for language models, yet this opportunity remains largely unexplored for industrial Click-Through Rate (CTR) prediction. CTR models suffer from a fundamental asymmetry: feature combinations well-represented in training yield confident predictions, while sparsely observed ones produce unreliable outputs. Existing training-phase solutions such as adaptive gating learn a fixed selection function subject to the same sparsity, offering no per-instance recourse at deployment.We propose UTTSI (Uncertainty-Triggered Test-Time Selective Inference), a training-free model-agnostic framework that scales inference depth proportionally to per-instance uncertainty. A dual-signal estimator combining model logit confidence with a data-level frequency prior distinguishes epistemic uncertainty from aleatoric ambiguity. Every instance undergoes adaptive feature filtering to remove unreliable embeddings; uncertain instances additionally receive stochastic feature-path explorations whose predictions are aggregated via consistency-weighted ensembling. Confident instances bypass exploration entirely, keeping average overhead at approximately $2.8\times$ base model cost with worst-case latency unchanged.Experiments on four datasets with three backbone architectures demonstrate consistent, statistically significant gains over all training-phase baselines. A seven-day online A/B test further confirms a 5.3% relative CTR gain ($p < 0.01$), establishing selective test-time compute allocation as a practical complement to training-phase advances for CTR prediction.

</details>


### [284] [Mitigating Gradient Pathology in PINNs through Aligned Constraint](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25001)
*Yichen Luo,Peiyu Zhu,Dongxiao Hu,Jia Wang,Tailin Wu,Dapeng Lan,Yu Liu,Zhibo Pang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CAML method addresses PINN gradient pathology by reformulating constraints and adding delay factors, improving stability and efficiency in solving complex PDEs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PINNs often suffer from gradient pathology where gradients from PDE residuals and boundary constraints oppose each other, trapping models in local minima. Existing solutions like adaptive weighting or hard constraints fail to fundamentally resolve this ill-conditioning or are limited to simple geometries.

Method: The method involves: 1) systematic analysis of gradient pathology causes from loss landscapes and optimization dynamics perspectives; 2) reformulating zeroth-order terms into aligned constraints (Constraint-Aligned loss); 3) introducing a manifold lifting technique; 4) adding a delay factor to help optimizers skip high-curvature areas.

Result: Experiments show that CAML significantly enhances numerical stability and efficiency in highly complex PINN problems. The method effectively mitigates gradient conflicts in PINN training.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that the proposed CAML method effectively mitigates gradient conflicts in PINN training, addressing a fundamental pathology in current approaches. It significantly enhances numerical stability and efficiency for solving complex PDE problems.

Abstract: While Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) are powerful for solving Partial Differential Equations (PDEs), their training is often paralyzed by gradient pathology. The gradients from the PDE residuals and boundary constraints oppose each other, trapping the model in local minima. Current solutions, such as adaptive weighting or hard constraints, either fail to fundamentally resolve this ill-conditioning or are limited to simple geometries. In this study, we systematically analyze the possible causes of this gradient pathology from the perspectives of loss landscapes and optimization dynamics. Based on the obtained conclusion, we propose Constraint-Aligned loss with Manifold Lifting (CAML). By reformulating all zeroth-order terms into aligned constraints, our method effectively mitigates gradient conflicts. In addition, we introduce a delay factor to help the optimizer skip the high-curvature area. Experiments demonstrate that our CAML significantly enhances numerical stability and efficiency in highly complex PINN problems. Our code is open-sourced on https://github.com/YichenLuo-0/CAML.

</details>


### [285] [Metropolis-Scale Resilient and Trustworthy Traffic Flow Inference Using Multi-Source Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25004)
*Qishen Zhou,Yifan Zhang,Michail A. Makridis,Anastasios Kouvelas,Yibing Wang,Simon Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TA-ANP is a probabilistic framework for resilient global traffic state inference that fuses sparse sensor data using task-aware neural processes, achieving state-of-the-art performance with trustworthy uncertainty quantification and improved sensing efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate, trustworthy traffic state inference from sparse observations is challenging due to underdetermined nature, sensing network disturbances, and conflicts among multiple inference sub-tasks when modeled jointly.

Method: Task-Aware Attentive Neural Process combining neural processes with task-aware multi-query attention having spatiotemporal inductive biases, Monte Carlo Dropout for uncertainty, and trained on the Metropolitan Multi-Source Traffic Dataset (MMTD) integrating multiple data sources.

Result: State-of-the-art performance across all sub-tasks under deterministic and probabilistic metrics on MMTD; well-calibrated uncertainties enable more efficient fixed-sensor placement; superior resilience in disturbance absorption, performance recovery, and adaptability to unseen sensing configurations.

Conclusion: TA-ANP presents a resilient, trustworthy probabilistic framework for global traffic state inference that outperforms existing methods, offers well-calibrated uncertainty quantification, improves sensing network efficiency, and maintains robust performance throughout the sensing lifecycle.

Abstract: Inferring network-wide traffic states from sparse observations with high accuracy and trustworthy uncertainty quantification is essential for intelligent transportation systems, yet it remains challenging due to the underdetermined nature of the problem, multifaceted disturbances in sensing networks, and the inherent conflicts among multiple inference sub-tasks when modeled jointly. We propose the Task-Aware Attentive Neural Process (TA-ANP), a unified probabilistic framework for resilient and trustworthy global traffic state inference (GTSI) by fusing floating car data (FCD) with sparse fixed-detector measurements. By casting GTSI as a stochastic process, TA-ANP leverages the meta-learning properties of neural processes to adapt rapidly to changes in sensing configurations without retraining. A task-aware multi-query attention module with distinct spatiotemporal inductive biases is introduced to jointly handle three GTSI sub-tasks, while mitigating cross-task interference. For uncertainty quantification, we combine neural processes with Monte Carlo Dropout to capture both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty. To support metropolis-scale evaluation, we construct the Metropolitan Multi-Source Traffic Dataset (MMTD), integrating fixed-loop sensor measurements, FCD statistics, and OpenStreetMap road-network data over an urban network of 2,371 road segments. Experiments on MMTD show that TA-ANP achieves state-of-the-art performance across all sub-tasks under deterministic and probabilistic metrics. The resulting well-calibrated uncertainties enable more efficient fixed-sensor placement with fewer sensor deployments. Under a Damage-Repair-Addition sensing lifecycle, TA-ANP demonstrates superior resilience in terms of disturbance absorption, performance recovery, and adaptability to unseen sensing configurations.

</details>


### [286] [A perspective on fluid mechanical environments for challenges in reinforcement learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25011)
*Shruti Mishra,Michael Chang,Vamsi Spandan,Shmuel M. Rubinstein*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes using fluid mechanics simulations as a testbed for developing RL agents that can interact with high-dimensional, evolving environments, demonstrating initial navigation agents in stationary fluid environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to develop practical RL agents that can efficiently interact with open worlds where they only witness and affect small parts. Fluid mechanics problems, particularly nonlinear instabilities (like droplet breakup, fluid mixing, rogue waves), present compelling test cases because they involve high-dimensional, evolving environments with preserved representations across changing dynamics that agents can leverage.

Method: The authors propose two problem descriptions of agents interacting with fluid mechanical environments, specifying state/action spaces and reward functions. They identify nonstationary aspects and preserved invariances in these environments. They utilize open-source simulators Dedalus and JAX-CFD for environment generation and demonstrate initial RL agents that learn to navigate in stationary Dedalus-simulated environments.

Result: The paper demonstrates successful implementation of RL agents that learn to navigate in stationary fluid environments simulated using Dedalus. This establishes a foundation for future development of RL agents that can meaningfully interact with simulated fluid environments representing scientific challenges in natural and industrial flows.

Conclusion: Fluid mechanics simulations provide a valuable testbed for developing RL agents capable of interacting with complex, evolving environments. The initial demonstration with stationary environments sets the stage for future work on agents that can handle the full complexity of nonlinear instabilities in fluid dynamics, advancing both RL methodology and applications to scientific and industrial problems.

Abstract: We consider the challenge of developing agents that efficiently interact with high-dimensional, evolving environments, towards a view of practical reinforcement learning (RL) agents interacting with open worlds, of which they witness and affect only a small part. We argue that canonical fluid mechanics problems, and their simulations, present a compelling testbed for the development of such methods. These problems arise in nonlinear instabilities, where small disturbances can grow to transform the dynamics of a system. Nonlinear instabilities represent several open scientific challenges with industrial applications -- the droplet breakup of a liquid jet, mixing at an interface between two fluids, and the appearance of unusually tall rogue waves in the ocean. In these settings, agents may leverage preserved representations across the changing dynamics to learn efficiently.
  We present two problem descriptions of agents interacting with a fluid mechanical environment, and describe the state and action spaces, and reward functions, for these agents. For these examples, we specify the aspects of the environment which are nonstationary and the preserved invariances. We note Dedalus and JAX-CFD as open-source simulators that can be used for the development of reinforcement learning methods (Burns et al., 2016; Kochkov et al., 2021)) We demonstrate the use of Dedalus for environment generation by creating RL agents that learn to navigate in a stationary environment that is simulated using Dedalus. This sets the stage for future development of RL agents that learn to meaningfully interact with simulated environments that represent scientific challenges in natural and industrial flows.

</details>


### [287] [MimirRAG: A Multi-Agent RAG Framework for Financial Data Retrieval with Metadata Integration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25030)
*Magnus Samuelsen,Wilmer Nyström,Somnath Mazumdar,Mansoor Hussain,Mikkel Strange*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MimirRAG is a multi-agent RAG system for financial analysis that combines metadata integration, table-aware chunking, and agentic workflows to extract insights from mixed financial documents with 89.3% accuracy on FinanceBench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RAG systems need to reduce hallucinations and improve answer accuracy for reliable financial analysis, but current systems struggle with extracting meaningful insights from mixed financial documents and integrating into analyst workflows.

Method: MimirRAG uses a modular pipeline including: structure-preserving PDF parsing, table-aware chunking, metadata extraction, agent-based retrieval with query planning and hybrid search, validation, and context-aware generation with numerical reasoning support.

Result: The system achieved 89.3% accuracy on FinanceBench, outperforming original benchmark baselines. Key technical enablers identified: metadata integration, table-aware chunking, and agentic workflows.

Conclusion: Combining multi-agent RAG architecture with human-centric design principles (calibrated trust, comprehensive data integration, user personalization) improves extraction of meaningful insights in financial analysis.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems offer a promising approach to reduce hallucinations and improve answer accuracy in large language models (LLMs), a requirement for reliable, financial analysis where answers must be grounded in verifiable evidence from filings rather than generated from model priors. However, designing RAG systems that extract meaningful insights from mixed financial documents and integrate into analyst workflows remains challenging. This paper introduces MimirRAG (Metadata-Integrated Multi-Agent Information Retrieval), a multi-agent RAG system developed iteratively to address these challenges. MimirRAG features a modular pipeline encompassing structure-preserving parsing of PDF filings, table-aware chunking, metadata extraction, agent-based retrieval with query planning and hybrid search, validation, and context-aware generation with numerical reasoning support. Our ablation study identifies three key technical enablers for effective financial RAG: metadata integration, table-aware chunking, and an agentic workflow. MimirRAG was evaluated quantitatively using FinanceBench and qualitatively through expert validation with four financial analysts. The system achieved 89.3% accuracy on FinanceBench, outperforming the original benchmark baselines. Expert feedback highlighted that successful deployment also requires calibrated trust, comprehensive data integration, and user personalization. We conclude that combining multi-agent RAG architecture with human-centric design principles can improve the extraction of meaningful insights in financial analysis.

</details>


### [288] [Scale When Needed: Adaptive Neuron-level Mixed Precision Quantization Aware Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25054)
*Ayush K. Varshney,Konstantinos Vandikas,Šarūnas Girdzijauskas,Adam Orucu,Aneta Vulgarakis Feljan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neuron-Level Mixed-Precision QAT (NMP-QAT) enables each neuron to independently learn its optimal discrete precision during training, achieving superior compression-accuracy trade-offs for edge AI deployments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deploying deep neural networks on resource-constrained 6G edge devices requires aggressive compression with minimal accuracy loss. Existing mixed-precision quantization methods operate at coarse granularity (layer- or channel-level) using heuristic or search-based strategies, potentially overlooking fine-grained neuron-level variability.

Method: NMP-QAT allows each neuron to independently learn its own discrete precision during training using differentiable surrogates and straight-through estimators. Starting from low-bit precision, it expands bit-width only when training signals demand it while maintaining a fully discrete inference graph. The method adapts to both weights and activations to reduce memory movement.

Result: Evaluated on telecom and non-telecom datasets across MLP and tabular foundation model architectures, NMP-QAT achieves superior compression-accuracy trade-offs compared to mixed-precision QAT baselines.

Conclusion: NMP-QAT's fine-grained neuron-level precision adaptation makes it well-suited for Green AI deployments at the network edge, offering efficient compression while maintaining accuracy for resource-constrained 6G edge devices.

Abstract: Deploying deep neural networks on resource-constrained 6G edge devices demands aggressive compression with minimal accuracy loss. Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) has emerged as a leading compression approach; however, existing mixed-precision methods typically operate at coarse layer- or channel-level granularity. These methods often rely on heuristic or search-based bit-allocation strategies, which may overlook fine-grained variability at the neuron level. We propose Neuron-Level Mixed-Precision QAT (NMP-QAT), where each neuron independently learns its own discrete precision during training. Starting from low-bit precision, NMP-QAT expands bit-width only when training signals demand it, via differentiable surrogates and straight-through estimators, while preserving a fully discrete inference graph. This adaptability extends to both weights and activations, reducing memory movement. Evaluated on telecom and non-telecom datasets across MLP and tabular foundation model architectures, NMP-QAT achieves superior compression-accuracy trade-offs over mixed-precision QAT baselines, making it well-suited for Green AI deployments at the network edge.

</details>


### [289] [GL-LFGNN:A Global-Local Dual-branch Causal Graph Neural Network Based on Liang-Kleeman Information Flow for EEG Emotion Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25061)
*Ziyi Wang,Dongyang Kuang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GL-LFGNN is a novel global-local dual-branch causal graph neural network for EEG emotion recognition that replaces traditional symmetric adjacency matrices with neurophysiologically interpretable directed causal graphs based on Liang-Kleeman information flow theory, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with only 10% of the parameters of existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GNN approaches for EEG emotion recognition rely on symmetric adjacency matrices derived from spatial proximity or functional correlations, which capture statistical associations rather than directed causal influences. This conflicts with the inherently asymmetric, causally-driven nature of neural information flow in the brain.

Method: Proposes GL-LFGNN (Global-Local Dual-branch Causal Graph Neural Network) based on Liang-Kleeman information flow theory. Unlike Granger causality, this approach quantifies causal strength from a dynamical systems perspective, creating neurophysiologically interpretable directed graphs. The dual-branch architecture integrates whole-brain connectivity (global branch) with region-specific processing aligned to functional neuroanatomy (local branch).

Result: On the MEEG dataset, GL-LFGNN achieves 86.17% accuracy for Arousal and 86.71% accuracy for Valence with only 37K parameters. This represents approximately 10% of the parameters used by current state-of-the-art methods while maintaining or improving accuracy.

Conclusion: Principled causal modeling based on Liang-Kleeman information flow theory can simultaneously enhance interpretability, generalization, and computational efficiency in EEG-based emotion recognition, demonstrating that directed causal graphs better capture the asymmetric nature of neural information flow compared to traditional symmetric approaches.

Abstract: EEG-based emotion recognition holds significant promise for objective diagnosis of mood disorders. Graph neural networks (GNNs) have emerged as the dominant paradigm for modeling inter-channel dependencies in EEG, yet existing approaches rely on symmetric adjacency matrices derived from spatial proximity or functional correlations that fundamentally capture statistical associations rather than directed causal influences, which conflicts with the inherently asymmetric, causally-driven nature of neural information flow. To bridge this gap, we propose GL-LFGNN, a Global-Local Dual-branch Causal Graph Neural Network grounded in Liang-Kleeman information flow theory. Unlike Granger causality that merely assesses temporal precedence, our approach rigorously quantifies causal strength from a dynamical systems perspective, yielding neurophysiologically interpretable directed graphs. A dual-branch architecture further integrates whole-brain connectivity with region-specific processing aligned to established functional neuroanatomy. On the MEEG dataset, GL-LFGNN achieves 86.17% (Arousal) and 86.71% (Valence) accuracy with only 37K parameters -- approximately 10% of the current state-of-the-art -- demonstrating that principled causal modeling can simultaneously enhance interpretability, generalization, and computational efficiency. Code will be released.

</details>


### [290] [Reinforcement Learning for Laser Additive Manufacturing Scan-Order Optimisation: A Bilevel Proxy--FEA Diagnostic Framework for Reward and World-Model Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25063)
*Xian Wu,Haoran Li,Dongbin Zhao,Ruiyao Zhang,Yuanqi Chu,Bin Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Bilevel Proxy-FEA diagnostic framework for RL scan-order optimization reveals stress-distortion trade-off; current proxies align poorly with FEA references, highlighting critical need for sparse FEA guidance in reward design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the central challenge in applying reinforcement learning to laser additive manufacturing: the fidelity of reward functions and world models. Full finite-element analysis is computationally prohibitive for in-the-loop evaluation, while cheap proxy metrics may only capture partial aspects of true thermo-mechanical objectives, potentially leading to misaligned RL training.

Method: The paper proposes a bilevel Proxy-FEA diagnostic framework with two levels: a lower level using lightweight scan-path and thermo-inspired proxies for rapid candidate generation and preliminary policy screening, and an upper level employing sparse Abaqus FEA simulations to provide reference labels. This framework was tested on a simplified LDED32 stripe benchmark with ten representative scan strategies.

Result: Results revealed an observed stress-distortion trade-off rather than a single monotonic quality objective. The center_out strategy emerged as a robust compromise candidate, while raster_left_to_right and edge_in formed opposing endpoints of the trade-off. Proxy-FEA alignment analysis showed that current path-based metrics predominantly capture distortion-related behavior and exhibit weak correlation with sparse FEA reference labels.

Conclusion: The study concludes that proxy-only reward designs risk misalignment in RL training for scan-order optimization, underscoring the critical need for sparse FEA reference signals to guide reward and world-model refinement before large-scale policy optimization. The center_out strategy emerged as a robust compromise candidate balancing the identified stress-distortion trade-off.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning offers a promising approach for scan-order optimisation in laser additive manufacturing, where sequential scan decisions critically influence thermal accumulation, residual stress, distortion, and final part quality. A central challenge in applying RL to this domain lies in reward and world-model fidelity: full finite-element analysis is computationally prohibitive for dense in-the-loop evaluation, while cheap thermo-inspired proxy metrics, though efficient, may capture only partial aspects of the true thermo-mechanical objectives. This paper investigates a bilevel Proxy--FEA diagnostic framework for reward and world-model diagnosis in reinforcement-learning-guided scan-order optimisation. The lower level employs lightweight scan-path and thermo-inspired proxies for rapid candidate generation and preliminary policy-side screening, while the upper level utilises sparse Abaqus FEA simulations to provide simulation-based reference labels. The framework is examined on a simplified whole-track heating LDED32 stripe benchmark comprising ten representative scan strategies. Final-cooling residual Mises stress, U3 vertical distortion, and PEEQ plasticity metrics reveal an observed stress--distortion trade-off rather than a single monotonic quality objective. Within the evaluated set, the center_out strategy emerges as a robust compromise candidate, while raster_left_to_right and edge_in form opposing endpoints of the trade-off. Proxy--FEA alignment analysis shows that current cheap path-based metrics predominantly capture distortion-related (U3) behaviour and exhibit only weak correlation with the sparse FEA reference labels. These findings highlight that proxy-only reward designs risk misalignment in future RL training and underscore the value of sparse FEA reference signals for diagnostic-guided reward and world-model refinement prior to large-scale policy optimisation.

</details>


### [291] [Leveraging Gauge Freedom for Learning Non-Gradient Population Dynamics of Stochastic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25107)
*Jules Berman,Tobias Blickhan,Benjamin Peherstorfer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NGIF algorithm infers non-gradient population dynamics using weak formulation of continuity equation, improving distribution accuracy and capturing non-potential transport beyond gradient-restricted baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing population dynamics inference often focuses on gradient flows that minimize kinetic energy, but this restricts the types of vector fields that can be inferred. There's gauge freedom in selecting vector fields based on different criteria, and the authors want to leverage this to infer more general non-gradient population dynamics.

Method: Propose Non-Gradient Inference Flows (NGIF) algorithm using a weak formulation of the continuity equation. This allows parameterization of general vector fields and selection of criteria beyond minimal kinetic energy. The approach handles both low- and high-dimensional physics problems.

Result: Demonstrated on various physics problems that NGIF improves distributional accuracy over gradient-restricted baselines and better captures non-potential transport phenomena in population dynamics.

Conclusion: By moving beyond gradient flows and leveraging the gauge freedom in vector field selection, NGIF provides a more flexible and accurate framework for inferring population dynamics, particularly for capturing non-potential transport mechanisms.

Abstract: Existing work on population dynamics inference often focuses on flows arising from vector fields that are the gradients of scalar potentials. Among all admissible flows that are compatible with the population dynamics, gradient flows are optimal in a specific sense: they minimize kinetic energy. The selection of fields based on different criteria corresponds to a gauge freedom when determining population dynamics, which we leverage in this work. We propose Non-Gradient Inference Flows (NGIF), an algorithm to infer non-gradient population dynamics using a weak formulation of the continuity equation. This allows us to parameterize general vector fields and choose other selection criteria beyond minimal kinetic energy. We demonstrate on a variety of low- and high-dimensional physics problems that this more general approach improves distributional accuracy over gradient-restricted baselines and better captures non-potential transport.

</details>


### [292] [Revisiting Pre-Propagation GNNs: Robust Diffusion Operators and Hidden-State Re-Propagation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25111)
*Zichao Yue,Zhiru Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes enhanced PPGNNs with robust diffusion operators and re-propagation that match message-passing GNN accuracy while keeping efficiency benefits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PPGNNs offer computational efficiency benefits but suffer from expressivity limitations and performance gaps compared to message-passing GNNs, particularly on heterophilic graphs.

Method: Introduces robust graph diffusion operators for preprocessing and a few-shot hidden-state re-propagation scheme during training.

Result: The methods improve validation and test accuracy of PPGNNs, enabling them to match the accuracy of message-passing GNNs while maintaining training efficiency.

Conclusion: The proposed robust graph diffusion operators and few-shot hidden-state re-propagation scheme effectively bridge the performance gap between PPGNNs and message-passing GNNs, allowing PPGNNs to achieve comparable accuracy while maintaining their training efficiency advantages.

Abstract: Pre-propagation graph neural networks (PPGNNs) decouple node feature propagation from transformation: graph diffusion is performed once as preprocessing, and training reduces to dense per-node transformations. This design enables mini-batch training without inter-node dependencies, avoids repeated sparse matrix--matrix multiplications, and better matches modern accelerators optimized for dense compute. However, their expressivity remains unclear, and empirical results show a gap between PPGNNs and their message-passing counterparts on commonly used graph benchmarks, especially heterophilic ones. In this paper, we propose a suite of robust graph diffusion operators for preprocessing and a few-shot hidden-state re-propagation scheme during training. Our methods improve the validation and test accuracy of PPGNNs, enabling them to match the accuracy of message-passing GNNs while maintaining training efficiency.

</details>


### [293] [Courant: a State-Adaptive Perceiver-Based Neural Surrogate with Local Support and Interpretable Field Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25115)
*Anuj Kumar,Josiah Bjorgaard,Nikolaos Bouklas,Matteo Salvador,Alexander Lavin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Courant is a Perceiver-based encoder-processor-decoder model with adaptive specialization latents that behave like adaptive hp-refinement, achieving competitive accuracy on scientific ML benchmarks with interpretable, geometry-aware representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional numerical solvers and scientific machine learning need adaptive refinement capabilities similar to hp-refinement schemes, which allow for multi-resolution, geometry-aware representations that can track coherent structures in time-dependent simulations.

Method: Proposes Courant architecture: Perceiver-based encoder-processor-decoder with shared random Fourier feature coordinate embedding, state-adapted latent queries, and lightweight decoder. Trained end-to-end with steady/transient simulation data using standard L2 loss in physical space.

Result: Achieves competitive accuracy on benchmarks. The model's latents develop multiscale geometric specialization in simulation domains, track coherent structures in time-dependent cases (like evolving spatial basis functions), and enable compact, geometry-anchored decomposition similar to partition-of-unity methods.

Conclusion: Courant demonstrates that ML architectures can achieve adaptive hp-refinement-like functionality with interpretable latents that develop geometric specialization and track coherent structures, bridging traditional numerical solver capabilities with scientific ML approaches.

Abstract: We introduce "Courant", a Perceiver-based encoder-processor-decoder surrogate model that has latent features exhibiting adaptive specialization and local support in the physical space, enabling functionality akin to an adaptive hp-refinement scheme, an attribute that is highly desirable in traditional numerical solvers and scientific machine learning broadly. The proposed architecture combines a shared random Fourier feature coordinate embedding, state-adapted latent queries, and a light-weight decoder. Courant is trained end-to-end with steady or transient simulation data and only a standard L_2 prediction loss in the physical space, achieving competitive accuracy on benchmarks. We demonstrate that Courant's inductive biases yield latents that are interpretable by design: they develop multiscale geometric specialization in the simulation domain and track coherent structures in the time-dependent case, acting analogously to time-evolving spatial basis functions and allowing for decoding a compact, geometry-anchored, partition-of-unity-like decomposition of the simulated field.

</details>


### [294] [Inference-Time Alignment of Diffusion Models via Trust-Region Iterative Twisted Sequential Monte Carlo](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25123)
*Weixin Wang,Yu Yang,Wei Deng,Pan Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TRI-TSMC introduces a trust-region framework to learn better twisting functions for SMC-based alignment of diffusion models, overcoming stability issues of existing methods and improving sample efficiency with theoretical guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to improve inference-time alignment for diffusion-based generative models without updating model weights. While SMC-based methods exist, they suffer from limitations: they require large particle budgets, suffer from weight degeneracy, and produce high-variance estimates. Existing learnable twisting methods are unstable for diffusion-based alignment with high-dimensional state spaces and complex reward functions.

Method: The paper proposes TRI-TSMC, a trust-region framework for learning twisting functions in SMC-based inference-time alignment. Each iteration: 1) Computes an exact KL-constrained update in path space with closed-form solution via tempered importance reweighting, 2) Projects the target back to the parameterized twisted family using weighted maximum likelihood. This addresses the instability of existing twisting methods when applied to diffusion-based alignment.

Result: Theoretical results: 1) Formalized value-function interpretation of optimal twisting function yielding zero-variance sampler, 2) Proved trust-region update follows an escort path toward target distribution, 3) Showed weighted maximum-likelihood update is forward-KL projection, 4) Demonstrated path reduces residual importance-weight variance. Empirical results: TRI-TSMC improves primary alignment objectives on both discrete diffusion text generation and text-to-image generation under matched inference-time budgets.

Conclusion: This paper presents TRI-TSMC as an effective method for improving inference-time alignment in diffusion models. The proposed trust-region framework systematically reduces variance and improves sample efficiency compared to existing SMC-based methods, particularly when dealing with high-dimensional state spaces and complex reward functions.

Abstract: We study inference-time alignment for diffusion-based generative models, aiming to steer a base model toward high-reward outputs without updating its weights. Recent Sequential Monte Carlo (SMC)-based steering methods approximate reward-tilted target distributions in a principled way, but their proposals remain largely tied to the base sampler. Since reward information is mainly used after propagation through particle reweighting and resampling, these methods can require large particle budgets and suffer from weight degeneracy and high-variance estimates. One way to reduce variance and improve particle efficiency is to iteratively learn twisting functions that provide look-ahead guidance, as in twisted SMC. However, existing learnable twisting methods are developed mainly for classical sequential inference and can be unstable when applied to diffusion-based alignment with high-dimensional state spaces and terminal, noisy, or black-box rewards. We propose Trust-Region Iterative Twisted Sequential Monte Carlo (TRI-TSMC), a trust-region framework for learning twisting functions in SMC-based inference-time alignment. Each iteration computes an exact KL-constrained update in path space, which admits a closed-form solution by tempered importance reweighting, and projects this target back to the parameterized twisted family by weighted maximum likelihood. Theoretically, we formalize the value-function interpretation of the optimal twisting function and show that it yields a zero-variance sampler. We prove that the trust-region update follows an escort path toward the target distribution, that the weighted maximum-likelihood update is a forward-KL projection, and that the path reduces residual importance-weight variance. Empirically, TRI-TSMC improves primary alignment objectives on discrete diffusion text generation and text-to-image generation under matched inference-time budgets.

</details>


### [295] [Optimizing Multidimensional Scaling in Gini Metric Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25124)
*Cassandra Mussard,Stéphane Mussard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The Gini Multidimensional Scaling framework extends Euclidean MDS with a Gini pseudo-distance based on values and ranks, offering robustness to noise/outliers, GPU-accelerated implementation, and superior performance on noisy data compared to standard MDS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Euclidean multidimensional scaling lacks robustness to noise and outliers, which limits its effectiveness in real-world applications with noisy data.

Method: Introduces a Gini pseudo-distance based on both values and their ranks with a tunable hyperparameter, extending Euclidean MDS to create the Gini MDS framework for flexible latent configuration exploration.

Result: Gini MDS outperforms Euclidean MDS on 16 UCI datasets with outliers and MNIST images with noise, showing robustness to noise/outliers. The PyTorch implementation provides GPU acceleration and efficient computation compared to sklearn's MDS.

Conclusion: Gini MDS offers a robust, flexible alternative to Euclidean MDS for noisy real-world data, with computational advantages through GPU acceleration.

Abstract: The Gini Multidimensional Scaling (Gini MDS) framework extends the Euclidean multidimensional scaling. We introduce a Gini pseudo-distance based on values and their ranks that depends on a fine-tunable hyperparameter. This pseudo-distance allows flexible exploration of latent configurations, enabling embeddings that best match observed dissimilarities. The Gini MDS is shown to be robust to noise and outliers, making it well-suited for real-world applications. We provide experiments on 16 UCI datasets with outliers and on MNIST images with noise to show that the Gini MDS outperforms the Euclidean MDS on noisy data. Finally, a tensor-based implementation in \texttt{PyTorch} provides GPU acceleration and efficient computation compared to the standard MDS of the \texttt{sklearn} library.

</details>


### [296] [Blocked Gibbs meets Diffusion Transformers: Unsupervised Learning for Constraint Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25129)
*Yudong W. Xu,Wenhao Li,Xiaoyu Wang,Scott Sanner,Elias B. Khalil*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BloGDiT introduces a Blocked Gibbs Diffusion Transformer that outperforms existing methods on constraint optimization problems by using blocked Gaussian denoising instead of standard joint denoising, enabling targeted variable edits for better feasibility/optimality attainment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current diffusion models for constraint optimization are limited to binary variables and rely on graph neural networks, restricting their application to problems with general discrete variables or global constraint structures. Additionally, there's a fundamental mismatch between standard diffusion (small incremental denoising) and constraint solving (requiring substantial variable subset alterations).

Method: BloGDiT replaces standard joint Gaussian denoising with blocked Gaussian denoising, using iterative block resampling and annealing the block size over time to facilitate large, targeted edits within variable blocks, addressing the diffusion-constraint mismatch.

Result: Across four benchmark problems (Sudoku, Graph Coloring, Maximum Independent Set, and MaxCut), BloGDiT matches or outperforms existing methods, demonstrating the effectiveness of blocked Gibbs-style diffusion as an inductive bias for Transformer-based constraint solving.

Conclusion: Blocked Gibbs diffusion provides a highly effective inductive bias for Transformer-based constraint satisfaction and optimization, overcoming limitations of previous diffusion-based approaches and enabling better handling of general discrete variable problems requiring global reasoning.

Abstract: Diffusion models have shown promise in learning to solve constraint optimization problems. However, they are mostly restricted to problems with binary variables and rely on graph neural networks, hindering their application to a broader range of problems such as those with general discrete variables or constraint structures that necessitate global rather than local reasoning. We investigate the use of Diffusion Transformers to address the aforementioned limitations. A naive implementation performs poorly due to a fundamental mismatch between the standard diffusion process and constraint solving: while the former applies small, incremental denoising across all variables, the latter requires substantially altering specific subsets of variables to attain feasibility or optimality. Our method, Blocked Gibbs Diffusion Transformer (BloGDiT), is the first to address this limitation by replacing standard joint Gaussian denoising with blocked Gaussian denoising. BloGDiT uses iterative block resampling and anneals the block size over time to facilitate large, targeted edits within a block of variables. Across Sudoku, Graph Coloring, Maximum Independent Set, and MaxCut, BloGDiT matches or outperforms existing methods, demonstrating that blocked Gibbs-style diffusion provides a highly effective inductive bias for Transformer-based constraint satisfaction and optimization.

</details>


### [297] [Theoretical Analysis of Sparse Optimization with Reparameterization, Weight Decay, and Adaptive Learning Rate](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25134)
*Huangyu Xu,Jingqin Yang,Qianqian Xu,Jiaye Teng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel sparse optimization method called ReWA (Reparameterization, Weight decay, Adaptive learning rate) is introduced to overcome instability issues in ℓₚ regularization for 0<p<1, achieving better sparsity than ℓ₁ regularization while maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse optimization is important for practical applications, and ℓₚ regularization is a popular approach. However, when 0<p<1, ℓₚ regularization suffers from optimization instability due to unbounded gradients, which limits its effectiveness.

Method: The proposed ReWA method combines three components: 1) Reparameterization, 2) Weight decay, and 3) Adaptive learning rate. This approach is connected to ℓₚ-regularization but reveals a different optimization landscape that helps mitigate the instability issues.

Result: Experiments on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet datasets using ResNets show that ReWA leads to significant sparsity improvements over traditional ℓ₁-regularization approaches while preserving test accuracy.

Conclusion: ReWA provides an effective alternative to ℓₚ regularization for sparse optimization, overcoming the gradient instability problem for 0<p<1 and achieving better sparsity than ℓ₁ regularization without sacrificing accuracy.

Abstract: Sparse optimization is a fundamental challenge in various practical applications. A popular approach to sparse optimization is $\ell_p$ regularization. However, it may encounter optimization instability due to the unbounded gradients when $0<p<1$. In this paper, we introduce a novel approach to sparse optimization termed ReWA, based on Reparameterization, Weight decay, and Adaptive learning rate. ReWA is closely connected to $\ell_p$-regularization, yet it unveils a distinct optimization landscape that helps mitigate instability issues. Experiments on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet with ResNets demonstrate that ReWA leads to significant sparsity improvements over the $\ell_1$-regularization approach while preserving test accuracy.

</details>


### [298] [ASTRO: Adaptive Spatio-Temporal Reinforcement Optimization for GNN Powered Anomly Detection in Cyber Physical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25135)
*Rai Ali Yar,Umaisa Lail,Anwar Shah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ASTRO introduces a reinforcement learning-based anomaly detection framework for IIoT systems that dynamically optimizes detection thresholds using DQN, GNNs, and attention mechanisms, achieving state-of-the-art performance on industrial benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The increasing complexity of sensor networks and interconnected control loops in Industrial IoT makes it difficult to identify anomalous behavior in high-dimensional, time-dependent signals. Anomaly detection is essential to protect Industrial Control Systems and Cyber-Physical Systems from false data injection and other malicious attacks.

Method: ASTRO integrates Deep Q-Network (DQN) with Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), temporal modeling, and Multi-Head Attention mechanisms. GNNs model spatial relations among sensors, temporal models capture time series dependencies, and attention layers highlight informative time steps. The model generates continuous anomaly scores transformed into binary decisions using an adaptive threshold optimized via DQN.

Result: ASTRO achieves exceptional performance with F1 score of 0.990 on SWaT dataset and 0.788 on complex WADI dataset (127 end devices), outperforming state-of-the-art baselines by nearly 14%. Results show consistent generalization and stability across multiple runs.

Conclusion: ASTRO is a highly practical and scalable framework for strengthening large-scale cyber-physical infrastructures, demonstrating that reinforcement learning-based dynamic threshold optimization can effectively address anomaly detection challenges in complex industrial environments.

Abstract: Anomaly detection in Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) environments is essential to protect the Industrial Control Systems (ICS) and Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) from occuring run time false data injection and other malicious attacks. The increasing complexity of sensor networks and interconnected control loops makes it difficult to identify anomalous behavior hidden within high-dimensional and time-dependent signals. To address these challenges, this article introduces Adaptive Spatio-Temporal Reinforcement Optimization ASTRO (ASTRO), a novel anomaly detection framework that pioneers the use of reinforcement learning for dynamic threshold optimization. By integrating a Deep Q-Network (DQN) with Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), temporal modelling and a Multi-Head Attention mechanism, ASTRO continuously adapts its decision boundaries to improve detection accuracy. The GNN component models the spatial relations among sensors, Temporal model captures time series dependencies and the attention layer highlights most informative time steps. The model generates continuous anomaly scores, which are transformed into binary decisions using an adaptive threshold, optimized via a Deep Q-Network (DQN). The ASTRO approach is evaluated on two real world industrial benchmarks: the Secure Water Treatment (SWaT) and Water Distribution (WADI) datasets. The proposed model achieves an exceptional performance on the SWaT with F1 score of 0.990. Moreover, on highly complex 127 end devices WADI dataset, it secures F1 score of 0.788, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines by nearly 14%. Results across multiple runs confirm consistent generalization and stability. These experiments demonstrate that the ASTRO framework is highly practical and scalable method for strengthening the large scale cyber physical infrastructures
