</details>


### [299] [Abduction-Deduction Entanglement: Domain Generalization via Representation Transplants](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25156)
*Kasra Jalaldoust,Elias Bareinboum*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel method for domain generalization that leverages causal assumptions and abduction-deduction factorization to find invariant prediction models across domains through representation transplants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prediction models trained on source distributions fail to generalize to different target distributions. The key challenge is that structural invariances between source and target data are non-identifiable from source data alone, limiting robust domain generalization.

Method: Proposes factorization of optimal prediction into abduction and deduction maps. Uses representation transplants - linear transformations in representation space that manipulate abduction content while retaining deduction components. Implements a learner-adversary game that searches plausible target distributions via parametric transplants to find invariant prediction models.

Result: The method achieves competitive performance on domain generalization benchmarks. Under ideal optimization, the learner-adversary game provably converges to minimax-optimal target prediction by leveraging causal invariances.

Conclusion: By exploiting the abduction-deduction entanglement and causal invariances, the proposed representation transplant framework provides a principled approach to domain generalization that can identify optimal predictions across domains despite non-identifiability challenges.

Abstract: Prediction models trained under the source distribution do not generalize well to a different target distribution. A valid inference about an unseen data distribution must be anchored by the invariance of certain causal mechanisms that generate the source and target data, however, these structural invariances are non-identifiable from the source data alone. Under mild causal assumptions about the data, we show that the optimal prediction in the target is in fact partially identifiable by the source distribution. The result rests on a simple observation: In any domain, the optimal prediction can be factorized into what we call a pair of abduction and deduction maps, where the abduction map makes inference about some unobserved variables (possibly confounders) from the observed variables and the deduction map predicts the label using both the observed and inferred quantities. Access to large source data pins down the optimal prediction, thus constrains the valid abduction-deduction ensembles that produce it -- a non-identifiability that we call the abduction-deduction entanglement. To leverage this, we parameterize the constrained family using what we call a representation transplant, that is a specific linear transformation in the representation space that manipulates the abduction content of the representation while retaining the deduction component. Invariance of the causal mechanism generating the label implies existence of an invariant deduction map between source and target. Thus, we can search the space of plausible target distributions via a parametric transplant. We use this scheme in a learner-adversary game that, under an idealistic optimization, provably terminates with the learner having the minimax-optimal target prediction. Evaluations verify the theory, showing that the method is competitive in DG benchmarks.

</details>


### [300] [AME-TS: Anchored Mixture-of-Experts for Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25166)
*Rui Wang,Renhao Xue,Ray Razi,Huan Song,Hannah R. Marlowe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AME-TS is a structure-guided sparse time series foundation model that uses interpretable temporal descriptors to guide MoE expert routing, achieving strong accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs and more stable expert specialization than standard MoE.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Transformer-based time series forecasting models process all series through shared dense computation despite heterogeneity in temporal structure, while standard MoE routing has weak expert specialization identification and instability during downstream adaptation.

Method: Proposes AME-TS with: 1) lightweight regime predictor to estimate series-level descriptors (forecastability, seasonality, trend, sparsity), 2) maps descriptors to soft structural prior over experts, 3) uses series-level prior to guide token-level routing during training for structure-aligned specialization.

Result: On GIFT-Eval benchmark: strong accuracy-efficiency tradeoff across scales, outperforms existing models at small scales, competitive with strongest models at larger scales while activating fewer parameters. On M5 fine-tuning: learns more interpretable routing geometry and substantially more stable expert specialization than standard MoE.

Conclusion: Structure-aware routing is an effective and reliable way to realize the benefits of sparse expert models for time series forecasting, with improved interpretability, stability, and efficiency.

Abstract: Time series forecasting models are increasingly scaled through large Transformer backbones, yet most existing approaches process all series through a shared dense computation path despite substantial heterogeneity in temporal structure. Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) offers a natural alternative by enabling conditional computation, but standard MoE routing leaves expert specialization weakly identified and often unstable during downstream adaptation. We propose AME-TS, a structure-guided sparse time series foundation model that aligns expert routing with interpretable temporal structure. AME-TS first uses a lightweight regime predictor to estimate series-level descriptors, including forecastability, seasonality, trend, and sparsity, and maps them to a soft structural prior over experts. This series-level prior guides token-level routing during training, encouraging structure-aligned specialization. On the GIFT-Eval benchmark, AME-TS delivers a strong accuracy-efficiency tradeoff across model scales: it substantially outperforms existing time series foundation models at small model scales and remains competitive with the strongest models at larger scales, while activating substantially fewer parameters through sparse routing. We further show that AME-TS learns more interpretable routing geometry and substantially more stable expert specialization than standard MoE during fine-tuning on the M5 dataset. These results suggest that structure-aware routing is an effective and reliable way to realize the benefits of sparse expert models for time series forecasting.

</details>


### [301] [Learning Treatment Effects during Resource Allocation via Priority-Queue Randomization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25169)
*JungHo Lee,Johnna Sundberg,Pim Welle,Bryan Wilder*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework for experimental design that randomizes applicants into priority queues based on risk scores, enabling treatment effect estimation while prioritizing high-need individuals in resource-constrained public service programs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Public service programs face challenges in resource allocation under uncertainty about benefits. While randomization is needed for credible evaluation, practical implementations often use waitlists with tiered priority queues that prioritize high-need individuals, making direct randomization difficult.

Method: Develops an experimental design framework where incoming applicants are randomized into priority queues based on assessed risk scores. Treatments are provided across queues in priority order (higher-risk first) and first-in-first-out within queue as budget becomes available.

Result: 1) Characterizes causal effects identified under priority-queue allocation: with exogenous arrivals, treatments are conditionally randomized; with endogenous arrivals, queue randomization provides an instrument for treatment. 2) Develops optimized queue-assignment designs trading statistical efficiency against prioritizing higher-need applicants, showing iid efficiency bounds remain justified despite treatment assignment dependence.

Conclusion: The framework enables experimental evaluation in practical settings where direct randomization is infeasible, allowing both causal inference and ethical prioritization of high-need individuals in resource-constrained public services.

Abstract: Public service programs often allocate limited resources under uncertainty about their benefits, creating a need for randomization to support credible evaluation. In practice, however, applicants commonly enter waitlists where resources are prioritized toward individuals judged to have higher need through tiered priority queues, making direct randomization difficult. Motivated by this, we develop an experimental design framework for learning treatment effects while treating those most in need where incoming applicants are randomized into priority queues based on their assessed risk scores. Treatments are then provided across queues in priority order and first-in-first-out within queue as budget becomes available. Our contributions are two-fold. First, we characterize what causal effects are identified under this priority-queue allocation. When arrivals are exogenous, treatments are conditionally randomized, and hence standard estimands are identified; when arrivals are endogenous, queue randomization instead provides an instrument for treatment, identifying local treatment effects induced by the queuing process. Second, we develop optimized queue-assignment designs that trade off statistical efficiency against prioritizing higher-need applicants. We show in the process that, despite dependence in treatment assignments induced by the design, usual iid efficiency bounds remain well-justified design objectives. We illustrate the proposed designs using data from a housing allocation program in a large U.S. county.

</details>


### [302] [Grow-Prune-Freeze Networks: Adaptive & Continual Learning Technique for Olfactory Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25170)
*Kordel K. France,Ovidiu Daescu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Grow-Prune-Freeze (GPF) networks for continual learning in olfactory navigation, showing they achieve 94% success on turbulent plume tasks and may generalize to other ML domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training data for olfaction is scattered and non-standardized, limiting representative world models. Olfactory navigation is dynamic and non-stationary, benefiting from real-time continual learning.

Method: Introduce GPF networks that enable continual learning by growing, pruning, and freezing early layers of policies in response to world complexity. Ground in non-linear random matrix theory, extending Pennington & Worth (2017) to n-layer continual-learning models.

Result: GPFs based on Expected SARSA achieve 94% success rate on turbulent plume navigation. Eigenvalue composition of network weights is preserved as successive layers are added. Evidence suggests GPFs may generalize to Atari RL, image classification, and language models.

Conclusion: GPF networks provide an effective adaptive framework for continual learning in non-stationary tasks like olfactory navigation, with potential for broader ML applications. Code and data are open-sourced to advance olfactory robotics research.

Abstract: Training data for olfaction is scattered through disparate, non-standardized datasets that limit the ability to build representative world models. Olfactory navigation is a highly dynamic and non-stationary task that benefits from real-time continual learning. We introduce an adaptive framework called Grow-Prune-Freeze (GPF) networks that enable an agent to continually learn through growing, pruning, and freezing early layers of its policy in response to world complexity. Grounding GPFs in non-linear random matrix theory, we show that the work of Pennington & Worth (2017) can be extended from single hidden layers to n-layer continual-learning models, and that eigenvalue composition of network weights is preserved as successive layers are added. We show that GPFs based on Expected SARSA achieve a 94% success rate on turbulent plume navigation - a partially observable, non-stationary task representative of the "big world" challenges that motivate adaptive learning in robotics - and provide supporting methodology for applying GPFs in other world models. Further experiments amount evidence that GPFs may generalize well to other machine learning tasks such as reinforcement learning in Atari, image classification, and autoregressive language models. We open source all code and data to encourage improvements on and more research in olfactory robotics.

</details>


### [303] [Directional Alignment Mitigates Reward Hacking in Reinforcement Learning for Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25189)
*Wenlong Deng,Jiaji Huang,Kaan Ozkara,Yushu Li,Christos Thrampoulidis,Xiaoxiao Li,Youngsuk Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reward hacking in language models occurs when models exploit shortcuts rather than solving tasks; this paper introduces trusted-direction projection to constrain gradient updates within a clean reference subspace, delaying shortcut exploitation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reward hacking is a failure mode where language models improve proxy rewards by exploiting shortcuts instead of solving the intended task, undermining the reliability of reinforcement learning in language models.

Method: The paper analyzes reward hacking through the geometry of reinforcement learning updates, examining dominant singular directions of parameter updates, and introduces trusted-direction projection which constrains gradients to remain within a clean reference subspace.

Result: Reward-hacking runs exhibit substantially larger directional change in parameter updates than clean runs; the proposed trusted-direction projection approach successfully delays shortcut exploitation and better preserves task performance across reward-hacking experiments on mathematical reasoning.

Conclusion: Reward hacking emerges from optimization drift away from stable low-dimensional learning trajectories, and constraining gradient updates to trusted directions within clean reference subspaces can mitigate this problem and improve the reliability of reinforcement learning in language models.

Abstract: Reward hacking arises when a model improves a proxy reward by exploiting shortcuts rather than solving the intended task. We study this failure mode through the geometry of reinforcement learning updates in language models and argue that hacking emerges when optimization drifts away from a stable low-dimensional learning trajectory. We analyze this drift through dominant singular directions of parameter updates and show that reward-hacking runs exhibit substantially larger directional change than clean runs. Motivated by this observation, we introduce trusted-direction projection, which constrains gradients to remain within a clean reference subspace. Across reward-hacking experiments on mathematical reasoning, the proposed approach delays shortcut exploitation and better preserves task performance.

</details>


### [304] [Localization then Neutralization: Gradient-guided Token Suppression against Visual Prompt Injection Attack](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25194)
*Dongpeng Zhang,Ke Ma,Yangbangyan Jiang,Gaozheng Pei,Longtao Huang,Qianqian Xu,Qingming Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GTM is a gradient-based defense that identifies and masks critical adversarial tokens in multimodal LLMs using hidden-state gradient analysis, achieving near-zero attack success with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current defenses against adversarial prompt injection attacks in multimodal LLMs lack principled understanding of attack mechanisms and struggle to balance efficiency with defense effectiveness.

Method: Gradient Token Masking (GTM) localizes critical adversarial tokens using Hidden-State Gradient Norm scores, which provide consistent ranking with full adversarial loss gradients, then neutralizes attacks by zeroing out high-scoring tokens.

Result: GTM reduces attack success rates to near zero for both prompt injection and multimodal jailbreak attacks while preserving model utility with negligible computational overhead (single forward-backward pass).

Conclusion: Successful adversarial attacks rely on a small subset of critical image tokens, and GTM provides an efficient, theoretically-grounded defense that accurately localizes and neutralizes these tokens without compromising model performance.

Abstract: Adversarial images pose a severe security threat to multimodal large language models through prompt injection. Existing defenses largely lack a principled understanding of the underlying mechanisms and struggle to balance efficiency and defense utility. In this work, we show that successful adversarial attacks do not rely on the entire image uniformly but instead depend on a small subset of critical image tokens. Based on this insight, we propose Gradient Token Masking (GTM), which localizes these tokens via gradient analysis and neutralizes them through masking. We find that attribution based on the first generated token's output probability fails when attacks preserve the predicted token. To overcome this, GTM utilizes the Hidden-State Gradient Norm score for generation-influence attribution under adversarial inputs. We prove that its ranking is consistent with that of the full adversarial loss gradient, providing a theoretical guarantee for accurate localization. Our method requires only a single forward-backward pass to identify and zero out a small number of high-scoring tokens, effectively disrupting the adversarial attack path. Extensive experiments on prompt injection and multimodal jailbreak attacks demonstrate that our approach reduces attack success rates (ASR) to near zero while preserving model utility with negligible computational overhead.

</details>


### [305] [Hide to Guide: Learning via Semantic Masking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25198)
*Ruitao Liu,Qinghao Hu,Alex Hu,Yecheng Wu,Shang Yang,Luke J. Huang,Zhuoyang Zhang,Han Cai,Song Han*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SMEPO is a semantic masking strategy for expert-guided RLVR that masks reward-relevant content in expert traces to prevent reward hacking while preserving problem-solving structure, improving accuracy and reducing training time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing expert-guided RLVR methods risk reward hacking where models copy reward-relevant content (like final answers or intermediate values) from expert traces rather than learning genuine reasoning. Current approaches control information exposure heuristically rather than strategically hiding specific harmful parts.

Method: SMEPO uses fine-grained semantic masking that selectively masks reward-relevant semantic spans along the critical path (final answers, intermediate values, executable code, answer-related entities) while preserving the expert's decomposition, plan, and procedural structure. This transforms problems into "fill-in-the-blank" exercises where models must reconstruct missing content themselves.

Result: SMEPO improves accuracy by up to 3.2 points over GRPO and reduces training time by up to 4.2x across diverse domains including math, code, and agentic search. It's simple to apply without changes to reward function or RL objective.

Conclusion: Semantic masking of reward-relevant content in expert traces effectively prevents reward hacking while maintaining the benefits of expert guidance, making RLVR more effective and efficient for reasoning-intensive tasks.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a powerful paradigm for improving language models on reasoning-intensive tasks, but its effectiveness is often limited by exploration. For example, models often fail on hard problems, leaving little useful reward signal. External expert traces offer a natural source of guidance, yet they may also expose reward-relevant content along the critical path to the verifier target, such as final answers, intermediate values, executable implementations, or answer-related entities. This content can create an unintended reward hacking channel, allowing the policy to obtain reward by copying the trace rather than learning the underlying reasoning or agentic behavior. Existing guided-RL methods reduce this risk by using partial trajectories, but they mainly control how much expert information is shown heuristically rather than which parts should be hidden. To this end, we propose Semantic Masked Expert Policy Optimization (SMEPO), a fine-grained semantic masking strategy for expert-guided RLVR. Instead of truncating traces coarsely or revealing them unchanged, SMEPO masks reward-relevant semantic spans along the critical path while preserving the expert's decomposition, plan, and procedural structure. This turns hard problems from reasoning from scratch into a fill-in-the-blank process: the policy can follow the expert's problem-solving route, but must still reconstruct the missing values, code, or entities by itself. SMEPO is simple to apply and requires no changes to the reward function or RL objective. Across diverse domains, including math, code, and agentic search, SMEPO improves accuracy by up to 3.2 points over GRPO and reduces training time by up to 4.2x. The code is available at https://github.com/mit-han-lab/SMEPO.

</details>


### [306] [Influence-Inspired Spectral Rotations for Extreme Low-Bit LLM Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25203)
*Gorgi Pavlov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BBT-spectral improves extreme low-bit (W2A16) LLM quantization by using Walsh-Hadamard Transform to rotate weight matrices and rescale columns based on Walsh-basis activation energy, biasing quantization toward high-spectral-energy channels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve extreme low-bit weight-only LLM quantization (W2A16) by addressing the challenge of maintaining model performance with severe bit constraints. The paper aims to leverage Walsh geometry from a companion theory paper to bias quantization toward more influential channels.

Method: 1) Apply WHT rotation to each linear layer's weight matrix; 2) Rescale columns by per-coordinate Walsh-basis activation energy; 3) Use Intel auto-round as reconstruction-error quantizer; 4) Extensions include per-head PCA matrix-Gamma replacement for attention layers, SO(2) rotation for RoPE compatibility, and MoE-aware fixes.

Result: Reduces wikitext-2 perplexity by 15-58% relative to vanilla auto-round at W2A16 across four decoder-only models (135M to 1.5B params). Extensions successfully applied to problematic architectures (Qwen3, Qwen2.5). W4 ablation shows minimal gains, confirming theoretical intuition about cost of unconcentrated influence vanishing at higher bit rates.

Conclusion: The BBT-spectral method provides practical engineering value for extreme low-bit LLM quantization, though it doesn't formally transfer the theoretical majorization argument. The approach effectively biases quantization toward high-spectral-energy channels, yielding significant perplexity improvements at W2 while being hardware-portable across Intel platforms.

Abstract: We apply the influence-adaptive Walsh geometry of a companion theory paper (arXiv:2605.01637) to extreme low-bit weight-only LLM quantization. The recipe is one math-invariant transformation: WHT-rotate each linear layer's weight matrix and rescale its columns by per-coordinate Walsh-basis activation energy before handing off to a reconstruction-error quantizer (Intel auto-round). This biases per-group integer rounding toward high-spectral-energy channels.
  On four pretrained decoder-only models from 135M to 1.5B parameters, BBT-spectral reduces wikitext-2 perplexity by 15-58% relative to vanilla auto-round at W2A16; we also report a TinyLlama-1.1B auxiliary data point. Three extensions transfer the recipe to families it failed on: a per-head PCA matrix-Gamma replacement of q_norm/k_norm for Qwen3 attention (PPL 136.76 -> 88.99 on Qwen3-0.6B); an SO(2) per-pair rotation that commutes with RoPE (PPL 36.93 -> 21.84 on Qwen2.5-1.5B); and an MoE-aware input-side absorption fix identified by architectural fuzzing of Laguna-style fused-expert layouts.
  A W2-vs-W4 ablation gives a deliberate negative control: the redistribution payoff falls within the +/-0.5 PPL noise floor at W4, consistent with the Schur-convexity intuition that the cost of unconcentrated influence vanishes as the noise budget shrinks. All quantized weights export to OpenVINO IR and run on Intel NPU + Arc dGPU + CPU with PPL invariant to device within +/-0.1.
  We do not claim a formal Boolean-to-real-valued transfer of the theory paper's majorization argument: the WHT activation energy used here is not the Boolean influence of the theory paper, the link is intuitive, and the contribution is engineering value rather than a transferred theorem. Head-to-head benchmarks against SpinQuant, QuaRot, QuIP-sharp, AQLM, OmniQuant, and ButterflyQuant at matched calibration are the main future-work item.

</details>


### [307] [Multi-Objective Learning for Diffusion Models: A Statistical Theory under Semi-Supervised Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25210)
*Ziheng Cheng,Yixiao Huang,Hanlin Zhu,Haoran Geng,Somayeh Sojoudi,Jitendra Malik,Pieter Abbeel,Xin Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a two-stage semi-supervised learning framework for diffusion models in multi-objective settings that first trains lightweight specialist models from limited paired data, then distills them into a generalist model using abundant unlabeled data and pseudo-samples, reducing sample complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models face challenges in multi-objective learning scenarios where multiple target distributions exist (e.g., different prompt domains in text-to-image, multiple environments in robotics). Achieving good Pareto trade-offs typically requires large generalist models with high capacity, increasing statistical costs due to sample complexity scaling with model complexity.

Method: Two-stage semi-supervised training: 1) Train lightweight specialist models from limited paired (labeled) data for each task, 2) Distill specialists into a generalist model using abundant unlabeled condition data by generating pseudo-samples. Extends to diffusion policies for sequential decision making with distribution shift considerations.

Result: Theoretical generalization bounds show that required paired samples only depend on specialist model complexity rather than generalist complexity. Extensive experiments on robotic control and image restoration tasks verify theoretical results.

Conclusion: The proposed framework enables efficient multi-objective learning for diffusion models with limited paired data by leveraging semi-supervised learning and distillation, reducing sample complexity while maintaining performance across multiple tasks.

Abstract: Diffusion models are increasingly used as powerful conditional generators, yet real deployments often involve multiple target distributions arising from different tasks, e.g., diverse prompt domains in text-to-image generation, or multiple environments in robotics with diffusion policies. This naturally leads to a multi-objective learning (MOL) problem. A key challenge is that achieving good Pareto trade-offs can require a generalist model class with substantially larger capacity than what suffices for solving any individual task, thereby increasing statistical cost since sample complexity typically scales with the model complexity. To reconcile this, we develop a principled MOL framework for diffusion models with limited data: a semi-supervised regime where paired (labeled) samples are scarce, but (unlabeled) condition data are abundant. We propose a two-stage training procedure that first fits lightweight specialist models from limited paired data, and then distills them into a generalist model by generating pseudo-samples. We establish generalization bounds showing that the required number of paired samples only depends on the complexity of the specialist model classes. We further extend the theory to diffusion policies for sequential decision making to account for distribution shift in on-policy rollouts. Extensive experiments on robotic control and image restoration tasks are conducted to verify our theoretical results.

</details>


### [308] [Evolving Causal Regulatory Networks (ECR-Net)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25211)
*Govind Vallabhasseri Binish,Abdhul Ahadh,Rano Roy Kavanal,Arya Ukunde*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ECR-Net is a bio-inspired framework that models causal discovery as evolving regulatory networks, enabling adaptation to non-stationary environments by dynamically modifying causal graph topology in response to environmental shocks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current machine learning models often fail to generalize out-of-distribution because they capture spurious correlations rather than true causal relationships. Existing causal discovery methods typically assume static graph structures, which limits their applicability to real-world systems that adapt and change over time across different environments.

Method: ECR-Net models data-generating processes as dynamic Gene Regulatory Network (GRN)-like systems with localized recursive functions. It uses evolutionary search algorithms to evolve populations of candidate regulatory graphs, optimizing for fitness based on how well simulated system dynamics reconstruct observed data. The system explicitly detects statistical property shifts as environmental shocks and adapts by making parsimonious modifications to causal graph topology (link inhibitions/activations).

Result: The framework represents a novel class of adaptive Structural Causal Models capable of discovering not just causal relationships, but also how and why a system's fundamental rules change over time, enabling adaptation to non-stationary environments.

Conclusion: ECR-Net offers a promising path toward robust generalization in complex, non-stationary systems by moving beyond static causal models to dynamically evolving regulatory networks that can adapt to environmental changes, potentially addressing key limitations in current machine learning's out-of-distribution generalization capabilities.

Abstract: Modern machine learning models excel at pattern recognition but remain brittle, often failing to generalize out of distribution (OOD) because they capture spurious correlations rather than the underlying causal data-generating process. Current causal discovery methods, while powerful, typically assume a static graph structure, rendering them unable to model systems that adapt or undergo structural changes across different environments. We introduce ECR-Net, Evolving Causal Regulatory Networks, a novel, bio-inspired framework for adaptive causal mechanism discovery. Our approach models the data-generating process not as a static graph, but as a dynamic system analogous to a Gene Regulatory Network (GRN), composed of localized, recursive functions where variables can activate and inhibit one another. To discover the latent structure of this network, we employ an evolutionary search algorithm that evolves a population of candidate regulatory graphs, optimizing for a fitness function that measures how well the simulated system dynamics reconstruct the observed data. The key innovation of ECR-Net is its ability to model structural adaptation, it explicitly ingests shifts in the data's statistical properties as signals of an environmental shock. In response, the evolutionary search identifies parsimonious modifications to the causal graph topology, such as link inhibitions or activations that explain the new data regime. We posit that ECR-Net represents a new class of adaptive Structural Causal Models capable of discovering how and why a system's fundamental rules change, offering a path toward robust generalization in complex, non-stationary systems.

</details>


### [309] [Personalized Federated Learning by Energy-Efficient UAV Communications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25212)
*Shiqian Guo,Jianqing Liu,Beatriz Lorenzo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a federated learning framework using UAVs with backbone-personalization head separation for data heterogeneity and gradient-based scheduling for energy-efficient device selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated learning in geographically dispersed systems (like sensor networks) faces challenges of data heterogeneity slowing convergence, and energy/communication costs from scheduling all devices, especially with UAVs' limited battery capacity.

Method: Adopts strict separation between globally shared backbone and permanently local personalization heads to mitigate data heterogeneity impact, and proposes gradient-based scheduling strategy that selects only top-α devices (ranked by gradient L2-norm) for backbone updates each round.

Result: Simulation results show the proposed scheme achieves higher learning accuracy than state-of-the-art approaches while significantly reducing UAV energy consumption.

Conclusion: The framework effectively addresses data heterogeneity and energy efficiency challenges in UAV-assisted federated learning systems through backbone-personalization separation and intelligent gradient-based device scheduling.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) is an effective paradigm for enhancing the learning capability of edge devices while preserving data privacy. In geographically dispersed FL systems, such as sensor networks in remote areas, unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) can flexibly establish high-quality communication links to support parameter exchange. However, device heterogeneity and the limited battery capacity of UAVs pose significant challenges. Specifically, data heterogeneity slows convergence, while scheduling all devices for global collaboration incurs excessive communication and energy costs. To overcome these challenges, we adopt a strict separation between a globally shared backbone and permanently local personalization heads, thereby mitigating the impact of data heterogeneity. Furthermore, we propose a gradient-based scheduling strategy that jointly considers energy efficiency and learning performance. In each communication round, the backbone is updated only by the top-$α$ devices ranked by gradient $\ell_{2}$-norm, ensuring that optimization focuses on the most informative updates. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme achieves higher learning accuracy than state-of-the-art approaches while significantly reducing UAV energy consumption.

</details>


### [310] [Continuous-Depth Field Theory for Transformer Patching and Mechanistic Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25225)
*David N. Olivieri,Antonio F. Pérez Rodríguez*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A field-theoretic framework for Transformer activation patching that treats the residual stream as a depth-token field, formulates interventions as localized sources, and predicts patch effects using sensitivity fields and empirical Green functions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mechanistic interpretability currently uses various techniques (activation patching, causal tracing, etc.) but lacks a unifying theoretical framework to organize and predict interventions systematically.

Method: Treats residual stream as depth-token field; formulates patching as localized source insertion; models patch effects with sensitivity fields; uses empirical Green functions for downstream propagation; tests in GPT-2-style Transformers.

Result: Identified bounded local linear regime; predicted patch effects from first-order sensitivities; measured structured anisotropic propagation; constructed response descriptions from high-sensitivity sites; showed prompt-induced residual displacements transfer answer behavior.

Conclusion: Response objects (sensitivities, propagated fields, Green-operator slices) provide practical language for organizing patching experiments and mathematical basis for patch-site inference and cross-scale transfer.

Abstract: Mechanistic interpretability often uses activation patching, causal tracing, path patching, and steering directions to reveal behaviorally meaningful directions in Transformer activation space. This paper develops a field-theoretic framework for organizing and predicting such interventions. Treating the residual stream as a depth-token field, we formulate patching as localized source insertion, patch effects as sensitivity-field predictions, downstream propagation as empirical Green-function response, and patch selection as an adjoint variational problem. Empirically, we test the forward response theory in GPT-2-style autoregressive Transformers by applying localized residual-field interventions and observing the induced residual-field differences and logit-difference responses. We identify a bounded local linear regime; predict patch effects from first-order sensitivities across residual sites; measure structured anisotropic propagation across depth and token position; construct response descriptions from high-sensitivity sites and sliced Green operators; and show that prompt-induced residual displacements can transfer answer behavior. These results establish response objects, namely sensitivities, propagated fields, and Green-operator slices, as a practical language for organizing patching experiments and as the forward mathematical basis for formulating patch-site inference and cross-scale transfer.formulated.

</details>


### [311] [A Blended Likelihood Approach for Achieving Fairness Using Naive Bayes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25228)
*John Arthur Junior,Abdul Lateef Yussif,Maame G. Asante-Mensah,Charles R. Haruna,Sandro Amofa,Elliot Attipoe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Developed Bias Mitigating Naive Bayes (BMNB) classifier with in-processing (blended likelihood) and post-processing (adaptive thresholding) interventions to reduce algorithmic bias while maintaining computational efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Naive Bayes classifiers lack fairness-awareness mechanisms and perpetuate historical biases in high-stakes domains like hiring, credit scoring, and criminal justice, despite being efficient and interpretable.

Method: Proposed BMNB classifier with two-stage approach: 1) In-processing blended likelihood using tunable parameter alpha to combine group-specific and pooled estimates, 2) Post-processing output calibration with adaptive thresholding for group-specific decision boundaries.

Result: BMNB achieved Disparate Impact (DI) values of 1.000, 1.171, 0.997 and Equal Opportunity Difference (EOD) values of -0.217, -0.226, -0.053 on Adult, ProPublica, and Framingham datasets while maintaining efficiency. Ablation studies showed combined approach outperforms individual techniques.

Conclusion: BMNB effectively mitigates bias in Naive Bayes classifiers through a hybrid in-processing and post-processing approach, balancing fairness and accuracy while preserving computational efficiency for practical deployment in sensitive domains.

Abstract: Concerns about algorithmic bias and fairness have increased as artificial intelligence has been incorporated into high-stakes decision-making. Traditional Naive Bayes classifiers, while efficient and interpretable, lack fairness-awareness mechanisms and perpetuate historical biases in sensitive domains such as hiring, credit scoring, and criminal justice. This study develops a fairness-aware extension of the Naive Bayes classifier that mitigates bias while maintaining computational efficiency. We propose the Bias Mitigating Naive Bayes (BMNB) classifier, integrating in-processing and post-processing interventions. The in-processing stage employs a blended likelihood approach combining group-specific and pooled likelihood estimates through a tunable blending parameter alpha to balance fairness and accuracy. The post-processing stage applies output calibration with adaptive thresholding to fine-tune group-specific decision boundaries. Experimental results indicate that BMNB attains Disparate Impact (DI) values of 1.000, 1.171, and 0.997 and Equal Opportunity Difference (EOD) values of -0.217, -0.226, and -0.053 on the Adult, ProPublica, and Framingham datasets, respectively, while maintaining computational efficiency. Ablation studies confirm that the combination of blended likelihood and adaptive thresholding yields superior performance compared to either technique in isolation.

</details>


### [312] [On the Epistemic Uncertainty of Overparametrized Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25234)
*David Rügamer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Epistemic uncertainty in overparametrized neural networks persists due to parameter non-identifiability, even when the underlying function is fully identified, challenging the conventional view that it vanishes with more data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The conventional view treats epistemic uncertainty as reducible uncertainty that disappears with more data, assuming parameter identifiability. However, in overparametrized neural networks, parameters are non-identifiable due to symmetries and redundant representations, leading to persistent uncertainty that needs better characterization.

Method: The authors analyze epistemic uncertainty through the lens of non-identifiability, characterizing both discrete and continuous sources of residual uncertainty. They focus specifically on one-hidden-layer ReLU networks and thoroughly analyze the resulting posterior structure.

Result: The analysis reveals that substantial parameter uncertainty can persist even when the underlying function is fully identified in overparametrized neural networks. The work provides theoretical insights into the posterior structure of such networks and validates these through empirical studies.

Conclusion: Epistemic uncertainty in neural networks should be understood through the framework of parameter non-identifiability rather than simply equating it with predictive variability. This perspective explains why parameter uncertainty can remain substantial even when function uncertainty is eliminated, providing a more nuanced understanding of uncertainty in overparametrized models.

Abstract: Epistemic uncertainty is often viewed as a reducible uncertainty that vanishes with increasing data. This perspective implicitly assumes parameter identifiability and equates epistemic uncertainty with predictive variability. In overparametrized neural networks, however, model parameters are typically non-identifiable due to symmetries and redundant representations. As a consequence, substantial parameter uncertainty can persist even when the underlying function is fully identified. In this work, we analyze epistemic uncertainty through the lens of non-identifiability and characterize both discrete and continuous sources of residual uncertainty. Focusing on one-hidden-layer ReLU networks, we thoroughly analyze the resulting posterior structure and validate our theoretical insights through empirical studies.

</details>


### [313] [Constraint-Anchored Attribution: Feasibility-Certified Counterfactuals and Bonferroni-PAC Sufficient Subsets for Neural CO Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25235)
*Sohaib Lafifi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents Λ-attribution, a method for explaining neural combinatorial optimization policies by decomposing decisions via LP-relaxation duals, certifying counterfactuals with combinatorial feasibility models, and providing PAC-guaranteed explanations through statistical subset testing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There's a need for interpretable attribution methods for neural combinatorial optimization policies that can provide certified counterfactuals and statistically-guaranteed explanations, especially given the black-box nature of neural networks in complex optimization tasks.

Method: The method: (1) decomposes decisions by constraint families using LP-relaxation duals, (2) certifies counterfactuals through a combinatorial feasibility model implemented as a CSP feasibility-decision model, and (3) bounds explanation size with a Bonferroni-corrected Hoeffding sufficient-subset test using greedy ordering.

Result: Across three CO problems (CVRPTW, Orienteering, Flexible Job-Shop Scheduling), Λ-attribution matches CF-derived signals at 96.5% and 77.2% for first two problems vs 75.0% and 35.2% for proxy gradient methods. Both backends agree on all CSP-certified flips in the third problem. Bonferroni-PAC subsets average 5.0 nodes per step with statistical parameters M=70, ε=δ=0.2, kmax=25.

Conclusion: The proposed Λ-attribution method provides certified and statistically-guaranteed explanations for neural combinatorial optimization policies, significantly outperforming gradient-based methods in alignment with counterfactual signals while maintaining practical efficiency.

Abstract: We give an attribution method for neural combinatorial-optimisation (CO) policies that (i) decomposes a decision by constraint families via LP-relaxation duals, (ii) certifies counterfactuals through a combinatorial feasibility model (implemented as a CSP feasibility-decision model), and (iii) bounds the size of a PAC-sufficient explanation with a Bonferroni-corrected Hoeffding sufficient-subset test along a greedy ordering. Across three CO problems and three seeds, our LP-anchored $Λ$-attribution matches the CF-derived signal at 96.5% on CVRPTW (n_cert=344) and 77.2% on the Orienteering Problem (n_cert=281) vs 75.0% and 35.2% for proxy gradient (paired diffs +0.215 and +0.420; McNemar exact $p \le 10^{-14}$). In the rank-aligned regime of the Flexible Job-Shop Scheduling Problem, both backends agree on every CSP-certified flip (n_cert=59), confirming the no-gain prediction. Bonferroni-PAC subsets average 5.0 nodes per step ($M=70$, $\varepsilon=δ=0.2$, $k_{\max}=25$). Reference implementation: https://github.com/sohaibafifi/neuro-co-cax

</details>


### [314] [Quantifying Empirical Compute-Supervision Tradeoffs in RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25252)
*Ryo Mitsuhashi,Patrick Chen,Isabelle Tseng,Jasin Cekinmez,Addison J. Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RL with noisy verifiers shows compute scaling can't fully compensate for imperfect supervision, with false negatives being particularly harmful and showing diminishing returns to compute scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While RL with verifiable rewards is standard for post-training LLMs, real-world verifiers are imperfect, and theoretical work suggests compute should overcome verification noise, but this hasn't been empirically tested.

Method: Empirical testing with Qwen2.5 (0.5B, 1.5B) using GRPO on GSM8K, injecting controlled false-positive/false-negative noise into binary correctness signals, and varying rollouts per prompt to create a compute axis.

Result: Validation accuracy gap persists under compute scaling with sharply diminishing returns; false negatives degrade performance faster than false positives; verifier quality and compute are not interchangeable.

Conclusion: Reducing false negatives is more effective than scaling compute alone; verifier quality cannot be compensated by more compute due to diminishing returns and structural asymmetry between error types.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a standard paradigm for post-training language models, but in practice, verifiers are rarely perfect. Recent theoretical work predicts that verifier noise affects the rate of learning but not its final outcome, implying that sufficient compute should close any gap induced by imperfect supervision. We test this prediction empirically by post-training Qwen2.5 (0.5B, 1.5B) with GRPO on GSM8K while injecting controlled false-positive and false-negative noise into the binary correctness signal, and varying rollouts per prompt as a compute axis. In practice, the gap in validation accuracy persists under substantial compute scaling, with returns to compute that are sharply diminishing. We further find a structural asymmetry where false negatives monotonically degrade performance quicker than with false positives. These findings suggest verifier quality and training compute are not interchangeable, and that reducing false negatives is a more effective lever than scaling compute alone.

</details>


### [315] [Latent Q-Barrier Shielding for Safe In-Context Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25267)
*Minjae Kwon,Amir Moeini,Shangtong Zhang,Lu Feng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A latent Q-Barrier shield improves safe in-context RL by filtering actions using remaining safety budget and predicted future costs, achieving better reward-safety tradeoffs without test-time parameter updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current safe in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) methods perform poorly under out-of-distribution deployment shifts because they only use frozen policy conditioning rather than explicit action-level safety checks against predicted future costs.

Method: Proposes a latent Q-Barrier shield that learns context representation, latent dynamics, and ensemble cost critic before deployment. At test time, it infers context from interaction history and filters/reweights candidate actions using remaining safety budget and predicted future costs without parameter updates.

Result: The shield improves deployment-time reward-safety tradeoffs across five safe ICRL benchmarks: after a short context window, it achieves higher return in four benchmarks while matching or lowering average episode cost in all five. Provides theoretical guarantees about barrier-margin safety.

Conclusion: The latent Q-Barrier shield enables more effective safe in-context RL by incorporating explicit safety checks at action level, maintaining safety budgets during deployment shifts without needing parameter updates, and demonstrating improved practical performance with theoretical safety guarantees.

Abstract: Safe in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) adapts online from interaction history without test-time parameter updates while controlling episode cost under a safety budget. Under out-of-distribution (OOD) deployment shifts, pretraining-only safe ICRL can give poor reward-safety tradeoffs because the remaining budget affects behavior only through frozen policy conditioning, not an explicit action-level check against predicted future cost. We propose a latent Q-Barrier shield that learns a context representation, latent dynamics, and an ensemble cost critic before deployment. Without parameter updates, the shield infers context from history and filters or softly reweights candidate actions using the remaining budget and predicted future cost. We prove a conditional, error-decomposed barrier-margin result: a Q-Barrier-satisfying action leaves the next latent-budget state with an approximately budget-safe continuation under the learned critic, up to Bellman and latent-prediction errors. Across five safe ICRL benchmarks, the shield improves deployment-time reward-safety tradeoffs over a strong safe-ICRL baseline: after a short context window, it achieves higher return in four of five benchmarks while matching or lowering average episode cost in all five.

</details>


### [316] [Label-NTK Alignments and A Tighter Convergence Bound in the NTK Regime](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25275)
*Ruchirinkil Marreddy,Chaoyue Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper develops improved convergence guarantees for neural network training by analyzing how data labels and residuals align with NTK eigenvectors, overcoming overly pessimistic worst-case bounds that depend on the smallest NTK eigenvalue.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing NTK-based convergence results are overly pessimistic and don't match practical training speeds because they rely on the smallest NTK eigenvalue, which is typically extremely small. There's a mismatch between theory (slow convergence) and practice (fast training).

Method: The authors develop sharper convergence guarantees by characterizing the interaction between data labels and the NTK eigen-spectrum. They identify two key phenomena: Label-NTK alignment (projections of labels onto NTK eigenvectors scale with corresponding eigenvalues) and Residual-NTK alignment (similar for residuals). They provide both empirical evidence and theoretical justification under mild data assumptions.

Result: The paper derives refined convergence bounds that depend on the full NTK spectrum rather than just the smallest eigenvalue. These new bounds closely match practical training dynamics and significantly improve over classical worst-case results. They also obtain improved generalization bounds. Experiments on MLPs and CNNs across multiple datasets validate the theory.

Conclusion: By leveraging Label-NTK and Residual-NTK alignment properties, the paper provides more accurate convergence guarantees for neural network training that better reflect practical observations, overcoming limitations of previous NTK-based analyses that were too conservative due to focusing only on worst-case eigenvalues.

Abstract: The Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) framework explains optimization in over-parameterized neural networks via approximately linearized dynamics, yielding exponential convergence guarantees. However, existing results are often overly pessimistic and do not match the fast training in practice, as they depend on the smallest NTK eigenvalue, which is typically extremely small in practice. In this work, we develop sharper convergence guarantees by characterizing the interaction between data labels and the NTK eigen-spectrum. We identify two key phenomena, Label-NTK alignment and Residual-NTK alignment, showing that projections of labels and residuals onto NTK eigenvectors scale with the corresponding eigenvalues. We provide empirical evidence and theoretical justification under mild data assumptions. Exploiting these alignment properties, we derive a refined convergence bound that depends on the full spectrum and closely matches practical training dynamics, significantly improving over classical worst-case results. We further obtain improved generalization bounds. Experiments on MLPs and CNNs across multiple datasets validate our theory.

</details>


### [317] [When Interpretability Becomes a Liability: Adversarial Attacks on CBM Concept Layers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25304)
*Aditya Sridhar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SPECTRA is a novel defense that hardens Concept Bottleneck Models against concept-level adversarial attacks by stabilizing semantic representations while preserving accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper identifies a previously unexplored vulnerability in Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) - their concept bottleneck layer itself represents a critical attack surface for adversarial manipulation of semantic representations, fundamentally undermining their interpretability guarantees.

Method: Developed a theoretical framework to quantify concept-space robustness, established novel vulnerability metrics, conducted extensive analysis on CUB-200-2011 dataset, and introduced SPECTRA (Semantic Perturbation-based Concept Training for Robustness against Attacks) - a principled stability regularization defense that hardens semantic representations.

Result: Standard CBMs show severe susceptibility to concept-level attacks, but SPECTRA increases the minimal perturbation norm required for successful attacks from 0.46 to over 4,200 (making attacks computationally prohibitive) while preserving baseline classification accuracy within 2.2%.

Conclusion: This work establishes concept-level attacks as a fundamentally distinct threat model, opening a new research frontier at the intersection of interpretable machine learning and adversarial robustness, while providing an effective defense that maintains both interpretability and security.

Abstract: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) have emerged as a cornerstone approach for interpretable machine learning, providing human-understandable intermediate representations through explicit concept activations. However, this interpretability fundamentally introduces a critical, previously unexplored attack surface: the concept bottleneck layer itself. We present a comprehensive, systematic study of concept-level adversarial vulnerabilities in CBMs, revealing that targeted, minimal perturbations operating on input pixels can induce catastrophic misclassification by manipulating semantic representations. We develop a rigorous theoretical framework to quantify concept-space robustness, establishing novel metrics that expose the vulnerability landscape of these architectures. Our extensive analysis on the CUB-200-2011 dataset demonstrates that standard CBMs exhibit severe susceptibility to concept-level manipulation. To address this critical weakness, we introduce SPECTRA (Semantic Perturbation-based Concept Training for Robustness against Attacks), a principled stability regularization defense. SPECTRA effectively hardens the semantic representation space, increasing the minimal perturbation norm required for a successful attack from 0.46 to over 4,200, rendering targeted concept manipulation computationally prohibitive. Furthermore, SPECTRA preserves baseline classification accuracy to within 2.2%. By establishing concept-level attacks as a fundamentally distinct threat model, this work opens a new research frontier at the intersection of interpretable machine learning and adversarial robustness.

</details>


### [318] [Electricity Consumption Forecasting: An Approach Using Cooperative Ensemble Learning with SHapley Additive exPlanations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25305)
*Eduardo Luiz Alba,Gilson Adamczuk Oliveira,Matheus Henrique Dal Molin Ribeiro,Érick Oliveira Rodrigues*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A study forecasting electricity consumption at university campuses using LSTM, RF, SVR, and XGBoost models with SHAP feature selection and GA/PSO optimization, finding that a novel WSB ensemble approach performed best.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Electricity management is challenging due to various influencing factors, and in universities, demand grows rapidly with institutional expansion while having significant environmental impact, requiring accurate forecasting for effective resource management.

Method: Used LSTM, RF, SVR, and XGBoost models trained on 7 years of historical electricity consumption data and climatic variables from two campuses; applied SHAP for feature selection and GA/PSO for hyperparameter optimization; developed a cooperative ensemble approach called Weaker Separator Booster (WSB).

Result: WSB achieved best performance: 13.90% sMAPE and 1990.87 kWh MAE for Palmas Campus, and 18.72% sMAPE and 465.02 kWh MAE for Coronel Vivida Campus. SHAP analysis showed lagged time-series values were most influential, while climatic variables had minimal impact.

Conclusion: The proposed WSB ensemble approach effectively forecasts electricity consumption in university settings, with lagged consumption patterns being more influential than weather variables, providing a valuable tool for campus electricity management and planning.

Abstract: Electricity expense management presents significant challenges, as this resource is susceptible to various influencing factors. In universities, the demand for this resource is rapidly growing with institutional expansion and has a significant environmental impact. In this study, the machine learning models long short-term memory (LSTM), random forest (RF), support vector regression (SVR), and extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost) were trained with historical consumption data from the Federal Institute of Paraná (IFPR) over the last seven years and climatic variables to forecast electricity consumption 12 months ahead. Datasets from two campuses were adopted. To improve model performance, feature selection was performed using Shapley additive explanations (SHAP), and hyperparameter optimization was carried out using genetic algorithm (GA) and particle swarm optimization (PSO). The results indicate that the proposed cooperative ensemble learning approach named Weaker Separator Booster (WSB) exhibited the best performance for datasets. Specifically, it achieved an sMAPE of 13.90% and MAE of 1990.87 kWh for the IFPR-Palmas Campus and an sMAPE of 18.72% and MAE of 465.02 kWh for the Coronel Vivida Campus. The SHAP analysis revealed distinct feature importance patterns across the two IFPR campuses. A commonality that emerged was the strong influence of lagged time-series values and a minimal influence of climatic variables.

</details>


### [319] [UWM-JEPA: Predictive World Models That Imagine in Belief Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25313)
*Santosh Kumar Radha,Oktay Goktas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UWM-JEPA introduces a density-matrix latent with unitary predictor for world modeling under partial observability, maintaining uncertainty through exact spectrum preservation during rollout, outperforming vector-latent baselines on counterfactual reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: World models for partially observed environments need to imagine multiple compatible hidden futures and handle uncertainty under counterfactual actions. Traditional vector-valued latents lack internal structure to preserve belief states about hidden continuations during blind rollout.

Method: Introduces Unitary World Model JEPA (UWM-JEPA) with a density-matrix latent on joint system-environment space and learned unitary predictor. This preserves joint-state spectrum exactly during rollout, preventing predictor dissipation of uncertainty.

Result: On hidden-velocity indicator task requiring 5-step forward simulation under given action sequence with masked target observation: UWM-JEPA achieves 0.77 accuracy, degrades monotonically with action perturbations; LSTM-JEPA collapses to majority-class accuracy (0.53). Under blind rollout, UWM-JEPA loses <10 points R² while baselines lose 41-68 points. Action sensitivity requires counterfactual target training.

Conclusion: For JEPA world models under partial observability, latent geometry and predictor dynamics matter more than frozen context-encoding capacity alone. The unitary parameterization enables proper uncertainty handling and counterfactual reasoning.

Abstract: World models for partially observed environments must imagine multiple compatible hidden futures and steer between them under counterfactual actions. Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) do this in latent space, but a vector-valued latent has no internal structure for carrying the belief over hidden continuations through blind rollout. We introduce the Unitary World Model JEPA (UWM-JEPA), a JEPA world model with a density-matrix latent on a joint system-environment space and a learned unitary predictor. The construction preserves the joint-state spectrum exactly during rollout, so the predictor itself cannot dissipate the represented uncertainty.
  On a hidden-velocity indicator task requiring five-step forward simulation under a given action sequence with the target observation masked, UWM-JEPA reaches 0.77 accuracy and degrades monotonically as actions are perturbed; a parameter-matched LSTM-JEPA trained under the same counterfactual-target objective and action head collapses to majority-class accuracy (0.53) under every action condition. Under blind rollout, UWM-JEPA loses fewer than ten points of probe R^2 at short horizons while vector-latent baselines lose forty-one and sixty-eight; both nevertheless tie on a held-out context probe, locating the separation in the predictor rather than the encoder. Action sensitivity itself requires training against counterfactual rather than teacher-forced targets, a finding that applies beyond the unitary parameterisation. For JEPA world models to imagine under partial observability, latent geometry and predictor dynamics matter, not frozen context-encoding capacity alone.

</details>


### [320] [CausalFlow: Causal Attribution and Counterfactual Repair for LLM Agent Failures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25338)
*Akash Bonagiri,Devang Borkar,Gerard Janno Anderias,Setareh Rafatirad,Houman Homayoun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CausalFlow is a framework that transforms failed LLM agent execution traces into minimal counterfactual repairs and reusable supervision through causal intervention analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents frequently fail on multi-step tasks involving reasoning, tool use, and environment interaction. Current approaches either log failures or use heuristic retries, missing the structured signals in failure traces about where execution broke down.

Method: CausalFlow models execution traces as sequential chains of dependent steps, computes Causal Responsibility Scores via step-level counterfactual intervention to identify failure-inducing steps, then generates minimally edited repairs that flip outcomes to success, producing validated contrastive pairs of (wrong step, corrected step).

Result: Across four benchmarks (mathematical reasoning, code generation, question answering, medical browsing), CausalFlow successfully converts failed executions into validated minimal repairs with high minimality and causal-consensus scores. It outperforms heuristic refinement in complex retrieval settings while producing more localized repairs throughout.

Conclusion: Interventional analysis over structured execution traces provides a principled and scalable mechanism for transforming agent failures into reliability gains and learning-ready supervision. Causal attribution is necessary for reliable improvement across diverse agent tasks.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents frequently fail on multi-step tasks involving reasoning, tool use, and environment interaction. While such failures are typically logged or retried heuristically, they contain structured signals about where execution broke down. We introduce CausalFlow, an interventional framework that converts failed agent traces into minimal counterfactual repairs and reusable supervision. CausalFlow models execution traces as sequential chains of dependent steps and computes Causal Responsibility Scores(CRS) via step-level counterfactual intervention to identify failure-inducing steps. For these steps, we generate minimally edited repairs that flip the final outcome to success, producing validated contrastive pairs of the form (wrong step, corrected step). CausalFlow supports two complementary uses: targeted test-time repair that recovers from failures with minimal behavioral drift, and training-time supervision suitable for offline preference optimization or reward modeling. Across four benchmarks spanning mathematical reasoning, code generation, question answering, and medical browsing, CausalFlow converts failed executions into validated minimal repairs with high minimality and causal-consensus scores, and demonstrates that causal attribution is necessary for reliable improvement across diverse agent tasks, outperforming heuristic refinement in complex retrieval settings while producing more localized repairs throughout. These results demonstrate that interventional analysis over structured execution traces provides a principled and scalable mechanism for transforming agent failures into reliability gains and learning-ready supervision.

</details>


### [321] [Certified Robustness from Approximate Gaussian Mixture Structures in Pretrained Latent Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25352)
*Konstantinos Emmanouilidis,Tianjiao Ding,Nghia Nguyen,Nicolas Loizou,René Vidal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework for designing certifiably robust classifiers that leverages approximate latent structure in data, achieving SOTA certified accuracy while maintaining clean performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning models are vulnerable to adversarial attacks, especially in safety-critical applications. While empirical defenses work in practice but lack formal guarantees, certified methods provide guarantees but often yield overly conservative bounds due to their inability to exploit complex data structure.

Method: Proposes a framework for certifiably robust classifiers using latent data structure. First analyzes Gaussian mixture setting to derive necessary/sufficient conditions for robust classifiers with closed-form certificates. Main contribution shows that exact structure isn't needed: if a pretrained encoder maps inputs to a distribution ε-close (in KL divergence) to a Gaussian mixture, certified accuracy degrades gracefully with explicit bounds.

Result: Method achieves state-of-the-art or competitive certified accuracy on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet benchmarks while maintaining strong clean performance and low computational overhead. Provides explicit bounds relating robustness under true and approximate distributions.

Conclusion: Approximate latent structure provides a practical and principled route to certifiable robustness, enabling direct use of pretrained models without requiring exact distributional assumptions.

Abstract: Deep learning models are vulnerable to adversarial perturbations, raising important concerns for safety-critical deployment. Empirical defenses can achieve strong robustness in practice, but lack formal guarantees, motivating the need for certifiably robust classifiers. While certified methods provide formal guarantees, they often yield overly conservative bounds due to their inability to exploit structure in complex data distributions. In this work, we propose a framework for designing certifiably robust classifiers that leverages latent structure in data representations. We first analyze the Gaussian mixture setting, deriving necessary and sufficient conditions for the existence of robust classifiers and constructing a classifier with a closed-form robustness certificate and generalization guarantees. Our main contribution is to show that exact structure is not required: we prove that if a pretrained encoder maps inputs to a latent distribution that is $\varepsilon$-close (in KL divergence) to a Gaussian mixture, then certified accuracy degrades gracefully, with an explicit bound relating robustness under the true and approximate distributions. This result enables the direct use of pretrained models without requiring exact distributional assumptions. Empirically, our method achieves state-of-the-art or competitive certified accuracy on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet, while maintaining strong clean performance and low computational overhead. Overall, our work establishes approximate latent structure as a practical and principled route to certifiable robustness.

</details>


### [322] [PDEInvBench: A Comprehensive Dataset and Design Space Exploration of Neural Networks for PDE Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25353)
*Divyam Goel,Nithin Chalapathi,Sanjeev Raja,Aditi S. Krishnapriyan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PDEInvBench introduces a comprehensive benchmark dataset for PDE inverse problems, provides design space exploration of neural networks, and reveals key insights including two-stage training, derivative features importance, and initial condition diversity benefits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a lack of comprehensive studies and benchmark datasets for PDE inverse problems, despite neural networks' capability to model function-to-function transformations. Existing benchmarks primarily focus on forward problems rather than the inverse mapping from solution fields to physical parameters.

Method: Introduced PDEInvBench dataset with numerical simulations for time-dependent and time-independent PDEs. Explored neural network design space along three dimensions: optimization procedures (supervised, self-supervised, test-time training), problem representations (architectural choices with inductive biases and conditioning strategies), and scaling (model and data size).

Result: Key findings: 1) Two-stage training (initial supervision with PDE parameters followed by test-time fine-tuning using PDE residual) performs best, 2) Incorporating PDE derivatives as input features consistently improves accuracy, 3) Increasing diversity of initial conditions yields greater performance gains than expanding range of PDE parameters.

Conclusion: The paper establishes a comprehensive benchmark for PDE inverse problems, provides practical insights for neural network design in this domain, and makes the dataset and codebase publicly available to advance research in PDE parameter estimation using machine learning.

Abstract: Inverse problems in partial differential equations (PDEs) involve estimating the physical parameters of a system from observed spatiotemporal solution fields.Neural networks are well-suited for PDE parameter estimation due to their capability to model function-to-function space transformations. While existing benchmarks of machine learning methods for PDEs primarily focus on the forward problem, there are no similar comprehensive studies and benchmark datasets on PDE inverse problems, i.e., mapping solution fields to underlying physical parameters. We fill this gap by introducing PDEInvBench, a comprehensive benchmark dataset consisting of numerical simulations for both time-dependent and time-independent PDEs across a wide range of physical behaviors and parameters. Our dataset includes evaluation splits that assess performance in both in-distribution and various out-of-distribution settings. Using our benchmark dataset, we comprehensively explore the design space of neural networks for PDE inverse problems along three key dimensions: (1) optimization procedures, analyzing the role of supervised, self-supervised, and test-time training objectives on performance, (2) problem representations, where we study the value of architectural choices with different inductive biases and various conditioning strategies, and (3) scaling, which we perform with respect to both model and data size. Our experiments reveal several practical insights: 1) neural networks perform best with a two-stage training procedure: initial supervision with PDE parameters followed by test-time fine-tuning using the PDE residual, 2) incorporating PDE derivatives as input features consistently improves accuracy, and 3) increasing the diversity of initial conditions in the training data yields greater performance gains than expanding the range of PDE parameters. We make our dataset and codebase publicly available.

</details>


### [323] [Not only where, But when: Temporal Scheduling for RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25381)
*Jinghao Zhang,Ruilin Li,Feng Zhao,Jiaqi Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Temporal scheduling of credit allocation criteria in RLVR training improves LLM policy evolution by prioritizing targeted tokens early and gradually shifting to general optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RLVR methods for LLM post-training use globally broadcast scalar rewards without differentiating heterogeneous policy behaviors along trajectories. Existing credit allocation methods (token-level advantage reweighting, selective token optimization) use stagnant allocation criteria throughout training, limiting resilient policy evolution.

Method: Introduces temporal dimension to RLVR optimization by scheduling credit allocation criteria over time. Uses trajectory percentiles to distinguish policy behaviors, prioritizing targeted tokens with specific behaviors early and gradually attenuating toward general optimization.

Result: Demonstrates consistent improvements across mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks. Shows that temporal scheduling yields healthier policy evolution dynamics compared to standard optimization which sacrifices policy entropy when accommodating heterogeneous behaviors.

Conclusion: When learning signals are scheduled is as important as where they are allocated across tokens. Temporal scheduling of credit allocation criteria leads to more stable and efficient learning dynamics in RLVR for LLMs, constituting a promising optimization dimension.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a core technique for post-training of Large Language Models (LLMs). While policy optimization is driven by all sampled tokens under a globally broadcast scalar reward, the heterogeneous policy behaviors exhibited along trajectories are largely overlooked without differentiation. Existing works address this by credit allocation, including token-level advantage reweighting, and selective token optimization, however, the allocation criterion are principally stagnant throughout training, limiting resilient policy evolution. In this work, we argue that \textit{when} learning signals are scheduled can be as important as \textit{where} they are allocated across tokens, and introduce the temporal dimension that scheduling the credit allocation criteria over the course of RLVR optimization. We find that prioritizing targeted tokens emphasized with specific policy behaviors, and gradually attenuating toward general optimization leads to more stable and efficient learning dynamics. Furthermore, we show that simple trajectory percentiles provide a natural perspective for distinguishing policy behaviors, and works effectively with temporal scheduling. Our analysis reveals that standard optimization substantially sacrifices policy entropy when simultaneously accommodating heterogeneous behaviors, whereas temporal scheduling yields healthier policy evolution dynamics. Experiments across mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements, suggesting that temporal scheduling constitutes a promising optimization dimension.

</details>


### [324] [ViroBench: Benchmarking Nucleotide Foundation Models on Viral Genomics Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25388)
*Dongxin Ye,Fang Hu,Han Hu,Shu Hu,Yang Tan,Wanli Ouyang,Stan Z. Li,Jie Cui,Nanqing Dong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ViroBench is the first comprehensive benchmark for evaluating nucleotide foundation models in viral genomics, assessing both biological understanding and biosecurity risks across 18 scenarios, revealing key limitations and insights about pretraining data diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is no unified standard or benchmark for evaluating nucleotide foundation models (NFMs) in viral genomics, despite their importance for biomedical advancement. Current approaches lack systematic evaluation of both biological understanding and biosecurity risks, hindering community development and safety.

Method: Introduced ViroBench - a comprehensive benchmark with 18 diverse scenarios across 4 task types. Evaluated 66 NFMs with different architectures. Used controlled ablation studies to analyze factors like pretraining data diversity and parameter scale.
