Result: 1) NFMs show performance degradation in biological understanding under phylogenetic/temporal shifts (weak extrapolation). 2) Generation tasks reveal decoupling between statistical likelihood and biological functional validity (biosecurity risk). 3) Taxonomic diversity in pretraining data outweighs parameter scale - lightweight model with diverse data achieved 67.5% performance gain.

Conclusion: ViroBench provides essential diagnostic evaluations and reproducible measurement framework for viral nucleotide foundation models, highlighting critical limitations in current approaches and emphasizing the importance of diverse pretraining data over model size. The benchmark enables safer, more effective development in viral genomics.

Abstract: Nucleotide sequences constitute the fundamental genetic basis of biological systems, rendering viral genomic analysis critical for biomedical advancement. Despite progress in biological foundation models, specifically nucleotide foundation models (NFMs), the field lacks a unified standard for viral genomics to facilitate community development and enforce biosecurity constraints. To address this, we introduce ViroBench, the first comprehensive and large-scale benchmark specifically designed for NFMs in viral settings. ViroBench evaluates models across two critical dimensions: biological understanding and latent biosecurity risk, covering 18 diverse scenarios within 4 task types. Extensive evaluation of 66 NFMs across diverse architectures yields three critical conclusions. Firstly, NFMs exhibit a performance degradation in biological understanding under phylogenetic and temporal shifts, indicating weak extrapolation capabilities. Secondly, generation tasks reveal a decoupling between statistical likelihood and biological functional validity, posing latent biosecurity risks. Thirdly, controlled ablation studies reveal that taxonomic diversity in pretraining data outweighs parameter scale. Specifically, a lightweight baseline trained on diverse data achieves a 67.5% performance gain over its original model. Overall, ViroBench provides interpretable, diagnostic evaluations and a reproducible measurement framework for future research on viral nucleotide foundation models. The datasets and code are publicly available at https://github.com/QIANJINYDX/ViroBench.

</details>


### [325] [A Context Augmented Multi-Play Multi-Armed Bandit Algorithm for Fast Channel Allocation in Opportunistic Spectrum Access](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25391)
*Ruiyu Li,Guangxia Li,Xiao Lu,Jichao Liu,Yan Jin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel restless contextual multi-play multi-armed bandit approach for channel allocation in spectrum access, incorporating channel noise as perturbations to reward functions, with linear and nonlinear correlation models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most existing MP-MAB methods are impractical for real-world opportunistic spectrum access systems due to assuming ideal conditions, high computational costs, and crucially ignoring channel noise impact on service quality.

Method: Models channel noise as perturbations of arm's reward function, uses channel state information as context, investigates linear and nonlinear correlations between context and perturbation, derives two index policies using linear models and neural networks to estimate noise and adjust upper confidence bounds.

Result: Numerical experiments show the proposed policies achieve lower regret and select sub-optimal arms in a more reasonable way compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: The proposed restless contextual MP-MAB framework provides a practical and effective solution for channel allocation in OSA systems by explicitly addressing channel noise impacts through context-aware perturbation modeling.

Abstract: We study the restless contextual multi-play multi-armed bandit (MP-MAB) problem for channel allocation in the opportunity spectrum access (OSA) scenario. Most existing MP-MAB methods are impractical for real-world OSA systems as they assume many ideal conditions, incur a heavy computational cost, and most importantly, ignore the impact of channel noise which is directly related to the quality of service. In this study, we embody this impact by modeling channel noise as a perturbation of the arm's reward function in MP-MAB. As there is an implicit correlation between channel state information and channel noise, we take the former as a context for MP-MAB to present the perturbation caused by the latter. We investigate two types of correlation between the context and the perturbation -- linear and nonlinear, and derive two index policies, respectively. These policies learn the correlations through a linear model and a neural network, and use estimated noise value to adjust the upper confidence bound. Numerical experiments demonstrate that the proposed policies can achieve lower regret and select sub-optimal arms in a more reasonable way.

</details>


### [326] [EMA-Nesterov: Stabilizing Nesterov's Lookahead for Accelerated Deep Learning Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25395)
*Chung-Yiu Yau,Dawei Li,Athanasios Glentis,Valentyn Boreiko,Hoi-To Wai,Mingyi Hong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EMA-Nesterov: A modified Nesterov acceleration method that replaces standard lookahead with exponential moving average of parameter updates to capture low-frequency trends and stabilize acceleration in deep learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard lookahead-based acceleration methods like Nesterov's momentum become unreliable in deep learning due to stochastic gradient noise and non-convex landscapes. Short-horizon update signals used in standard lookahead are noisy and lead to unstable extrapolation directions.

Method: EMA-Nesterov replaces Nesterov's standard lookahead direction with an exponential moving average (EMA) of parameter updates. This creates a stabilized lookahead direction that captures low-frequency trends via EMA's low-pass filtering, while remaining adaptive through EMA's geometric weighting structure.

Result: EMA-Nesterov retains theoretical accelerated convergence rate in convex problems analogous to Nesterov's accelerated gradient. Empirical evidence on language model pre-training shows broad applicability across various optimizers (Adam, SOAP, Muon, NanoGPT) with better performance than prior lookahead methods.

Conclusion: EMA-Nesterov effectively accelerates deep learning optimization by harnessing low-frequency trajectory trends through EMA filtering, avoiding instability of short-horizon lookahead and non-adaptivity of long-horizon lookahead, making it broadly applicable across modern deep learning optimizers.

Abstract: Lookahead-based acceleration methods, such as Nesterov's momentum, are widely used in optimization, but they often become unreliable in deep learning training mainly due to stochastic gradient noise and non-convex loss landscapes. In particular, standard lookahead relies on short-horizon update signals (e.g., differences between consecutive iterates), which are inherently noisy and can lead to unstable extrapolation directions. This work revisits Nesterov's acceleration from a trajectory perspective and argues that effective acceleration in deep learning should harness the low-frequency trends of optimization trajectories rather than extrapolating noisy one-step updates. Leveraging this insight, we propose EMA-Nesterov, a simple modification that replaces the standard Nesterov's lookahead direction with an exponential moving average (EMA) of parameter updates. This yields a stabilized lookahead direction that captures and harnesses the evolving trend of the training trajectory through a low-pass filter, while remaining adaptive to progressive changes via the geometric weighting structure of EMA. We show that EMA-Nesterov retains a theoretical accelerated convergence rate in convex problems that is analogous to Nesterov's accelerated gradient method. Furthermore, we provide empirical evidence on language model pre-training to verify that EMA-Nesterov is broadly applicable across a range of fine-tuned base optimizers, including Adam, SOAP, Muon, as well as complex optimizers that achieve state-of-the-art performance on optimization benchmarks (NanoGPT). Compared to prior lookahead methods, EMA-Nesterov achieves better performance by avoiding the instability of short-horizon lookahead and the non-adaptivity of long-horizon lookahead.

</details>


### [327] [Autoregression-Free Neural Operators for Time-Dependent PDEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25413)
*Jiaquan Zhang,Caiyan Qin,Haoyu Bian,Libin Cai,Yi Lu,Chaoning Zhang,Wei Dong,Yuanfang Guo,Yang Yang,Hen Tao Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AFNO (Autoregression-Free Neural Operators) learns latent vector fields for time-dependent PDEs using flow matching, eliminating autoregressive rollout and enabling stable long-horizon predictions with explicit physical parameter conditioning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neural operator methods for time-dependent PDEs use autoregressive rollout in high-dimensional physical field spaces, which leads to progressive error accumulation over long-horizon predictions due to continuous-time modeling limitations.

Method: AFNO maps time evolution of PDEs into a latent space and models continuous-time vector fields within it using flow matching. It avoids autoregressive rollout by learning the latent vector field and enables continuous evolution over extended horizons with explicit conditioning on physical parameters.

Result: Theoretical analysis and extensive experiments on six PDEs demonstrate that AFNO improves long-horizon prediction stability and consistently reduces rollout errors compared with baseline methods.

Conclusion: AFNO provides an effective autoregression-free framework for solving time-dependent PDEs that addresses the error accumulation problem in long-horizon predictions through latent space continuous-time modeling with physical parameter conditioning.

Abstract: Neural operators learn mappings from function-dependent inputs to solutions, providing an effective framework for solving partial differential equations (PDEs). For time-dependent PDEs, existing methods typically perform long-horizon prediction through autoregressive rollout directly in high-dimensional physical field spaces, where each predicted state is recursively fed back as the input for the next step. Although effective for short-term prediction, this autoregressive rollout and the lack of continuous-time modeling lead to progressive error accumulation over long-horizon rollouts. In this work, we propose Autoregression-Free Neural Operators (AFNO), which map the time evolution of PDEs into a latent space and model continuous-time vector fields within it. AFNO uses flow matching to learn the latent vector field, thereby enabling continuous evolution over extended horizons, avoiding autoregressive rollout and capturing dynamics under varying parameter configurations through explicit conditioning on physical parameters. Theoretical analysis and extensive experiments on six PDEs demonstrate that AFNO improves long-horizon prediction stability and consistently reduces rollout errors compared with the baselines.

</details>


### [328] [Capture-Calibrate-Coach: A Graph-Based Framework for Knowledge Monitoring Estimation and Adaptive Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25419)
*Gen Li,Li Chen,Cheng Tang,Boxuan Ma,Yuncheng Jiang,Daisuke Deguchi,Takayoshi Yamashita,Atsushi Shimada*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Capture-Calibrate-Coach (3C) framework that uses open-ended self-reports and graph neural networks to assess learners' knowledge monitoring abilities and provide personalized feedback based on both knowledge gaps and metacognitive calibration errors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current learning systems focus primarily on what learners know but fail to address how accurately learners perceive their own understanding (knowledge monitoring). This metacognitive dimension is crucial for self-regulated learning but remains underexplored in existing adaptive learning systems.

Method: The 3C framework has three phases: 1) Capture - extracts learners' perceived knowledge states from open-ended self-reports to build a heterogeneous graph linking learners and concepts; 2) Calibrate - uses heterogeneous graph neural networks to infer latent perceived states for unmentioned concepts; 3) Coach - classifies learners into five metacognitive patterns and delivers personalized feedback addressing both knowledge gaps and calibration errors.

Result: Evaluation with 684 students shows 85.21% AUC in predicting latent perceived states, significantly outperforming baseline methods. A user study with 47 participants reveals positive reception of feedback quality, with participants particularly valuing concrete feedback on knowledge gaps and actionable study guidance.

Conclusion: The 3C framework advances AI-based learning support toward metacognitive teammates that foster accurate self-awareness while supporting knowledge growth, bridging the gap between knowledge assessment and metacognitive calibration in adaptive learning systems.

Abstract: Effective learning support requires understanding not only what learners know but also how accurately they perceive their own understanding. This metacognitive dimension, known as knowledge monitoring, fundamentally influences self-regulated learning, yet this dimension remains underexplored in current systems. This paper introduces the Capture-Calibrate-Coach (3C) framework for adaptive learning support. The Capture phase extracts learners' perceived knowledge states from open-ended self-reports to construct a heterogeneous graph linking learners and knowledge concepts. The Calibrate phase applies a heterogeneous graph neural network to infer latent perceived states for concepts not explicitly mentioned, enabling systematic knowledge monitoring assessment. The Coach phase classifies learners into five metacognitive patterns and delivers personalized feedback addressing both knowledge gaps and calibration errors. Evaluation with 684 students demonstrates 85.21% AUC in predicting latent perceived states, significantly outperforming baseline methods. A user study with 47 participants shows positive reception of feedback quality, with participants particularly valuing concrete feedback on knowledge gaps and actionable study guidance. These findings advance AI-based learning support toward metacognitive teammates that foster accurate self-awareness while supporting knowledge growth.

</details>


### [329] [SeqRoute: Global Budget-Aware Sequential LLM Routing via Offline Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25424)
*Zhongling Xu,Shunan Zheng,Wei Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SeqRoute introduces a sequential routing framework for LLMs that treats user sessions as Markov Decision Processes, using offline RL to optimize resource allocation across multi-turn conversations while avoiding budget bankruptcy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM routing frameworks fail to account for the sequential nature of real-world user sessions with global computational budgets, leading to budget bankruptcy where early interactions exhaust resources, forcing later, potentially more complex queries onto inadequate models.

Method: Formulates multi-turn routing as a finite-horizon Markov Decision Process solved via offline reinforcement learning (Conservative Q-Learning). Introduces Hindsight Budget Relabeling (HBR) to overcome data scarcity by simulating historical trajectories under diverse hypothetical budgets, expanding 10,000 raw sessions into 2.38 million transitions. Uses dynamic λ-sweep mechanism for zero-shot navigation of cost-quality Pareto frontier at deployment.

Result: SeqRoute reduces operational costs by 6.0-73.5% while maintaining or improving quality, suppresses bankruptcy rates to under 1%, and strictly dominates behavior cloning, budget-aware heuristics, and static baselines across the entire Pareto frontier.

Conclusion: The sequential approach to LLM routing with budget-aware reinforcement learning and data augmentation techniques significantly outperforms existing methods by preventing budget bankruptcy and optimizing resource allocation across entire user sessions.

Abstract: Existing LLM routing frameworks treat queries as independent events, neglecting the sequential nature of real-world user sessions constrained by global computational budgets. This mismatch inevitably leads to budget bankruptcy: myopic routing policies exhaust resources on early interactions, forcing subsequent and often more complex queries onto inadequate models. We introduce SeqRoute, a framework that formulates multi-turn routing as a finite-horizon Markov Decision Process and solves it via offline reinforcement learning. By incorporating the remaining budget into the state space and training with Conservative Q-Learning (CQL), SeqRoute learns delayed gratification to strategically preserve resources for high-stakes turns later in the session. To overcome data starvation, we propose Hindsight Budget Relabeling (HBR). This technique retrospectively simulates historical trajectories under diverse hypothetical budgets, expanding 10,000 raw sessions into 2.38 million transitions enriched with critical bankruptcy signals. At deployment, a dynamic $λ$-sweep mechanism enables zero-shot navigation of the cost-quality Pareto frontier without retraining. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that SeqRoute reduces operational costs by 6.0-73.5% while maintaining or improving quality, and suppresses bankruptcy rates to under 1%, strictly dominating behavior cloning, budget-aware heuristics, and static baselines across the entire Pareto frontier.

</details>


### [330] [Rethinking Feature Alignment in Generalist Graph Anomaly Detection: A Relational Fingerprint-based Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25429)
*Yujing Liu,Yixin Liu,Yu Zheng,Alan Wee-Chung Liew,Xiaofeng Cao,Shirui Pan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReFi-GAD is a generalist graph anomaly detection approach that uses relational fingerprints to align heterogeneous features and transfer knowledge across unseen graphs without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generalist graph anomaly detection approaches rely on PCA-based projection to align heterogeneous features across domains, but this ignores feature semantics. This leads to failure in learning transferable semantic knowledge and can cause negative transfer on unseen graphs.

Method: Proposes ReFi-GAD which aligns heterogeneous raw features using a universal semantics-aware Relational Fingerprint (ReFi) that encodes anomaly-indicative cues from both contextual and structural perspectives. The model combines a transformer-based encoder to capture domain-invariant knowledge with an SNR-guided refinement module for domain-specific adaptation.

Result: Extensive experiments on 14 datasets demonstrate that ReFi-GAD significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in generalist graph anomaly detection.

Conclusion: The proposed relational fingerprint approach effectively addresses the semantic alignment problem in generalist graph anomaly detection, enabling better knowledge transfer across different graph domains without requiring graph-specific retraining.

Abstract: Generalist graph anomaly detection (GAD) aims to detect anomalies on unseen graphs without graph-specific retraining. Nevertheless, existing approaches primarily focus on aligning heterogeneous features across different data domains via PCA-based projection, which harmonizes feature dimensions ignores feature semantics. As a result, GAD models fail to learn transferable semantic knowledge, and even exhibit negative transfer on unseen graphs. To address this issue, we propose a Relational Fingerprint-based generalist GAD approach (ReFi-GAD for short), aligning heterogeneous raw features with a universal and semantics-aware Relational Fingerprint (ReFi) that encodes anomaly-indicative cues from both contextual and structural perspectives. Building on ReFi, we design a fingerprint-grounded generalist GAD model, which combines a transformer-based encoder to capture domain-invariant knowledge with an SNR-guided refinement module for domain-specific adaptation. Extensive experiments on 14 datasets demonstrate that ReFi-GAD significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [331] [Missing Pattern Recognized Diffusion Imputation Model for Missing Not At Random](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25439)
*Gyuwon Sim,Sumin Lee,Heesun Bae,Byeonghu Na,Doyun Kwon,Ju-Hee Hwang,Jae-Young Lim,Il-Chul Moon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PRDIM is a new diffusion-based imputation model that explicitly captures missing patterns to better handle Missing Not at Random (MNAR) data across various domains like time-series and images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world missing data often depends on the unobserved values themselves (MNAR), creating a challenging imputation problem that existing methods may not adequately address.

Method: The framework uses a pattern recognizer to approximate underlying missing patterns, then employs an EM algorithm to iteratively maximize the joint likelihood of observed values and missing masks, guiding inference toward plausible imputations.

Result: Extensive experiments show PRDIM consistently achieves strong imputation performance under MNAR settings across multiple data modalities.

Conclusion: PRDIM provides an effective solution for MNAR data imputation by explicitly modeling missing patterns through a novel diffusion framework with pattern recognition guidance.

Abstract: Missing data frequently arises across diverse domains, including time-series and image domains. In the real world, missing occurrences often depend on the unobservable values themselves, which are referred to as Missing Not at Random (MNAR). In this work, we introduce the Missing Pattern Recognized Diffusion Imputation Model (PRDIM), a novel framework that explicitly captures the missing pattern and precisely imputes unobserved values. PRDIM iteratively maximizes the likelihood of the joint distribution for observed values and missing mask under an Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm. In this sense, we first employ a pattern recognizer, which approximates the underlying missing pattern and provides guidance during every inference toward more plausible imputations with respect to the missing information. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that PRDIM consistently achieves strong imputation performance under MNAR settings across multiple data modalities.

</details>


### [332] [BigMac: Breaking the Pareto Frontier of Compute and Memory in Multimodal LLM Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25451)
*Zili Zhang,Chengxu Yang,Shenglong Zhang,Chenyu Wang,Yufan Zhang,Tuo Dai,Zhouyang Li,Yuhong Ge,Chao Jin,Xin Jin,Yuliang Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BigMac is a new training pipeline for multimodal LLMs that breaks the Pareto frontier between compute and memory efficiency by using a nested pipeline design to achieve both computational efficiency and reduced memory usage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training multimodal large language models faces challenges from both model and data heterogeneity. Existing systems are constrained by a Pareto frontier where improving compute efficiency comes at the expense of memory efficiency, and vice versa.

Method: BigMac elegantly nests encoder and generator computation into the original LLM pipeline, creating a dependency-safe nested pipeline structure. This reduces activation memory complexity of encoder and generator to O(1) while maintaining the LLM's activation memory complexity unchanged.

Result: BigMac achieves the same computational efficiency as idealized unlimited memory settings while reducing activation memory complexity. Evaluation shows 1.08×-1.9× training speedup over baseline systems with stable memory usage as batch size increases.

Conclusion: BigMac successfully breaks the Pareto frontier between computational efficiency and memory usage, enabling simultaneous optimization of both computation and memory in MLLM training through its innovative nested pipeline design.

Abstract: Training multimodal large language models (MLLMs) is challenged by both model and data heterogeneity. Existing systems redesign the training pipeline to address these challenges, but remain bound by a Pareto frontier between compute and memory efficiency, improving one only at the expense of the other. We present BigMac, a new training pipeline for multimodal LLMs. The core idea of BigMac is to elegantly nest the encoder and generator computation into the original LLM pipeline, forming a dependency-safe nested pipeline structure. With this design, BigMac reduces the activation memory complexity of the encoder and generator to O(1) while keeping the activation memory complexity of the LLM unchanged. At the same time, it achieves the same computational efficiency as the idealized setting with unlimited memory. As a result, BigMac breaks the Pareto frontier between computational efficiency and memory usage, enabling simultaneous optimization of both computation and memory in MLLM training. We evaluate BigMac on multiple MLLMs and training workloads. Experimental results show that BigMac achieves a 1.08$\times$-1.9$\times$ training speedup over baseline systems while maintaining stable memory usage as batch size increases.

</details>


### [333] [From Simulation to Enaction: Post-trained language models recognize and react to their own generations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25459)
*Asvin G.,Jack Lindsey*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Post-trained language models recognize when they are generating their own responses (on-policy) vs. responding to external text (off-policy), showing 3-4× lower entropy for on-policy outputs. This recognition is implicitly encoded in their output distributions and involves an internal representation of input surprise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Language models are typically pretrained as passive predictors that don't consider the consequences of their own outputs. Post-training changes this by allowing models to recognize when they are generating their own responses, which could affect their behavior and output characteristics.

Method: The researchers analyze post-trained models' behavior by comparing on-policy (self-generated) vs. off-policy (responding to external text) contexts. They measure output distribution entropy, examine internal representations of input surprise, test with open-ended prompts, and assess explicit vs. implicit recognition through verbal report tasks.

Result: Post-trained models show 3-4× lower entropy for on-policy outputs compared to off-policy across model families and sizes. They develop an internal representation of input surprise that causally modulates output entropy. Models collapse uncertainty about upcoming topics before generating first tokens, and can distinguish on-policy from off-policy contexts via explicit verbal reports, though this uses different mechanisms than implicit recognition.

Conclusion: Post-trained language models implicitly recognize when they are generating their own outputs, affecting their internal states and output characteristics. This recognition manifests in reduced entropy and involves distinct mechanisms for implicit vs. explicit awareness, suggesting models develop some form of self-awareness about their generative process during post-training.

Abstract: Language models are pretrained as passive predictors with no incentive to model the consequences of their own outputs. Post-training changes this: a model producing its own responses can benefit from recognizing that it is on-policy. We present evidence that post-trained models recognize their on-policy generations, and this recognition is implicitly encoded in their output distributions. In particular, on-policy output distribution entropy is 3--4$\times$ lower than off-policy entropy, across model families and size classes. We trace part of this effect to an internal representation of input surprise, tracking the unlikeliness of the most recent input token according to the model's prior predictions, that causally modulates output entropy. One example of these phenomena can be observed in response to open-ended prompts; post-trained models (unlike pretrained models) collapse their uncertainty over the topic of their upcoming response before the first output token; violating this cached intention with a different-topic prefill results in higher output entropy. We also tested whether models can distinguish on-policy contexts from prefills via explicit verbal report. We find that they can, but that interestingly, this explicit recognition routes through a different mechanism than implicit recognition.

</details>


### [334] [LETS Forecast: Learning Embedology for Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.06454)
*Abrar Majeedi,Viswanatha Reddy Gajjala,Satya Sai Srinath Namburi GNVV,Nada Magdi Elkordi,Yin Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DeepEDM combines nonlinear dynamical systems modeling with deep learning for time series forecasting, using time-delayed embeddings and kernel regression to learn underlying dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world time series have complex nonlinear dynamics that are crucial for accurate prediction, but many deep learning approaches don't explicitly model these dynamics. There's a gap between dynamical systems theory and practical deep learning methods for time series forecasting.

Method: DeepEDM integrates nonlinear dynamical systems modeling with deep neural networks, inspired by empirical dynamic modeling (EDM) and Takens' theorem. It learns a latent space from time-delayed embeddings and uses kernel regression to approximate underlying dynamics, leveraging efficient implementation of softmax attention for accurate prediction.

Result: Comprehensive experiments on synthetic nonlinear dynamical systems and real-world time series show DeepEDM is robust to input noise and outperforms state-of-the-art methods in forecasting accuracy.

Conclusion: DeepEDM successfully bridges the gap between dynamical systems theory and deep learning, providing an effective framework for time series forecasting that explicitly models underlying nonlinear dynamics while maintaining practical efficiency.

Abstract: Real-world time series are often governed by complex nonlinear dynamics. Understanding these underlying dynamics is crucial for precise future prediction. While deep learning has achieved major success in time series forecasting, many existing approaches do not explicitly model the dynamics. To bridge this gap, we introduce DeepEDM, a framework that integrates nonlinear dynamical systems modeling with deep neural networks. Inspired by empirical dynamic modeling (EDM) and rooted in Takens' theorem, DeepEDM presents a novel deep model that learns a latent space from time-delayed embeddings, and employs kernel regression to approximate the underlying dynamics, while leveraging efficient implementation of softmax attention and allowing for accurate prediction of future time steps. To evaluate our method, we conduct comprehensive experiments on synthetic data of nonlinear dynamical systems as well as real-world time series across domains. Our results show that DeepEDM is robust to input noise, and outperforms state-of-the-art methods in forecasting accuracy. Our code is available at: https://abrarmajeedi.github.io/deep_edm.

</details>


### [335] [JacQuant: STE-Free Quantization-Aware Training via Learned Jacobian Surrogates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25469)
*Kai Yi,Vignesh Vivekraja,Harshit Khaitan,Steven Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: JacQuant is a new QAT framework that uses a data-driven, lightweight surrogate model to learn local sensitivity to parameter changes, replacing the problematic Straight-Through Estimator for more stable and accurate ultra-low-bit quantization of LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current quantization-aware training (QAT) methods rely on the Straight-Through Estimator (STE), which forces gradients through non-differentiable quantizers. This makes training brittle near bin boundaries and poorly aligned with actual low-precision model behavior, especially for ultra-low-bit quantization of large language models.

Method: Introduces JacQuant, a QAT framework that learns a lightweight (diagonal or block-diagonal) surrogate model of the local sensitivity to parameter changes. This data-driven surrogate is used within standard variance-reduced optimizers to stabilize and accelerate training. The method is drop-in compatible with existing forward quantizers and requires no changes to quantization operations.

Result: Proven convergence for non-convex objectives with linear rates under PL conditions. JacQuant consistently achieves higher accuracy than STE-based QAT across LLM benchmarks at ≤2 bits. Runtime analysis shows negligible added cost with practical group sizes. Empirical validation focuses on ultra-low-bit LLM QAT.

Conclusion: JacQuant provides a theoretically grounded, practical solution to the limitations of STE-based QAT, offering more stable training and better accuracy for ultra-low-bit quantization of large models with minimal computational overhead.

Abstract: Quantization-aware training (QAT) is widely deployed but typically relies on the Straight-Through Estimator (STE), which passes gradients through non-differentiable quantizers by fiat. This often makes training brittle near bin boundaries and weakly aligned with the actual behavior of the low-precision model. We introduce JacQuant, a QAT framework that learns a lightweight surrogate of the model's local sensitivity to parameter changes and uses it to stabilize and accelerate training within standard variance-reduced optimizers. The surrogate is inexpensive (diagonal or block-diagonal), data-driven, and compatible with common weight and activation quantizers. On code-preserving training phases, we prove convergence for non-convex objectives and obtain linear rates under a PL condition, and we relate the learned sensitivity to end-to-end output fidelity via a simple calibration argument. Across LLM benchmarks at $\leq 2$ bits, JacQuant consistently reaches higher accuracy than STE-based QAT, and the runtime analyses on various models show that the added cost remains negligible under practical group sizes. The method is drop-in and requires no changes to the forward quantizers; our empirical claims are scoped to ultra-low-bit LLM QAT.

</details>


### [336] [Check Your LLM's Secret Dictionary! Five Lines of Code Reveal What Your LLM Learned (Including What It Shouldn't Have)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22005)
*Hisashi Miyashita*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SVD analysis of LLM lm_head weight matrices reveals interpretable semantic subspaces from model weights alone, enabling discovery of training data composition, ethically concerning content, and glitch tokens without model inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for understanding LLM internal representations typically require extensive model inference and probing. The paper aims to develop a simple, static analysis technique that reveals model characteristics directly from weights, particularly focusing on semantic organization and safety concerns.

Method: Apply singular value decomposition (SVD) to the lm_head weight matrices of transformer-based LLMs. Analyze left singular vectors to identify vocabulary token clusters. Introduce Vocabulary Cluster Score (VCS) to quantify subspace coherence and Weighted Projection Score (WPS) as a static glitch token detector.

Result: Analysis of GPT-OSS-120B, Gemma-2-2B, and Qwen2.5-1.5B reveals systematic differences: GPT shows graduated functional subspaces; Gemma is dominated by pre-19th-century English orthography; Qwen shows multilingual coverage with ethically concerning subspaces that persist through alignment. WPS successfully identifies known glitch tokens without inference.

Conclusion: lm_head SVD analysis provides a powerful, lightweight method for understanding LLM characteristics, revealing training data composition, ethical concerns, and model structure. The approach should be adopted as a standard pre-release safety auditing step and suggests directions for SVD-guided tokenizer optimization and more controllable LLM design.

Abstract: We show that singular value decomposition of the lm_head} weight matrix of a transformer-based large language model -- requiring only five lines of PyTorch and no model inference -- reveals interpretable semantic subspaces directly from the model weights. Each left singular vector identifies the vocabulary tokens most readily selected when the hidden state aligns with the corresponding singular direction; inspecting these clusters exposes the model's training data composition and curation philosophy.
  Analysing GPT-OSS-120B, Gemma-2-2B, and Qwen2.5-1.5B, we find that singular value spectra and vocabulary cluster structures differ systematically across models: GPT exhibits a graduated hierarchy of functionally differentiated subspaces; Gemma is dominated by pre-nineteenth-century English orthography, forming a stepwise clustering structure that may contribute to high output controllability; and Qwen exhibits broad multilingual coverage alongside subspaces whose vocabulary the authors have determined to be ethically inappropriate for direct publication.
  Base-instruct comparison reveals that ethically concerning subspaces originate in pretraining and are not removed by post-training alignment. We introduce the Vocabulary Cluster Score (VCS) to quantify subspace coherence, and the Weighted Projection Score (WPS) as a static glitch token detector; applying WPS to GPT-OSS-120B recovers shokubutsu-hyakka-tsu (ID 137606), a well-known glitch token widely reported in the CJK language community, without any model inference. We propose a taxonomy of root causes for problematic vocabulary content and call for lm_head} SVD analysis to be adopted as a standard pre-release safety auditing step. Our findings further suggest directions toward SVD-guided tokenizer optimisation and more controllable LLM design.

</details>


### [337] [SafetyRepro: Configuration-Conditional Rank Instability on Alignment Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25492)
*Yanhang Li,Zhichao Fan,Zexin Zhuang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Foundation model benchmark comparisons are unreliable because they can be flipped by changing benchmark configuration choices alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current foundation model benchmark comparisons are treated as quantitative verdicts but depend on unspecified configuration choices, leading to potentially misleading pairwise safety rankings.

Method: Developed a theoretical proposition linking measurable pairwise disagreement rates to configuration reversals, paired with a commit-stamped evaluation protocol applied to widely cited alignment benchmarks.

Result: On every benchmark tested, configuration choice alone can flip pairwise verdicts (e.g., from "A is safer than B" to "B is safer than A"). The proposition successfully isolates this strict-reversal failure mode.

Conclusion: Foundation model benchmarks have a fundamental flaw where pairwise safety rankings are not stable across configuration choices, undermining the reliability of current evaluation practices in AI alignment research.

Abstract: Pairwise model comparisons drawn from foundation-model benchmarks ("A is safer than B") are read as quantitative verdicts but hinge on harness choices benchmark papers under-specify. We close one theory-benchmark loop on this primitive: a finite-envelope proposition tying a measurable pairwise-disagreement rate to whether the strict ordering admits a configuration-pair reversal, paired with a commit-stamped evaluation protocol that operationalises it on widely cited alignment benchmarks. On every benchmark we test, configuration choice alone can flip the pairwise verdict; the proposition isolates this strict-reversal failure mode.

</details>


### [338] [Accelerated Dynamic Importance Weighting with Versatile Divergence-Minimizing Estimators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25499)
*Tongtong Fang,Nan Lu,Gang Niu,Kenji Fukumizu,Masashi Sugiyama*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ADIW is an accelerated version of Dynamic Importance Weighting that improves efficiency through warm-start gradient descent and generalizes to support multiple divergence measures for weight estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic IW (DIW) has shown promise for joint distribution shift in deep learning but has limitations: substantial computational overhead from solving optimization problems to convergence every mini-batch, and reliance only on KMM-based estimation despite diverse divergence-based methods existing in the IW literature.

Method: ADIW proposes: 1) Few lightweight projected gradient descent updates that warm-start from previously updated weights for efficiency; 2) A unified divergence-minimization framework supporting diverse weight-estimation methods (Kullback-Leibler divergence, squared distance, Wasserstein-1 distance) in a plug-and-play manner.

Result: ADIW achieves state-of-the-art IW performance while being substantially more efficient than previous methods, with established convergence guarantees under mild conditions.

Conclusion: ADIW provides a unified and efficient importance weighting framework for deep learning under joint distribution shift, addressing both computational efficiency and methodological flexibility limitations of previous DIW approaches.

Abstract: Importance weighting (IW) is a golden solver for joint distribution shift, where the joint distributions differ between the training and test data. To solve this problem, IW estimates test-to-training density ratios as importance weights and reweights the training losses accordingly. Recent advances in dynamic IW (DIW) integrate weight estimation into model training, enabling scalable IW for deep models and achieving strong performance on large modern datasets. Despite its promise, DIW remains limited in two aspects. First, it incurs substantial computational overhead by solving a kernel mean matching (KMM)-induced optimization problem to convergence in every mini-batch. Second, it relies solely on KMM for weight estimation, whereas the IW literature contains diverse estimation methods based on different divergence measures. In this paper, we propose accelerated DIW (ADIW), a unified and efficient IW framework for deep learning under joint distribution shift. ADIW performs a few lightweight projected gradient descent updates that warm-start from previously updated weights, substantially improving efficiency. Moreover, ADIW generalizes DIW into a unified divergence-minimization framework that supports diverse weight-estimation methods in a plug-and-play manner, including those based on the Kullback-Leibler divergence, squared distance, and Wasserstein-1 distance. We establish convergence guarantees for ADIW under mild conditions, and empirical results demonstrate that ADIW achieves state-of-the-art IW performance while being substantially more efficient.

</details>


### [339] [Relative Repairability: A Calibration-Based Diagnostic for High-Sparsity Post-Pruning Allocation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25508)
*Qishi Zhan,Liang He,Minxuan Hu,Ziheng Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Relative Repairability (RR), a diagnostic metric for high-sparsity neural network pruning that measures how much pruning damage can be recovered through lightweight repair procedures, helping optimize sparsity allocation across layers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: At high sparsity levels, pruning does more than just select which weights to keep - it determines where pruning-induced damage is placed across the network and whether that damage can be recovered through repair. Current pruning allocation methods don't consider repairability, which becomes crucial when standard methods lose reliability at extreme sparsity.

Method: The paper introduces Relative Repairability (RR), a calibration-based diagnostic that compares raw activation distortion from layerwise pruning with residual distortion after channelwise variance matching repair. RR estimates the fraction of local damage that remains after repair using only unlabeled calibration data. The method is evaluated on ResNet18, ResNet34, and VGG16 BN architectures on CIFAR10 and CIFAR100 datasets, comparing against existing methods like ERK and LAMP.

Result: RR is not universally dominant but is most useful near architecture-dependent recoverability transitions where standard allocation methods begin to fail but post-repair recovery hasn't fully collapsed. On CIFAR100 ResNet18, RR improves over ERK across the central transition band and surpasses LAMP near the upper part of this band. The paper also shows that capped ERK can over-protect projection layers, shifting excessive sparsity onto regular convolutions and reducing post-repair recovery.

Conclusion: High-sparsity pruning should consider not only which weights to retain but also how repairable the resulting damage will be. The Relative Repairability metric provides a useful diagnostic tool for optimizing sparsity allocation when operating near the limits of network recoverability through repair procedures.

Abstract: At very high sparsity, neural network pruning does more than decide which weights remain. It also determines where pruning induced damage is placed across the network, and whether that damage can be recovered by a fixed lightweight repair procedure. We study this problem through the lens of repair conditioned sparsity allocation. We introduce Relative Repairability (RR), a calibration based diagnostic that compares the raw activation distortion caused by layerwise pruning with the residual distortion left after channelwise variance matching repair. RR estimates the fraction of local damage that remains after repair, using only unlabeled calibration data. Across ResNet18, ResNet34, and VGG16 BN on CIFAR10 and CIFAR100, we find that RR is not a universally dominant allocation rule. Instead, it is most useful near an architecture dependent recoverability transition, where standard structural or magnitude based allocation priors begin to lose reliability but post repair recovery has not yet fully collapsed. On CIFAR100 ResNet18, a fine grained sweep shows that RR improves over ERK across the central transition band and surpasses LAMP near the upper part of this band. A projection forced ablation further shows that capped ERK can over protect projection layers, shifting excessive sparsity onto regular convolutions and reducing post repair recovery. These results suggest that high sparsity pruning should allocate not only retained weights, but also repairable damage.

</details>


### [340] [SAE-FD: Sparse Autoencoder Feature Distillation for Continual Learning of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25525)
*Mingxu Zhang,Yuhan Li,Lujundong Li,Dazhong Shen,Hui Xiong,Ying Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAFE-D uses sparse autoencoder feature distillation to address catastrophic forgetting in continual learning by anchoring representations in sparse feature space for more targeted regularization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Regularization-based continual learning methods suffer from feature superposition in dense representation spaces where multiple concepts overlap in the same dimensions, making it difficult to selectively protect previously learned knowledge without hindering new-task learning.

Method: Proposes Sparse Autoencoder Feature Distillation (SAFE-D), which anchors model representations in the sparse feature space of a pre-trained Sparse Autoencoder, where dense activations are decomposed into a sparse overcomplete basis that reduces representational entanglement.

Result: Experiments on two continual learning benchmarks across three model architectures show SAFE-D consistently outperforms existing regularization-based methods, achieving up to 52.70% average accuracy with only -0.46 backward transfer.

Conclusion: Sparse feature spaces from autoencoders provide a more effective basis for regularization in continual learning by reducing feature entanglement and enabling more targeted protection of learned knowledge with less interference to new-task learning.

Abstract: Continual learning enables large language models to adapt to evolving tasks without retraining from scratch, yet catastrophic forgetting remains a central obstacle. Among continual learning methods, regularization-based approaches are widely used to constrain model updates and reduce forgetting, operating in weight space, gradient space, or output space. However, these dense representation spaces suffer from feature superposition, where multiple concepts are encoded in overlapping dimensions, making it difficult to selectively protect previously learned knowledge without impeding new-task learning. To address this issue, we propose \method (Sparse Autoencoder Feature Distillation), which anchors model representations in the sparse feature space of a pre-trained Sparse Autoencoder, where dense activations are decomposed into a sparse overcomplete basis that reduces representational entanglement, enabling more targeted regularization with less interference to new-task learning. Experiments on two continual learning benchmarks across three model architectures show that \method consistently outperforms existing regularization-based methods, achieving up to 52.70% average accuracy with only -0.46 backward transfer.

</details>


### [341] [DeepSeekMath Meets Order Book: Group-Aware Policy Optimization for High-Frequency Directional Trading](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25527)
*Sayak Charabarty,Souradip Pal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper applies policy-gradient RL methods (PPO variants) to high-frequency trading using order-flow states, showing they outperform traditional Q-learning baselines in profitability and risk metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to improve high-frequency trading on limit order books by moving beyond traditional value-based RL methods like Q-learning, which may not adequately handle the complexities of order-flow dynamics and risk in financial markets.

Method: The method pairs order-flow-based state modeling with policy-gradient methods, specifically vanilla PPO and novel variants like GRPO and GSPO that incorporate group-normalized updates and downside-aware reward shaping. These are tested against Q-learning baselines in backtests.

Result: The new policy-based methods improve net average PnL, profitability, and drawdown metrics over Q-learning baselines when tested on AMZN, AAPL, and GOOG assets using spread-scaled rewards in simplified backtesting setups.

Conclusion: Two main conclusions: (1) Order-flow signals provide adequate state representations for policy RL in trading, and (2) group-aware PPO surrogates (like GRPO/GSPO) are preferable to value-based baselines for high-frequency trading applications.

Abstract: This paper studies reinforcement learning for high-frequency trading on limit order books by pairing an Order-Flow-based state model with policy-gradient methods. Instead of value-based RL techniques like tabular Q-learning, our approach deploys policy-based methods like vanilla PPO and DeepSeekMath-inspired variants like GRPO and GSPO, that use group-normalized updates and downside-aware shaping. On backtests with financial assets AMZN, AAPL, and GOOG under a simplified backtesting setup based on spread-scaled rewards, these new policies improve net average PnL, profitability, and drawdown over the Q-Learning baseline. Our results show that (1) Order-Flow signals are an adequate state for policy RL and (2) group-aware PPO surrogates are preferable over value-based baselines.

</details>


### [342] ['Si'multaneous 'S'patial-'T'emporal Message Passing for Dynamic Graph Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25548)
*Shubhajit Roy,Anirban Dasgupta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SiST-GNN introduces a simultaneous spatial-temporal graph neural network that fuses spatial and temporal signals in a single message-passing operation rather than chaining them sequentially, achieving state-of-the-art performance across multiple dynamic graph benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current dynamic graph neural networks (DGNNs) fall into two rigid categories: temporal-first (building temporal embeddings then spatial aggregation) or spatial-first (graph convolution then temporal module). This sequential approach prevents joint reasoning over topology and evolution, as message-passing operators cannot weight neighbor contributions by their past trajectories.

Method: SiST-GNN maintains recurrent hidden states per node to summarize history, pairs them with current feature vectors, and treats each pair as two nodes joined by a cross-time edge. Running standard graph convolution on this temporally augmented graph allows simultaneous processing of spatial and temporal signals within a single message-passing operation.

Result: Extensive evaluation across 9 baselines and 14 model-dataset combinations shows SiST-GNN sets new state-of-the-art: 109-277% improvement over strongest prior methods in fixed-split link prediction, 68-194% improvement in live-update setting. On dynamic node classification tasks, it beats discrete-time baselines by 7-22% and matches continuous-time methods using raw event streams.

Conclusion: The SiST-GNN framework successfully overcomes limitations of sequential spatial-temporal processing by enabling simultaneous joint reasoning, achieving significant performance gains across various dynamic graph tasks and outperforming both sequential and continuous-time approaches.

Abstract: Dynamic graph neural networks (DGNNs) that operate on snapshot sequences typically fall into one of two categories. \emph{Temporal-first} approaches build per-node temporal embeddings and only afterwards perform spatial aggregation, whereas \emph{Spatial-first} approaches invert this order, feeding the output of a graph convolution into a downstream temporal module. In either case, the rigid sequencing forces the second stage to consume an already-compressed summary produced by the first, ruling out joint reasoning over topology and evolution; concretely, the message-passing operator never gets to weight a neighbor's contribution by that neighbor's \emph{past} trajectory. This paper introduces \textbf{SiST-GNN} (\textbf{Si}multaneous \textbf{S}patial-\textbf{T}emporal \textbf{GNN}), which fuses the two signals inside a single message-passing operation rather than chaining them. Concretely, at each snapshot we maintain a recurrent hidden state per node that summarises its history, pair it with the node's current feature vector, and treat the pair as two nodes joined by a cross-time edge; running a standard graph convolution on this temporally augmented graph yields the updated representation. Our empirical study spans nine public baselines and fourteen model-dataset combinations, covering both fixed-split and live-update evaluation regimes. Across every public benchmark, SiST-GNN sets a new state of the art in link prediction task over the strongest prior method by $109$--$277\%$ in the fixed-split setting and by $68$--$194\%$ in the live-update setting. We additionally construct three dynamic node-classification tasks by discretising the underlying continuous-time event streams; here SiST-GNN beats the leading discrete-time (DTDG) baseline by $7$--$22\%$ and matches continuous-time (CTDG) methods that consume the raw events directly.

</details>


### [343] [Learning Permutation from Structure Without Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25551)
*Ran Eisenberg,Ofir Lindenbaum*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive entropy-controlled Gumbel-Sinkhorn enables selective sharpening of confident permutation assignments while preserving exploration for uncertain ones, improving stability and quality in unsupervised permutation learning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning permutations without supervision leads to non-uniform uncertainty - some assignments become confident early while others remain ambiguous. Existing methods use a single global temperature that forces all assignments to sharpen or diffuse simultaneously, causing instability at scale.

Method: Proposes an entropy-adaptive formulation of Gumbel-Sinkhorn that locally modulates temperature based on assignment uncertainty, allowing confident assignments to discretize early while preserving exploration where uncertainty remains.

Result: Adaptive entropy control improves training stability and final permutation quality across sorting, jigsaw reconstruction, and routing-style tasks, particularly as problem size and assignment ambiguity increase.

Conclusion: Local modulation of temperature based on assignment uncertainty in differentiable permutation learning leads to more stable training and better results than fixed-temperature approaches, especially for larger and more ambiguous problems.

Abstract: Many learning problems require uncovering a hidden ordering that reveals structure in unordered data, such as monotonicity in sorting or spatial continuity in jigsaw reconstruction. In these settings, permutations can be learned as latent operators by optimizing objectives defined directly on the reordered output, often without access to ground-truth orderings. Differentiable relaxations such as Gumbel-Sinkhorn make this approach practical by approximating permutation matrices with doubly stochastic matrices. However, learning from structure without supervision induces a non-uniform uncertainty: some assignments become confident early, while others remain ambiguous. Existing methods control this process using a single global temperature, forcing all assignments to sharpen or diffuse simultaneously and leading to instability at scale. We introduce an entropy-adaptive formulation of Gumbel-Sinkhorn that locally modulates temperature based on assignment uncertainty. This allows confident assignments to discretize early while preserving exploration where uncertainty remains. Across sorting and jigsaw reconstruction tasks and in routing-style settings, adaptive entropy control improves training stability and final permutation quality relative to fixed-temperature baselines, particularly as problem size and assignment ambiguity increase.

</details>


### [344] [RotMoLE: Enhancing Mixture of Low-Rank Experts through Rotational Gating Mechanism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25565)
*Mengyang Sun,Maochuan Dou,Tao Feng,Dan Zhang,Yihao Wang,Junpeng Liu,Yifan Zhu,Jie Tang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RotMoLE enhances MoE-LoRA with rotation gates for low-rank adapters, enabling better expert specialization beyond simple scalar reweighing, particularly when expert candidates are limited.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional MoE gating mechanisms apply only scalar reweighing to selected experts, limiting their representation and generalization capacity, especially when adapting LLMs to complex scenarios with diverse specialized knowledge.

Method: Proposes RotMoLE, a specialized MoE framework for low-rank experts featuring an additional rotation gate that implements rotation mechanisms (beyond simple scaling) for each selected expert to enable superior expert exploitation and specialization.

Result: Empirical results on complex multi-task and multilingual training scenarios validate the effectiveness of RotMoLE in enhancing expert specialization for learning diverse data.

Conclusion: RotMoLE provides a superior framework for MoE-LoRA that goes beyond conventional scalar gating mechanisms, enabling better adaptation of LLMs to complex scenarios with diverse specialized knowledge through rotation-based expert exploitation.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) are commonly fine-tuned to handle domain-specific tasks before being applied to vertical applications, adapting them to complex scenarios with diverse specialized knowledge remains challenging. Meanwhile, Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture has risen as a crucial paradigm for training LLMs, and some recent works have also incorporated MoE into Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) to propose the Mixture of Low-rank Experts (MoE-LoRA), to enhance the power of low-rank adapters for learning complicated knowledge. However, conventional gating mechanisms in MoE typically apply only a scalar reweighing to selected experts, thereby limiting their underlying capacity of representation and generalization. Motivated and enabled by the low-rank structures in MoE-LoRA, we propose RotMoLE, a specialized MoE framework for low-rank experts featuring an additional rotation gate. Beyond simple scaling, RotMoLE implements a rotation mechanism for each selected expert, enabling superior expert exploitation and specialization for learning diverse data, especially when expert candidates are limited. Empirical results on complex multi-task and multilingual training scenarios validate our effectiveness.

</details>


### [345] [Geometric Flow Matching for Molecular Conformation Generation via Manifold Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25577)
*Yunqing Liu,Yi Zhou,Wenqi Fan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GO-Flow is a geometric-aware generative model that decomposes molecular conformation generation into three physically motivated subspaces (translation, rotation, and conformation) to align with molecular degrees of freedom, achieving state-of-the-art performance with efficient sampling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion/flow matching models treat molecules as unstructured point clouds in Cartesian space, ignoring the hierarchical mechanics where bond lengths/angles are stiff while torsion angles are flexible. This leads to physically implausible structures and forces models to relearn fundamental geometric constraints.

Method: GO-Flow decomposes generation into three subspaces: translation space with linear optimal transport, rotation space with geodesic flows on SO(3), and conformation space with entropic optimal transport. This injects geometric inductive biases and aligns with molecular degrees of freedom when combined with equivariant neural architectures.

Result: Extensive experiments on GEOM-Drugs and GEOM-QM9 demonstrate state-of-the-art generation quality. The method enables high-fidelity sampling with as few as 50 steps by learning straighter probability paths on the correct manifolds naturally.

Conclusion: GO-Flow effectively bridges the gap between structural precision and computational efficiency by aligning generative modeling with molecular geometry through manifold decomposition, overcoming limitations of existing approaches that treat molecules as unstructured point clouds.

Abstract: The generation of accurate 3D molecular conformations is a pivotal challenge in computational chemistry and drug discovery. Recently, diffusion and flow matching models have achieved remarkable success. However, there is a critical misalignment between their mathematical formulation and the physical reality of molecules. Existing approaches predominantly treat molecules as unstructured point clouds in Cartesian space, overlooking the intrinsic hierarchical mechanics where bond lengths and bond angles are relatively stiff, whereas torsion angles constitute the dominant flexible degrees of freedom. This lack of manifold awareness forces models to relearn fundamental geometric constraints from scratch, often leading to physically implausible intermediate structures. To address this, we propose GO-Flow that aligns generative modeling with molecular geometry via manifold decomposition. Instead of forcing motion through Euclidean space, GO-Flow decomposes the generation process into three physically motivated subspaces: translation space with linear optimal transport, rotation space with geodesic flows on $SO(3)$, and conformation space with entropic optimal transport. This decomposition injects geometric inductive biases and makes the generative paths better aligned with molecular degrees of freedom. When combined with equivariant neural architectures, it encourages rotation-consistent generation and improves geometric validity. Extensive experiments on GEOM-Drugs and GEOM-QM9 demonstrate that GO-Flow achieves state-of-the-art generation quality. Notably, by learning straighter probability paths on the correct manifolds naturally, our method enables high-fidelity sampling with as few as 50 steps, effectively bridging the gap between structural precision and computational efficiency.

</details>


### [346] [Learning Latent Dynamical Causal Processes for Single-Cell Perturbation Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25581)
*Wenkang Jiang,Yuhang Liu,Erdun Gao,Ehsan Abbasnejad,Lina Yao,Javen Qinfeng Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CITE-VAE is a latent dynamical causal generative model that captures both the latent cellular programs and their perturbation-driven temporal evolution from single-cell sequencing data, enabling improved generalization to unseen perturbations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for single-cell perturbation prediction capture only partial aspects: latent causal approaches treat effects as static, while temporal models don't recover the underlying causal mechanisms. In reality, perturbation effects are both latent (through unobserved cellular programs) and dynamical (evolving over time).

Method: Proposes a latent dynamical causal generative model that jointly captures: 1) latent cellular programs, 2) perturbation-conditioned mechanisms, and 3) temporal evolution. Provides identifiability analysis showing latent causal variables are recoverable up to standard equivalence classes. Develops CITE-VAE learning framework guided by this analysis.

Result: Causal-3DIdent experiments validate theoretical results in controlled settings. Real-world CRISPR-based single-cell perturbation data shows improved generalization to unseen perturbations compared with state-of-the-art baselines, demonstrating practical robustness.

Conclusion: The proposed latent dynamical causal approach effectively captures both the latent and temporal aspects of perturbation effects, enabling better out-of-distribution generalization and providing a more complete computational route to understanding how interventions reshape cellular programs over time.

Abstract: Single-cell perturbation prediction aims to infer how cells respond to unseen interventions and to achieve out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization, providing a computational route to understanding how perturbations reshape cellular programs over time. Existing machine learning methods have made important progress, but typically capture only one side of the response. Latent causal approaches seek mechanisms that support generalization and interpretation, yet often treat perturbation effects as static outcomes. Temporal models describe how gene expression changes across time, but usually do not explicitly recover the latent causal generative mechanisms driving these changes. In practice, perturbation effects are both latent and dynamical: interventions act through unobserved cellular programs, whose states evolve over time and give rise to observed expression profiles. Motivated by this view, we propose a latent dynamical causal generative model for single-cell perturbation data that jointly captures latent cellular programs, perturbation-conditioned mechanisms, and temporal evolution. We further provide an identifiability analysis showing that, under suitable conditions, the latent causal variables are recoverable up to standard equivalence classes. Guided by this analysis, we develop CITE-VAE, a learning framework for recovering latent cellular programs and their perturbation-driven dynamics from single-cell sequencing data. Experiments on Causal-3DIdent validate the theoretical results and the effectiveness of the proposed method in controlled settings. Additional experiments on real-world CRISPR-based single-cell perturbation data show improved generalization to unseen perturbations compared with state-of-the-art baselines, highlighting the practical robustness of our approach.

</details>


### [347] [Extreme Region Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25582)
*Changyu Chen,Xiting Wang,Rui Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ERPD decouples sample efficiency from KL efficiency by using aggressive off-policy optimization to extract training signals, then distilling them into the base policy under trust-region constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL for LLMs faces a trade-off between sample efficiency (on-policy discards data after single update) and asymptotic performance (off-policy reuse causes distribution mismatch). Current trust-region methods enforce conservative optimization that underutilizes rich training signals.

Method: Two-stage Extreme Region Policy Distillation: 1) Weakly constrained off-policy optimization on fixed data to maximally extract training signals, creating token-level supervision. 2) Distill these signals into base policy under trust-region constraints, filtering harmful drift while preserving useful signals.

Result: ERPD achieves comparable or better performance with substantially smaller KL divergence than aggressive off-policy methods. Works with both strong and weak teachers - even degenerate teachers provide effective supervision via alternative signal construction. Validated on mathematical reasoning, showing gains for strong base models where on-policy training plateaus.

Conclusion: ERPD successfully decouples sample efficiency from KL efficiency by separating aggressive signal extraction from safe distillation, enabling better utilization of training data while avoiding performance degradation from distribution drift.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning for large language models faces a fundamental trade-off between sample efficiency and asymptotic performance: strictly on-policy methods discard trajectories after a single update, while off-policy reuse introduces distribution mismatch that existing trust-region techniques mitigate primarily by enforcing conservative optimization, often leaving rich training signals underutilized. To investigate this, we perform extensive off-policy updates on fixed data. Our experiments reveal that aggressive multi-step optimization brings rapid initial gains, but excessive updates cause trajectory probabilities to deviate and entropy to collapse, with performance plateauing early. Tightening KL constraints merely lowers the ceiling without resolving the degradation. This motivates Extreme Region Policy Distillation (ERPD), a two-stage framework that decouples sample efficiency from KL efficiency. The first stage performs weakly constrained off-policy optimization on fixed data to maximally extract training signals. The resulting policy provides token-level supervision. In the second stage, we distill these signals into the base policy under trust-region constraints, filtering harmful drift while preserving useful signals. The distilled policy achieves comparable or better performance with substantially smaller KL divergence, indicating that much of the first-stage divergence was spent on unnecessary drift rather than genuine improvement. Crucially, ERPD accommodates both strong and weak teachers: when aggressive optimization yields no stronger policy, even degenerate teachers provide effective supervision via alternative signal construction strategies. We validate ERPD on mathematical reasoning, showing gains for strong base models where on-policy training plateaus, and reliable improvements with weak teachers.

</details>


### [348] [Generalized Evidential Deep Learning: From a Bayesian Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25599)
*Yuanye Liu,Yibo Gao,Yuanyang Chen,Xiahai Zhuang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EDL gets a Bayesian makeover: This paper provides theoretical foundations for Evidential Deep Learning by interpreting it through a generalized Bayesian framework, proposes GEDL as a unified framework that relates to existing variants, and validates it experimentally.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) and its variants have achieved success but lack systematic theoretical investigation of their underlying structure and relationships. There's a need to establish principled theoretical foundations and understand the connections between different EDL approaches.

Method: 1. Interpret EDL within a generalized Bayesian framework covering prior specification, posterior update, and training objectives. 2. Characterize evidential uncertainty via Bayesian distributional uncertainty through asymptotic analysis. 3. Propose Generalized Evidential Deep Learning (GEDL) as a unified framework that disentangles component roles and systematically relates to existing variants.

Result: GEDL yields comparable results on classification, uncertainty estimation, and out-of-distribution (OOD) detection tasks, while providing theoretical grounding that was previously missing from EDL approaches.

Conclusion: The paper successfully establishes theoretical foundations for EDL through Bayesian interpretation, proposes a unified GEDL framework that systematically relates to existing variants, and demonstrates practical effectiveness across multiple tasks with theoretical grounding.

Abstract: Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) has emerged as an efficient, sampling-free strategy for uncertainty estimation. A series of EDL variants have been proposed to address specific limitations of the original framework, achieving notable success. However, the underlying theoretical structure of EDL and the relationships among these variants have received limited systematic investigation. In this work, we establish a principled theoretical foundation for EDL by interpreting it within a generalized Bayesian framework that includes prior specification, posterior update, and training objective. We further characterize evidential uncertainty from a Bayesian distributional uncertainty viewpoint, established via asymptotic analysis. Building on this perspective, we further propose Generalized Evidential Deep Learning (GEDL), a unified and extensible framework that explicitly disentangles the roles of individual components and systematically relates GEDL to existing variants. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GEDL yields comparable results on classification, uncertainty estimation and OOD detections, with theoretical grounding.

</details>


### [349] [Towards the Connection between Activation Sparsity and Flat Minima](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25612)
*Ze Peng,Jian Zhang,Lei Qi,Yang Gao,Yinghuan Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper shows that MLP activation sparsity in Transformers relates to loss landscape flatness, proposes derivative sparsity for more stable pruning, and introduces methods to increase sparsity for computational efficiency gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Activation sparsity in MLP blocks of Transformers offers computational efficiency benefits, but existing theoretical explanations rely on strong assumptions not applicable to standard deep networks trained for many steps.

Method: Establishes connection between MLP activation sparsity and loss landscape flatness via a ratio formula. Proposes derivative sparsity concept for backward propagation pruning. Introduces three plug-and-play methods to decrease the ratio (increase sparsity).

Result: Experiments on ImageNet-1K and C4 show 36%+ improvement on inference sparsity and 50%+ improvement on training sparsity over vanilla Transformers, enabling significant computation cost reduction.

Conclusion: Loss landscape flatness provides a natural theoretical framework for understanding activation sparsity in deep Transformers, and the proposed methods effectively increase sparsity for both inference and training efficiency.

Abstract: The observation that activation sparsity emerges in MLP blocks of standardly trained Transformers offers an opportunity to drastically reduce computation costs without sacrificing performance. To theoretically explain this phenomenon, existing works have shown that activation sparsity does not result from the data properties or data fitting but from the implicit bias of the training process. However, these connections are obtained with strong assumptions, which cannot be applied to deep models standardly trained with a large number of steps. Different from these works, we find that the flatness of loss landscapes is also closely related to the MLP activation sparsity and can serve as a weaker and naturally emerging assumption standard deep networks. Specifically, we find that 1) the MLP activation sparsity equals a ratio between "augmented flatness" (a weighted sum of flatness measures) and the product of the input norm and activation gradient of the MLP. We empirically find that this ratio decreases during training, leading to sparse activations. 2) We also propose the notion of derivative sparsity, which reduces to activation sparsity under ReLU, but further enables pruning in the backward propagation and is more stable than activation sparsity. With the theoretical findings, we can further encourage activation sparsity by decreasing the numerator and increasing the denominator of the ratio using three methods. These plug-and-play modifications can effectively reduce the ratio and produce sparser activations. Experiments on ImageNet-1K and C4 demonstrate relative improvements of at least 36% on inference sparsity and at least 50% on training sparsity over vanilla Transformers, indicating further potential cost reduction in both inference and training

</details>


### [350] [Courtroom Analogy: New Perspective on Uncertainty-Aware Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25616)
*Taeseong Yoon,Heeyoung Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MoDEX is a single-pass neural architecture for classification uncertainty quantification that models uncertainty aggregation as a structured debate among class-specific advocates, producing interpretable uncertainty estimates with state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing single-pass uncertainty quantification methods for classification focus mainly on distribution expressiveness but provide limited insight into how predictive uncertainty is structured and aggregated, resulting in weak interpretability of uncertainty estimates.

Method: Introduces the courtroom analogy where each class acts as an advocate forming a probabilistic opinion.
