Proposes Mixture of Dirichlet EXperts (MoDEX), a neural architecture that predicts Dirichlet distributions for each class, with concentration parameters decomposed into shared evidence and class-specific advocacy, creating a semantically interpretable mixture model.

Result: MoDEX demonstrates strong theoretical properties and achieves state-of-the-art uncertainty quantification performance across diverse benchmarks, producing interpretable uncertainty estimates with meaningful semantic structure.

Conclusion: The courtroom framework provides a novel, interpretable approach to uncertainty quantification that explicitly models uncertainty aggregation, and MoDEX effectively implements this framework while maintaining efficiency and achieving superior performance.

Abstract: Single-pass uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods for classification represent uncertainty by predicting a tractable distribution over the class probability vector. While existing approaches primarily focus on enhancing the expressiveness of this distribution, they often provide limited insight into how predictive uncertainty is structured and aggregated, resulting in weak interpretability. We introduce the courtroom analogy, which conceptualizes uncertainty-aware classification as a structured debate among class-specific advocates. Each advocate forms a probabilistic opinion, and a final verdict is reached by aggregating these opinions using input-dependent plausibility weights. In this framework, each advocate's opinion is modeled as a Dirichlet distribution whose concentration parameter is decomposed into shared evidence and class-specific advocacy. This yields a structured mixture of Dirichlet distributions with semantically interpretable parameters. To instantiate this formulation, we propose Mixture of Dirichlet EXperts (MoDEX), a single-pass neural architecture that predicts the courtroom parameters, enabling efficient and expressive UQ while explicitly modeling uncertainty aggregation. We demonstrate that MoDEX enjoys strong theoretical properties and achieves state-of-the-art UQ performance across diverse benchmarks, yielding interpretable uncertainty estimates with meaningful semantics.

</details>


### [351] [Analogies between Transformer Layers and Power Method](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25619)
*Chenglong Li,Claudio Altafini*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformers have an analogy with the power method where each layer aligns tokens toward the principal eigenvector of the weight matrix product.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the mathematical operations in transformer layers by drawing an analogy with the power method, which is a fundamental numerical algorithm for computing eigenvectors.

Method: The paper shows an analogy between transformer layer operations (projections and layer normalizations) and steps in the power method, analyzing how tokens evolve through layers toward the principal eigenvector of the product of output and value weight matrices.

Result: In transformers, tokens become increasingly aligned with the principal eigenvector of the weight matrix product through each layer. This alignment is particularly evident and analytically provable for transformers with shared weights across all layers.

Conclusion: The power method analogy provides insight into transformer behavior and suggests a method for steering transformer outputs toward desired directions in token space by manipulating this eigenvector alignment.

Abstract: In the paper we show that there is an analogy between the operations occurring in a layer of a transformer (projections and layer normalizations, disregarding the feedforward neural network) and a step in the power method. Coherently with this analogy, we show that passing through a layer the tokens tend to be tilted towards the principal eigenvector of a matrix which is the product of the output and value weight matrices of that layer. In the special case of a transformer with shared weights (i.e., in which all layers have identical weights) then the alignment with this principal eigenvector is particularly evident empirically, and can also be shown analytically. The analogy also suggests a method to steer the output of the transformer towards an arbitrary desired direction in token space.

</details>


### [352] [Closed-Form Node Classification with Exact Graph Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25662)
*Aditya Gaur,Charu Sharma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Routed closed-form solvers for node classification match or beat gradient-based GNNs across benchmarks, enabling exact graph unlearning with massive speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite gradient-based GNNs achieving strong performance, the authors ask how much performance can be recovered by deterministic closed-form solvers and what guarantees this enables, particularly for enabling exact unlearning and localization in graph modifications.

Method: Proposes a routed closed-form framework based on adjusted homophily: for assortative graphs, uses SGC-style propagation with Ridge regression; for heterophilous graphs, introduces LCF-Net, a layer-wise closed-form graph feature-refinement network with Gaussian kernel-Ridge head. Solves deterministic linear systems rather than gradient descent.

Result: Matches or beats best vanilla 2-layer GCN/SAGE/GAT on 9/9 datasets, ties tuned deep recipes within one standard deviation on 9/12 small benchmarks, exceeds OGB-leaderboard plain GCN on large graphs. Enables exact graph unlearning with 21-45× speedups over re-solving and ~10^6× speedups over gradient retraining.

Conclusion: Closed-form solvers can recover most performance of gradient-based GNNs while enabling strong theoretical guarantees for exact unlearning, localization, and privacy quantification that gradient methods cannot provide.

Abstract: Graph neural networks for node classification are typically trained by gradient descent over hundreds or thousands of epochs. Recent work has shown that, when properly tuned, classic GCN/SAGE/GAT architectures can match graph transformers on many node-classification benchmarks. We ask a complementary question: how much of this performance can be recovered by deterministic closed-form solvers, and what guarantees does this enable?
  We introduce a routed closed-form framework selected by adjusted homophily. For assortative graphs, we use SGC-style propagation followed by Ridge regression; for heterophilous graphs, we introduce LCF-Net, a layer-wise closed-form graph feature-refinement network whose per-layer Ridge solves are capped by a Gaussian kernel-Ridge head. Across 14 benchmarks, including ogbn-arxiv and ogbn-proteins, our closed-form predictors match or beat the best vanilla 2-layer GCN/SAGE/GAT on 9 of 9 measured datasets, tie tuned deep recipes within one standard deviation on 9 of 12 small benchmarks, and exceed the OGB-leaderboard plain GCN on both large graphs. The remaining heterophilous gap closely tracks the gain from vanilla 2-layer to deep SAGE, suggesting that the residual difference is primarily architectural.
  Because our predictors are explicit solutions of deterministic linear systems, modified graph inputs can be re-solved to obtain retrain-equivalent parameters. We formalize exact graph-object unlearning for label, feature, edge, node, and subgraph modifications, prove K-hop locality for Ridge components, and verify exactness across 109 configurations. On ogbn-arxiv, localized updates give $21$--$45\times$ speedups over full re-solving and roughly $10^{6}\times$ speedups over gradient retraining. Structural-inversion experiments further quantify the privacy floor of exact retraining and the additional leakage of approximate graph-unlearning methods.

</details>


### [353] [Opportunistic Target Selection: Early Directional Commitment for Query-Efficient Black-Box Adversarial Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25663)
*Florent Tariolle,Florian Yger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OTS is a lightweight wrapper that converts untargeted black-box attacks to targeted attacks by opportunistically selecting a non-true class that's currently leading, improving attack efficiency without gradient access or target knowledge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Untargeted black-box attacks suffer from class drift - perturbations wander without committing to a specific adversarial class, wasting queries on undirected progress. This inefficiency motivates the need for a method to lock onto a target class early without requiring prior target knowledge or architectural changes.

Method: Opportunistic Target Selection (OTS) - a lightweight wrapper that switches untargeted attacks to targeted objectives early in their trajectory. It locks onto whichever non-true class currently leads, requires no architectural modification to underlying attacks, no gradient access, and no a priori target-class knowledge.

Result: Validated on three score-based attacks (SimBA, Square Attack, Bandits) across five ImageNet classifiers (4,500 runs). On random-search attacks, OTS closely tracks oracle performance with gains up to +27 percentage points in success rate and 43% relative reduction in censored-mean iterations on ResNet-50. On gradient-estimation attacks and margin loss attacks, OTS is redundant, confirming it serves as a margin-loss surrogate. On adversarially-trained models, bimodal difficulty distribution eliminates benefits.

Conclusion: OTS effectively addresses class drift in untargeted black-box attacks by opportunistically selecting targets, significantly improving efficiency for random-search attacks while being unnecessary for gradient-estimation attacks where it essentially serves as a margin-loss surrogate.

Abstract: Black-box adversarial attacks that minimize only the ground-truth confidence suffer from class drift: perturbations wander through the feature space without committing to a specific adversarial class, wasting queries on diffuse, undirected progress. We introduce Opportunistic Target Selection (OTS), a lightweight wrapper that switches an untargeted attack to a targeted objective early in its trajectory, locking onto whichever non-true class currently leads. OTS requires no architectural modification to the underlying attack, no gradient access, and no a priori target-class knowledge.
  We validate OTS on three score-based attacks (SimBA, Square Attack with cross-entropy loss, and Bandits) across five standard ImageNet classifiers (4,500 runs). On random-search attacks, OTS closely tracks oracle performance, with gains up to +27 pp in success rate and 43% relative reduction in censored-mean iterations on ResNet-50. On gradient-estimation attacks (Bandits) and attacks with margin loss, OTS is redundant, a negative result that reinforces our interpretation of OTS as a margin-loss surrogate. On adversarially-trained models, a bimodal difficulty distribution eliminates the regime where targeting helps.

</details>


### [354] [Stochastic Estimation of the Layer-wise Hessian Trace for Monitoring Neural-network Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25674)
*Maxim Bolshim,Alexander Kugaevskikh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A stochastic estimator for Hessian trace per-layer enables monitoring of neural network training regimes by tracking curvature changes, revealing pathological behaviors like label memorization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Loss and gradient norms weakly distinguish healthy vs. pathological training regimes, while Hessian curvature shows qualitative differences but is computationally infeasible to compute directly for large models (P~10^6-10^8 parameters).

Method: Develops a stochastic estimator combining Hutchinson trace estimator with single Hessian-vector product to compute unbiased per-layer Hessian trace estimates in one backward pass. Handles weight sharing correctly by assembling layer-wise Hessian before differentiation. Derives variance analysis and identifies optimal probe count K*.

Result: The estimator detects label-memorization regimes in ResNet-18, ResNet-34, and VGG-11 on CIFAR-10/100 with 179/180 detection power at 16/120 false-alarm rate using calibrated cumulative-sum decision rule. Practical recommendation: K∈[5,10] probes.

Conclusion: The stochastic Hessian trace estimator provides an efficient, practical tool for monitoring neural network training regimes, enabling early detection of pathological behaviors like memorization through curvature analysis without prohibitive computational cost.

Abstract: The loss and the norm of its gradient separate the healthy and the pathological regimes of neural-network training only weakly, whilst the curvature of the empirical risk differs qualitatively between them but is inaccessible explicitly at parameter counts $P\sim 10^{6}-10^{8}$. We present a stochastic estimator of the trace of the diagonal blocks of the Hessian matrix of the empirical risk of a neural network. The procedure combines the Hutchinson stochastic trace estimator with a single Hessian-vector product over the whole parameter vector and recovers unbiased estimates of every per-layer trace in one backward pass through the computational graph. We show that correctness under weight sharing requires the layer-wise Hessian to be assembled before the second differentiation: unrolling shared weights into independent coordinates introduces a systematic bias whose sign and magnitude are governed by the cross-instance blocks of the unrolled Hessian. A closed-form expression for the variance of the estimator at a fixed Hessian is derived, together with a decomposition of the total variance under the mini-batch sampling distribution. This decomposition yields a critical probe count $K^{\star}$ that balances the two sources of randomness and supports the practical recommendation $K\in[5,10]$ in the on-line monitoring regime. The estimator is applied to the detection of the label-memorisation regime of ResNet-18, ResNet-34, and VGG-11 on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, where a calibrated cumulative-sum decision rule attains an empirical detection power of $179/180$ at a false-alarm rate of $16/120$.

</details>


### [355] [Don't Retrain, Just Reuse: Recovering Dual-Target Molecules from Single-Target Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25681)
*Qingyuan Zeng,Pengxiang Cai,Zixin Guan,Ziyang Chen,Anglin Liu,Lang Qin,Xinyao Lai,Jintai Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: REUSE is a hierarchical evolutionary input-space search framework that generates dual-target drug molecules without modifying pretrained single-target diffusion models, achieving better dual-target affinity and balance than existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dual-target drug generation is challenging because existing methods either require costly retraining of generators (which can be unstable with sparse dual-target supervision) or modify diffusion processes during sampling (which is sensitive to target balancing and competing update directions). This motivates a generator-preserving alternative that keeps pretrained single-target models intact while recovering dual-target candidates from their input space.

Method: REUSE formulates dual-target generation as a constrained multi-objective optimization problem. It uses a hierarchical evolutionary input-space search framework that combines pair-conditioned exploration with structured multi-stage selection to enforce dual-target affinity, chemical quality, and diversity. The key innovation is working with frozen single-target diffusion models without modifying their parameters or denoising dynamics.

Result: Compared to methods that modify the diffusion process, REUSE consistently improves dual-target affinity and balance. It achieves a 20.9-percentage-point gain in Dual High Affinity over the strongest prior baseline while maintaining competitive molecular quality.

Conclusion: REUSE demonstrates that effective dual-target drug candidates can be recovered from the input space of frozen single-target diffusion models through evolutionary search, providing a promising generator-preserving alternative to existing dual-target generation methods that require model modification.

Abstract: Designing a single molecule that modulates two targets is a promising strategy for polypharmacology, but it remains substantially harder than standard single-target generation because one candidate must satisfy two binding requirements while preserving drug-likeness and synthesizability. Existing dual-target generative methods typically introduce dual-target capability by either retraining the generator or intervening in the diffusion process during sampling. The former can be costly and difficult to stabilize when dual-target supervision is sparse, while the latter may be sensitive to denoising-time target balancing and competing update directions. These limitations motivate a generator-preserving alternative that keeps the pretrained prior intact: can dual-target candidates instead be recovered from the input space of a frozen single-target diffusion model, without modifying its parameters or denoising dynamics? We formulate this task as a constrained multi-objective optimization problem and propose REUSE, a hierarchical evolutionary input-space search framework that combines pair-conditioned exploration with structured multi-stage selection to enforce dual-target affinity, chemical quality, and diversity. Experiments show that, compared with methods that modify the diffusion process, REUSE consistently improves dual-target affinity and balance, achieving a 20.9-percentage-point gain in Dual High Affinity over the strongest prior baseline while maintaining competitive molecular quality.

</details>


### [356] [Evaluating passing decision-making in professional football: An enhanced MPNN approach to Receiver Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25696)
*Gabriel Masella,Giuseppe Alessio D'Inverno,Max Goldsmith,Gianluigi Rozza*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A Graph Neural Network framework predicts optimal passing targets in football by modeling player interactions as dynamic graphs, achieving competitive accuracy and enabling rapid analysis of thousands of passes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Decision-making in football involves complex spatial positioning, opponent pressure, and player intent interactions that need systematic modeling to identify optimal passing targets and enhance performance analysis.

Method: Develops a Graph Neural Network framework where players are nodes with positional/contextual features, passing lines are weighted edges based on distance/angle/pressure, using Message-Passing Neural Network trained on synchronized tracking and event data from professional matches.

Result: Model achieves competitive accuracy in identifying actual chosen receivers and state-of-the-art accuracy within top-three suggestions, plus quantifies option likelihood, threat, and creativity, enabling analysis of over 1,000 passes in seconds.

Conclusion: The GNN framework effectively models football decision-making dynamics for receiver selection, providing a powerful tool for performance analysts to evaluate passing options at scale with quantified metrics.

Abstract: The process of decision-making in football is characterized by a complex interplay between spatial positioning, opponent pressure, and player intent. This work introduces a Graph Neural Network (GNN) framework designed to predict Receiver Selection, the optimal passing target, by modeling on-field interactions as dynamic graphs. Each player is represented as a node with positional and contextual features, while potential passing lines form weighted edges characterized by distance, angle, and pressure metrics. A Message-Passing Neural Network (MPNN) has been developed and trained using a combination of tracking data and event data from professional matches, synchronized through a robust pipeline based on an optimized version of the Needleman-Wunsch Algorithm. The model achieves competitive accuracy in identifying the actual chosen receiver and state-of-the-art accuracy within its top three suggestions. Our model further offers quantification of each option's likelihood, threat, and creativity, enabling performance analysts to evaluate over 1,000 passes in seconds.

</details>


### [357] [How Should LLMs Consume High-Quality Data? Optimal Data Scheduling via Quality-Aware Functional Scaling Laws](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25698)
*Zhitao Zhu,Xili Wang,Shizhe Wu,Jiawei Fu,Xiaoqing Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a theoretical framework for scheduling data quality and batch size in LLM training, revealing two regimes for high-quality data usage, and proposes Drop-Stable-Rampup scheduling that improves performance over existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-quality training data is scarce for LLMs, and there's no theoretical guidance on how to schedule its use jointly with training dynamics like batch size scheduling.

Method: Extend functional scaling laws to include data-quality dimension, solve joint data-quality and batch-size scheduling problem analytically, identify two regimes (noise-limited and signal-limited), and propose Drop-Stable-Rampup scheduling that drops batch size upon quality transition, holds it stable, then ramps up.

Result: On a 15B Mixture-of-Experts model trained on 108B tokens, Drop-Stable-Rampup improves average accuracy by +1.70 over Warmup-Stable-Decay and +2.98 over Cosine-decay, with particularly large gains on mathematical reasoning benchmarks (GSM8K +4.23, MATH +2.80).

Conclusion: High-quality data serves dual roles (signal amplifier in noise-limited regime, noise suppressor in signal-limited regime), and optimal scheduling requires adjusting batch size dynamics when transitioning to cleaner data, which existing curriculum-style pipelines miss. The proposed Drop-Stable-Rampup effectively exploits both roles.

Abstract: High-quality data is scarce in large language model (LLM) training, yet how to schedule its use jointly with training dynamics lacks theoretical guidance. We extend functional scaling laws by incorporating a data-quality dimension, and solve the joint data-quality and batch-size scheduling problem in asymptotic closed form. The solution reveals two regimes and a dual role of high-quality data. In the noise-limited regime, high-quality data should be used as a signal amplifier: lowering the batch size converts cleaner data into more signal without amplifying noise. In the signal-limited regime, it should be used as a noise suppressor: late placement reduces terminal noise without sacrificing signal accumulation. Existing curriculum-style pipelines primarily exploit the second role by placing cleaner data late, but miss the first role because conventional decay schedules reduce update intensity exactly when high-quality data becomes available. Guided by this, we propose Drop-Stable-Rampup for LLM midtraining: upon the quality transition, drop the batch size, hold it stable to accumulate signal, then ramp up to suppress terminal noise. On a 15B Mixture-of-Experts model midtrained on 108B tokens, Drop-Stable-Rampup improves average accuracy over Warmup-Stable-Decay (WSD) by +1.70 and over Cosine-decay by +2.98, with particularly large gains on mathematical reasoning benchmarks such as GSM8K (+4.23) and MATH (+2.80).

</details>


### [358] [The Behavioral Credibility Trilemma: When Calibrated Autonomy Becomes Impossible](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25739)
*Lauri Lovén,Nam Do,Hassan Mehmood,Dinesh Kumar Sah,Sasu Tarkoma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: No RL policy with confidence-gated autonomy can simultaneously achieve maximum helpfulness, optimal calibration, and full autonomy under rational oversight when tasks exceed agent competence - this is the Behavioral Credibility Trilemma, a fundamental impossibility result.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the fundamental tension in designing trustworthy AI agents with confidence-gated autonomy, where agents can decide when to act autonomously based on their confidence. The motivation is to understand whether it's possible to create RL policies that are simultaneously maximally helpful, optimally calibrated, and fully autonomous under rational oversight.

Method: The paper uses geometric impossibility proofs and formal analysis. It introduces the Behavioral Perturbation Lemma to quantify confidence inflation, proves optimal oversight rules must be non-affine, formalizes the Confidence-Gated Decision Problem, and conducts a 540-configuration Best-of-N experiment with five pre-registered hypotheses.

Result: The paper proves an unconditional impossibility: adding any non-affine autonomy incentive to a strictly proper scoring rule destroys strict properness. Agents rewarded for both calibrated confidence and autonomous action systematically inflate reported confidence on tasks below approval thresholds. Experimental results strongly confirm all five hypotheses with effect sizes d=1.10 to 5.32, showing plateau-truncated frontier geometry.

Conclusion: The Behavioral Credibility Trilemma is fundamental and unconditional - no RL policy can simultaneously achieve maximum helpfulness, optimal calibration, and full autonomy when tasks exceed reliable competence. The paper identifies two constructive resolution pathways (commitment, domain separation) and shows the impossibility is optimizer-independent across log-concave policy families.

Abstract: We prove that no reinforcement learning policy with confidence-gated autonomy can simultaneously achieve maximum helpfulness, optimal calibration, and full autonomy under rational oversight, whenever some tasks exceed the agent's reliable competence: the Behavioral Credibility Trilemma. The impossibility is geometric -- adding any non-affine autonomy incentive to a strictly proper scoring rule destroys strict properness, so an agent rewarded for both calibrated confidence and autonomous action systematically inflates its reported confidence on tasks below the principal's approval threshold. The Behavioral Perturbation Lemma quantifies the inflation (scaling as $w_A/(2 w_C)$ for the Brier score) and shows detection requires $Ω(1/Δ^2)$ observations. We prove the principal's optimal oversight rule is necessarily non-affine, making the impossibility unconditional and optimizer-independent across log-concave-density policy families. We formalize the Confidence-Gated Decision Problem, map existing methods onto the trilemma, and identify two constructive resolution pathways (commitment, domain separation). A 540-configuration Best-of-N experiment tests five pre-registered hypotheses, all strongly confirmed (effect sizes $d = 1.10$ to $5.32$), and adds a descriptive analysis of the achievable-$(H, C, A)$ surface geometry showing a plateau-truncated frontier consistent with the predicted inflation saturation.

</details>


### [359] [Latent Representation Alignment for Offline Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25740)
*Hyungkyu Kang,Byeongchan Kim,Min-hwan Oh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LAVL (Latent-Aligned Value Learning) is a new offline goal-conditioned RL algorithm that addresses value function generalization issues through latent representation and hierarchical planning, achieving state-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper identifies that erroneous generalization in goal-conditioned value functions is a fundamental bottleneck in offline GCRL, especially for long-horizon tasks. Current methods struggle with reliable value function learning from fixed datasets, leading to performance degradation in challenging scenarios.

Method: Proposes Latent-Aligned Value Learning (LAVL), which integrates latent-representation-based value generalization with hierarchical planning in a unified framework. The approach uses appropriate inductive biases in the value function to address generalization issues.

Result: Extensive experiments on OGBench show LAVL consistently outperforms existing offline GCRL methods, achieving highest performance on 20 out of 22 datasets. Notably, it demonstrates strong performance in long-horizon tasks and trajectory stitching datasets where prior methods fail significantly.

Conclusion: LAVL successfully addresses the value function generalization bottleneck in offline GCRL through its integrated approach of latent representation and hierarchical planning, making it particularly effective for long-horizon goal-reaching tasks from fixed datasets.

Abstract: Offline goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL) provides a practical framework for obtaining goal-reaching policies from fixed datasets. However, learning a reliable goal-conditioned value function in long-horizon tasks remains challenging. In this paper, we identify erroneous generalization in goal-conditioned value functions as a fundamental bottleneck, and demonstrate that appropriate inductive bias in the value function is crucial for addressing the bottleneck. Building on these findings, we propose Latent-Aligned Value Learning (LAVL), an offline GCRL algorithm that integrates latent-representation-based value generalization with hierarchical planning in a unified framework. Extensive experiments on OGBench demonstrate that LAVL consistently outperforms existing offline GCRL methods, achieving the highest performance on 20 out of 22 datasets. Notably, LAVL exhibits strong performance in long-horizon tasks and trajectory stitching datasets, where prior methods suffer significant performance degradation. Our code is available at https://github.com/oh-lab/LAVL.git.

</details>


### [360] [Invariant-Based Weight Sharing for Message Passing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25750)
*Florian Seiffarth*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ShareGNNs introduce structure-aware weight sharing based on graph invariants to enhance MPNN expressivity beyond standard models while maintaining control over complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard MPNNs have limited ability to capture structural patterns because their weights only act on features, not directly incorporating graph structure information.

Method: Proposes a structure-aware weight sharing principle where weights are indexed directly by user-chosen graph invariants (functions preserved under node permutations). This enables systematic reuse across structurally equivalent subgraphs. Instantiated as ShareGNNs with a simple encoder-decoder architecture, resulting in an MPNN with learnable adjacency and transformer-like connectivity.

Result: ShareGNNs achieve at least as strong expressivity as the discriminative power of chosen invariants, providing explicit control over model complexity. Experiments show consistent improvements over standard MPNNs, competitive expressivity beyond the 1-WL test, and scalability to large datasets across synthetic, real-world, and subgraph counting tasks.

Conclusion: ShareGNNs successfully incorporate structural information directly into weight sharing, overcoming limitations of standard MPNNs while maintaining practical efficiency and providing theoretical guarantees about expressivity.

Abstract: Message-passing neural networks (MPNNs) are a powerful framework for learning representations of graph-structured domains. However, weights in MPNNs act on features only, limiting their ability to capture structural patterns. We introduce a novel structure-aware weight sharing principle that explicitly incorporates information inherent to the graph structure. Weights are indexed directly by user-chosen graph invariants, i.e., functions preserved under node permutations, enabling systematic reuse across structurally equivalent subgraphs. We present ShareGNNs, which instantiate this principle within a simple encoder-decoder architecture, resulting in an MPNN with learnable adjacency and transformer-like connectivity. We show that their expressivity is at least as strong as the discriminative power of the chosen invariants, providing explicit control over the model complexity. Experiments on synthetic and real-world data, as well as subgraph counting tasks, demonstrate consistent improvements over standard MPNNs, competitive expressivity beyond the 1-WL test, and scalability to large datasets.

</details>


### [361] [MDGMIX: Boundary-Aware Subgraph Mixing for Multi-Domain Graph Pre-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25771)
*Ziyu Zheng,Yaming Yang,Ziyu Guan,Wei Zhao,Xinyan Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MDGMIX is an efficient multi-domain graph pre-training framework that reduces data redundancy through boundary-aware subgraph mixing and hierarchical discrimination, achieving better performance with less computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-domain graph pre-training methods suffer from high computational costs due to jointly training all source domains, and it's unclear whether all source domain data contribute equally to effective transfer. The authors discovered significant data redundancy in current approaches.

Method: Proposes MDGMIX framework with: (1) boundary-aware subgraph mixing that selects boundary nodes to create challenging mixed-domain subgraphs, (2) hierarchical discrimination using coarse-grained domain discrimination and fine-grained domain decomposition losses to decouple shared patterns from domain-specific patterns, and (3) lightweight prompt weighting mechanism for adaptation.

Result: MDGMIX consistently outperforms strong baselines in few-shot classification tasks while exhibiting superior time and memory efficiency compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: The proposed framework successfully addresses data redundancy in multi-domain graph pre-training, achieving effective cross-domain generalization with improved computational efficiency through careful selection and mixing of boundary-aware subgraphs and hierarchical discrimination strategies.

Abstract: Multi-domain graph pre-training is a crucial step in constructing foundational graph models with cross-domain generalization capabilities. However, existing methods predominantly rely on jointly training all source domain graphs, resulting in high computational costs. Furthermore, it remains unclear whether all source domain graph data contribute equally to effective transfer. This paper empirically reveals significant data redundancy in multi-domain graph pre-training. Based on this finding, we propose the Multi-domain Graph Pre-training Framework, MDGMIX, which combines boundary-aware subgraph mixing with hierarchical discrimination. By selecting boundary nodes to construct challenging mixed-domain subgraphs, MDGMIX employs coarse-grained domain discrimination and fine-grained domain decomposition losses to decouple shared patterns from domain-specific patterns. During adaptation, MDGMIX employs a lightweight prompt weighting mechanism to transfer source domain knowledge. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MDGMIX consistently outperforms strong baselines in few-shot classification tasks while exhibiting superior time and memory efficiency. The code is available at: https://github.com/zhengziyu77/MDGMIX.

</details>


### [362] [NPSolver: Neural Poisson Solver with Iterative Physics Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25786)
*Bocheng Zeng,Rui Zhang,Runze Mao,Mengtao Yan,Xuan Bai,Yang Liu,Zhi X. Chen,Hao Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NPSolver: A neural Poisson solver trained without labeled data using iterative physics supervision and a specialized boundary-aware architecture for efficient solving on irregular domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Solving Poisson equations on complex irregular domains is computationally expensive with classical methods. Existing neural operators either require large labeled datasets or suffer from unstable training with physics-informed losses, creating a need for stable, efficient, label-free neural solvers.

Method: Uses iterative physics supervision where predictions are refined with a few preconditioned conjugate gradient (PCG) steps instead of fully converged solutions or raw residuals. Introduces Boundary-Aware Transolver (BA-Transolver) architecture that separates interior and boundary tokenization to better handle mixed boundary conditions. Features stop-gradient design for optimization stability.

Result: Outperforms both physics-informed and data-driven baselines on 2D and 3D irregular geometries. Demonstrates capability for efficient gradient-based boundary control in downstream thermal control tasks. Provides stable training without solution labels through well-conditioned error proxy.

Conclusion: NPSolver presents an effective neural Poisson solver that combines iterative physics supervision with boundary-aware architecture design, enabling stable label-free training and superior performance on complex domains while supporting practical applications like boundary control.

Abstract: Efficiently solving Poisson equations on complex, irregular domains remains a fundamental challenge in scientific computing, as classical iterative solvers often suffer from prohibitive runtime due to ill-conditioned systems. While neural operators offer a fast alternative, they typically rely on large-scale labeled datasets or struggle with unstable training dynamics when using physics-informed residual losses. We propose \textsc{NPSolver}, a neural Poisson solver trained without solution labels via iterative physics supervision. Instead of relying on fully converged numerical solutions or raw PDE residuals, \textsc{NPSolver} utilizes a small number of preconditioned conjugate gradient (PCG) steps to refine its own predictions, providing a more stable and well-scaled training signal. Theoretical analysis confirms that this iterative supervision serves as a well-conditioned error proxy and that a stop-gradient design is essential for optimization stability. To better capture boundary-driven features under mixed boundary conditions, we further introduce the Boundary-Aware Transolver (\textsc{BA-Transolver}) architecture that explicitly separates interior and boundary tokenization. Extensive evaluations on 2D and 3D irregular geometries demonstrate that \textsc{NPSolver} outperforms both physics-informed and data-driven baselines. Furthermore, a downstream thermal control task highlights the model's capability for conducting efficient and reliable gradient-based boundary control. We will release our codes and data at https://github.com/intell-sci-comput/NPSolver.

</details>


### [363] [On the Benefits of Free Exploration for Regret Minimization in Multi-Armed Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25789)
*Yunlong Hou,Zixin Zhong,Vincent Y. F. Tan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Novel bandit algorithm with free exploration phase that strategically uses exploration budget before regret accumulation to achieve less total regret than traditional approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classic bandit problems either minimize regret or focus on pure exploration, but there's an unexplored middle ground where an agent gets free exploration before regret accumulates. This setting arises in practical scenarios like medical trials or system testing where initial exploration has lower costs.

Method: Proposed UFE-KLUCB-H: a two-phase algorithm with UFE (free exploration policy) and KLUCB-H (history-aware regret minimization). Introduced (α,β)-probably saving policies and developed instance-dependent upper bounds. Used novel multi-instance perturbation arguments for lower bounds tailored to free-exploration setting.

Result: UFE-KLUCB-H accumulates strictly less regret than policies without free exploration. Near-optimality demonstrated for two-valued bandits. Sharp phase transitions in accumulated regret depending on free exploration budget revealed. Simulations show forced exploration and adaptivity lead to greater regret savings.

Conclusion: The free exploration paradigm bridges gap between regret minimization and pure exploration. The proposed algorithm effectively leverages free exploration budget to reduce total regret, with theoretical guarantees showing near-optimal performance.

Abstract: We study a stochastic multi-armed bandit problem where an agent is granted a free exploration budget before regret accumulates, a setting not captured by the classic regret minimization or pure exploration paradigms. The goal is to design an adaptive policy that strategically explores the bandit instance in the initial free exploration phase and minimizes the cumulative regret in the subsequent phase. We formalize this regret minimization with free exploration problem and identify an interesting regime where the free exploration budget scales logarithmically with the time horizon. To quantify the amount of regret saved with high probability as a result of the availability of the free exploration phase, we introduce a novel set of policies known as $(α,β)$-probably saving policies. We propose a two-phase, probably saving algorithm, UFE-KLUCB-H, which consists of a principled free exploration policy, UFE, and a history-aware regret minimization policy KLUCB-H. Instance-dependent upper bounds on UFE-KLUCB-H are derived, showing that UFE-KLUCB-H accumulates strictly less regret than policies that do not have access to a free exploration phase. Complementarily, we derive instance-dependent lower bounds based on novel multi-instance perturbation arguments tailored to the free-exploration setting, demonstrating the near-optimality of UFE-KLUCB-H for two-valued bandits. Our upper and lower bounds reveal sharp phase transitions in the accumulated regret depending on the amount of available free exploration. Simulations are conducted to demonstrate that forced exploration and adaptivity in the algorithm lead to greater regret savings.

</details>


### [364] [On Reliability of Efficient Membership Inference Vulnerability Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25819)
*Joonas Jälkö,Gauri Pradhan,Ossi Räisä,Antti Honkela*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Current membership inference attack evaluation methods have calibration issues when aggregating scores across multiple samples, and the popular LiRA implementation suffers from finite population bias that overestimates vulnerability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper identifies two key weaknesses in the efficient MIA evaluation pipeline: 1) Current methods for evaluating TPR using scores concatenated across multiple individuals are not properly calibrated for low FPR regimes, making them unreliable for differential privacy auditing. 2) There's a finite population bias in the commonly used efficient LiRA implementation that leads to overestimation of per-sample vulnerability.

Method: The authors propose a post-processing method to effectively calibrate the FPR across different samples to address the first issue. They also identify and analyze the finite population bias problem in the efficient LiRA implementation, showing how it creates positive bias in vulnerability assessment.

Result: The paper demonstrates that current MIA evaluation practices using aggregated scores across multiple individuals yield uncalibrated FPR measurements, particularly problematic for low FPR regimes. It also reveals that the widely-used efficient LiRA implementation from Carlini et al. 2022 contains systematic bias that inflates measured vulnerability.

Conclusion: Current efficient MIA evaluation methods need correction: FPR calibration across samples is essential for reliable differential privacy auditing, and the finite population bias in LiRA must be addressed to obtain accurate vulnerability assessments. Both issues need to be fixed for trustworthy privacy analysis.

Abstract: Membership inference attacks (MIAs) are popular methods for empirically assessing the leakage of sensitive information in the training data through models or statistics learned from the data. The MIA vulnerability is often evaluated through false positive rate (FPR) and true positive rate (TPR) of a binary classifier that tries to predict whether a particular sample was in the training data. However, in order to reliably estimate the TPR especially for low FPR values, a lot of observations are needed, which in case of MIA translates to many target models, leading to large computational cost. To avoid excessive compute requirements, the MIA scores are often averaged over multiple individuals and multiple targeted models. We demonstrate two key weaknesses in this efficient MIA evaluation pipeline. First, we show that evaluating the TPR based on MIA scores concatenated across multiple individuals, commonly used to study vulnerabilities in the very low FPR regime, is not calibrated across the per-sample FPRs. This makes it unreliable as a tool for auditing differential privacy. To solve this, we propose a post-processing method to effectively calibrate the FPR across different samples. Second, we identify a finite population bias in the commonly used efficient likelihood-ratio attack (LiRA) implementation proposed by Carlini et al. 2022, leading to a positive bias in the per-sample vulnerability.

</details>


### [365] [Visual-Redundancy-Controlled Parallel Decoding for Diffusion-Based Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25820)
*Yulin Yuan,Hongshuo Zhao,Xiangming Meng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VRCD is a training-free decoding method for diffusion-based multimodal LLMs that reduces visual redundancy in parallel token prediction by prioritizing visually complementary positions using token-to-image attention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing confidence-based decoding in dMLLMs commits top-K confident tokens in parallel without considering whether they provide complementary visual grounding, leading to visual redundancy that hampers later decoding steps.

Method: Proposes Visual-Redundancy-Controlled Decoding (VRCD) that uses token-to-image attention to measure and control visual grounding overlap (via Visual Redundancy Index) and prioritizes visually complementary positions during decoding.

Result: VRCD reduces visual redundancy and remaining-position entropy with modest runtime overhead, achieving relative accuracy gains of up to 18.8% on M^3CoT and 6.9% on MMBench over confidence-based decoding.

Conclusion: Controlling visual redundancy during decoding significantly improves multimodal reasoning performance in dMLLMs, with VRCD providing an effective training-free solution that better utilizes complementary visual information.

Abstract: Diffusion-based multimodal large language models (dMLLMs) decode by iteratively predicting tokens at multiple masked positions in parallel. This turns each decoding step into a position-selection problem: the model must choose not only which predictions are reliable in isolation, but also which positions should be committed together as context for later decoding steps. Existing confidence-based decoding ranks masked positions independently and commits the top-K positions, largely ignoring whether the committed tokens provide complementary visual grounding. We identify a step-level limitation of this strategy in multimodal settings: high-confidence tokens selected in the same step can rely on overlapping visual grounding, introducing visual redundancy among the committed tokens and leaving less complementary visual grounding available for later decoding. To quantify this effect, we introduce the Visual Redundancy Index (VRI), which measures visual grounding overlap among tokens committed in parallel. To control this redundancy during decoding, we propose Visual-Redundancy-Controlled Decoding (VRCD), a training-free inference-time decoding method that uses token-to-image attention to prioritize visually complementary positions. Across diverse multimodal benchmarks, VRCD reduces visual redundancy and remaining-position entropy with modest runtime overhead. In longer decoding experiments, it also achieves relative accuracy gains of up to 18.8% on M^3CoT and 6.9% on MMBench over confidence-based decoding. Code will be released at https://github.com/infiniteYuanyl/VRCD.

</details>


### [366] [Context-Instrumental Data Distillation for Kubernetes Manifest Generation: Method and Experimental Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25835)
*Andrey Kozachok,Anatoliy Bakaev,Aleksandr Kozachok,Shamil Magomedov,Artem Noev*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SLMs up to 4B parameters can be specialized for DSL generation using context-instrumental data distillation, with Kubernetes manifests as target domain, achieving 91.5% success on test set through strict format requirements rather than more training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Small Language Models (SLMs) need effective methods for specializing in domain-specific languages (DSL) generation, with Kubernetes manifests as a practical target domain requiring high accuracy and correct syntax.

Method: Context-instrumental data distillation: source corpus from synthetic generation (DeepSeek-V4 Flash API as teacher) and reverse instruction generation from real Kubernetes YAML files. Pairs included only upon passing external validators and matching domain context model. Fine-tuning Qwen2.5-Coder-1.5B-Instruct via LoRA on CPU, supervised fine-tuning on instrumentally verified examples.

Result: On K8s-Distill-Pilot corpus (train_1200, validation_100, test_200): achieved full-pass@1 = 91.5% (183/200) with strict prompt formulation and max_new_tokens=768. Key finding: quality depended more on strict output format requirements than on increasing training examples.

Conclusion: Context-instrumental data distillation is effective for SLM specialization in DSL generation, with strict format requirements being more crucial than training data quantity for Kubernetes YAML generation quality.

Abstract: This paper examines the specialization of Small Language Models (SLMs) with up to 4 billion parameters for generating artifacts in domain-specific languages (DSL). Kubernetes manifests are chosen as the target domain. We propose the context-instrumental data distillation method: the source corpus is formed through synthetic generation and, in an extended scheme, through reverse instruction generation from real Kubernetes YAML files, with pairs included in training only upon passing external validators and matching the domain context model. Unlike classical KL-divergence knowledge distillation, the baseline implementation reduces to supervised fine-tuning on instrumentally verified examples. The experimental section presents a pilot implementation under resource-constrained conditions: the DeepSeek-V4 Flash API serves as the teacher for synthetic generation, while Qwen2.5-Coder-1.5B-Instruct is fine-tuned via LoRA on CPU. On the K8s-Distill-Pilot corpus (train_1200, validation_100, test_200), we achieved full-pass@1 = 91.5% (183/200) with a stricter prompt formulation and max_new_tokens=768. The key empirical finding is that for Kubernetes YAML, result quality in the pilot depended more on strict output format requirements than on simply increasing the number of training examples.

</details>


### [367] [Geometric Evolution Maps: Extracting Stable Concept Probes from Transformer Residual Streams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25848)
*James Henry*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Geometric Evolution Maps (GEMs) track concept direction changes in transformer models to identify stable "handoff layers" for more reliable concept probing than fixed-layer approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current concept probing practices at fixed late layers or peak separation scores ignore that concept representations undergo substantial directional rotation during assembly and only stabilize after a characteristic handoff layer, making existing probes unreliable.

Method: GEMs track full directional trajectories of concepts through residual stream activations, identify handoff layers where rotation ceases, and extract settled probe directions from those layers rather than fixed layers.

Result: Across 23 architectures (70M to 14B parameters) and 17 concept types: 68.5% of GEM-extracted probes outperform peak-layer probes, with MHA models favoring handoff in 78.3% cases vs. 47.1% for GQA models. Cosine similarity within CAZs has mean 0.233, showing directions change substantially.

Conclusion: Probes should be extracted from handoff layers where concept directions stabilize rather than fixed layers, as concept representations undergo substantial directional rotation during assembly. GEMs provide a systematic method for identifying optimal probing layers across architectures.

Abstract: Concept probes extracted from transformer residual streams are only as reliable as the layer from which they are extracted. The common practice of probing at a fixed late layer or at the peak of a separation score function ignores a fundamental structural feature: concept representations undergo substantial directional rotation during their assembly phase, and do not settle into a stable direction until a characteristic handoff layer after the primary Concept Allocation Zone (CAZ). We introduce Geometric Evolution Maps (GEMs), which track the full directional trajectory of a concept through residual stream activations, identify the handoff layer where rotation ceases, and extract the settled probe direction from that layer. Across 23 architectures spanning 70M to 14B parameters and 17 concept types, the entry-to-exit cosine similarity within CAZs has a mean of 0.233, showing that probe direction at CAZ entry does not reliably predict probe direction at exit. Ablation experiments across 391 concept x model pairs (23 models x 17 concepts) show that GEM-extracted probes are at least as precise as peak-layer probes in 268/391 trials (68.5%), and strictly outperform in 259/391 (66.2%). The architecture split is pronounced: MHA models favour the handoff in 173/221 trials (78.3%); GQA models favour the handoff in only 56/119 trials (47.1%). Model-level Wilcoxon: W=214, N=23, p=0.010 (one-sided). An adaptive ablation width rule targets the 79/391 near-final-layer cases: it improves probe quality in 60/79 triggered cases (75.9%), mean gain +7.44pp. A direction-specificity control confirms the ablation effect is concept-direction specific: median 377x suppression rate versus random-direction ablation (99.1% of concept directions beat all 10 random seeds). Reference implementation: rosetta_tools v1.3.1 (doi:10.5281/zenodo.20361433).

</details>


### [368] [When Self-Belief Misleads: Active Label Acquisition for Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25864)
*Li Wang,Xiaodong Lu,Xiaohan Wang,Yikun Ban,Jiajun Chai,Wei Lin,Tianhao Peng,Guojun Yin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RLAVR (Reinforcement Learning with Active Verifiable Rewards) is a method that combines active acquisition of ground-truth labels for selected samples with pseudo-labels to stabilize training and improve performance under limited annotation budgets, addressing training collapse in unsupervised RLVR approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RLVR methods rely on expensive ground-truth labels, while unsupervised RLVR using pseudo-labels suffers from training collapse issues. Different samples have varying annotation values, and existing approaches don't efficiently allocate limited annotation budgets.

Method: Proposes RLAVR which actively acquires ground-truth labels for valuable samples and integrates them with pseudo-labels. Introduces CAG metric to identify valuable samples, and CARE system that implements a practical pre-query acquisition policy based on the oracle CAG criterion to improve training stability.

Result: Extensive experiments across diverse domains, model families, and model scales demonstrate the effectiveness and generality of the approach. The method stabilizes training dynamics and improves performance under limited annotation budgets.

Conclusion: RLAVR with CARE provides an effective solution to the training collapse problem in unsupervised RLVR by intelligently selecting which samples to annotate with ground-truth labels, making RL more practical in real-world scenarios with limited annotation budgets.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable advancements in reasoning capabilities empowered by Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). Nonetheless, RLVR intrinsically relies on ground-truth labels for reward computation, the acquisition of which is often prohibitively expensive in real-world scenarios. While unsupervised RLVR paradigms attempt to circumvent this by training on pseudo-labels, they are notoriously susceptible to training collapse. Moreover, different samples often exhibit varying annotation values. In this paper, we propose Reinforcement Learning with Active Verifiable Rewards (RLAVR), which actively acquires ground-truth labels for a small set of selected samples and integrates them with pseudo-labels, thereby stabilizing training dynamics and improving performance under limited annotation budgets. To identify valuable samples, we propose the Corrective Advantage Gap (CAG) metric and analyze the sample-level supervision value. Building on this, we introduce Correction-Aware Reliability Estimation for RLAVR (CARE), which translates the oracle CAG criterion into a practical pre-query acquisition policy to substantially improve training stability. Extensive experiments across diverse domains, model families, and model scales demonstrate the effectiveness and generality of our approach. Our code is available at https://github.com/Lumina04/CARE.

</details>


### [369] [UNATE: UNsupervised ATomic Embedding for crystal structures property prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25866)
*Laura Solà-Garcia,Àlex Solé,Javier Ruiz-Hidalgo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UNATE is an unsupervised framework that learns atomic embeddings from unlabeled crystal structures to improve property prediction with limited labeled data, achieving up to 10% improvement with only 25% labeled data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Materials discovery is hampered by scarce labeled crystal data and expensive computational calculations needed for theoretical property prediction, creating a need for methods that can leverage unlabeled structural information.

Method: UNATE combines unsupervised denoising autoencoder with self-supervised contrastive learning to extract robust atomic representations from unlabeled crystal structures, which are then used as node features for downstream property prediction tasks.

Result: Replacing raw atomic numbers with UNATE-pretrained embeddings yields 2.7% improvement over full-data baseline, with benefits increasing to up to 10% improvement when using only 25% of labeled data.

Conclusion: UNATE effectively leverages unlabeled structural data to create transferable atomic representations that significantly improve crystal property prediction, especially valuable in data-scarce scenarios common in materials science.

Abstract: Accurately predicting crystal properties is critical for accelerating materials discovery, but it is often limited by scarce labeled data and costly theoretical calculations. To alleviate this, we propose UNATE (Unsupervised Atomic Embedding), a framework that leverages structural information extracted from unlabeled crystal structures. UNATE integrates an unsupervised denoising autoencoder with self-supervised contrastive learning to learn robust atomic representations, which are then used as input features for downstream property prediction. Experimental results show that replacing raw atomic numbers with UNATE-pretrained node embeddings yields a 2.7\% improvement over the full-data baseline. Notably, the benefits become more pronounced in scenarios with limited labeled data, reaching improvements of up to 10\% when only 25\% of the labeled data is used.

</details>


### [370] [The Quantization Benefits of Residual-Free Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25880)
*Yiping Ji,Mahalakshmi Sabanayagam,Peyman Moghadam,Hemanth Saratchandran,Simon Lucey*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Residual connections in transformers create heavy-tailed activations that make low-bit quantization highly lossy, while residual-free architectures maintain near-Gaussian activations and offer much better quantization robustness with modified training techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale transformer training and deployment are constrained by the transfer of activations, gradients, and optimizer states across accelerators. Low-bit quantization could help, but transformer activations are heavy-tailed and outlier-dominated due to residual connections, making simple quantization highly lossy.

Method: 1) Conducted controlled comparisons between residual and residual-free transformers to analyze quantization error and accuracy degradation. 2) Used excess kurtosis analysis to show how residual mixing amplifies non-Gaussianity while dense mixing in residual-free contracts it. 3) Made residual-free transformers trainable using orthogonal initialization, spectral/second-order optimization, and depth-aware scaling of attention temperature.

Result: Residual-free transformers maintain near-Gaussian activations and exhibit significantly improved robustness to low-bit quantization. While there's a small drop in full precision performance for language tasks, the quantization-friendly properties allow for much more effective compression.

Conclusion: There's an accuracy-compressibility trade-off in transformer design. Residual connections drive activations away from Gaussianity and worsen quantization performance, while residual-free architectures can be made trainable and offer better quantization robustness, motivating architecture-level approaches for quantization-friendly foundation models.

Abstract: Large-scale transformer training and deployment are increasingly constrained by the transfer of activations, gradients, and optimizer states across accelerators. Low-bit quantization offers a natural remedy, but transformer activations are often heavy-tailed and outlier-dominated, making simple quantization highly lossy. We show that this difficulty is not only a property of the quantizer, but also of the architecture. Specifically, residual connections can drive transformer activations away from Gaussianity during training. Using controlled comparisons between residual and residual-free transformers, we demonstrate that this effect leads to substantially higher quantization error and accuracy degradation at low precision in residual models. We explain the phenomenon through an excess kurtosis analysis, showing that residual mixing can amplify non-Gaussianity, whereas dense mixing in residual-free contracts non-Gaussianity. We then show that residual-free transformers can be made trainable using orthogonal initialization, spectral or second-order optimization, and depth-aware scaling of attention temperature. In language tasks, while there is a small drop in full precision performance, these models retain near-Gaussian activations and exhibit significantly improved robustness to low-bit quantization. Our results identify an accuracy--compressibility trade-off in transformer design and motivate architecture-level approaches to quantization-friendly foundation models.

</details>


### [371] [Conformalised imprecise inference for robust extrapolation under limited data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25882)
*Yu Chen,Scott Ferson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A conformalized imprecise inference framework that provides robust uncertainty quantification with guaranteed coverage under distributional shifts, improving reliability for extrapolation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing uncertainty quantification methods in machine learning often lack rigorous guarantees when generalizing beyond training domains, especially under distributional shifts. While there's increasing emphasis on distinguishing aleatory vs epistemic uncertainty, current approaches don't sufficiently ensure reliable predictions during extrapolation.

Method: Proposes a model-agnostic conformalized imprecise inference framework that augments predictive models with both imprecision and distance awareness. The method produces imprecise predictions (probability boxes) that maintain statistical coverage guarantees while adaptively expanding uncertainty in extrapolation regimes.

Result: Experiments on synthetic and benchmark datasets show improved robustness and reliable coverage compared to standard probabilistic approaches, particularly under limited data conditions. The framework maintains valid coverage under distributional shift.

Conclusion: The proposed framework successfully addresses limitations of existing uncertainty quantification methods by providing robust extrapolation capabilities with guaranteed coverage, adapting uncertainty appropriately in extrapolation regimes while remaining model-agnostic.

Abstract: Recent advances in uncertainty quantification increasingly emphasise the distinction between aleatory and epistemic uncertainty in machine learning, motivating the need for more unified frameworks. However, despite much progress in producing reliable predictions, existing methods often lack rigorous guarantees when generalising beyond the training domain. We propose a conformalised imprecise inference framework for robust extrapolation, which is model-agnostic and augments predictive models with imprecision and distance awareness. The proposed approach yields imprecise predictions (probability boxes) that remain valid under distributional shift, maintaining coverage while adaptively expanding uncertainty in extrapolation regimes. Experiments on synthetic and benchmark datasets demonstrate improved robustness and reliable coverage compared to standard probabilistic approaches, particularly under limited data.

</details>


### [372] [Optimal and Order-optimal Gated Priority-based Greedy Policies for Two-layer Multi-item Order Fulfillment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25888)
*Xi Chen,Yuze Chen,Ziyi Chen,Yuan Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper develops competitive online algorithms for real-time order fulfillment in e-commerce distribution networks, showing that simple greedy policies with priority rules can perform nearly as well as optimal offline solutions even with unknown future demand.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: E-commerce firms face the challenge of making real-time fulfillment decisions in multi-layer distribution networks when customer orders with multiple items arrive sequentially and future demand is unknown. The key tension is whether to use scarce local inventory to save current fulfillment costs or preserve it for potentially more valuable future orders that could benefit from local fulfillment.

Method: Formulated as an adversarial online model with multiple front distribution centers (FDCs), one regional distribution center (RDC), multi-unit multi-item orders, and item-specific time-varying costs. Developed a family of Gated Priority-based Greedy policies and derived competitive-ratio guarantees under both time-varying and time-invariant cost structures. Established matching or near-matching lower bounds for any online algorithm.

Result: The proposed policies achieve strong competitive-ratio guarantees and perform well in numerical experiments compared to generalized myopic and forecast-based benchmarks. The analysis provides insights into when local inventory should be protected, when order splitting is beneficial despite fixed costs, and how cost structures affect the value of more sophisticated optimization.

Conclusion: Simple, interpretable, and implementable fulfillment rules can perform nearly as well as optimal clairvoyant planners in e-commerce distribution networks. The research provides both theoretical guarantees and practical managerial guidance for real-time order fulfillment decisions under demand uncertainty.

Abstract: We study how an e-commerce firm should make real-time fulfillment decisions in a two-layer distribution network when multi-item customer orders arrive sequentially and future demand is unknown. The central managerial tension is whether to use scarce front distribution center (FDC) inventory to save current fulfillment cost or preserve that inventory for future orders that may be more valuable to serve locally. We formulate an adversarial online model with multiple FDCs, one regional distribution center (RDC), multi-unit multi-item orders, and item-specific and time-varying variable costs. Our theoretical objective is to characterize when simple, interpretable, and implementable fulfillment rules can perform nearly as well as an optimal clairvoyant planner. We develop a family of Gated Priority-based Greedy policies, derive competitive-ratio guarantees under both time-varying and time-invariant cost structures, and establish matching or near-matching lower bounds for any online algorithm. Numerical experiments show that the proposed policies perform strongly relative to generalized myopic and forecast-based benchmarks. The analysis yields managerial guidance on when local inventory should be protected, when splitting orders is worth the fixed-cost burden, and how the relative magnitudes of fixed and variable costs determine the value of more sophisticated optimization.

</details>


### [373] [Merge-Bench: Resolve Merge Conflicts with Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25890)
*Benedikt Schesch,Michael D. Ernst*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces LLMergeJ, a machine learning system that uses reinforcement learning to train an LLM for resolving merge conflicts in version control, achieving competitive performance with commercial LLMs on a dataset of real-world Java merge conflicts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Version control merging is a difficult and important task in software development that often requires manual resolution of conflicts. Existing approaches are limited, and there's a need for automated, scalable solutions to handle merge conflicts more effectively.

Method: 1) Created Merge-Bench dataset with 7938 real-world merge conflict hunks from 1439 GitHub repositories using automated ground truth from developer commits. 2) Developed LLMergeJ using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), an online reinforcement learning method to train a 14B parameter LLM for Java merge conflict resolution. 3) Conducted comprehensive evaluations comparing against commercial LLMs across multiple programming languages.

Result: LLMergeJ outperformed 3 commercial LLMs on Java merge conflicts, trailing only Gemini 2.5 Pro. Commercial LLM performance was stable across 11 programming languages. The best models correctly resolved less than 60% of merge conflicts, indicating the difficulty of the task.

Conclusion: Machine learning, particularly reinforcement learning on LLMs, shows promise for automating merge conflict resolution but still has significant room for improvement given the current success rate below 60%. The scalable dataset construction methodology enables further research in this important software engineering task.

Abstract: This paper applies machine learning to the difficult and important task of version control merging. (1) We constructed a dataset, Merge-Bench, of 7938 real-world merge conflict hunks from 1439 GitHub repositories. The ground truth is the merge resolution that developers committed to the repository. Our dataset construction methodology is scalable to arbitrary amounts of data since no manual labeling is required. (2) We trained a model, LLMergeJ, to resolve merge conflicts in Java programs. Our approach uses Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), an online reinforcement learning method, to train a Large Language Model (LLM). (3) We performed two evaluations of the performance of LLMs on resolving merge conflicts. On Java programs, LLMergeJ with 14B parameters outperforms 3 commercial LLMs, trailing only Gemini 2.5 Pro. Across 11 programming languages, commercial LLM performance is largely stable from language to language. The best models correctly resolve less than 60% of merge conflicts.

</details>


### [374] [Predicting Stock Price Direction on Earnings Announcement Days using Multi-modal Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25894)
*Manuel Noseda,Nathan Soldati,Marco Paina*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformer model with news sentiment outperforms LSTM and baselines in predicting stock price movements during earnings announcements, achieving higher F1-score by better identifying volatile movements while LSTM uses safer conservative strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predicting stock price movements during Earnings Announcements (EAs) is challenging due to market noise and high-impact price discontinuities. The study aims to evaluate whether pre-announcement news sentiment, firm fundamentals, and recent market dynamics can jointly predict directional price movements on EA days.

Method: Constructed multi-modal feature space combining 15 fundamental metrics, 3 price-based technical indicators, and sentiment scores from financial news articles processed using FinBERT. Compared LSTM network and Transformer architecture against logistic regression baseline, with and without sentiment features to quantify incremental value.

Result: Transformer model exhibited superior sensitivity in identifying volatile movements, achieving higher macro F1-score. LSTM demonstrated higher precision through conservative safe-bet strategy. Ablation experiments showed consistent benefit from incorporating news sentiment features.

Conclusion: News sentiment provides valuable incremental predictive power for earnings announcement price movements, with Transformer architecture showing particular strength in capturing complex patterns and volatile price movements compared to traditional LSTM and baseline models.

Abstract: Predicting stock price movements during Earnings Announcements (EAs) is a significant challenge due to market noise and high-impact price discontinuities. In this study, we evaluate whether pre-announcement news sentiment, firm fundamentals, and recent market dynamics jointly predict the directional price movement of equities on EA days. We construct a multi-modal feature space combining 15 fundamental metrics, 3 price-based technical indicators and sentiment scores derived from financial news articles processed using FinBERT. We compare a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network and a Transformer-based architecture against a logistic regression baseline, and further assess all models with and without sentiment features to quantify their incremental value. Our results indicate that while the LSTM demonstrates higher precision through a conservative safe-bet strategy, the Transformer model exhibits superior sensitivity in identifying volatile movements, achieving a higher macro F1-score, with ablation experiments showing a consistent benefit from incorporating news sentiment.

</details>


### [375] [Reading the Finetuning Prior: Verbatim Content Recovery via Contrastive Decoding Diffing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25902)
*Michał Brzozowski,Zuzanna Dubanowska,Enrico Cassano,Neo Christopher Chung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CDD is a model diffing method that recovers implanted facts from finetuned models using only output-level logits, outperforming white-box methods while being faster and requiring less access.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for auditing what models have been taught require white-box access to model weights and internals. There's a need for practical transparency tools that work with less access (grey-box) to enable accountability in deployed AI systems.

Method: Contrastive Decoding Diffing (CDD) operates on output-level logit distributions only, with no weight access. It uses three key ideas: 1) bypassing chat templates to expose raw finetuning prior, 2) seeding generation with maximally vague pre-fills, and 3) amplifying logit-space differences between finetuned and base models at each decoding step.

Result: CDD recovers implanted facts verbatim across four architectures (1B-32B parameters), uniformly outperforming Activation Difference Lens (ADL) despite requiring less access and running ~170x faster. It also surfaces unintended data pipeline artifacts and achieves near-perfect recovery in real-domain finetuning settings.

Conclusion: CDD demonstrates that grey-box methods can outperform white-box baselines for model auditing, providing practical utility for transparency and accountability. Its success shows the importance of output-level analysis for recovering finetuning information without requiring full model internals access.

Abstract: Narrowly finetuned language models memorize implanted content verbatim, but auditing what a deployed model has been taught, without access to its weights or training data, remains an open challenge. Recent work shows that activation differences between base and finetuned models carry readable traces of the finetuning domain; the state-of-the-art Activation Difference Lens (ADL) recovers a vague domain-level description but requires full "white-box" access to model internals. We introduce Contrastive Decoding Diffing (CDD), a model diffing method that operates on output-level logit distributions only, with no weight access, no layer selection, and no per-model tuning, yet recovers implanted facts. CDD consists of three ideas: bypassing the chat template to expose the raw finetuning prior, seeding generation with maximally vague pre-fills, and amplifying the logit-space difference between finetuned and base models at each decoding step. A single default configuration recovers implanted facts verbatim -- exact drug names, vote counts, physical measurements, and procedural details -- across four architectures (1B--32B parameters), uniformly outperforming ADL despite less access and running ~170x faster. Furthermore, CDD surfaces unintended data pipeline artifacts: a fictional persona introduced by the LLM data generator via mode collapse leaked into model weights and was extracted by CDD, constituting to our knowledge the first demonstrated end-to-end fingerprinting chain from data generator artifact to model weights to recovered output. We validate on real-domain finetuning settings, achieving near-perfect recovery across all single-dataset non-CoT variants and correctly identifying all four datasets in the mixed-dataset setting. CDD's success as a grey-box method outperforming white-box baselines underscores its practical utility for transparency and accountability in AI systems.

</details>


### [376] [Joint Optimization of Training and Inference in Federated Edge Learning via Constrained Multi-Objective Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25916)
*Zhen Li,Jun Cai,Chao Yang,Haoran Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents C-MOPPO, a constrained multi-objective proximal policy optimization algorithm for joint federated training and inference management in resource-constrained edge devices, aiming to balance inference accuracy with latency and energy efficiency.
