<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated edge learning (FEEL) enables collaborative model training across edge devices while protecting data privacy, but faces challenges in jointly managing training and inference tasks on resource-constrained devices. There's a need to maximize inference accuracy while minimizing latency and energy consumption in dynamic real-world environments.

Method: The authors introduce a tandem-queue-inspired conversion mechanism linking inference requests and training data, incorporate data and model freshness into accuracy formulation, and transform the joint optimization problem into a multi-objective Markov decision process (MOMDP). They develop the C-MOPPO algorithm that first learns policies with different preference weights across three objectives (accuracy, latency, energy), then uses constrained policy optimization to enrich the Pareto front.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that C-MOPPO achieves well-balanced trade-offs among objectives and significantly outperforms baseline methods under various system configurations.

Conclusion: The proposed C-MOPPO framework effectively addresses the NP-hard joint optimization problem in federated edge learning, providing high-quality solutions for managing both training and inference tasks while balancing multiple competing objectives in resource-constrained edge environments.

Abstract: Federated edge learning (FEEL) has recently emerged as a promising paradigm for achieving edge intelligence (EI) via enabling collaborative model training across edge devices while protecting data privacy. In this paper, we put forth an online optimization framework that jointly manages federated training and inference on resource-constrained edge devices. We introduce a tandem-queue-inspired conversion mechanism that bridges inference requests and training data, and further incorporate both data and model freshness into the accuracy formulation to capture temporal dynamics in real-world environments. To maximize inference accuracy while minimizing latency and energy consumption, the mode selections, communication, and computation resource allocations of edge devices are jointly optimized. We formulate this optimization as a multi-objective optimization problem, which is NP-hard and further complicated by the online setting. To address these challenges, we transform the problem into a multi-objective Markov decision process (MOMDP) and develop a \underline{c}onstrained \underline{m}ulti-\underline{o}bjective \underline{p}roximal \underline{p}olicy \underline{o}ptimization (C-MOPPO) algorithm. Specifically, C-MOPPO first learns a set of policies with different preferences across three objectives, then leverages constrained policy optimization to enrich the Pareto front and obtain high-quality, dense solutions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that C-MOPPO achieves well-balanced trade-offs among objectives and significantly outperforms baselines under various system configurations.

</details>


### [377] [Quantitative Evaluation of the Severity of Posttraumatic Stress Disorder through Transfer Learning from Specific Phobia Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25933)
*Nicolas Ricka,Gauthier Pellegrin,Denis A. Fompeyrine,Thomas Rohaly,Leah Enders,Heather Roy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Machine learning approach using multivariate kernel density estimation on physiological signals (HR and GSR) achieves 86% accuracy in classifying PTSD status with potential for objective clinical assessment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current PTSD assessments rely on subjective evaluations that are time-consuming, costly, and prone to human bias, creating a need for objective assessment methods.

Method: Used multivariate kernel density estimation (MKDE) technique on heart rate and galvanic skin response signals collected from 21 participants during immersive simulation. Trained fear-response model on public arachnophobia dataset, then extracted predictive PTSD features from fear-response curves estimated on military dataset.

Result: Model achieved 86% accuracy in classifying PTSD status (using PCL-M threshold of 36), with average MAE of 5.6 and mean absolute percentage error of 17% in estimating clinical PTSD severity scale.

Conclusion: The ML-based approach demonstrates promising potential for objective, low-effort PTSD evaluation using physiology, suggesting clinical utility in both screening and follow-up settings.

Abstract: Posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) is a prevalent and debilitating mental health condition with significant personal and societal impacts. Current clinical assessments of PTSD often rely on subjective evaluations, which can be time-consuming, costly, and prone to human bias. This study proposes a machine learning (ML) approach based on multivariate kernel density estimation (MKDE) technique for the objective evaluation of PTSD severity. We collected heart rate (HR) and galvanic skin response (GSR) signals as well as PTSD Checklist - Military Version (PCL-M) labels from 21 participants during an immersive simulation. A fear-response model was trained on a public arachnophobia dataset, and predictive features of PTSD were extracted from the fear-response curves estimated on the military dataset. The model achieved an accuracy of 86\% in classifying PTSD status, effectively distinguishing participants with and without PTSD (PCL-M threshold of 36). The average mean absolute error (MAE) of the models is 5.6, and it estimated a clinical PTSD severity scale with a mean absolute percentage error of 17\%. Our algorithm demonstrates promising potential for enhancing estimation of PTSD severity and followup by offering an objective and low-effort evaluation approach using physiology. These findings suggest clinical utility in both screening and follow-up settings.

</details>


### [378] [From Latent Space to Training Data: Explainable Specialization in Minimal MLPs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25939)
*Enrique Alba,Ezequiel Lopez-Rubio*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Coverage regularization in one-hidden-layer MLPs improves prototype reconstruction by encouraging neurons to specialize on training samples, while repulsive losses (separation, overlap) without attractors can degenerate prototype geometry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper investigates whether training biases can make hidden neurons specialize in minimal MLPs, and whether such specialization improves prototype-based reconstruction from learned weights. The researchers want to understand how different structural losses affect the recoverability of prototypes from trained networks.

Method: The study uses Gaussian-activation MLPs with width equal to dataset size. They compare three structural losses: coverage (encouraging prototypes to cover training samples), separation (encouraging separation between neuron-induced prototypes), and overlap (encouraging low overlap of hidden responses), against a standard fitting baseline. Experiments are conducted on uniformly sampled one-dimensional datasets from N=3 to N=100 across 480 controlled runs.

Result: Coverage regularization gives the lowest mean reconstruction error at every tested dataset size and raises the prototype-usage specialization ratio relative to the standard baseline. Separation has mixed effects, while overlap penalties are systematically harmful. The harm is not an optimization failure - overlap-active approaches fit data as well as overlap-free ones but route the optimizer to degenerate equilibria where prototype centers are pushed outside the convex hull of training inputs. Coverage cannot reward this expulsion and acts as an attractor, while separation admits it only at large temperature and overlap admits it at nominal hyperparameters.

Conclusion: The findings yield a simple design principle for prototype-recoverability-aware training: every repulsive structural loss must be compensated by a compatible attractor, or it will collapse the latent geometry it was meant to refine. Coverage regularization effectively serves as such an attractor that improves prototype reconstruction.

Abstract: We here study whether training biases can make hidden neurons specialize in minimal one-hidden-layer MLPs, and whether such specialization improves prototype-based reconstruction of the training dataset from the learned weights. We consider Gaussianactivation MLPs of width equal to dataset size and compare three structural losses that respectively encourage coverage of the training samples, separation between neuron-induced prototypes, and low overlap of hidden responses, against the standard fitting baseline. Experiments on uniformly sampled one-dimensional datasets show a stable pattern from N = 3 to N = 100 across 480 controlled runs. Coverage regularization gives the lowest mean reconstruction error at every tested size and raises the prototype-usage specialization ratio relative to the standard baseline, while separation has mixed effects and overlap penalties are systematically harmful. We show that the harm is not an optimization failure: overlap-active approaches fit the data as well as overlap-free ones but route the optimizer to a degenerate equilibrium in which prototype centers are pushed outside the convex hull of the training inputs. Coverage cannot reward this expulsion and acts as an attractor: separation admits it only at large temperature and overlap admits it at the nominal hyperparameter choice. A direct τ-sweep on the separation-only mask and a prototype-position visualization at N = 100 confirm the mechanism. The findings yield a simple design principle for prototype-recoverability-aware training: every repulsive structural loss must be compensated by a compatible attractor, or it will collapse the latent geometry it was meant to refine.

</details>


### [379] [STaT: Resolving Shape Distortion in Non-Stationary Time Series via Tri-Modal Synergy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25943)
*Hui Cheng,Jinsheng Guo,Zhenhao Weng,Yan Qiao,Meng Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STaT is a multimodal architecture that combines symbolic, temporal, and textual modalities for time series forecasting, achieving better performance while reducing shape distortion compared to existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-modal time series forecasting approaches prioritize minimizing average errors, which leads to overly smooth forecasts that miss important fluctuations and turning points in non-stationary environments.

Method: STaT introduces Symbolic-Temporal-Textual Alignment with three synergistic modalities: 1) symbolic modality converts continuous time series to discrete tokens for structural pattern identification, 2) temporal modality extracts sequential dependencies, and 3) textual modality uses domain semantics to guide macroscopic forecasting trends.

Result: On eight real-world benchmarks, STaT improves conventional magnitude indicators by up to 8.9% while simultaneously reducing shape distortion by up to 8.5%.

Conclusion: STaT effectively addresses the limitation of overly smooth forecasts in multi-modal time series forecasting by aligning symbolic, temporal, and textual information, resulting in more accurate forecasts that preserve important fluctuations.

Abstract: Recent research in time series forecasting frequently investigates the integration of textual and visual modalities with numerical models to better navigate non-stationary environments. Despite delivering solid numerical results, existing multi-modal approaches usually encounter a dilemma: prioritizing the minimization of average errors can result in excessively smooth forecasts that overlook essential fluctuations. To resolve this limitation, we introduce STaT, an innovative multimodal architecture for Symbolic-Temporal-Textual Alignment, which seamlessly unites three synergistic modalities. Specifically, the symbolic modality converts continuous time series into discrete tokens, facilitating the accurate identification of structural patterns and turning points; the temporal modality extracts inherent sequential dependencies; and the textual modality leverages domain semantics to steer the macroscopic forecasting trends. Comprehensive evaluations on eight real-world benchmarks indicate that STaT delivers exceptional performance, enhancing conventional magnitude indicators by up to 8.9% while simultaneously decreasing shape distortion by up to 8.5%.

</details>


### [380] [Small Models, Strong Priors: Architectural Inductive Bias for Parameter-Efficient Neural PDE Solvers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25949)
*Shyam Sankaran,Hanwen Wang,Paris Perdikaris*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: WaveLiT combines wavelet transforms with linear attention to create parameter-efficient neural PDE solvers that compete with foundation models 100-1000× larger by leveraging structured architectural priors instead of brute-force scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural PDE solvers have been following the scaling trends of vision/language models, but the authors argue that scale is a poor substitute for architectural inductive bias in scientific computing domains. They believe structured priors offer better parameter efficiency and that analyzing where these priors succeed/fail provides valuable insights.

Method: WaveLiT architecture combines: 1) discrete wavelet transform for lossless multi-resolution tokenization, 2) augmented linear attention block, 3) shared-weight multiscale feature pyramid, and 4) wavelet-domain auxiliary loss. The pipeline trains on a single GPU with 1-10M parameters.

Result: WaveLiT models (1-10M parameters) compete with foundation models 100-1000× larger across eight TheWell benchmarks, with largest gains on wave/acoustic-dominated problems where wavelet-multiscale prior fits the dynamics. A 10M-parameter foundation variant shows structured, physically interpretable transfer patterns - strongest where wavelet prior matches dynamics, weakest on chaotic advection-dominated flows.

Conclusion: Small-model PDE performance is shaped by architectural inductive bias rather than scale, and the structure of a prior's failures provides useful empirical signal about its content. Structured priors deliver superior parameter efficiency in scientific computing domains.

Abstract: Neural PDE solvers have followed the scaling trajectory of vision and language, with recent foundation models reaching billions of parameters. We argue that scale is a poor substitute for architectural inductive bias in this domain: structured priors deliver outsized parameter efficiency, and the pattern of where they succeed and fail is itself informative about what they capture. We instantiate this argument in WaveLiT, an architecture combining a discrete wavelet transform for lossless multi-resolution tokenization, an augmented linear attention block, a shared-weight multiscale feature pyramid, and a wavelet-domain auxiliary loss. Bespoke 1-10M-parameter WaveLiT models compete with foundation models of 100-1000$\times$ their size across eight TheWell benchmarks, with the largest gains on wave and acoustic-dominated benchmarks where the wavelet-multiscale prior fits the dominant dynamical structure and small per-step errors do not compound geometrically under rollout. Trained jointly across all eight benchmarks, a 10M-parameter foundation variant exhibits a structured, physically interpretable transfer pattern -- strongest where the wavelet-multiscale prior matches the dynamics, weakest on chaotic advection-dominated flows. The entire pipeline trains on a single GPU. The results suggest that small-model PDE performance is shaped by architectural inductive bias rather than scale, and that the structure of a prior's failures is a useful empirical signal about its content.

</details>


### [381] [Step-TP: A Grounded, Step-Level Dataset with Chain-of-Thought Reasoning for LLM-Guided Tensor Program Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25954)
*Mengfan Liu,Da Zheng,Junwei Su,Chuan Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Step-TP introduces a dataset with step-level supervision for LLM-guided tensor program optimization, providing verifiable intermediate representations and structured reasoning chains to enable reliable multi-step optimization decisions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-guided tensor program optimization approaches face challenges due to lack of verifiable step-level supervision. Existing datasets only provide end-to-end optimized program pairs using token-inefficient representations, which makes LLMs struggle to make reliable single-step decisions in large combinatorial optimization spaces.

Method: The authors create Step-TP dataset with four key design principles: (1) token-efficient, verifiable intermediate representation that deterministically lowers to TVM TIR; (2) atomic/composable optimization strategies decomposing complex trajectories into interpretable single-step decisions; (3) structured chain-of-thought supervision with explicit IR-to-IR state transitions; (4) strategy filtering to balance coverage while preventing shortcut exploitation.

Result: The Step-TP dataset provides grounded, atomic, step-level supervision with structured CoT reasoning, forming a closed reasoning loop over intermediate program states that enables reliable multi-step optimization rather than outcome imitation.

Conclusion: Step-TP addresses the limitations of current LLM-guided tensor program optimization by providing step-level supervision with verifiable representations, which should improve LLMs' ability to make reliable optimization decisions through interpretable, multi-step reasoning processes.

Abstract: Despite the strong reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs), optimizing the execution efficiency of tensor programs remains challenging due to the need for precise, composable transformation decisions. Recent LLM-guided approaches frame tensor program optimization as an iterative decision process, but existing datasets provide only end-to-end optimized program pairs using token-inefficient representations, lacking verifiable step-level supervision and interpretability. As a result, LLMs struggle to make reliable single-step decisions in large combinatorial optimization spaces. We introduce Step-TP, a post-training dataset for tensor program optimization that provides grounded, atomic, step-level supervision with structured chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning. Step-TP forms a closed reasoning loop over intermediate program states, enabling reliable multi-step optimization rather than outcome imitation. Its design is guided by four principles: (i) a token-efficient, verifiable intermediate representation (IR) that deterministically lowers to TVM TIR; (ii) atomic and composable optimization strategies that decompose complex trajectories into interpretable single-step decisions; (iii) structured CoT supervision coupled with explicit IR-to-IR state transitions; and (iv) strategy filtering to balance coverage while preventing shortcut exploitation. The dataset and implementation are available at a GitHub link, https://github.com/LIUMENGFAN-gif/StepTP.

</details>


### [382] [Mapping the Schedule x Bit-Width Boundary in Sub-100M Quantisation-Aware Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25966)
*Christian Brandt Thomassen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: For sub-100M language models, the optimal learning rate schedule (33% warmdown) is consistent across FP16, INT8, and INT6 quantization-aware training, falsifying the hypothesis that lower bit-widths need different schedules. INT4 shows a size-dependent pattern: decisive schedule preference above 50M parameters, noise-dominated below.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether optimal learning-rate schedules for quantization-aware training (QAT) depend on bit-width, specifically testing if lower-precision training (INT6/INT4) requires different schedule tuning compared to higher precision (FP16/INT8).

Method: Large-scale factorial grid experiments with 720 runs (Phase 2) across bit-widths (FP16/INT8/INT6), model sizes (15M-100M), warmdown fractions, learning rate magnitudes, and seeds. Follow-up 625-run experiment (Phase 5) extended testing to AdamW optimizer, cosine schedule, longer training, wider size range (5M-350M), and INT4 quantization from 3M to 100M. Used statistical analysis and weight-to-grid-distance probing.

Result: Optimal 33% warmdown schedule works consistently for FP16, INT8, and INT6 across all tested sizes, falsifying the main hypothesis. INT6 follows log-linear scaling laws that accurately predict performance. INT4 shows a clear threshold: decisive preference for 33% warmdown at 50M+ parameters, but noise-dominated results below 50M with no statistically significant schedule preference.

Conclusion: For sub-100M models, tune learning rate schedule once at FP16 and apply unchanged to INT8/INT6 QAT. For INT4, use 33% warmdown above 50M parameters; below 50M, schedule choice doesn't matter significantly as results are dominated by seed-level noise.

Abstract: We test whether the optimal learning-rate schedule depends on bit-width during from-initialisation quantisation-aware training (QAT) for sub-100M decoder language models. A 720-run factorial grid (Phase 2) over bit-width x warmdown fraction x LR magnitude x model size x seed (FP16/INT8/INT6, 15M-100M, 5 seeds) finds the optimal warmdown is 33% at every (bit-width, size) cell. The primary hypothesis -- that INT6 QAT requires a different schedule than higher-precision training -- is falsified at FP16/INT8/INT6. A 625-run follow-up (Phase 5) probes the null along five axes: optimiser (AdamW), schedule shape (cosine), training length (up to 9x more iterations), an extended size sweep (5M-350M), and an INT4 sweep from 3M to 100M. The null is robust under all three setup changes. The INT6 penalty follows a log-linear scaling law whose fit on Phase 2 predicts the five held-out Phase 5 sizes (5M, 8M, 175M, 250M, 350M) within their 95% prediction intervals (5/5). For INT4 the picture is sharper than the higher precisions: at 50M and 100M, wd33 is decisively optimal (paired z ~ 12-15, 10/10 seeds); below 50M, across the six tested sizes from 3M to 30M, no individual size shows a statistically significant schedule preference and the per-size mean penalty oscillates within seed-level noise. The boundary is therefore a transition between a noise-dominated regime below 50M and a decisive wd33 regime at and above 50M, not a clean wd10 region. A weight-to-grid-distance probe falsifies the simplest mechanism for the FP16/INT8/INT6 null result (rapid grid-snapping): pre-warmdown, INT6-QAT weights sit at essentially the same distance from the INT6 grid as FP16 weights (ratio ~ 1.04). Practical recommendation: at sub-100M scale, tune the LR schedule once at FP16 and apply unchanged to INT8/INT6 QAT; for INT4 at 50M+ use wd33; for INT4 below 50M the schedule choice is in the noise.

</details>


### [383] [Hidden in Plain Tokens: Simply Robust, Gradient-Free Watermark for Synthetic Audio](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25967)
*Georgios Milis,Yubin Qin,Yihan Wu,Heng Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel gradient-free watermarking method for synthetic audio that leverages vocabulary redundancy via community detection, achieving state-of-the-art detectability and robustness without requiring model finetuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current inference-time watermarks for autoregressive models don't work well for continuous modalities like audio due to discretization inconsistencies, and existing solutions require finetuning tokenizers which removes the training-free advantage of watermarks.

Method: Proposes a gradient-free watermarking method for synthetic audio that exploits vocabulary redundancy in discretization. Uses community detection to obtain a reduced vocabulary, theoretically analyzes token error impact on detection, and effectively mitigates errors through the reduced vocabulary approach.

Result: The method boosts watermark detectability by several orders of magnitude compared to existing approaches, achieves built-in robustness to audio modifications, and establishes a new state-of-the-art for token-level watermarks in multimedia.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that powerful and robust audio watermarking can be achieved without model finetuning by leveraging the inherent redundancy in discrete representation learning, establishing a new state-of-the-art approach for synthetic audio provenance.

Abstract: As policy catches up with the capabilities of generative AI, watermarking is central to content provenance efforts. Inference-time watermarks for autoregressive models are unfit for continuous modalities due to discretization inconsistencies. Existing methods overcome this by finetuning the modality tokenizers, nullifying the watermark's training-free advantage. In this work, motivated by the vocabulary redundancy of discretization, we propose an elegant solution for powerful and robust watermarking of synthetic audio. We theoretically analyze the impact of token errors on watermark detection, and effectively mitigate them using a reduced vocabulary obtained via community detection. Thorough experiments showcase that our gradient-free method can boost detectability by several orders of magnitude, while also achieving built-in robustness to audio modifications. Broadly, we discover a new state-of-the-art for token-level watermarks in multimedia, which simply arises from the nature of discrete representation learning.

</details>


### [384] [Fuzzy PyTorch: Rapid Numerical Variability Evaluation for Deep Learning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25991)
*Inés Gonzalez-Pepe,Hiba Akhaddar,Tristan Glatard,Yohan Chatelain*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fuzzy PyTorch is a framework that integrates stochastic arithmetic into PyTorch to efficiently evaluate numerical variability in deep learning models, achieving 5-60x runtime improvements over existing tools while scaling from 1 to 341 million parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning is increasingly applied to diverse tasks, making understanding floating-point arithmetic variability essential for robust and reliable model performance. Current tools for assessing such variability need to be scalable, efficient, and integrate seamlessly with existing frameworks with minimal code modifications.

Method: Integrates stochastic arithmetic into PyTorch through Probabilistic Rounding with Instruction Set Management, which interfaces with Verificarlo (a numerical analysis compiler). The library offers stochastic rounding mode and a novel "up-down rounding" mode.

Result: Fuzzy PyTorch maintains model performance while achieving runtime reductions of 5x to 60x compared to Verrou (state-of-the-art tool). Successfully scales from models with 1 to 341 million parameters, confirming applicability across small and large architectures.

Conclusion: Fuzzy PyTorch provides an efficient, scalable, and practical solution for assessing numerical variability in deep learning, enabling researchers and practitioners to quantify and manage floating-point uncertainty without compromising performance or computational efficiency.

Abstract: We introduce Fuzzy PyTorch, a framework for rapid evaluation of numerical variability in deep learning (DL) models. As DL is increasingly applied to diverse tasks, understanding variability from floating-point arithmetic is essential to ensure robust and reliable performance. Tools assessing such variability must be scalable, efficient, and integrate seamlessly with existing frameworks while minimizing code modifications. Fuzzy PyTorch enables this by integrating stochastic arithmetic into PyTorch through Probabilistic Rounding with Instruction Set Management, a novel library interfacing with Verificarlo, a numerical analysis compiler. The library offers stochastic rounding mode and a novel mode; up-down rounding. Comparative evaluations show Fuzzy PyTorch maintains model performance and achieves runtime reductions of 5x to 60x versus Verrou, a state-of-the-art tool. We further demonstrate scalability by running models from 1 to 341 million parameters, confirming applicability across small and large DL architectures. Overall, Fuzzy PyTorch provides an efficient, scalable, and practical solution for assessing numerical variability in deep learning, enabling researchers and practitioners to quantify and manage floating-point uncertainty without compromising performance or computational efficiency.

</details>


### [385] [Deployment-complete benchmarking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25997)
*El Mustapha Mansouri,Keigo Arai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces deployment-complete benchmarking, which tests whether benchmark evidence actually supports specific deployment actions, revealing that traditional benchmark scores often fail to determine appropriate deployment decisions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmarks increasingly guide real-world deployment decisions, procurement, and scientific screening, but current benchmark scores only measure specific responses without necessarily supporting the actual deployment actions they're meant to inform.

Method: The authors propose deployment-complete benchmarking, which evaluates whether benchmark evidence determines deployment actions. They analyze evidence fibers (sets of evidence producing the same benchmark score), identifying mixed fibers where evidence is ambiguous. They use completion curves to quantify evidence needed to resolve ambiguity and apply conformal coverage methods to test practical deployment scenarios.

Result: Results show poor transfer from controlled benchmarks to unmeasured deployment scenarios (94.98% coverage dropping to 10.07%). Even zero benchmark error certified only 45.4% of candidates. Public audits revealed widespread incompleteness, including 97.9% mixed fibers in Tox21 and zero median certifiable fraction in main benchmarks. The certify-then-acquire approach reduced false decisions dramatically (from 1.19% to 0.027% in Tox21, 20.3% to 0.128% in JARVIS).

Conclusion: Traditional benchmark scores are insufficient for deployment decisions. Deployment-ready benchmarks should report evidence, supported actions, ambiguity, and completion costs rather than just scores. The proposed framework helps identify when benchmark evidence actually supports deployment actions and reduces false deployment decisions.

Abstract: Benchmarks increasingly guide deployment, procurement and scientific screening, yet a score supports only the response it records, not necessarily the deployment action. We introduce deployment-complete benchmarking, which tests whether benchmark evidence determines a deployment action. A benchmark is complete for a claim exactly when the action is constant on each evidence fiber; mixed fibers expose missing deployment information, and completion curves quantify the evidence required to resolve ambiguity. In controlled response spaces, benchmark-channel conformal coverage of 94.98% transferred poorly to an unmeasured deployment channel (10.07%), whereas response-rank intervals achieved 94.91% coverage; even zero benchmark error certified only 45.4% of candidates at the largest residual size. Public audits revealed incompleteness, including 97.9% mixed Tox21 fibers and zero median certifiable fraction in main Matbench and JARVIS audits. In held-out replays, certify-then-acquire reduced false decisions from 1.19% to 0.027% in Tox21 and from 20.3% to 0.128% in JARVIS, while changing model choice and identifying deployment-relevant probes. Deployment-ready benchmarks should report evidence, supported actions, ambiguity and completion cost rather than scores alone.

</details>


### [386] [Causal methods for LLM development and evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25998)
*Dennis Frauen,Marie Brockschmidt,Konstantin Hess,Haorui Ma,Yuchen Ma,Abdurahman Maarouf,Maresa Schröder,Jonas Schweisthal,Yuxin Wang,Athiya Deviyani,Sonali Parbhoo,Rahul G. Krishnan,Stefan Feuerriegel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper argues that causal inference methods are underutilized but essential for reliable LLM development and evaluation, as many key questions are inherently causal in nature.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM development relies heavily on empirical iteration but many central questions are inherently causal, yet causal methods are surprisingly underrepresented despite their suitability for intervention-based questions.

Method: The paper explains how causal methods address LLM development challenges, maps opportunities across the entire LLM pipeline (pretraining, alignment, routing, agentic workflows, evaluation), and discusses new research opportunities.

Result: The analysis demonstrates that causal methods are well-suited for addressing challenges like confounding in logged data, biased evaluation judges, and non-stationary deployment environments that make purely predictive approaches fragile.

Conclusion: Causal methods are potentially underutilized but essential for ensuring reliable and scientifically grounded LLM development and evaluation, offering principled approaches to identification and estimation.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) development is currently driven by large-scale empirical iteration over data mixtures, reward models, routing strategies, and evaluation pipelines. Here, we argue that many central questions in LLM development and evaluation are inherently causal: What is the effect of adding a data domain during pretraining? How do annotator preferences change when LLMs generate text in a different style? Should a prompt be routed to a larger or smaller model given inference cost constraints? In general, causal methods are well-suited to such settings where interventions change outcomes but, surprisingly, are underrepresented in LLM development. Our contribution is threefold: (1) We explain how causal methods can help develop modern LLM development and evaluation: LLM development relies heavily on logged data, which are often subject to confounding and distribution shifts; evaluation uses learned but potentially biased judges; and deployment environments are non-stationary. These conditions make purely predictive approaches fragile and create opportunities for principled identification and estimation methods from causal inference. (2) We further map opportunities for causal methods in the entire LLM development pipeline, including pretraining, alignment, routing, agentic workflows, and evaluation. (3) We discuss new research opportunities around leveraging causal methods for LLM development and evaluation. Overall, we argue that causal methods are potentially underutilized for the LLM development and evaluation pipeline, despite the fact that such methods can ensure a reliable and scientifically grounded design.

</details>


### [387] [Learning in Low-Dimensional Subspaces: Orthogonal Bottlenecks for Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26012)
*Aleksandar Todorov,Matthia Sabatelli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes using fixed orthonormal projection bottlenecks in deep RL representations to constrain features to low-dimensional subspaces without harming performance, demonstrating that task-relevant structure is often intrinsically low-dimensional.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep RL agents typically use high-dimensional neural representations, but evidence suggests task-relevant value and policy structure may be intrinsically low-dimensional. The authors aim to exploit this low-dimensional structure by imposing representation-level constraints.

Method: A simple fixed orthonormal projection bottleneck is inserted to constrain encoder features to a low-dimensional subspace. This requires no auxiliary objectives, pretraining, or changes to the underlying RL algorithm. The approach is architecture-agnostic and lightweight.

Result: Empirical results show baseline performance is either matched or improved once bottleneck dimension exceeds a small task-dependent threshold. Value representations can often be compressed to extremely low dimensions without performance loss, with minimal sufficient dimension depending more on environment complexity than encoder width.

Conclusion: The work supports a representation-space interpretation of the manifold hypothesis in RL and positions orthogonal bottlenecks as an effective, lightweight mechanism for shaping RL representations by exploiting intrinsic low-dimensional structure.

Abstract: Deep reinforcement learning (RL) agents commonly rely on high-dimensional neural representations, despite growing evidence that task-relevant value and policy structure may be intrinsically low-dimensional. In this work, we present a simple yet effective representation-level prior that inserts a fixed orthonormal projection to constrain encoder features to a low-dimensional subspace, requiring no auxiliary objectives, pretraining, or changes to the underlying RL algorithm. Under a linear realizability assumption, we prove that when the bottleneck dimension exceeds the intrinsic rank of the optimal value function in feature space, the bottleneck preserves expressivity and leaves the induced gradient dynamics unchanged up to an equivalent low-dimensional parameterization. Empirically, we find that across both single and multi-task benchmarks, baseline performance is either matched or improved once the bottleneck dimension exceeds a small task-dependent threshold; in many cases, value representations can be compressed to extremely low dimensions without loss, and the minimal sufficient dimension depends far more on environment complexity than encoder width. In addition, we analyze representation geometry and find that orthogonal bottlenecks stabilize feature norms and are associated with higher effective rank. Together, these results support a representation-space interpretation of the manifold hypothesis in reinforcement learning and position orthogonal bottlenecks as a lightweight, architecture-agnostic mechanism for shaping RL representations.

</details>


### [388] [AdvantageFlow: Advantage-Weighted Least Squares for RL in Flow Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26013)
*Branislav Kveton,Anup Rao,Subhojyoti Mukherjee,Krishna Kumar Singh,Viet Dac Lai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AdvantageFlow is a forward-process reinforcement learning algorithm for rectified flow models that optimizes advantage-weighted forward-process prediction, stabilized by rollout policy regularization, outperforming existing methods on image generation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods like Flow-GRPO optimize the reverse process in rectified flow models, but there's a need for more effective forward-process reinforcement learning approaches for improved image generation performance.

Method: AdvantageFlow optimizes an advantage-weighted forward-process prediction loss instead of the reverse process. To address instability issues (when advantages are negative and loss becomes non-convex), the method introduces rollout policy regularization to reduce variance, derived from fitting a local reward-improving target distribution.

Result: AdvantageFlow outperforms both Flow-GRPO and a state-of-the-art forward-process RL baseline (negative-aware fine-tuning) on image generation tasks using Stable Diffusion 3.5 Medium.

Conclusion: Forward-process optimization with advantage-weighting and proper stabilization via policy regularization provides a superior approach for reinforcement learning in rectified flow models, offering better performance than existing reverse-process and forward-process methods.

Abstract: We introduce AdvantageFlow, a forward-process reinforcement learning algorithm for rectified flow models. Unlike Flow-GRPO, which optimizes the reverse process, we optimize an advantage-weighted forward-process prediction loss. This optimization problem is unstable when advantages are negative and the loss becomes non-convex. We stabilize it by rollout policy regularization, which reduces variance and arises from fitting a local reward-improving target distribution. We evaluate AdvantageFlow on image generation tasks with Stable Diffusion 3.5 Medium. It outperforms both Flow-GRPO and a state-of-the-art forward-process RL baseline based on negative-aware fine-tuning.

</details>


### [389] [Retrieval-Augmented Detection of Potentially Abusive Clauses in Chilean Terms of Service](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26019)
*Christoffer Loeffler,Tomás Rey Pizarro,Daniel Ignacio Miranda Vásquez,Andrea Martínez Freile*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A retrieval-augmented generation framework for detecting abusive clauses in Chilean Terms of Service contracts, using local models with hybrid retrieval methods and a new annotated corpus of 100 contracts with 10,029 clauses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Online Terms of Service often function as contracts of adhesion with asymmetries that expose consumers to potentially abusive clauses, and Chilean law makes assessment challenging due to varying legal standards - some clauses clearly violate consumer law while others depend on broader principles like good faith and contractual imbalance.

Method: A retrieval-augmented generation framework combining efficient clause detection, hybrid dense-sparse retrieval, reranking, and prompt augmentation to support medium-sized open-weight language models for local execution. Also introduces the Chilean Abusive Terms of Service Extended corpus with 100 contracts and 10,029 annotated clauses across 24 legally grounded categories (illegal, dark, and gray clauses).

Result: Experiments show retrieval-augmented prompting substantially improves performance, enabling local models to approach larger cloud-based systems at lower computational and token cost. The framework successfully detects and classifies abusive clauses in Chilean Terms of Service.

Conclusion: The study provides an effective framework for automated detection of abusive clauses in consumer contracts, contributes a refined legal annotation scheme, and offers a practical design for AI-assisted consumer contract review that balances performance with computational efficiency for local deployment.

Abstract: Online Terms of Service often function as contracts of adhesion, creating asymmetries that may expose consumers to potentially abusive clauses. In Chile, assessing such clauses is legally challenging because some provisions clearly violate mandatory consumer law, whereas others depend on broader standards such as good faith and contractual imbalance. We present a retrieval-augmented generation framework for the automated detection and classification of potentially abusive clauses in Chilean Terms of Service. Designed for local execution, it combines efficient clause detection, hybrid dense--sparse retrieval, reranking, and prompt augmentation to support medium-sized open-weight language models. We also introduce the Chilean Abusive Terms of Service Extended corpus, comprising 100 contracts and 10,029 annotated clauses in 24 legally grounded categories spanning illegal, dark, and gray clauses. Experiments comparing commercial and open-weight language models, fine-tuned encoders, and traditional baselines show that retrieval-augmented prompting substantially improves performance and enables local models to approach larger cloud-based systems at lower computational and token cost. The study also contributes a refined legal annotation scheme and a practical design for AI-assisted consumer contract review.

</details>


### [390] [Length Generalization with Log-Depth Recurrent Units](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26035)
*Charles Pert,Dalal Alrajeh,Alessandra Russo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MLP-LDRU (Log-Depth Recurrent Unit) achieves near-perfect length generalization on 21 regular language tasks by approximating recurrence through parallel reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Length generalization is a persistent challenge where recurrent models suffer from positional biases and transformers are limited by fixed computational depth. Regular languages provide a useful testbed for evaluating length generalization capabilities.

Method: Proposes MLP-LDRU, a Log-Depth Recurrent Unit that captures associativity-biased operators designed to approximate recurrence through parallel reduction. This architecture aims to overcome the limitations of both recurrent and attention-based models.

Result: On 21 regular-language tasks (standard benchmarks and new prefix languages), MLP-LDRU achieves 100% out-of-distribution accuracy on 18 tasks and ≥99.9% on the remaining 3 when increasing max training length, outperforming comparable recurrent and attention-based models. Also performs competitively on ListOps and NLP classification benchmarks.

Conclusion: MLP-LDRU demonstrates superior length generalization capabilities on regular languages while maintaining competitive performance on broader tasks, suggesting it successfully addresses fundamental limitations of existing architectures for sequence processing.

Abstract: Length generalization remains a persistent challenge for neural networks: recurrent models tend to suffer from positional biases, while transformers are constrained by fixed computational depth. Regular languages provide a frequently used testbed for evaluating length generalization, as label prediction can be checked for any sequence length. We propose MLP-LDRU, a type of Log-Depth Recurrent Unit, which captures a class of associativity-biased operators designed to approximate recurrence through parallel reduction. We evaluate MLP-LDRU on 21 regular-language tasks, consisting of standard benchmarks and new prefix languages, where it achieves 100% out-of-distribution accuracy on 18 tasks and at least 99.9% on the remaining 3 when increasing max training length, outperforming comparable recurrent and attention-based models. We further evaluate MLP-LDRU beyond regular languages on ListOps and NLP classification benchmarks, where it performs competitively.

</details>


### [391] [Neuronal Stochastic Attention Circuit (NSAC) for Probabilistic Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26061)
*Waleed Razzaq,Yun-Bo Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NSAC is a biologically-inspired continuous-time attention architecture that models attention logits as Ornstein-Uhlenbeck SDE solutions, producing probabilistic outputs with principled uncertainty estimates for both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current continuous-time representation learning lacks reliable uncertainty quantification, especially within CT attention architectures. There's a need for principled uncertainty estimates that can handle irregular continuous-time data and provide interpretable probabilistic outputs.

Method: Introduces Neuronal Stochastic Attention Circuit (NSAC), which reformulates attention logit computation as solution to Ornstein-Uhlenbeck stochastic differential equation modulated by input-dependent nonlinear gates derived from C.elegans Neuronal Circuit Policies wiring. Uses Gaussian distribution over logits that propagates through logistic-normal distribution over attention weights. Employs two-term objective function combining Gaussian negative log-likelihood with epistemic-separation regularizer for joint aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty quantification.

Result: Empirical evaluation across diverse tasks (irregular CT function approximation, multivariate regression, long-range forecasting, Industry 4.0, autonomous vehicle lane-keeping) shows NSAC remains competitive in accuracy against baselines, produces well-calibrated uncertainty estimates, and offers interpretability at neuronal cell level.

Conclusion: NSAC provides a novel biologically-inspired CT attention architecture that enables reliable quantification of both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty while maintaining competitive accuracy and offering neuronal-level interpretability, addressing a significant gap in continuous-time representation learning.

Abstract: Reliable quantification of uncertainty estimates in continuous-time (CT) representation learning remains nascent, particularly within CT attention architectures. We introduce the Neuronal Stochastic Attention Circuit (NSAC), a novel biologically-inspired CT attention architecture that reformulates attention logit computation as the solution of an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck stochastic differential equation modulated by input-dependent, nonlinear interlinked gates derived from repurposed C.elegans Neuronal Circuit Policies (NCPs) wiring mechanism. It induces Gaussian distribution over logits that propagates principled stochasticity through logistic-normal distribution over attention weights to yield probabilistic output. A two-term objective function combining Gaussian negative log-likelihood with an epistemic-separation regularizer enforces higher predictive variance and enables joint quantification of aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty. Empirically, we implement NSAC in a diverse set of learning tasks including: (i) irregular CT function approximation; (ii) multivariate regression; (iii) long-range forecasting; (iv) Industry 4.0; and (v) the lane-keeping of autonomous vehicles. We observe that the NSAC remains competitive against several baselines in terms of accuracy and produces reasonably well-calibrated uncertainty estimates while being interpretable at the neuronal cell level.

</details>


### [392] [Conditional KRR: Injecting Unpenalized Features into Kernel Methods with Applications to Kernel Thresholding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26067)
*Rustem Takhanov,Zhenisbek Assylbekov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Conditional kernel ridge regression (conditional KRR) outperforms standard KRR when the regression function's component in the function class F is dominant, with theoretical risk analysis showing a reduction to standard KRR plus an O(1/√N) term.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conditionally positive definite kernels and their associated native spaces provide learning methods that can be viewed as linear regression with features specified by function class F, followed by standard KRR on residuals. These methods have attracted increasing attention but need better statistical understanding.

Method: Analyze conditional KRR by reducing its behavior to standard KRR with a residual kernel. Theoretical analysis shows this reduction is possible with an additional O(1/√N) term in expected test risk. Study two settings: 1) when F consists of first k principal eigenfunctions from Mercer decomposition, and 2) when F consists of k random features from random feature representation.

Result: The reduction from conditional KRR to standard KRR with residual kernel is possible with an additional O(1/√N) term. Conditional KRR outperforms standard KRR when the F-component of the regression function is more pronounced than the residual part. The two settings (principal eigenfunctions vs random features) are closely related.

Conclusion: Conditional KRR is a theoretically sound method that can outperform standard KRR when the regression function has significant components in the specified function class F. The reduction framework provides a way to analyze its statistical properties, and both theoretical and experimental results confirm its advantages in relevant scenarios.

Abstract: Conditionally positive definite (CPD) kernels are defined with respect to a function class $\mathcal{F}$. It is well known that such a kernel $K$ is associated with its native space (defined analogously to an RKHS), which in turn gives rise to a learning method -- called conditional kernel ridge regression (conditional KRR) due to its analogy with KRR -- where the estimated regression function is penalized by the square of its native space norm. This method is of interest because it can be viewed as classical linear regression, with features specified by $\mathcal{F}$, followed by the application of standard KRR to the residual (unexplained) component of the target variable. Methods of this type have recently attracted increasing attention.
  We study the statistical properties of this method by reducing its behavior to that of KRR with another fixed kernel, called the residual kernel. Our main theoretical result shows that such a reduction is indeed possible, at the cost of an additional term in the expected test risk, bounded by $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{N})$, where $N$ is the sample size and the hidden constant depends on the class $\mathcal{F}$ and the input distribution.
  This reduction enables us to analyze conditional KRR in the case where $K$ is positive definite and $\mathcal{F}$ is given by the first $k$ principal eigenfunctions in the Mercer decomposition of $K$. We also consider the setting where $\mathcal{F}$ consists of $k$ random features from a random feature representation of $K$. It turns out that these two settings are closely related. Both our theoretical analysis and experiments confirm that conditional KRR outperforms standard KRR in these cases whenever the $\mathcal{F}$-component of the regression function is more pronounced than the residual part.

</details>


### [393] [Rethinking Weak Supervision in Anomaly Detection: A Comprehensive Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26068)
*Xu Yao,Siyuan Zhou,Wu Zhenbo,Chaochuan Hou,Shuang Liang,Shiping wang,Hailiang Huang,Songqiao Han,Minqi Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: WSADBench is the first unified benchmark for Weakly Supervised Anomaly Detection, evaluating 36 algorithms across 4 modalities under varying label conditions to reveal fundamental insights about current WSAD approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current weakly supervised anomaly detection research has developed in three isolated directions (incomplete, inexact, and inaccurate supervision) without a unified framework to assess whether they address unique challenges or share fundamental mechanics. There's a need for comprehensive evaluation across different weakly supervised scenarios.

Method: The paper introduces WSADBench, a unified benchmark that establishes standardized protocols to evaluate 36 algorithms across 4 modalities by systematically varying label quantity, granularity, and quality. It conducts over 700K experiments to benchmark diverse approaches from specialized WSAD methods to advanced tabular foundation models.

Result: Four key insights: 1) Strong correlations exist between weak supervision scenarios, challenging current isolated research. 2) Specialized WSAD algorithms excel only in extreme label-scarcity but are dominated by tabular foundation models and general classification methods as supervision increases or in OOD scenarios. 3) Unlabeled data shows inconsistent utility with marginal gains compared to label refinement. 4) Models exhibit asymmetric sensitivity to different types of label noise.

Conclusion: WSADBench provides the first comprehensive benchmark for weakly supervised anomaly detection, revealing that current isolated research directions are fundamentally connected. It demonstrates that tabular foundation models and general classification methods outperform specialized WSAD algorithms in many scenarios, and it establishes a standardized framework for future WSAD research, released as open-source.

Abstract: Weakly supervised anomaly detection (WSAD) has developed in three primary directions: incomplete, inexact, and inaccurate supervision. However, these directions remain isolated, lacking a unified framework to assess whether they address unique challenges or share fundamental mechanics. This paper introduces WSADBench, the first benchmark that unifies evaluation across distinct weakly supervised scenarios, benchmarking diverse approaches from specialized WSAD methods to advanced tabular foundation models. WSADBench establishes standardized protocols to evaluate 36 algorithms across 4 modalities by systematically varying label quantity, granularity, and quality, revealing the performance boundaries of various methods. Based on over 700K experiments, WSADBench reveals four critical insights: (i) Strong intrinsic correlations exist between these weak supervision scenarios, challenging the isolation of current research directions. (ii) Specialized WSAD algorithms excel only in extreme label-scarcity regimes but are quickly dominated by tabular foundation models and general classification methods as supervision increases or in OOD scenarios. (iii) Unlabeled data shows inconsistent utility across settings, with marginal gains compared to label refinement. (iv) Models exhibit asymmetric sensitivity to different types of label noise. We release WSADBench as an open-source benchmark with code and datasets to facilitate future WSAD research: https://github.com/SUFE-AILAB/WSADBench.

</details>


### [394] [Active Query Synthesis for Preference Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26072)
*Namrata Nadagouda,Nauman Ahad,Maegan Tucker,Mark A. Davenport*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Info-Synth: A novel active query synthesis framework with confidence-aware response modeling that generates optimal queries in continuous space for efficient preference learning, overcoming computational bottlenecks and handling ambiguous pairwise comparisons.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficient learning of user preferences is crucial but requires costly labeled data. Standard active learning methods are computationally expensive due to pool-based evaluation, and they assume all query feedback is equally reliable, ignoring that pairwise queries between nearly identical or entirely dissimilar items yield ambiguous, low-confidence responses.

Method: The paper introduces: 1) A confidence-aware response model that explicitly accounts for ambiguous comparisons; 2) Info-Synth, an active query synthesis framework that generates optimal queries by maximizing a mutual information-based objective within continuous space; 3) Two strategies (Pair M-dist and Pair Opt-dist) that extend Info-Synth to select effective queries even when restricted to finite query pools.

Result: The framework demonstrates versatility and performance across three domains: synthetic preference learning, constrained text summary datasets, and subjective, continuous-space controller gain tuning for a simulated mobile robot.

Conclusion: The proposed Info-Synth framework with confidence-aware modeling addresses both the computational limitations of pool-based active learning and the reliability issues of pairwise query feedback, enabling more efficient and robust preference learning across diverse applications.

Abstract: Efficient learning of user preferences is crucial for many modern decision making systems but typically requires costly labeled data. Active learning reduces this cost, yet standard methods are computationally expensive due to pool-based evaluation. Further, most methods assume all query feedback is equally reliable, ignoring that pairwise queries between nearly identical or entirely dissimilar items yield ambiguous, low-confidence responses. To address the issue of feedback reliability, we introduce a novel confidence aware response model that explicitly accounts for these ambiguous comparisons. To overcome the computational bottleneck of pool-based evaluation, we propose an active query synthesis framework, Info-Synth that generates optimal queries by maximizing a mutual information-based objective within a continuous space. Moreover, we propose two strategies, Pair M-dist and Pair Opt-dist, that extend Info-Synth to select effective queries even when restricted to finite query pools. We demonstrate our framework's versatility and performance across synthetic preference learning, constrained text summary datasets, and subjective, continuous-space controller gain tuning for a simulated mobile robot.

</details>


### [395] [Global Convergence of Wasserstein Policy Gradient for Entropy-Regularized Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26078)
*Zhaoyu Zhu,Rui Gao,Shuang Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Wasserstein Policy Gradient (WPG) for RL has favorable global convergence properties due to Bellman-induced Polyak-Lojasiewicz geometry, despite lacking standard convexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While WPG is appealing for continuous-control RL due to its optimal-transport approach, its global convergence properties remain poorly understood because standard Langevin analysis doesn't apply to RL's Bellman recursive structure.

Method: Develop global convergence theory by exploiting Bellman structure: using Bellman residual's KL representation, Bellman contraction relating residual to optimality gap, Bellman resolvent connecting value improvement to relative Fisher information, combined with uniform log-Sobolev inequality for evolving Gibbs policies.

Result: Established distributional Polyak–Łojasiewicz condition, proved regularity and uniform bounds for discretization error control, and showed geometric contraction up to discretization bias.

Conclusion: Entropy-regularized RL exhibits favorable PL-type geometry induced by Bellman recursion, enabling global convergence of WPG despite lacking traditional convexity, with practical discretization analysis.

Abstract: Wasserstein policy gradient (WPG) is a policy optimization method for reinforcement learning (RL) that exploits the optimal-transport geometry of action distributions. For the entropy-regularized RL objective, WPG evolves each state-conditional policy by transporting it along the action gradient of the soft Q-function together with a Langevin-type diffusion. Despite its appeal for continuous-control problems, its global convergence properties remain poorly understood. Standard Langevin analyses do not directly apply, because the RL objective depends on the policy through the Bellman recursion rather than through a static convex functional, and the Langevin drift is determined by the soft Q-function, whose regularity must be controlled along the policy iterates.
  In this paper, we develop a global convergence theory for WPG by exploiting the Bellman structure of entropy-regularized RL. We show that the role usually played by convexity can be replaced by a Bellman-based argument: the soft Bellman residual admits a statewise KL representation with respect to a Gibbs policy; Bellman contraction relates this residual to the global optimality gap; and a Bellman resolvent identity connects value improvement to relative Fisher information. Combined with a uniform log-Sobolev inequality (LSI) for the evolving Gibbs family, these ingredients yield a distributional Polyak--Łojasiewicz condition. We further establish the regularity and uniform bounds needed to control the discretization error, thereby obtaining geometric contraction up to a discretization bias. Conceptually, our analysis shows that although entropy-regularized RL is not convex in the usual flat sense, the Bellman recursion induces a favorable Polyak--Lojasiewicz-type (PL) geometry that supports global convergence of WPG.

</details>


### [396] [OrpQuant: Geometric Orthogonal Residual Projection for Multiplier-Free Power-of-Two Transformer Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26092)
*Maoyang Xiang,Bo Wang,Tao Luo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ORP is an algorithm-hardware co-design framework that addresses structural limitations of Power-of-Two quantization for edge deployment of LLMs/ViTs by using orthogonal residual projection to enhance angular resolution while maintaining hardware efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Edge deployment of LLMs and Vision Transformers faces memory limitations and timing bottlenecks from dense MAC arrays. Power-of-Two quantization offers hardware efficiency but suffers from Low Angular Resolution Regime, especially at sub-4-bit thresholds, degrading high-dimensional feature manifolds.

Method: Orthogonal Residual Projection (ORP) - a dual-basis geometric projection approach that formulates quantization as orthogonal decomposition to synthesize higher-resolution residual lattice using strictly shift-and-add operations, with analytical solver reducing calibration time significantly.

Result: ORP achieves 6.10 perplexity on LLaMA-2-7B under 3-bit constraints, outperforming MAC-intensive baselines like AWQ. Reduces calibration time to ~15 minutes for LLaMA-2-7B. Hardware synthesis shows mitigation of timing bottlenecks from dense multiplier trees.

Conclusion: ORP successfully addresses geometric limitations of PoT quantization through algorithm-hardware co-design, enabling efficient edge deployment of large models while maintaining accuracy and hardware efficiency across modalities.

Abstract: The deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision Transformers (ViTs) on edge devices is significantly constrained by memory limitations and the critical timing bottlenecks introduced by dense Multiply-Accumulate (MAC) arrays. In the ultra-low bit regime, logarithmic Power-of-Two (PoT) quantization provides a hardware-efficient alternative by replacing MAC operations with bit-shifts. However, the non-uniform exponential lattice is inherently limited by a \textbf{Low Angular Resolution Regime}, a structural flaw that becomes particularly pronounced at sub-4-bit thresholds, leading to a notable degradation of high-dimensional feature manifolds.
  To address this geometric limitation, we propose Orthogonal Residual Projection (ORP), an algorithm-hardware co-design framework. By formulating quantization as a dual-basis geometric projection, ORP adaptively synthesizes a higher-resolution residual lattice using strictly shift-and-add operations. Furthermore, ORP's analytical solver offers a practical alternative to computationally intensive gradient-based optimization, reducing the full-model calibration time for LLaMA-2-7B to approximately \textbf{15 minutes}.
  Extensive evaluations demonstrate ORP's applicability across modalities and its hardware efficiency. Under the 3-bit (W3/A16) constraint, ORP achieves a perplexity of 6.10 on LLaMA-2-7B, comparing favorably to conventional MAC-intensive baselines like AWQ without relying on asymmetric scaling, while maintaining competitive accuracy in 4-bit scenarios. At the silicon level, standard-cell RTL synthesis at a 28nm node indicates that ORP effectively mitigates the timing bottlenecks associated with dense multiplier trees.

</details>


### [397] [Goal-driven Bayesian Optimal Experimental Design for Robust Decision-Making Under Model Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26093)
*Jinwoo Go,Xiaoning Qian,Byung-Jun Yoon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GoBOED is a goal-driven Bayesian optimal experimental design framework that optimizes experiments specifically for downstream decision quality rather than general information gain about parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional BOED maximizes information gain about model parameters, but in decision-critical settings, not all parameter uncertainty reduction improves actual decisions. Only parameter directions relevant to the decision objective matter for decision quality.

Method: GoBOED combines an amortized variational posterior surrogate with a differentiable convex decision layer, enabling gradient-based design optimization that is fully decision-focused. This allows the framework to focus experimental design on parameter directions that actually affect decision outcomes.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows GoBOED gradients are insensitive to parameter directions irrelevant to decision objectives. Empirically, GoBOED outperforms goal-agnostic BOED in source localization, epidemic management, and pharmacokinetic control tasks, achieving better decision alignment and revealing wider near-optimal design windows.

Conclusion: Goal-driven experimental design via GoBOED provides superior decision quality compared to information-gain maximization approaches, with formal justification for why focusing on decision-relevant parameters leads to more flexible and effective experimental designs.

Abstract: Bayesian optimal experimental design (BOED) selects experiments to maximize information gain about model parameters. However, in decision-critical settings, reducing parameter uncertainty does not necessarily improve downstream decisions, as only specific parameter directions relevant to the objective truly matter. We propose GoBOED, a goal-driven BOED framework that directly optimizes experimental designs for a specified decision-making objective. GoBOED combines an amortized variational posterior surrogate with a differentiable convex decision layer, enabling gradient-based design optimization that is fully decision-focused. We theoretically show that GoBOED gradients are insensitive to parameter directions irrelevant to the decision objective, providing a formal justification for why goal-driven design achieves equivalent decision quality over a wider set of experimental designs than information-gain maximization. Empirically, across source localization, epidemic management, and pharmacokinetic control, GoBOED identifies designs that better align with downstream decision objectives and reveals that near-optimal design windows are substantially wider than those predicted by goal-agnostic BOED approaches.

</details>


### [398] [Forgetting in Language Models: Capacity, Optimization, and Self-Generated Replay](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26097)
*Martin Marek,Dongkyu Cho,Shikai Qiu,Rumi Chunara,Pavel Izmailov,Andrew Gordon Wilson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Language models can use their own generated samples as effective replay data to prevent forgetting during finetuning, overcoming the traditional need for stored exemplars.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional approaches to mitigate catastrophic forgetting in continual learning require replaying stored exemplars from prior tasks, which is often impractical. The paper aims to find a more practical solution for language models.

Method: The method uses language models' ability to sample from their own training distribution, treating these self-generated samples as replay data during finetuning. The approach is evaluated under different capacity conditions and learning rates.

Result: Self-generated samples serve as effective replay data that nearly eliminates forgetting. However, forgetting persists when models are pretrained close to saturation (little remaining capacity). Replay enables fast, high-learning-rate finetuning without forgetting when capacity is not limiting.

Conclusion: Language models can effectively mitigate forgetting by using their own generated samples as replay data, providing a practical alternative to storing exemplars, though capacity constraints remain an important consideration for models near saturation.

Abstract: Models trained on a new task typically degrade on prior tasks, a phenomenon known as forgetting. Traditionally, mitigating forgetting has required replaying stored exemplars from prior tasks, which is often impractical. By contrast, language models can sample from their own training distribution, and we show that these self-generated samples serve as effective replay data, nearly eliminating forgetting. We find that forgetting nonetheless persists when the model has little remaining capacity: models pretrained close to saturation cannot absorb new information without overwriting prior knowledge. When capacity is not the limiting factor, low learning rates reduce forgetting but require substantially more training steps. Replay breaks this tradeoff, enabling fast, high-learning-rate finetuning without forgetting.

</details>


### [399] [Looped Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26106)
*Sanghyun Lee,Chunsan Hong,Seungryong Kim,Jonghyun Lee,Jongho Park,Dongmin Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoopMDM introduces selective looping of early-middle transformer layers in masked diffusion models, achieving better training efficiency and performance with flexible compute scaling at inference time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Masked diffusion models (MDMs) show promise for language modeling, but optimal transformer architecture design for MDMs remains underexplored. There's a need for more efficient training and flexible compute scaling.

Method: Selectively looping early-middle transformer layers in masked diffusion models. This approach allows training-time depth-scaling without adding parameters and enables varying number of loops for flexible compute scaling at inference time.

Result: LoopMDM matches performance of same-size MDMs with up to 3.3× fewer training FLOPs, outperforms them on reasoning benchmarks (up to 8.5 points on GSM8K), surpasses deeper non-looped MDMs with comparable compute, and enables adaptive inference-time compute scaling.

Conclusion: Selective looping in MDMs is an effective architectural innovation that improves training efficiency and model performance while enabling flexible compute scaling. The approach is more effective than naive depth scaling and works by promoting interactions among masked positions.

Abstract: Masked diffusion models (MDMs) have emerged as a promising alternative to autoregressive models for language modeling, yet the effective design of transformer architectures for MDMs remains underexplored. In this paper, we show that selectively looping the early-middle transformer layers significantly improves both training efficiency and model performance in MDMs. We call this approach LoopMDM(Looped Masked Diffusion Model), which brings two key benefits: looping layers at training-time yields a depth-scaling effect without adding parameters, while varying the number of loops at inference-time enables flexible compute scaling. Despite the simplicity, the results are striking: across multiple pre-training corpora, LoopMDM matches the performance of same-size MDMs with up to 3.3 fewer training FLOPs, while its final performance outperforms them on various reasoning benchmarks, including up to 8.5 points on GSM8K. It even surpasses deeper non-looped MDMs trained with comparable per-step compute, indicating that selective looping is more effective than naive depth scaling. Furthermore, LoopMDM can scale inference-time compute by increasing the number of loops. Adaptively adjusting the number of loops throughout the sampling process further yields additional gains in compute efficiency while maintaining performance. Lastly, with attention analysis, we provide evidence that looping is effective in MDMs by promoting interactions among masked positions. Our code and weights will be publicly released.

</details>


### [400] [Prism: A Plug-in Reproducible Infrastructure for Scalable Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26110)
*Jun-Tao Tang,Yu-Cheng Shi,Zhen-Hao Xie,Da-Wei Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Prism is a plug-in codebase for Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning that separates algorithmic development from backbone implementation via plugin registration, enabling reproducible and scalable MCIT research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world deployment of Multimodal Large Language Models requires continuous adaptation to emerging tasks (MCIT), but current research suffers from severe engineering bottlenecks where methods are implemented by directly modifying base MLLM codebases, causing substantial implementation overhead, method-specific architectures, limited code reuse, and hindered fair comparison.

Method: Introduces Prism - a plug-in reproducible codebase that separates algorithmic development from backbone implementation via lightweight plugin registration mechanism, allowing new strategies to be integrated as independent plugins without modifying the underlying MLLM codebase. It natively supports large-scale training pipelines.

Result: Prism eliminates structural fragmentation and accelerates method development for MCIT research. The codebase is publicly available and enables reproducible and scalable MCIT experimentation.

Conclusion: Prism addresses the engineering bottlenecks in Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning research by providing a unified, plugin-based codebase that facilitates fair comparison, code reuse, and accelerates methodological development in this important emerging field.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) achieve versatility by reformulating diverse tasks into a unified instruction-following framework via instruction tuning. However, real-world deployment requires continuous adaptation to emerging tasks, motivating Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning (MCIT). Despite its growing importance, current MCIT research is hindered by severe engineering bottlenecks. Existing methods are typically implemented by directly modifying the base MLLM codebase, which imposes substantial implementation overhead and yields method-specific architectures that severely limit code reuse and fair comparison. To address this, we introduce Prism, a plug-in reproducible codebase specifically designed for scalable MCIT research. It separates algorithmic development from the backbone implementation via a lightweight plugin registration mechanism, enabling new strategies to be integrated as independent plugins without modifying the underlying MLLM codebase, thereby eliminating structural fragmentation and accelerating method development. Prism natively supports widely used large-scale training pipeline, thereby enabling reproducible and scalable MCIT experimentation. Code is available at https://github.com/LAMDA-CL/Prism.

</details>


<div id='cs.MA'></div>

# cs.MA [[Back]](#toc)

### [401] [TEAM-SimHRA: A Team-Based Simulation Framework for Human Reliability Analysis Using Multi-Agent Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23927)
*Xingyu Xiao,Jiejuan Tong,Jingang Liang,Haitao Wang*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: TEAM-SimHRA uses multi-agent LLM simulation to model team-level failure in nuclear control rooms as emergent interaction dynamics rather than individual errors, validated against real accidents with promising results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current human reliability analysis methods fail to capture team-level failures in nuclear control rooms, which arise from complex interaction dynamics, delayed diagnosis, suppressed dissent, and authority-driven error propagation rather than isolated individual errors.

Method: TEAM-SimHRA is a multi-agent large language model simulation framework that reconceptualizes human reliability as an interaction-driven emergent property. It models collective cognition, role-conditioned authority dynamics, and real-time communication suppression across evolving accident scenarios, unlike traditional static error probability approaches.

Result: Validated against Three Mile Island (1979) and Chernobyl (1986) accidents, the framework achieved face-validity pass rates of 43.5% and 52.6% respectively. It reproduced near-historical decision delays (134.8 vs. 138 min), perfect communication suppression stability, and full authority pressure cascades at historically accurate propagation depth.
